topics.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromagnetic Radiation (EMR)** is the propagation of energy through space in the form of mutually perpendicular, self-sustaining electric ($\vec{E}$) and magnetic ($\vec{H}$) field oscillations, traveling at the speed of light in a direction orthogonal to both fields. In the context of **Illumination Engineering**, EMR forms the foundation of all lighting phenomena, as visible light is simply a narrow band of the electromagnetic spectrum detectable by the human eye.

The relationship governing all EMR is the fundamental wave equation:

$$c = f \cdot \lambda$$

where:
- $c$ = **speed of light in vacuum** = **$2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s** (often approximated as $3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- $f$ = frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**
- $\lambda$ = wavelength in **meters (m)**

### 1.2 The Electromagnetic Spectrum — KTU Definition

The **electromagnetic spectrum** is the continuous distribution of all possible frequencies of electromagnetic radiation, ranging from extremely low frequency (ELF) radio waves to high-energy gamma rays. For illumination engineering, only a tiny portion — the **visible band (380 nm to 780 nm)** — is of direct interest, but adjacent bands (UV and IR) heavily influence photometry, vision, and material interactions.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of EMR like ripples on a pond.** When you drop a stone in still water, you see circular ripples moving outward. Now imagine the "stone" is an accelerating electron, and instead of water ripples, it creates synchronized, invisible **electric and magnetic ripples** that travel through empty space. Light is just one particular "size" of ripple that our eyes happen to be tuned to detect. Bigger ripples (longer wavelengths) = radio waves; smaller ripples (shorter wavelengths) = X-rays. The "speed of the ripple" is always the same — the speed of light.

### 1.4 Dual Nature of Light

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Electromagnetic radiation exhibits a **wave-particle duality**.
> - **Wave nature:** explains interference, diffraction, polarization.
> - **Particle (quantum) nature:** explains photoelectric effect, blackbody radiation. Each quantum of light is a **photon** with energy:
>
> $$E = h \cdot f = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$$
>
> where **Planck's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**.

This duality is the bedrock for understanding **luminous efficacy**, **spectral power distribution**, and **quantum efficiency** of modern light sources like LEDs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Electromagnetic Spectrum — Band-by-Band Breakdown

The spectrum is divided into regions based on wavelength (or equivalently, frequency or photon energy). For illumination technology, the critical bands are:

| Band | Wavelength Range | Key Property | Illumination Relevance |
|------|------------------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Ultraviolet (UV)** | **1 nm – 380 nm** | Invisible, photochemical | Sterilization, curing, blacklight, UV LEDs |
| **Visible Light** | **380 nm – 780 nm** | Detected by human eye | Primary subject of illumination engineering |
| **Infrared (IR)** | **780 nm – 1 mm** | Heat radiation | Thermal discomfort, IR heating, night vision |
| **Microwave** | 1 mm – 1 m | Penetrates non-conductors | Microwave lamps (specialty) |
| **Radio / Others | > 1 m | Lowest energy | Not relevant to illumination |

### 2.2 Blackbody Radiation — The Heart of Light Source Physics

> [!NOTE]
> A **blackbody** is an idealized object that absorbs **all** incident EMR (absorptivity = 1) and emits the **maximum possible thermal radiation** at every wavelength for a given temperature. Real lamps (tungsten filament, sun, incandescent globes) approximate blackbody behavior.

#### Key Laws of Blackbody Radiation (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):

**1. Planck's Radiation Law** — Spectral emissive power per unit wavelength:

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \left[ \frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k T)} - 1} \right] \;\; \text{W/m}^2 \text{ per meter}$$

where $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is the Boltzmann constant.

**2. Stefan-Boltzmann Law** — Total emitted power per unit area:

$$E = \sigma T^4 \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²K⁴** is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

**3. Wien's Displacement Law** — Peak emission wavelength:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m·K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wien's law explains why an incandescent lamp at 2800 K peaks in the **near-infrared** (most energy is wasted as heat, not light — hence low luminous efficacy ~15 lm/W), while the sun at 5778 K peaks near **500 nm** (green-yellow, giving high luminous efficacy). This is the fundamental reason LEDs and fluorescent lamps outperform incandescent sources.

### 2.3 Radiometric vs. Photometric Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **radiometric** (physical energy) and **photometric** (eye-weighted) units. Radiometric units measure **total energy** at all wavelengths; photometric units weight energy by the **CIE V(λ) luminous efficacy function**, which peaks at **683 lm/W at 555 nm**.

| Quantity | Radiometric (Energy) | Photometric (Visual) | Unit Conversion |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Power | Radiant Flux ($\Phi_e$) | Luminous Flux ($\Phi_v$) | $\Phi_v = 683 \int V(\lambda) \Phi_{e,\lambda} d\lambda$ |
| Intensity | Radiant Intensity ($I_e$) | Luminous Intensity ($I_v$) | candela (cd) |
| Exitance | Irradiance / Radiant Exitance | Luminous Exitance | lm/m² |
| Incidence | Irradiance ($E_e$) | Illuminance ($E_v$) | lux (lx) |
| Brightness | Radiance ($L_e$) | Luminance ($L_v$) | cd/m² |
| Efficacy | — | Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$) | **lm/W** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Formulas You MUST Memorize for Module 1:**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $c = f \lambda$ | Wave equation | m/s |
| 2 | $E = h f = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $E = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann total emission | W/m² |
| 4 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wien's displacement law | m·K |
| 5 | $\Phi_v = 683 \int V(\lambda) \Phi_{e,\lambda} d\lambda$ | Photometric conversion | lumens (lm) |

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Lamp design:** Choosing filament temperature to balance visible output vs. heat loss.
- **Solar energy:** Photovoltaic cells respond to specific wavelength bands; matching cell bandgap to solar spectrum maximizes efficiency.
- **Optical fiber communication:** IR wavelengths are chosen for minimum attenuation in silica fibers.
- **Health & safety:** UV-C (100–280 nm) germicidal action; IR-A (780–1400 nm) retinal hazard; understanding these bands is critical for lighting safety standards (IEC 62471).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Wave Equation to Photon Energy

> **Goal:** Show how the wave equation $c = f\lambda$ combines with Planck's quantization to yield the photon energy formula $E = hf$.

**Step 1 — Start with the classical wave relationship.**

Every electromagnetic wave obeys:

$$c = f \lambda$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to express frequency.**

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Planck's quantum hypothesis.**

Max Planck postulated (1900) that energy is exchanged in discrete quanta:

$$E = h f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $f$.**

$$E = h \cdot \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Final photon energy equation.**

$$\boxed{E = \frac{h c}{\lambda} = h f}$$

**Numerical check (KTU-style):** A sodium street lamp emits yellow light at $\lambda = 589$ nm. Find the photon energy.

$$E = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{589 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.89 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$E = 3.375 \times 10^{-19} \;\; \text{J}$$

Convert to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):

$$E = \frac{3.375 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.107 \;\; \text{eV}$$

> **Interpretation:** A single photon of sodium-yellow light carries **2.107 eV** of energy. This is exactly the energy of the famous sodium D-line electronic transition (3p → 3s).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

> **Goal:** Integrate Planck's spectral law over all wavelengths to obtain the total emitted power.

**Step 1 — Planck's spectral radiance (per unit wavelength):**

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k T)} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Total emitted power (sum over all wavelengths):**

$$E(T) = \int_0^{\infty} E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the dimensionless variable** $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T}$, so that $\lambda = \frac{hc}{x k T}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^2 k T} dx$.

**Step 4 — The integral becomes (after substitution):**

$$E(T) = \frac{2 \pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the definite integral (standard result):**

$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$E(T) = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the constant as Stefan-Boltzmann constant:**

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

**Step 8 — Final Stefan-Boltzmann Law:**

$$\boxed{E = \sigma T^4}$$

**Numerical Check:** A tungsten filament operates at 2800 K. What is its radiant exitance?

$$E = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2800)^4$$

$$E = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.1466 \times 10^{13}$$

$$E = 3.485 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$

> **Interpretation:** A small filament area (e.g., $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4}$ m²) emits ~348 W. The actual visible (luminous) portion is only **~10%** of this — the rest is IR, illustrating why incandescent lamps are thermally inefficient.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Wien's Displacement Law (Conceptual)

> **Goal:** Find the wavelength at which $E_\lambda$ is maximum for a given $T$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dE_\lambda}{d\lambda} = 0$$

**Step 2 — The transcendental equation obtained is:**

$$5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$$

where $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\max} k T}$.

**Step 3 — Numerical solution yields:**

$$x = 4.9651$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda_{\max}$:**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{hc}{4.9651 \, k T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} \;\; \text{m·K}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m·K}}$$

**Numerical Check:** The sun's surface temperature is $T = 5778$ K. Find $\lambda_{\max}$.

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{5778} = 5.016 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{m} = 501.6 \;\; \text{nm}$$

> **Interpretation:** Peak emission lies in the **green-yellow** region, exactly where the human eye is most sensitive (V(λ) peaks at 555 nm). This is **not coincidence** — human vision evolved under sunlight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Electromagnetic Spectrum — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RADIO WAVES\n greater than 1 m] --> B[MICROWAVES\n 1 mm to 1 m]
    B --> C[INFRARED IR\n 780 nm to 1 mm]
    C --> D[VISIBLE LIGHT\n 380 to 780 nm]
    D --> E[ULTRAVIOLET UV\n 1 nm to 380 nm]
    E --> F[X RAYS\n 0.01 to 10 nm]
    F --> G[GAMMA RAYS\n less than 0.01 nm]

    D --> D1[Violet\n 380 to 450 nm]
    D --> D2[Blue\n 450 to 495 nm]
    D --> D3[Green\n 495 to 570 nm]
    D --> D4[Yellow\n 570 to 590 nm]
    D --> D5[Orange\n 590 to 620 nm]
    D --> D6[Red\n 620 to 780 nm]

    style A fill:#9999cc
    style B fill:#9999cc
    style C fill:#cc6666
    style D fill:#ffff66
    style E fill:#6666cc
    style F fill:#333399,color:#fff
    style G fill:#660066,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#ccccff
    style D2 fill:#9999ff
    style D3 fill:#99ff99
    style D4 fill:#ffff99
    style D5 fill:#ffcc99
    style D6 fill:#ff9999
```

### 4.2 Blackbody Radiation — Energy vs Wavelength Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Increase Temperature T] --> T2[Spectral curve shifts\n to shorter wavelength]
    T2 --> T3[Wien Law:\n lambda max inversely proportional to T]
    T2 --> T4[Total area under curve\n increases as T to the power 4]
    T4 --> T5[Stefan Boltzmann Law:\n E equals sigma T to the power 4]

    T3 --> T6[Engineering Impact:\n Hotter filament peaks in visible\n Higher luminous efficacy]

    T5 --> T7[Engineering Impact:\n More total power radiated\n But most is wasted as IR heat]

    style T1 fill:#ffcc66
    style T2 fill:#ffff99
    style T3 fill:#99ff99
    style T4 fill:#99ccff
    style T5 fill:#cc99ff
    style T6 fill:#ff9999
    style T7 fill:#ff9999
```

### 4.3 Radiometric vs Photometric Quantities — Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Electromagnetic Source] --> R[Radiometric Energy\n Measured in W]
    S --> P[Photometric Energy\n Measured in lm]

    R --> R1[Radiant Flux Phi e]
    R --> R2[Radiant Intensity I e]
    R --> R3[Irradiance E e]
    R --> R4[Radiance L e]

    P --> P1[Luminous Flux Phi v]
    P --> P2[Luminous Intensity I v]
    P --> P3[Illuminance E v]
    P --> P4[Luminance L v]

    R1 --> CONV{Weight by\n V lambda}
    CONV --> P1
    R2 --> CONV2{Weight by\n V lambda}
    CONV2 --> P2
    R3 --> CONV3{Weight by\n V lambda}
    CONV3 --> P3
    R4 --> CONV4{Weight by\n V lambda}
    CONV4 --> P4

    CONV -.-> K[Constant 683 lm per W\n at 555 nm peak]

    style S fill:#ffcc66
    style R fill:#99ccff
    style P fill:#99ff99
    style K fill:#ff9999
    style CONV fill:#ffff99
    style CONV2 fill:#ffff99
    style CONV3 fill:#ffff99
    style CONV4 fill:#ffff99
```

### 4.4 EM Wave Structure — Electric and Magnetic Field Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z[Direction of Propagation\n Z axis] --> WAVE[EM Wave Traveling Forward]
    WAVE --> EF[Electric Field E\n Oscillates in X axis]
    WAVE --> MF[Magnetic Field H\n Oscillates in Y axis]
    EF --> REL[E perpendicular to H]
    MF --> REL
    REL --> BOTH[Both perpendicular to propagation\n Self sustaining wave]

    style Z fill:#6699ff,color:#fff
    style WAVE fill:#ffcc66
    style EF fill:#ff6666,color:#fff
    style MF fill:#66cc66,color:#fff
    style REL fill:#ffff99
    style BOTH fill:#cc99ff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define electromagnetic radiation. State the relationship between wavelength, frequency, and the speed of light.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Electromagnetic radiation is the form of energy that propagates through space as coupled, mutually perpendicular, oscillating electric and magnetic fields, traveling at the speed of light in a direction perpendicular to both fields.
>
> **Relationship (1 mark):**
>
> $$c = f \lambda$$
>
> where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $f$ is frequency in Hz, $\lambda$ is wavelength in m.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
State and explain **Wien's displacement law**. Mention its significance in illumination engineering.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1.5 marks):** The wavelength at which a blackbody's spectral emission is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:
>
> $$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m·K}$$
>
> **Explanation (1 mark):** As temperature increases, $\lambda_{\max}$ shifts to shorter wavelengths (e.g., red-hot → white-hot).
>
> **Significance (0.5 mark):** It explains why incandescent filaments are designed at temperatures that shift peak emission toward the visible band to improve luminous efficacy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE from the following — full 14 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **electromagnetic spectrum** and the position of the **visible band** within it. Mention the wavelength range of each color in the visible spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** from **Planck's radiation law**. A tungsten filament at 2900 K has a surface area of $0.5$ cm². Calculate the total radiant power emitted. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Electromagnetic Spectrum and Visible Band (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to the spectrum block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the full layout.]

**Explanation (3 marks):** The electromagnetic spectrum is the continuous range of all types of EMR, classified by wavelength or frequency. The principal bands in increasing order of frequency (decreasing wavelength) are:

| Band | Wavelength Range |
|------|------------------|
| Radio waves | > 1 m |
| Microwaves | 1 mm – 1 m |
| Infrared | 780 nm – 1 mm |
| **Visible** | **380 nm – 780 nm** |
| Ultraviolet | 1 nm – 380 nm |
| X-rays | 0.01 – 10 nm |
| Gamma rays | < 0.01 nm |

**Visible band color breakdown (1 mark):**

| Color | Wavelength (nm) |
|-------|------------------|
| Violet | 380 – 450 |
| Blue | 450 – 495 |
| Green | 495 – 570 |
| Yellow | 570 – 590 |
| Orange | 590 – 620 |
| Red | 620 – 780 |

> The human eye is sensitive **only** to this narrow 380–780 nm range, making it the foundation of photometric (visual) measurement.

---

**(b) Stefan-Boltzmann Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):** [Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes. The key steps are:]

Starting from Planck's spectral law, integrate over all $\lambda$ from 0 to ∞, substitute $x = hc/(\lambda kT)$, evaluate the standard integral $\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$, and obtain:

$$E = \sigma T^4, \quad \sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

**Numerical (2 marks):**

Given: $T = 2900$ K, $A = 0.5$ cm² = $0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m² = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m².

Total emitted power:

$$P = A \cdot \sigma T^4$$

$$P = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2900)^4$$

$$P = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 7.0728 \times 10^{13}$$

$$P = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 4010.3$$

$$\boxed{P = 0.2005 \;\; \text{W} \approx 200.5 \;\; \text{mW}}$$

> **Note:** Most of this power is in the IR region; only a fraction becomes visible light.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **dual nature of electromagnetic radiation**. Derive the relationship between photon energy and wavelength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sodium-vapour street lamp emits at 589 nm. Calculate: (i) the frequency of emission, (ii) the photon energy in joules and electron-volts, and (iii) the radiant power per steradian if 1 watt of luminous flux is radiated isotropically. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dual Nature of EMR + Energy Derivation (7 marks):**

**Conceptual explanation (3.5 marks):**

> Electromagnetic radiation exhibits **wave-particle duality**, meaning it behaves as both a classical wave and a stream of discrete energy packets (**photons**).
> - **Wave behavior** is evident in **interference** (Young's double slit), **diffraction** (single-slit patterns), and **polarization** (Malus's law). These phenomena require a continuous oscillating field.
> - **Particle behavior** is evident in the **photoelectric effect** (explained by Einstein in 1905 using photon theory) and **Compton scattering**. A photon of frequency $f$ carries discrete energy $E = hf$.

Both descriptions are valid and complementary; the appropriate model depends on the phenomenon.

**Derivation (3.5 marks):** [Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes.]

Starting from the wave equation:

$$c = f\lambda \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

Applying Planck's quantum hypothesis $E = hf$:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}}$$

---

**(b) Sodium Lamp Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $\lambda = 589$ nm = $589 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $\Phi_v = 1$ W (luminous, but treated as radiant in the sub-question for the power per steradian), $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.

**(i) Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{589 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.093 \times 10^{14} \;\; \text{Hz}$$

**(ii) Photon energy (3 marks):**

$$E = h f = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 5.093 \times 10^{14}$$

$$E = 3.375 \times 10^{-19} \;\; \text{J}$$

Converting to eV:

$$E = \frac{3.375 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.107 \;\; \text{eV}$$

**(iii) Radiant intensity (radiant power per steradian) (2 marks):**

For isotropic emission, total solid angle $\Omega = 4\pi$ steradians. With total power $P = 1$ W:

$$I_e = \frac{P}{\Omega} = \frac{1}{4\pi} = 0.0796 \;\; \text{W/sr}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit mismatches:** Always convert nm → m before plugging into $E = hc/\lambda$. Forgetting this is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing eV and J:** KTU expects both units when the question asks for energy — partial marks are lost if only one is given.
> 3. **Integration limits:** In the Stefan-Boltzmann derivation, students often forget the upper limit is $\infty$, not $\lambda_{\max}$. Remember: total power = area under entire curve, not just the peak.
> 4. **Radiometric vs photometric confusion:** "Watt" is radiometric; "lumen" is photometric. Never write "lm" when the formula is in watts.
> 5. **Wien's law sign error:** $\lambda_{\max}$ decreases as $T$ increases — do **not** write the inverse proportionality as $\lambda_{\max} \propto T$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electromagnetic Radiation**

- ✅ Electromagnetic radiation consists of mutually perpendicular **E** and **H** fields propagating at the **speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ Master equation: $c = f\lambda$. Higher frequency ⇒ shorter wavelength ⇒ higher photon energy.
- ✅ **Photon energy:** $E = hf = hc/\lambda$. Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.
- ✅ **Visible spectrum:** 380–780 nm. Remember the order: **V**iolet **B**lue **G**reen **Y**ellow **O**range **R**ed.
- ✅ **Blackbody radiation:** An ideal emitter; real lamps approximate it.
- ✅ **Planck's law:** Spectral emissive power distribution; the most general blackbody equation.
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann law:** $E = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ — gives **total** power radiated.
- ✅ **Wien's displacement law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K — gives the **peak** wavelength.
- ✅ **Dual nature:** Wave (interference, diffraction) AND particle (photoelectric effect, photons).
- ✅ **Photometric vs radiometric:** Multiply radiometric by $V(\lambda)$ and the constant **683 lm/W** to get photometric.
- ✅ **Engineering takeaway:** Hotter sources peak in the visible — this is why modern lamps push operating temperatures up (halogen, HID, white LEDs).
- ✅ **Wien shift toward blue:** As temperature rises, peak emission shifts from red → yellow → white → blue (think: red-hot poker heating up).

---

*End of Module 1 — Electromagnetic Radiation Notes*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PEEET418 Illumination Technology*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visible Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Visible Spectrum # 🌈 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Visible Spectrum** is the narrow band of the **Electromagnetic (EM) Spectrum** that the human eye can perceive as distinct colors, lying between the ultraviolet (UV) and infrared (IR) regions. According to the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE)** standard adopted in illumination engineering, the visible spectrum spans approximately: $$\lambda \in [380 \text{ nm}, 780 \text{ nm}]$$ where $\lambda$ is the **wavelength** measured in **nanometers (nm)** or equivalently in **Angstroms (Å)** where $1 \text{ nm} = 10 \text{ Å}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The visible spectrum is a foundational concept in Module 1 because the entire discipline of **Illumination Engineering** is concerned with quantifying, designing, and controlling optical radiation within this narrow band for human visual comfort and task performance. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a vast musical keyboard stretching across many octaves — from extremely low bass notes (radio waves, kilometers long) to piercingly high treble notes (gamma rays, sub-atomic scale). On this "cosmic keyboard," the **visible spectrum occupies less than one single octave** — an incredibly tiny sliver of the entire electromagnetic range. Now imagine each wavelength of visible light is a different musical note: | Light Wavelength Analogy | Musical Note | |---|---| | Red light (~700 nm) | Low note | | Yellow light (~580 nm) | Middle note | | Violet light (~380 nm) | High note | When all these "notes" play together in equal proportion, you perceive **white light** (sunlight). When isolated, each wavelength produces a distinct **spectral color** (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** A rainbow is nature's perfect prism demonstration. Sunlight entering each raindrop gets refracted (bent) at slightly different angles depending on wavelength — short violet bends most, long red bends least. This separates white light into the visible spectrum we see as the rainbow arc. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ (≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s) | | Lower wavelength limit | $\lambda_{min}$ | **380 nm** (Violet end) | | Upper wavelength limit | $\lambda_{max}$ | **780 nm** (Red end) | | Frequency range | $f$ | **≈ 384 THz to 789 THz** | | Reference wavelength for photometry | $\lambda_{ref}$ | **555 nm** (peak human eye sensitivity) | --- ## 1.4 The Seven Spectral Colors (VIBGYOR) When white light passes through a prism or diffraction grating, it separates into a continuous band of colors: > [!NOTE] > **VIBGYOR Mnemonic:** **V**iolet → **I**ndigo → **B**lue → **G**reen → **Y**ellow → **O**range → **R**ed - **Violet (380–450 nm)** — shortest wavelength, highest frequency - **Indigo (450–475 nm)** - **Blue (475–495 nm)** - **Green (495–570 nm)** — peak sensitivity region for human eye - **Yellow (570–590 nm)** - **Orange (590–620 nm)** - **Red (620–780 nm)** — longest wavelength, lowest frequency > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always remember — the color sequence by wavelength is **VIBGYOR** (short → long), but the frequency sequence is the **reverse (ROYGBIV)** because $c = f\lambda$. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Electromagnetic Spectrum — Position of Visible Light The electromagnetic spectrum is the complete distribution of electromagnetic radiation arranged by wavelength or frequency. The visible spectrum is sandwiched between two non-visible bands: - **Ultraviolet (UV) region:** $\lambda < 380 \text{ nm}$ - **Visible region:** $380 \text{ nm} \le \lambda \le 780 \text{ nm}$ - **Infrared (IR) region:** $\lambda > 780 \text{ nm}$ ### Why only this narrow band? The human retina contains two types of photoreceptors: 1. **Cones** — responsible for **photopic (daylight) vision** and color discrimination. Three subtypes (S, M, L) are sensitive to short, medium, and long wavelengths respectively. 2. **Rods** — responsible for **scotopic (low-light) vision**, highly sensitive but **achromatic** (no color perception). The biochemical photopigments in these receptors (rhodopsin in rods, photopsins in cones) can only absorb photons whose energy lies within the visible window — a biological constraint set by millions of years of evolution. --- ## 2.2 Fundamental Governing Equations ### 2.2.1 Wave-Equation of Light Light behaves as an electromagnetic wave satisfying Maxwell's equations. The fundamental wave equation linking wavelength, frequency, and speed is: $$c = f \lambda$$ where: - $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (speed of light in vacuum) - $f$ = frequency in Hertz (Hz) - $\lambda$ = wavelength in meters (m) ### 2.2.2 Photon Energy Relation Light also has a particle nature (wave-particle duality). The energy of a single photon is given by Planck's equation: $$E = hf = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$ where: - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$ (Planck's constant) - $E$ = photon energy in Joules (J) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This dual nature is why some illumination phenomena (LED emission, photovoltaic conversion) are best explained using photon energy, while others (interference, diffraction) require the wave model. --- ## 2.3 Wavelength–Frequency Conversion Logic The fundamental relationship $c = f\lambda$ can be rearranged for practical conversions: $$f = \frac{c}{\lambda} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$ **Conversion shortcut for visible light:** $$f(\text{THz}) \approx \frac{300{,}000}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$$ --- ## 2.4 Photometric vs. Radiometric Quantities (KTU High-Yield) Illumination engineering distinguishes between **radiometric** (physical) and **photometric** (visual) quantities. This distinction is **CRITICAL** for KTU exams: | Physical Quantity (Radiometric) | Visual Quantity (Photometric) | Symbol Pair | |---|---|---| | Radiant Flux | Luminous Flux | $\Phi_e$ vs. $\Phi_v$ | | Radiant Intensity | Luminous Intensity | $I_e$ vs. $I_v$ | | Irradiance | Illuminance | $E_e$ vs. $E_v$ | | Radiance | Luminance | $L_e$ vs. $L_v$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why two systems?** Radiometric quantities measure *total* electromagnetic energy, regardless of whether the human eye can perceive it. Photometric quantities weight that energy by the **spectral luminous efficacy function** $V(\lambda)$ — essentially, "how strongly does the eye respond at wavelength $\lambda$?" The conversion relation: $$\Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_{e,\lambda} \cdot V(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$ where: - $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ (maximum spectral luminous efficacy at 555 nm) - $V(\lambda)$ = dimensionless relative spectral luminous efficacy (peaks at 1.0 at 555 nm) --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The visible spectrum concept is foundational to: - **LED lighting design:** Engineers select semiconductor bandgaps to emit specific wavelengths. - **Colorimetry:** Quantifying color using CIE XYZ or CIE RGB color spaces. - **Architectural lighting:** Choosing correlated color temperature (CCT) for ambience. - **Display technology:** RGB pixel design relies on primary visible wavelengths. - **Plant growth lighting:** Optimizing red (660 nm) and blue (450 nm) ratios for photosynthesis. --- ## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $c = f\lambda$ | Speed-wavelength-frequency relation | m/s, Hz, m | | 2 | $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (J) | | 3 | $f \approx \dfrac{3 \times 10^{17}}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | Frequency from wavelength | Hz | | 4 | $\lambda \approx \dfrac{3 \times 10^{17}}{f(\text{Hz})}$ | Wavelength from frequency | nm | | 5 | $\Phi_v = K_m \int \Phi_{e,\lambda} V(\lambda) d\lambda$ | Luminous flux from spectral radiant flux | lumens (lm) | | 6 | $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ | Max spectral luminous efficacy | lm/W | | 7 | $\lambda_{peak} = 555 \text{ nm}$ | Peak human eye sensitivity | nm | | 8 | Visible range: $380 \text{ nm} \le \lambda \le 780 \text{ nm}$ | CIE-defined visible window | nm | --- ## 2.7 Boundary Conditions & Special Cases - **Below 380 nm:** UV radiation — invisible but can cause photochemical damage (skin burn, eye cataract). - **Above 780 nm:** IR radiation — felt as heat but not seen. - **At 555 nm:** Human eye has maximum sensitivity (1.0 on relative scale). - **At 700 nm vs 400 nm:** Both are near the visibility limit but the eye is much more sensitive to green-yellow than violet. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Frequency Boundaries of the Visible Spectrum **Given:** - Lower wavelength limit of visible spectrum: $\lambda_{min} = 380 \text{ nm} = 380 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ - Upper wavelength limit of visible spectrum: $\lambda_{max} = 780 \text{ nm} = 780 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ **To Find:** The frequency boundaries $f_{min}$ and $f_{max}$ corresponding to the wavelength limits. **Step 1:** Starting from the fundamental wave equation: $$c = f \lambda$$ **Step 2:** Solve for frequency: $$f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute for the upper wavelength (lowest frequency): $$f_{min} = \frac{c}{\lambda_{max}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{780 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$ $$f_{min} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{7.8 \times 10^{-7}} \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_{min} = 0.3846 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz} = 3.846 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_{min} \approx 384.6 \text{ THz}}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute for the lower wavelength (highest frequency): $$f_{max} = \frac{c}{\lambda_{min}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{380 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$ $$f_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3.8 \times 10^{-7}} \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_{max} = 0.7895 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz} = 7.895 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 789.5 \text{ THz}}$$ **Conclusion:** The visible spectrum spans a frequency range of approximately **384.6 THz to 789.5 THz**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Photon Energy at 555 nm **Problem:** Calculate the energy of a single photon at the peak sensitivity wavelength of the human eye ($\lambda = 555$ nm). **Given Data:** - Wavelength: $\lambda = 555 \text{ nm} = 555 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$ - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ **Step 1:** Write the Planck photon energy equation: $$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values: $$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}) \times (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s})}{555 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the numerator: $$h \cdot c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26}$$ $$h \cdot c = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$ **Step 4:** Divide by wavelength: $$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{555 \times 10^{-9}} \text{ J}$$ $$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.55 \times 10^{-7}} \text{ J}$$ $$E = 0.3582 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 3.582 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 2.237 \text{ eV}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion check:** $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$. So $E = 3.582 \times 10^{-19} / 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \approx 2.236 \text{ eV}$ ✓ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Number of Photons from a 60 W Light Source **Problem:** A 60 W incandescent lamp emits approximately 8% of its electrical power as visible light. If the average wavelength of emitted light is 600 nm, calculate the number of visible photons emitted per second. **Given:** - Electrical power: $P = 60 \text{ W}$ - Visible fraction: $\eta = 0.08$ - Average wavelength: $\lambda = 600 \text{ nm}$ **Step 1:** Compute visible power: $$P_{vis} = \eta \times P = 0.08 \times 60 = 4.8 \text{ W} = 4.8 \text{ J/s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute photon energy at 600 nm: $$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{600 \times 10^{-9}} \text{ J}$$ $$E_{photon} = 3.313 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$ **Step 3:** Compute photons per second (photon flux): $$N = \frac{P_{vis}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{4.8}{3.313 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$N \approx 1.449 \times 10^{19} \text{ photons/s}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 1.45 \times 10^{19} \text{ photons per second}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This enormous quantity explains why illumination can be treated as a continuous wave phenomenon in most engineering applications — the discrete photon nature is only relevant at very low light levels or in quantum detectors. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Radiometric vs Photometric | Property | Radiometric Quantity | Photometric Quantity | Eye Response Curve | |---|---|---|---| | Variable measured | Total EM power | Eye-weighted power | $V(\lambda)$ | | Detector needed | Thermal or quantum detector | Eye / photometric sensor | Standardized | | Unit | Watt (W) | Lumen (lm) | Dimensionless | | Peak reference | Independent of wavelength | At $\lambda = 555$ nm | $V(555) = 1.0$ | | Useful for | Solar engineering, laser power | Indoor lighting design | Colorimetry | | Conversion factor | — | $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ | — | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Radio Waves
Lambda greater than 1 mm"] --> B["Microwaves
1 mm to 1 micrometer"] B --> C["Infrared IR
780 nm to 1 mm"] C --> D["VISIBLE SPECTRUM
380 nm to 780 nm"] D --> E["Ultraviolet UV
10 nm to 380 nm"] E --> F["X Rays
0.01 nm to 10 nm"] F --> G["Gamma Rays
Lambda less than 0.01 nm"] D -.-> D1["VIOLET 380 450 nm"] D -.-> D2["BLUE 450 495 nm"] D -.-> D3["GREEN 495 570 nm"] D -.-> D4["YELLOW 570 590 nm"] D -.-> D5["RED 590 780 nm"] style A fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#d8d8d8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#d4a000,stroke-width:3px style E fill:#ccccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D1 fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#333 style D2 fill:#6699ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style D3 fill:#66cc66,stroke:#333 style D4 fill:#ffff66,stroke:#333 style D5 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.2 Visible Spectrum Color Resolution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P["White Light Source
Sunlight or Lamp"] --> Q["Dispersion Element
Prism or Grating"] Q --> R1["380 nm Violet"] Q --> R2["450 nm Blue"] Q --> R3["495 nm Green"] Q --> R4["570 nm Yellow"] Q --> R5["590 nm Orange"] Q --> R6["620 nm Red"] R1 --> S1["Shortest wavelength
Highest photon energy
Highest frequency"] R6 --> S2["Longest wavelength
Lowest photon energy
Lowest frequency"] style P fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R1 fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#333 style R2 fill:#6699ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style R3 fill:#66cc66,stroke:#333 style R4 fill:#ffff66,stroke:#333 style R5 fill:#ffaa66,stroke:#333 style R6 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style S1 fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#333 style S2 fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.3 Photometric vs Radiometric Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Electromagnetic Source
Sun Lamp LED"] --> B["Spectral Power Distribution
Phi e lambda"] B --> C{"Conversion via
V lambda Curve"} C --> D["Radiometric Output
Watts"] C --> E["Photometric Output

Solar Energy Lasers"] E --> G["Engineering Use
Indoor Lighting Displays"] style A fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333 style E fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333 style F fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333 style G fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.4 Human Eye Spectral Response Curve (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["380 nm Violet"] --> M["V lambda Photopic Curve
Peaks at 1.0 at 555 nm"] B["450 nm Blue"] --> M C["555 nm Green Yellow
PEAK SENSITIVITY"] --> M D["650 nm Red"] --> M E["780 nm Deep Red"] --> M M --> N["Relative Response
V lambda equals 0 to 1.0"] N --> O["Luminous Flux
Phi v in lumens"] style A fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#333 style B fill:#6699ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#66cc66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style D fill:#ff6666,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style E fill:#990000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style M fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style N fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#333 style O fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333 ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the visible spectrum. State its wavelength range as per CIE standards.** **Model Answer:** The visible spectrum is the narrow band of electromagnetic radiation that the normal human eye can detect and interpret as light and color. It lies between the ultraviolet region (shorter wavelength) and the infrared region (longer wavelength). According to the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE)** standard: $$\lambda \in [380 \text{ nm}, 780 \text{ nm}]$$ This corresponds to a frequency range of approximately **384 THz to 789 THz**. The spectrum decomposes into seven recognizable colors: **Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, and Red (VIBGYOR)**. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Wavelength range (1 mark) + VIBGYOR list (1 mark). --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Explain why the human eye is most sensitive at 555 nm wavelength.** **Model Answer:** The human eye's photopic (cone-mediated) vision has a peak sensitivity at **555 nm** (yellow-green region) because: 1. **Photopigment absorption:** The medium-wavelength (M) and long-wavelength (L) cone photopsins have their peak absorption spectra near 555 nm. 2. **Solar spectral match:** Sunlight reaching Earth's surface peaks near 555 nm, so evolution tuned our eyes to this brightest natural wavelength. 3. **CIE standardization:** The CIE defined the photopic spectral luminous efficacy function $V(\lambda)$ which equals 1.0 at 555 nm by definition, with $K_m = 683$ lm/W as the conversion constant. $$\Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_{e,\lambda} \cdot V(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$ > **Mark Distribution:** Photopigment reasoning (1 mark) + CIE standardization (1 mark) + Mathematical relation (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### ⭐ **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the electromagnetic spectrum with a neat diagram. Discuss the position and significance of the visible spectrum within it. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A monochromatic light source emits at 650 nm. Calculate (i) the frequency, (ii) the photon energy in Joules and eV, and (iii) the wave number. **(7 Marks)** #### **Solution to (a):** The electromagnetic spectrum is the complete range of all possible electromagnetic radiation frequencies/wavelengths, classified into seven regions based on generation method, penetration capability, and biological effects. **Diagram (description for answer sheet):** | Region | Wavelength Range | Source | |---|---|---| | Radio waves | > 1 mm | Antennas, oscillators | | Microwaves | 1 mm – 1 µm | Klystron, magnetron | | Infrared | 780 nm – 1 mm | Hot bodies, IR LEDs | | **Visible** | **380 – 780 nm** | **Sun, lamps, LEDs** | | Ultraviolet | 10 nm – 380 nm | Mercury vapor, sun | | X-rays | 0.01 nm – 10 nm | X-ray tubes | | Gamma rays | < 0.01 nm | Radioactive decay | **Significance of visible spectrum in illumination:** - Only this band is detectable by the human eye. - Photometric units (lumen, lux, candela) are defined using $V(\lambda)$. - All light sources (incandescent, fluorescent, LED) are characterized by their output in this band. #### **Solution to (b):** **Given:** $\lambda = 650 \text{ nm} = 650 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$ **(i) Frequency:** $$f = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{650 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.615 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f \approx 4.615 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} = 461.5 \text{ THz}}$$ **(ii) Photon energy:** $$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{650 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{650 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.058 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$ In electron-volts: $$E = \frac{3.058 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.909 \text{ eV}$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 3.058 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 1.909 \text{ eV}}$$ **(iii) Wave number:** $$\bar{\nu} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{650 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} = 1.538 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{\nu} \approx 1.538 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{-1} = 15385 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$ --- ### ⭐ **Question Choice B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q. (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the difference between radiometric and photometric quantities. Why is the factor 683 lm/W used in the conversion? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An LED emits light at 460 nm with a luminous flux of 100 lm. Calculate (i) the radiant flux, (ii) the number of photons emitted per second, and (iii) the energy emitted per second. **(7 Marks)** #### **Solution to (a):** **Definitions:** - **Radiometric quantities** measure total electromagnetic power radiated by a source, irrespective of whether the human eye can perceive it. The base unit is the **Watt (W)**. - **Photometric quantities** measure EM power weighted by the human eye's spectral sensitivity. The base unit is the **Lumen (lm)**. **Block Diagram (text representation for exam):** ``` SOURCE → EM Radiation → [Radiometric Detector] → Watts ↓ [V(λ) Filter] ↓ Photometric Detector → Lumens ``` **Conversion equation:** $$\Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_{e,\lambda} \cdot V(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$ **Why $K_m = 683$ lm/W?** By definition, $K_m$ is the maximum possible value of spectral luminous efficacy, occurring at $\lambda = 555$ nm where $V(\lambda) = 1$. It represents the maximum number of lumens produced per watt of monochromatic radiation at the most efficient visible wavelength. At this wavelength, 1 W of radiant power at 555 nm = 683 lm exactly. #### **Solution to (b):** **Given:** $\lambda = 460 \text{ nm}$, $\Phi_v = 100 \text{ lm}$, $V(460) \approx 0.06$ (from CIE photopic curve) **(i) Radiant flux:** For monochromatic emission: $$\Phi_e = \frac{\Phi_v}{K_m \cdot V(\lambda)} = \frac{100}{683 \times 0.06}$$ $$\Phi_e = \frac{100}{40.98} \approx 2.44 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{\Phi_e \approx 2.44 \text{ W}}$$ **(ii) Number of photons per second:** $$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{460 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.321 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$ $$N = \frac{\Phi_e}{E_{photon}} = \frac{2.44}{4.321 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 5.65 \times 10^{18} \text{ photons/s}}$$ **(iii) Energy emitted per second:** $$E_{total} = \Phi_e \times 1 \text{ s} = 2.44 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{E_{total} \approx 2.44 \text{ J/s}}$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Confusing nm with m. Always convert: $460 \text{ nm} = 460 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$. Forgetting this $10^{-9}$ factor is the #1 cause of wrong answers. > > 2. **VIBGYOR vs ROYGBIV:** Students often write the color sequence wrong. By wavelength (increasing): **VIBGYOR**. By frequency (increasing): **ROYGBIV**. > > 3. **Forgetting $V(\lambda)$ in Photometric Conversion:** A common mistake is dividing luminous flux (lm) directly by 683 to get watts — this only works at exactly 555 nm. At other wavelengths, you MUST include $V(\lambda)$. > > 4. **Confusing Radiometric and Photometric Units:** Writing "lumen" for radiometric power or "watt" for luminous flux costs full marks. > > 5. **Speed of Light Value:** Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (exact to 3 sig figs), not $2.998 \times 10^8$ unless specified for high-precision problems. > > 6. **Planck's Constant:** Always state $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s in numerical problems to score full method marks. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Visible Spectrum** ✅ Visible spectrum range (CIE): **380 nm – 780 nm** ✅ Fundamental equation: $c = f\lambda$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s ✅ Photon energy: $E = hc/\lambda$ with $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s ✅ Color sequence by wavelength: **VIBGYOR** (Violet→Red) ✅ Color sequence by frequency: **ROYGBIV** (reverse order) ✅ Peak human eye sensitivity: **555 nm** (yellow-green) ✅ Maximum spectral luminous efficacy: $K_m = 683$ lm/W ✅ Radiometric quantity units: **Watts** ✅ Photometric quantity units: **Lumens (lm)**, **Lux (lx)**, **Candela (cd)** ✅ Conversion relation: $\Phi_v = K_m \int \Phi_{e,\lambda} V(\lambda) d\lambda$ ✅ Frequency range of visible spectrum: **≈ 384 THz to 789 THz** ✅ Wave number: $\bar{\nu} = 1/\lambda$ measured in cm⁻¹ or m⁻¹ ✅ Above visible → **Infrared (IR)** | Below visible → **Ultraviolet (UV)** ✅ Human eye receptors: **Cones (photopic, color vision)** vs **Rods (scotopic, low-light)** ✅ White light = superposition of all visible wavelengths; can be dispersed by a **prism** or **diffraction grating**. ✅ KTU high-yield numbers to memorize: $380$, $555$, $780$, $683$, $3 \times 10^8$, $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ --- > **End of Module 1 — Visible Spectrum Notes** | Prepared per **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)** Standards

topics.title: Human Eye and Light Perception

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Human Eye and Light Perception ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Light Perception? **Light Perception** is the physiological and psychological process by which the human visual system detects electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (approximately **380 nm to 780 nm**) and translates it into neurological signals, which are subsequently interpreted by the brain as visual images, brightness, and color. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > *"Light perception is the dual-process mechanism involving the photo-chemical stimulation of retinal photoreceptors (rods and cones) and the subsequent neural processing in the visual cortex that converts radiant energy into perceived brightness, contrast, and chromatic information."* The science of quantifying this perceptual response is called **Photometry**, as distinct from **Radiometry** (which measures raw radiant energy). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Eye as a Biological Camera Imagine the human eye as an extraordinarily sophisticated, self-adjusting digital camera: | Camera Component | Human Eye Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | Lens (objective) | **Cornea + Crystalline Lens** | Refracts and focuses light | | Aperture diaphragm | **Iris** | Controls amount of light entering | | Aperture opening | **Pupil** | Variable diameter hole | | Image sensor (CCD/CMOS) | **Retina (with rods & cones)** | Converts photons into electrical/neural signals | | Sensor memory card | **Optic Nerve → Visual Cortex** | Stores and processes the image | | Auto-exposure algorithm | **Pupillary Light Reflex + Adaptation** | Adjusts sensitivity to lighting | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Just as a camera has different ISO sensitivities and color filters (RGB), the eye has **rod cells** (high-sensitivity, black-and-white, low-light vision) and **three types of cone cells** (lower sensitivity, color vision). This dual-channel design allows humans to see across an enormous luminance range — from a candle flame (~10⁻³ cd/m²) to bright sunlight (~10⁶ cd/m²) — a span of **over nine decades**. ### 1.3 Why Does This Matter for Illumination Engineering? Every lighting design specification (lux levels, color rendering index, glare ratings, contrast ratios) is fundamentally calibrated to the **human visual response**, not to raw physical energy. Without understanding the eye, an illumination engineer cannot design lighting that is efficient, comfortable, or visually effective. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 Mapping):** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** of PEEET418: *"Understand the fundamental principles of light, vision, and the photopic/scotopic response of the human eye for designing lighting systems."* ### 1.4 Core Terminology Box - **Luminance (L):** The photometric measure of luminous intensity per unit projected area, in **cd/m²** (formerly nit). - **Illumination (E):** Luminous flux incident per unit area, in **lux (lx) = lm/m²**. - **Luminous Efficacy (K):** The ratio of luminous flux to radiant flux, in **lm/W**. Maximum theoretical value is **683 lm/W at 555 nm** for photopic vision. - **Visual Acuity:** The sharpness of vision, defined as the reciprocal of the angular resolution in minutes of arc. - **Adaptation:** The eye's ability to adjust its sensitivity to varying luminance levels. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomical Structure of the Human Eye — Detailed Breakdown The human eye is a roughly spherical organ (~24 mm diameter) composed of the following optically and neurologically active components: #### A. Optical Components (Light-Gathering System) 1. **Cornea** - Transparent, avascular front surface. - Provides ~**2/3 of the total refractive power** (~43 dioptres out of ~60 D total). - Fixed-focus, no accommodation. 2. **Aqueous Humor** - Clear watery fluid between cornea and lens. - Index of refraction ≈ **1.336**. 3. **Iris** - Pigmented, muscular diaphragm controlling pupil diameter. - Pupil diameter varies from **~2 mm (bright light)** to **~8 mm (dark)**. 4. **Pupil** - The variable-aperture opening, controlled by the iris. - Acts as the **f-stop** of the eye: $f/8.3$ (bright) to $f/2.1$ (dark). 5. **Crystalline Lens** - Flexible, biconvex, accommodates by changing curvature. - Refractive index ranges from **1.386 to 1.406**. - Provides ~**1/3 of refractive power** plus all accommodation. 6. **Vitreous Humor** - Gelatinous fluid filling ~80% of the eyeball. - Index of refraction ≈ **1.337**. #### B. Neural Components (Light-Detection System) 1. **Retina** - Photosensitive inner lining containing ~**120 million rods** and ~**6 million cones**. - Inverted sensor layer — light must pass through nerve fibres before reaching photoreceptors. 2. **Fovea Centralis** - Small (~1.5 mm) central pit of the retina. - Highest visual acuity region; contains **only cones** (~200,000 cones/mm²). - Responsible for sharp central vision and color. 3. **Blind Spot** - Region where the optic nerve exits the retina. - Contains **no photoreceptors** (~15° temporal to fovea). 4. **Optic Nerve** - Bundle of ~1 million nerve fibres transmitting signals to the brain. ### 2.2 Photoreceptor Cells: Rods vs. Cones | Feature | **Rods** | **Cones** | |---|---|---| | Quantity | ~120 million | ~6 million | | Sensitivity | **High** (scotopic) | **Lower** (photopic) | | Color Vision | **No** (achromatic) | **Yes** (trichromatic) | | Distribution | Peripheral retina | Concentrated at fovea | | Light Level | Dim light (≤ 10⁻² cd/m²) | Bright light (≥ 10 cd/m²) | | Visual Acuity | Low | High | | Response Time | Slow (~100 ms) | Fast (~25 ms) | | Photopigment | **Rhodopsin** | **Iodopsin / Photopsins** (3 types) | | Peak Spectral Response | **~507 nm (blue-green)** | **~555 nm (yellow-green)** for the standard observer | > [!NOTE] > **Three Types of Cones (Young-Helmholtz Trichromatic Theory):** > - **S-cones (Short):** Peak sensitivity ~**420–440 nm** (Blue) > - **M-cones (Medium):** Peak sensitivity ~**530–540 nm** (Green) > - **L-cones (Long):** Peak sensitivity ~**560–580 nm** (Red) ### 2.3 Spectral Sensitivity of the Eye — The V(λ) Function The eye does **not** respond equally to all wavelengths. The **spectral luminous efficacy** $V(\lambda)$ defines the relative sensitivity of the **standard photometric observer** as a function of wavelength. There are **two standardized curves**: - **$V(\lambda)$ — Photopic Luminous Efficiency Function:** For light-adapted (daytime) vision, governed primarily by cones. **Peak at 555 nm.** - **$V'(\lambda)$ — Scotopic Luminous Efficiency Function:** For dark-adapted (nighttime) vision, governed solely by rods. **Peak at 507 nm.** The shift in peak sensitivity from 555 nm to 507 nm as luminance drops is known as the **Purkinje Shift (or Purkinje Effect)**, named after Czech physiologist Jan Evangelista Purkyně (1819). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mesopic Vision:** In the intermediate range (luminance between ~0.001 cd/m² and ~10 cd/m²), both rods and cones contribute. This is modeled using the CIE 191:2010 mesopic photometry system, which interpolates between $V(\lambda)$ and $V'(\lambda)$ based on adaptation luminance. ### 2.4 Light Adaptation and Dark Adaptation **Light Adaptation:** When moving from dim to bright light, the eye rapidly (~30–60 seconds) becomes less sensitive. Mechanisms: - Pupil constriction (fast, ~1 second) - Photopigment bleaching (medium, seconds) - Neural adaptation in retina and cortex **Dark Adaptation:** When moving from bright to dim light, the eye slowly (~30–45 minutes for full adaptation) increases sensitivity. The classic **dark adaptation curve** shows a characteristic "kink" at ~7–10 minutes, known as the **Kohlrausch break**, where rod sensitivity overtakes cone sensitivity. ### 2.5 Glare and Visual Comfort **Glare** is the sensation caused by bright areas within the field of view, reducing visual comfort and performance. Two principal types: - **Disability Glare:** Reduces the ability to see (e.g., oncoming headlights while driving). - **Discomfort Glare:** Causes annoyance/fatigue without necessarily impairing visibility (e.g., poorly shielded office luminaires). The **Discomfort Glare Index** is often evaluated using the **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** formula, defined by the CIE: $$ UGR = 8 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.25}{L_b} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i^2 \, \omega_i}{p_i^2}\right) $$ Where: - $L_b$ = background luminance (cd/m²) - $L_i$ = luminance of the i-th glare source (cd/m²) - $\omega_i$ = solid angle of the i-th glare source (sr) - $p_i$ = Guth position index for the i-th source > UGR < 19 → imperceptible glare; 19–22 → just perceptible; > 28 → disturbing. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $K = \frac{\Phi_v}{\Phi_e}$ | Luminous efficacy (ratio of luminous flux to radiant flux) | **lm/W** | | 2 | $K(\lambda) = K_m \cdot V(\lambda)$ | Spectral luminous efficacy, where $K_m = 683$ lm/W at 555 nm | lm/W | | 3 | $\Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_{e,\lambda} \, V(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Luminous flux from spectral radiant flux | **lm** | | 4 | $L = \frac{I}{A \cos \theta}$ | Luminance definition | **cd/m²** | | 5 | $E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$ | Inverse-square illumination law | **lux** | | 6 | $VA = \frac{1}{\alpha_{min}}$ | Visual acuity (reciprocal of minimum resolvable angle in arc-minutes) | dimensionless | | 7 | $\alpha_{min} = \frac{d}{f}$ (radians) | Minimum resolvable angle ≈ $\frac{1}{60}^\circ = 1$ arc-minute for normal eye | rad or arc-min | | 8 | $D_p = 2 - 3 \log_{10}(L_{avg})$ (Hecht formula approximation) | Pupil diameter vs. adaptation luminance | **mm** | | 9 | $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.25}{L_b} \sum \frac{L_i^2 \omega_i}{p_i^2}\right)$ | Unified Glare Rating | dimensionless | | 10 | $S/N_{threshold} \approx 60$ | Rose criterion: ~60 quanta at retina needed for threshold detection | quanta | | 11 | $N_{rods} \approx 1.2 \times 10^8$ | Total rod cells in retina | cells | | 12 | $N_{cones} \approx 6 \times 10^6$ | Total cone cells in retina | cells | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Application:** In lighting design, photopic quantities are used for indoor/office lighting (luminance ≥ 10 cd/m²), scotopic for night road/street lighting, and mesopic for transitional zones like parking lots and tunnel entrances/exits. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Street Lighting Design:** Modern LED street luminaires are specified using **mesopic** photometry because drivers experience low ambient luminances at night. - **Display Technology:** Color displays (TVs, monitors) target the **CIE 1931 RGB color matching functions**, which are derived from cone responses. - **Aviation Lighting:** Runway and approach lights must be visible under both photopic and scotopic conditions; pilots dark-adapted at high altitudes require scotopic-tuned intensities. - **Medical Lighting:** Surgical lamps require high color rendering index (CRI ≥ 95) to leverage cone-based color discrimination. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Photometric / Radiometric Conversion of Monochromatic Light **Problem Setup:** A monochromatic light source emits at wavelength $\lambda = 555$ nm with a radiant flux $\Phi_e = 1$ W. Determine the luminous flux $\Phi_v$, the spectral luminous efficacy $K(\lambda)$, and verify the maximum photometric constant $K_m$. **Given:** - $\lambda = 555$ nm = $555 \times 10^{-9}$ m - $\Phi_e = 1$ W - $V(555) = 1.0$ (by CIE definition, peak) **Step 1 — Definition of Luminous Flux** Luminous flux $\Phi_v$ is defined as the radiant flux weighted by the eye's spectral sensitivity: $$ \Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_{e,\lambda} \, V(\lambda) \, d\lambda $$ **Step 2 — Simplification for Monochromatic Source** For a monochromatic source, $\Phi_{e,\lambda} = \Phi_e \cdot \delta(\lambda - \lambda_0)$. The Dirac delta property $\int f(\lambda) \delta(\lambda - \lambda_0) d\lambda = f(\lambda_0)$ simplifies the integral: $$ \Phi_v = K_m \cdot \Phi_e \cdot V(\lambda_0) $$ **Step 3 — Substitution at Peak Wavelength** Since $\lambda_0 = 555$ nm and $V(555) = 1.0$: $$ \Phi_v = K_m \cdot \Phi_e \cdot V(555) = 683 \cdot 1 \cdot 1.0 $$ $$ \Phi_v = 683 \text{ lumens} $$ **Step 4 — Verification of $K_m$** The spectral luminous efficacy at this wavelength: $$ K(555) = K_m \cdot V(555) = 683 \cdot 1.0 = 683 \text{ lm/W} $$ This is the **theoretically maximum luminous efficacy** achievable, since $V(\lambda) \leq 1$ at all other wavelengths. **Step 5 — Comparison at $\lambda = 650$ nm (Red LED)** For a typical red LED at 650 nm, $V(650) \approx 0.107$: $$ \Phi_v = 683 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.107 = 73.08 \text{ lumens} $$ > This demonstrates why monochromatic green-yellow sources are photometrically efficient while red and blue sources are inherently less efficient — a fundamental constraint in illumination design. ### 3.2 Derivation: Visual Acuity from Diffraction Limit **Problem Setup:** Determine the theoretical minimum resolvable angle (visual acuity limit) of the human eye from diffraction physics. **Given:** - Pupil diameter under daylight: $D = 2$ mm = $2 \times 10^{-3}$ m - Wavelength of light: $\lambda = 550$ nm = $550 \times 10^{-9}$ m **Step 1 — Rayleigh Criterion (Airy Disk Resolution)** The minimum angular resolution limited by diffraction is given by: $$ \theta_{min} = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} $$ The factor 1.22 arises from the first zero of the Bessel function $J_1$ in the Airy pattern: $1.22 = \pi / 1.6167$ (approximately the first root of $J_1$). **Step 2 — Numerical Substitution** $$ \theta_{min} = 1.22 \cdot \frac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} $$ **Step 3 — Arithmetic** $$ \theta_{min} = 1.22 \cdot 2.75 \times 10^{-4} = 3.355 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Arc-Minutes** Since 1 radian = $\frac{180 \times 60}{\pi}$ arc-minutes $\approx 3437.75$ arc-min/rad: $$ \theta_{min} = 3.355 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 3437.75 \approx 1.153 \text{ arc-minutes} $$ **Step 5 — Visual Acuity Value** $$ VA = \frac{1}{\theta_{min}} = \frac{1}{1.153} \approx 0.867 $$ This corresponds to the standard **6/6 (20/20) vision** Snellen chart equivalent. > **Realistic eye:** Actual measured minimum angle is ~**1 arc-minute** (≈ 0.0003 rad), which is consistent with the diffraction limit. Cone spacing at the fovea (~2.5 μm) supports this resolution. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Vision Regimes Summary | Regime | Luminance Range (cd/m²) | Receptor | Peak Sensitivity | Color? | Adaptation Time | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Scotopic** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ | Rods only | **507 nm** | No | 30–45 min | | **Mesopic** | $10^{-2}$ to $10$ | Rods + Cones | Variable (507–555 nm) | Reduced | Several min | | **Photopic** | $10$ to $10^{6}$ | Cones only | **555 nm** | Yes (full) | 1–5 min | ### 3.4 Derivation: Scotopic-to-Photopic Luminous Flux Ratio (S/P Ratio) **Problem Setup:** For a light source with known spectral power distribution $P(\lambda)$, derive the scotopic-to-photopic ratio $S/P$, used to characterize light sources for mesopic and night-time applications. **Step 1 — Define Photopic Luminous Flux** $$ \Phi_{v,phot} = K_m \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, V(\lambda) \, d\lambda $$ **Step 2 — Define Scotopic Luminous Flux** The scotopic luminous efficacy constant is $K'_m = 1700$ lm/W (at 507 nm): $$ \Phi_{v,scot} = K'_m \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, V'(\lambda) \, d\lambda $$ **Step 3 — Define S/P Ratio** $$ S/P = \frac{\Phi_{v,scot}}{\Phi_{v,phot}} = \frac{1700}{683} \cdot \frac{\int P(\lambda) V'(\lambda) d\lambda}{\int P(\lambda) V(\lambda) d\lambda} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Constant** $$ \frac{K'_m}{K_m} = \frac{1700}{683} \approx 2.489 $$ So: $$ S/P \approx 2.489 \cdot \frac{\int P(\lambda) V'(\lambda) d\lambda}{\int P(\lambda) V(\lambda) d\lambda} $$ **Example (White LED, CCT = 4000 K):** $S/P \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ **Example (High-Pressure Sodium Lamp):** $S/P \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ (rod-poor, photopically efficient) **Example (Cool White Fluorescent 6500 K):** $S/P \approx 2.3$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** A high S/P ratio (>1.5) means the source appears **brighter at night** than its photopic lumen value suggests. This is exploited in street-lighting LED selection for better peripheral detection. ### 3.5 Derivation: Threshold Detection and Rose's Criterion The minimum number of photons required at the retina for threshold detection under ideal scotopic conditions: **Given:** - Quantum efficiency of rhodopsin: $\eta \approx 0.1$ (10% of photons trigger a rod response) - Rose criterion: $S/N = 5$ to 10 (signal-to-noise for reliable detection) **Step 1 — Threshold Photons** The empirical **Rose criterion** states that approximately **60–100 photons** must reach the retina within an integration time of ~0.1 s for a 50% probability of detection. **Step 2 — Photons Actually Absorbed** $$ N_{absorbed} = N_{incident} \cdot \eta = N_{incident} \cdot 0.1 $$ For $N_{absorbed} = 6$ photons (one photon per ~10 rods), $N_{incident} = 60$ photons. **Step 3 — Energy Required** For $\lambda = 507$ nm: $$ E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 3 \times 10^8}{507 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.92 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{total} = 60 \cdot 3.92 \times 10^{-19} = 2.35 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J} $$ This is the order-of-magnitude of the **absolute threshold of human vision**. ### 3.6 KTU-Style Numerical Example: Complete Problem **Question:** A monochromatic light source emits at $\lambda = 480$ nm with radiant intensity $I_e = 10$ W/sr. Determine: (a) Luminous intensity in candelas. (b) Luminous flux if the source is isotropic. **Given:** - $\lambda = 480$ nm - $I_e = 10$ W/sr - $V(480) \approx 0.139$ (CIE photopic standard) **Solution (a):** $$ I_v = K_m \cdot I_e \cdot V(\lambda) = 683 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.139 $$ $$ I_v = 683 \cdot 1.39 = 949.4 \text{ cd} $$ **Solution (b):** For an isotropic source, total solid angle $\Omega = 4\pi$ sr: $$ \Phi_v = I_v \cdot \Omega = 949.4 \cdot 4\pi $$ $$ \Phi_v = 949.4 \cdot 12.566 = 11{,}930 \text{ lm} \approx 11.93 \text{ klm} $$ **Verification using radiant flux:** $$ \Phi_e = I_e \cdot 4\pi = 10 \cdot 4\pi = 125.66 \text{ W} $$ $$ \Phi_v = K_m \cdot \Phi_e \cdot V(480) = 683 \cdot 125.66 \cdot 0.139 = 11{,}930 \text{ lm} \checkmark $$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Human Visual Perception Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Light Source
