EN 12464] H --> I ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define photopic and scotopic vision. Why does the peak spectral sensitivity of the eye shift between these two regimes?** **Model Answer:** **Photopic Vision:** - Vision mediated by the **cone photoreceptors** of the retina, operative at **high luminance levels** (≥ 10 cd/m²). - Provides **high visual acuity** and **full color discrimination** (trichromatic). - Standardized by the CIE **$V(\lambda)$ curve**, peaking at **555 nm**. **Scotopic Vision:** - Vision mediated by the **rod photoreceptors**, operative at **very low luminance levels** (≤ 10⁻² cd/m²). - Provides **high sensitivity** but **no color discrimination** (achromatic). - Standardized by the CIE **$V'(\lambda)$ curve**, peaking at **507 nm**. **Reason for the Spectral Shift (Purkinje Shift):** - Rods contain **rhodopsin**, whose absorption spectrum peaks at ~507 nm (blue-green). - Cones (L-cones) peak at ~555 nm (yellow-green). - In bright light, cones dominate, so sensitivity peaks at 555 nm. - In dim light, rods take over, shifting the effective peak to 507 nm. - This shift makes blue objects appear relatively brighter at night than during the day (e.g., blue flowers more visible in twilight). **(3 marks: 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for explanation of Purkinje shift)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is the Unified Glare Rating (UGR)? State its mathematical expression and explain the significance of each parameter.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** is a numerical measure, defined by the CIE, that quantifies the **discomfort glare** produced by a lighting installation. It is used in indoor lighting design (per EN 12464-1) to limit visual discomfort from luminaires within the field of view. **Mathematical Expression:** $$ UGR = 8 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.25}{L_b} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i^2 \, \omega_i}{p_i^2}\right) $$ **Parameter Significance:** | Parameter | Meaning | |---|---| | $L_b$ | Background luminance (cd/m²) — the overall field luminance excluding glare sources | | $L_i$ | Luminance of the i-th glare source (cd/m²) — the luminaire's brightness as seen by the observer | | $\omega_i$ | Solid angle subtended by the i-th source at the observer's eye (steradians) | | $p_i$ | **Guth position index** — accounts for the angular position of the source from the line of sight (sources directly in view have $p = 1$; sources at 90° to line of sight have $p \approx 0$) | | $n$ | Total number of glare sources | **Interpretation:** | UGR Value | Perception | |---|---| | < 13 | Imperceptible | | 13 – 16 | Just noticeable | | 19 – 22 | Noticeable, acceptable for offices | | 25 – 28 | Disturbing | | > 28 | Intolerable | **(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for parameter table)** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice]** **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the structure of the human eye. Describe the function of each optical component in the image-formation process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 60 W incandescent bulb emits radiation approximated by a black-body curve at 2856 K. Estimate: (i) The total radiant flux emitted in the visible band (380–780 nm). (ii) The luminous flux if we assume $V(\lambda)$ may be approximated by a flat response over the visible band with an average value of $\overline{V(\lambda)} \approx 0.4$. (iii) Comment on why actual incandescent lamp efficacy is much lower than 683 lm/W. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 mermaid block diagram. **Description of Optical Components:** 1. **Cornea:** Transparent front layer (~0.5 mm thick), provides ~43 dioptres of refractive power. Fixed focus; responsible for most refraction. **(1 mark)** 2. **Aqueous Humor:** Clear fluid between cornea and lens (index 1.336). Maintains intraocular pressure and nourishes cornea. **(0.5 marks)** 3. **Iris & Pupil:** Pigmented muscular diaphragm. Controls pupil diameter from ~2 mm (bright) to ~8 mm (dark). Acts as variable aperture, regulating light flux reaching retina. **(1 mark)** 4. **Crystalline Lens:** Flexible biconvex structure (~9 mm diameter). Adjusts shape via ciliary muscles to focus near/far objects on retina (accommodation). Refractive index 1.386–1.406. Provides ~17 D of refractive power. **(1 mark)** 5. **Vitreous Humor:** Gel-like substance (index 1.337) filling the posterior chamber. Maintains shape of eyeball and supports retina. **(0.5 marks)** 6. **Retina:** Innermost photosensitive layer containing rods and cones. Acts as the image sensor. Light is focused on the retina by the combined cornea-lens system to form a real, inverted image. **(1 mark)** 7. **Optic Nerve:** Transmits processed signals from retina to the brain's visual cortex. **(0.5 marks)** 8. **Fovea:** Central pit of the retina; highest density of cones; responsible for sharpest vision and color. **(0.5 marks)** 9. **Blind Spot:** Point where optic nerve exits; no photoreceptors; creates a small gap in visual field (~15° from fovea, nasal side). **(0.5 marks)** 10. **Sclera:** Tough white outer protective coat. **(0.5 marks)** **Image Formation Process:** Light enters through the cornea, is refracted, passes through the pupil (whose diameter is regulated by the iris), is further focused by the crystalline lens (which accommodates for object distance), and is projected onto the retina as an inverted, real image. Photoreceptors transduce photons into neural signals, which are relayed via bipolar and ganglion cells to the optic nerve and ultimately to the visual cortex. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - Electrical power input: $P_{elec} = 60$ W - Operating temperature: $T = 2856$ K - Wavelength range: $\lambda_1 = 380$ nm, $\lambda_2 = 780$ nm - $\overline{V(\lambda)} \approx 0.4$ **Step 1 — Use Planck's Blackbody Radiation Law** Spectral emissive power per unit area: $$ M_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1} $$ where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **Step 2 — Compute Fraction of Energy in Visible Band** Define dimensionless product $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$. At $\lambda = 380$ nm: $$ x_1 = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 3 \times 10^8}{380 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 2856} $$ $$ x_1 = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{1.499 \times 10^{-29}} \approx 13{,}260 $$ At $\lambda = 780$ nm: $$ x_2 = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 3 \times 10^8}{780 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 2856} $$ $$ x_2 = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{3.078 \times 10^{-29}} \approx 6459 $$ Both values are very large, so the Wien approximation $M_\lambda \approx \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} e^{-hc/\lambda k_B T}$ is valid. **Step 3 — Compute Total Radiant Flux in Visible Band (Numerical Integration)** Using numerical integration (trapezoidal rule over 10 nm intervals), the fraction of blackbody power in the 380–780 nm band at 2856 K is approximately **$F_{vis} \approx 0.105$** (i.e., 10.5%). **Total radiant flux emitted by bulb** (assuming ~80% electrical-to-radiative conversion, but using electrical input as approximation): $$ \Phi_{e,total} \approx 0.105 \cdot 60 = 6.30 \text{ W} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Visible Radiant Flux** $$ \Phi_{e,vis} \approx 6.30 \text{ W} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Luminous Flux (Part ii)** $$ \Phi_v = K_m \cdot \Phi_{e,vis} \cdot \overline{V(\lambda)} $$ $$ \Phi_v = 683 \cdot 6.30 \cdot 0.4 = 1721.16 \text{ lm} $$ **Step 6 — Compute Efficacy** $$ \eta = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{elec}} = \frac{1721.16}{60} \approx 28.7 \text{ lm/W} $$ **Step 7 — Comments on Lower Efficacy (Part iii)** The actual luminous efficacy of incandescent lamps (~14–17 lm/W for 60 W bulb) is much lower than 683 lm/W due to: **(2 marks)** 1. **Spectral mismatch:** A 2856 K blackbody radiates predominantly in the **infrared** (>78% of energy is outside the visible band). Only ~10% is visible. 2. **$V(\lambda)$ weighting:** Within the visible band, much of the energy is at red wavelengths (650–780 nm) where $V(\lambda)$ is low (~0.05–0.2). The flat average of 0.4 overestimates. 3. **Filament geometry losses:** Tungsten filament operates below melting point; not all electrical energy is converted to radiation (~80–90% efficiency). 4. **Glass envelope absorption:** Some visible radiation is absorbed by the bulb glass. 5. **Realistic $V(\lambda)$ average** for incandescent spectrum is closer to 0.20, reducing computed efficacy to ~14 lm/W — matching real-world values. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 Internal Choice]** **Q. (a)** Explain the phenomenon of Purkinje shift. Describe the spectral luminous efficiency curves for photopic and scotopic vision with appropriate diagrams. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two monochromatic sources operate at 450 nm and 700 nm, each emitting 1 W of radiant flux. Determine: (i) The luminous flux of each source. (ii) Which source appears brighter to a dark-adapted (scotopic) observer, and by what factor? (iii) Sketch the relative spectral power distribution and mark the peak sensitivity points. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Purkinje Shift Definition:** The Purkinje shift (or Purkinje effect), discovered by Czech physiologist Jan Evangelista Purkyně in 1825, is the change in the eye's peak spectral sensitivity from ~555 nm (photopic, cone-dominated) to ~507 nm (scotopic, rod-dominated) as the eye adapts to lower light levels. **(2 marks)** **Mechanism:** - At photopic levels (≥ 10 cd/m²), the L-cones dominate and the CIE $V(\lambda)$ curve peaks at 555 nm. - At scotopic levels (≤ 0.01 cd/m²), only rods are active, and the $V'(\lambda)$ curve peaks at 507 nm. - The result: at low light, **blue objects appear relatively brighter** and **red objects relatively dimmer** than at high light. **Diagrams:** $V(\lambda)$ and $V'(\lambda)$ curves showing peaks at 555 nm and 507 nm respectively, both normalized to 1.0 at their peaks. | Property | Photopic $V(\lambda)$ | Scotopic $V'(\lambda)$ | |---|---|---| | Receptors | Cones (3 types) | Rods only | | Peak wavelength | **555 nm** | **507 nm** | | Spectral range | 380–780 nm | 380–780 nm (same physical range) | | Color | Yes | No | | $K_m$ | 683 lm/W | 1700 lm/W | | FWHM | ~100 nm | ~90 nm | The two curves cross at approximately **555 nm and 790 nm**, with scotopic exceeding photopic at $\lambda < 555$ nm. **(3 marks)** **Mesopic Zone:** Between 0.01 and 10 cd/m², the CIE 191:2010 system interpolates between $V(\lambda)$ and $V'(\lambda)$ based on the adaptation luminance. **(1 mark)** **Practical Implication:** - Aviation signal lights: Red signals appear much dimmer at night → use larger/longer signal lights. - Astronomical observation: Astronomers use red illumination in darkrooms to preserve scotopic adaptation (since red light minimally stimulates rods). - Stage lighting: Magenta/blue-rich gels look more vivid at low intensities. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - Source 1: $\lambda_1 = 450$ nm, $\Phi_{e1} = 1$ W - Source 2: $\lambda_2 = 700$ nm, $\Phi_{e2} = 1$ W - $V(450) \approx 0.038$, $V(700) \approx 0.0041$ (CIE photopic) - $V'(450) \approx 0.589$, $V'(700) \approx 0.0001$ (CIE scotopic, approximate) **Step 1 — Part (i): Photopic Luminous Flux** For Source 1 (450 nm): $$ \Phi_{v1,phot} = 683 \cdot 1 \cdot V(450) = 683 \cdot 0.038 = 25.95 \text{ lm} $$ For Source 2 (700 nm): $$ \Phi_{v2,phot} = 683 \cdot 1 \cdot V(700) = 683 \cdot 0.0041 = 2.80 \text{ lm} $$ **Step 2 — Part (ii): Scotopic Luminous Flux and Comparison** For Source 1 (450 nm) under scotopic conditions: $$ \Phi_{v1,scot} = 1700 \cdot 1 \cdot V'(450) = 1700 \cdot 0.589 = 1001.3 \text{ lm}_{scot} $$ For Source 2 (700 nm) under scotopic conditions: $$ \Phi_{v2,scot} = 1700 \cdot 1 \cdot V'(700) = 1700 \cdot 0.0001 = 0.17 \text{ lm}_{scot} $$ **Ratio under scotopic conditions:** $$ \frac{\Phi_{v1,scot}}{\Phi_{v2,scot}} = \frac{1001.3}{0.17} \approx 5890 $$ > The 450 nm blue source appears approximately **5890 times brighter** than the 700 nm red source to a dark-adapted observer. **Step 3 — Part (iii): Sketch Description** ``` Spectral Sensitivity 1.0 | V(λ) V'(λ) | / \ / \ | / \ / \ 0.8 | / \ / \ | / \ / \ 0.6 | / \/ \ | / \ \ 0.4 | / \ \ | / \ \ 0.2 | / \ \ | / \ \ 0.0 |_/______\_____________\___________\__________ 380 450 507 555 650 700 780 Wavelength (nm) ▲ ▲ Scotopic peak Photopic peak (507 nm) (555 nm) ``` The 450 nm source lies near the rising edge of the scotopic curve (high $V'$), while 700 nm lies in the deep red tail where both $V$ and $V'$ are very low. **(2 marks)** **Conclusion:** The dramatic reversal of perceived brightness (blue is brighter at night, red is brighter in daytime) is a direct, measurable consequence of the Purkinje shift. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing photopic and scotopic $K_m$ values:** Many students interchange 683 lm/W and 1700 lm/W. **Remember: 683 = photopic (555 nm); 1700 = scotopic (507 nm).** > > 2. **Forgetting units in UGR problems:** $L_b$ and $L_i$ MUST be in **cd/m²** (not lux). $\omega_i$ must be in **steradians** (not square degrees). > > 3. **Applying photopic values to night-time lighting designs:** Street lighting, tunnel lighting, and aviation signal design require **mesopic or scotopic** considerations. Using photopic lumen values alone is a major error. > > 4. **Incorrect V(λ) values at extremes:** $V(380) = V(780) = 0$ (boundary). $V(555) = 1.0$ (peak). Common errors: using V(450) ≈ 0.5 (actual is ~0.04). > > 5. **Missing the inversion of image on retina:** Many students forget that the image formed on the retina is **inverted and reversed** (upside-down, left-right swapped) — yet the brain interprets it as upright. > > 6. **Confusing rods with cones' distribution:** Rods dominate in **peripheral** retina; cones concentrate at the **fovea**. Many students invert this. > > 7. **No mention of Kohlrausch break** in dark adaptation answers → loses 1 mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEEET418 Module 1 — Human Eye and Light Perception:** - ⭐ **Eye as an optical system:** Total refractive power ≈ **60 dioptres** (43 D cornea + 17 D lens). Image formed on retina is **real, inverted, and reduced**. - ⭐ **Photoreceptor counts:** **120 million rods** (scotopic, peripheral, achromatic) and **6 million cones** (photopic, foveal, trichromatic). - ⭐ **Three cone types (S, M, L):** Peak at ~420 nm (Blue), ~530 nm (Green), ~560 nm (Red) respectively — basis of trichromatic color vision. - ⭐ **Photopic peak:** **555 nm** with $V(\lambda) = 1.0$ and $K_m = 683$ lm/W. - ⭐ **Scotopic peak:** **507 nm** with $V'(\lambda) = 1.0$ and $K'_m = 1700$ lm/W. - ⭐ **Purkinje Shift:** Peak sensitivity moves from 555 nm → 507 nm as luminance decreases. Implication: **blue relatively brighter at night**. - ⭐ **Visual acuity formula:** $VA = 1/\theta_{min}$ where $\theta_{min} \approx 1$ arc-minute (≈ 3 × 10⁻⁴ rad) for normal vision. - ⭐ **Rose's criterion:** ~**60 quanta at retina** = absolute threshold for scotopic detection. - ⭐ **Pupil diameter:** **2 mm (bright)** to **8 mm (dim)**; adjustment time ~1 s. - ⭐ **Dark adaptation:** 30–45 minutes full; **Kohlrausch break** at ~7–10 min (rod takeover). - ⭐ **Light adaptation:** 1–5 minutes; faster than dark adaptation. - ⭐ **Glare types:** **Disability glare** (impairs vision) vs **Discomfort glare** (causes annoyance). - ⭐ **UGR formula:** $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.25}{L_b} \sum \frac{L_i^2 \omega_i}{p_i^2}\right)$; UGR < 19 recommended for offices. - ⭐ **Mesopic range:** $10^{-2}$ to $10$ cd/m²; use CIE 191:2010 for design. - ⭐ **Photometric-radiometric conversion (monochromatic):** $\Phi_v = 683 \cdot \Phi_e \cdot V(\lambda)$. - ⭐ **S/P ratio:** $S/P \approx 2.489 \cdot \frac{\int P(\lambda) V'(\lambda) d\lambda}{\int P(\lambda) V(\lambda) d\lambda}$; high S/P (>1.5) → better night-time efficacy. - ⭐ **Blind spot:** ~15° temporal to fovea; no photoreceptors where optic nerve exits. - ⭐ **Fovea:** Highest cone density (~200,000 cones/mm²); only cones; highest acuity region.

topics.title: Visible Light Production by Black Body Radiation and Emission Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visible Light Production by Black Body Radiation and Emission Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Black Body Radiation** is the electromagnetic radiation emitted by an idealized physical body (a *black body*) that absorbs all incident electromagnetic radiation at every wavelength and reflects none. When held at thermal equilibrium at a definite temperature **T**, such a body becomes the most efficient possible emitter of thermal radiation. The spectral distribution of this emitted energy is uniquely determined by the temperature alone — not by the body’s material, shape, or surface texture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Statement (verbatim for board answers):**
> *"A black body is a perfect absorber and a perfect emitter of radiation. It absorbs 100% of incident radiation (absorptivity $\alpha = 1$) across all wavelengths, and consequently emits the maximum possible spectral radiant exitance at every wavelength for a given temperature, in accordance with Kirchhoff’s law of thermal radiation."*

The **Emission Spectrum** is the set of wavelengths (or frequencies) of electromagnetic radiation emitted by a source, plotted as intensity vs. wavelength/frequency. Visible light occupies the narrow band of the electromagnetic spectrum between approximately **380 nm** (violet) and **780 nm** (red).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Cavity Radiator Furnace"**
> Imagine a sealed metal oven (a *cavity*) with a tiny pinhole. Light entering the pinhole gets trapped inside, bouncing off the walls thousands of times, losing energy on every bounce until it is completely absorbed. To an external observer, the hole looks perfectly black — no light reflects back. This is the *ideal* black body. Now heat the oven: the walls glow at the pinhole. The light that emerges is *pure thermal radiation* whose color depends *only* on the temperature. Heat it to 800 K → dull red. Heat it to 3000 K → orange-yellow (like a tungsten filament). Heat it to 5800 K → white (like the Sun). This is exactly how incandescent lamps and even stars produce light.

The visible portion of this emitted spectrum (the rainbow of colors) is the foundation of all natural daylight and most artificial lighting sources based on heating.

## 1.3 Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Constant | Value (SI) | Significance |
|--------|----------|------------|--------------|
| $h$ | Planck’s constant | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}}$ | Quantum of action |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}$ | Universal constant |
| $k$ | Boltzmann’s constant | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}}$ | Thermal energy per degree |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{5.670 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{K}^{4}\text{)}}$ | Total emitted power coefficient |
| $b$ | Wien’s displacement constant | $\mathbf{2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K}}$ | Peak wavelength $\times$ temperature |
| $\lambda_{\text{vis}}$ | Visible range | $\mathbf{380 \le \lambda \le 780\ \text{nm}}$ | Human eye response window |

## 1.4 The Visible Window in Context

| Region | Wavelength Range | Energy (eV/photon) | Source / Example |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Ultraviolet (UV) | 10 – 380 nm | 3.26 – 124 eV | Sun, mercury arc |
| **Visible (VIS)** | **380 – 780 nm** | **1.59 – 3.26 eV** | **Sun, filament, LED** |
| Near-Infrared (NIR) | 780 nm – 1 mm | 0.00124 – 1.59 eV | Heater, IR LED |
| Far-Infrared / Microwave | 1 mm – 1 m | $\lt 0.00124$ eV | Cosmic background |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study black bodies in Illumination Technology?**
> The Sun, incandescent lamps, halogen lamps, and even some high-intensity discharge (HID) sources are modeled as **near-black-body** radiators. Their color temperature, luminous efficacy, and spectral power distribution (SPD) are all derived from black-body theory. KTU examiners *routinely* test the link between temperature and emitted color (Part A & Part B).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Black Body — Operational Principles

A black body is an *idealization* that satisfies three physical conditions simultaneously:

1. **Perfect Absorption:** It absorbs all incident radiation at *every* wavelength and at *every* temperature.
   $\alpha(\lambda, T) \equiv 1$ for all $\lambda$, $T$.

2. **Maximum Emissivity:** By Kirchhoff’s Law, its spectral emissivity equals its spectral absorptivity at every wavelength.
   $\epsilon(\lambda, T) = \alpha(\lambda, T) = 1$ → so it emits *more* than any real body at the same temperature.

3. **Lambertian Source:** A black-body surface is a *perfect diffuse emitter* — radiance is independent of viewing angle. The projected area follows the cosine law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Illumination Engineering:**
> Real lamps are characterized by *how close* they are to a black body. This closeness is measured by **Color Rendering Index (CRI)** and **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)** — both rely fundamentally on the black-body locus on the CIE chromaticity diagram.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Laws of Black-Body Radiation

### 2.2.1 Planck’s Law (1900) — The Master Equation
This is the *exact* spectral distribution. Every other law is derived from it.

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{\,h c / (\lambda k T)} - 1}$$

- $E_{\lambda}$ = spectral radiant exitance (W/m² per meter of wavelength)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Limiting cases** (both worth memorizing — they’re favorite exam follow-ups):

- **Short wavelength (high frequency) limit** $hc \gg \lambda kT$:
$$E_{\lambda} \approx \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-h c / (\lambda k T)} \quad \text{(Wien's Radiation Law)}$$

- **Long wavelength (low frequency) limit** $hc \ll \lambda kT$:
$$E_{\lambda} \approx \frac{2 \pi c k T}{\lambda^{4}} \quad \text{(Rayleigh–Jeans Law — classical, fails at short } \lambda\text{)}$$

### 2.2.2 Stefan–Boltzmann Law (1879 / 1884)
Integrating Planck’s law over all wavelengths yields the *total* radiated power per unit area:

$$P = \sigma T^{4} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\, T^{4} \quad \left[\text{W/m}^{2}\right]$$

The $T^{4}$ dependence is extraordinarily strong — doubling temperature increases radiated power by a factor of **16**.

### 2.2.3 Wien’s Displacement Law (1893)
Differentiating Planck’s law and setting $\partial E_{\lambda}/\partial \lambda = 0$ gives the wavelength of peak emission:

$$\lambda_{\max}\, T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$

As $T$ increases, the peak shifts to *shorter* wavelengths (toward the blue) — this is the *physics* behind an iron bar going from red → orange → white as it is heated.

## 2.3 Kirchhoff’s Law of Thermal Radiation

For any body in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings at temperature $T$:

$$\frac{E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)}{a(\lambda, T)} = E_{b,\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

where $E_{b,\lambda}$ is the black-body spectral exitance. This is the *bridge* linking absorbers and emitters and is the reason a perfect absorber is also a perfect emitter.

## 2.4 The Emission Spectrum — Three Classes

| Class | Description | Width / Character | Example Source |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Continuous Spectrum** | All wavelengths present, smooth curve | Broad, follows Planck curve | Sun, incandescent lamp, halogen |
| **Line Spectrum** | Emission at discrete, well-defined wavelengths | $\Delta \lambda \approx 10^{-3}$ nm | Sodium (Na), Mercury (Hg) vapor lamps |
| **Band Spectrum** | Groups of closely spaced lines forming bands | Bands $\sim$ few nm wide | Molecular gases (e.g., $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$) — fluorescent tubes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Mapping:** Continuous spectra are dominated by *thermal* (black-body) mechanisms; line and band spectra are dominated by *electronic transitions* in atoms/molecules. Most *modern* lighting (LED, fluorescent, sodium, mercury) produces **non-thermal** radiation and therefore does *not* obey black-body laws — but their color is still characterized using the black-body locus (CCT concept).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print & Memorize)

| # | Law / Formula | Expression | Typical Use | Key Boundary |
|---|---------------|------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | Planck’s law (exact) | $E_{\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}\left(e^{hc/(\lambda k T)} - 1\right)}$ | Full SPD | All $\lambda, T$ |
| 2 | Wien’s radiation (approx) | $E_{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}}\,e^{-hc/(\lambda k T)}$ | Short-$\lambda$ tail | $hc \gg \lambda kT$ |
| 3 | Rayleigh–Jeans (approx) | $E_{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{2\pi c k T}{\lambda^{4}}$ | Long-$\lambda$ limit | $hc \ll \lambda kT$ |
| 4 | Stefan–Boltzmann | $P = \sigma T^{4}$ | Total radiated flux | $T > 0$ |
| 5 | Wien’s displacement | $\lambda_{\max} T = b$ | Peak wavelength | $b = 2.898\times10^{-3}$ m·K |
| 6 | Kirchhoff | $\epsilon(\lambda,T) = \alpha(\lambda,T)$ | Emissivity = absorptivity | Thermodynamic eq. |
| 7 | Energy per photon | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | $h = 6.626\times10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 8 | Photopic peak | $\lambda_{p} = 555$ nm | Max eye response | Daylight vision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In lighting design, the black-body model is the *reference* for CCT (Correlated Color Temperature). A 2700 K lamp appears “warm white” (similar to old incandescent); 5000 K appears “cool daylight” (similar to noon Sun at 5800 K). Black-body formulas are used to predict the SPD of incandescent sources and to extrapolate the *color temperature* of any non-thermal source by finding the closest point on the Planckian locus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stefan–Boltzmann Law from Planck’s Law

We integrate Planck’s spectral exitance over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$P = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)\, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c / (\lambda k T)} - 1}\, d\lambda$$

**Step 1 — Variable substitution.** Let $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k T}$, so $d\lambda = -\dfrac{h c}{k T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{x^{2}}\, dx$, and $\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{k T x}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the integrand.** We obtain
$$P = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\left(\frac{h c}{k T}\right)^{4}} \cdot \frac{(k T)^{4}}{h c} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Identify the standard integral.**
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

This is a definite integral that appears in the derivation of Planck’s law and is well-known. Its value comes from recognizing the integrand as a series
$$\frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} = x^{3}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{-n x} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} x^{3} e^{-n x}$$
and integrating term by term.

**Step 4 — Substitute back and simplify.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{h^{4} c^{4} / k^{4} T^{4}} \cdot \frac{k^{4} T^{4}}{h c} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \\
&= \left(\frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}}\right) T^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the constant.** The bracketed coefficient is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{K}^{4}\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form.**
$$\boxed{P = \sigma T^{4}}$$

This is the *total* radiated power per unit area of a black body — the foundational relation for understanding incandescent lamp efficiency and heat-load calculations in lighting.

## 3.2 Derivation of Wien’s Displacement Law

Start with Planck’s law and differentiate with respect to $\lambda$ to locate the peak:
$$\frac{\partial E_{\lambda}}{\partial \lambda} = 0$$

Writing $E_{\lambda} = \dfrac{C_{1}}{\lambda^{5}(e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} - 1)}$ with $C_{1} = 2\pi h c^{2}$ and $C_{2} = h c / k$, then differentiating and setting the derivative to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= \frac{d}{d\lambda}\left[\lambda^{-5}(e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} - 1)^{-1}\right] \\
0 &= -5\lambda^{-6}(e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} - 1)^{-1} + \lambda^{-5} \cdot \frac{e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} \cdot (C_{2}/\lambda^{2} T)}{(e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} - 1)^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying through by $\lambda^{6}(e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} - 1)^{2}$:

$$0 = -5(e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} - 1) + \frac{C_{2}}{\lambda T}\, e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)}$$

Let $y = C_{2}/(\lambda T)$. Then:
$$5(e^{y} - 1) = y\, e^{y} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 5 - 5 e^{-y} = y$$

This transcendental equation has the numerical solution $y \approx 4.965\,114$.

Hence:

$$\frac{h c}{\lambda_{\max} k T} = 4.965 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda_{\max}\, T = \frac{h c}{4.965\, k}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$\lambda_{\max}\, T = \frac{6.626\times10^{-34}\cdot 2.998\times10^{8}}{4.965\cdot 1.381\times10^{-23}} = 2.898\times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max}\, T = 2.898\times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K} = b}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-Style Problems)

### Example 1 — Peak wavelength of a tungsten filament
A tungsten filament operates at $T = 2900$ K. Find the wavelength of peak emission and identify the color.

**Solution:**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898\times 10^{-3}}{2900} = 9.993\times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 999\ \text{nm}$$

This is in the **near-infrared** (NIR) — *not* in the visible band. Most of the radiation is invisible heat. Only the *short-wavelength tail* of the Planck curve falls in the visible range, which is precisely why incandescent lamps have low luminous efficacy (≈ 15 lm/W) compared to their thermal power dissipation.

### Example 2 — Total power radiated by a black-body sphere
A black-body sphere of radius $R = 5$ cm is at $T = 1000$ K. Find the total radiated power.

**Solution:**

$$P = \sigma T^{4} \cdot 4\pi R^{2} = 5.670\times 10^{-8}\cdot (1000)^{4}\cdot 4\pi(0.05)^{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 5.670\times 10^{-8}\cdot 10^{12}\cdot 4\pi\cdot 2.5\times 10^{-3} \\
&= 5.670\times 10^{4}\cdot 4\pi\cdot 2.5\times 10^{-3} \\
&= 5.670\times 10^{4}\cdot 0.031416 \\
&\approx 1.781\times 10^{3}\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

So the sphere radiates **≈ 1.78 kW** — illustrating the dramatic energy loss that limits incandescent efficiency.

### Example 3 — Identify source temperature from peak wavelength
A star has its peak emission at $\lambda_{\max} = 500$ nm (cyan-green). Estimate its surface temperature and color.

**Solution:**

$$T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{2.898\times 10^{-3}}{500\times 10^{-9}} = 5796\ \text{K}$$

This is essentially the Sun’s photosphere (≈ 5778 K), consistent with the Sun’s white-with-slight-yellow appearance.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Limiting Cases of Planck’s Law

| Condition | Dominant Regime | Approximate Law | Failure Region | Why It Fails |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|----------------|--------------|
| $hc \gg \lambda kT$ | Short $\lambda$ (UV) | Wien’s law $E_\lambda \propto \lambda^{-5} e^{-hc/(\lambda kT)}$ | Long $\lambda$ (IR) | Exponential decay becomes negligible; $e^{x}-1 \approx 1$ would diverge |
| $hc \ll \lambda kT$ | Long $\lambda$ (IR/microwave) | Rayleigh–Jeans $E_\lambda \propto \lambda^{-4}$ | Short $\lambda$ (UV) | Diverges as $\lambda \to 0$ → “ultraviolet catastrophe” |
| All $\lambda, T$ | Full range | Planck’s law | None | Quantum nature accounted for |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner’s Note:** When a problem gives a specific $T$ and asks for a particular $\lambda$, *first check* which limiting law applies using $x = hc/(\lambda kT)$. This single decision will earn (or lose) 2–3 marks in a typical Part B sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture: From Cavity to Visible Light

This diagram shows the *complete functional flow* from the abstract definition of a black body to the visible emission we perceive.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Isothermal Cavity at Temperature T] --> B[Internal Walls Re-emit Absorbed Energy]
    B --> C[Equilibrium Energy Density u_lambda_T]
    C --> D[Kirchhoff's Law: alpha equals emissivity]
    A --> E[Pinhole Acts as Perfect Absorber]
    E --> F[Apparent Blackness to External Observer]
    F --> G[Cavity is a Perfect Emitter of Radiation]
    G --> H[Radiation Escapes Through Pinhole]
    H --> I{Planck's Spectral Distribution}
    I --> J[Integrate over Wavelength]
    J --> K[Stefan-Boltzmann Law P equals sigma T to the 4]
    I --> L[Find Peak by Differentiation]
    L --> M[Wien's Displacement Law lambda_max T equals b]
    I --> N[Filter by Human Eye Response V of lambda]
    N --> O[Visible Spectrum 380 to 780 nm]
    O --> P[Perceived Color and Luminous Flux]
    P --> Q[KTU Application: Color Temperature CCT]
```

## 4.2 Emission Spectrum Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Light Source] --> S1{Type of Excitation}
    S1 -->|Thermal| S2[Black Body / Incandescent]
    S1 -->|Electronic Transition| S3[Atomic Line Emission]
    S1 -->|Molecular Transition| S4[Band Emission]

    S2 --> O1[Continuous Spectrum]
    S3 --> O2[Line Spectrum narrow discrete peaks]
    S4 --> O3[Band Spectrum grouped lines]

    O1 --> X1[Example: Tungsten Lamp, Sun]
    O2 --> X2[Example: Sodium Vapour Lamp, Mercury Vapour]
    O3 --> X3[Example: Fluorescent Tube, Hydrogen Lamp]
```

## 4.3 Planck Distribution at Different Temperatures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[T equals 1000 K] --> P1[Peak near 2900 nm Far IR]
    T2[T equals 2900 K] --> P2[Peak near 1000 nm Near IR Filament]
    T3[T equals 5800 K] --> P3[Peak near 500 nm Visible Sun]
    T4[T equals 10000 K] --> P4[Peak near 290 nm UV Blue Star]
    P1 --> R1[Curve shifts Left and Broadens with Rising T]
    P2 --> R1
    P3 --> R1
    P4 --> R1
    R1 --> EYE[Multiply by V of lambda and Integrate]
    EYE --> LUM[Luminous Flux and Luminous Efficacy]
```

## 4.4 Black-Body vs. Real Source Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BB[Ideal Black Body] --> BB1[epsilon equals 1 for all lambda]
    BB1 --> BB2[SPD follows Planck exactly]
    BB2 --> BB3[Locus on CIE: Planckian Locus]

    RB[Real Source e.g. Tungsten] --> RB1[epsilon less than 1 depends on lambda]
    RB1 --> RB2[SPD deviates from Planck]
    RB2 --> RB3[Closest point on Planckian Locus gives CCT]

    BB3 --> APP1[Reference for Colour Temperature]
    RB3 --> APP2[Allows warm white / cool white rating]

    APP1 --> KTU[Exam Application: Distinguish CCT from CRI]
    APP2 --> KTU
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. Define a black body. State any two of its characteristic properties. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

A *black body* is an idealized physical body that absorbs **100 %** of all incident electromagnetic radiation at every wavelength and every temperature (i.e., its absorptivity $\alpha(\lambda, T) \equiv 1$). By Kirchhoff’s law, this makes it the most efficient possible emitter of thermal radiation at that temperature.

**Two characteristic properties:**
1. It is a *perfect absorber* and a *perfect emitter*: emissivity $\epsilon(\lambda, T) = 1$ for all wavelengths.
2. It is a *Lambertian (diffuse) emitter* — radiance is independent of the direction of observation.
3. Its spectral power distribution depends *only* on temperature, given by Planck’s law.

*(Any two properties — 3 marks: definition 1 + 2 properties 1 each)*

---

### Question 2. State Wien’s displacement law. The peak emission wavelength of a sodium-vapour street-lamp tube wall is $589$ nm. Estimate its operating temperature. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Wien’s Displacement Law:** The wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which a black body emits maximum radiation is *inversely proportional* to its absolute temperature $T$.

$$\lambda_{\max}\, T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{589 \times 10^{-9}} = 4920\ \text{K}$$

So the tube-wall temperature is approximately **4920 K** (≈ 4647 °C).

*(3 marks: statement 1, formula 1, numerical 1)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A `(i)` *(Module Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive Planck’s law of black-body radiation. Show clearly the role of the assumption of *discrete energy quanta* in avoiding the **ultraviolet catastrophe**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using Planck’s law, obtain the **Wien’s displacement law** and the **Stefan–Boltzmann law** as limiting and integral cases, respectively. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a)

**Planck’s Postulates (1900):**

1. The walls of the cavity consist of a large number of *resonators* (oscillators), each with characteristic frequency $\nu$.
2. Each resonator can absorb or emit energy *only* in **discrete quanta** $E = h\nu$, where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s is Planck’s constant. This was the revolutionary break from classical theory.
3. The probability of a resonator having energy $E$ at temperature $T$ follows the *Boltzmann distribution* of statistical mechanics.

**Step 1 — Energy distribution of resonators.** The average energy per resonator of frequency $\nu$ is

$$\langle E \rangle = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n h \nu \, e^{-n h \nu / k T}}{\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} e^{-n h \nu / k T}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the sums.** The denominator is a geometric series:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} e^{-n h \nu / k T} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-h \nu / k T}}$$

The numerator is

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n h \nu\, e^{-n h \nu / k T} = \frac{h \nu\, e^{-h \nu / k T}}{\left(1 - e^{-h \nu / k T}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the ratio.**

$$\langle E \rangle = \frac{h \nu}{e^{h \nu / k T} - 1}$$

*Classical (Rayleigh–Jeans) result*: if $h \nu \ll k T$, expanding the exponential gives $\langle E \rangle \approx k T$ — the equipartition theorem. *Quantum result* (Planck): at high $\nu$ the exponential dominates and $\langle E \rangle \to 0$ exponentially, killing the divergence at short wavelengths.

**Step 4 — Mode density in the cavity.** The number of electromagnetic modes per unit volume in the wavelength interval $[\lambda, \lambda + d\lambda]$ is

$$dN = \frac{8 \pi}{\lambda^{4}}\, d\lambda$$

**Step 5 — Assemble Planck’s law.** Energy density is $u_\lambda = dN \cdot \langle E \rangle / d\lambda$, and spectral exitance is $E_\lambda = (c/4) u_\lambda$. After simplification:

$$E_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c/(\lambda k T)} - 1} \quad \text{(Planck's Law)}$$

**Why it resolves the UV catastrophe:** As $\lambda \to 0$, the exponential term $e^{hc/(\lambda kT)}$ grows infinitely fast, forcing $E_\lambda \to 0$ exponentially. The classical Rayleigh–Jeans law $E_\lambda \propto \lambda^{-4}$ would diverge to infinity at $\lambda \to 0$ — the *ultraviolet catastrophe*. Quantum discretization (Step 2) regularizes this divergence.

*(7 marks: postulates 2, derivation 3, UV-catastrophe resolution 2)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b)

**Wien’s Displacement Law (from Planck):**

We require $dE_\lambda / d\lambda = 0$. Letting $x = h c / (\lambda k T)$ and proceeding as in Section 3.2 of these notes, the condition reduces to the transcendental equation

$$5 (1 - e^{-x}) = x \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x = 4.965$$

Hence

$$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{h c}{4.965\, k} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K} = b$$

**Physical interpretation:** As $T$ increases, $\lambda_{\max}$ shifts to shorter wavelengths — the *displacement* of the peak. A cool star peaks in IR, a hot star in UV.

---

**Stefan–Boltzmann Law (from Planck):**

Integrate $E_\lambda$ over all wavelengths:

$$P = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_\lambda\, d\lambda = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}}\, T^{4} = \sigma T^{4}$$

with $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{K}^{4}\text{)}$. (See Section 3.1 for the full derivation steps.) This $T^{4}$ dependence means a small rise in filament temperature dramatically increases radiated power — the *limiting factor* in incandescent-lamp design.

*(7 marks: Wien derivation 3 + interpretation 1; Stefan derivation 3)*

---

### Question B `(ii)` *(Module Choice 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of an **emission spectrum**. With neat sketches, describe the three principal types — *continuous*, *line*, and *band* — and give one engineering example of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 100 W incandescent tungsten filament has a surface area of $0.5\ \text{cm}^{2}$ and operates at 2900 K. Calculate *(i)* the radiated power per unit area, *(ii)* the peak emission wavelength, and *(iii)* the percentage of electrical input that is radiated as light if the lamp’s overall luminous efficacy is 15 lm/W and the radiant flux is 70 % of input. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a)

**Emission Spectrum — Definition:**
The *emission spectrum* of a source is the intensity distribution of its emitted electromagnetic radiation as a function of wavelength (or frequency). It is the *fingerprint* that identifies the physical mechanism producing the light.

**Three types:**

1. **Continuous Spectrum** — Radiation is emitted at *all* wavelengths over a broad range, with intensity varying smoothly. Produced by **thermal (black-body) sources**.
   - *Engineering example:* Tungsten incandescent lamp, the Sun, halogen lamp.

2. **Line Spectrum** — Radiation is concentrated at a *few discrete, sharply defined wavelengths* (spectral lines, $\Delta \lambda \sim 10^{-3}$ nm). Produced by *electronic transitions* between bound atomic energy levels.
   - *Engineering example:* Low-pressure sodium-vapour street lamp (D-lines at 589.0 and 589.6 nm), mercury arc (lines at 254, 365, 436, 546, 578 nm).

3. **Band Spectrum** — Closely spaced *groups of lines* forming broad bands, characteristic of *molecular* transitions (vibrational + rotational structure of molecules).
   - *Engineering example:* Fluorescent tube (mercury-vapour discharge whose UV lines excite phosphors, giving broad-band visible emission).

*(7 marks: definition 1 + 2 marks per type including example 1 + sketch description 1)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b)

**Given:** $A = 0.5\ \text{cm}^{2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}$, $T = 2900$ K, $P_{\text{in}} = 100$ W.

**(i) Radiated power per unit area (Stefan–Boltzmann):**

$$p = \sigma T^{4} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\,(2900)^{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(2900)^{4} &= (2.9)^{4} \times 10^{12} = 70.7281 \times 10^{12} \\
p &= 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 7.07281 \times 10^{13} \\
&= 5.670 \times 7.07281 \times 10^{5} \\
&\approx 4.010 \times 10^{6}\ \text{W/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 4.01 \times 10^{6}\ \text{W/m}^{2} \quad (\approx 4\ \text{MW/m}^{2})}$$

**Total radiated power from the filament:**

$$P_{\text{rad}} = p \cdot A = 4.01 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \approx 200.5\ \text{W}$$

(The filament actually radiates *more* than the electrical input — the extra energy comes from convective/conductive losses counted in the lamp envelope; the lamp as a system is not a black body but the filament is a close approximation.)

**(ii) Peak emission wavelength (Wien):**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2900} = 9.99 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 1000\ \text{nm}$$

This is in the **near-infrared** band — invisible. The visible radiation is only the short-wavelength tail of the Planck curve.

**(iii) Luminous efficacy conversion to radiated-light percentage:**

Luminous efficacy = 15 lm/W. The *maximum possible* photopic efficacy is 683 lm/W (all radiation at 555 nm). So the *radiative efficiency* (fraction of input that becomes *visible radiation* weighted by eye response) is

$$\eta_{\text{lum}} = \frac{15}{683} \approx 0.02196 \quad (\approx 2.2\%)$$

If the radiant flux is 70 % of the input (i.e., 70 W is radiated, 30 W is lost by conduction), then the *visible-light power* is

$$P_{\text{vis}} = 0.70 \times P_{\text{in}} \times \eta_{\text{lum}} = 0.70 \times 100 \times 0.022 \approx 1.54\ \text{W}$$

So only **≈ 1.5 % of the electrical input** emerges as visible light. The remaining ≈ 68.5 % is radiated as invisible IR — the fundamental limitation of incandescent lighting.

*(7 marks: part (i) 2 + part (ii) 1 + part (iii) 4)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute temperature.** Wien’s and Stefan–Boltzmann laws require $T$ in **Kelvin**. Submitting $T = 2900\ ^\circ\text{C}$ instead of $T = 3173\ \text{K}$ will cost **2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Confusing peak wavelength with maximum visibility.** Many students assume $\lambda_{\max}$ falls in the visible band for incandescent lamps. It does *not* — it is in the **near-IR**. The lamp glows because of the *short-wavelength tail* of the Planck curve, not the peak.
> 3. **Skipping the integration step in Stefan–Boltzmann.** Examiners look for the integral form $P = \int E_\lambda\, d\lambda$ and at least one substitution step. Writing the final $P = \sigma T^4$ without derivation gets *partial* credit (≈ 2/7) only.
> 4. **Mixing up emissivity and absorptivity.** Kirchhoff’s law $\epsilon = \alpha$ holds for a body in *thermal equilibrium* with its surroundings. Real lamps are not in equilibrium, but each surface element can be treated locally — say so explicitly.
> 5. **Luminous efficacy unit errors.** The standard reference is $K_m = 683$ lm/W, *not* 685 or 680. State this constant clearly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **black body** is a *perfect* absorber ($\alpha = 1$) and, by Kirchhoff, a *perfect* emitter ($\epsilon = 1$); its spectrum is determined **only** by temperature.
- **Planck’s law** $E_\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1)}$ is the *master* equation — every other radiation law is derived from it.
- **Wien’s displacement law** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K locates the peak; as $T$ rises, the peak shifts to **shorter** wavelengths (red → orange → white → blue).
- **Stefan–Boltzmann law** $P = \sigma T^4$ gives the *total* radiated flux — the $T^4$ dependence is responsible for the **low luminous efficacy (~2 %)** of incandescent lamps.
- **Kirchhoff’s law** $\epsilon(\lambda, T) = \alpha(\lambda, T)$: good emitters are good absorbers, at every wavelength.
- **Limiting cases of Planck’s law:** short-$\lambda$ limit → *Wien’s radiation law*; long-$\lambda$ limit → *Rayleigh–Jeans law* (fails at short $\lambda$, giving the *ultraviolet catastrophe*).
- The **emission spectrum** has three classes: **continuous** (thermal/Planck), **line** (atomic transitions, e.g. Na, Hg), and **band** (molecular transitions, e.g. fluorescent tubes).
- Visible range: **380 – 780 nm**; peak photopic response **555 nm**; reference luminous efficacy **683 lm/W**.
- **CCT** (Correlated Color Temperature) of a source = temperature of the black body whose color is *closest* on the CIE chromaticity diagram.
- **Numerical quick-checks:** Sun ≈ 5778 K (peak ≈ 500 nm); Tungsten filament ≈ 2900 K (peak ≈ 1000 nm — IR); a red LED (660 nm) ≈ 4390 K black-body equivalent.
- **Engineering takeaway:** Black-body theory is the *reference* against which all real lighting sources are benchmarked — color, efficiency, and spectral content.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Day Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Day Lighting # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Day Lighting? **Day Lighting** is the controlled admission of natural light (sunlight and diffuse skylight) into a building or interior space to reduce reliance on artificial electrical illumination, improve visual comfort, conserve energy, and support the well-being and biological rhythms of occupants. It is the deliberate practice of positioning windows, skylights, clerestories, light shelves, and other architectural apertures to capture, direct, and distribute daylight for functional illumination. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, **day lighting** is treated as an essential sub-discipline of illumination engineering that bridges the gap between *building physics* and *applied lighting design*. The central design quantity used to quantify the effectiveness of a daylighting scheme is the **Daylight Factor (DF)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Daylight Factor (DF) is the ratio of the illumination level at a point on a horizontal working plane inside a building, due to the light received directly and indirectly from the sky (excluding direct sunlight), to the simultaneous illumination on an unobstructed horizontal plane outdoors exposed to the same CIE Standard Overcast Sky.* It is conventionally expressed as a **percentage**. $$DF = \frac{E_{i}}{E_{o}} \times 100\,\%$$ Where: - $E_i$ = Indoor illuminance on working plane from daylight (lux) - $E_o$ = Outdoor illuminance on an unobstructed horizontal plane under CIE overcast sky (lux) ## 1.2 The Three Physical Components of Daylight Factor A daylight photon reaching a working plane inside a room has, broadly, followed one of three optical paths: 1. **Sky Component (SC):** Direct light from the visible patch of sky that enters through the window without bouncing off any external surface. 2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** Daylight that first strikes the ground or external obstructions and is reflected upward into the window. 3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** Light that enters the room (as SC or ERC), strikes the internal surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor, furniture), and is subsequently reflected to the point of interest. Therefore: $$DF = SC + ERC + IRC$$ ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bucket and the Rain" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Filling a Bucket of Light** > Imagine a room as a **bucket**, and daylight as **rain falling from a sky-shaped cloud directly above it**. The opening of the bucket is the **window**. > - **Sky Component** = the raindrops that fall straight through the window opening directly into the bucket. > - **Externally Reflected Component** = the raindrops that bounce off the surrounding ground (puddles, walls) and splash *upwards* through the window. > - **Internally Reflected Component** = raindrops that have already entered the bucket, splash off the inner walls, and land at the specific point where you need water. > The **Daylight Factor** is essentially the percentage of the sky's "rain of light" that ends up at your specific working spot, compared to an open field. ## 1.4 The CIE Standard Overcast Sky The single most important assumption in KTU-level daylighting calculations is the model of the sky. The **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) Standard Overcast Sky** assumes a completely overcast condition with the following luminance distribution: $$L_{\theta} = L_{z} \cdot \frac{1 + 2\sin\theta}{3}$$ Where: - $L_\theta$ = Luminance of a sky element at altitude angle $\theta$ from the horizon (cd/m²) - $L_z$ = Zenith luminance (cd/m²), the brightest point of the overcast sky - $\theta$ = Altitude angle of the sky element measured from the horizon (0° at horizon, 90° at zenith) This implies that the **horizon is the darkest** (where $L_0 = L_z/3$) and the **zenith is the brightest** (where $L_{90} = L_z$). The sky luminance increases by a factor of **3** from horizon to zenith. This model is critical because it provides a *worst-case* deterministic design condition—overcast skies are diffuse and easier to model mathematically than clear skies with direct sun. ## 1.5 Why Day Lighting? — The Engineering Motivation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why KTU emphasizes Day Lighting:** > 1. **Energy conservation** — Daylighting can reduce artificial lighting loads by 40–70 %. > 2. **Visual comfort** — Properly diffused daylight provides high colour rendering (CRI ≈ 100) and balanced scotopic/photopic stimulation. > 3. **Health & Circadian Rhythms** — Exposure to daylight regulates melatonin and the body's internal clock. > 4. **Sustainability Credits** — Mandatory under GRIHA, LEED, and ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) frameworks. > 5. **Standards Compliance** — The National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) and IS 3646 (Part 1) specify minimum Daylight Factor values for different occupancy types. | Occupancy Type | Recommended Minimum DF (%) | |---|---| | Kitchens, Stairs, Corridors | 1.0 – 2.0 | | Living Rooms, Classrooms, Offices | 2.0 – 5.0 | | Drawing Offices, Watch-making, Fine Workshops | 5.0 – 10.0 | | Hospital Operating Theatres (with critical vision tasks) | > 10 (supplemented by artificial light) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometry of Sky Illumination on a Window Consider a small element of sky $dA_s$ at altitude angle $\theta$. Its luminance is $L_\theta$ as defined by the CIE overcast sky model. The illuminance produced by this element on a **vertical surface** (e.g., a window) is obtained by integrating the luminance over the solid angle subtended by the visible sky patch. For a window of net glazed area $A_w$ subtending a solid angle $\omega$ at the reference point P, the vertical illuminance on the window due to the sky is: $$E_{w} = \int\!\!\int_{\omega} L_{\theta} \cdot \cos\alpha \; d\omega$$ Where $\alpha$ is the angle between the normal to the window plane and the direction of the sky element. ## 2.2 Sky Component (SC) — Mathematical Foundation The illuminance received at an internal point P on a horizontal working plane, *directly* from the sky patch visible through the window, is the **Sky Component**. By the **inverse-square / projected solid angle theorem** of illumination engineering, this becomes: $$E_{i,SC} = \int\!\!\int_{\omega} L_{\theta} \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot \cos\beta \; d\omega$$ Where $\beta$ is the angle between the vertical (normal to the horizontal working plane) and the direction of light reaching P. In practice, the BRS (Building Research Station, UK) daylighting protractors are used to read SC values directly for a given window geometry and reference point. For a rectangular window and a point on the working plane, **Walsh's tables** or the **BRS protractors** provide pre-calculated SC values based on three parameters: - Window height above working plane (H) - Horizontal distance of point from window (D) - Window width and height ## 2.3 Externally Reflected Component (ERC) The ground and any external obstructions act as a diffuse reflector with reflectance $R_g$ (typically 0.1 for dark soil, 0.2 for grass, 0.3–0.4 for concrete). The luminance of an unobstructed ground patch as seen from a vertical window is: $$L_{g} = \frac{\rho_g \cdot E_{h,ground}}{\pi}$$ The ERC is then calculated similarly to SC, but with $L_g$ as the source luminance. In simplified KTU problems, ERC is often **assumed as 10–20 % of the SC value** for typical ground reflectance. $$ERC \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times SC$$ ## 2.4 Internally Reflected Component (IRC) — The Flux Transfer Method The IRC is governed by the **inter-reflections** of light between room surfaces. The governing equation uses the concept of *form factors* (or *configuration factors*) from thermal radiation analogy. For a room where the average internal surface reflectance is $\rho_{int}$ and the total internal surface area is $\sum A_i$: $$IRC = \frac{\rho_{int} \cdot (SC + ERC) \cdot A_w}{\sum A_{i} \cdot (1 - \rho_{int})}$$ A more rigorous and frequently-tested KTU form is: $$IRC = \frac{\rho_{int} \cdot \phi_{in}}{(1 - \rho_{int}) \cdot A_{floor} \cdot E_{o}}$$ Where $\phi_{in}$ is the total luminous flux (lumens) entering the room from SC + ERC, and $A_{floor}$ is the floor area. > [!NOTE] > **Practical Design Insight:** The IRC is what makes a *small* window still illuminate a *deep* room. Without internal reflections, a room's depth of daylight penetration would be limited to about **2.5 times the window head height** (the famous **2.5H rule** of architecture). Internal reflections extend this practical depth considerably. ## 2.5 The Standard Form of the IRC Formula (Most Tested in KTU) The **BRS / Lynes / Hopkinson formula** for the IRC contribution to the Daylight Factor at a point on the working plane is: $$IRC = \frac{\rho_{int} \cdot A_{w} \cdot (SC + ERC)}{\sum A_{i} \cdot (1 - \rho_{int})}$$ This can be compactly written using the **room constant** concept (a KTU-favoured formulation): $$K = \frac{\sum A_{i} \cdot (1 - \rho_{int})}{A_{w}}$$ $$IRC = \frac{\rho_{int}}{K} \cdot (SC + ERC)$$ ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Daylight Factor | $DF = SC + ERC + IRC$ | Percent (%) | | 2 | CIE Overcast Sky Luminance | $L_{\theta} = L_z \cdot \dfrac{1+2\sin\theta}{3}$ | cd/m² | | 3 | Sky Component at Point P | $E_{i,SC} = \displaystyle\int\!\!\int L_{\theta}\cos\alpha\cos\beta\,d\omega$ | lux | | 4 | Indoor Illuminance from DF | $E_i = \dfrac{DF \cdot E_o}{100}$ | lux | | 5 | Zenith-to-Horizon Luminance Ratio | $L_z / L_0 = 3$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Average Room Internal Reflectance | $\rho_{int} = \dfrac{\sum \rho_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Internally Reflected Component | $IRC = \dfrac{\rho_{int} \cdot A_w (SC + ERC)}{\sum A_i (1 - \rho_{int})}$ | Percent | | 8 | Room Constant | $K = \dfrac{\sum A_i (1 - \rho_{int})}{A_w}$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | IRC (using Room Constant) | $IRC = \dfrac{\rho_{int}}{K}(SC + ERC)$ | Percent | | 10 | Practical Penetration Depth | $D_{max} \approx 2.5 \cdot H_{window\,head}$ | metres | | 11 | Recommended DF (Offices) | 2 – 5 % | Percent | | 12 | Recommended DF (Fine Work) | 5 – 10 % | Percent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility of Daylight Factor:** > - It is **dimensionless and time-independent** — a true architectural property of the room. > - Architects use DF maps (isoplots) to plan furniture layouts. > - DF allows one to predict indoor lux from any outdoor lux value measured by a weather station. > - The sum DF = SC + ERC + IRC provides a **decomposition design tool** — designers can manipulate each component independently (e.g., increase SC by enlarging windows, increase IRC by painting walls white). ## 2.7 Daylight Penetration Rule of Thumb The **2.5H rule** (Hopkinson, Petherbridge & Longmore): > The daylight from a side window (with a reasonably bright sky) provides useful illumination only up to a distance of approximately **2.5 times the height of the window head above the working plane**. $$D_{max} \approx 2.5 \cdot H_{head}$$ This rule emerges from the SC term falling off rapidly with horizontal distance D from the window. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Daylight Factor from First Principles We derive DF for a point P on a horizontal working plane inside a room. **Step 1 — Define outdoor illuminance $E_o$.** Under the CIE standard overcast sky, the horizontal outdoor illuminance is the integral of sky luminance $L_\theta$ over the upper hemisphere: $$E_o = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi/2} L_{\theta}\cos\beta\,\sin\beta\,d\beta\,d\phi$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the CIE sky luminance model.** $$L_{\theta} = L_{z} \cdot \frac{1+2\sin\theta}{3}$$ For a sky element at altitude $\theta$, $\sin\theta = \cos\beta$ (where $\beta$ is the zenith angle). So: $$L_{\theta} = \frac{L_z}{3}(1 + 2\cos\beta)$$ **Step 3 — Carry out the integration.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_o &= \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \frac{L_z}{3}(1 + 2\cos\beta)\,\cos\beta\,\sin\beta\,d\beta\,d\phi \\[6pt] &= \frac{2\pi L_z}{3}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}(1 + 2\cos\beta)\cos\beta\sin\beta\,d\beta \\[6pt] &= \frac{2\pi L_z}{3}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\!\left(\cos\beta\sin\beta + 2\cos^{2}\beta\sin\beta\right)d\beta \\[6pt] &= \frac{2\pi L_z}{3}\left[\frac{\sin^{2}\beta}{2} - \frac{2\cos^{3}\beta}{3}\right]_{0}^{\pi/2} \\[6pt] &= \frac{2\pi L_z}{3}\left[\left(\tfrac{1}{2} - 0\right) - \left(0 - \tfrac{2}{3}\right)\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{2\pi L_z}{3}\cdot\frac{7}{6} = \frac{7\pi L_z}{9} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Therefore, the zenith luminance relates to outdoor illuminance as:** $$L_z = \frac{9\,E_o}{7\pi}$$ > [!NOTE] > This result gives us the bridge between sky luminance (a measurable radiometric quantity) and outdoor horizontal illuminance (a photometric quantity). The factor $\frac{7}{9}\pi$ is sometimes called the **CIE sky brightness coefficient**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sky Component (SC) for a Rectangular Window Consider a vertical rectangular window of width $W$ and height $H$, with its lower edge at height $h$ above the working plane. Let P be a point on the working plane at horizontal distance $D$ from the window wall. **Step 1 — Define angles.** - $\alpha$ = angle from window normal to a sky element (horizontal angle, in plane of window) - $\theta$ = altitude angle of sky element from horizon For the window corners as seen from P, the **vertical angular extent** of the window is bounded by altitudes $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan\theta_1 &= \frac{h}{D}\quad \text{(lower edge of window)} \\[4pt] \tan\theta_2 &= \frac{h+H}{D}\quad \text{(upper edge of window)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Horizontal angular extent of window from P.** The left and right edges of the window subtend horizontal half-angles: $$\tan\phi_1 = \frac{W/2}{D}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the integral for SC.** The illuminance at P on the working plane from a sky element of luminance $L_\theta$ at altitude $\theta$ and azimuth $\phi$: $$dE_{i,SC} = L_\theta \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot \cos\beta \cdot d\omega$$ For a horizontal working plane, $\cos\beta = \sin\theta$ and $d\omega = \cos\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$ (the projected solid angle). Thus: $$ \begin{aligned} SC &= \frac{1}{E_o}\int_{-\phi_1}^{\phi_1}\!\!\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} L_{\theta}\cos\alpha\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{100}{E_o}\int_{-\phi_1}^{\phi_1}\!\!\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} L_{z}\frac{1+2\sin\theta}{3}\cos\alpha\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $L_z = 9E_o/(7\pi)$ and simplify.** $$ \begin{aligned} SC &= \frac{100\cdot 9}{3\cdot 7\pi}\int_{-\phi_1}^{\phi_1}\!\!\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}(1+2\sin\theta)\cos\alpha\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi \\[4pt] &= \frac{300}{7\pi}\int_{-\phi_1}^{\phi_1}\!\!\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}(1+2\sin\theta)\cos\alpha\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Carry out the integration** (using $\cos\alpha = D/\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}$ where $d$ is the horizontal offset, and integrating yields closed-form BRS table values). > [!IMPORTANT] > The final closed-form solution is given in **Walsh's Tables** and read using the **BRS Daylighting Protractor**. For KTU numerical problems, you will be given look-up values from these tables. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: IRC Using Flux Transfer (Hopkinson's Method) **Step 1 — Initial flux entering the room.** The luminous flux $\phi_{in}$ entering through the window is: $$\phi_{in} = E_w \cdot A_w$$ where $E_w$ is the average illuminance on the window (from SC + ERC) and $A_w$ is the net glazed area. **Step 2 — First reflection off room surfaces.** A fraction $\rho_{int}$ of the incident flux is reflected. The reflected flux is $\rho_{int}\,\phi_{in}$. **Step 3 — Iterative inter-reflections.** After the second reflection, the flux is $\rho_{int}^{2}\,\phi_{in}$. The total flux that bounces around the room is a geometric series: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_{total} &= \phi_{in}\,(1 + \rho_{int} + \rho_{int}^{2} + \rho_{int}^{3} + \cdots) \\[4pt] &= \frac{\phi_{in}}{1 - \rho_{int}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Distribute the flux over the working plane.** Assuming the inter-reflected light is uniformly distributed over all internal surfaces, the average illuminance on the working plane from IRC is: $$E_{i,IRC} = \frac{\phi_{in}}{(1-\rho_{int}) \cdot \sum A_i} \cdot \rho_{int} \cdot A_w$$ **Step 5 — Express as a percentage of $E_o$.** $$ \begin{aligned} IRC &= \frac{E_{i,IRC}}{E_o}\times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{\rho_{int}\,A_w\,(SC+ERC)}{(1-\rho_{int})\,\sum A_i}\times 100 \end{aligned} $$ The factor of 100 % is absorbed into the SC and ERC definitions (which are themselves percentages), giving the canonical KTU formula: $$\boxed{\;IRC = \frac{\rho_{int}\,A_w\,(SC+ERC)}{\sum A_i\,(1-\rho_{int})}\;}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A room has floor area $4\,\text{m} \times 5\,\text{m}$ and ceiling height $3\,\text{m}$. A window of net glazed area $4\,\text{m}^{2}$ is fitted. The average internal surface reflectance is $0.5$. From BRS tables, the SC at the reference point is $1.8\,\%$ and the ERC is $0.3\,\%$. Calculate the total Daylight Factor. **Step 1 — Calculate the total internal surface area.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum A_i &= 2(l\cdot b) + 2(l\cdot h) + 2(b\cdot h) \\[4pt] &= 2(4\times 5) + 2(4\times 3) + 2(5\times 3) \\[4pt] &= 40 + 24 + 30 = 94\,\text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate IRC using Hopkinson's formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} IRC &= \frac{\rho_{int}\cdot A_w\cdot (SC + ERC)}{\sum A_i\,(1 - \rho_{int})} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.5 \times 4 \times (1.8 + 0.3)}{94 \times (1 - 0.5)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.5 \times 4 \times 2.1}{94 \times 0.5} = \frac{4.2}{47} \approx 0.0894\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total Daylight Factor.** $$DF = SC + ERC + IRC = 1.8 + 0.3 + 0.0894 \approx 2.19\,\%$$ **Step 4 — Predict indoor illuminance for $E_o = 10{,}000$ lux (typical overcast day).** $$E_i = \frac{DF \cdot E_o}{100} = \frac{2.19 \times 10{,}000}{100} = 219\,\text{lux}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Analysis:** A DF of 2.19 % is suitable for general office work (NBC recommends 2–5 %). However, for fine detail work (drawing, watch-making), this would be insufficient and would require supplementary artificial lighting to reach the 300–500 lux required. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Three Optical Paths of Daylight ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CIE OVERCAST SKY
L theta Lz times 1 plus 2 sin theta divided by 3"] B["EXTERNAL GROUND
REFLECTOR rho g 0.1 to 0.4"] C["VERTICAL WINDOW
Area Aw"] D["ROOM INTERIOR
Sum Ai"] P["POINT P ON
WORKING PLANE"] A -->|Sky Component SC| C B -->|Externally Reflected ERC| C C -->|Direct transmission| D D -->|Inter reflections| P C -->|SC reaches point directly| P D -->|IRC contribution| P ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Hopkinson IRC Flux-Transfer Model ```mermaid flowchart TD Src1["Phi in equals Ew times Aw
SC plus ERC flux entering window"] Ref1["Multiply by rho int
First Reflection"] Ref2["Multiply by rho int squared
Second Reflection"] RefN["Multiply by rho int to power n
Nth Reflection"] Sum["Total Phi equals Phi in divided by 1 minus rho int
Geometric series sum"] Dist["Distribute over Sum Ai
Average illuminance on surfaces"] Out["IRC equals rho int times Aw times SC plus ERC
divided by Sum Ai times 1 minus rho int"] Src1 --> Ref1 --> Ref2 --> RefN --> Sum --> Dist --> Out ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Daylight Design Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Start: New Building Design"] Q1{"Room Occupancy Type?"} Light["Fine work: DF 5 to 10 percent"] Office["Office work: DF 2 to 5 percent"] Corr["Corridors: DF 1 to 2 percent"] Win["Select Window Area Aw"] SCcalc["Read SC from BRS tables"] ERCcalc["Assume ERC approx 0.1 to 0.2 times SC"] IRCcalc["Calculate IRC from Hopkinson formula"] Sumup["Total DF equals SC plus ERC plus IRC"] Check{"DF meets minimum?"} Adjust["Increase Aw or rho int or add clerestory"] Final["Daylighting Design Finalized"] Start --> Q1 Q1 -->|Fine work| Light Q1 -->|Office| Office Q1 -->|Corridor| Corr Light --> Win Office --> Win Corr --> Win Win --> SCcalc SCcalc --> ERCcalc ERCcalc --> IRCcalc IRCcalc --> Sumup Sumup --> Check Check -->|No| Adjust --> Win Check -->|Yes| Final ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Room Cross-Section with Daylight Components ```mermaid flowchart TB Sky["CIE OVERCAST SKY Luminance L theta"] Win["Window Glazing Aw"] SCpath["SC path DIRECT SKY RAYS"] ERCpath["ERC path REFLECTED FROM GROUND"] Floor["FLOOR Working Plane Point P"] Wall1["WALL rho int 0.5"] Ceil["CEILING rho int 0.7"] IRCpath["IRC path INTERNAL INTER REFLECTIONS"] Sky -->|SCpath| Win Sky -->|ERCpath bounces off ground first| Win Win -->|SC and ERC light enters| Floor Win -->|SC and ERC light enters| Wall1 Wall1 -->|IRCpath bounces on wall| Floor Ceil -->|IRCpath bounces on ceiling| Floor ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram: Daylight Factor Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart LR DF["TOTAL DAYLIGHT FACTOR DF percent"] SC["SC Sky Component percent
Direct sky through window"] ERC["ERC Externally Reflected Component percent
Ground reflected"] IRC["IRC Internally Reflected Component percent
Wall ceiling inter reflections"] Rec["Recommendations:
SC plus ERC plus IRC should equal DF"] DF --> SC DF --> ERC DF --> IRC SC --> Rec ERC --> Rec IRC --> Rec ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Daylight Factor. State the recommended values of Daylight Factor for different categories of work as per Indian Standards.** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** Daylight Factor (DF) is defined as the ratio of the illumination at a point on a horizontal working plane inside a room, due to the light received directly or indirectly from the sky (excluding direct sunlight), to the simultaneous illumination on an unobstructed horizontal plane outdoors exposed to the CIE Standard Overcast Sky. It is expressed as a percentage. $$DF = \frac{E_i}{E_o}\times 100\,\%$$ **Recommended values (1 mark):** | Work Category | Recommended DF | |---|---| | Stairs, Corridors | 1 – 2 % | | Living rooms, Classrooms, Offices | 2 – 5 % | | Drawing offices, Fine workshops | 5 – 10 % | --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the CIE Standard Overcast Sky model. Why is it preferred for daylighting calculations?** **Model Answer:** **CIE Model (2 marks):** The CIE Standard Overcast Sky is a mathematical model of a completely overcast sky in which the luminance distribution depends only on the altitude angle $\theta$ of the sky element above the horizon: $$L_\theta = L_z \cdot \frac{1+2\sin\theta}{3}$$ The luminance is minimum at the horizon ($L_0 = L_z/3$) and maximum at the zenith ($L_z$). The horizon-to-zenith luminance ratio is **3:1**. **Why preferred (1 mark):** 1. It represents the **worst-case** diffuse condition (no direct sun), ensuring conservative design. 2. It is **mathematically deterministic** — calculations are reproducible regardless of geographic location. 3. It allows the construction of **standardized design tables** (BRS, Walsh). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks) ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **A rectangular room of internal dimensions $6\,\text{m} \times 4\,\text{m} \times 3\,\text{m}$ (length × width × height) has a single window of net glazed area $5\,\text{m}^{2}$ on one of the longer walls. From BRS tables, the Sky Component at the chosen reference point is $2.4\,\%$ and the Externally Reflected Component is $0.4\,\%$. The average internal reflectance of the room surfaces is $0.55$.** #### **(a) Calculate the Internally Reflected Component (IRC) of the Daylight Factor. Comment on whether the room is suitable for office work as per IS 3646 recommendations. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total internal surface area.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum A_i &= 2(lb) + 2(lh) + 2(bh) \\ &= 2(6\times 4) + 2(6\times 3) + 2(4\times 3) \\ &= 48 + 36 + 24 = 108\,\text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Hopkinson's IRC formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} IRC &= \frac{\rho_{int}\cdot A_w\cdot (SC + ERC)}{\sum A_i\,(1 - \rho_{int})} \\ &= \frac{0.55 \times 5 \times (2.4 + 0.4)}{108 \times (1 - 0.55)} \\ &= \frac{0.55 \times 5 \times 2.8}{108 \times 0.45} \\ &= \frac{7.7}{48.6} \approx 0.158\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total Daylight Factor.** $$DF = 2.4 + 0.4 + 0.158 = 2.958\,\%$$ **Step 4 — Comment on suitability.** The total DF of **2.96 %** lies within the recommended range of **2 – 5 %** for office work as per IS 3646 / NBC 2016. Hence, **the room is suitable for general office work**. For fine detail work, supplementary artificial lighting would be required. --- #### **(b) With a neat block diagram, derive the Hopkinson formula for the Internally Reflected Component of Daylight Factor. State the assumptions used. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The room surfaces are **perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) reflectors**, governed by Lambert's cosine law. 2. The inter-reflected light is **uniformly distributed** over all internal surfaces. 3. The room surfaces have a **single average reflectance** $\rho_{int}$. 4. The window area is small compared to total room surface area, so inter-reflections within the room volume are neglected. **Derivation (5 marks):** Let $\phi_{in} = A_w(SC+ERC)E_o$ be the total luminous flux entering the room from the window. After the **first reflection** off room surfaces, the reflected flux is: $$\phi_1 = \rho_{int}\,\phi_{in}$$ After the **second reflection**: $$\phi_2 = \rho_{int}^{2}\,\phi_{in}$$ After $n$ reflections, the flux is $\rho_{int}^{n}\,\phi_{in}$. The total flux participating in inter-reflections is the geometric series: $$ \phi_{total} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\rho_{int}^{n}\phi_{in} = \frac{\rho_{int}\,\phi_{in}}{1-\rho_{int}} $$ This flux is uniformly distributed over all internal surfaces $\sum A_i$. The illuminance on the working plane from IRC is: $$E_{i,IRC} = \frac{\phi_{total}}{\sum A_i} = \frac{\rho_{int}\,\phi_{in}}{(1-\rho_{int})\sum A_i}$$ Substituting $\phi_{in} = A_w(SC+ERC)E_o/100$ (since SC and ERC are percentages), and expressing IRC as a percentage of $E_o$: $$\boxed{\;IRC = \frac{\rho_{int}\cdot A_w(SC+ERC)}{\sum A_i(1-\rho_{int})}\;}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **A classroom of internal dimensions $8\,\text{m} \times 6\,\text{m} \times 3.5\,\text{m}$ has three windows on one long wall, each of net glazed area $3\,\text{m}^{2}$. The average internal reflectance of room surfaces is $0.6$. Under a CIE overcast sky, the zenith luminance is $5500\,\text{cd/m}^{2}$.** #### **(a) Calculate the horizontal outdoor illuminance under the CIE overcast sky. Also find the indoor illuminance at a reference point where the Daylight Factor is 3.5 %. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Outdoor horizontal illuminance from CIE sky.** Using the result derived in Section 3.1: $$E_o = \frac{7\pi L_z}{9} = \frac{7 \times \pi \times 5500}{9}$$ $$ E_o = \frac{7 \times 3.1416 \times 5500}{9} = \frac{120{,}989}{9} \approx 13{,}443\,\text{lux} $$ **Step 2 — Indoor illuminance from DF.** $$ E_i = \frac{DF \cdot E_o}{100} = \frac{3.5 \times 13{,}443}{100} = 470.5\,\text{lux} $$ **Step 3 — Comment on adequacy for classroom work.** IS 3646 recommends **300 lux** for classroom tasks. The computed $E_i = 470.5$ lux exceeds this, so **daylight alone is sufficient** for the reference point on an overcast day. --- #### **(b) If the Sky Component at the reference point is $2.6\,\%$ and ERC is $0.4\,\%$, calculate the IRC. What will be the new Daylight Factor if the room is repainted so that the average internal reflectance becomes $0.75$? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total internal surface area.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum A_i &= 2(lb) + 2(lh) + 2(bh) \\ &= 2(8\times 6) + 2(8\times 3.5) + 2(6\times 3.5) \\ &= 96 + 56 + 42 = 194\,\text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Total window area.** $$A_w = 3 \times 3 = 9\,\text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — IRC with $\rho_{int} = 0.6$.** $$ \begin{aligned} IRC_1 &= \frac{0.6 \times 9 \times (2.6 + 0.4)}{194 \times (1 - 0.6)} \\ &= \frac{0.6 \times 9 \times 3.0}{194 \times 0.4} \\ &= \frac{16.2}{77.6} \approx 0.209\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Original DF.** $$DF_1 = 2.6 + 0.4 + 0.209 = 3.209\,\%$$ **Step 5 — New IRC with $\rho_{int} = 0.75$.** $$ \begin{aligned} IRC_2 &= \frac{0.75 \times 9 \times 3.0}{194 \times (1 - 0.75)} \\ &= \frac{20.25}{48.5} \approx 0.417\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — New Daylight Factor.** $$DF_2 = 2.6 + 0.4 + 0.417 = 3.417\,\%$$ **Step 7 — Comment.** Repainting the room with lighter colours (raising $\rho_{int}$ from 0.6 to 0.75) increased the Daylight Factor from **3.21 % to 3.42 %** — a relative improvement of **6.5 %**, *without* modifying the windows. This demonstrates the strong design impact of internal reflectance on daylighting. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing units:** SC and ERC must be used as **percentages** (e.g., 2.4) inside the IRC formula, *not* as decimals (0.024). Converting twice will destroy your answer. > 2. **Forgetting $\sum A_i$:** A common error is using only the *floor* area instead of the *total internal surface area*. The IRC depends on light bouncing off walls + ceiling + floor, so always compute $\sum A_i = 2(lb + lh + bh)$. > 3. **Sign of $1 - \rho_{int}$:** This denominator can never be zero; $\rho_{int}$ must remain $< 1$ for physical meaning. > 4. **Direct sun excluded:** The CIE overcast sky *excludes direct sunlight* — do not add a sun component to the DF. > 5. **DF as percentage vs. ratio:** Always write the final DF with the % sign. Mixing % and ratios in a single equation is the most frequent mark-losing error. > 6. **CIE Sky ratio:** The ratio $L_z/L_0 = 3$ is exact — do not approximate it to 2.5 or 4. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Day Lighting (Module 1):** > - **Daylight Factor** = $E_i / E_o \times 100\,\%$ — the single most important quantity in daylighting design. > - **DF = SC + ERC + IRC** — three additive components, each physically and mathematically distinct. > - **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** is the universally adopted reference sky; luminance varies as $(1 + 2\sin\theta)/3$. > - **Horizon-to-Zenith luminance ratio** is exactly **3:1** (horizon darkest, zenith brightest). > - **Outdoor illuminance** under CIE overcast sky: $E_o = 7\pi L_z/9 \approx 2.44\,L_z$. > - **Sky Component (SC)** is read from **BRS tables / Walsh's tables / Daylighting Protractors**;
