depends on window geometry, room geometry, and reference point. > - **ERC** is typically **10–20 % of SC** for ordinary ground reflectance; for snow-covered ground, can rise to **30–50 %**. > - **IRC** is calculated using **Hopkinson's formula** involving total internal surface area and average reflectance. > - **Hopkinson's IRC formula**: $IRC = \rho_{int}\,A_w(SC+ERC) / [\sum A_i(1-\rho_{int})]$ — must use **percent** values for SC, ERC. > - **Room Constant** $K = \sum A_i(1-\rho_{int})/A_w$ is a useful auxiliary quantity; $IRC = (\rho_{int}/K)(SC+ERC)$. > - **2.5H rule**: Useful daylight penetration depth from a side window ≈ 2.5 × window head height. > - **Recommended DF values**: 1–2 % (corridors), 2–5 % (offices, classrooms), 5–10 % (fine work). > - **Practical Design Impact**: Higher $\rho_{int}$ (lighter paint) substantially increases DF; enlarging $A_w$ increases both SC and IRC. > - **Energy Linkage**: A well-designed daylighting system can save 40–70 % of artificial lighting energy. > - **Assumptions of CIE Model**: Completely overcast, no direct sun, luminance only a function of altitude angle. > - **Limitations of CIE Model**: Underestimates brightness in tropical/clear-sky regions; the CIE Clear Sky and CIE Intermediate Sky models exist for those cases. > - **Integration with Artificial Light**: Modern buildings use **photosensor-controlled dimming** to blend daylight and electric light seamlessly. > - **Shading Devices**: Overhangs control direct sun; side fins control low-angle sky brightness; both affect DF by modifying the visible sky patch. > - **NBC 2016 / IS 3646 (Part 1)** is the Indian standard governing minimum DF for different occupancy types. --- *End of Module 1 — Day Lighting Notes* *PEEET418 | Illumination Technology | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Artificial Lighting: Qualities of Good Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Artificial Lighting: Qualities of Good Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Artificial Lighting** refers to the deliberate generation and distribution of visible light through electrical means (lamps, luminaires, control gear) in the absence of, or in supplementation to, natural daylight, in order to support human visual performance, comfort, safety, and aesthetics within a defined interior or exterior space.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework (PEEET418 – Illumination Technology, Module 1), **"Qualities of Good Lighting"** constitute the qualitative performance parameters that distinguish an acceptable lighting installation from a merely luminous one. They are evaluated against physiological, psychological, and engineering criteria codified by the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES)**, the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)**, and the **National Lighting Code (NBC 2016, Part 8 – Building Services)**.

The seven universally accepted qualities of good artificial lighting are:

1. **Adequate Illumination Level**
2. **Uniformity of Illumination**
3. **Freedom from Glare** (Direct and Reflected)
4. **Good Colour Rendering and Appropriate Colour Appearance**
5. **Absence of Harsh Shadows**
6. **Freedom from Flicker**
7. **Suitability of Luminaire / Aesthetic Integration**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a high-frequency 8–14 mark question. Students are expected to define each quality, justify its physiological basis, and correlate it with the relevant Indian Standard (IS 3646 / IS 6665 / NBC 2016) values. Memorization of numerical thresholds (lux levels, UGR indices, CRI values) is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef preparing a gourmet dish in your kitchen**. Even if you have the most expensive gas stove and the finest ingredients, the dish will be ruined if:
- The **light is too dim** (you cannot see the colour of the spices),
- A **single tube light flickers above your head** (causing eye strain and distraction),
- A **naked bulb shines directly into your eyes** (glare from the bare lamp),
- **Dark shadows fall across the chopping board** (poor distribution from a single point source).

Similarly, good artificial lighting is **not just about the wattage of the bulb** — it is about creating a luminous environment that supports the *visual task*, the *human comfort*, and the *aesthetic intent* of the space. A poorly lit conference room or factory floor may have enough total light but still fail on glare, uniformity, and colour rendering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mental Hook:** Think of lighting as "**photons packaged for human use**." The *Qualities of Good Lighting* are the *quality control checks* on this packaging — ensuring the photons arrive at the right *quantity*, at the right *place*, with the right *colour*, and at the right *stability*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics and their units are foundational to the rest of this module:

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** Measured in **lumen (lm)**. Total light emitted by a source.
- **Luminous Intensity ($I$):** Measured in **candela (cd)**. Light emitted per unit solid angle.
- **Illuminance ($E$):** Measured in **lux (lx) = lm/m²**. Light incident on a surface.
- **Luminance ($L$):** Measured in **cd/m²**. Light reflected/emitted per unit projected area.
- **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$):** Measured in **lm/W**. Lumens produced per watt of input.
- **Colour Rendering Index (CRI / $R_a$):** Dimensionless (0–100). Accuracy of colour appearance.
- **Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT):** Measured in **Kelvin (K)**. Warmth/coolness of light.
- **Unified Glare Rating (UGR):** Dimensionless (typically 10–30). Lower is better.
- **Illuminance Uniformity Ratio ($U_0$):** $E_{min}/E_{avg}$ (dimensionless).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Convention:** The seven base SI units (metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) include the **candela**, which is the only one tied to a human-perceived quantity (photometric weighting via the $V(\lambda)$ curve peaking at 555 nm).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Qualities — Engineering Breakdown

### Quality 1: Adequate Illumination Level
- **What it means:** The average illuminance on the working plane must be sufficient for the visual task.
- **Why it matters:** Below threshold, visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, and task speed all drop sharply (Ricketson's law). Above threshold, it wastes energy without proportional benefit.
- **How it is set:** Determined by **IS 3646** (for interiors) and **NBC 2016**. Values range from **100 lx** (rough assembly) to **2000 lx** (fine electronics inspection).
- **Key constant:** For a horizontal task plane at 0.85 m above the floor.

### Quality 2: Uniformity of Illumination
- **What it means:** The ratio of minimum to average illuminance across the working plane.
- **Formula:** 
$$U_0 = \frac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$$
- **Why it matters:** Uneven lighting forces the eye to constantly adapt, causing fatigue.
- **KTU Threshold Values:**
  - General offices: $U_0 \geq 0.7$
  - Classrooms: $U_0 \geq 0.7$
  - Industrial fine work: $U_0 \geq 0.8$
  - Road lighting: $U_0 \geq 0.4$ (longitudinal)

### Quality 3: Freedom from Glare
- **What it means:** Limiting the luminance contrast between the luminaire and the background that would cause discomfort or disability.
- **Two types:**
  - **Discomfort Glare:** Causes annoyance, measured by **UGR**.
  - **Disability Glare:** Reduces visibility (veiling luminance).
- **Engineering control:** Use diffusers, louvers, indirect lighting, recessed luminaires.

### Quality 4: Colour Rendering and Appearance
- **Colour Rendering Index (CRI / $R_a$):** Measures how accurately a light source reveals colours vs. a reference source of the same CCT.
- **KTU Thresholds:**
  - General interiors: $R_a \geq 80$
  - Colour-critical tasks (printing, hospitals): $R_a \geq 90$
- **CCT Guidance:**
  - < 3300 K → Warm (residential, hospitality)
  - 3300–5300 K → Neutral (offices, schools)
  - > 5300 K → Cool (industrial, task-oriented)

### Quality 5: Absence of Harsh Shadows
- **What it means:** Task lighting should not produce deep, sharp shadows that obscure detail.
- **Engineering control:** Use multiple diffused sources, indirect lighting, or supplemental task lighting rather than a single high-intensity point source.

### Quality 6: Freedom from Flicker
- **What it means:** Light output should remain stable in time, particularly important at low frequencies.
- **Why it matters:** Flicker below ~100 Hz (especially around 8–15 Hz) can trigger **photosensitive epilepsy** and contributes to **sick building syndrome** and eye strain.
- **Engineering control:** Use high-frequency electronic ballasts (> 20 kHz) for fluorescent/LED.

### Quality 7: Suitability of Luminaire
- **What it means:** The luminaire must match the architectural intent, be easy to maintain, and have an appropriate **Ingress Protection (IP) rating** for its environment.
- **IP Coding:** Two-digit code. E.g., **IP65** = dust-tight + protected against water jets.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical KTU Value / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance (Inverse Square Law) | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | lx (lm/m²) | Task-dependent |
| 2 | Luminous Flux (from Solid Angle) | $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ | lm (cd·sr) | — |
| 3 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P_{input}}$ | lm/W | LED: 80–150; Incandescent: 10–17 |
| 4 | Mean Spherical Candle Power | $I_{mscp} = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi}$ | cd | — |
| 5 | Uniformity Ratio | $U_0 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | $\geq 0.7$ (offices) |
| 6 | Room Index | $K = \dfrac{L \cdot W}{H_m(L+W)}$ | dimensionless | 0.8–5.0 typical |
| 7 | Maintenance Factor (MF) | $MF = LLMF \times LSF \times RSMF$ | dimensionless | 0.6–0.8 typical |
| 8 | Number of Luminaires | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot MF \cdot UF \cdot n}$ | integer | rounded up |
| 9 | Glare Index (UGR) | $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.25}{L_b}\sum \dfrac{L^2 \omega}{p^2}\right)$ | dimensionless | $\leq 19$ (offices) |
| 10 | Scotopic / Photopic Ratio (S/P) | $S/P = \dfrac{\Phi_{scotopic}}{\Phi_{photopic}}$ | dimensionless | LEDs: 1.0–2.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All vertical bars in table cells have been replaced with $\vert$ or rewritten to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the field, these qualities collectively determine:
- **Energy code compliance** (ECBC / ECBC+ in India).
- **Worker productivity** in factories (illuminance and CRI directly impact defect rates).
- **Student learning outcomes** in classrooms (uniformity and CCT affect concentration).
- **Patient recovery times** in hospitals (good lighting reduces stress, prevents falls).
- **Legal liability** in workplaces (NBC 2016 minimums are enforceable under the Factories Act).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverse Square Law of Illuminance

The most fundamental photometric law used in lighting design.

**Starting Principle:** A point source emits luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions with intensity $I$. At a distance $d$, the flux is spread over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$.

**Step 1 — Surface area of the sphere at distance d:**

$$A = 4\pi d^2$$

**Step 2 — Illuminance (flux per unit area) at distance d:**

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Phi = 4\pi I$ (since $I$ = flux per unit solid angle integrated over full sphere):**

$$E = \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Account for the angle of incidence (Lambert's Cosine Law):** If the surface is not perpendicular to the light ray, the projected area increases by $1/\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the incident ray and the surface normal.

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the distance reduces illuminance to **one-quarter**. This is why pendant lights in high-bay factories (10–15 m) require luminaires rated tens of thousands of lumens.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Luminaires Required

This is the most frequently asked numerical question in KTU ESE.

**Given:** Room area $A$ in m², required illuminance $E$ in lx, luminous flux per lamp $\Phi$ in lm, number of lamps per luminaire $n$, maintenance factor $MF$, and utilization factor $UF$.

**Step 1 — Total luminous flux required on the working plane:**

$$\Phi_{required} = E \times A$$

**Step 2 — Total flux that each luminaire (with $n$ lamps) must deliver from source to compensate for losses:**

$$\Phi_{per\ luminaire\ at\ source} = \frac{\Phi_{required}}{MF \times UF} = \frac{E \cdot A}{MF \cdot UF}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by flux per lamp times lamps per luminaire:**

$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{MF \cdot UF \cdot \Phi \cdot n}$$

**Step 4 — Always round UP to the next integer:**

$$N_{final} = \lceil N \rceil$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Standard):**
A classroom of $12 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$ requires $E = 500$ lx. Use luminaires with 2 fluorescent lamps each, $\Phi = 4000$ lm per lamp. $MF = 0.8$, $UF = 0.6$.

$$N = \frac{500 \times 96}{0.8 \times 0.6 \times 4000 \times 2} = \frac{48000}{3840} = 12.5 \Rightarrow 13 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Re-layout rule:** 13 is awkward; use 14 (4 rows × 2 columns would be 8, so consider 7 rows × 2 = 14) or recalculate spacing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maintenance Factor (MF) Decomposition

The maintenance factor accounts for the depreciation of light output over time. It is the **product of three independent factors**:

**Step 1 — Define each factor:**
- **LLMF** (Lamp Lumen Maintenance Factor): Lamp's lumen depreciation with age.
- **LSF** (Lamp Survival Factor): Probability that the lamp is still operating.
- **RSMF** (Room Surface Maintenance Factor): Reflectance change due to dirt on walls/ceiling.

**Step 2 — The multiplicative relationship:**

$$MF = LLMF \times LSF \times RSMF$$

**Step 3 — Example at end of maintenance cycle (typical 3 years for LED, 1 year for fluorescent):**

$$MF = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.85 = 0.726$$

**Engineering Insight:** If you fail to apply MF, the *installed* illuminance will be much higher than the *maintained* illuminance, leading to premature visual fatigue when the system ages.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cause-Effect of Each Quality

| Quality | If Ignored → Consequence | KTU Standard / Reference | Engineering Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adequate Level | Visual fatigue, errors, accidents | IS 3646 | Increase luminaire flux or count |
| Uniformity | Constant pupil adaptation, fatigue | $U_0 \geq 0.7$ | Spacing-to-height ratio $\leq 1.5$ |
| Glare Control | Headaches, reduced visibility | UGR $\leq 19$ | Louvers, diffusers, indirect |
| Colour Rendering | Misjudged colour-critical tasks | $R_a \geq 80$ (general), $\geq 90$ (colour-critical) | Use CRI 90+ LEDs or halogen |
| Shadow Control | Hidden details, eye strain | Visual task requirement | Multiple sources, indirect light |
| Flicker Control | Epilepsy, headaches | IEEE 1789, IS 16103 | High-frequency electronic drivers |
| Luminaire Suitability | Safety hazards, aesthetic mismatch | IP rating per IS 12063 | Select IP44/65 for wet areas |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a high-yield KTU answer scaffold. Reproducing this table in an exam (with examples) often secures full marks in 8-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Seven Qualities of Good Lighting — Interrelationship

The qualities are **not independent**; they form a coupled system. A change in one affects the others.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Light Source and Luminaire System] --> B[Quality 1: Adequate Illuminance Level]
    A --> C[Quality 2: Uniformity of Illumination]
    A --> D[Quality 3: Glare Control]
    A --> E[Quality 4: Colour Rendering and CCT]
    A --> F[Quality 5: Shadow Management]
    A --> G[Quality 6: Flicker Control]
    A --> H[Quality 7: Luminaire Suitability]

    B --> I[Human Visual Performance]
    C --> I
    D --> J[Human Visual Comfort]
    E --> K[Aesthetic and Task Accuracy]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> K

    I --> L[Acceptable Lighting Installation]
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Compliance with IS 3646 and NBC 2016]
    M --> N[Engineering Goal Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram: How a Quality is Quantified

This diagram shows the engineering workflow from a quality parameter to a measurable metric and finally a corrective design action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Identify Visual Task] --> P2[Select KTU/IS Standard Threshold]
    P2 --> P3[Measure or Calculate Photometric Quantity]
    P3 --> P4{Compare with Threshold}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Compliant Installation]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Identify Cause of Deviation]
    P6 --> P7[Apply Corrective Design Action]
    P7 --> P3
```

**Example for Glare:**
- **Identify Task:** Office computer work
- **Standard Threshold:** UGR ≤ 19
- **Measure:** $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.25}{L_b}\sum \frac{L^2 \omega}{p^2}\right)$
- **Compare:** If calculated UGR = 22, the installation fails.
- **Corrective Action:** Replace bare fluorescent tubes with parabolic louver recessed luminaires; raise mounting height; reduce lamp lumens.

## 4.3 Cross-Impact Matrix Diagram (Block-Level)

This shows how each quality affects every other quality. Read row → column to see impact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph core[Photometric Core]
        Q1[Q1: Illuminance]
        Q2[Q2: Uniformity]
        Q3[Q3: Glare]
        Q4[Q4: Colour]
        Q5[Q5: Shadows]
        Q6[Q6: Flicker]
        Q7[Q7: Luminaire]
    end

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 --> Q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** Increasing illuminance (Q1) by adding luminaires can worsen glare (Q3) and reduce uniformity (Q2) if spacing is not adjusted. This is why lighting design is always an **iterative optimization**, not a one-shot calculation.

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right CCT and CRI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Identify Space Type] --> S2{Is colour accuracy critical?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[CCT 4000K, CRI greater than 90]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is the space a hospital or studio?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[CCT 5000K to 6500K, CRI greater than 95]
    S4 -- No --> S6{Is the space residential or hospitality?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[CCT 2700K to 3000K, CRI greater than 80]
    S6 -- No --> S8{Is the space office or classroom?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[CCT 4000K, CRI greater than 80]
    S8 -- No --> S10[CCT 5000K, CRI greater than 70]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "illumination level" as a quality of good lighting. State the recommended illuminance for (a) a general office and (b) a hospital operating theatre as per IS 3646.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Illumination level is the quantity of luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface, measured in **lux (lx = lm/m²)**. It is the most fundamental quantitative quality of good lighting, ensuring the visual task is performed with adequate visibility.
- **Recommended values (2 marks):**
  - (a) General office: **300 lx to 500 lx** (typical maintained value).
  - (b) Hospital operating theatre: **10,000 lx to 20,000 lx** (with shadow-free, high-CRI surgical luminaires).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is meant by "glare" in artificial lighting? Differentiate between discomfort glare and disability glare.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Glare (1 mark):** Glare is the sensation produced by luminances within the visual field that are sufficiently greater than the luminance to which the eyes are adapted, causing annoyance, discomfort, or loss of visual performance.
- **Discomfort Glare (1 mark):** Causes a feeling of discomfort or annoyance without necessarily reducing the ability to see. Quantified by **UGR (Unified Glare Rating)**.
- **Disability Glare (1 mark):** Reduces visual performance by scattering light in the eye (veiling luminance), as when driving toward the low sun. Measured by equivalent veiling luminance.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven qualities of good artificial lighting** with relevant formulae and Indian Standard references. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory hall of dimensions **30 m × 15 m × 6 m** (L × W × H) requires an average maintained illuminance of **400 lx**. Use the following data: **UF = 0.5, MF = 0.8, Luminaire: 2-lamp fluorescent, $\Phi_{lamp}$ = 5000 lm.** Determine the number of luminaires required and the luminaire spacing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

1. **Adequate Illumination Level:** Defined by the average maintained illuminance on the working plane (0.85 m above floor). Standards per **IS 3646** (e.g., 150 lx for rough workshop, 1000 lx for fine bench work). Governed by $E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ (Inverse Square Law).

2. **Uniformity of Illumination:** Defined by $U_0 = \frac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$. For offices and classrooms, $U_0 \geq 0.7$ is required. Achieved by maintaining a **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)** $\leq 1.5$ for most fluorescent luminaires.

3. **Freedom from Glare:** Achieved by controlling the **UGR** (Unified Glare Rating). For office work, UGR $\leq 19$. Controlled by using diffusers, parabolic louvers, and recessed luminaires.

4. **Colour Rendering and Appearance:** $R_a$ (CRI) and CCT (Kelvin). For interiors, $R_a \geq 80$ is the KTU/IS 3646 minimum. CCT selection depends on application.

5. **Absence of Harsh Shadows:** Controlled by using **multiple diffused light sources** or indirect lighting, rather than a single high-intensity point source.

6. **Freedom from Flicker:** Avoid flicker by using **high-frequency electronic ballasts** (> 20 kHz). Flicker is governed by **IEEE 1789** and **IS 16103** standards.

7. **Suitability of Luminaire:** The luminaire must have an appropriate **IP rating** (per **IS 12063**), be aesthetically integrated, and be easy to maintain.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given Data:**
- $A = 30 \times 15 = 450 \text{ m}^2$
- $E = 400 \text{ lx}$
- $UF = 0.5$, $MF = 0.8$
- $\Phi_{lamp} = 5000 \text{ lm}$, $n = 2$ lamps/luminaire

**Step 1 — Apply the standard formula:**

$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi \cdot n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$N = \frac{400 \times 450}{0.5 \times 0.8 \times 5000 \times 2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.40$$
$$0.40 \times 5000 = 2000$$
$$2000 \times 2 = 4000$$

**Step 4 — Compute N:**

$$N = \frac{180000}{4000} = 45 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 5 — Determine spacing:** With 45 luminaires, choose a practical layout:
- 9 rows × 5 columns = 45
- Row spacing along length: $30 / 9 = 3.33 \text{ m}$
- Column spacing along width: $15 / 5 = 3.0 \text{ m}$

**Verification:** Mounting height to be assumed ~ 0.7 m from ceiling for suspension. Check **SHR $\leq 1.5$**: With 3.33 m and 3.0 m spacings, this is well within the 1.5 ratio for fluorescent luminaires, so uniformity $U_0 \geq 0.7$ is satisfied.

**Final Answer:** **45 luminaires**, arranged in **9 rows × 5 columns**, with spacings of **3.33 m × 3.0 m**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** and **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)**. Why are they considered essential qualities of good artificial lighting? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **classroom of 10 m × 7 m** must achieve **500 lx** using LED panels of $\Phi = 3600$ lm each. $UF = 0.65$, $MF = 0.85$. Determine the number of panels required and check for UGR compliance if the panels are recessed with parabolic louvers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Unified Glare Rating (UGR):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** UGR is a numerical index (10–30) quantifying discomfort glare from a lighting installation, defined by the CIE. Lower values = less glare.
- **Formula (2 marks):**
$$UGR = 8 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.25}{L_b}\sum \frac{L^2 \omega}{p^2}\right)$$
where $L_b$ is the background luminance, $L$ is the luminance of each luminaire, $\omega$ is the solid angle subtended, and $p$ is the Guth position index.
- **Why essential (1 mark):** Excessive glare causes discomfort, fatigue, and reduced productivity, violating the "freedom from glare" quality. UGR is the internationally accepted metric mandated by IS 3646 for indoor workplaces.

**Colour Rendering Index (CRI / $R_a$):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** CRI is a dimensionless index (0–100) measuring how accurately a light source renders the colours of objects compared to a reference illuminant of the same CCT. $R_a = 100$ is a perfect match.
- **Why essential (1 mark):** Low-CRI lighting (e.g., low-pressure sodium, $R_a \approx 20$) distorts colours, causing misjudgment in tasks like medical diagnosis, textile inspection, and food grading. IS 3646 mandates $R_a \geq 80$ for general interior work.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given Data:**
- $A = 10 \times 7 = 70 \text{ m}^2$
- $E = 500 \text{ lx}$, $UF = 0.65$, $MF = 0.85$
- $\Phi = 3600 \text{ lm}$ per LED panel

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$N = \frac{500 \times 70}{0.65 \times 0.85 \times 3600}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator calculation:**

$$0.65 \times 0.85 = 0.5525$$
$$0.5525 \times 3600 = 1989$$

**Step 4 — Compute N:**

$$N = \frac{35000}{1989} \approx 17.6 \Rightarrow 18 \text{ panels}$$

**Step 5 — Layout:** 6 rows × 3 columns = 18. Spacings: $10/6 = 1.67 \text{ m} \times 7/3 = 2.33 \text{ m}$.

**Step 6 — UGR check:** With recessed parabolic-louver LED panels, $L$ is significantly reduced, and the solid angle $\omega$ is small. The typical UGR for such an installation in a classroom is $\leq 19$ (per manufacturer data and IS 3646 Table 5). Therefore, the installation is **UGR compliant**.

**Final Answer:** **18 LED panels**, UGR $\leq 19$ (compliant).

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Maintenance Factor (MF):** Many students compute $N = E \cdot A / (UF \cdot \Phi)$ and forget $MF$. The maintained illuminance is **lower** than the initial illuminance; without MF, the design is **non-compliant within months** of operation. *Loss: 2–3 marks per question.*
> 2. **Confusing UGR (Discomfort) with Disability Glare:** UGR quantifies *annoyance*, not visibility loss. Examiners specifically look for the CIE 117 formula and the threshold UGR $\leq 19$ for offices. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Quoting CCT without application context:** Students memorize CCT ranges (e.g., "warm = 3000 K") but fail to **justify** the choice with the activity (residential, office, industrial). Always tie CCT to the task. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Wrong units in Inverse Square Law:** Writing $E = I/d^2$ without $\cos\theta$ for an inclined surface. The cosine correction is mandatory. *Loss: 2 marks in numerical problems.*
> 5. **Rounding DOWN the number of luminaires:** Always round **up**. If $N = 12.3$, the answer is **13**, not 12. *Loss: 1 mark.*

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The 7 Qualities:** Adequate Level, Uniformity, Glare Control, Colour Rendering, Shadow Control, Flicker Control, Luminaire Suitability.
- **Core Formula (most tested):** $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi \cdot n}$
- **Inverse Square Law:** $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$
- **Uniformity Ratio:** $U_0 = E_{min}/E_{avg} \geq 0.7$ (offices, classrooms).
- **UGR Threshold:** $\leq 19$ (offices), $\leq 22$ (industry, less critical), $\leq 16$ (drawing offices).
- **CRI Threshold:** $\geq 80$ (general), $\geq 90$ (colour-critical, surgical, art galleries).
- **CCT Selection:** Warm (2700–3300 K) for residential, Neutral (3300–5300 K) for offices, Cool (> 5300 K) for industrial.
- **MF = LLMF × LSF × RSMF** (typical product = 0.6–0.8).
- **SHR (Spacing-to-Height Ratio) ≤ 1.5** for most fluorescent/LED panel luminaires.
- **Standards to memorize:** IS 3646 (interior lighting), IS 6665 (industrial), IS 16103 (LED safety), NBC 2016 Part 8, CIE S 008 (CIE standards), IEEE 1789 (flicker).
- **Numerical type questions** are guaranteed: practice the $N = \dots$ formula with at least 5 variations.
- **Conceptual questions** often ask for the *justification* of each quality with a physiological basis (e.g., why flicker causes epilepsy).
- **IP Ratings** for luminaires: IP20 (indoor dry), IP44 (bathroom), IP65 (outdoor/wet), IP68 (submersible).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting the Lighting: Glare (Discomfort and Disability Glare)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Factors Affecting the Lighting: Glare (Discomfort and Disability Glare) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Glare — The Fundamental Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Glare** is defined as the visual condition in which a portion of the field of view is excessively bright in relation to the surrounding luminance, causing discomfort, annoyance, reduction in visibility, or a combination of these effects. (CIE International Lighting Vocabulary) In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, glare is classified as a **critical visual degradation factor** that directly impacts both the *visual comfort* and *visual performance* of an observer in any illuminated environment. > [!NOTE] > **CIE Reference Range for Safe Luminance Ratios (Office Task Areas):** > - Task luminance to adjacent surroundings: **1 : 1/3 to 3 : 1** > - Maximum task to remote surface ratio: **10 : 1** > - Maximum luminaire luminance: **≤ 2000 cd/m²** for angles above 45° from the downward vertical. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine driving on a **dark country road at night**. Suddenly, an oncoming vehicle with **high-beam headlights** flashes into your eyes. For a fraction of a second: - You feel a sharp **sensation of discomfort** (you instinctively squint or look away). - Simultaneously, a **veiling haze** appears across your retina, making the road markings nearly **invisible** — you cannot read the signs clearly. > This is **Glare** in its purest form: an *uncontrolled burst of brightness* that the human eye cannot gracefully adapt to. - The **squinting and instinctive eye-closure** you experience = **Discomfort Glare** (subjective annoyance). - The **blurring/haze over the road** that stops you from seeing the sign = **Disability Glare** (objective loss of visual acuity). In an office, a bare fluorescent tube visible at the edge of your vision produces discomfort glare. A polished screen reflecting a bright luminaire into your eye produces disability glare by scattering light inside the eyeball. --- ## 1.3 Broad Classification of Glare (KTU Syllabus Map) Glare is broadly divided into two physiologically distinct categories: | # | Type | Primary Effect | Measurement Focus | |---|------|----------------|-------------------| | 1 | **Discomfort Glare** | Annoyance, fatigue, instinctive aversion | Subjective (Rating scales) | | 2 | **Disability Glare** | Loss of visual acuity / visibility | Objective (Contrast reduction) | A third supporting concept is **Veiling Glare**, which is the optical mechanism by which disability glare is produced inside the eye. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** > Discomfort ≠ Disability. A glare source can be uncomfortable WITHOUT reducing vision (e.g., a faint LED indicator at night), and it can reduce vision WITHOUT being uncomfortable (e.g., uniform bright fog on a windshield). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Discomfort Glare — Theory of Origin Discomfort Glare is a **psychophysiological** response. It does **not** blur the retinal image directly. Instead, it triggers a stress response in the **autonomic nervous system** due to: 1. **Excessive Luminance of Source (L):** A source brighter than ~1000 cd/m² begins to irritate. 2. **Solid Angle of Source (ω):** Larger area of bright source = more discomfort. 3. **Position of Source in Field of View:** Sources near the line of sight (small angles) cause maximum discomfort. 4. **Background Luminance (L_b):** A dark background with one bright source increases discomfort drastically. 5. **Duration of Exposure:** Longer exposure compounds the discomfort exponentially. > [!NOTE] > **Guth's Position Index (p):** A numerical multiplier (1.0 to ∞) based on the angular displacement of the glare source from the line of sight. Smaller angle → Larger p → Higher glare. --- ## 2.2 Disability Glare — Theory of Origin Disability glare is a **purely optical/physical** phenomenon. It occurs when light from a bright source **scatters within the ocular media** (cornea, lens, vitreous humor, retina) of the eye. This scattered light forms a luminous "veil" superimposed on the retinal image, reducing the **contrast** of the object being viewed. Mathematically, the **Equivalent Veiling Luminance (L_eq)** is added to the actual background luminance, effectively washing out the image: $$L_{background}^{effective} = L_{background} + L_{eq}$$ This explains why, in disability glare, even if you can still sense a bright object, you **cannot distinguish its edges and details** because the contrast (L_object − L_background) has been compressed. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **most heavily tested** set of formulas for glare in KTU board exams. Memorize units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application | |---|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** | $$UGR = 8 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.25}{L_b}\sum_{s=1}^{N} \dfrac{L_s^{2}\,\omega_s}{p_s^{2}}\right)$$ | L_b = bg luminance (cd/m²)
L_s = source luminance (cd/m²)
ω_s = solid angle (sr)
p_s = Guth position index | Indoor office glare index | | 2 | **Equivalent Veiling Luminance (CIE Stiles–Holladay)** | $$L_{eq} = \dfrac{k \cdot E_{eye}}{\theta^{2}}$$ | E_eye = illuminance at eye (lx)
θ = angle from line of sight (deg)
k = constant ≈ 10 (for θ in degrees) | Disability glare quantification | | 3 | **Glare Index (British – older)** | $$GI = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.45}{L_b}\sum \dfrac{L_s^{1.6}\,\omega_s^{0.8}}{p_s^{1.6}}\right)$$ | All in SI units | Legacy UK method (still in viva questions) | | 4 | **Solid Angle subtended by luminaire** | $$\omega = \dfrac{A_{proj}}{d^{2}}$$ | A_proj = projected area (m²)
d = distance (m) | Computing ω for UGR | | 5 | **Contrast Reduction due to Veiling** | $$C' = \dfrac{L_{obj} - L_b - L_{eq}}{L_{obj} + L_b + L_{eq}}$$ | All luminances in cd/m² | Disability glare effect | | 6 | **Acceptable UGR Limits (CIE S 008)** | Office ≤ 19, Classrooms ≤ 16, Industrial fine ≤ 16, Industrial rough ≤ 25 | UGR is dimensionless | Design verification | > [!WARNING] > **UGR Pitfall:** UGR uses the **SQUARE** of L_s (L_s²), not L_s. This is because glare sensitivity grows faster than linear with brightness. The logarithmic compression (8 log₁₀) maps the raw index onto a perceptually uniform scale. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility | Domain | Use of Glare Analysis | |--------|----------------------| | **Indoor lighting design** | UGR calculation is mandatory in software like DIALux, AGi32, Relux to certify visual comfort. | | **Street lighting** | Threshold Increment (TI) method quantifies disability glare from oncoming headlights. | | **Sports lighting** | CIE 112 governs maximum permissible glare ratings for televised games. | | **Automotive headlamp design** | Cut-off lines and projector lamps minimize oncoming driver disability glare. | | **Architectural facades** | Curtain wall reflectance modelling prevents reflected disability glare. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Veiling Luminance (Stiles–Holladay Model) The CIE model for disability glare is built on the **assumption that a single point source** at angular position θ produces a luminous scatter veil at the fovea. **Step 1 — Geometric Setup** A point source of luminous intensity I (in candelas) is located at an angle θ (in degrees) from the observer's line of sight, at distance d (in meters). **Step 2 — Illuminance at the Eye** The illuminance E_eye produced at the observer's cornea is computed using the inverse-square law: $$E_{eye} = \dfrac{I \cos \alpha}{d^{2}}$$ where α is the angle of incidence to the eye. For a point source approximately in front, α ≈ 0, so: $$E_{eye} \approx \dfrac{I}{d^{2}} \quad [\text{lux}]$$ **Step 3 — Scattering Function** Empirical studies (Stiles 1929, Holladay 1926) showed that the **veiling effect decreases rapidly with increasing angular offset θ**. The data fit a relationship of the form: $$L_{eq} \propto \dfrac{E_{eye}}{\theta^{n}}$$ The CIE standardized exponent is **n = 2** for angles between 1° and 30°. **Step 4 — Introduction of Empirical Constant** Combining the proportionality and adding the empirical constant k (which has units deg²·cd/m²/lx) gives the CIE formula: $$L_{eq} = k \cdot \dfrac{E_{eye}}{\theta^{2}} \quad [\text{cd/m}^{2}]$$ with k ≈ 10 when θ is in degrees and E_eye in lux. **Step 5 — Final Working Equation** For a point source: $$L_{eq} = \dfrac{10 \cdot E_{eye}}{\theta^{2}} \quad [\text{cd/m}^{2}]$$ For an extended source, the equation is integrated over the source area. This is the equation used in **disability glare photometry** and forms the theoretical basis of the CIE 146:2002 disability glare model. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Disability Glare) > **Problem:** A street lamp of luminous intensity **2400 cd** is positioned at **8 m** from an observer and at an angle of **5°** above the line of sight. Find the equivalent veiling luminance at the eye. The background luminance is **2 cd/m²**. Does the effective contrast of a pedestrian wearing clothes of luminance **8 cd/m²** fall below the 0.4 threshold of detectability? **Given Data:** - I = 2400 cd - d = 8 m - θ = 5° - L_b = 2 cd/m² - L_obj = 8 cd/m² - C_min (threshold) = 0.4 **Step 1 — Compute Illuminance at the Eye** $$E_{eye} = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{2400}{8^{2}} = \dfrac{2400}{64} = 37.5 \text{ lx}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Equivalent Veiling Luminance** $$L_{eq} = \dfrac{10 \cdot E_{eye}}{\theta^{2}} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 37.5}{5^{2}} = \dfrac{375}{25} = 15 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Original Contrast (without glare)** $$C_{original} = \dfrac{L_{obj} - L_b}{L_{obj} + L_b} = \dfrac{8 - 2}{8 + 2} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.60$$ **Step 4 — Compute Effective Contrast with Veiling Glare** $$C_{effective} = \dfrac{L_{obj} - (L_b + L_{eq})}{L_{obj} + (L_b + L_{eq})} = \dfrac{8 - (2 + 15)}{8 + (2 + 15)} = \dfrac{8 - 17}{8 + 17} = \dfrac{-9}{25} = -0.36$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation** > [!IMPORTANT] > The effective contrast is **negative** (|−0.36|), meaning the pedestrian appears as a **darker silhouette against a brighter veiling field**. The **absolute value 0.36 < 0.40 (threshold)**, so the pedestrian is **NOT reliably detectable** under KTU's recommended contrast threshold of 0.4. > > This is precisely why disability glare is dangerous in night driving — the pedestrian becomes **invisible** despite being physically present. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Discomfort Glare vs Disability Glare > [!NOTE] > This comparison matrix is a **guaranteed KTU exam table** — students who reproduce it score full marks in "Compare" questions. | Parameter | Discomfort Glare | Disability Glare | |-----------|------------------|-------------------| | **Nature** | Subjective / Psychological | Objective / Optical | | **Primary Cause** | Excessive luminance contrast | Light scatter within ocular media | | **Effect on Vision** | Annoyance, fatigue (no image loss) | Reduced contrast, image obscured | | **Measurement** | UGR, Glare Index, rating scales | Equivalent Veiling Luminance (cd/m²) | | **Governing Formula** | UGR = 8 log₁₀(...) | L_eq = k·E_eye/θ² | | **Position Sensitivity** | High (Guth index p) | Moderate (∝ 1/θ²) | | **Cure** | Diffusers, louvers, indirect light | Anti-reflective coatings, baffles, headlamp leveling | | **Standards Body** | CIE S 008/E2001, IES RP-1 | CIE 146:2002, IESNA RP-30 | | **Example** | Bare fluorescent at the edge of vision | Oncoming high-beam in fog | | **Wavelength Dependence** | Strong (blue-rich LEDs are more discomforting) | Weak | | **Age Dependence** | Increases with age (crystalline lens yellowing) | Increases with age and cataracts | --- ## 3.4 Factors Affecting Glare (Comprehensive List) For the KTU board exam, the examiner expects a **systematic listing** of glare factors: 1. **Luminance of the light source** (L_s) — Higher L_s = Higher glare. 2. **Luminance of the background** (L_b) — Lower L_b increases contrast → Higher discomfort. 3. **Solid angle subtended by the source** (ω) — Larger source = More glare. 4. **Angular position of source from line of sight** (θ) — Closer to line of sight = Worse. 5. **Number of sources** in the field of view. 6. **Duration of exposure** to the source. 7. **Age of observer** — Older eyes scatter more light internally (worsens disability glare). 8. **Spectral content** — Cool-white (5000–6500 K) sources cause more glare. 9. **Reflectance of surrounding surfaces** — High-gloss desks add reflected glare. 10. **Mounting height & shielding of luminaire** — Determines whether the source is directly visible. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Glare Classification — Concept Map ```mermaid graph TD A[GLARE
Excessive Brightness in Field of View] A --> B[Discomfort Glare] A --> C[Disability Glare] A --> D[Veiling Glare
Optical Mechanism] B --> B1[Sensation of Annoyance] B --> B2[Visual Fatigue] B --> B3[Headaches and Stress] C --> C1[Loss of Visual Acuity] C --> C2[Reduced Contrast Sensitivity] C --> C3[Image Washout] D --> D1[Internal Eye Scattering] D --> D2[Equivalent Veiling Luminance] D --> D3[Causes Disability Effect] B2 --> E[Measured by UGR] C2 --> F[Measured by L_eq] D3 --> F E --> G[Corrective Measures] F --> G G --> G1[Diffusers and Louvres] G --> G2[Indirect Lighting] G --> G3[Anti Reflective Coatings] G --> G4[Proper Mounting Height] G --> G5[Spectrally Tuned Light Sources] ``` ## 4.2 UGR Calculation — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid graph LR In1[Identify Luminaires in
Field of View] --> P1[Compute Solid Angle ws] In2[Measure Source Luminance Ls] --> P2[Compute Guth Position Index p] In3[Measure Background Luminance Lb] --> P3[Sum of Ls squared times ws
divided by p squared] P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4[Apply UGR Formula
8 log base 10] In3 --> P4 P4 --> Out[UGR Score] Out --> Cmp{Compare with
CIE Limit} Cmp -->|Pass| OK[Design Approved] Cmp -->|Fail| Red[Revise Layout
Use Louvres] ``` ## 4.3 Disability Glare Optical Mechanism ```mermaid graph TD Src[Bright Light Source] -->|Direct Ray| Eye[Observer Eye] Src -->|Scattered Rays| Eye Eye --> Lens[Ocular Media
Cornea and Lens] Lens -->|Focused Image| Retina[Retina] Lens -->|Scattered Veil| Retina Retina --> Brain[Visual Cortex] Obj[Object being viewed] -->|Image L_obj| Retina Obj -->|Reduced Contrast| Brain Brain --> Perception[Observer Cannot See
Object Clearly] Perception --> Label[DISABILITY GLARE] ``` ## 4.4 Glare Reduction Strategy Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[Glare Mitigation] --> B[At Source] A --> C[At Path] A --> D[At Observer] B --> B1[Use Low Luminance Luminaires] B --> B2[Indirect Lighting] B --> B3[Spectrally Warm LEDs] C --> C1[Louvres and Baffles] C --> C2[Diffusers] C --> C3[Reflectors with Cut Off] D --> D1[Anti Reflective Spectacles] D --> D2[Adjustable Task Position] D --> D3[Increase Background Luminance] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Glare. Distinguish between Discomfort and Disability Glare.** *(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for distinction)* **Model Answer:** Glare is the visual condition in which a portion of the field of view is excessively bright in relation to the surrounding luminance, causing discomfort, reduction in visibility, or both. (CIE) | Aspect | Discomfort Glare | Disability Glare | |--------|------------------|------------------| | Nature | Subjective annoyance | Objective loss of visibility | | Cause | High luminance contrast | Intra-ocular light scatter | | Formula | UGR | Equivalent Veiling Luminance | > *Examiner expects: clear definition + 2-3 line difference.* --- ### **Q2. State and explain the CIE formula for Equivalent Veiling Luminance used in disability glare.** *(2 marks formula + 1 mark explanation)* **Model Answer:** $$L_{eq} = \dfrac{k \cdot E_{eye}}{\theta^{2}} \quad [\text{cd/m}^{2}]$$ where k ≈ 10, E_eye is the illuminance at the eye (lx), and θ is the angular displacement of the glare source from the line of sight (degrees). The formula quantifies the "veiling haze" superimposed on the retinal image due to light scattering inside the ocular media, which reduces image contrast. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a)** Explain the Unified Glare Rating (UGR) system in detail. Derive the UGR formula and discuss the significance of Guth's Position Index. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark)** The Unified Glare Rating (UGR) is a numerical measure developed by the CIE (S 008/E:2001) to evaluate discomfort glare in indoor installations. It replaces older systems such as the Visual Comfort Probability (VCP) and the British Glare Index. **Step 2 — Formula Derivation (4 marks)** The UGR formula is: $$UGR = 8 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.25}{L_b}\sum_{s=1}^{N} \dfrac{L_s^{2}\,\omega_s}{p_s^{2}}\right)$$ Derivation logic: - Each light source contributes glare proportional to its **luminance squared** (L_s²) because glare sensitivity is non-linear. - Contribution is proportional to the **solid angle** ω_s subtended at the eye (larger area = more glare). - Contribution is **inversely proportional to the square of the Guth Position Index** p_s, because a source near the line of sight (large p) produces more discomfort than one at the periphery. - The 0.25/L_b term normalises by background luminance — dark backgrounds amplify glare. - The factor 8 log₁₀ compresses the value into a perceptually uniform scale of 10–30. **Step 3 — Guth Position Index (2 marks)** Guth (1963) conducted experiments where observers rated discomfort from sources at various angles. He defined the position index p such that: - p = 1.0 for sources on the line of sight (most discomfort). - p increases rapidly as the source moves off-axis (e.g., p ≈ 1.5 at 5°, p ≈ 4.5 at 20°, p → ∞ above 60°). The squared dependence on p reflects the strong reduction of discomfort with peripheral placement. --- #### **(b)** An office has 8 luminaires, each with luminance **3500 cd/m²** at the eye, solid angle **0.012 sr**, and Guth position index **1.8**. The background luminance is **80 cd/m²**. Calculate the UGR and comment on whether the design complies with the CIE limit of 19 for offices. (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Substitute into UGR formula** $$\sum_{s=1}^{8} \dfrac{L_s^{2}\,\omega_s}{p_s^{2}} = 8 \times \dfrac{(3500)^{2}(0.012)}{(1.8)^{2}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute numerator** $$8 \times \dfrac{12{,}250{,}000 \times 0.012}{3.24} = 8 \times \dfrac{147{,}000}{3.24}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify** $$= 8 \times 45{,}370.37 = 362{,}962.96$$ **Step 4 — Compute the argument of log** $$\dfrac{0.25}{L_b} \times 362{,}962.96 = \dfrac{0.25}{80} \times 362{,}962.96$$ $$= 0.003125 \times 362{,}962.96 = 1134.26$$ **Step 5 — Compute UGR** $$UGR = 8 \log_{10}(1134.26) = 8 \times 3.0546 = 24.44$$ **Step 6 — Conclusion** > [!WARNING] > **UGR = 24.44 > 19 (CIE limit for offices).** The design **DOES NOT COMPLY** and must be revised. Recommendations: introduce diffusers, use luminaires with lower L_s, or raise the mounting height to increase p_s. (Examiner expects a clear pass/fail conclusion and a remedial suggestion.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* #### **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the mechanism of Disability Glare and derive the CIE Equivalent Veiling Luminance equation. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Diagram (1 mark)** A simple ASCII/mermaid diagram showing the glare source, the eye, the scatter veil on the retina, and the object being viewed. **Step 2 — Mechanism (3 marks)** Disability glare occurs because of light scattering within the ocular media. Light from a bright source enters the eye; while part of it is properly imaged on the retina, a portion is scattered by the cornea, lens, and vitreous humor. This scattered light forms a uniform "veil" of luminance L_eq that is superimposed on the retinal image, reducing the contrast between the object and the background. **Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks)** $$L_{eq} = \dfrac{k \cdot E_{eye}}{\theta^{2}}$$ The illuminance at the eye is E_eye = I/d² (from inverse square law). The veiling effect falls off as 1/θ², validated by Stiles–Holladay experiments. The constant k ≈ 10 when θ is in degrees and E_eye in lux. --- #### **(b)** A vehicle headlamp of intensity **50,000 cd** is located **50 m** from a driver at an angle of **3°** above the line of sight. Compute (i) the equivalent veiling luminance, (ii) whether the original contrast of 0.7 falls below 0.4 due to disability glare. Take background luminance = 1 cd/m². (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Illuminance at eye** $$E_{eye} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{50^{2}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{2500} = 20 \text{ lx}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent Veiling Luminance** $$L_{eq} = \dfrac{10 \times 20}{3^{2}} = \dfrac{200}{9} = 22.22 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Effective contrast** $$C_{eff} = \dfrac{C_{original}(L_{obj}+L_b) - L_{eq}}{L_{obj}+L_b+L_{eq}}$$ We need L_obj from original contrast: $$C_{original} = \dfrac{L_{obj}-L_b}{L_{obj}+L_b} = 0.7$$ Solving: L_obj = (0.7(1) + 0.7(0) + ...). Let ratio r = L_obj/L_b. Then (r − 1)/(r + 1) = 0.7 → r − 1 = 0.7r + 0.7 → 0.3r = 1.7 → r = 5.667. So L_obj = 5.667 cd/m². $$C_{eff} = \dfrac{(5.667 - 1) - 22.22}{(5.667 + 1) + 22.22} = \dfrac{4.667 - 22.22}{28.887} = \dfrac{-17.55}{28.887} = -0.608$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation** > [!IMPORTANT] > |C_eff| = 0.608 > 0.4 (threshold). The sign has reversed, meaning the object is now perceived as **darker than the veiling background** — strong disability glare has caused a **complete contrast reversal**, making the object unrecognisable in the field. This demonstrates why night-time driving with oncoming high beams is so dangerous. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Glare Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the square on L_s in UGR** — It is L_s², not L_s. Students lose 2 marks instantly. > 2. **Unit confusion in E_eye** — Many students use lumens instead of lux. The E_eye must be in **illuminance (lx)**, not intensity (cd). > 3. **Swapping k = 10 with k = 1** — k ≈ 10 only when θ is in degrees. If θ is in radians, k ≈ 0.0025. Always state the unit convention. > 4. **Mixing discomfort and disability glare formulas** — These are different. UGR is for discomfort; L_eq is for disability. Examiners deduct for cross-application. > 5. **Skipping the Conclusion** in numerical problems. KTU awards 1 full mark just for writing "Design does/does not comply" with a numerical comparison. > 6. **Insufficient explanation of Guth's Position Index** — A 1-line answer gets 0.5 marks; examiners expect the trend (small angle = large p) with a small table. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Glare (Discomfort & Disability):** - [x] **Glare** = excessive uncontrolled brightness in the field of view; governed by **CIE S 008** and **CIE 146**. - [x] **Discomfort Glare** = *subjective annoyance* without loss of vision → measured by **UGR**. - [x] **Disability Glare** = *objective loss of contrast* due to intra-ocular scatter → measured by **L_eq**. - [x] **UGR formula:** $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.25}{L_b}\sum \dfrac{L_s^{2}\omega_s}{p_s^{2}}\right)$ — remember **L_s²** and **8 log₁₀**. - [x] **L_eq formula:** $L_{eq} = 10 \cdot E_{eye} / \theta^{2}$ — remember k = 10 with θ in degrees. - [x] **CIE UGR limits:** Office ≤ 19, Classroom ≤ 16, Industrial fine ≤ 16, Industrial rough ≤ 25. - [x] **Guth Position Index p:** 1.0 on-axis, ∞ at periphery. Squared in denominator of UGR. - [x] **Solid angle ω = A_proj / d²** — required for UGR summation. - [x] **Effective contrast** with veiling glare: $C' = (L_{obj} - L_b - L_{eq})/(L_{obj} + L_b + L_{eq})$. - [x] **Glare factors (10):** L_s, L_b, ω, θ, number of sources, exposure duration, age, spectral content, reflectance, mounting. - [x] **Mitigation methods:** Diffusers, louvers, indirect lighting, anti-reflective coatings, proper mounting height, spectrally warm LEDs. - [x] **Veiling Glare** is the optical *mechanism* by which disability glare occurs; do NOT list it as a third separate type. - [x] **Stiles–Holladay (1926–1929)** empirical experiments established the 1/θ² dependence. - [x] **UGR scale is perceptually uniform**: UGR +3 = doubling of discomfort; UGR −3 = halving. - [x] **Always end numerical answers** with a compliance check and a recommendation.

topics.title: Visual Comfort Probability (VCP) and Unified Glare Rating (UGR) to Measure Glare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visual Comfort Probability (VCP) and Unified Glare Rating (UGR) to Measure Glare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Glare — The Prerequisite Concept

**Glare** is the sensation experienced by the human eye when **excess luminance** within the visual field causes discomfort, reduces visibility, or produces both effects simultaneously. Glare is broadly classified into:

- **Discomfort Glare** — Causes annoyance or pain without necessarily reducing visual performance (e.g., bright luminaire in peripheral vision).
- **Disability Glare** — Reduces visual performance (e.g., scattered light inside the eye reducing contrast).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> Discomfort glare is the dominant phenomenon in interior illumination design. Therefore, **VCP** and **UGR** are both **discomfort glare metrics** — they do NOT measure disability glare.

## 1.2 Visual Comfort Probability (VCP)

### Formal Definition
**Visual Comfort Probability (VCP)** is a numerical rating, expressed as a percentage, indicating the proportion of observers (in a standard observer population) who would judge a given lighting installation as **visually comfortable** under specific viewing conditions.

The metric was developed by the **Illuminating Engineering Society of North America (IESNA)** in **1966** based on the extensive laboratory investigations carried out by **S.K. Guth and J. Eastman** at the General Electric Company, Cleveland.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a classroom of **100 students** asked to rate a particular lighting condition in a room as "comfortable" or "uncomfortable". If **75 students** find it comfortable, the **VCP = 75**. It is essentially a **democratic comfort vote** conducted under standardized laboratory viewing.

### Physical Range of VCP
- **VCP = 100** → All observers are comfortable (ideal)
- **VCP = 70** → Minimum acceptable for general office work (IESNA)
- **VCP = 80** → Preferred for office work
- **VCP = 50** → Marginal
- **VCP = 0** → All observers are uncomfortable

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:**
> *"VCP gives a PERCENTAGE of HAPPY people."*

## 1.3 Unified Glare Rating (UGR)

### Formal Definition
**Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** is a numerical, **mathematically computed** glare metric adopted by the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE)** in **CIE 117:1995** to provide a single unified method for evaluating discomfort glare from artificial lighting installations in indoor workplaces.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine the lighting engineer placing a sensor (a mathematical model of the human eye) at a fixed observer position in the room. The sensor looks at **each bright luminaire**, registers its **brightness, apparent size, and position relative to the line of sight**, and then combines all these readings into a **single discomfort number**. The bigger the number, the worse the glare.

### Physical Range of UGR
Theoretical range: **UGR ∈ [10, 30]**, with practical limits:
- **UGR ≤ 16** → Technical drawing / precision work
- **UGR ≤ 19** → Offices, reading, classrooms (CIE standard for office work)
- **UGR ≤ 22** → Light industrial work
- **UGR ≤ 25** → Heavy industrial tasks
- **UGR ≤ 28** → Rough mechanical work, shipyards

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:**
> *"UGR gives a single GLARE NUMBER — the LOWER, the BETTER."*

## 1.4 Side-by-Side Comparison (KTU Quick Reference)

| Property | VCP | UGR |
|---|---|---|
| Developed by | IESNA (1966) | CIE (1995) |
| Origin country | North America | International |
| Type of metric | Empirical (observer study) | Analytical (math formula) |
| Output scale | 0 – 100 (%) | 10 – 30 (unitless) |
| Direction of comfort | Higher = Better | Lower = Better |
| Office standard | VCP ≥ 70 | UGR ≤ 19 |
| Based on | Glare Index (British) | Guth's position index |
| Calculation | Graphical / luminaire tables | Direct mathematical summation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework Behind VCP

The development of VCP is rooted in three sequential layers of theory:

### Step 1: Glare Index (GI) — The British Precursor
The British Glare Index (used in IES Code, 1961) was the first widely accepted discomfort glare formula:

$$
GI = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ \frac{K_1}{L_B} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L_i^{1.6} \cdot \omega_i^{0.8}}{p_i^{1.6}} \right]
$$

Where:
- $K_1$ = a constant (typically 0.478)
- $L_i$ = luminance of the i-th luminaire (in cd/m²)
- $L_B$ = background luminance (cd/m²)
- $\omega_i$ = solid angle subtended by the i-th luminaire at the eye (steradians)
- $p_i$ = Guth's position index for the i-th luminaire
- $N$ = total number of visible luminaires

### Step 2: Guth's Position Index $p$
The **position index $p$** is a dimensionless number introduced by **S.K. Guth (1954)** to quantify how disturbing a glare source is based on its position relative to the line of sight.

Empirically, $p$ depends on:
- The **angle $\theta$** between the line of sight and the direction of the glare source
- The **size and shape** of the source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key physical insight:** Glare sources near the line of sight (small $\theta$) have a **large $p$** and therefore cause **less discomfort** per unit luminance compared to sources in the peripheral field. The denominator $p^{1.6}$ in the GI formula makes peripheral luminaires far more disturbing.

### Step 3: Conversion to VCP
Guth and Eastman's observer study produced a **monotonically decreasing empirical curve** that maps GI to VCP. The simplified relationship is:

$$
\text{VCP} = 100 - \left( \frac{GI}{f} \right)^g \quad \text{(empirical curve fit)}
$$

A commonly used approximation for office luminaires (IESNA):

$$
\text{VCP} \approx 100 - 1.18 \cdot (GI)^{0.83} \quad \text{(typical curve fit)}
$$

The exact curve was published as a chart in the **IES Lighting Handbook** for different room geometries and luminaire types.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework Behind UGR

The CIE Unified Glare Rating directly evolved from the Glare Index by simplifying constants and exponents for international harmonization. The standard formula is:

$$
\boxed{\text{UGR} = 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ \frac{0.25}{L_B} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L_i^{2} \cdot \omega_i}{p_i^{2}} \right] }
$$

### Where:

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $L_i$ | Luminance of the i-th luminaire, in the direction of the observer's eye | cd/m² |
| $\omega_i$ | Solid angle subtended at the observer's eye by the i-th luminous element | sr (steradians) |
| $p_i$ | Guth's position index for the i-th source (function of viewing angle) | dimensionless |
| $L_B$ | Background luminance of the room (adaptive reference) | cd/m² |
| $N$ | Total number of luminous elements visible in the field of view | count |

### Why the exponents changed (GI → UGR)
- GI used $L^{1.6}$ and $\omega^{0.8}$.
- UGR standardized these to **$L^{2}$** and $\omega^{1.0}$ (i.e., $\omega / p^2$).
- The constant **0.25** replaces $K_1$ and provides normalization.
- The multiplier **8** sets the resulting numbers into the familiar 10–30 range.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:**
> Memorize the UGR formula structure. KTU often asks students to write down the UGR formula and identify the variables.

## 2.3 The Role of Guth's Position Index in Detail

For a typical rectangular luminaire, the position index $p$ is obtained from a table indexed by two parameters:

- $r$ = ratio of vertical offset of the luminaire to its size
- $d/h$ = ratio of distance of the observer to the luminaire's vertical height

Common empirical values (single-luminaire, Guth 1954):

| Vertical angle from line of sight ($\theta$) | Position index $p$ |
|---|---|
| 0° (directly ahead) | 1.00 |
| 5° | 1.10 |
| 10° | 1.20 |
| 20° | 1.50 |
| 30° | 2.00 |
| 40° | 2.60 |
| 50° | 3.40 |
| 60° | 4.60 |
| 70° | 6.00 |
| 80° | 9.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** $p$ becomes very large at large angles — meaning sources far from the line of sight are "less dangerous" per unit luminance, but their effective contribution also depends on $L$ and $\omega$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $GI = 10 \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{0.478}{L_B} \sum \dfrac{L_i^{1.6}\,\omega_i^{0.8}}{p_i^{1.6}}\right]$ | British predecessor of UGR |
| 2 | $\text{UGR} = 8 \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{0.25}{L_B} \sum \dfrac{L_i^{2}\,\omega_i}{p_i^{2}}\right]$ | **Standard international metric** |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{A}{r^{2}}$ (for a source of area $A$ at distance $r$) | Solid angle of small source |
| 4 | $p = f(\theta, d/h)$ from Guth's tables | Position index |
| 5 | VCP ≥ 70 | IESNA minimum for offices |
| 6 | UGR ≤ 19 | CIE standard for offices |
| 7 | UGR ≤ 16 | Precision / technical drawing |
| 8 | UGR ≤ 25 | Industrial work |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Glare Index (GI) to Unified Glare Rating (UGR)

The CIE adopted the following logical steps in standardizing glare evaluation:

### Step 1: Recognize the GI Form
The Glare Index (British) was originally:

$$
GI = 10 \log_{10}\left[ \frac{0.478}{L_B} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L_i^{1.6}\, \omega_i^{0.8}}{p_i^{1.6}} \right]
$$

### Step 2: Round the Exponents to Simpler Values
The fractional exponents 1.6 and 0.8 were chosen empirically. The CIE replaced them with **2 and 1**, since:
- $L^{2}$ is easier to compute (just square the luminance).
- $\omega^{1}$ avoids fractional powers of the solid angle.

$$
\text{Modified} = 10 \log_{10}\left[ \frac{C_1}{L_B} \cdot \sum \frac{L_i^{2}\, \omega_i}{p_i^{2}} \right]
$$

### Step 3: Adjust the Multiplier from 10 to 8
Switching exponents changes the numerical scale. CIE chose the multiplier **8** to keep the resulting numbers in a convenient 10–30 range typical of practical installations:

$$
\text{UGR} = 8 \log_{10}\left[ \frac{0.25}{L_B} \cdot \sum \frac{L_i^{2}\, \omega_i}{p_i^{2}} \right]
$$

### Step 4: Confirm the Constant 0.25
The constant **0.25** normalizes the formula so that a standard reference installation (with all $L_i = L_B$, $\omega_i = $ small, $p_i = 1$) yields **UGR = 0** (no discomfort). This is a built-in mathematical consistency check.

## 3.2 Worked Example: UGR Calculation (KTU Style Numerical)

### Problem Statement
An office room is observed by a worker whose eye level is at 1.2 m. The room has **two identical luminaires** whose parameters from the worker's viewpoint are:

| Luminaire | $L_i$ (cd/m²) | $\omega_i$ (sr) | $p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminaire 1 | 1500 | 0.012 | 1.5 |
| Luminaire 2 | 1500 | 0.008 | 2.0 |

The background luminance $L_B = 50$ cd/m². **Find the UGR.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A:** Compute the term $\dfrac{L_i^{2}\, \omega_i}{p_i^{2}}$ for each luminaire.

**For Luminaire 1:**

$$
\frac{L_1^{2}\, \omega_1}{p_1^{2}} = \frac{(1500)^{2} \times 0.012}{(1.5)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{2\,250\,000 \times 0.012}{2.25} = \frac{27\,000}{2.25} = 12\,000 \;\; \text{cd}^2/\text{m}^4 \cdot \text{sr}
$$

**For Luminaire 2:**

$$
\frac{L_2^{2}\, \omega_2}{p_2^{2}} = \frac{(1500)^{2} \times 0.008}{(2.0)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{2\,250\,000 \times 0.008}{4.0} = \frac{18\,000}{4.0} = 4\,500 \;\; \text{cd}^2/\text{m}^4 \cdot \text{sr}
$$

**Step B:** Sum the contributions.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{2} \frac{L_i^{2}\, \omega_i}{p_i^{2}} = 12\,000 + 4\,500 = 16\,500
$$

**Step C:** Compute the bracket.

$$
\frac{0.25}{L_B} \cdot \sum = \frac{0.25}{50} \times 16\,500 = 0.005 \times 16\,500 = 82.5
$$

**Step D:** Apply the UGR formula.

$$
\text{UGR} = 8 \cdot \log_{10}(82.5)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(82.5) = \log_{10}(8.25 \times 10^{1}) = 1 + \log_{10}(8.25)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(8.25) \approx 0.9165
$$

$$
\text{UGR} = 8 \times (1 + 0.9165) = 8 \times 1.9165 \approx 15.33
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{UGR} \approx 15.3}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since 15.3 ≤ 16, the installation is acceptable for **detailed / precision work** as well as standard office use. CIE limit is met with margin.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use VCP vs UGR

| Design Situation | Recommended Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. commercial building code | VCP | IESNA-based codes (e.g., ASHRAE 90.1) |
| International (EU, IS, ISO) project | UGR | CIE standard, globally accepted |
| Quick estimation in early design | UGR | Direct formula; no chart lookup |
| Final certified design with luminaire data | VCP or UGR | Both are accepted; UGR is computationally simpler |
| Residential / hospitality | UGR | CIE 117 covers it |
| Research publication | UGR | Reproducible, no proprietary charts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow — Glare Evaluation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define observer position in room] --> B[Identify all visible luminaires]
    B --> C[For each luminaire i]
    C --> D[Measure L_i cd per sq m]
    C --> E[Compute solid angle omega_i]
    C --> F[Determine Guth position index p_i]
    D --> G[Compute term L_i squared omega_i over p_i squared]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Summation over all N luminaires]
    H --> I[Measure background luminance L_B]
    I --> J[Plug into UGR formula]
    J --> K{UGR less than or equal to 19?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Acceptable for office work]
    K -->|No| M[Modify design: louvre, lower luminance, reposition]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Mapping — VCP and UGR Origins

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discomfort Glare Problem 1950s] --> B[Luckiesh and Guth Studies]
    B --> C[Guth Position Index 1954]
    C --> D[British Glare Index 1961]
    D --> E[IESNA VCP 1966]
    C --> F[CIE Study 1991 to 1995]
    F --> G[Unified Glare Rating UGR 1995]
    E --> H[North American Codes]
    G --> I[International CIE Standards]
    I --> J[ISO 8995 and EN 12464]
    H --> K[Modern Lighting Design]
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — VCP versus UGR Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observer in room] --> B{Choose metric}
    B -->|VCP| C[Refer to luminaire table in IES handbook]
    C --> D[Read standard VCP for that fixture layout]
    D --> E[Compare to IESNA criterion 70]
    B -->|UGR| F[Compute L_i, omega_i, p_i for each fixture]
    F --> G[Apply UGR formula]
    G --> H[Compare to CIE limit 19 for offices]
    E --> I[Design verdict]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 UGR Acceptance Thresholds Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UGR threshold] --> B[UGR le 16]
    A --> C[UGR le 19]
    A --> D[UGR le 22]
    A --> E[UGR le 25]
    A --> F[UGR le 28]
    B --> B1[Technical drawing and precision tasks]
    C --> C1[Offices, classrooms, reading]
    D --> D1[Light industrial work]
    E --> E1[Heavy industrial work]
    F --> F1[Rough mechanical work, shipyards]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Visual Comfort Probability (VCP). What is the IESNA criterion for office lighting?**

### Model Answer
**Visual Comfort Probability (VCP)** is a numerical rating, expressed as a percentage, that indicates the proportion of observers in a standard population who would judge a given lighting installation as visually comfortable. The IESNA criterion for office lighting is **VCP ≥ 70**, with **VCP ≥ 80** being preferred for office work.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Write the CIE Unified Glare Rating (UGR) formula and define all its terms.**

### Model Answer

$$
\text{UGR} = 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ \frac{0.25}{L_B} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L_i^{2}\, \omega_i}{p_i^{2}} \right]
$$

Where:
- $L_i$ = luminance of the i-th luminaire in the direction of the observer's eye (cd/m²)
- $\omega_i$ = solid angle of the i-th luminaire at the observer's eye (sr)
- $p_i$ = Guth's position index for the i-th source (dimensionless)
- $L_B$ = background luminance (cd/m²)
- $N$ = total number of visible luminaires

The CIE limit for office work is **UGR ≤ 19**.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Glare Index (GI)** and describe the role of **Guth's position index** in discomfort glare evaluation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A room contains **three** identical luminaires. For the worst-case observer position:

| Luminaire | $L_i$ (cd/m²) | $\omega_i$ (sr) | $p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1800 | 0.010 | 1.4 |
| 2 | 1800 | 0.015 | 1.8 |
| 3 | 1800 | 0.008 | 2.5 |

Background luminance $L_B = 60$ cd/m².

**(i)** Calculate the UGR of the installation. **(5 marks)**
**(ii)** Comment on whether the installation meets the CIE office standard. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Glare Index and Guth's Position Index (7 marks):**

The **Glare Index (GI)** was developed in the UK and adopted in the 1961 IES Code as a discomfort glare metric. Its general form is:

$$
GI = 10 \log_{10}\left[ \frac{0.478}{L_B} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L_i^{1.6}\, \omega_i^{0.8}}{p_i^{1.6}} \right]
$$

The **position index $p$**, introduced by S.K. Guth in 1954, is a dimensionless correction factor that accounts for the fact that a luminaire placed in the **peripheral field of view** is far more disturbing than one near the line of sight, even if the luminance and solid angle are the same. Empirically, $p$ is a function of:
- The angle $\theta$ between the line of sight and the direction to the luminaire
- The shape and apparent size of the source

Physically, $p$ represents the **angular displacement sensitivity** of the human eye. Sources directly ahead ($\theta \approx 0°$) have small $p$ (≈1) and therefore contribute more strongly to the discomfort summation; sources in the periphery have large $p$, which in the denominator reduces their contribution. The exponents 1.6 and 0.8 in the GI formula were chosen to match Guth's observer data.

**(b) UGR Calculation:**

**(i) Step A — Compute each term:**

For Luminaire 1:

$$
\frac{L_1^{2}\, \omega_1}{p_1^{2}} = \frac{(1800)^{2} \times 0.010}{(1.4)^{2}} = \frac{3\,240\,000 \times 0.010}{1.96} = \frac{32\,400}{1.96} \approx 16\,530.6
$$

For Luminaire 2:

$$
\frac{L_2^{2}\, \omega_2}{p_2^{2}} = \frac{(1800)^{2} \times 0.015}{(1.8)^{2}} = \frac{3\,240\,000 \times 0.015}{3.24} = \frac{48\,600}{3.24} = 15\,000
$$

For Luminaire 3:

$$
\frac{L_3^{2}\, \omega_3}{p_3^{2}} = \frac{(1800)^{2} \times 0.008}{(2.5)^{2}} = \frac{3\,240\,000 \times 0.008}{6.25} = \frac{25\,920}{6.25} \approx 4\,147.2
$$

**Step B — Sum:**

$$
\sum = 16\,530.6 + 15\,000 + 4\,147.2 \approx 35\,677.8
$$

**Step C — Compute bracket:**

$$
\frac{0.25}{L_B} \cdot \sum = \frac{0.25}{60} \times 35\,677.8 = 0.0041667 \times 35\,677.8 \approx 148.66
$$

**Step D — UGR:**

$$
\text{UGR} = 8 \cdot \log_{10}(148.66)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(148.66) \approx 2.1722
$$

$$
\text{UGR} = 8 \times 2.1722 \approx 17.38
$$

**(ii) Interpretation:** Since **UGR = 17.4 ≤ 19**, the installation **meets the CIE standard for office work**. It also satisfies UGR ≤ 16 marginally misses, so it is good for general office use but not for high-precision technical drawing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **VCP and UGR** in terms of origin, methodology, output, and typical acceptance limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reading room has a single-row linear luminaire producing an effective glare source of luminance $L = 2000$ cd/m², solid angle $\omega = 0.025$ sr, and position index $p = 1.6$. The background luminance is $L_B = 40$ cd/m².

**(i)** Calculate the UGR. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** If the luminaire is replaced with a low-glare louvre design that reduces its luminance to 1000 cd/m² and increases $p$ to 2.4 (with the same $\omega$ and $L_B$), what is the new UGR? **(3 marks)**
**(iii)** Comment on the improvement. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Aspect | VCP | UGR |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | IESNA, North America (1966) | CIE, International (1995) |
| Methodology | Empirical — based on Guth-Eastman observer study | Analytical — direct mathematical formula |
| Output | Percentage (0–100) | Dimensionless number (10–30) |
| Direction | Higher = better | Lower = better |
| Office limit | ≥ 70 (IESNA) | ≤ 19 (CIE) |
| Calculation method | Refer to luminaire table / chart | Direct summation in formula |
| Adaptability | Limited to standard luminaires | Any installation can be evaluated |
| Global use | North American projects | International standard (ISO 8995, EN 12464) |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Initial UGR:**

$$
\text{UGR}_1 = 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ \frac{0.25}{40} \times \frac{(2000)^{2} \times 0.025}{(1.6)^{2}} \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 0.00625 \times \frac{4\,000\,000 \times 0.025}{2.56} \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 0.00625 \times \frac{100\,000}{2.56} \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 0.00625 \times 39\,062.5 \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 244.14 \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \times 2.3876 \approx 19.10
$$

**(ii) New UGR with louvre:**

$$
\text{UGR}_2 = 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ \frac{0.25}{40} \times \frac{(1000)^{2} \times 0.025}{(2.4)^{2}} \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 0.00625 \times \frac{1\,000\,000 \times 0.025}{5.76} \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 0.00625 \times \frac{25\,000}{5.76} \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 0.00625 \times 4\,340.28 \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ 27.13 \right]
$$

$$
= 8 \times 1.4335 \approx 11.47
$$

**(iii) Comment:** The louvre reduces UGR from **19.1 → 11.5**, a drop of **7.6 units**, which is a substantial improvement. The installation now comfortably meets the UGR ≤ 16 criterion for precision work.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Squaring $L_i$** — Students frequently forget to square the luminance. UGR uses $L_i^{2}$, NOT $L_i^{1.6}$ (that is the older GI formula). Forgetting the square costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Solid angle in steradians** — $\omega$ is in sr, not degrees. Do not confuse with the visual angle in degrees.
> 3. **Logarithm base 10** — $\log_{10}$, not natural log $\ln$.
> 4. **Direction interpretation** — VCP HIGH = GOOD, UGR LOW = GOOD. Reversing this is a common 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Numerical accuracy** — Carry at least 4 significant figures in intermediate steps; KTU awards 1 mark for correctly arriving at the final UGR.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VCP** (IESNA, 1966): A **percentage** of observers finding lighting comfortable. Higher = better. **Office limit ≥ 70**.
- **UGR** (CIE, 1995): A **dimensionless** glare rating from a mathematical formula. **Lower = better**. **Office limit ≤ 19**.
- **UGR Formula:** $\text{UGR} = 8 \log_{10}\left[ \dfrac{0.25}{L_B} \sum \dfrac{L_i^{2}\, \omega_i}{p_i^{2}} \right]$
- **Key variables:** $L_i$ (cd/m²), $\omega_i$ (sr), $p_i$ (Guth's position index), $L_B$ (cd/m²), $N$ (number of visible luminaires).
- **Guth's Position Index $p$:** Increases with angular distance from the line of sight; reduces effective contribution of peripheral sources.
- **UGR standard thresholds:** 16 (precision), 19 (office), 22 (light industry), 25 (heavy industry), 28 (rough work).
- **VCP is empirical, UGR is analytical** — for KTU numerical problems, always default to UGR.
- **Constant 0.25** in UGR normalizes the formula so that a reference case (no glare) gives UGR = 0.
- **Multiplier 8** keeps the practical UGR range in the 10–30 band.
- For KTU 2024 Scheme ESE: Memorize the UGR formula, the role of $p$, and the CIE thresholds.
- **Anti-pattern:** Do not write $\log$ without specifying base 10. KTU strict valuation may deduct 0.5 marks.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: VCP and UGR**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shadow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Shadow # Module 1 — Shadow ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In illumination engineering, a **shadow** is defined as a region of reduced or zero illuminance formed on a surface when an **opaque object** obstructs the path of light rays travelling from one or more luminous sources to that surface. The shadow is the geometric projection of the obstructing body onto the receiving plane, governed by the **rectilinear propagation of light** principle. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A shadow is the region of geometrical darkness produced by the interception of light rays from a luminous source by an opaque body. The shape, size, and intensity of the shadow depend upon the position, size, and luminance of the source relative to the obstructing object." The **intensity of the shadow** is characterised by the ratio of the illuminance at the shadowed point to the illuminance that would have existed in the absence of the obstruction.
