This ratio is termed the **Shadow Factor (SF)**. ### 1.2 Components of a Shadow A shadow is not a single uniform region; it contains two distinct sub-regions: 1. **Umbra** — The inner, fully-dark core of the shadow where the light source is *completely* blocked by the opaque object. No direct rays from the source reach this region. 2. **Penumbra** — The outer, partially-lit fringe surrounding the umbra where the light source is *partially* blocked. Some portion of the luminous area is visible from a point in the penumbra. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Umbra exists only when the light source is *extended* (i.e., not a point source).** A true point source produces only a sharp umbra with no penumbra. Conversely, a sufficiently large diffuse source can produce a penumbra without any umbra (e.g., the shadows on a cloudy day are mostly penumbra). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lamp-Post at Dusk" Imagine you are walking along a road at night, and there is a tall lamp-post (point source) casting light in all directions. A pedestrian walking in front of the lamp-post obstructs the light. The dark patch formed on the ground directly behind the pedestrian is the **umbra** — a sharp, well-defined silhouette matching the pedestrian's outline. Now imagine the moon (an extended source) is partially covered by the Earth during a solar eclipse. The darkest part of the shadow cone is the **umbra** (where the sun is *totally* eclipsed), while the lighter, gradient edge is the **penumbra** (where the sun is *partially* eclipsed). The same geometric principle governs shadows in any illumination setup. > [!TIP] > **Memorise this:** Umbra = *U*nseen (totally dark); Penumbra = *P*artial (semi-dark). The prefix *pen-* in Latin means "almost," so *penumbra* literally means "almost-shadow." ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The key photometric quantity used to characterise a shadow is the **Shadow Factor (SF)**, a dimensionless ratio ranging from **0** (total darkness) to **1** (no shadow at all): $$SF = \frac{E_{shadowed}}{E_{unshadowed}}$$ where $E_{shadowed}$ is the illuminance at the point of interest when the obstruction is present, and $E_{unshadowed}$ is the illuminance at the same point without the obstruction. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference:** In KTU 2024 scheme evaluations, examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between *Umbra*, *Penumbra*, and *Shadow Factor* as three separate definitional elements. Conflating these terms in a definition question is a guaranteed mark-deduction. ### 1.5 Classification of Light Sources by Shadow Behaviour | Source Type | Shadow Produced | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | True point source | Sharp umbra, no penumbra | Searchlights, pinhole projection | | Small extended source | Small umbra + wide penumbra | Household incandescent lamp | | Large extended source | No umbra, only penumbra (soft shadow) | Diffuse ceiling panel, overcast sky | | Multiple sources | Multiple overlapping shadows | Stage lighting, display windows | | Coherent source (laser) | Sharp umbra, diffraction fringes | Holography, optical experiments | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometric Construction of a Shadow The shadow of an opaque object is a **conical (or cylindrical) projection** of the object's silhouette in the direction of the light source. The exact geometry depends on: 1. The **shape and size** of the luminous source. 2. The **shape and size** of the opaque object. 3. The **relative distances** between source, object, and receiving surface. ### 2.2 Umbra Geometry — Point Source Case For a **point source** $S$ and a spherical (or arbitrarily shaped) opaque object $O$ of radius $r$ placed at distance $d$ from $S$, the umbra is a cone extending behind the object. The half-angle $\alpha$ of the umbra cone is determined by the line connecting the source to the **limb** of the object. For a **circular disc-shaped** source of radius $R_s$ and an opaque disc of radius $R_o$ aligned coaxially at distance $L$ apart, the geometry produces: - **Umbra region** of radius $R_u$ at a distance $x$ beyond the object: $$R_u = R_o - (R_o - R_s) \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$ - **Penumbra outer boundary** of radius $R_p$ at the same distance $x$: $$R_p = R_o + (R_o + R_s) \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$ The umbra exists only for $R_s < R_o$ (smaller source than object), which is the common case for localised sources like bulbs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When the source is larger than the object ($R_s > R_o$), the umbra vanishes entirely and only penumbra exists. This is the *geometric* explanation for why overcast daylight produces virtually no sharp shadows. ## 2.3 Shadow Factor — Derivation of the General Expression Consider two point sources $S_1$ and $S_2$ illuminating a point $P$ on a working plane. An opaque obstruction $O$ blocks the light from $S_1$ but not from $S_2$. The shadow factor at $P$ is: $$SF = \frac{E_{S_2}}{E_{S_1} + E_{S_2}}$$ If **n** sources are present and **k** of them are blocked at point $P$: $$SF = \frac{\sum_{i \in \text{unblocked}} E_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}$$ > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility:** The shadow factor is critical in **interior lighting design** for hospitals, operating theatres, machine shops, and reading rooms where excessive contrast from harsh shadows causes visual fatigue. A higher SF (closer to 1) means softer, more uniform lighting — desirable for most working environments. ## 2.4 Soft Shadows and Multiple Sources When **n** identical point sources are symmetrically placed around an opaque object, the shadow region on the receiving plane receives no light from the source directly behind the object. However, the surrounding sources contribute partial illumination. The shadow factor at the centre of the geometric shadow is: $$SF_{\text{center}} = \frac{(n-1) \cdot E_0}{n \cdot E_0} = \frac{n-1}{n}$$ where $E_0$ is the illuminance contributed by a single unobstructed source at the same point. | Number of Sources (n) | SF at Shadow Centre | Visual Effect | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.000 | Pitch-black, hard shadow | | 2 | 0.500 | Dark grey shadow | | 3 | 0.667 | Medium shadow | | 4 | 0.750 | Soft shadow | | 6 | 0.833 | Very soft | | ∞ | 1.000 | No perceptible shadow | ## 2.5 Shadows in Polar Curves and Photometric Data Illumination engineers often use the **polar curve** of a luminaire to predict shadow behaviour. The shadow cast by a luminaire is the geometric shadow of the luminaire's own housing as seen from the receiving plane. Downward-facing luminaires (direct lighting) cast minimal self-shadow, while upward-facing indirect luminaires cast significant self-shadow on the ceiling. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $SF = \dfrac{E_{shadowed}}{E_{unshadowed}}$ | Shadow Factor (general) | dimensionless | | 2 | $SF = \dfrac{n-1}{n}$ | SF at centre for n identical symmetric sources | dimensionless | | 3 | $R_u = R_o - (R_o - R_s) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Umbra radius at distance x | metres (m) | | 4 | $R_p = R_o + (R_o + R_s) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Penumbra outer radius at distance x | metres (m) | | 5 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$ | Inverse Square + Cosine Law (illuminance at shadow plane) | lux (lx) | | 6 | $L_u = L \cdot \dfrac{R_o}{R_o - R_s}$ | Length of umbra cone (from object) | metres (m) | | 7 | $E_{total} = \sum E_i$ | Superposition principle for multiple sources | lux (lx) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical separators in absolute-value or set-membership expressions have been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve the markdown table integrity. The same convention must be used in your exam answer scripts. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of Shadow Analysis 1. **Architectural Lighting Design:** Predicting cast shadows of beams, columns, and furniture to plan supplementary accent lighting. 2. **Road and Tunnel Lighting:** Avoiding dangerous contrast patches that may obscure obstacles. 3. **Stage and Theatrical Lighting:** Deliberate *creation* of shadows for dramatic effect (chiaroscuro). 4. **Security and Surveillance Lighting:** Eliminating shadowed "blind spots" around CCTV cameras. 5. **Solar Energy Engineering:** Predicting shadow patterns from buildings/trees to size PV arrays correctly. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Umbra Length for a Point Source and a Spherical Opaque Object **Setup:** A point source $S$ of negligible size is located at distance $L$ from a spherical opaque object of radius $R_o$. We wish to find the length of the umbra cone behind the object. **Step 1 — Identify the tangent rays.** The umbra boundary is defined by the rays from $S$ that are *tangent* to the sphere. These tangent rays just graze the sphere at its limb and then diverge to form the umbra cone. **Step 2 — Construct the right triangle.** From source $S$, draw a line to the centre $C$ of the sphere (length $L$). From the same source, draw a tangent ray to the limb of the sphere. The tangent point $T$, the centre $C$, and the source $S$ form a right-angled triangle because the radius $CT$ is perpendicular to the tangent ray $ST$ at the point of tangency. **Step 3 — Apply Pythagoras' theorem.** $$\begin{aligned} ST^2 &= SC^2 - CT^2 \\ ST^2 &= L^2 - R_o^2 \\ ST &= \sqrt{L^2 - R_o^2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Determine the umbra cone half-angle $\alpha$.** $$\begin{aligned} \sin \alpha &= \frac{CT}{SC} = \frac{R_o}{L} \\ \alpha &= \arcsin\left(\frac{R_o}{L}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the umbra cone length $L_u$.** The umbra cone terminates where the source would need to have zero angular size (i.e., where the tangent rays converge if extended backwards toward the source). For a *point* source, the umbra extends to infinity. For an *extended* source of radius $R_s$, the umbra has a finite length. **Step 6 — Extended source case: finite umbra length.** The umbra length $L_u$ measured from the sphere is found by similar triangles. The cone narrows from radius $R_o$ at the sphere to radius $0$ at the umbra tip. The equivalent cone from the source side has radius $R_s$ at distance $L$ and radius $0$ at the umbra tip. $$\begin{aligned} \frac{R_o}{L} &= \frac{R_s}{L + L_u} \\ R_o (L + L_u) &= R_s \cdot L \\ L_u &= \frac{R_s \cdot L}{R_o} - L \\ L_u &= L \cdot \frac{R_s - R_o}{R_o} \\ L_u &= L \cdot \left(\frac{R_s}{R_o} - 1\right) \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** If $R_s < R_o$ (small source, large object — the common case), then $L_u < 0$ in the literal sense, which physically means the umbra cone diverges and the shadow has no finite tip — it extends to infinity. This matches everyday experience: the shadow of your hand held in front of a table lamp extends across the entire wall. ## 3.2 Derivation: Shadow Factor for Two Identical Point Sources **Setup:** Two point sources $S_1$ and $S_2$ of equal luminous intensity $I$ are placed symmetrically at distance $d$ on either side of a vertical opaque rod. The receiving plane is at horizontal distance $D$ from the rod. Find the shadow factor at a point $P$ on the receiving plane directly behind the rod. **Step 1 — Express illuminance from each source without obstruction.** For a point directly below the rod on the receiving plane, both sources are at oblique distance $r$: $$r = \sqrt{d^2 + D^2}$$ The illuminance contribution from each source (by inverse square law): $$E_0 = \frac{I}{r^2} = \frac{I}{d^2 + D^2}$$ **Step 2 — Total unshadowed illuminance.** $$E_{unshadowed} = E_1 + E_2 = 2 \cdot \frac{I}{d^2 + D^2}$$ **Step 3 — Illuminance at the shadowed point.** The rod blocks $S_1$ completely at point $P$, leaving only $S_2$ contributing: $$E_{shadowed} = \frac{I}{d^2 + D^2}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the shadow factor.** $$\begin{aligned} SF &= \frac{E_{shadowed}}{E_{unshadowed}} \\ SF &= \frac{\dfrac{I}{d^2 + D^2}}{2 \cdot \dfrac{I}{d^2 + D^2}} \\ SF &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** For *n* identical symmetrically placed sources with a single opaque object blocking exactly one source's view, the shadow factor at the centre of the shadow is always $\dfrac{n-1}{n}$. This is a *direct result of the symmetry* and does not depend on the distances or intensities. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns | Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | |---|---|---|---| | Single source, opaque disc | Source radius $R_s$, object radius $R_o$, distance $L$ | Umbra radius at distance $x$ | $R_u = R_o - (R_o - R_s)\dfrac{x}{L}$ | | Multiple symmetric sources | Number of sources $n$ | SF at shadow centre | $SF = \dfrac{n-1}{n}$ | | Two-source asymmetric | $I_1, I_2, d_1, d_2, D$ | SF at specific point | $SF = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$ | | Umbra length | Source radius, object radius, separation | Length of umbra cone | $L_u = L \left(\dfrac{R_s}{R_o} - 1\right)$ | | Penumbra width | All radii and distances | Penumbra ring width | $R_p - R_u$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shadow Formation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Luminous Source] --> B{Ray Emission Type} B -- Point Source --> C[Sharp Umbra Only] B -- Extended Source --> D[Umbra + Penumbra] B -- Multiple Sources --> E[Overlapping Shadow Regions] C --> F[Shadow Factor SF 0] D --> G[SF Varies 0 to 1] E --> H[SF n minus 1 divided by n] F --> I[Hard Shadow Visual Effect] G --> J[Soft Gradient Shadow] H --> K[Multi Source Softened Shadow] I --> L[Application Theatrical Lighting] J --> M[Application Interior Office Lighting] K --> N[Application Stadium Sports Lighting] L --> O[Design Decision High Contrast Dramatic] M --> P[Design Decision Uniform Visual Comfort] N --> Q[Design Decision Elimination of Pitch Dark Zones] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shadow Region Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Source_Side Src[Luminous Source S] end Src -->|Tangent ray 1| Opq[Opaque Object O] Src -->|Tangent ray 2| Opq Opq -->|Tangent ray 1| Umbra[Umbra Region

e.g. 4 Lamp Cluster] Q3 --> Q4{Required SF value?} Q4 -- SF greater than 0.8 --> R3[Use 6 or more sources] Q4 -- SF 0.5 to 0.8 --> R4[Use 2 to 4 sources] R1 --> End[Final Layout] R2 --> End R3 --> End R4 --> End ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above render the *functional flow* of shadow formation and the *design-decision logic* for shadow control in illumination engineering, as physical ray-tracing schematics are not natively renderable in Mermaid. For board examinations, supplement these block diagrams with a hand-drawn ray diagram showing tangent rays from the source to the limb of the object and the resulting umbra and penumbra cones. ## 4.4 Hand-Drawn Schematic (ASCII Description for Notes) For a typical KTU exam answer, the expected ray diagram is: ``` S (Point Source) | | ← Tangent ray | \ ___|___\___ / | \ \ ← Opaque sphere of radius Ro | | | | \___|____/___/ | / | / ← Tangent ray |/ * ← Tip of umbra (at infinity for point source) | | ← Penumbra spreads | _______\|/_______ ← Receiving plane | Umbra |Penum| |_______|__fra|__ ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Define the terms *Umbra* and *Penumbra* as applied to illumination engineering. With a neat sketch, explain how the size of the light source affects the relative size of these two shadow regions.** **Model Answer:** **Umbra:** The umbra is the inner core region of a shadow where the luminous source is *completely* obstructed by the opaque object. No direct light from the source reaches any point within the umbra. The illuminance in the umbra is theoretically zero (in the ideal case of a single source). **Penumbra:** The penumbra is the outer, partially-shaded region of a shadow where the luminous source is *partially* blocked. From any point in the penumbra, only a fraction of the luminous area of the source is visible. The illuminance in the penumbra is non-zero but less than the unshadowed illuminance. **Effect of source size:** - **Point source** → Umbra exists, penumbra is negligible (zero in the ideal case). The shadow has a sharp, well-defined edge. - **Small extended source** → Both umbra and penumbra exist. The penumbra forms a soft fringe around the umbra. - **Large extended source** (larger than the object) → Umbra disappears entirely, leaving only penumbra. The shadow is very soft and diffuse. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently state that the penumbra is "less dark" without specifying that the penumbra exists *because* the source has finite angular extent. If you do not mention the *finite size* of the source, the answer is incomplete and loses 1 mark. --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Define Shadow Factor. A working plane is illuminated by four identical lamps placed symmetrically around a vertical opaque pillar. Find the shadow factor at a point on the working plane directly behind the pillar.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Shadow Factor (SF) is the ratio of the illuminance at a point on a surface when a particular light source is obstructed, to the illuminance at the same point when no obstruction is present. $$SF = \frac{E_{shadowed}}{E_{unshadowed}}$$ SF is a dimensionless quantity, with values ranging from 0 (total darkness) to 1 (no shadow). **Numerical Solution:** For *n* = 4 identical symmetric sources, the obstruction blocks exactly one source's view of the point directly behind the pillar. $$\begin{aligned} SF &= \frac{n - 1}{n} \\ SF &= \frac{4 - 1}{4} \\ SF &= \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \end{aligned}$$ The shadow factor at the specified point is **0.75 (dimensionless)**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common mistake is writing $SF = \frac{1}{4}$ by inverting the ratio. Always remember: *shadowed* illuminance is in the *numerator* (it is the smaller quantity), and *unshadowed* (total) illuminance is in the *denominator*. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based) ### Question A (Choice 1) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain with a neat diagram the formation of umbra and penumbra during a solar eclipse. Discuss how the relative sizes of the Sun, Earth, and Moon determine whether a total, annular, or partial eclipse occurs.** **(b)** [7 Marks] **A circular luminous source of radius 5 cm is placed 50 cm from an opaque disc of radius 10 cm. Calculate (i) the length of the umbra cone, and (ii) the radius of the umbra at a distance of 30 cm behind the opaque disc.** #### Model Solution to (a) A **solar eclipse** occurs when the Moon passes between the Sun and the Earth, obstructing sunlight from reaching a portion of the Earth's surface. The shadow cast by the Moon is divided into: 1. **Umbra** — the cone of complete shadow. Observers within the umbra experience a **total solar eclipse** (the Sun is fully obscured). 2. **Penumbra** — the diverging cone of partial shadow surrounding the umbra. Observers within the penumbra experience a **partial solar eclipse** (only a portion of the Sun is obscured). 3. **Antumbra** — the region beyond the tip of the umbra cone where the Moon appears smaller than the Sun, producing an **annular eclipse** (a ring of sunlight remains visible around the Moon). **Type of eclipse determined by observer's position:** | Observer Position | Eclipse Type | Visual Appearance | |---|---|---| | Inside umbra cone | Total eclipse | Sun completely blocked | | At umbra tip (boundary) | Hybrid/Edge of totality | Brief totality | | In antumbra (beyond umbra tip) | Annular eclipse | Bright ring around Moon | | In penumbra | Partial eclipse | Crescent-shaped Sun | **Eclipse type based on geometry:** - **Total eclipse** — Umbra cone reaches the Earth's surface. This occurs when the Moon is *closer* to the Earth (near perigee) and the Moon's apparent angular size is *larger* than the Sun's. - **Annular eclipse** — Umbra cone falls *short* of the Earth's surface. The Moon is farther from Earth (near apogee) and its apparent angular size is *smaller* than the Sun's. - **Partial eclipse** — Observer is in the penumbra but outside the umbra. **Diagram expectation:** A neat diagram showing the Sun (large sphere on the left), Moon (small sphere in the middle), Earth (sphere on the right), with umbra cone terminating on the Earth's surface (total eclipse case), and penumbra cone diverging outward. #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** - Source radius: $R_s = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Opaque disc radius: $R_o = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - Distance between source and object: $L = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$ - Distance behind the object to the point of interest: $x = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$ **Part (i): Length of the umbra cone** $$\begin{aligned} L_u &= L \cdot \left(\frac{R_s}{R_o} - 1\right) \\ L_u &= 50 \cdot \left(\frac{5}{10} - 1\right) \\ L_u &= 50 \cdot (0.5 - 1) \\ L_u &= 50 \cdot (-0.5) \\ L_u &= -25 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** The negative value of $L_u$ indicates that the umbra cone *diverges* (does not converge to a tip). Since the source is smaller than the opaque object, the umbra extends to infinity — there is no finite umbra tip. Physically, the shadow on any receiving plane will have a non-zero umbra region. **Part (ii): Radius of the umbra at x = 30 cm behind the object** $$\begin{aligned} R_u &= R_o - (R_o - R_s) \cdot \frac{x}{L} \\ R_u &= 10 - (10 - 5) \cdot \frac{30}{50} \\ R_u &= 10 - 5 \cdot 0.6 \\ R_u &= 10 - 3 \\ R_u &= 7 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$ **The umbra radius at 30 cm behind the opaque disc is 7 cm.** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget the *sign convention* and report a negative umbra length as a positive quantity with a magnitude of 25 cm, losing 1 mark. The correct interpretation is that the umbra *diverges* (no finite length). Always state the physical interpretation explicitly. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** [7 Marks] **Derive the expression for the shadow factor at a point on a working plane illuminated by *n* identical point sources placed symmetrically around a vertical opaque obstruction.** **(b)** [7 Marks] **Two point sources of luminous intensities 200 cd and 400 cd are placed at heights of 3 m and 4 m respectively, both directly above a point P on a horizontal working plane. The horizontal distance between the two sources is 2 m. An opaque object placed between the sources and the point P completely blocks the contribution of the 200 cd source. Calculate the shadow factor at point P.** #### Model Solution to (a) **Setup:** Consider *n* identical point sources, each of luminous intensity $I$, placed symmetrically around a vertical opaque rod at equal horizontal distance $d$ from the rod. A receiving plane lies at horizontal distance $D$ from the rod. We wish to determine the shadow factor at a point $P$ on the plane directly behind the rod. **Step 1 — Illuminance from one unobstructed source at P.** The oblique distance from each source to P is: $$r = \sqrt{d^2 + D^2}$$ By the inverse square law, the illuminance from a single source is: $$E_0 = \frac{I}{r^2} = \frac{I}{d^2 + D^2}$$ **Step 2 — Total illuminance without obstruction.** By the principle of superposition, with *n* sources all contributing: $$E_{unshadowed} = n \cdot E_0 = \frac{n \cdot I}{d^2 + D^2}$$ **Step 3 — Illuminance with obstruction.** The rod blocks exactly one source's view of P (by the symmetry of the arrangement). The remaining $(n-1)$ sources illuminate P unobstructed: $$E_{shadowed} = (n - 1) \cdot E_0 = \frac{(n-1) \cdot I}{d^2 + D^2}$$ **Step 4 — Shadow Factor calculation.** $$\begin{aligned} SF &= \frac{E_{shadowed}}{E_{unshadowed}} \\ SF &= \frac{\dfrac{(n-1) \cdot I}{d^2 + D^2}}{\dfrac{n \cdot I}{d^2 + D^2}} \\ SF &= \frac{n - 1}{n} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** The shadow factor at point P is $\dfrac{n-1}{n}$, which depends *only* on the number of sources and is *independent* of the distances and individual intensities (as long as the sources are identical and symmetrically placed). **Verification for special cases:** - *n* = 1: $SF = 0$ (pitch-black shadow — only one source, fully blocked). - *n* = 2: $SF = 0.5$. - *n* → ∞: $SF$ → 1 (no perceptible shadow — infinite source array). #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** - Source 1: $I_1 = 200 \text{ cd}$, height $h_1 = 3 \text{ m}$ - Source 2: $I_2 = 400 \text{ cd}$, height $h_2 = 4 \text{ m}$ - Horizontal separation between sources: $\Delta x = 2 \text{ m}$ - Working plane is horizontal, point P is directly below the midpoint (by default). - Source 1 (200 cd) is blocked. **Step 1 — Geometry setup.** Place P at the origin. Source 1 is at horizontal position $x_1 = -1$ m, vertical height $h_1 = 3$ m. Source 2 is at horizontal position $x_2 = +1$ m, vertical height $h_2 = 4$ m. **Step 2 — Distances from each source to P.** $$\begin{aligned} d_1 &= \sqrt{(-1)^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{1 + 9} = \sqrt{10} \text{ m} \\ d_2 &= \sqrt{(+1)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 16} = \sqrt{17} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Illuminance from each source (assuming normal incidence, $\cos \theta = \cos 0 = 1$ for a horizontal plane illuminated by a vertical source).** For a source at height $h$ directly above P (or in general), the illuminance on the horizontal plane is: $$E = \frac{I \cos^3 \theta}{h^2}$$ where $\theta$ is the angle from the vertical. For our geometry, $\cos \theta_i = h_i / d_i$. **For Source 1:** $$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta_1 &= \frac{3}{\sqrt{10}} \\ \cos^3 \theta_1 &= \frac{27}{10\sqrt{10}} \\ E_1 &= \frac{200 \cdot 27}{10 \cdot 10\sqrt{10}} = \frac{5400}{100\sqrt{10}} = \frac{54}{\sqrt{10}} \text{ lux} \end{aligned}$$ **For Source 2:** $$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta_2 &= \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}} \\ \cos^3 \theta_2 &= \frac{64}{17\sqrt{17}} \\ E_2 &= \frac{400 \cdot 64}{17 \cdot 17\sqrt{17}} = \frac{25600}{289\sqrt{17}} \text{ lux} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Numerically evaluate (for shadow factor ratio, we can keep symbolic).** The unshadowed illuminance is $E_1 + E_2$, and the shadowed illuminance is $E_2$ (since Source 1 is blocked): $$SF = \frac{E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $E_1$ numerically.** $$E_1 = \frac{54}{\sqrt{10}} = \frac{54}{3.1623} = 17.08 \text{ lux}$$ **Step 6 — Compute $E_2$ numerically.** $$E_2 = \frac{25600}{289 \cdot \sqrt{17}} = \frac{25600}{289 \cdot 4.1231} = \frac{25600}{1191.58} = 21.49 \text{ lux}$$ **Step 7 — Compute the shadow factor.** $$\begin{aligned} SF &= \frac{21.49}{17.08 + 21.49} \\ SF &= \frac{21.49}{38.57} \\ SF &= 0.557 \end{aligned}$$ **The shadow factor at point P is approximately 0.557 (dimensionless).** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** The most common error in this problem is forgetting the $\cos^3 \theta$ factor when the source is *not* directly above P (i.e., when the source is at an oblique angle). Always identify whether the source is directly overhead or at an angle, and apply the cosine correction accordingly. A second common error is using the inverse square law alone (without cosine) for a horizontal working plane. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Shadow** ### Definition Layer - **Shadow** is the geometric projection of an opaque object onto a receiving surface, formed by the rectilinear propagation of light. - **Umbra** = region of *complete* obstruction (zero direct illuminance). - **Penumbra** = region of *partial* obstruction (reduced direct illuminance). - **Shadow Factor** = $SF = \dfrac{E_{shadowed}}{E_{unshadowed}}$, dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$. ### Geometry Layer - **Point source** → sharp umbra, no penumbra. - **Extended source smaller than object** → umbra + penumbra, umbra cone diverges to infinity. - **Extended source larger than object** → penumbra only, no umbra. - Umbra length: $L_u = L \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_s}{R_o} - 1\right)$ (negative value means diverging cone). - Umbra radius at distance $x$: $R_u = R_o - (R_o - R_s) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$. - Penumbra outer radius at distance $x$: $R_p = R_o + (R_o + R_s) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$. ### Multi-Source Layer - *n* identical symmetric sources, one blocked: $SF = \dfrac{n-1}{n}$. - This expression is *independent* of distance, intensity, and individual geometry. - More sources → softer shadow → higher SF → better visual uniformity. ### Application Layer - **Acceptable shadows** — theatrical lighting, accent lighting, art galleries. - **Undesirable shadows** — operating theatres, reading rooms, machine shops, road lighting. - **Shadow mitigation strategies** — diffuse sources, indirect lighting, multiple symmetric sources, large-area luminaires. ### KTU Exam Pointers - Always define *all three* terms (umbra, penumbra, shadow factor) explicitly in definition questions. - Always include a neat ray diagram in shadow geometry questions. - Always interpret the *sign* of umbra length calculations. - Always state units (or note dimensionless) for shadow factor. - Numericals typically test either the *n-1/n formula* or the *umbra geometry* formulas — practice both. > [!TIP] > **One-Line Memory Aid:** "**U**mbra = **U**nseen core, **P**enumbra = **P**artial ring, **SF** = what remains ÷ what should be." ---

topics.title: Colour Rendering and Colour Rendering Index (CRI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Colour Rendering and Colour Rendering Index (CRI)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Colour Rendering

**Colour Rendering** is formally defined (per the **CIE 13.3:1995** standard and reaffirmed in **CIE 177:2007**) as the *effect of an illuminant on the perceived colour of objects by comparison with their appearance under a reference illuminant of the same Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT)*.

In simpler terms: it is the ability of a light source to **reproduce the true colours** of illuminated objects faithfully, as compared to how those objects would appear under a natural or ideal reference light source (like daylight or a blackbody radiator).

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Colour Rendering Index (CRI)

The **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)**, denoted as **Ra**, is a quantitative metric (a single numerical value, usually on a **0 to 100** scale) that describes how accurately a light source renders the colours of objects compared to a natural reference light source of the same CCT. A higher Ra value indicates better colour fidelity.

$$\text{Ra} = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Perfect colour rendering (no colour shift)}$$

$$\text{Ra} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Worst possible colour rendering (all colours appear identical to reference)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The CIE standard test uses **8 pastel test colour samples (TCS01–TCS08)** for the general CRI (Ra), with **6 additional saturated samples (TCS09–TCS14)** for special CRI evaluations (Ri).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Artist's Studio Light"

Imagine you are an artist trying to match the exact shade of fabric in your studio. If you work under a **fluorescent tube light** that has strong green spikes in its spectrum, the red fabric may look brownish or muddy — your colour matching will be wrong. But if you work under a **halogen bulb** with a continuous spectrum (CRI ≈ 100), the red fabric looks genuinely red, and you can match it perfectly.

> **Real-world translation:** When you buy clothes in a shop lit by a cheap LED (low CRI), the same shirt may look different in sunlight (high CRI) when you step outside. That mismatch is a direct consequence of poor colour rendering.

### 1.4 Why CRI Matters in Illumination Engineering

- **Retail stores, art galleries, hospitals, photography studios, textile industry, and printing presses** all require high CRI (typically **Ra ≥ 90**).
- **Street lighting and warehouse lighting** can tolerate moderate CRI (Ra 60–80) since colour accuracy is not critical.
- **Safety-critical applications** (surgical lights, forensic labs) mandate **Ra ≥ 95** to detect subtle colour cues in tissue or evidence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Exams:** CCT (warm/cool appearance of light) and CRI (colour accuracy of objects) are **independent parameters**. A light source can have CCT = 3000 K (warm) and still have CRI = 90, or CCT = 6500 K (cool) with CRI = 60. Always treat these as two separate specifications.

### 1.5 Quick Reference Standards and Bodies

| Standard / Body | Full Form | Role in CRI |
|---|---|---|
| **CIE 13.3** | International Commission on Illumination, 1995 | Original CRI calculation procedure |
| **CIE 177** | CIE, 2007 | Reaffirmation and clarification of CRI |
| **CRI (Ra)** | General Colour Rendering Index | Average of R1–R8 |
| **IES TM-30** | Illuminating Engineering Society, 2015 | Modern improved metric (Rf, Rg) |
| **TM-30 Rf** | Fidelity Index | Modern alternative to CRI |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: How is CRI Measured?

The measurement of CRI is a **comparative, multi-step procedure** that must be understood stepwise for KTU examination answers. Below is the structured logic flow:

#### Step 1 — Test Source & Reference Source Selection
- A **test illuminant** (the lamp being evaluated) is spectrally characterized.
- A **reference illuminant** is chosen:
  - For **CCT < 5000 K**: Planckian (blackbody) radiator of the same CCT.
  - For **CCT ≥ 5000 K**: CIE standard daylight illuminant (D-series) of the same CCT.

#### Step 2 — Illumination of 8 (or 14) Test Colour Samples (TCS)
- The **CIE 13.3** defines **TCS01–TCS08** as the standard pastel set. Each sample is a **Munsell colour** with specific spectral reflectance.
- Each TCS is illuminated by both the **test source** and the **reference source**, and its reflected colour is computed in **CIE 1960 UCS** chromaticity space (u, v).

#### Step 3 — Chromatic Adaptation Correction (Von Kries Transform)
- Since the two illuminants have different chromaticities, the eye/brain performs a **chromatic adaptation**.
- The Von Kries transform adjusts the tristimulus values to compensate:
$$X_a = X \cdot \frac{X_{nw}}{X_{nref}}, \quad Y_a = Y, \quad Z_a = Z \cdot \frac{Z_{nw}}{Z_{nref}}$$
  where subscript `nw` is the test source white and `nref` is the reference white.

#### Step 4 — Compute Colour Difference (ΔEi)
- After chromatic adaptation, the Euclidean distance between the test sample chromaticity `(ut, vt)` and the reference sample chromaticity `(ur, vr)` in the **CIE 1960 UCS** diagram is computed:
$$\Delta E_i = \sqrt{(u_{t,i} - u_{r,i})^2 + (v_{t,i} - v_{r,i})^2}$$
- An **adaptive shift factor** of 0.5 is applied to desaturate large shifts (a legacy of the original 1965 CIE procedure).

#### Step 5 — Convert ΔEi to Special CRI (Ri)
- The colour difference is mapped to a 0–100 scale:
$$R_i = 100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E_i$$
- The constant **4.6** is calibrated so that a CCT-matched blackbody reference (ideal case) gives **Ri = 100**.

#### Step 6 — General CRI (Ra)
- The general CRI is the **arithmetic mean** of R1 through R8:
$$R_a = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} R_i$$
- Special CRIs (R9–R14) for **saturated red, yellow, green, blue, skin, and leaf green** are computed separately but **not averaged into Ra**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1:** A direct comparison only makes sense if both sources have the **same colour temperature** — otherwise the eye's adaptation alone would account for the difference.
- **Step 2:** Eight samples are statistically enough to capture average colour shifts without making the procedure unwieldy.
- **Step 3:** Without adaptation correction, a warm source would always score poorly against a cool reference even if both rendered colours equally well post-adaptation.
- **Step 4:** CIE 1960 UCS is used (instead of CIE 1976 u'v') because the CRI procedure was frozen at this older standard for historical comparability.
- **Step 5:** Linear scaling gives a familiar 0–100 score; a ΔE of about 21.7 yields Ri = 0.
- **Step 6:** Averaging hides bad performance on individual samples — this is a known **limitation of CRI** that TM-30 tries to fix.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{8} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{8} R_i$ | General CRI (average of R1–R8) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_i = 100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E_i$ | Special CRI for each sample | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\Delta E_i = \sqrt{(u_{t,i} - u_{r,i})^2 + (v_{t,i} - v_{r,i})^2}$ | Colour difference in CIE 1960 UCS | dimensionless |
| 4 | $u = \dfrac{4X}{X + 15Y + 3Z}, \quad v = \dfrac{6Y}{X + 15Y + 3Z}$ | CIE 1960 UCS chromaticity coordinates | dimensionless |
| 5 | $X_a = X \cdot \dfrac{X_{nw}}{X_{nref}}$ | Von Kries chromatic adaptation (X) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Y_a = Y, \quad Z_a = Z \cdot \dfrac{Z_{nw}}{Z_{nref}}$ | Von Kries adaptation (Y, Z) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta E_i = 0 \Rightarrow R_i = 100$ | Perfect match condition | — |
| 8 | $\Delta E_i \approx 21.74 \Rightarrow R_i = 0$ | Worst match condition | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize formula (1), (2), and (3). They appear in almost every Part B question on this topic. Use the **CIE 1960 UCS** space (not CIE 1976 or CIE 1931 xy) for ΔEi calculation — this is a common trap.

### 2.4 CRI Categories (IES/Industry Practice)

| CRI Range | Quality | Typical Sources |
|---|---|---|
| **Ra ≥ 90** | Excellent | Halogen, premium LED, ceramic metal halide |
| **Ra = 80–89** | Good | Standard LED, fluorescent triphosphor |
| **Ra = 60–79** | Fair | Older fluorescent, low-quality LED |
| **Ra < 60** | Poor | High-pressure sodium (HPS), low-pressure sodium (LPS) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Museum lighting:** Accurately reproducing the colour of paintings and artefacts requires Ra ≥ 95.
- **Medical/surgical lighting:** Detecting cyanosis, jaundice, and tissue colour requires Ra ≥ 90.
- **Textile and paint industry:** Colour matching to a reference sample under controlled lighting (D65 standard, Ra = 100).
- **Residential interior design:** Warm-tone LEDs with Ra ≥ 80 are mandated under **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) India 2017** for habitable spaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Tristimulus Values to CIE 1960 UCS Coordinates

The CIE 1931 colour-matching functions are **X(λ), Y(λ), Z(λ)**. For any reflected light from a TCS under an illuminant with spectral power distribution **S(λ)** and surface reflectance **ρ(λ)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, \rho(\lambda)\, \overline{x}(\lambda)\, d\lambda \\[4pt]
Y &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, \rho(\lambda)\, \overline{y}(\lambda)\, d\lambda \\[4pt]
Z &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, \rho(\lambda)\, \overline{z}(\lambda)\, d\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ is a normalizing constant so that **Y = 100** for a perfect white diffuser.

> **Conversion logic:** The CIE 1960 UCS chromaticity coordinates are obtained by a linear projection of XYZ into a 2D plane, designed so that equal geometric distances on the (u, v) diagram approximate equal perceived colour differences (MacAdam ellipses are more circular here than in xy).

$$
u = \frac{4X}{X + 15Y + 3Z}, \qquad v = \frac{6Y}{X + 15Y + 3Z}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The denominator $X + 15Y + 3Z$ is a weighted sum chosen empirically so that the (u, v) space is approximately perceptually uniform — that is, equal Δu, Δv correspond to roughly equal perceptual colour steps.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Computing CRI for a Hypothetical LED

**Given:**
- Test illuminant: Hypothetical white LED with **CCT = 4000 K**
- Reference illuminant: Planckian radiator at 4000 K (since CCT < 5000 K)
- TCS01 (light reddish orange) under test source: $(u_t, v_t) = (0.2530, 0.3270)$
- TCS01 under reference source: $(u_r, v_r) = (0.2500, 0.3260)$

**Find:** $R_1$ and its contribution to $R_a$.

**Step 1 — Apply Von Kries adaptation:**
We assume the test and reference whites are sufficiently close that the adaptation factor is unity (or already applied in the given chromaticities). No further correction needed.

**Step 2 — Compute colour difference ΔE₁:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_1 &= \sqrt{(u_t - u_r)^2 + (v_t - v_r)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{(0.2530 - 0.2500)^2 + (0.3270 - 0.3260)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{(0.0030)^2 + (0.0010)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{9.0 \times 10^{-6} + 1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
&= 3.1623 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
&= 0.003162
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** We have computed the Euclidean distance in (u, v) space — this is the fundamental unit of perceived colour shift that drives the entire CRI system.

**Step 3 — Convert ΔE₁ to special CRI R₁:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E_1 \\[4pt]
&= 100 - 4.6 \times 0.003162 \\[4pt]
&= 100 - 0.01455 \\[4pt]
&= 99.985 \approx 99.99
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor 4.6 is the slope of the linear mapping chosen so that the reference source (ΔE = 0) always gives Ri = 100 and a theoretical "worst case" ΔE ≈ 21.74 gives Ri = 0. For this sample, the LED is essentially perfect.

**Step 4 — General CRI (Ra) — full calculation assuming all 8 samples are equal:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_a &= \frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} R_i = \frac{8 \times 99.99}{8} = 99.99
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Ra is the simple arithmetic mean — no weighting — of the eight special CRIs. The simplicity is intentional so that practising engineers can quote a single number to a customer.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Common Sources

| Light Source | Typical CCT (K) | Typical Ra (CRI) | Sample R9 (saturated red) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Incandescent (Tungsten)** | 2700 | **100** | ~95 | Continuous spectrum, gold standard |
| **Halogen (Tungsten-Halogen)** | 3000 | **100** | ~95 | Continuous spectrum |
| **Daylight (D65 reference)** | 6500 | **100** | ~95 | Used as reference for CCT ≥ 5000 K |
| **Ceramic Metal Halide** | 4200 | **90–96** | 60–85 | Excellent for retail and studios |
| **Triphosphor Fluorescent (T8/T5)** | 3000–6500 | **80–89** | 30–70 | Common in offices |
| **Standard White LED (Pc-LED)** | 2700–6500 | **70–85** | 0–50 | R9 (red) often very poor |
| **High-Pressure Sodium (HPS)** | 2000 | **20–25** | -50 to 0 | Used in street lighting only |
| **Low-Pressure Sodium (LPS)** | 1800 | **-44 to 0** | -100 | Monochromatic 589 nm; no colour rendering |
| **Phosphor-Converted Amber LED** | 1800–2200 | **40–60** | 5–20 | Used in turtle-friendly outdoor lighting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation from the matrix:** Sources with **line spectra** (HPS, LPS) score very low because they cannot excite all the cone cells in the human retina (L, M, S). Sources with **continuous spectra** (incandescent, halogen, daylight) score near 100. Modern **phosphor-converted LEDs** strike a balance by combining a blue pump LED with a yellow (and sometimes red) phosphor.

### 3.4 Limitations of CRI (KTU Expected Discussion Points)

1. **Averaging hides individual sample failures** — A lamp can have Ra = 80 but still render saturated red (R9) terribly.
2. **Only 8 (or 14) samples** are used; many real-world object colours are not represented.
3. **CRI is unreliable for CCT < 2000 K and CCT > 25000 K**.
4. **CRI cannot distinguish between sources that enhance or suppress saturation** — it is biased toward "fidelity" only.
5. **The use of CIE 1960 UCS** (an outdated space) introduces inaccuracies that **TM-30 Rf** has corrected using CIE 1976.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — CRI Calculation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the full procedural flow for computing the general Colour Rendering Index Ra as defined in CIE 13.3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start CRI Evaluation] --> B[Measure Spectral Power Distribution of Test Lamp S_lambda]
    B --> C[Determine CCT of Test Lamp]
    C --> D{CCT less than 5000 K?}
    D -- Yes --> E1[Use Planckian Blackbody Reference at same CCT]
    D -- No --> E2[Use CIE Daylight D Series Reference at same CCT]
    E1 --> F[Compute XYZ tristimulus values for all 8 TCS under BOTH sources]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Convert XYZ to CIE 1960 UCS coordinates u and v]
    G --> H[Apply Von Kries Chromatic Adaptation Transform]
    H --> I[Compute Colour Difference Delta E_i for each TCS using Euclidean Distance in uv space]
    I --> J[Compute Special CRI R_i as 100 minus 4.6 times Delta E_i]
    J --> K[Repeat for all 8 samples R1 to R8]
    K --> L[Compute General CRI R_a as Arithmetic Mean of R1 to R8]
    L --> M[Optional: Compute Special CRI R9 to R14 for Saturated Colours]
    M --> N[Output R_a value on 0 to 100 Scale]
    N --> O[End CRI Evaluation]
```

> **Figure description:** The flow begins with the spectral measurement of the test source, branches to choose the proper reference based on CCT (Planckian or Daylight), then proceeds through tristimulus integration, chromatic adaptation, colour difference computation, and finally the averaging step to yield Ra.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison: CCT vs CRI (Independent Parameters)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lamp Specification] --> B[CCT: Correlated Colour Temperature]
    A --> C[CRI: Colour Rendering Index Ra]
    B --> D[Describes Warm vs Cool Appearance of Light Source Itself]
    C --> E[Describes Accuracy of Object Colours under the Source]
    D --> F[Independent Axes]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Lamp can be: 2700K Warm AND Ra 90 High Quality]
    F --> H[Lamp can be: 6500K Cool AND Ra 60 Poor Quality]
```

> **Figure description:** This diagram reinforces the KTU-critical concept that **CCT** and **CRI** are orthogonal specifications. A warm lamp is not necessarily low-CRI, and a cool lamp is not necessarily high-CRI.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — CRI Quality Bands and Application Suitability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Ra] --> B{Ra greater than or equal 90}
    B -- Yes --> C1[Excellent Quality Band]
    C1 --> D1[Use Cases: Museums, Hospitals, Surgical Theatres, Art Galleries, High End Retail]
    B -- No --> C2{Ra between 80 and 89}
    C2 -- Yes --> D2[Good Quality Band]
    D2 --> E2[Use Cases: Offices, Schools, Residential Interiors, General Commercial]
    C2 -- No --> C3{Ra between 60 and 79}
    C3 -- Yes --> D3[Fair Quality Band]
    D3 --> E3[Use Cases: Warehouses, Parking Lots, Industrial Task Lighting]
    C3 -- No --> F[Poor Quality Band Ra less than 60]
    F --> G[Use Cases: Street Lighting with HPS or LPS, Decorative Only]
```

> **Figure description:** Maps each CRI band to a typical engineering application. This matrix is a high-yield visual aid for KTU Part A questions asking where a particular Ra is appropriate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the term Colour Rendering Index (CRI). What is the range of values it can take, and what do the extreme values signify?**

**Model Answer:**

The Colour Rendering Index (CRI), denoted as Ra, is a quantitative measure of the ability of a light source to faithfully reproduce the colours of illuminated objects as compared to a reference illuminant of the same Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT). It is computed per the **CIE 13.3:1995** standard using 8 standard test colour samples (TCS01–TCS08) and is the arithmetic mean of the special CRIs (R1–R8).

- **Range:** $0 \leq R_a \leq 100$
- **Ra = 100:** Perfect colour rendering; the test source renders all 8 TCS identically to the reference source.
- **Ra = 0:** Worst possible colour rendering; strong colour shift between test and reference sources.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for range, 1 for extreme value significance)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**State the formula for computing the general CRI (Ra). How is the special CRI Ri calculated from the colour difference ΔEi?**

**Model Answer:**

The **general Colour Rendering Index** is the arithmetic mean of the special CRIs of the first 8 CIE test colour samples:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} R_i$$

The **special CRI** for each TCS is obtained from the colour difference ΔEi in the CIE 1960 UCS chromaticity diagram using the linear relation:

$$R_i = 100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E_i$$

where ΔEi is computed as the Euclidean distance in the (u, v) plane between the test-sample chromaticity and the reference-sample chromaticity after Von Kries chromatic adaptation.

**(3 marks — 1.5 marks each formula)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for computing the Colour Rendering Index (Ra) of a light source as per CIE 13.3. Include a clear statement of the reference illuminant selection rule and the role of the Von Kries chromatic adaptation transform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hypothetical LED source has CCT = 3500 K. The following (u, v) chromaticity values are obtained for the 8 test colour samples (after Von Kries adaptation) under the test LED and a Planckian reference at 3500 K:

| TCS | Test (u_t, v_t) | Reference (u_r, v_r) |
|---|---|---|
| TCS01 | 0.2520, 0.3260 | 0.2500, 0.3260 |
| TCS02 | 0.2580, 0.3290 | 0.2600, 0.3300 |
| TCS03 | 0.2400, 0.3200 | 0.2410, 0.3200 |
| TCS04 | 0.2700, 0.3320 | 0.2700, 0.3330 |
| TCS05 | 0.2650, 0.3250 | 0.2640, 0.3260 |
| TCS06 | 0.2750, 0.3280 | 0.2740, 0.3280 |
| TCS07 | 0.2450, 0.3220 | 0.2460, 0.3220 |
| TCS08 | 0.2620, 0.3270 | 0.2610, 0.3280 |

Calculate the general Colour Rendering Index Ra of the LED. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — CRI Procedure (7 marks)**

1. **Spectral measurement (1 mark):** The spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$ of the test lamp is measured using a spectroradiometer across 380–780 nm.

2. **Reference selection (1.5 marks):** The CCT of the test lamp is first determined. If CCT < 5000 K, a Planckian (blackbody) radiator of the same CCT is the reference. If CCT ≥ 5000 K, a CIE standard daylight illuminant (D-series) of the same CCT is used. This ensures a fair comparison on equal colour-temperature footing.

3. **Tristimulus integration (1.5 marks):** For each of the 8 standard test colour samples (TCS01–TCS08), the reflected-light tristimulus values X, Y, Z are computed under both the test and reference sources using the standard colour-matching functions.

4. **Chromatic adaptation (1.5 marks):** Because the two sources have different chromaticities, the Von Kries transform is applied to the test-source values, scaling X and Z so that the adapted white matches the reference white. This simulates the eye's natural adaptation.

5. **Colour difference (1 mark):** The CIE 1960 UCS coordinates (u, v) are computed, and ΔEi is calculated as the Euclidean distance in (u, v) space.

6. **Special and General CRI (1 mark):** Each Ri = 100 − 4.6 ΔEi, and Ra is the average of R1 through R8.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Using the formula $\Delta E_i = \sqrt{(u_{t,i} - u_{r,i})^2 + (v_{t,i} - v_{r,i})^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_1 &= \sqrt{(0.0020)^2 + (0)^2} = 0.00200 \\
\Delta E_2 &= \sqrt{(-0.0020)^2 + (-0.0010)^2} = \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.00224 \\
\Delta E_3 &= \sqrt{(-0.0010)^2 + (0)^2} = 0.00100 \\
\Delta E_4 &= \sqrt{(0)^2 + (-0.0010)^2} = 0.00100 \\
\Delta E_5 &= \sqrt{(0.0010)^2 + (-0.0010)^2} = 0.00141 \\
\Delta E_6 &= \sqrt{(0.0010)^2 + (0)^2 = 0.00100} \\
\Delta E_7 &= \sqrt{(-0.0010)^2 + (0)^2} = 0.00100 \\
\Delta E_8 &= \sqrt{(0.0010)^2 + (-0.0010)^2} = 0.00141
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing the special CRIs, $R_i = 100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00200) = 100 - 0.00920 = 99.99 \\
R_2 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00224) = 100 - 0.01030 = 99.99 \\
R_3 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00100) = 100 - 0.00460 = 99.995 \\
R_4 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00100) = 100 - 0.00460 = 99.995 \\
R_5 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00141) = 100 - 0.00649 = 99.994 \\
R_6 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00100) = 100 - 0.00460 = 99.995 \\
R_7 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00100) = 100 - 0.00460 = 99.995 \\
R_8 &= 100 - 4.6(0.00141) = 100 - 0.00649 = 99.994
\end{aligned}
$$

Now the general CRI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_a &= \frac{R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5 + R_6 + R_7 + R_8}{8} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{99.99 + 99.99 + 99.995 + 99.995 + 99.994 + 99.995 + 99.995 + 99.994}{8} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{799.948}{8} \\[4pt]
&= 99.99
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The LED has an excellent general CRI of **Ra ≈ 99.99**, indicating near-perfect colour rendering for these 8 samples.

**(Full marks: 7 — show all 8 ΔE calculations, all 8 Ri, and the averaging step explicitly.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT)** and **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)**. Why is it wrong to assume that a high-CCT lamp necessarily has a high CRI? Give two real-world examples illustrating this independence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the **8 standard CIE test colour samples (TCS01–TCS08)** and their typical Munsell hue families. Explain why a single-number CRI (Ra) is **insufficient** for evaluating modern LED sources, citing at least two specific limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — CCT vs CRI (7 marks)**

| Aspect | CCT (Correlated Colour Temperature) | CRI (Colour Rendering Index) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Temperature of a Planckian radiator whose perceived colour most closely matches that of the light source | Measure of how accurately the source renders object colours versus a reference of the same CCT |
| **Unit** | Kelvin (K) | Dimensionless (0–100) |
| **What it describes** | The colour appearance of the light source *itself* (warm to cool) | The colour fidelity of *objects* under the source |
| **Standard** | CIE 15:2004 | CIE 13.3:1995 |
| **Range** | ~1000 K to 25000 K+ | 0 to 100 |

**Independence explained (2 marks):**
CCT describes a single property of the light source — its warmth or coolness — while CRI describes how it interacts with *reflected* light from objects. A cool-white fluorescent tube (CCT = 6500 K) can have Ra = 75, while a warm-white halogen (CCT = 2800 K) has Ra ≈ 100. Conversely, an RGB-mixed white LED can have CCT = 4000 K but Ra = 50 because its spectrum has only three narrow peaks.

**Real-world examples (2 marks):**
1. **Halogen lamp:** CCT = 3000 K (warm) but Ra = 100 (excellent rendering). This disproves "warm = poor colour."
2. **High-pressure sodium (HPS) street light:** CCT ≈ 2000 K (very warm) but Ra ≈ 25 (very poor rendering). This disproves "warm = good colour."

**Part (b) — TCS samples and CRI limitations (7 marks)**

**The 8 CIE TCS (3 marks):**
| TCS | Munsell Hue Family |
|---|---|
| TCS01 | Light greyish red |
| TCS02 | Dark greyish yellow |
| TCS03 | Strong yellow green |
| TCS04 | Moderate yellowish green |
| TCS05 | Light bluish green |
| TCS06 | Light blue |
| TCS07 | Light violet |
| TCS08 | Light reddish purple |

**Limitations of single-number CRI (4 marks):**

1. **Averaging masks sample-specific failures:** A modern white LED often has Ra = 82 (acceptable) but **R9 (saturated red) ≈ 10** (catastrophic). A customer purchasing meat, cosmetics, or red décor would see a brown/grey appearance, yet the manufacturer can still advertise "CRI 80."

2. **Small sample size:** Only 8 (or 14) samples are used. Many real-world object colours — particularly deep reds, skin tones, and high-saturation hues — are underrepresented.

3. **Outdated chromaticity space:** The CRI uses CIE 1960 UCS, which is non-uniform for highly saturated colours. The modern **IES TM-30** uses the CIE 1976 u'v' space, which is more uniform, and also provides the **Gamut Index Rg** (saturation) in addition to the **Fidelity Index Rf**.

4. **Inaccuracy at extreme CCTs:** CRI is unreliable below 2000 K and above 25000 K because the reference illuminant selection rule becomes ill-defined at these extremes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using CIE 1931 xy space instead of CIE 1960 UCS** for ΔEi — full marks lost. Always use (u, v) for CRI.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 4.6** in $R_i = 100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E_i$ — losing 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Confusing CCT with CRI** in exam answers — examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this conceptual error in Part A.
> 4. **Averaging R9–R14 into Ra** — this is incorrect; only R1–R8 form the general Ra.
> 5. **Skipping the reference illuminant selection rule** in long answers — examiners expect this explicitly (Planckian if CCT < 5000 K, daylight otherwise).
> 6. **Forgetting Von Kries adaptation** — omitting the chromatic adaptation step in long answers loses 1.5 marks in Part (a) of a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CRI is a 0-to-100 dimensionless number** — the higher, the better. **Ra = 100** means perfect colour rendering.
- **General CRI (Ra)** = arithmetic mean of **R1 to R8** (the 8 CIE pastel test colour samples).
- **Special CRI (Ri)** = $100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E_i$ where ΔEi is computed in **CIE 1960 UCS** (u, v) space.
- **Reference illuminant rule:** Planckian blackbody if **CCT < 5000 K**; CIE Daylight (D-series) if **CCT ≥ 5000 K**.
- **Von Kries chromatic adaptation** is applied to test-source XYZ values to simulate the eye's white-balance.
- **CCT and CRI are independent** — a warm lamp can have high CRI and a cool lamp can have low CRI.
- **CRI ≥ 90:** Museums, hospitals, surgical theatres. **CRI 80–89:** Offices, homes. **CRI 60–79:** Warehouses, industrial. **CRI < 60:** Street lighting.
- **R9 (saturated red) is the most critical special CRI** for skin tones, food, and cosmetics — often not reported but vital.
- **CRI is a 1995-standard metric**; the modern replacement is **IES TM-30** with **Rf (fidelity)** and **Rg (gamut)**.
- **Common mistake:** Using xy space instead of uv space. Always use **CIE 1960 UCS** for CRI calculations.
- **HPS and LPS lamps** have very low CRI (20–25 and below 0) because of their line spectra.
- **Incandescent, halogen, and D65 daylight** all have **Ra = 100** by definition (they are reference sources for their respective CCTs).
- **For KTU exams:** State the formula $R_a = \frac{1}{8}\sum_{i=1}^{8} R_i$ explicitly; show ΔE computation in uv space; mention Von Kries; conclude with the practical Ra band of the source.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroboscopic Effect and Methods to Reduce It
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stroboscopic Effect and Methods to Reduce It

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stroboscopic Effect (Stroboscopy):** It is an optical phenomenon in which a **rotating** or **reciprocating** object illuminated by a **pulsating** (time-varying) light source appears to be **stationary**, rotating at a **slower speed**, or rotating in the **reverse direction** to the actual motion.

In the context of Illumination Engineering (KTU PEEET418), the stroboscopic effect is most commonly observed when machinery such as **lathe spindles, fans, printing press rollers, drilling machines, and textile loom shuttles** are illuminated by **gaseous discharge lamps** (fluorescent tubes, mercury vapour, sodium vapour) operated on a normal **50 Hz AC supply**.

The fundamental reason these lamps produce a stroboscopic effect is that their **light output is not continuous** — it pulsates at twice the supply frequency (i.e., **100 Hz** for a 50 Hz mains). Each pulsation "freezes" the motion of the rotating part for an instant, and if this frozen image overlaps with the previous one, the brain perceives the object as **stationary**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1):**
> The stroboscopic effect is treated as a **lighting hazard** in industrial illumination design. The KTU syllabus mandates that students understand the *cause*, the *mathematical condition for apparent standstill*, and at least **three practical methods** to mitigate it.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are watching a **carousel spinning at 60 RPM** (i.e., one revolution per second) under a **strobe light** flashing exactly **once per second**. Because the carousel completes exactly one full turn between two consecutive flashes, your brain sees it in the *same position* every flash — and you wrongly conclude that **the carousel has stopped**.

Now translate this to a fluorescent-lit factory:

- The fluorescent tube **"flashes" 100 times every second** (since it has two light pulses per AC cycle of $\frac{1}{50}$ s).
- A **drilling machine** rotating at **3000 RPM** completes **50 revolutions per second**.
- Since $50 = \frac{100}{2}$, the drill bit advances by *half a turn* between two consecutive flashes — to your eye, it looks like it is barely moving or completely still.
- A worker, thinking the drill is stopped, reaches out to clear a chip — and suffers a severe **hand injury**.

This is **not science fiction** — it is a documented industrial hazard. The stroboscopic effect is the reason why strict illumination codes (like the **IS 3646** and **NBC 2016** of India) recommend specific lamp types for rotating machinery areas.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally used in KTU numerical problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indian AC supply frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** | Hz |
| Flicker frequency of discharge lamp | $f_{light}$ | $2f =$ **100 Hz** | Hz |
| Period of AC supply | $T$ | $\frac{1}{50} =$ **0.02 s** | s |
| Period of light pulsation | $T_{light}$ | $\frac{1}{100} =$ **0.01 s** | s |
| Critical Fusion Frequency (CFF) | $f_{CFF}$ | **50–60 Hz** (approx.) | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Fusion Frequency (CFF):** Below this frequency, the human eye perceives individual flickers as **discrete pulses**. Above the CFF, the eye integrates the pulses and perceives a **steady, continuous light**. The stroboscopic danger zone lies **well below** the CFF for most industrial machines, making the hazard very real.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why Does the Effect Occur?

The stroboscopic effect is a consequence of three intertwined physical phenomena. Let us break them down step by step:

### Step 1 — Light Output of a Discharge Lamp is Pulsating, Not Continuous
A fluorescent tube (or any low-pressure/High-Intensity Discharge lamp) cannot follow the instantaneous AC voltage. The discharge is extinguished twice per AC cycle (once near each zero crossing of the voltage waveform) and re-strikes after the voltage rises above the re-ignition voltage.

Mathematically, for a sinusoidal supply:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$$

The light output (luminous flux) follows the **envelope of the supply voltage**, not the voltage itself, because the discharge process has a small thermal inertia. Hence, the light pulses twice per cycle:

$$L(t) \approx L_m \cdot \vert \sin(2\pi f t) \vert^n$$

where $n \approx 1$ to $2$ depending on the lamp type and ballast.

**Result:** Light frequency = $2 \times$ supply frequency $= 2 \times 50 = 100$ Hz.

### Step 2 — The Human Eye + Brain has a "Persistence of Vision"
The retina retains an image for approximately **$\frac{1}{16}$ to $\frac{1}{25}$ s** (i.e., 40–60 ms) after the stimulus is removed. This is called **persistence of vision** (or **after-image effect**).

If two light pulses arrive within this retention window, the brain *fuses* them into a single continuous perception. This is the same principle that makes a **cinema film** (24 frames/s) appear as smooth motion.

### Step 3 — Mathematical Coincidence of Frequencies
When the rotating machinery has a rotational frequency ($N$ in rev/s) that is an integer sub-multiple of the flicker frequency, the eye sees the rotor in the **same angular position** at every flash — it appears **stationary**. When it is *almost* a sub-multiple, the brain perceives a **slow rotation** (forward or reverse).

> [!TIP]
> **Why the perceived rotation may appear "reversed":**
> If the actual speed is *slightly greater* than the strobe-coincidence speed, the rotor advances by a small positive angle between flashes — the brain interprets successive small advances as **slow forward motion**. If the actual speed is *slightly less*, the brain fills in the missing angle in the reverse direction → **apparent backward motion**. This "wagon-wheel effect" is the same phenomenon that makes helicopter rotor blades and car wheels in movies look like they spin backwards.

---

## 2.2 The Three Perception Conditions

Let $N$ = actual rotational speed of the machine (in rev/s) and $f_L$ = flicker frequency of the lamp (in Hz). Then:

| Condition on $N$ | Perception |
|---|---|
| $N = \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ for any integer $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ | **Apparent standstill** (most dangerous) |
| $N > \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ by a small amount | **Apparent slow forward rotation** |
| $N < \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ by a small amount | **Apparent slow reverse rotation** |

For an Indian 50 Hz supply with a fluorescent lamp: $f_L = 100$ Hz.
**Dangerous speeds** (where standstill occurs) are therefore $N = 100, 50, 33.33, 25, 20, \ldots$ rev/s
i.e. **6000, 3000, 2000, 1500, 1200, … RPM**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorise these for the KTU board exam.**

$$
\boxed{\,f_{L} = 2 \cdot f_{supply}\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_{dangerous} = \dfrac{f_{L}}{k} \;\;(k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots)\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_{dangerous} \;(\text{in RPM}) = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_{L}}{k} = \dfrac{120 \cdot f_{supply}}{k}\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Average Luminous Flux} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T L(t)\,dt\,}
$$

**Talbot's Law** (applies when flicker is above the CFF, i.e. light is perceived as steady):

$$
L_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{t_1 + t_2}\left(\int_0^{t_1} L_1\,dt + \int_0^{t_2} L_2\,dt\right)
$$

For a square-wave flicker of equal on/off duration: $L_{avg} = \dfrac{L_{max}}{2}$.

**Condition for elimination of stroboscopic effect using high-frequency electronic ballast:**

$$
\boxed{\,f_{L,HF} \gg f_{CFF} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_{L,HF} \geq 20\,\text{kHz (typical)}\,}
$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Safety:** The stroboscopic effect dictates lamp selection in machine shops, textile mills, printing presses, and conveyor inspection bays. It is one of the primary reasons why many Indian factories have *legally switched* to LED panels with high-frequency drivers, even where fluorescent tubes were otherwise acceptable on energy grounds.
- **Design Codes:** **IS 3646 (Part 1)** explicitly requires that lighting in areas with rotating machinery be free from perceptible flicker — this directly translates to the stroboscopic effect mitigation requirement.
- **Stroboscopic Tachometers:** Interestingly, the *same* phenomenon is *exploited* in stroboscopic tachometers to measure the RPM of a rotating shaft *without physical contact* (a non-contact speed measurement technique).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Light Pulse Frequency of a Discharge Lamp

**Statement to Prove:** The light output of a fluorescent lamp operated on a sinusoidal AC supply of frequency $f$ pulsates at a frequency of $2f$.

**Derivation:**

Let the instantaneous supply voltage be:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$$

The instantaneous power dissipated in the discharge is:

$$p(t) = \dfrac{v^2(t)}{R_{arc}} = \dfrac{V_m^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)}{R_{arc}}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$
p(t) = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_{arc}} - \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_{arc}}\cos(4\pi f t)
$$

Hence the power (and the light output, assuming linear conversion) has two components:
- A **DC component** of value $\dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_{arc}}$
- An **AC component** of frequency $4\pi f$ rad/s, i.e. $\dfrac{4\pi f}{2\pi} = 2f$ Hz.

**Conclusion:** The light output contains a fluctuating term of frequency $2f$. Therefore, the dominant visible flicker rate is $2f$.

For $f = 50$ Hz (India): $f_L = 2 \times 50 = 100$ Hz.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dangerous (Stationary-Appearance) Speeds of a Machine

**Statement to Prove:** A machine rotating at $N$ rev/s appears stationary if $N = \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ for any positive integer $k$.

**Derivation:**

Let the rotor have a distinguishing mark on it (e.g., a dot). Let the angular position of the mark at time $t$ be:

$$\theta(t) = 2\pi N t + \theta_0$$

A light flash occurs at times:

$$t_m = \dfrac{m}{f_L} \;\;\;\;(m = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots)$$

The angular position of the mark at the $m$-th flash is:

$$
\theta(t_m) = 2\pi N \cdot \dfrac{m}{f_L} + \theta_0
$$

The rotor will **appear stationary** if the angular increment between two consecutive flashes is a multiple of $2\pi$:

$$
\Delta\theta = 2\pi N \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_L} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{f_L} = 2\pi k
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
N = k \cdot f_L \;\;\;(\text{this gives } k \text{ full revs per flash period})
$$

But we must also consider that **half-revolutions** can be mistaken for full revolutions by the eye (since a circular object looks the same after rotating by $\pi$ if it has bilateral symmetry, or by $2\pi/k$ if it has $k$-fold symmetry). For a *general* mark, the more general condition is:

$$
\boxed{\,N = \dfrac{f_L}{k} \;\;\; \text{or} \;\;\; N = \dfrac{m \cdot f_L}{k}\,}
$$

For KTU problems, the standard assumption is $N = \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ where $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

**Numerical example:** $f_L = 100$ Hz, $k = 2$:

$$
N = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ rev/s} = 3000 \text{ RPM}
$$

This is a typical **drill press** speed — hence the practical danger.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Apparent Slow Rotation Frequency

**Statement to Prove:** If the actual speed is $N$ rev/s and $N$ is *not exactly* equal to $\dfrac{f_L}{k}$, then the apparent speed is:

$$
N_{app} = N - \dfrac{f_L}{k}
$$

**Derivation:**

The angular advance per flash interval is $\dfrac{2\pi N}{f_L}$. The eye interprets this advance as a slow rotation whose frequency is the *residual* angular speed:

$$
\omega_{app} = 2\pi N - 2\pi k f_L = 2\pi \left(N - k f_L\right)
$$

If we want the apparent speed in *cycles per second*:

$$
N_{app} = N - k f_L
$$

But this is for the case where $N$ is *just above* $k f_L$. For the more general case (where $N$ is close to $\dfrac{f_L}{k}$), we write:

$$
\boxed{\,N_{app} = \left\vert N - \dfrac{f_L}{k}\right\vert\,}
$$

**Sign convention (for KTU answers):**
- $N > \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ → apparent **forward** slow rotation
- $N < \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ → apparent **reverse** slow rotation
- $N = \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ → apparent **standstill**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Mitigation Methods

> [!TIP]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU table. Memorise the principles, advantages, and limitations.**

| Sl. No. | Method | Operating Principle | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Use Incandescent / Halogen Lamps** | Filament has high thermal inertia → light output is essentially **constant** (flicker $\approx 0$ Hz) | Completely eliminates the effect; simple | High energy consumption (~80% as heat); short life (~1000 h) |
| 2 | **High-Frequency Electronic Ballast (HFEB)** | Lamp is operated at 20–50 kHz instead of 50 Hz. Flicker becomes $2f = 40$–$100$ kHz, far above the CFF (~50 Hz) | Modern, efficient, near-elimination of flicker & stroboscopic effect; lighter ballast | Higher initial cost; electronic ballasts may produce EMI |
| 3 | **Three-Phase Operation with Three Lamps** | Three lamps connected in star, fed from 3-phase supply. Light pulses from the three lamps are **120° out of phase**; the combined light is nearly constant | Good for industrial halls; no electronics needed | Requires 3-phase supply; only ~$\frac{1}{3}$ of the flicker remains |
| 4 | **Twin-Lamp Lead-Lag Circuit** | Two lamps in parallel — one with **leading** PF ballast (capacitive), one with **lagging** PF ballast (inductive). Their light peaks are out of phase | Cost-effective retrofit; significant reduction | Some flicker remains at $2f$; complex wiring |
| 5 | **Increase the Number of Lamps per Phase** | When many lamps are connected, the random arc-reignition delays statistically **smooth out** the light envelope | Simple, no circuit change | Only partial reduction; requires more luminaires |
| 6 | **Use Natural Daylight** | Sunlight has a constant luminous flux (DC-like) | Zero flicker; free; healthy | Not always available; needs skylights, glare control |
| 7 | **Use LED Lamps with Quality Driver** | LEDs respond to current instantly; a good driver uses **constant-current PWM at > 1 kHz** which is above CFF | Very low flicker, very long life, energy-efficient | Cheap LED drivers may flicker at 100 Hz — must verify with "flicker meter" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Cause-and-Effect Chain of the Stroboscopic Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 50 Hz] --> B[Sinusoidal Voltage v of t]
    B --> C[Discharge Lamp Voltage Across Tube]
    C --> D[Arc Strikes Twice Per Cycle]
    D --> E[Light Output L of t Pulsates at 2f = 100 Hz]
    E --> F{Is Rotating Machine Present?}
    F -- No --> G[No Stroboscopic Effect]
    F -- Yes --> H[Calculate Ratio N divided by fL]
    H --> I{Matches Sub Multiple Condition?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Apparent Standstill DANGER]
    I -- Slightly Above --> K[Apparent Slow Forward Motion]
    I -- Slightly Below --> L[Apparent Slow Reverse Motion]
    J --> M[Industrial Accident Risk]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Apply Mitigation Methods]
    N --> N1[Use HF Electronic Ballast]
    N --> N2[Use 3 Phase 3 Lamp Circuit]
    N --> N3[Use Lead Lag Twin Lamp]
    N --> N4[Switch to LED or Incandescent]
    N1 --> O[Safe Illumination]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    N4 --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Frequency Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supply f = 50 Hz] --> B[Rectifier of Inverter]
    B --> C[High Frequency Drive f sub HF = 20 to 50 kHz]
    C --> D[Discharge Lamp]
    D --> E[Light Pulsation at 2 f sub HF = 40 to 100 kHz]
    E --> F{Human Eye CFF approx 50 Hz}
    F -- Yes --> G[Eye Integrates Pulses]
    F -- No --> H[Perceives Flicker]
    G --> I[No Stroboscopic Effect]
    H --> J[Stroboscopic Effect Present]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Lead-Lag Twin-Lamp Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Phase R] --> B1[Ballast L1 Inductive]
    B1 --> C1[Lamp 1 Tube 1]
    C1 --> D1[Neutral]
    A2[Phase R] --> B2[Ballast L2 Inductive]
    B2 --> C2[Capacitor C in Series with Lamp 2]
    C2 --> D2[Neutral]
    C1 -. Lagging Current .-> E[Light Peak of Lamp 1]
    C2 -. Leading Current .-> F[Light Peak of Lamp 2]
    E --> G[Out of Phase Light Pulses]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Smoothed Combined Illumination]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Three-Phase Three-Lamp Connection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3 Phase Supply 50 Hz] --> B[Phase R 0 Degree]
    A --> C[Phase Y 120 Degree]
    A --> D[Phase B 240 Degree]
    B --> E[Lamp R on Phase R]
    C --> F[Lamp Y on Phase Y]
    D --> G[Lamp B on Phase B]
    E --> H[Light Peak at 0 deg]
    F --> I[Light Peak at 120 deg]
    G --> J[Light Peak at 240 deg]
    H --> K[Combined Light Sum]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Near Constant Illumination]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Q1. Define stroboscopic effect. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The stroboscopic effect is an optical illusion in which a **rotating or reciprocating object** illuminated by a **pulsating light source** appears to be **stationary**, rotating at a **slower speed**, or rotating in the **reverse direction**.

It is most commonly observed in industrial environments where **gaseous discharge lamps** (fluorescent tubes, mercury vapour lamps, sodium vapour lamps) are used. These lamps, when operated on a **50 Hz AC supply**, produce a light output that pulsates at **100 Hz** (twice the supply frequency). If the rotational speed of the machine is an integer sub-multiple of this flicker frequency, the eye perceives the rotor as **frozen** in position.

This effect is dangerous because an operator may wrongly assume a machine has stopped, attempt to touch or adjust it, and suffer a serious injury.

**Keywords for full marks:** *pulsating light source*, *rotating/reciprocating object*, *apparent standstill/slow/reverse motion*, *discharge lamps on 50 Hz AC*, *industrial hazard*.

---

### Q2. Mention any **three** methods to reduce the stroboscopic effect. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Use of high-frequency electronic ballast (HFEB):** The discharge lamp is operated at a very high frequency (typically 20–50 kHz) instead of 50 Hz, so the flicker frequency becomes 40–100 kHz — far above the Critical Fusion Frequency (CFF) of the human eye. The light is then perceived as steady.

2. **Use of three-phase supply with three lamps:** Three fluorescent lamps connected across the three phases of a 3-phase supply produce light pulses that are mutually displaced by 120°, so the combined illumination is nearly constant.

3. **Use of lead-lag twin-lamp circuit:** Two lamps are connected in parallel — one through an inductive (lagging) ballast and the other through a capacitive (leading) ballast. The two lamps' light peaks occur at different instants, reducing the depth of flicker.

*(Alternative valid methods: use of incandescent/halogen lamps, use of LED lamps with quality drivers, use of natural daylight, increasing the number of lamps in the area.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of the stroboscopic effect in detail. Derive the condition under which a rotating machine illuminated by a fluorescent lamp on a 50 Hz supply will appear to be stationary. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A fluorescent tube operating on a 50 Hz AC mains illuminates a drilling machine. Calculate the rotational speeds (in RPM) at which the drill bit will appear (i) stationary, (ii) rotating forward at half the actual speed. Take the critical flicker condition as $N = \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ where $k$ is a positive integer. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Definition (2 marks):**
