The stroboscopic effect is the optical illusion in which a moving object illuminated by a time-varying (pulsating) light source appears to be stationary or to move at a different speed/direction. It arises because gaseous discharge lamps produce light in pulses, not continuously.

**Cause (2 marks):**
A fluorescent lamp on a 50 Hz supply extinguishes near each zero-crossing of the AC waveform. The light output thus contains a fluctuating component of frequency:

$$f_L = 2 f = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

The human eye, with its persistence of vision (~50 ms), cannot resolve these individual pulses when the flicker rate is above the CFF, but can still be "tricked" into seeing apparent motion patterns for rotating objects.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $N$ = rotational speed in rev/s, and $f_L$ = flicker frequency. Between two consecutive light pulses, the time elapsed is $\dfrac{1}{f_L}$ seconds. The angle swept by the rotor in this interval is:

$$\Delta\theta = 2\pi N \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_L} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{f_L}$$

For the rotor to **appear stationary**, this angle must equal an integer multiple of $2\pi$ (so the mark returns to the same perceived position):

$$\dfrac{2\pi N}{f_L} = 2\pi k \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = k f_L$$

Equivalently, for sub-multiples (i.e. multiple pulses per revolution):

$$\boxed{\,N = \dfrac{f_L}{k}\,} \;\;\; k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

This is the **standstill condition** for a rotating machine under stroboscopic illumination.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, hence $f_L = 2f = 100$ Hz.

**(i) Apparent Stationary (3 marks):**
For standstill, $N = \dfrac{f_L}{k} = \dfrac{100}{k}$ rev/s.

Converting to RPM (multiply by 60):

$$N_{RPM} = \dfrac{60 \times 100}{k} = \dfrac{6000}{k}$$

For $k = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

| $k$ | $N$ (rev/s) | $N$ (RPM) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 6000 |
| 2 | 50 | 3000 |
| 3 | 33.33 | 2000 |
| 4 | 25 | 1500 |

**Result:** The drill bit will appear stationary at **6000, 3000, 2000, 1500, 1200, … RPM**.

**(ii) Apparent Half Actual Speed — Forward (4 marks):**
If the drill bit appears to rotate forward at **half** its actual speed, then the **apparent** speed $N_{app} = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{2}$.

Using the formula:

$$N_{app} = N_{actual} - \dfrac{f_L}{k}$$

Substituting $N_{app} = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{2}$:

$$
\dfrac{N_{actual}}{2} = N_{actual} - \dfrac{f_L}{k}
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_L}{k} = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{2}
$$

$$
N_{actual} = \dfrac{2 f_L}{k} = \dfrac{2 \times 100}{k} = \dfrac{200}{k} \text{ rev/s}
$$

In RPM: $N_{actual} = \dfrac{60 \times 200}{k} = \dfrac{12000}{k}$ RPM.

For $k = 1, 2, 3$:

| $k$ | $N_{actual}$ (rev/s) | $N_{actual}$ (RPM) | $N_{app}$ (RPM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 12000 | 6000 |
| 2 | 100 | 6000 | 3000 |
| 3 | 66.67 | 4000 | 2000 |

**Result:** The drill bit will appear to rotate forward at half its actual speed when its true speed is **12000, 6000, 4000, … RPM**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **three-phase, three-lamp** method of reducing the stroboscopic effect. Why is this method preferred over single-phase fluorescent lighting in industries? [7 Marks]

**(b)** Discuss the **high-frequency electronic ballast** method in detail. Why is the flicker frequency raised to 20–50 kHz? What is the corresponding light pulsation frequency? State two other benefits of this method. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid schematic in **Section 4.4** above.

**Explanation (3 marks):**
In this method, three fluorescent lamps are connected in **star (Y)** across the three phases (R, Y, B) of a 3-phase, 50 Hz supply. Each lamp sees a sinusoidal voltage that is **120° out of phase** with the other two.

Since the light output of each lamp peaks twice per cycle (i.e. at 100 Hz), the light peaks of the three lamps are **displaced by 120° × 2 = 240° in time** of the light pulsation cycle.

The instantaneous illumination on the work surface is the **sum of the three lamps' outputs**. Because the three sinusoidal light envelopes are shifted by $\frac{1}{3}$ of the pulsation period, the **sum is mathematically a constant** (since three sinusoids of equal amplitude, mutually 120° apart, sum to zero ripple in the fundamental — the residual is at 3× the original frequency, which is 300 Hz, well above the CFF).

**Why preferred in industries (2 marks):**
- The flicker depth is reduced from **~50%** (single-phase) to **< 5%** (three-phase).
- No special electronic ballast is required — only a 3-phase supply, which is already available in industrial premises.
- It is the most economical large-scale solution for **machine shops, assembly lines, and inspection bays**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A high-frequency electronic ballast first **rectifies** the 50 Hz AC supply to DC, and then uses a **self-oscillating inverter** (or a PWM-driven MOSFET/IGBT bridge) to convert the DC to a high-frequency AC (typically 20–50 kHz). This HF AC drives the fluorescent tube through a small inductor or even directly in some "instant-start" designs.

The lamp arc, which would normally extinguish at 100 Hz, is now sustained continuously at 40–100 kHz. The light pulsation frequency is:

$$f_{L, HF} = 2 \times f_{HF} = 2 \times (20\, \text{to}\, 50\,\text{kHz}) = 40\, \text{to}\, 100\,\text{kHz}$$

**Why 20–50 kHz specifically? (2 marks):**
The human eye's **Critical Fusion Frequency (CFF)** is approximately 50–60 Hz. To completely eliminate any perception of flicker or stroboscopic effect, the light pulsation frequency must be at least **5–10 times the CFF**, i.e. above 500 Hz. A safety margin pushes this to **> 20 kHz**. Operating above 20 kHz also keeps the inverter's switching frequency above the audible range, so the ballast is silent.

**Two Other Benefits (2 marks):**
1. **Higher luminous efficacy:** The lamp operates at a lower arc voltage drop at high frequency, leading to a **10–15% improvement** in lumens per watt.
2. **Instant start, no flicker at switch-on, no hum, lighter weight** (no heavy 50 Hz choke), and longer lamp life due to controlled electrode heating.

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Writing the flicker frequency as 50 Hz** — it is **100 Hz** (twice the supply frequency) for a single-phase discharge lamp. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rev/s to RPM** — always multiply by 60 in the final answer unless the question specifically asks for rev/s.
> 3. **Listing only 2 mitigation methods** — KTU's 3-mark Part-A question on "methods to reduce stroboscopic effect" requires **at least three** methods with brief explanations. Listing only names without one-line explanations will fetch only **partial marks**.
> 4. **Mixing up the lead-lag circuit** — the *inductive* (lagging) ballast and the *capacitive* (leading) ballast must be clearly identified. A wrong connection will not produce the desired out-of-phase light peaks.
> 5. **Confusing the CFF with persistence of vision** — these are related but distinct. CFF is the flicker rate at which flicker just disappears; persistence of vision is the time the retina holds an image. Use the correct term.
> 6. **In derivation, skipping the integration step** — the KTU board examiner expects you to explicitly write $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$ and identify the $2f$ term.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise**

- **Stroboscopic effect** = apparent standstill/slow/reverse motion of a moving object under pulsating light.
- **Cause:** Gaseous discharge lamps on 50 Hz AC produce light at **100 Hz** (twice the supply frequency).
- **Persistence of vision** ≈ 50 ms; **Critical Fusion Frequency (CFF)** ≈ 50–60 Hz.
- **Standstill condition:** $N = \dfrac{f_L}{k}$ rev/s, where $f_L = 2 f_{supply}$ and $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- **In RPM:** $N = \dfrac{120 f}{k}$ for standstill.
- **Apparent slow forward motion** when $N > \dfrac{f_L}{k}$; **apparent reverse** when $N < \dfrac{f_L}{k}$.
- **Most dangerous Indian industrial speeds:** 6000, 3000, 2000, 1500, 1200 RPM (on a 50 Hz mains with fluorescent lamp).
- **Methods to reduce the effect (memorise 5+):**
  1. Incandescent / halogen lamps (thermal inertia).
  2. High-frequency electronic ballast (20–50 kHz).
  3. Three-phase three-lamp star connection.
  4. Lead-lag twin-lamp circuit (L + C).
  5. Increase number of lamps in the area.
  6. Natural daylight.
  7. Quality LED luminaires with constant-current drivers.
- **Industrial use:** It is a **safety hazard**, not just an aesthetic issue. IS 3646 and NBC 2016 mandate flicker-free lighting in machine shops.
- **Useful application:** The same effect is *deliberately used* in **stroboscopic tachometers** for non-contact RPM measurement.
- **Talbot's Law** gives the average luminous flux when flicker is above the CFF: $L_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T L(t)\,dt$.

> **End of Notes — Stroboscopic Effect & Methods to Reduce It | Module 1 | PEEET418**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Artificial Lighting Schemes: Direct, Indirect, Semi-Direct, Semi-Indirect and Diffused Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods of Artificial Lighting Schemes: Direct, Indirect, Semi-Direct, Semi-Indirect and Diffused Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

According to the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES)** classification, **artificial lighting schemes** refer to the systematic arrangement and orientation of luminaires (light fixtures) that determine the directional distribution of luminous flux emitted by the source, thereby defining the quantity, quality, and character of illumination on the working plane.

The classification is fundamentally based on the **proportion of total luminous flux** directed **upwards** versus **downwards** relative to the horizontal plane passing through the luminaire. This is expressed as a percentage ratio of upward flux to downward flux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The five standard methods classified by the IES (now IESNA) are: **Direct, Semi-Direct, Indirect, Semi-Indirect, and General/Diffused** lighting schemes. These are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive for the purpose of board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are sitting in your hostel room at night studying. You have five different ways a lamp could light up your desk:

1. **Direct Lighting** → A bare LED bulb hanging directly over your book. All light slams down on the page. Shadows are harsh, but you see the print clearly.
2. **Semi-Direct** → The same bulb but with a slightly translucent shade. Most light still goes down, but a little escapes upward and bounces off the ceiling.
3. **Diffused (General)** → A frosted globe lamp. Light is thrown equally up and down. The room looks evenly lit with mild shadows.
4. **Semi-Indirect** → A bowl-shaped fixture with the opening facing up. Most light bounces off the ceiling first, then trickles down. Soft, glare-free, like moonlight indoors.
5. **Indirect** → A completely upward-facing fixture hidden behind a cornice or cove. Zero direct light reaches your eyes. Everything you see is reflected light from the ceiling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** Because lighting engineers must *choose* a scheme based on the task (reading, surgery, factory work) and aesthetics. The exam tests whether you can justify *which* scheme suits *which* application and *why*.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** Measured in **lumens (lm)** — total light energy radiated per second.
- **Luminous Intensity ($I$):** Measured in **candela (cd)** — flux per unit solid angle.
- **Illuminance ($E$):** Measured in **lux (lx)** or **lm/m²** — flux incident per unit area.
- **Luminance ($L$):** Measured in **cd/m²** — intensity per unit projected area (perceived "brightness").
- **Solid Angle ($\omega$):** Measured in **steradians (sr)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for Exams:** Luminance is a *physiological* quantity (what the eye perceives), while illuminance is a *physical* quantity (what actually falls on a surface). Examiners love to test this difference in 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five IES Lighting Schemes — Detailed Breakdown

The classification is defined by the **Upper Flux Fraction (UFF)** — the percentage of total emitted luminous flux that travels *upward* (above the horizontal) versus downward.

### 2.1.1 Direct Lighting Scheme
- **Upward Flux:** 0% – 10%
- **Downward Flux:** 90% – 100%
- **Optics:** Bare lamps, reflectors, or opaque metallic mirrors that direct all light downward.
- **Optical Efficiency:** Highest (typically **70% – 90%**).
- **Glare:** Maximum (severe harsh shadows).
- **Examples:** Industrial high-bay fixtures, streetlight downlighters, desk task lamps, recessed downlights.

**Why it works:** Light takes the shortest path to the work plane, losing minimal flux to intermediate reflections. This is why factories with high mounting heights prefer it.

### 2.1.2 Semi-Direct Lighting Scheme
- **Upward Flux:** 10% – 40%
- **Downward Flux:** 60% – 90%
- **Optics:** Translucent diffusers or reflector shades with small openings at the top.
- **Optical Efficiency:** High (around **70% – 80%**).
- **Glare:** Reduced compared to direct.
- **Examples:** Pendant fixtures with opal glass bottoms, commercial office downlights with a slight upward leak.

**Why it works:** The small upward component softens the ceiling by illuminating it slightly, reducing the contrast between the bright luminaire and the dark ceiling — this contrast is what causes eye strain.

### 2.1.3 General / Diffused Lighting Scheme
- **Upward Flux:** 40% – 60%
- **Downward Flux:** 40% – 60%
- **Optics:** Fully translucent globes or opal diffusers (e.g., frosted bulbs, milk-glass spheres).
- **Optical Efficiency:** Moderate (**50% – 70%**).
- **Glare:** Low.
- **Examples:** Bare frosted bulbs, decorative pendants, hospital ward general lighting.

**Why it works:** It is the visual middle-ground. Light reaches every surface — ceiling, walls, floor — and creates a soft, even ambience. There are no harsh shadows.

### 2.1.4 Semi-Indirect Lighting Scheme
- **Upward Flux:** 60% – 90%
- **Downward Flux:** 10% – 40%
- **Optics:** Enclosed bowl with all light directed upward; only a small fraction escapes downward through the bottom.
- **Optical Efficiency:** Lower (**40% – 60%**).
- **Glare:** Very low.
- **Examples:** Wall-mounted bowl fixtures, restaurant mood lighting, drawing-office luminaires.

**Why it works:** The ceiling acts as a giant secondary diffuser. The result is uniform, shadowless light with no direct glare source visible to the eye.

### 2.1.5 Indirect Lighting Scheme
- **Upward Flux:** 90% – 100%
- **Downward Flux:** 0% – 10%
- **Optics:** Fully enclosed fixtures with reflectors aimed at the ceiling. Lamps themselves are hidden from view (cove lighting).
- **Optical Efficiency:** Lowest (**30% – 50%**).
- **Glare:** Practically zero.
- **Examples:** Cove lighting in auditoriums, concealed cornice lighting in luxury hotels, museum galleries.

**Why it works:** The light source is *never* seen — only its reflected effect. This is the most aesthetically pleasing but energy-inefficient scheme because every reflection loses 10–30% of flux.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula** | **Symbol Description** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| $\Phi = E \times A$ | $\Phi$ = Luminous flux (lm), $E$ = Illuminance (lx), $A$ = Area (m²) | Computes total lumens required for a given task illuminance. |
| $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Inverse of above | Used to verify achieved illuminance on a work plane. |
| $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$ | $I$ = Intensity (cd), $\theta$ = angle of incidence, $d$ = distance (m) | Inverse Square Law with cosine correction — point-source illuminance. |
| $\eta_{\text{luminaire}} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{utilized}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}} \times 100\%$ | Luminaire efficiency | Determines flux wastage inside the fixture. |
| $UF = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{received on work plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{emitted by lamps}}}$ | Utilization Factor (UF) — dimensionless | **KTU favourite.** Tells how much flux actually reaches the task. UF depends on scheme type. |
| $MF = \dfrac{1}{DF} = \dfrac{1}{(1 + \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD})}$ | MF = Maintenance Factor, DF = Depreciation Factor, LLD = Lamp Lumen Depreciation, LDD = Luminaire Dirt Depreciation | Accounts for ageing and dirt accumulation. |
| $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times UF \times MF}$ | $N$ = Number of luminaires required | The *master design equation* — every lighting design problem ends with this. |
| $\text{Spacing} : \text{Height ratio (SHR)}$ | Ratio of maximum luminaire spacing to mounting height | Determines uniformity; differs per scheme. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR) trick for KTU exams:** Direct lighting has the **highest SHR (≈ 1.5)** because light is concentrated downward and overlaps easily. Indirect lighting has the **lowest SHR (≈ 0.7 – 1.0)** because reflected light spreads unevenly. The examiner may ask: *"Why does indirect lighting require closer luminaire spacing?"* — answer: because reflected light has poor directional control.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **Recommended Scheme** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy machinery factory | Direct | High efficiency, shadows acceptable for safety, mounting height high. |
| Office / classroom | Semi-direct or Diffused | Reduces eye strain; ceiling gets some illumination. |
| Hospital operating theatre | Diffused (specialized) | No harsh shadows on the surgeon's hands. |
| Cinema hall / auditorium | Indirect | Audience comfort, decorative effect, zero glare. |
| Drawing office / inspection bay | Semi-indirect or Indirect | Eliminates cast shadows on the drawing board. |
| Street lighting | Direct (cut-off type) | Directs light to road, prevents upward wastage (light pollution control). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Luminaire-Number Formula

This is the most important derivation a KTU student must memorize, because almost every design-numerical in Part B ends with this equation.

**Starting Point — The Total Flux Required:**

The illuminance $E$ (in lux) on a work plane of area $A$ (in m²) requires a total incident flux of:

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = E \times A \quad [\text{lumens}]
$$

**Accounting for Utilization Factor (UF):**

Of the flux emitted by the lamps, only a fraction $UF$ actually reaches the work plane. The rest is lost in luminaire optics, walls, ceiling, etc. So the lamps must emit:

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp,total}} = \dfrac{E \times A}{UF} \quad [\text{lumens}]
$$

**Accounting for Maintenance Factor (MF):**

Over time, lamps dim (LLD) and luminaires collect dust (LDD). To maintain the design illuminance $E$ throughout the lamp's life, we must over-design initially:

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp,total,design}} = \dfrac{E \times A}{UF \times MF} \quad [\text{lumens}]
$$

**Converting to Number of Luminaires:**

If each luminaire contains $n$ lamps, each lamp rated at $\Phi_{\text{lamp}}$ lumens, then total flux per luminaire is $n \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}}$. Therefore:

$$
N = \dfrac{E \times A}{n \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times UF \times MF}
$$

**Final Master Equation:**

$$
\boxed{N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times UF \times MF \times n}}
$$

where:
- $N$ = number of luminaires
- $E$ = design illuminance (lux)
- $A$ = floor area (m²)
- $\Phi_{\text{lamp}}$ = rated flux per lamp (lumens)
- $UF$ = utilization factor (dimensionless, typically 0.4 – 0.8)
- $MF$ = maintenance factor (dimensionless, typically 0.7 – 0.8)
- $n$ = number of lamps per luminaire

## 3.2 Numerical Example (Worked Out Step-by-Step)

> **[KTU University Exam Style Numerical]**

**Problem:** A factory hall of dimensions **30 m × 15 m × 6 m** is to be illuminated to a level of **150 lux**. Using **metal halide lamps**, each rated at **20,000 lumens**, fitted in industrial reflector luminaires (2 lamps per luminaire). Given:
- Utilization Factor (UF) = 0.5
- Maintenance Factor (MF) = 0.8

Calculate:
(a) The number of luminaires required.
(b) The total connected load in kW if each lamp is rated at 400 W.

### Solution — Part (a): Number of Luminaires

**Step 1:** Calculate the floor area $A$.

$$
A = \text{length} \times \text{width} = 30 \times 15 = 450 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the given data.
- $E = 150 \; \text{lux}$
- $A = 450 \; \text{m}^{2}$
- $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 20{,}000 \; \text{lm}$
- $n = 2 \; \text{lamps/luminaire}$
- $UF = 0.5$
- $MF = 0.8$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the master equation.

$$
N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times n \times UF \times MF}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{150 \times 450}{20{,}000 \times 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.8}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator separately.

$$
\text{Numerator} = 150 \times 450 = 67{,}500
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 20{,}000 \times 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 = 20{,}000 \times 0.8 = 16{,}000
$$

**Step 5:** Final division.

$$
N = \dfrac{67{,}500}{16{,}000} = 4.21875
$$

**Step 6:** Round up to the next whole number (you cannot install a fraction of a luminaire).

$$
\boxed{N = 5 \; \text{luminaires}}
$$

### Solution — Part (b): Total Connected Load

**Step 1:** Total number of lamps = $N \times n = 5 \times 2 = 10$ lamps.

**Step 2:** Total wattage = $10 \times 400 \; \text{W} = 4000 \; \text{W}$.

**Step 3:** Convert to kilowatts.

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Load} = 4 \; \text{kW}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Scheme vs. Parameters)

| **Parameter** | **Direct** | **Semi-Direct** | **Diffused** | **Semi-Indirect** | **Indirect** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Upward flux (%) | 0 – 10 | 10 – 40 | 40 – 60 | 60 – 90 | 90 – 100 |
| Downward flux (%) | 90 – 100 | 60 – 90 | 40 – 60 | 10 – 40 | 0 – 10 |
| Glare index | Very High | High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| Luminaire efficiency | 70 – 90% | 70 – 80% | 50 – 70% | 40 – 60% | 30 – 50% |
| Shadow harshness | Very sharp | Sharp | Soft | Very soft | Almost nil |
| Ceiling brightness | Dark | Slightly bright | Moderately bright | Bright | Very bright |
| Wall brightness | Dark | Slightly bright | Moderate | Bright | Bright |
| Typical UF | 0.5 – 0.7 | 0.4 – 0.6 | 0.35 – 0.55 | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.25 – 0.4 |
| Spacing-Height Ratio (SHR) | 1.2 – 1.5 | 1.0 – 1.4 | 1.0 – 1.3 | 0.8 – 1.0 | 0.7 – 1.0 |
| Best application | Factories, streets | Offices, shops | Homes, hospitals | Drawing rooms, lounges | Auditoriums, galleries |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Highest |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IES Classification of Lighting Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IES Classification of Artificial Lighting Schemes] --> B[Direct Lighting]
    A --> C[Semi-Direct Lighting]
    A --> D[General / Diffused Lighting]
    A --> E[Semi-Indirect Lighting]
    A --> F[Indirect Lighting]

    B --> B1[Upward Flux 0 to 10 percent]
    B --> B2[Downward Flux 90 to 100 percent]
    B --> B3[Application: Heavy Industry, Streets]

    C --> C1[Upward Flux 10 to 40 percent]
    C --> C2[Downward Flux 60 to 90 percent]
    C --> C3[Application: Offices, Classrooms]

    D --> D1[Upward Flux 40 to 60 percent]
    D --> D2[Downward Flux 40 to 60 percent]
    D --> D3[Application: Hospitals, Residences]

    E --> E1[Upward Flux 60 to 90 percent]
    E --> E2[Downward Flux 10 to 40 percent]
    E --> E3[Application: Drawing Rooms, Lounges]

    F --> F1[Upward Flux 90 to 100 percent]
    F --> F2[Downward Flux 0 to 10 percent]
    F --> F3[Application: Auditoriums, Museums]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Light Distribution Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DS[Direct Scheme]
        DS_L[Lamp] --> DS_R[Opaque Reflector]
        DS_R --> DS_WP[Work Plane]
        DS_R -.->|Negligible| DS_CE[Ceiling]
    end

    subgraph SD[Semi-Direct Scheme]
        SD_L[Lamp] --> SD_D[Translucent Diffuser]
        SD_D --> SD_WP[Work Plane]
        SD_D -.->|Minor| SD_CE[Ceiling]
    end

    subgraph GD[General Diffused Scheme]
        GD_L[Lamp] --> GD_OD[Opal Glass Globe]
        GD_OD --> GD_WP[Work Plane]
        GD_OD --> GD_CE[Ceiling]
    end

    subgraph SI[Semi-Indirect Scheme]
        SI_L[Lamp] --> SI_B[Bowl Reflector]
        SI_B --> SI_CE[Ceiling]
        SI_B -.->|Minor| SI_WP[Work Plane]
    end

    subgraph IS[Indirect Scheme]
        IS_L[Hidden Lamp] --> IS_R[Concealed Reflector]
        IS_R --> IS_CE[Ceiling]
        IS_CE --> IS_WP[Work Plane via Reflection]
    end

    style DS fill:#FFE4E1
    style SD fill:#FFFACD
    style GD fill:#E0FFE0
    style SI fill:#E0F5FF
    style IS fill:#F0E0FF
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic of Five Schemes (Luminous Flux Distribution)

```
        CEILING (horizontal reference plane)
   ==============================================================
                  |                 |
                  |                 |
   [INDIRECT]     |  [SEMI-INDRCT]  |  [GENERAL/DIFFUSED]
   ↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑    |  ↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑       |     ↑↑↑↑↑
   (90-100% up)   |  (60-90% up)    |     (40-60% up)
                  |                 |
   ---------------------------- LUMINAIRE ----------------------------
                  |                 |
   ↓ (0-10%)      |  ↓↓ (10-40%)    |  ↓↓↓↓ (40-60%)
   [INDIRECT]     |  [SEMI-INDRCT]  |  [GENERAL/DIFFUSED]
                  |                 |
                  |                 |
   [SEMI-DIRECT]  |  [DIRECT]
   ↑ (10-40%)     |  ↑ (0-10%)
   ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓ (60-90%)  |  ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓ (90-100%)
   ==============================================================
        WORKING PLANE (task surface)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"List the five methods of artificial lighting schemes as classified by the IES and state the percentage of upward flux in each case."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five IES classification methods of artificial lighting are:

1. **Direct Lighting:** Upward flux = **0 – 10%**, Downward flux = **90 – 100%**.
2. **Semi-Direct Lighting:** Upward flux = **10 – 40%**, Downward flux = **60 – 90%**.
3. **General / Diffused Lighting:** Upward flux = **40 – 60%**, Downward flux = **40 – 60%**.
4. **Semi-Indirect Lighting:** Upward flux = **60 – 90%**, Downward flux = **10 – 40%**.
5. **Indirect Lighting:** Upward flux = **90 – 100%**, Downward flux = **0 – 10%**.

*(1 mark for naming, 1 mark for the flux split, 1 mark for correct percentage ranges.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is a Utilization Factor (UF)? Why does it differ for direct and indirect lighting schemes?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Utilization Factor (UF)** is the ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total flux emitted by the lamps. It is dimensionless and lies between 0 and 1.

**Why it differs:**
- In **Direct lighting**, flux travels in straight lines from the luminaire to the work plane, so very little is lost to walls or ceiling. Hence UF is **high (≈ 0.5 – 0.7)**.
- In **Indirect lighting**, flux must first strike the ceiling, then reflect, and only a fraction reaches the work plane. Multiple reflections cause heavy losses, so UF is **low (≈ 0.25 – 0.4)**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for direct UF, 1 mark for indirect UF reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the five methods of artificial lighting schemes classified by IES with neat sketches. State the flux distribution in each case. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the five schemes on the basis of: (i) luminaire efficiency, (ii) glare, (iii) utilization factor, (iv) typical application. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks**

The five IES lighting schemes are:

**1. Direct Lighting (1.5 marks):**
- 90–100% of flux is directed **downward** to the work plane.
- Achieved using opaque reflectors, mirrored surfaces, or recessed downlights.
- Light source may be visible; high glare is a drawback.
- **Sketch:** Lamp with downward-pointing metallic reflector; arrows showing 100% flux downward.

**2. Semi-Direct Lighting (1 mark):**
- 60–90% flux downward, 10–40% upward.
- Uses translucent shades with a small top opening.
- The upward portion slightly illuminates the ceiling, reducing contrast.
- **Sketch:** Translucent pendant with small upward leak.

**3. General / Diffused Lighting (1.5 marks):**
- 40–60% flux in each direction.
- Achieved by fully translucent globes (opal glass, frosted plastic).
- Even, soft light with no harsh shadows.
- **Sketch:** Spherical frosted globe with arrows in all directions.

**4. Semi-Indirect Lighting (1 mark):**
- 10–40% flux downward, 60–90% upward.
- Achieved by enclosed bowl-shaped luminaires with a downward-facing opening.
- Ceiling becomes the primary light source.
- **Sketch:** Bowl fixture with dominant upward arrows.

**5. Indirect Lighting (1.5 marks):**
- 90–100% flux upward, 0–10% downward.
- Lamp completely concealed; cove or cornice mounting.
- Zero direct glare; light is fully reflected.
- **Sketch:** Hidden lamp reflecting off ceiling onto work plane.

*(0.5 mark reserved for overall diagram clarity and labeling.)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks**

| **Parameter** | **Direct** | **Semi-Direct** | **Diffused** | **Semi-Indirect** | **Indirect** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Luminaire Efficiency | 70–90% (Highest) | 70–80% | 50–70% | 40–60% | 30–50% (Lowest) |
| (ii) Glare | Very High | High | Moderate | Low | Negligible |
| (iii) Utilization Factor | 0.5–0.7 | 0.4–0.6 | 0.35–0.55 | 0.3–0.5 | 0.25–0.4 |
| (iv) Typical Application | Heavy industry, streets, floodlights | Offices, classrooms, retail shops | Hospitals, homes, general areas | Drawing rooms, lounges, restaurants | Auditoriums, art galleries, cinema halls |

**Key Justification Points (for full marks):**
- Direct lighting has highest efficiency because flux travels directly to the task without intermediate reflection.
- Indirect lighting has highest aesthetic quality but poorest efficiency due to multiple reflections.
- The trade-off is **always** between efficiency and visual comfort.

*(1.5 marks per row, 0.5 mark for overall presentation and accuracy of numbers.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — Internal Optional]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with their SI units: (i) Luminous Flux, (ii) Luminous Intensity, (iii) Illuminance, (iv) Luminance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A drawing office of area **20 m × 10 m** is to be illuminated to **750 lux**. Using fluorescent tube lights, each rated at **5000 lumens**, fitted in suitable luminaires (2 tubes per luminaire). Given UF = 0.6, MF = 0.75. Calculate: (i) the number of luminaires, (ii) the total connected load in kW if each tube consumes 65 W. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** (2 marks)
The total quantity of light energy emitted per second by a source, weighted by the standard photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ of the human eye. 
- **Unit:** **lumen (lm)**
- **Formula:** $\Phi = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ where $Q$ is light energy (lumen-second).

**(ii) Luminous Intensity ($I$):** (1.5 marks)
The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction by a source.
- **Unit:** **candela (cd) = lm/sr**
- **Formula:** $I = \dfrac{d\Phi}{d\omega}$

**(iii) Illuminance ($E$):** (1.5 marks)
The luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface.
- **Unit:** **lux (lx) = lm/m²**
- **Formula:** $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$ (Inverse Square Law).

**(iv) Luminance ($L$):** (2 marks)
The luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction. It is the *physiological* brightness perceived by the eye.
- **Unit:** **candela per square metre (cd/m²)** or **nit**
- **Formula:** $L = \dfrac{I}{A \cos \theta}$

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**Given Data:**
- Area $A = 20 \times 10 = 200 \; \text{m}^{2}$
- Illuminance $E = 750 \; \text{lux}$
- Lamp flux $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 5000 \; \text{lumens}$
- Number of lamps per luminaire $n = 2$
- UF = 0.6, MF = 0.75
- Power per tube = 65 W

**Part (i) — Number of Luminaires (4 marks):**

Using the master design equation:

$$
N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times n \times UF \times MF}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
N = \dfrac{750 \times 200}{5000 \times 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.75}
$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$
750 \times 200 = 150{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$
5000 \times 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.75 = 5000 \times 0.9 = 4500
$$

**Step 3 — Division:**

$$
N = \dfrac{150{,}000}{4500} = 33.33
$$

**Step 4 — Rounding up:**

$$
\boxed{N = 34 \; \text{luminaires}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Total Connected Load (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total number of tubes = $N \times n = 34 \times 2 = 68$ tubes.

**Step 2:** Total wattage = $68 \times 65 \; \text{W} = 4420 \; \text{W}$.

**Step 3:** Convert to kilowatts:

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Load} = 4.42 \; \text{kW}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Verified from KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Luminance and Illuminance (–2 marks):** Students frequently interchange these. Remember: *Illuminance falls on a surface; Luminance is emitted/reflected from a surface.* Luminance has units of **cd/m²**; Illuminance has units of **lux**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to round UP the number of luminaires (–1 mark):** Always round **up**, never to the nearest integer. 33.33 → 34, not 33. A fractional luminaire is physically impossible.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Direct with Indirect in the Flux Table (–1 mark):** Direct has 90–100% **downward**; Indirect has 90–100% **upward**. Examiners place this as a deliberate trap in Part A.
> 
> 4. **Missing the factor 'n' (lamps per luminaire) in the formula (–1 mark):** The formula requires $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times n$ in the denominator. Students often write $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times UF \times MF}$ and forget to multiply by $n$ or divide by it appropriately.
> 
> 5. **Not drawing flux direction arrows in sketches (–1 mark):** In sketches, arrows showing flux direction are mandatory. A luminaire drawing without arrows gets partial credit only.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up UF and MF (–1 mark):** UF is a function of the *room geometry and luminaire type*. MF is a function of *ageing and dirt*. They are not interchangeable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins (Pin these to your wall before the exam):**

- ⚡ **Five IES schemes** = Direct, Semi-Direct, Diffused (General), Semi-Indirect, Indirect. Memorize in this order — they form a sequence of decreasing downward flux.
- ⚡ **Mnemonic for Flux Direction:** **"D-S-D-S-I"** (Direct, Semi-Direct, Diffused, Semi-Indirect, Indirect). Downward flux decreases in this order: 100% → 90% → 60% → 40% → 10% → 0%.
- ⚡ **Direct lighting = highest efficiency, highest glare, lowest cost.**
- ⚡ **Indirect lighting = lowest efficiency, lowest glare, highest cost.**
- ⚡ **Diffused lighting = balanced scheme; best for residential and medical use.**
- ⚡ **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR):** Direct > Semi-Direct > Diffused > Semi-Indirect > Indirect.
- ⚡ **Master Design Formula:** $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times n \times UF \times MF}$ — this is the **most important equation** of Module 1.
- ⚡ **Always round N UP.** Never truncate.
- ⚡ **Luminance is cd/m² (perceived); Illuminance is lux (incident).**
- ⚡ **Inverse Square Law:** $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$ — applicable for point sources, NOT for linear/area sources.
- ⚡ **IES = Illuminating Engineering Society (now IESNA in North America, IEI in India for the Indian arm).**
- ⚡ **UF × MF** must always appear in the denominator; never multiply with E or A.
- ⚡ For **K-series KTU exam**: 14-mark questions are split 7+7. Always answer both sub-parts. Skipping one costs 7 marks instantly.
- ⚡ **Sketch Quality:** Neat flux-direction arrows, clear ceiling/work-plane reference lines, and labeled luminaire are the three essentials for full marks in 7-mark sketch questions.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Artificial Lighting Schemes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Lighting and Task Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Lighting and Task Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — General Lighting and Task Lighting

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 General Lighting — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **General Lighting (also called "Ambient Lighting")** is a uniformly distributed lighting scheme designed to provide a baseline level of illumination across an entire interior space **without regard to the location of any specific visual task**. It establishes the overall brightness, visual comfort, and aesthetic ambience of a room.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, general lighting is characterized by:

- **Uniformity Ratio (U)** : The ratio of minimum illuminance ($E_{min}$) to average illuminance ($E_{avg}$) across the working plane.
- **Illuminance Level (E)** : Measured in **lux (lx)** or **lumens per square meter (lm/m²)**.
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI, Ra)** : A measure (0–100) of how faithfully a light source reveals object colors.
- **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)** : Expressed in **Kelvin (K)**, indicating the warmth or coolness of light.

The governing standard for interior workplace lighting in India is **IS 3646 (Part 1)** and internationally the **CIE S 008/E:2001** standard, which KTU references in its syllabus.

### 1.2 Task Lighting — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Task Lighting** is a localized, directional, and high-contrast lighting arrangement specifically aimed at illuminating a **defined work area, workstation, or visual task** to enhance visibility, accuracy, productivity, and visual comfort.

Task lighting supplements general lighting and is typically:

- **Localized** over a desk, workbench, kitchen counter, or inspection station.
- **Adjustable** in direction and sometimes intensity.
- Designed to meet the **specific lux requirements** of the visual task (e.g., 500 lx for reading, 1000+ lx for precision electronics work).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Room & Spotlight" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a large classroom during the day. The sunlight streaming through the windows lights up the **entire room uniformly** — this is **General Lighting**. Now, the teacher switches on a focused reading lamp over a student's open book on the desk — that focused beam of light, concentrated only on the book, is **Task Lighting**.

The analogy extends:

| Real-Life Object | General Lighting Analogy | Task Lighting Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Classroom | Sunlight / ceiling tube-lights | Reading lamp on a desk |
| Kitchen | Ceiling LED panel | Under-cabinet LED strip over the chopping board |
| Factory floor | High-bay LED floods | Inspection lamp on a PCB assembly line |
| Hospital ward | Recessed ceiling luminaires | Bedside examination light |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$)** : **1 lumen (lm)** = luminous flux emitted into a solid angle of 1 steradian by a point source having a uniform intensity of **1 candela (cd)**.
- **Luminous Intensity (I)** : Measured in **candela (cd)**.
- **Illuminance (E)** : **1 lux (lx) = 1 lm/m²**.
- **Luminance (L)** : Measured in **candela per square meter (cd/m²)**.
- **Standard Reference Illuminance** : The human eye's comfortable working range is **300 – 750 lux** for general office work; **1500 – 3000 lux** for detailed/precision work.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Lighting Design Philosophy

Modern illumination engineering follows a **layered approach**, where general and task lighting occupy distinct tiers:

- **Layer 1 — Ambient (General) Lighting** : Establishes the global luminous environment.
- **Layer 2 — Task Lighting** : Provides localized, high-E zones where visual work occurs.
- **Layer 3 — Accent Lighting** : Aesthetic/architectural highlights (not in this topic's scope).

### 2.2 Design Principles Governing General Lighting

The structured logic for designing general lighting schemes:

- **Step 1 — Determine Visual Task Class** : Use **IS 3646** or **CIE** tables to identify the visual task category (e.g., "Casual reading," "Office work," "Inspection").
- **Step 2 — Assign Required Illuminance Level ($E_{req}$)** : Each task class has a **standard maintained illuminance** (e.g., 300 lx for general office).
- **Step 3 — Select Light Source** : Choose lamp type based on CRI, CCT, efficacy ($\eta$ in lm/W), and lamp life.
- **Step 4 — Compute Number of Luminaires (N)** using the **Lumen Method**:

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_L \times UF \times MF \times n}$$

where:
- $E$ = required illuminance (lux)
- $A$ = area of the working plane (m²)
- $\Phi_L$ = luminous flux per lamp (lumens)
- $UF$ = Utilization Factor (room index dependent)
- $MF$ = Maintenance Factor (typically 0.7 – 0.8)
- $n$ = number of lamps per luminaire

- **Step 5 — Verify Uniformity** : Ensure $U = \frac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}} \geq 0.7$ for general lighting (per **CIE** norms).

### 2.3 Design Principles Governing Task Lighting

Task lighting is engineered around **three core parameters**:

- **Task Contrast (C)** : The luminance ratio between an object and its immediate background. Higher task contrast → lower lux requirement.
- **Glare Control** : Task luminaires must shield the light source from the user's eye; **UGR (Unified Glare Rating) ≤ 19** for offices.
- **Task Area Illuminance ($E_{task}$)** : Usually **1.5× to 3×** the surrounding ambient level to create a perceptible brightness gradient that guides the eye.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Halo Effect":** Task lighting, when properly designed, creates a "halo" of brightness around the work zone. The eye is naturally drawn to brighter regions, so a well-lit task area subconsciously increases focus and reduces visual fatigue.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Intuition

- **Why Uniformity?** Visual discomfort and eye strain arise when the eye must continually adapt to brightness variations; uniformity stabilizes pupil response.
- **Why Maintenance Factor?** Lamps lose output (lumen depreciation) and luminaires collect dust (luminaire dirt depreciation); MF compensates for this real-world degradation.
- **Why Task-to-Ambient Ratio?** A **3:1 or less** task-to-ambient luminance ratio prevents "spotlight glare" where a bright pool of task light feels isolated against a dark room.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a **high-yield reference** for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). Memorize all units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | lux (lx) = lm/m² | $1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$ |
| 2 | Inverse Square Law | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ | lx, cd, m | Valid for point source, perpendicular incidence |
| 3 | Lambert's Cosine Law | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$ | lx | $\theta$ = angle of incidence to normal |
| 4 | Lumen Method | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot UF \cdot MF \cdot n}$ | dimensionless | $UF \in [0.4,\, 0.8]$; $MF \in [0.6,\, 0.9]$ |
| 5 | Room Index (k) | $k = \dfrac{L \cdot W}{H_m(L + W)}$ | dimensionless | $L, W$ = room dims; $H_m$ = mounting height above working plane |
| 6 | Uniformity Ratio | $U = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | $U \geq 0.7$ for general (CIE); $U \geq 0.5$ acceptable for task zones |
| 7 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | lm/W | Incandescent $\approx 15$; LED $\approx 100$–$180$ |
| 8 | Glare Index (simplified) | $GR = \sum \dfrac{L_s^{1.6} \cdot \omega^{0.8}}{L_b \cdot p^{1.6}}$ | dimensionless | $L_s$ = source luminance; $\omega$ = solid angle; $L_b$ = background luminance |
| 9 | Visual Comfort Probability | $VCP = f(GR, \text{position})$ | % | $VCP \geq 70$ for offices (IESNA) |
| 10 | Task/Ambient Ratio | $TAR = \dfrac{E_{task}}{E_{ambient}}$ | ratio | Recommended $TAR \leq 3:1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In KTU valuation, do **not** confuse the vertical bar `$\vert$` (absolute value / magnitude) with the markdown table delimiter `|`. In LaTeX, always write `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$`, never `|x|` inside table cells.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Code Compliance** : Indian **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** mandates lux levels & lighting power density (LPD in W/m²) for general lighting in commercial buildings.
- **Occupational Health** : Inadequate task lighting causes **Computer Vision Syndrome (CVS)**, **myopia progression**, and reduced productivity — a major HR/ergonomic concern in IT offices.
- **Industrial Safety** : Under-lit general areas in factories correlate with higher accident rates; IS 3646 mandates specific lux levels for hazardous zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lambert's Cosine Law (Foundation of General + Task Lighting)

**Starting Premise:** For a point source of luminous intensity $I$ candela located at distance $d$ from a surface element $dA$, the illuminance on the surface when light strikes at angle $\theta$ to the surface normal must be derived from first principles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Law of Solid Angle (Foundation Step):** A point source of intensity $I$ candela emits $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ lumens into a solid angle $\omega$ (in steradians, sr).

**Step 1 — Express the solid angle subtended by surface $dA$ at the source:**

The surface element $dA$ is tilted at angle $\theta$ from the incoming light ray. The projected area perpendicular to the light direction is:

$$dA_{\perp} = dA \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Convert projected area to solid angle:**

The solid angle subtended at distance $d$ is:

$$\omega = \frac{dA_{\perp}}{d^2} = \frac{dA \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of luminous flux:**

The flux intercepted by $dA$ equals the flux emitted by the source into the solid angle $\omega$:

$$\Phi = I \cdot \omega = I \cdot \frac{dA \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by area to get illuminance:**

By definition, $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{dA}$, so:

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

This is **Lambert's Cosine Law**, the cornerstone of illumination engineering. Every general-lighting layout (where ceiling fixtures shine downward at varying angles) and every task-light design (where a desk lamp is tilted) ultimately relies on this equation.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — General Lighting Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style numerical problem — modeled on past board patterns. Practice solving such problems within the 14-mark Part B structure.**

**Problem Statement:**
Design a general lighting scheme for a conference hall of dimensions $20\,\text{m} \times 10\,\text{m} \times 4\,\text{m}$ (length $\times$ width $\times$ height). Working plane is at $0.8\,\text{m}$ above floor. Required maintained illuminance $E = 500\,\text{lx}$. Each LED luminaire carries 4 lamps of $4000\,\text{lm}$ each. $UF = 0.6$, $MF = 0.8$.

**Step 1 — Compute Room Area:**

$$A = L \times W = 20 \times 10 = 200 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mounting Height above Working Plane:**

$$H_m = H_{ceiling} - H_{working} = 4 - 0.8 = 3.2 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Room Index (k):**

$$k = \frac{L \cdot W}{H_m \cdot (L + W)} = \frac{20 \cdot 10}{3.2 \cdot (20 + 10)} = \frac{200}{3.2 \cdot 30} = \frac{200}{96} \approx 2.08$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Luminous Flux Required:**

$$\Phi_{required} = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF} = \frac{500 \cdot 200}{0.6 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{100{,}000}{0.48} = 208{,}333 \, \text{lm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Flux per Luminaire:**

$$\Phi_{luminaire} = 4 \times 4000 = 16{,}000 \, \text{lm}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Number of Luminaires:**

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{required}}{\Phi_{luminaire}} = \frac{208{,}333}{16{,}000} \approx 13.02$$

**Step 7 — Round Up and Verify Layout:**

Round up to $N = 14$ luminaires. A practical layout: arrange in **2 rows × 7 columns** along the $20\,\text{m}$ length for uniform distribution.

**Step 8 — Spacing Verification:**

Spacing between luminaires along length = $20/7 \approx 2.86\,\text{m}$. Maximum allowable spacing per CIE for $H_m = 3.2\,\text{m}$ is $\approx 1.5 \cdot H_m = 4.8\,\text{m}$. Since $2.86 < 4.8$, the layout is valid for general lighting uniformity.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Task Lighting Design

**Problem Statement:**
A precision inspection workstation requires $E_{task} = 1500\,\text{lx}$. The task luminaire is mounted at $d = 0.5\,\text{m}$ above the work surface, tilted at $\theta = 30°$ from vertical. Lamp intensity $I = 375\,\text{cd}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Lambert's Cosine Law:**

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$E = \frac{375 \cdot \cos 30°}{(0.5)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\cos 30°$:**

$$\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**Step 4 — Final computation:**

$$E = \frac{375 \cdot 0.866}{0.25} = \frac{324.75}{0.25} = 1299 \, \text{lx}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with requirement:**

Since $1299 < 1500$, **the lamp does not meet the requirement**. Choose a more intense lamp or reduce distance:

If we use $d = 0.45\,\text{m}$ and the same $I$:

$$E = \frac{375 \cdot 0.866}{(0.45)^2} = \frac{324.75}{0.2025} \approx 1604 \, \text{lx} \;\; \checkmark$$

This satisfies the task requirement.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — General vs. Task Lighting

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU Part A question** (often worth 3 marks for direct comparison).

| Parameter | General Lighting | Task Lighting |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Purpose | Uniform ambient illumination | Localized high-contrast illumination |
| Coverage Area | Entire room / large zone | Specific workstation / task zone |
| Typical Lux Range | 100 – 500 lx | 500 – 3000+ lx |
| Source Distribution | Wide-beam, evenly spaced | Narrow-beam, focused |
| Directionality | Mostly downward / diffuse | Highly directional |
| Typical Fixtures | Troffers, panel lights, downlights | Desk lamps, under-cabinet LEDs, magnifier lamps |
| Energy Share (typical office) | ~70% of total lighting energy | ~30% of total lighting energy |
| Glare Consideration | UGR $\leq 19$ (offices) | Individual shield / hood |
| Color Rendering | CRI $\geq 80$ for general | CRI $\geq 90$ for color-critical tasks |
| Failure Impact | Mild visual discomfort | Severe task impairment / safety risk |
| Design Standard | IS 3646 / CIE S 008 | IS 3646 (task-specific annex) / IES RP-1 |
| Control Strategy | Centralized (on/off, dimming) | Individual / occupant-controlled |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision-tree architecture** a lighting designer follows when choosing between general and task lighting schemes:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lighting Requirement Initiated] --> B{Is there a defined visual task?}
    B -- No --> C[Deploy General Lighting Only]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is task area well-defined?}
    D -- No --> E[Hybrid Scheme: General + Zone Task]
    D -- Yes --> F[Deploy Task Lighting over workstation]
    C --> G[Compute N via Lumen Method]
    E --> H[General Layer + Localized Task Layer]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Verify Uniformity U >= 0.7]
    H --> J[Verify TAR <= 3 to 1]
    I --> K[Layout Luminaires in Grid]
    J --> L[Position Task Luminaire for Glare Control]
    K --> M[Final Commissioning and Lux Measurement]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Layered Lighting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Power Supply Mains 230V AC] --> DR[Driver or Ballast Unit]
    DR --> LS1[Light Source Array - LEDs]
    LS1 --> OPT[Optical Assembly Lens or Reflector]
    OPT --> ENV[Target Illuminated Environment]
    ENV --> WP1[General Working Plane]
    ENV --> WP2[Specific Task Zone]
    CTRL[Control System - Dimmers or Sensors] --> DR
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Side-by-Side Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GeneralLayer[GENERAL LIGHTING LAYER]
        G1[Multiple Ceiling Luminaires] --> G2[Uniform Distribution Grid]
        G2 --> G3[Working Plane E uniform]
    end
    subgraph TaskLayer[TASK LIGHTING LAYER]
        T1[Single Focused Luminaire] --> T2[Directional Beam]
        T2 --> T3[Task Zone E high local]
    end
    GeneralLayer --> Combined[Combined Visual Environment]
    TaskLayer --> Combined
```

## 4.4 Glare and Visual Comfort Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Compute Glare Index or UGR] --> R{UGR <= 19?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Acceptable for Office]
    R -- No --> T[Add Shielding or Diffuser]
    T --> Q
    S --> U[Verify Task to Ambient Ratio TAR]
    U --> V{TAR within 1 to 3?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Design Approved]
    V -- No --> X[Re-balance Source Positions]
    X --> U
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between General Lighting and Task Lighting. Mention any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | General Lighting | Task Lighting |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Uniform illumination across an entire area | Localized illumination over a specific work zone |
| Lux Level | 100 – 500 lx (typical) | 500 – 3000 lx (typical) |
| Coverage | Whole room | Specific workstation |
| Control | Centralized | Often individual |

**Applications:**
- **General Lighting** : Office ceiling panels, street lighting, factory high-bay floods, classroom tube-lights.
- **Task Lighting** : Desk reading lamp, surgical operation light, under-cabinet kitchen LED strip, PCB inspection lamp.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if both the distinction AND applications are clearly stated. Mere definitions without examples fetch only 2 marks.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Uniformity Ratio. State its recommended value for general lighting as per CIE standards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Uniformity Ratio ($U$) is the ratio of minimum illuminance to the average illuminance on the working plane.

$$U = \frac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$$

**Recommended value:** As per **CIE S 008/E:2001**, for general interior lighting, **$U \geq 0.7$**. For task areas, $U \geq 0.5$ is acceptable.

**Significance:** Higher uniformity reduces visual discomfort and pupil adaptation strain.

*(Full marks require the formula, the numeric value, and a brief statement of importance.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the design procedure for General Lighting of an office room using the Lumen Method. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Lumen Method (also called the **Lumen or Light Flux Method**) is the standard technique for designing general lighting installations. The step-by-step procedure is:

- **Step 1 — Determine Room Dimensions and Working Plane Height:** Measure $L$, $W$, and $H_{ceiling}$. Working plane height is typically $0.8\,\text{m}$ above floor for offices.
- **Step 2 — Identify Required Illuminance ($E$):** Refer to **IS 3646** or **CIE** standards. For general office work, $E = 300$–$500\,\text{lx}$.
- **Step 3 — Compute Room Index (k):** 
$$k = \frac{L \cdot W}{H_m(L+W)}$$
where $H_m$ = mounting height of luminaire above working plane.
- **Step 4 — Determine Utilization Factor (UF):** From the luminaire manufacturer's photometric data tables, using $k$ and the room reflectance values (ceiling, wall, floor).
- **Step 5 — Determine Maintenance Factor (MF):** Typically 0.7 – 0.8, accounting for lamp lumen depreciation and luminaire dirt accumulation.
- **Step 6 — Compute Number of Luminaires ($N$):**
$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot UF \cdot MF \cdot n}$$
where $\Phi_L$ = luminous flux per lamp and $n$ = number of lamps per luminaire.
- **Step 7 — Layout Luminaires:** Arrange on a grid ensuring spacing $\leq 1.5 \cdot H_m$ for acceptable uniformity.
- **Step 8 — Verify:** Check actual illuminance via point-by-point method if critical.

**(b) A workshop of $15\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m}$ with $4\,\text{m}$ ceiling height needs $E = 400\,\text{lx}$. Luminaires have 2 LED lamps each of $3000\,\text{lm}$. $UF = 0.55$, $MF = 0.75$. Working plane is $0.85\,\text{m}$ above floor. Calculate number of luminaires and suggest a layout. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$A = 15 \times 8 = 120 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mounting Height:**
$$H_m = 4 - 0.85 = 3.15 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Room Index:**
$$k = \frac{15 \cdot 8}{3.15 \cdot (15+8)} = \frac{120}{3.15 \cdot 23} = \frac{120}{72.45} \approx 1.66$$

**Step 4 — Flux per Luminaire:**
$$\Phi_{lum} = 2 \times 3000 = 6000 \, \text{lm}$$

**Step 5 — Number of Luminaires:**
$$N = \frac{400 \cdot 120}{6000 \cdot 0.55 \cdot 0.75} = \frac{48{,}000}{2475} \approx 19.39$$

Round up: **$N = 20$ luminaires**.

**Step 6 — Layout Suggestion:**
Arrange in **4 rows × 5 columns** (or 5 × 4). Spacing along 15 m length = $15/5 = 3\,\text{m}$; spacing along 8 m width = $8/4 = 2\,\text{m}$. Maximum recommended spacing $\approx 1.5 \cdot H_m = 1.5 \cdot 3.15 \approx 4.73\,\text{m}$. Since 3 m and 2 m both $< 4.73\,\text{m}$, uniformity criterion is satisfied.

**Final Answer:** 20 luminaires in a 4 × 5 grid layout.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the design considerations for Task Lighting with reference to visual comfort, glare control, and illuminance ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Task lighting design is governed by **ergonomic and photometric** considerations that differ significantly from general lighting:

- **Visual Comfort:** The eye should not need to constantly readapt between bright (task) and dim (ambient) zones. Hence the illuminance ratio between task and ambient must be controlled.
- **Illuminance Ratio (Task-to-Ambient Ratio, TAR):** The recommended TAR is between **1.5:1 and 3:1**. A TAR greater than 3:1 causes "spotlight fatigue" — the user feels the surrounding area uncomfortably dark.
- **Glare Control:** Task luminaires must shield the source from the user's direct line of sight. Common techniques include **baffles, hoods, diffusers, and asymmetric reflectors**. The **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** should be $\leq 19$ for office tasks.
- **Color Rendering:** For color-critical tasks (printing, textile inspection, medical diagnosis), CRI $\geq 90$ is mandated.
- **Position and Adjustability:** The luminaire should be positioned to avoid reflections on glossy surfaces (e.g., VDU screens) and ideally allow the user to adjust direction and intensity.
- **Heat and Flicker:** Modern LED task lights should be flicker-free (flicker percentage $< 5\%$) to avoid headaches and eye strain.
- **Luminance Contrast:** The task luminance should be higher than background by a perceptible margin (typically $\geq 1.5$ times) to attract visual focus naturally.

**(b) An engineering drawing office requires $E_{task} = 750\,\text{lx}$ at the work surface. A task luminaire is placed at $d = 0.6\,\text{m}$ above the surface, tilted at $\theta = 45°$ from the vertical. Determine the luminous intensity of the lamp required. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Use Lambert's Cosine Law:**
$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $I$:**
$$I = \frac{E \cdot d^2}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**
- $E = 750\,\text{lx}$
- $d = 0.6\,\text{m}$
- $\theta = 45°$, so $\cos 45° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$

**Step 4 — Compute $d^2$:**
$$d^2 = (0.6)^2 = 0.36 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**
$$I = \frac{750 \cdot 0.36}{0.7071} = \frac{270}{0.7071} \approx 381.86 \, \text{cd}$$

**Final Answer:** The task lamp must have a luminous intensity of approximately **$382\,\text{cd}$** in the direction of the work surface.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (read carefully before writing your exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting $H_m$ adjustment:** Students often use total ceiling height instead of mounting height above working plane. **This loses 2 marks.**
> 2. **Incorrect units in Lambert's Law:** Mixing up $d$ in cm vs. m. Always convert to metres before squaring.
> 3. **Omitting $n$ (lamps per luminaire):** A frequent omission in the Lumen Method formula; **costs 1 full mark.**
> 4. **Using $\theta$ from wrong reference:** Angle $\theta$ in Lambert's Law is measured from the **surface normal**, not from the surface itself.
> 5. **Rounding down $N$:** Always round **up** the number of luminaires; rounding down may give under-illuminance.
> 6. **Not stating the standards:** Always mention **IS 3646** or **CIE** when recommending lux levels — valuators appreciate code references.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **General Lighting** = uniform, ambient, room-wide; **Task Lighting** = localized, directional, work-zone specific.
- The **Lumen Method** formula $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot UF \cdot MF \cdot n}$ is the cornerstone of general-lighting design.
- **Lambert's Cosine Law** $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ governs both general and task illuminance calculations at an angle.
- Recommended **Uniformity Ratio** for general lighting is **$\geq 0.7$** (CIE).
- **Task-to-Ambient Ratio (TAR)** should lie between **1.5:1 and 3:1** for visual comfort.
- **UGR $\leq 19$** is the benchmark for office-task glare control.
- Standard illuminance: **300 – 500 lx** for general office, **750 – 1500 lx** for detailed tasks, **1500 – 3000+ lx** for precision tasks.
- **Maintenance Factor (0.7 – 0.8)** and **Utilization Factor (0.4 – 0.8)** are non-negotiable inputs in design calculations.
- **CRI $\geq 80$** for general lighting, **CRI $\geq 90$** for color-critical tasks.
- Always round the number of luminaires **up**; verify layout spacing $\leq 1.5 H_m$ for acceptable uniformity.
- Indian standard: **IS 3646 (Part 1)**; International: **CIE S 008/E:2001**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Areas of Usage of Such Lighting Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Areas of Usage of Such Lighting Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lighting Scheme** is a defined geometric and photometric arrangement of luminaires designed to distribute luminous flux in a controlled proportion between the **upper hemisphere** (ceiling/upper walls) and the **lower hemisphere** (working plane/lower walls) of an interior space. The classification of lighting schemes — as standardized by the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES)** and adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus — is based on the fraction of total luminous flux emitted by the luminaire above and below the horizontal plane passing through the luminaire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Lighting schemes are classified into five categories based on the upward-to-downward flux ratio (UFR). The selection of a scheme for any given area depends on task visibility, glare control, shadow suppression, aesthetic quality, and luminous efficacy of the installation.*

### 1.2 The Five Standard Lighting Schemes

| Scheme | Upward Flux (%) | Downward Flux (%) | Primary Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | 0 – 10 | 90 – 100 | Downward |
| **Semi-Direct** | 10 – 40 | 60 – 90 | Predominantly Downward |
| **General Diffuse** | 40 – 60 | 40 – 60 | Uniformly Both |
| **Semi-Indirect** | 60 – 90 | 10 – 40 | Predominantly Upward |
| **Indirect** | 90 – 100 | 0 – 10 | Upward |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact percentages may slightly differ between IES and CIE references. The KTU 2024 scheme uses the IES convention shown above.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a lighting scheme as a **watering can's spray pattern**:
- A **Direct** scheme is like pouring water straight down on a plant — efficient, focused, but creates hard "shadows" (dark spots).
- An **Indirect** scheme is like misting water upward so it falls naturally like rain — soft, glare-free, but a lot of water (energy) is "wasted" hitting the ceiling first.
- A **General Diffuse** scheme is like a gentle, even rainfall from a sprinkler — uniform and pleasant, but you need more total water (lumens) for the same ground wetness.

The "area of usage" simply answers the question: **For which indoor environment does each spray pattern work best?**

### 1.4 Why "Areas of Usage" Matters in KTU Examinations

The phrase "Areas of Usage of Such Lighting Schemes" is a frequently tested concept because it requires students to:
1. Recall the flux distribution of each scheme.
2. Map each scheme to a real-world application (industry, office, home, hospital, etc.).
3. Justify the selection based on physical reasoning (glare, shadows, efficiency).

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often frame the question as: *"Discuss the various lighting schemes with their areas of application."* — a classic 14-mark Module 1 question in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Each Lighting Scheme

**A. Direct Lighting Scheme**
- **Optical Principle:** The luminaire's reflector or optical assembly directs nearly all flux downward onto the working plane.
- **Why it works:** Maximum luminous flux reaches the task → highest **utilization factor (UF)**, typically 0.6 – 0.9.
- **Why it fails:** Creates harsh **glare**, strong **shadows**, and a "cave effect" (dark ceiling).
- **Energy Profile:** Most **energy-efficient** scheme for industrial task lighting.

**B. Semi-Direct Lighting Scheme**
- **Optical Principle:** 60–90% downward, 10–40% upward.
- **Why it works:** The small upward component softly illuminates the ceiling, reducing the "cave effect" without sacrificing much efficiency.
- **Why it's chosen:** Best compromise between efficiency and visual comfort in offices.

**C. General Diffuse Lighting Scheme**
- **Optical Principle:** Roughly equal upward and downward flux (40–60% each).
- **Why it works:** Produces almost **shadow-free** illumination with uniform luminance on all room surfaces.
- **Trade-off:** Lowest utilization factor (0.3 – 0.5) → requires more lumens → more energy.

**D. Semi-Indirect Lighting Scheme**
- **Optical Principle:** 60–90% upward, 10–40% downward.
- **Why it works:** Ceiling acts as a large secondary diffuser, producing soft, glare-free light.
- **Application logic:** Used in spaces where aesthetics and visual comfort outweigh efficiency.

**E. Indirect Lighting Scheme**
- **Optical Principle:** 90–100% flux directed to ceiling and upper walls, which then reflect down.
- **Why it works:** **Zero direct glare**; the luminaire itself is invisible to the eye.
- **Why it fails:** Lowest UF (0.2 – 0.4); requires high ceiling reflectance (ρ_ceiling ≥ 0.8).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Utilization Factor (UF)** | $UF = \dfrac{\text{Lumens received on working plane}}{\text{Total lumens emitted by source}}$ | dimensionless | Efficiency of light delivery to task |
| **Lumen Method** | $\Phi = \dfrac{E \times A}{UF \times MF}$ | lumens (lm) | Total flux required for given illuminance |
| **Maintenance Factor (MF)** | $MF = LLMF \times LSF \times RSMF \times BM$ | dimensionless | Accounts for lamp lumen depreciation, etc. |
| **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)** | $SHR = \dfrac{\text{Spacing between luminaires}}{\text{Mounting height above working plane}}$ | dimensionless | Determines uniformity |
| **Room Index (RI)** | $RI = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$ | dimensionless | Used in UF lookup tables |
| **Reflectance (ρ)** | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Reflected flux}}{\text{Incident flux}}$ | dimensionless | Surface property (0 to 1) |
| **Luminous Efficacy** | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | lm/W | Lumen per watt efficiency of source |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU problems, the **Lumen Method** equation $E = \dfrac{\Phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$ is the most frequently tested formula. Always solve for $\Phi$ (required lumens) when designing a scheme.

### 2.3 Decision Logic — How an Engineer Selects a Scheme

1. **Identify the Task Criticality** → Industrial inspection = Direct.
2. **Assess Glare Tolerance** → VDT screens in offices = Semi-direct or General diffuse.
3. **Evaluate Aesthetic Requirements** → Hotels, lobbies = Semi-indirect or Indirect.
4. **Check Ceiling Reflectance** → Low ceiling reflectance excludes Indirect.
5. **Compute Required Lumens** → Use Lumen Method; compare energy consumption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lighting scheme selection directly impacts:
- **Energy Star Rating** of buildings (BEE, ECBC compliance in India).
- **Worker productivity** in factories and offices (CIE 190-2010).
- **Visual comfort probability (VCP)** ratings.
- **Green Building certifications** (GRIHA, LEED-India).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lumen Method for Required Number of Luminaires

**Problem Framework:** A KTU-style numerical problem typically asks: *"A factory hall of dimensions 30 m × 15 m × 6 m (ceiling height) requires an illuminance of 200 lux. Using the direct lighting scheme with metal halide lamps of 400 W (luminous efficacy 80 lm/W), UF = 0.7, MF = 0.8, and a SHR of 1.2, determine the number and layout of luminaires."*

**Step 1 — Compute the total luminous flux required**

$$
\Phi_{required} = \dfrac{E \times A}{UF \times MF}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\Phi_{required} = \dfrac{200 \times (30 \times 15)}{0.7 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
\Phi_{required} = \dfrac{200 \times 450}{0.56} = \dfrac{90000}{0.56}
$$

$$
\Phi_{required} \approx 160714.3 \text{ lm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute flux per luminaire**

Lamp flux:

$$
\Phi_{lamp} = P \times \eta = 400 \times 80 = 32000 \text{ lm}
$$

Assuming **1 lamp per luminaire**:

$$
\Phi_{luminaire} = 32000 \text{ lm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute number of luminaires**

$$
N = \dfrac{\Phi_{required}}{\Phi_{luminaire}} = \dfrac{160714.3}{32000}
$$

$$
N \approx 5.02 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 6 \text{ luminaires}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Always round **up**, never down, to ensure design illuminance is met or exceeded.

**Step 4 — Layout using Spacing-to-Height Ratio**

Mounting height above working plane (working plane at 0.85 m):

$$
H_m = 6 - 0.85 = 5.15 \text{ m}
$$

Maximum spacing:

$$
S_{max} = SHR \times H_m = 1.2 \times 5.15 = 6.18 \text{ m}
$$

With 6 luminaires in a 30 m × 15 m hall:

$$
\text{Longitudinal spacing} = \dfrac{30}{3} = 10 \text{ m} \quad (\text{too large!})
$$

**Adjustment:** Increase to **2 rows × 5 columns = 10 luminaires** to satisfy spacing.

$$
\text{Row spacing} = \dfrac{15}{2} = 7.5 \text{ m} \quad (\text{still large!})
$$

**Final practical layout:** 3 rows × 5 columns = **15 luminaires**, giving spacings of 10 m × 5 m, with the 5 m spacing below the maximum of 6.18 m. ✓

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Scheme Selection by Application

| Application Area | Recommended Scheme | Key Justification | Typical Illuminance (lux) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heavy Industry (Foundry, Forge)** | Direct | High efficiency, shadow reveals defects | 200 – 300 |
| **Precision Assembly (Electronics)** | Direct / Semi-direct | Task accuracy demands strong contrast | 500 – 1000 |
| **General Office** | Semi-direct | Glare control on VDTs + ceiling brightness | 300 – 500 |
| **Conference Room** | General Diffuse | Uniform, shadow-free for video conferencing | 300 – 500 |
| **Hospital OT / ICU** | Indirect | Zero glare for surgeon visual acuity | 1000 – 2000 |
| **Hotel Lobby / Showroom** | Semi-indirect / Indirect | Aesthetics, decorative, soft ambience | 100 – 300 |
| **Classroom** | Semi-direct | Reduces blackboard glare, even illumination | 300 – 500 |
| **Domestic Living Room** | Indirect / Semi-indirect | Comfort, decorative appeal | 100 – 200 |
| **Sports Hall (Indoor)** | Direct | High flux for fast-moving objects | 500 – 750 |
| **Drawing Office / Drafting** | Semi-direct | Shadow-free, glare-controlled, accurate color | 750 – 1500 |
| **Warehouses** | Direct (high-bay) | Maximum efficiency at high mounting | 100 – 200 |
| **Cinema / Auditorium** | Indirect | Critical glare control for screen visibility | 50 – 100 |

### 3.3 Comparative Performance Index

Let us compute the **relative energy required** for a reference 1 m² working plane at 100 lux across schemes:

| Scheme | UF (typical) | Relative Lumens Required | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct | 0.8 | 125 (lowest) | 1 (most efficient) |
| Semi-direct | 0.65 | 154 | 2 |
| General Diffuse | 0.45 | 222 | 3 |
| Semi-indirect | 0.35 | 286 | 4 |
| Indirect | 0.25 | 400 (highest) | 5 (least efficient) |

The relative lumen requirement is computed as:

$$
\Phi_{rel} = \dfrac{100}{UF \times 1.0}
$$

(assuming MF = 1.0 for comparative analysis).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Luminaire Mounted] --> B{Light Distribution Direction}
    B --> C[90 to 100 percent Downward]
    B --> D[60 to 90 percent Downward]
    B --> E[40 to 60 percent Both Ways]
    B --> F[60 to 90 percent Upward]
    B --> G[90 to 100 percent Upward]
    C --> C1[DIRECT SCHEME]
    D --> D1[SEMI DIRECT SCHEME]
    E --> E1[GENERAL DIFFUSE SCHEME]
    F --> F1[SEMI INDIRECT SCHEME]
    G --> G1[INDIRECT SCHEME]
    C1 --> H1[Application Heavy Industry Warehouses Sports Halls]
    D1 --> H2[Application Offices Classrooms Drafting Rooms]
    E1 --> H3[Application Conference Rooms Hospitals Corridors]
    F1 --> H4[Application Hotel Lobbies Restaurants Lounges]
    G1 --> H5[Application Cinema Halls OT Rooms High End Residences]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Scheme Selection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Task Type] --> ENGINE
    INPUT2[Glare Tolerance] --> ENGINE
    INPUT3[Ceiling Reflectance] --> ENGINE
    INPUT4[Energy Budget] --> ENGINE
    INPUT5[Aesthetic Priority] --> ENGINE
    ENGINE{SCHEME SELECTION LOGIC}
    ENGINE --> OUT1[Industrial Use DIRECT]
    ENGINE --> OUT2[Office Use SEMI DIRECT]
    ENGINE --> OUT3[Commercial Use GENERAL DIFFUSE]
    ENGINE --> OUT4[Hospitality Use SEMI INDIRECT]
    ENGINE --> OUT5[Premium Comfort Use INDIRECT]
    OUT1 --> VAL[Validate via Lumen Method]
    OUT2 --> VAL
    OUT3 --> VAL
    OUT4 --> VAL
    OUT5 --> VAL
    VAL --> FINAL[Final Layout and Number of Luminaires]
```

### 4.3 Flux Distribution Schematic (Visual Summary)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[DIRECT 90 percent DOWN]:::dir --> WP1[Working Plane 100 percent]
    L2[SEMI DIRECT 75 percent DOWN]:::sd --> WP2[Working Plane]
    L2 --> CE2[Ceiling 25 percent]
    L3[GENERAL DIFFUSE 50 50]:::gd --> WP3[Working Plane]
    L3 --> CE3[Ceiling]
    L4[SEMI INDIRECT 75 percent UP]:::si --> CE4[Ceiling]
    L4 --> WP4[Working Plane 25 percent]
    L5[INDIRECT 100 percent UP]:::ind --> CE5[Ceiling Diffuser]
    classDef dir fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef sd fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#994C00,color:#000000
    classDef gd fill:#FFFFB3,stroke:#999900,color:#000000
    classDef si fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#004C99,color:#000000
    classDef ind fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
```

> [!TIP]
> For physical sketches in answer scripts, KTU examiners expect a **simple line diagram** showing the luminaire, downward arrows, upward arrows, and the working plane. The above Mermaid block is a logical substitute when digital notes are used.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by a "semi-direct" lighting scheme? Mention any two areas where it is commonly used.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A semi-direct lighting scheme is one in which **60% to 90% of the total luminous flux** is directed downward onto the working plane, while the remaining **10% to 40%** is directed upward to illuminate the ceiling. This scheme provides a good balance between high efficiency and reduced "cave effect."

**Two areas of usage:**
1. **General offices** — provides adequate task lighting with controlled glare on VDT screens.
2. **Classrooms** — illuminates blackboards uniformly without harsh shadows, while softly lighting the ceiling to reduce contrast fatigue.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 marks per area, and 1 mark for justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is the indirect lighting scheme preferred for cinema halls and operation theatres?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The indirect lighting scheme directs **90–100% of the flux upward** toward the ceiling, which then acts as a large secondary diffuser. This produces **completely glare-free, shadow-free, and uniformly diffused illumination** on the working plane.

**Reasons for preference:**
1. **Cinema halls:** Glare would wash out the projected image on the screen; indirect lighting keeps the luminaire itself invisible to the audience.
2. **Operation theatres:** Surgeons require sustained visual acuity under high illuminance; any direct glare from the luminaire would cause eye fatigue and compromise precision.

*(Award 1 mark for scheme characteristics, 1 mark for cinema justification, 1 mark for OT justification.)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)
