**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Classify the various lighting schemes used in interior illumination with neat sketches. **7 Marks**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **areas of application** of each scheme, justifying your choice with reference to efficiency, glare, and aesthetic considerations. **7 Marks**

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Classification **7 Marks**

The classification of interior lighting schemes is based on the **proportion of luminous flux** emitted in the **upward hemisphere** versus the **downward hemisphere** with respect to the horizontal plane through the luminaire.

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Direct Lighting Scheme:**
90–100% of flux is directed downward. Achieved using **reflectors, diffusers, or downlights**. Example: industrial high-bay luminaires.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Semi-Direct Lighting Scheme:**
60–90% downward, 10–40% upward. Achieved using partially transmitting diffusers. Reduces the dark-ceiling effect.

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — General Diffuse Scheme:**
40–60% in each direction. Achieved with opal glass or translucent bowls. Produces almost shadow-free light.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Semi-Indirect Scheme:**
10–40% downward, 60–90% upward. Ceiling becomes the primary light source.

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Indirect Scheme:**
90–100% upward. Luminaires hidden in coves or pelmets. Zero direct glare.

**Step 6 (2 Marks) — Sketch:**
Student should draw a luminaire with:
- A horizontal reference line.
- Arrows showing flux distribution.
- A working plane below.
- Percentages labeled clearly.

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Areas of Application **7 Marks**

| Scheme | Areas of Usage | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | Heavy industry, foundries, warehouses, indoor sports halls | Highest UF (0.6–0.9), maximum task illuminance, shadows help reveal 3D defects |
| **Semi-direct** | Offices, classrooms, drafting rooms, retail stores | Glare-controlled, good UF (0.5–0.7), ceiling softly lit |
| **General Diffuse** | Conference rooms, hospitals, corridors, laboratories | Shadow-free, uniform luminance, good for inspection tasks |
| **Semi-indirect** | Hotel lobbies, restaurants, lounges, jewelry showrooms | Soft, decorative, ceiling appears luminous |
| **Indirect** | Cinema halls, operation theatres, art galleries, executive cabins | Zero direct glare, premium aesthetic, ceiling is the primary emitter |

**Justification Framework (allocated 1 mark):**
The choice is governed by:
- **Glare index (CIE 117)** — must be below 19 for offices.
- **Utilization factor** — must be maximized to reduce energy.
- **Visual comfort probability (VCP)** — must exceed 70 for prolonged work.
- **Aesthetic and psychological needs** — particularly in hospitality.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **direct** and **indirect** lighting schemes. Compare their merits and demerits. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A workshop of size **20 m × 10 m × 5 m** requires an average illuminance of **300 lux** using **direct lighting** with fluorescent lamps. Given **UF = 0.6, MF = 0.75**, and each lamp provides **3000 lumens**, determine:
   1. The number of lamps required. **3 Marks**
   2. A suitable layout if the working plane is at 0.8 m. **4 Marks**

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Direct vs Indirect **7 Marks**

**Direct Lighting Scheme (3.5 marks):**
- 90–100% flux downward.
- **Merits:** Highest efficiency, strong shadows reveal surface defects, lower initial cost, high illuminance on task.
- **Demerits:** Harsh glare, dark ceilings cause "cave effect," poor aesthetics, eye fatigue in prolonged exposure.

**Indirect Lighting Scheme (3.5 marks):**
- 90–100% flux upward; ceiling is the secondary source.
- **Merits:** Zero direct glare, soft shadows, uniform luminance distribution, premium aesthetic, reduced eye strain.
- **Demerits:** Low UF (0.2–0.4), requires highly reflective ceiling (ρ ≥ 0.8), higher energy consumption, higher initial cost, lumens are "lost" in the double-reflection process.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Numerical **7 Marks**

**Part (1) — Number of Lamps (3 Marks):**

Using the Lumen Method:

$$
\Phi_{required} = \dfrac{E \times A}{UF \times MF}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\Phi_{required} = \dfrac{300 \times (20 \times 10)}{0.6 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{60000}{0.45} = 133333.3 \text{ lm}
$$

Number of lamps:

$$
N = \dfrac{133333.3}{3000} = 44.4 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 45 \text{ lamps}
$$

**Part (2) — Layout (4 Marks):**

Mounting height above working plane:

$$
H_m = 5 - 0.8 = 4.2 \text{ m}
$$

For fluorescent direct lighting, recommended **SHR ≈ 1.5**:

$$
S_{max} = 1.5 \times 4.2 = 6.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Trial Layout 1:** 5 rows × 9 columns = 45 lamps
- Longitudinal spacing = 20 / 9 ≈ 2.22 m ✓
- Row spacing = 10 / 5 = 2.0 m ✓
- Both spacings well within 6.3 m → **Layout is acceptable.**

**Final Answer:** 45 fluorescent lamps arranged in **5 rows × 9 columns**, with each row spaced 2.0 m apart and each column spaced 2.22 m apart, mounted at 4.2 m above the working plane.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** the number of lamps (always round to the next whole number; rounding down loses a mark).
> 2. **Using area instead of (Length × Width)** in the Lumen Method — students often write "300 × 30" instead of "300 × 200".
> 3. **Confusing SHR with spacing** — SHR is a ratio, not a distance.
> 4. **Mixing up semi-direct and semi-indirect** — remember: the **dominant** flux direction defines the scheme.
> 5. **Omitting justification** in Part B (b) — examiners specifically reward the *reasoning*, not just the answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **five lighting schemes** are classified by the **upward-to-downward flux ratio**.
- **Direct** is the most **efficient** (highest UF) but causes the most glare.
- **Indirect** is the most **comfortable** (zero glare) but least efficient.
- **Semi-direct** and **Semi-indirect** are the **compromise schemes** most widely used in modern interiors.
- **General Diffuse** is chosen for **shadow-free** environments like operating rooms and conference halls.
- The **Lumen Method** formula $E = \dfrac{\Phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$ is the cornerstone numerical formula of this module.
- **Industrial applications** → Direct; **Offices** → Semi-direct; **Hospitals/OT** → Indirect; **Hotels** → Semi-indirect.
- Always compute **Room Index** before consulting UF tables.
- **Ceiling reflectance** must exceed **0.7** for indirect and semi-indirect schemes to be viable.
- For KTU 14-mark answers, expect **3.5 marks for classification + 3.5 marks for areas + 4 marks for sketches/justification**, or similar splits.
- **MTBF and Maintenance Factor** are essential for real designs, not just theoretical calculations.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **"Direct → Industry, Semi-Direct → Office, Diffuse → Conference, Semi-Indirect → Lobby, Indirect → Cinema/OT"** mapping — this single line covers 70% of the "areas of usage" questions in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Lamp and Luminaire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of Lamp and Luminaire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Lamp

A **Lamp** is a source of optical radiation (light) that is produced as a result of the conversion of electrical energy into visible light. In the context of illumination engineering, a lamp is fundamentally a *replaceable light-generating component* that emits luminous flux, typically through processes such as **incandescence**, **gas discharge**, or **electroluminescence**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *lamp* (also called a *bulb* or *tube* in common parlance) is a device that produces light by the passage of electric current through a filament, gas, or semiconductor. The lamp itself is *only* the light-generating element — it does **not** include the housing, reflectors, or mounting hardware.

Mathematically, a lamp is characterized by its **luminous flux output** $\Phi_v$, measured in **lumens (lm)**. The efficiency of a lamp is given by its **luminous efficacy**:

$$\eta_{lamp} = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{input}} \quad \text{(in lm/W)}$$

where $P_{input}$ is the electrical power consumed by the lamp in **watts (W)**.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of a Luminaire

A **Luminaire** is a *complete lighting unit* consisting of one or more lamps, along with the components designed to distribute the light, position and protect the lamps, and connect them to the electrical supply. The term is internationally standardized under **IEC 60598** and **IS 1913**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A *luminaire* = **Lamp** + **Auxiliaries** (ballast, ignitor, capacitor, starter) + **Optical Components** (reflector, diffuser, refractor, lens) + **Mechanical Components** (housing, mounting, gasket) + **Electrical Components** (wiring, socket, switch).

In other words, a luminaire is the **engineered light fixture** as installed in a building, street, or industrial setting.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Car Headlight):**
>
> | Component | Car Analogy | Illumination Equivalent |
> |-----------|-------------|------------------------|
> | The bulb inside the headlight | The **engine** of the car | **Lamp** (light-generating core) |
> | The full headlight assembly (reflector, lens, casing, wiring) | The **entire car** | **Luminaire** (complete fixture) |
>
> Just as an *engine* alone cannot drive a car, a *lamp* alone cannot illuminate a room — it needs a *luminaire* to direct, protect, and connect it.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi_v$):** Total light emitted by a source, measured in **lumens (lm)**.
- **Luminous Intensity ($I_v$):** Flux per unit solid angle, in **candela (cd)**.
- **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$):** Lumens per watt, in **lm/W**.
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI / $R_a$):** A unitless measure from **0 to 100** indicating how accurately the lamp renders colors compared to a reference source.
- **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT):** Expressed in **Kelvin (K)**, indicating the "warmth" or "coolness" of the emitted light.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Luminaire

A luminaire is an engineered assembly comprising **five functional subsystems**. Understanding each subsystem is essential for KTU Module 1 questions.

#### 2.1.1 The Lamp (Light Source)
- The actual generator of photons.
- Can be **incandescent**, **fluorescent**, **HID (High-Intensity Discharge)**, or **LED (Light Emitting Diode)**.
- The lamp's performance dictates the luminaire's photometric output.

#### 2.1.2 Ballast / Driver
- A current-limiting device required by **gas discharge lamps** and **LEDs**.
- In fluorescent and HID lamps: an *electromagnetic* or *electronic ballast*.
- In LEDs: an *LED driver* (constant-current supply).
- **Purpose:** Prevents runaway current that would destroy the lamp.

#### 2.1.3 Optical System
- **Reflector:** Redirects light from the lamp towards the desired direction (specular, diffused, or mixed).
- **Diffuser:** Softens and spreads the light to reduce glare.
- **Refractor / Lens:** Controls the light distribution pattern (narrow beam, wide beam, asymmetric).
- **Louvre / Baffle:** Cuts off direct glare at certain viewing angles.

#### 2.1.4 Mechanical / Housing System
- **Body / Housing:** Protects internal components from dust, moisture, and mechanical impact. Rated by **IP (Ingress Protection)** codes.
- **Mounting Brackets:** Allow ceiling, wall, pole, or recessed installation.
- **Heat Sink (for LEDs):** Critical for thermal management; prolongs LED life.

#### 2.1.5 Electrical System
- **Lamp Holder / Socket:** Mechanically supports the lamp and provides electrical contact.
- **Wiring & Terminal Block:** Connects the luminaire to the mains supply.
- **Earthing Terminal:** Safety connection to prevent electric shock.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all key formulas** required for problems on this topic in the KTU ESE. Memorize the symbols and units.

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Description** |
|--------------|------------|-------------|----------|-----------------|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi_v$ | — | **lumen (lm)** | Total light energy emitted per second |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | $\eta = \Phi_v / P$ | **lm/W** | Efficiency of lamp/luminaire |
| Luminaire Efficiency | $\eta_{lum}$ | $\eta_{lum} = \dfrac{\Phi_{lum}}{\Phi_{lamp}} \times 100$ | **%** | Fraction of lamp flux delivered by luminaire |
| Luminous Intensity | $I_v$ | $I_v = d\Phi_v / d\Omega$ | **candela (cd)** | Flux per unit solid angle |
| Illuminance | $E_v$ | $E_v = \Phi_v / A$ | **lux (lx)** | Flux incident per unit area |
| Inverse Square Law | $E_v$ | $E_v = I_v / d^2$ | **lx** | Illuminance at distance $d$ from a point source |
| Luminance | $L_v$ | $L_v = I_v / (A \cos\theta)$ | **cd/m²** | Brightness as perceived by the eye |
| Solid Angle | $\Omega$ | $\Omega = A / r^2$ | **steradian (sr)** | 3-D angular measure |
| Lamp Life | $L_{70}$ | Hours to 70% flux | **hours (h)** | Useful life criterion |
| Utilance (UF) | $UF$ | $UF = \Phi_{utilized} / \Phi_{lum}$ | **dimensionless** | Fraction of luminaire flux reaching working plane |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Caution:** The vertical bar $\vert$ symbol has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in formulas where needed. Do not confuse **efficacy** (lm/W) with **efficiency** (%).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Luminaire Design

The lamp by itself radiates light **uniformly in all directions** (spherical emission). Without a luminaire:

1. **Light goes where it is not needed** → wasted energy.
2. **Glare is uncontrolled** → visual discomfort and accidents.
3. **The lamp is exposed to dust, moisture, and impact** → short life.
4. **No thermal management** → LEDs overheat and fail prematurely.

Thus, the luminaire exists to **control, protect, and optimize** the light output of the lamp for a specific application.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Street Lighting:** Asymmetric luminaires focus light onto the road, reducing upward light pollution.
- **Hospital Operating Theatres:** Shadowless luminaires use multiple reflectors and diffusers for uniform illumination.
- **Industrial High-Bay:** Heavy-duty IP65 luminaires with aluminum reflectors for warehouses and factories.
- **Architectural:** Recessed downlights with anti-glare baffles for offices and hotels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Luminaire Efficiency

The **Luminaire Efficiency** ($\eta_{lum}$) is the single most important KTU-asked formula in this topic. We derive it rigorously below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** Not all the light produced by a lamp emerges from the luminaire. Some light is absorbed by the reflector, diffuser, and housing. The ratio of the light *output* of the luminaire to the light *output* of the bare lamp defines the luminaire efficiency.

**Step 1:** Let the total luminous flux emitted by the bare lamp be $\Phi_{lamp}$ (in lumens). This is the flux the lamp produces in a free-air test, measured using an **integrating sphere**.

**Step 2:** When the same lamp is installed inside a luminaire (reflector + diffuser + housing), the flux emerging from the luminaire is $\Phi_{lum}$. Due to absorption, scattering, and occlusion:

$$\Phi_{lum} \leq \Phi_{lamp}$$

**Step 3:** By definition, luminaire efficiency is the ratio of these two fluxes, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_{lum} = \frac{\Phi_{lum}}{\Phi_{lamp}} \times 100 \quad \text{(\%)}$$

**Step 4:** Expressing in words: *Luminaire efficiency* is the percentage of the bare lamp's luminous flux that is actually delivered by the complete luminaire to the surrounding space.

**Step 5 (Typical Values):**
- A bare **incandescent lamp** in open air: $\eta_{lum} = 100\%$ (no luminaire).
- A **fluorescent luminaire** with acrylic diffuser: $\eta_{lum} \approx 60\% \text{ to } 75\%$.
- An **LED downlight** with anti-glare baffle: $\eta_{lum} \approx 70\% \text{ to } 85\%$.
- A **high-bay industrial luminaire** with aluminum reflector: $\eta_{lum} \approx 80\% \text{ to } 90\%$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked for luminaire efficiency, students often confuse it with **luminous efficacy** (lm/W). Remember: *Efficiency* is a **percentage** (ratio, unitless × 100); *Efficacy* is in **lm/W**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Inverse-Square Law for a Point Source

> [!NOTE]
> The inverse-square law is the foundation for all illumination calculations and is a common Part B question in KTU ESE.

**Step 1:** A point source emits a total luminous flux $\Phi_v$ uniformly in all directions, i.e., over a solid angle of $4\pi$ steradians.

**Step 2:** The luminous intensity $I_v$ (candela) is flux per unit solid angle:

$$I_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3:** At a distance $d$ from the source, this flux passes through a spherical surface of area $A = 4\pi d^2$.

**Step 4:** The illuminance $E_v$ is flux per unit area on this sphere:

$$E_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{I_v}{d^2}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$E_v = \frac{I_v}{d^2} \quad \text{(in lux, when } I_v \text{ is in cd and } d \text{ is in metres)}$$

> [!TIP]
> This equation is valid **only for point sources** and for surfaces **perpendicular** to the direction of light. For tilted surfaces, the cosine law must be applied: $E_v = \dfrac{I_v \cos\theta}{d^2}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Lamp vs. Luminaire

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison table is a **high-frequency KTU question** (asked as a 3-mark or 7-mark sub-part).

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Lamp** | **Luminaire** |
|-----------|---------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Definition | Light-generating device only | Complete light fixture including lamp and accessories |
| 2 | Composition | Filament / gas / LED chip | Lamp + ballast + reflector + diffuser + housing + wiring |
| 3 | Function | Converts electrical energy to light | Distributes, controls, and protects the light |
| 4 | Replaceable? | **Yes** (designed to be replaced) | **No** (fixed installation) |
| 5 | Photometric Property | Luminous flux (lm), efficacy (lm/W) | Luminaire efficiency (%), light distribution pattern |
| 6 | Example | A 60 W incandescent bulb | A ceiling-mounted light fixture with reflector and shade |
| 7 | Standards | IS 9974, IEC 60064 | IS 1913, IEC 60598 |
| 8 | Cost | Lower (per unit) | Higher (assembly cost) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Luminaire

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a luminaire, showing how the lamp interacts with the auxiliary, optical, mechanical, and electrical subsystems. This is the standard block diagram expected in KTU Module 1 answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mains Supply 230V AC] --> B[Electrical Subsystem]
    B --> C[Wiring and Terminal Block]
    C --> D[Lamp Holder Socket]
    D --> E[Lamp Light Source]
    E --> F[Optical Subsystem]
    F --> G[Reflector]
    F --> H[Diffuser or Refractor]
    F --> I[Louvre or Baffle]
    G --> J[Controlled Light Output]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    D --> K[Ballast or LED Driver]
    K --> E
    B --> K
    L[Mechanical Subsystem] --> M[Housing and Mounting Bracket]
    M --> N[Heat Sink for LED]
    M --> O[IP Rated Enclosure]
    E --> N
    O --> P[Complete Luminaire Unit]
    N --> P
    M --> P
    F --> P
    B --> P
    J --> P
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Light Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Light Sources Lamps] --> I[Incandescent]
    S --> D[Discharge Lamps]
    S --> L[LED Solid State]
    I --> I1[GLS General Lighting Service]
    I --> I2[Tungsten Halogen]
    I --> I3[Carbon Arc Historical]
    D --> D1[Low Pressure Discharge]
    D --> D2[High Pressure Discharge]
    D1 --> F1[Fluorescent Tube]
    D1 --> F2[Sodium Vapour Low Pressure]
    D2 --> H1[Mercury Vapour HPMV]
    D2 --> H2[Sodium Vapour HPSV]
    D2 --> H3[Metal Halide MH]
    L --> L1[Standard LED Package]
    L --> L2[COB Chip on Board LED]
    L --> L3[OLED Organic LED]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Bare Lamp → Luminaire → Illuminated Surface

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Bare Lamp Filament or Arc] -->|Produces Phi Lamp lumens| B[Optical Control Reflector Diffuser]
    B -->|Absorption Loss 10 to 40 percent| C[Controlled Light Output Phi Luminaire]
    C -->|Inverse Square and Cosine Law| D[Work Plane Illuminance E in lux]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#B0E0E6
    style C fill:#98FB98
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers must be crisp and to-the-point.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a lamp. List any four types of lamps used in illumination engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *lamp* is a device that converts electrical energy into light through processes such as incandescence, gas discharge, or electroluminescence. It is the light-generating component of a lighting system.

**Four types of lamps (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Incandescent lamp** (e.g., GLS bulb)
2. **Fluorescent lamp** (low-pressure mercury discharge)
3. **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) lamp** (e.g., HPSV, Metal Halide)
4. **LED (Light Emitting Diode)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "luminaire". State its main functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *luminaire* is a complete lighting unit consisting of one or more lamps together with the components designed to distribute the light, position and protect the lamps, and connect them to the electrical supply (as per IS 1913 / IEC 60598).

**Main Functions (2 marks — 1 mark per function pair):**

1. **Optical Function:** To direct and distribute the light from the lamp to the area to be illuminated, using reflectors, refractors, and diffusers.
2. **Mechanical Function:** To provide physical support, protection from dust/moisture/impact (IP rating), and heat dissipation.
3. **Electrical Function:** To provide a safe connection to the supply through wiring, sockets, ballasts/drivers, and earthing.
4. **Aesthetic Function:** To blend with the architectural/decorative requirements of the installation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** each, typically split as (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. You must attempt **one full question** out of the choice provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the main components of a luminaire and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Define *luminaire efficiency*. A fluorescent luminaire uses a 40 W lamp having a luminous flux of 3000 lumens. The luminaire delivers 2400 lumens to the working plane. Calculate the luminaire efficiency and the overall luminous efficacy of the luminaire. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A:

**(a) Components of a Luminaire (7 marks):**

A luminaire consists of the following five subsystems:

| **Subsystem** | **Components** | **Function** |
|---------------|----------------|--------------|
| 1. Lamp (Light Source) | Filament / Discharge tube / LED chip | Converts electrical energy into light (1 mark) |
| 2. Optical System | Reflector, Diffuser, Refractor, Louvre | Controls the direction, spread, and glare of emitted light (1.5 marks) |
| 3. Electrical System | Wiring, Terminal block, Socket, Ballast/Driver | Provides safe electrical connection and current limiting (1.5 marks) |
| 4. Mechanical System | Housing, Mounting bracket, Heat sink, Gaskets | Provides physical support, IP protection, and thermal management (1.5 marks) |
| 5. Auxiliary | Capacitor, Ignitor, Starter | Aids lamp starting and power factor correction (1.5 marks) |

*(The block diagram from SECTION 4.1 can be reproduced in the answer sheet for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Luminaire Efficiency & Efficacy Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Lamp power: $P = 40 \text{ W}$
- Lamp luminous flux: $\Phi_{lamp} = 3000 \text{ lm}$
- Luminaire output flux: $\Phi_{lum} = 2400 \text{ lm}$

**Step 1: Calculate Luminaire Efficiency (3 marks)**

Using the formula:

$$\eta_{lum} = \frac{\Phi_{lum}}{\Phi_{lamp}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{lum} = \frac{2400}{3000} \times 100 = 80 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Luminous Efficacy of the Luminaire (4 marks)**

$$\eta_{eff} = \frac{\Phi_{lum}}{P_{input}}$$

$$\eta_{eff} = \frac{2400 \text{ lm}}{40 \text{ W}} = 60 \text{ lm/W}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Luminaire Efficiency = **80%**
- Luminous Efficacy = **60 lm/W**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Examiner Pitfalls):**
> 1. Confusing *luminaire efficiency* (%) with *luminous efficacy* (lm/W). KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. Using the lamp flux (3000 lm) instead of luminaire output (2400 lm) in the efficacy calculation.
> 3. Forgetting the **× 100** in the efficiency formula, giving 0.8 instead of 80%.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
**(a)** Differentiate clearly between a *lamp* and a *luminaire*. Why is a luminaire considered an engineered assembly rather than just a light source? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 100 W incandescent lamp produces 1500 lumens. It is fitted in a luminaire that delivers 1200 lumens to the room. Calculate the (i) luminaire efficiency, (ii) luminous efficacy of the bare lamp, and (iii) overall luminous efficacy of the luminaire. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B:

**(a) Difference Between Lamp and Luminaire (7 marks):**

A tabular comparison must be presented (any **5 distinct points**, 1.4 marks each):

| **S.No.** | **Lamp** | **Luminaire** |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | A bare light-generating device | A complete engineered light fixture |
| 2 | Consists only of filament/gas/LED | Consists of lamp + reflector + ballast + housing + wiring |
| 3 | Emits light uniformly in all directions | Directs and controls light to a specific area |
| 4 | Replaceable consumable | Fixed installation; not designed for frequent replacement |
| 5 | Rated by wattage and luminous efficacy (lm/W) | Rated by luminaire efficiency (%) and light distribution curve |
| 6 | Standards: IS 9974, IEC 60064 | Standards: IS 1913, IEC 60598 |

**Why a luminaire is an "engineered assembly" (2 marks — bundled in the 7):**

A lamp on its own is *incomplete* for practical illumination because:
- It produces **uncontrolled, omnidirectional light** leading to glare and wastage.
- It has **no protection** from dust, moisture, or impact.
- It has **no heat dissipation** mechanism (critical for LEDs).
- It has **no safe electrical termination** for high-voltage mains.
- It cannot **conform to photometric design standards** without controlled optics.

A luminaire integrates the lamp with carefully designed optical, mechanical, electrical, and thermal subsystems — hence it is a **fully engineered assembly**.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Lamp wattage: $P = 100 \text{ W}$
- Lamp flux: $\Phi_{lamp} = 1500 \text{ lm}$
- Luminaire output flux: $\Phi_{lum} = 1200 \text{ lm}$

**(i) Luminaire Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{lum} = \frac{1200}{1500} \times 100 = 80 \text{ \%}$$

**(ii) Luminous Efficacy of the Bare Lamp (2.5 marks):**

$$\eta_{lamp} = \frac{\Phi_{lamp}}{P} = \frac{1500}{100} = 15 \text{ lm/W}$$

**(iii) Overall Luminous Efficacy of the Luminaire (2.5 marks):**

$$\eta_{eff,overall} = \frac{\Phi_{lum}}{P} = \frac{1200}{100} = 12 \text{ lm/W}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Luminaire Efficiency = **80%**
- (ii) Lamp Efficacy = **15 lm/W**
- (iii) Luminaire Efficacy = **12 lm/W**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Examiner Pitfalls):**
> 1. Students often forget to convert percentage in (i) and write 0.8.
> 2. In (iii), the *overall* efficacy uses the **same wattage** (100 W) but the **luminaire flux** (1200 lm), not the lamp flux.
> 3. Unit errors: writing "lm" instead of "lm/W" for efficacy costs **½ mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Lamp** = *only* the light source (bulb/tube/LED chip). Replaceable. Rated in watts and lumens.
- **Luminaire** = *complete* light fixture: **Lamp + Auxiliaries + Optics + Mechanics + Electricals**.
- A luminaire has **five subsystems**: lamp, optical (reflector/diffuser), electrical (ballast/wiring), mechanical (housing/heat sink), auxiliary (capacitor/ignitor).
- **Luminaire Efficiency** $\eta_{lum} = \dfrac{\Phi_{lum}}{\Phi_{lamp}} \times 100$ — expressed in **%**.
- **Luminous Efficacy** $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ — expressed in **lm/W**. Do NOT confuse with efficiency.
- **Inverse Square Law**: $E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^2}$ — valid for point sources only.
- **Cosine Law**: $E_v = \dfrac{I_v \cos\theta}{d^2}$ — for tilted surfaces.
- **Standards to remember**: IS 1913 (luminaires), IS 9974 (lamps), IEC 60598 (luminaires international).
- **Typical luminaire efficiency ranges**: 60–75% (fluorescent), 70–85% (LED), 80–90% (high-bay industrial).
- **CRI** is unitless (0–100); **CCT** is in **Kelvin (K)**; **Flux** is in **lumens (lm)**.
- **IP rating** of the luminaire housing indicates dust and water protection (e.g., IP65 = dust-tight + water-jet protected).
- A lamp alone **cannot illuminate a room** properly — it needs the engineered luminaire to direct, protect, and control the light.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of Incandescent and Halogen Lamps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Working of Incandescent and Halogen Lamps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Incandescent Lamp — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **incandescent lamp** is a temperature-radiant artificial light source in which an electric current is passed through a thin, high-melting-point metallic filament (conventionally **tungsten, W**, melting point **3422 °C**), heating it to a typical operating temperature of **2500 K – 3000 K**. At this temperature, the filament emits visible light as a result of **thermal (blackbody) radiation** governed by the **Stefan–Boltzmann** and **Planck's laws** of radiation.

The filament is housed inside a sealed **glass envelope** (bulb) that is either **evacuated** (vacuum lamps, ≤ 40 W) or back-filled with an **inert gas** (Argon / Nitrogen / Krypton, gas-filled lamps, ≥ 40 W) to suppress the rate of tungsten evaporation and prolong lamp life.

### 1.2 Halogen Lamp — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **tungsten-halogen lamp** (commonly called a **halogen lamp**) is an advanced variant of the incandescent lamp in which a small quantity of a **halogen gas** (Iodine, I₂, or Bromine, Br₂) is added to the inert filler gas inside a **fused-quartz envelope**. The halogen enables a closed-loop **regenerative chemical cycle** (the **Halogen Cycle**) that redeposits evaporated tungsten back onto the filament, permitting higher filament temperatures, longer life, and superior luminous efficacy.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Incandescent Lamp = "Heated Iron Rod"**
> Imagine heating an iron rod in a blacksmith's furnace. As the rod gets hotter, it first glows dull red, then bright orange, then nearly white. A tungsten filament does exactly the same thing — but in milliseconds and inside a sealed bulb. The hotter the filament, the more its radiation shifts into the visible band. The incandescent lamp is essentially a *controlled, electrically heated blackbody*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Halogen Cycle = "Sweeper on a Dirty Floor"**
> Picture a busy hallway where dust (tungsten vapour) constantly falls off a hot ceiling (filament). In an ordinary bulb, the dust settles on the cold bulb wall and permanently blackens it. In a halogen bulb, a chemical "sweeper" (iodine/bromine vapour) catches the falling dust and carries it back to the ceiling, where the heat breaks the bond and re-deposits the tungsten. The bulb stays clean, and the filament stays intact much longer.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Design Metrics

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |
| Wien's displacement constant | $b$ | **2.898 × 10⁻³** | m·K |
| Tungsten melting point | $T_{m,W}$ | **3422** | °C |
| Typical filament temperature (incandescent) | $T_f$ | **2500 – 3000** | K |
| Typical filament temperature (halogen) | $T_f$ | **3000 – 3200** | K |
| Bulb-wall temperature (halogen) | $T_w$ | **> 600** | °C |
| Halogen gas (low-pressure) | I₂ / Br₂ | trace | — |
| Filler gas | Ar / Kr / N₂ | 1 – 8 atm | atm |
| Efficacy (incandescent) | $\eta$ | **10 – 18** | lm/W |
| Efficacy (halogen) | $\eta$ | **15 – 25** | lm/W |
| Average life (incandescent) | L | **~ 1000** | hours |
| Average life (halogen) | L | **2000 – 4000** | hours |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of the Incandescent Lamp

- **Step 1 — Power input:** Mains voltage is applied across the two lead-in wires terminating at the **tungsten filament**.
- **Step 2 — Joule heating:** Current $I$ flowing through filament resistance $R_f$ dissipates $P = I^2 R_f$ watts, raising filament temperature.
- **Step 3 — Thermal radiation:** The hot filament behaves as a near-**blackbody radiator**. The total radiant exitance is governed by **Stefan–Boltzmann law**.
- **Step 4 — Visible emission:** At $\sim$ 2800 K, a significant fraction of the radiated spectrum lies in the **380 – 780 nm** visible band.
- **Step 5 — Filament design:** The filament is wound as a **coiled coil** to (a) increase the emitting surface area within a compact length and (b) reduce convective heat losses via the inert gas.
- **Step 6 — Failure mode:** Tungsten evaporates continuously; the filament eventually thins at a hot spot and breaks — end of lamp life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why inert gas?** Tungsten evaporation rate follows a Langmuir-type exponential in temperature. Even a partial-pressure of Argon (~ 80 kPa) reduces the **evaporation rate by ~ 10×** by blocking evaporating W atoms (the **Langmuir sheath effect**).

### 2.2 Why Tungsten? — A Key Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Tungsten is chosen because it has the **highest melting point of all metals (3422 °C)** and a very **low vapour pressure** at incandescent operating temperatures, allowing it to be heated to brilliance without rapid disintegration.

### 2.3 Working Principle of the Halogen Lamp

The **Halogen Regenerative Cycle** operates in four steps:

- **Step 1 — Evaporation:** Tungsten atoms evaporate from the hot filament ($\sim$ 3200 K) and diffuse outward into the cooler bulb region.
- **Step 2 — Halide formation:** At the lower bulb-wall temperature ($>$ 600 K but $<$ filament temperature), the W atoms chemically combine with halogen vapour to form a **tungsten halide** (e.g., $\text{WI}_2$ or $\text{WBr}_2$).
- **Step 3 — Convection drift:** The volatile halide remains gaseous and is carried back toward the filament by natural convection currents.
- **Step 4 — Dissociation & redeposition:** Near the filament ($\sim$ 3200 K), the halide is **thermodynamically unstable** and dissociates, re-depositing tungsten on the filament and releasing free halogen to repeat the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cycle is **endothermic** in the reverse direction (filament side) and **exothermic** in the forward direction (bulb-wall side). The result is **zero net loss of filament material** during normal operation, and the bulb wall remains crystal-clear.

### 2.4 Engineering Necessities of the Halogen Cycle

- **Quartz envelope** is mandatory because ordinary soft glass would melt at the required bulb-wall temperature ($>$ 600 °C). Fused quartz can withstand up to $\sim$ 1200 °C.
- **Compact bulb size** keeps the wall-to-filament distance small, raising wall temperature and accelerating the cycle.
- **Halogen partial pressure** must be precisely controlled — too little and the cycle stalls; too much and self-absorption losses rise.
- **Horizontal burning** is preferred to maintain uniform wall temperature; tilted operation can shorten life by 50 %.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units | Validity / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann Law — total radiant exitance of a blackbody | $\sigma$ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴, $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | Emitted power per unit area |
| $M_{\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1}$ | Planck's spectral distribution | $h$ = Planck const, $c$ = speed of light, $k$ = Boltzmann const, $\lambda$ = wavelength | Spectral radiance vs. $\lambda$ |
| $\lambda_m T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | **Wien's Displacement Law** — peak emission wavelength | $\lambda_m$ = peak wavelength (m), $T$ = temperature (K) | Locates peak of Planck curve |
| $\eta_{\text{lamp}} = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Luminous efficacy (performance metric) | $\Phi$ = luminous flux (lm), $P$ = power (W) | $\eta$ in lm/W |
| $L \propto V^{-n}$ | Lamp life vs. applied voltage | $L$ = life (h), $V$ = voltage (V), $n \approx 12$ to $14$ | Higher $V$ drastically shortens life |
| $\Phi \propto V^{3.5}$ | Luminous flux vs. voltage | $V$ = supply voltage | Empirical, KTU standard |
| $P \propto V^{1.6}$ | Power vs. voltage | $V$ = supply voltage | Empirical, KTU standard |
| $\eta_{v} \propto V^{1.9}$ | Efficacy vs. voltage | $V$ = supply voltage | Derived from above two |
| $R_f = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$ | Filament hot resistance | $R_0$ = cold resistance, $\alpha$ = temp. coeff. | Cold-to-hot ratio $\sim$ 1:12 for W |
| $K = \dfrac{\Phi_V}{\Phi_e} = 683 \dfrac{\text{lm}}{\text{W}} \cdot V(\lambda)$ | Luminous efficacy of radiation (K) | $V(\lambda)$ = photopic luminous efficiency | $K$ = 683 lm/W at 555 nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - The **Stefan–Boltzmann** law sets the upper bound on how much power an incandescent source can convert to radiation.
> - **Wien's law** is used by lamp designers to choose filament temperature such that $\lambda_m$ falls inside the visible band (380 – 780 nm).
> - The **lamp-life vs. voltage** relations are critical for KTU design calculations on over-voltage / under-voltage operation and for protective relay coordination.

### 2.6 Voltage Variation Effects (Numerically Critical for KTU)

If a 230 V incandescent lamp is operated at $V$:
- **Power consumed** scales as $P = P_{rated} (V/230)^{1.6}$.
- **Light output** scales as $\Phi = \Phi_{rated} (V/230)^{3.5}$.
- **Lamp life** scales as $L = L_{rated} (V/230)^{-13}$ (taking $n=13$).
- **Luminous efficacy** scales as $\eta = \eta_{rated} (V/230)^{1.9}$.

> [!WARNING]
> A **5 % over-voltage** (e.g., 241 V on a 230 V lamp) reduces lamp life by **≈ 50 %** but boosts light output by only **≈ 19 %**. This is a frequently tested KTU numerical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Lamp Radiation Basis)

Starting from **Planck's spectral radiance** for a blackbody cavity:

$$
B_{\lambda}(T) = \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c / (\lambda k T)} - 1}
$$

**Step 1 — Total exitance is the integral over all wavelengths:**

$$
E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \pi B_{\lambda}(T) \, d\lambda
$$

The factor $\pi$ accounts for hemispherical emission from a Lambertian surface.

**Step 2 — Substitute Planck's formula:**

$$
E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c / (\lambda k T)} - 1} \, d\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the change of variable** $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k T}$, so $d\lambda = -\dfrac{h c}{k T x^{2}} \, dx$:

$$
E = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{(h c / k T)^{4}} \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the standard integral** $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x}-1} dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$.

$$
E = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2} (k T)^{4}}{h^{4} c^{4}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot T^{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Stefan–Boltzmann constant:**

$$
\sigma \equiv \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670374419 \times 10^{-8} \;\; \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}
$$

**Step 6 — Final form:**

$$
E = \sigma T^{4}
$$

This is the **fundamental thermal-radiation equation** that determines the upper limit of radiant power output of any incandescent / halogen lamp filament.

### 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law

**Step 1 — Differentiate Planck's spectral radiance with respect to $\lambda$** and equate to zero to find the peak:

$$
\frac{d B_{\lambda}}{d \lambda} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — This transcendental equation reduces to:**

$$
5 (1 - e^{-x}) = x \quad \text{where} \quad x = \frac{h c}{\lambda_m k T}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical solution** gives $x \approx 4.9651$.

**Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda_m$:**

$$
\lambda_m T = \frac{h c}{k \cdot 4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m·K}
$$

**Result:** $\lambda_m = \dfrac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T}$ metres.

**Worked example (KTU style):** For a 100 W incandescent lamp with $T = 2856$ K:

$$
\lambda_m = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2856} = 1.015 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{m} = 1015 \;\; \text{nm}
$$

This lies in the **near-infrared**, which is why incandescent lamps are notorious for wasting $\sim$ 90 % of their input energy as IR heat.

### 3.3 Derivation of Filament Hot-to-Cold Resistance Ratio

**Step 1 — Tungsten resistivity at room temperature ($T_0 = 300$ K):** $\rho_0 = 5.65 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\text{·m}$.

**Step 2 — Tungsten temperature coefficient:** $\alpha \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — Filament temperature when lamp is ON:** $T_h = 2800$ K.

**Step 4 — Apply the linear-resistance model:**

$$
R_h = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T_h - T_0)\right] = R_0 \left[1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2500\right] = R_0 \times 12.25
$$

**Result:** $R_h \approx 12.25 \, R_0$.

This is why an incandescent lamp draws an **inrush current ~ 12× its steady-state value** at switch-on — a key KTU design consideration for MCB / fuse rating.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Incandescent vs. Halogen Lamp

| Parameter | Incandescent Lamp | Halogen Lamp |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Pure thermal (blackbody) radiation | Thermal radiation + regenerative halogen cycle |
| Filament | Tungsten, coiled-coil | Tungsten, coiled-coil (more compact) |
| Filament temperature | 2500 – 3000 K | 3000 – 3200 K |
| Bulb material | Soft soda-lime glass | **Fused quartz** (high melting point) |
| Bulb size | Large (heat dissipation area) | **Very small** (wall must stay hot) |
| Gas filling | Argon / Nitrogen (0.8 – 1 atm) | Ar / Kr + trace **I₂ or Br₂** (several atm) |
| Bulb-wall temperature | 40 – 100 °C | **600 – 900 °C** (required for cycle) |
| Luminous efficacy | 10 – 18 lm/W | **15 – 25 lm/W** |
| Average life | 1000 h | **2000 – 4000 h** |
| Blackening of bulb | Yes (tungsten deposits) | **No** (cycle keeps wall clean) |
| Color temperature | 2700 – 2900 K | **3000 – 3200 K** (whiter) |
| Colour Rendering Index (CRI) | 100 (continuous spectrum) | 100 (continuous spectrum) |
| Restart time (hot) | Slow (cools first) | **Instant** (always at full output) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate (2 – 5× incandescent) |
| Dimmable | Yes (with care) | Yes (with care) |
| Power range | 5 W – 1500 W | 5 W – 5000 W |
| Main failure mode | Filament burn-out (thinning) | Filament burn-out (cycle cannot fully compensate) |
| Examples | Household bulbs | Car headlamps, studio lighting, projector lamps |

### 3.5 Derivation of Voltage Sensitivity (Lamp Life Exponent)

**Step 1 — Empirical starting point:** For tungsten filaments, evaporation rate $dm/dt \propto e^{-W/kT}$ where $W$ is the work function.

**Step 2 — Filament temperature depends on voltage:** $T \propto V^{0.4}$ (experimentally).

**Step 3 — Hence evaporation rate** $\propto e^{+\,k'V^{0.4}}$ for some constant $k'$.

**Step 4 — Lamp life** is inversely proportional to evaporation rate:

$$
L \propto e^{-k'V^{0.4}} \approx V^{-n}, \quad n \approx 12 \text{ to } 14
$$

This explains the **strong inverse power-law** between voltage and life used in KTU numerical problems.

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 230 V, 100 W incandescent lamp gives 1380 lm. The same lamp when operated at 240 V consumes 108 W and gives 1620 lm. Determine (a) the percentage change in efficacy, (b) the expected reduction in lamp life. Take $n = 13$.

**Solution (a):**

Efficacy at 230 V: $\eta_1 = 1380/100 = 13.8$ lm/W.
Efficacy at 240 V: $\eta_2 = 1620/108 = 15.0$ lm/W.
Change: $\Delta\eta = (15.0 - 13.8)/13.8 \times 100\% = +8.7\%$.

**Solution (b):**

Voltage ratio: $V_2/V_1 = 240/230 = 1.0435$.
Life ratio: $L_2/L_1 = (V_2/V_1)^{-13} = (1.0435)^{-13}$.

$$
L_2/L_1 = e^{-13 \times \ln(1.0435)} = e^{-13 \times 0.04256} = e^{-0.5533} = 0.575
$$

So $L_2 = 0.575 \, L_1$, i.e., a **42.5 % reduction in lamp life** for a **4.35 % over-voltage**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Incandescent Lamp

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[AC Mains Supply] --> SW[Switch and Fuse]
    SW --> DIC[Base / Cap / Edison Screw]
    DIC --> LE1[Lead-in Wire 1]
    DIC --> LE2[Lead-in Wire 2]
    LE1 --> STEM[Glass Stem Press Mount]
    LE2 --> STEM
    STEM --> FIL[Tungsten Coiled Filament]
    STEM --> FIL
    FIL -. Joule Heating .-> HTR[Temperature Rise to 2800 K]
    HTR -. Stefan Boltzmann .-> RAD[Blackbody Radiation]
    RAD --> VIS[Visible Light Output]
    RAD --> IR[Infrared Heat Loss approx 90 percent]
    FIL -. Tungsten Evaporation .-> BLK[Bulb Wall Blackening]
    GASSFILL[Inert Gas Filler Ar N2] -. Suppresses Evaporation .-> FIL
    BULB[Sealed Glass Envelope] --> GASSFILL
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Halogen Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Filament Surface approx 3200 K] --> EVAP[Tungsten Atoms Evaporate]
    EVAP --> DIFF[Diffuse Outward into Bulb]
    DIFF --> WALL[Reach Bulb Wall approx 600 to 900 K]
    WALL --> REACT1[Combine with Halogen I2 or Br2]
    REACT1 --> FORM[Form Volatile Tungsten Halide e.g. WI2]
    FORM --> CONV[Convection Currents Carry Halide Back]
    CONV --> NEAR[Approach Hot Filament again]
    NEAR --> REACT2[Halide Thermodynamically Unstable]
    REACT2 --> DEPO[Tungsten Re-deposited on Filament]
    REACT2 --> REL[Free Halogen Released]
    REL --> REACT1
    DEPO --> START
    QUARTZ[Fused Quartz Envelope Maintains Wall Temperature] -. enables .-> WALL
    HALGAS[Trace Halogen I2 or Br2 partial pressure] -. enables .-> REACT1
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Architectural Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LMP[HALOGEN LAMP ASSEMBLY]
        direction TB
        CAP[Edison or Bayonet Metal Cap] --> LEADR[Lead-in Wire right]
        CAP --> LEADL[Lead-in Wire left]
        LEADR --> STEMP[Quartz Stem Press]
        LEADL --> STEMP
        STEMP --> FILMNT[Coiled-Coil Tungsten Filament]
        STEMP --> SUPP[Internal Support Wires]
        SUPP --> FILMNT
        QENV[Fused Quartz Envelope] --> STEMP
        QENV --> FILMNT
        GASF[Inert Gas + Halogen Vapour at High Pressure] --> QENV
    end
    LMP --> LIGHT[White Light Output CRI approx 100]
    LMP --> HEAT[IR Output approx 80 percent]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Flow — Why Halogen Outperforms Incandescent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Higher Filament Temperature 3200 K vs 2800 K] --> B[More Visible Light per Watt]
    A --> C[Whiter Light Color Temperature]
    D[Halogen Cycle Re-deposits Tungsten] --> E[No Bulb Blackening]
    D --> F[Longer Lamp Life]
    G[Quartz Envelope Tolerates Heat] --> A
    G --> D
    H[Compact Size Keeps Wall Hot] --> D
```

### 4.5 Spectral Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INC[INCANDESCENT LAMP 2800 K]
        IPK[Peak Wavelength approx 1035 nm IR]
        IVIS[Small Visible Fraction]
    end
    subgraph HAL[HALOGEN LAMP 3200 K]
        HPK[Peak Wavelength approximately 905 nm IR]
        HVIS[Larger Visible Fraction]
    end
    INC -. lower .-> EFFIC[Wall-plug Efficacy]
    HAL -. higher .-> EFFIC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEET418]`
**State and explain Stefan–Boltzmann law. How is it relevant to the working of an incandescent lamp?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Stefan–Boltzmann law states that the total radiant exitance of a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature:

$$
E = \sigma T^{4}, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}
$$

In an incandescent lamp, the tungsten filament behaves approximately as a blackbody. When current heats it to $\sim$ 2800 K, the power radiated per unit area is $E = \sigma T^4 \approx 5.67\times10^{-8} \times (2800)^4 \approx 3.5$ MW/m². The $T^4$ dependence is the **fundamental reason** why a small temperature rise produces a large increase in light output — and why a 5 % over-voltage shortens lamp life drastically.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEET418]`
**Why is the bulb of a halogen lamp made of fused quartz and not ordinary glass?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For the halogen regenerative cycle to operate, the **bulb wall temperature must exceed 600 °C**. Ordinary soda-lime glass softens at $\sim$ 500 °C and would fail. **Fused quartz** (silica, SiO₂) has a softening point above 1600 °C and a very low thermal expansion coefficient, allowing the wall to be safely maintained at 600 – 900 °C. Additionally, quartz is transparent to UV, but its small size and thermal strength make it ideal for high-pressure, high-temperature halogen operation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEEET418, Module 1]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of an incandescent lamp**. Discuss why tungsten is preferred as the filament material and explain the role of inert gas filling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Stefan–Boltzmann law** starting from Planck's spectral distribution. Hence show that **doubling the filament temperature increases radiated power by a factor of 16**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of an Incandescent Lamp — 7 marks**

- **(i) Construction (2 marks):** Glass bulb (evacuated or gas-filled), tungsten coiled-coil filament, lead-in wires, stem press, base/cap (Edison screw / bayonet). 
- **(ii) Working (3 marks):** When mains voltage is applied, current passes through the filament, raising its temperature to 2500 – 3000 K by Joule heating ($P = I^2R$). The hot filament radiates energy as per $E = \sigma T^4$, with a significant fraction in the visible band. Filament is coiled-coiled to increase surface area and reduce convective loss.
- **(iii) Why tungsten (1 mark):** Highest melting point (3422 °C), low vapour pressure, good ductility for coiling, high tensile strength.
- **(iv) Role of inert gas (1 mark):** Argon / Nitrogen at $\sim$ 0.8 atm suppresses tungsten evaporation (Langmuir sheath effect) and reduces filament evaporation by an order of magnitude, increasing life.

**(b) Derivation of Stefan–Boltzmann Law — 7 marks**

- Start with Planck's spectral radiance $B_\lambda(T) = \dfrac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT}-1}$.
- Integrate over all wavelengths: $E = \int_0^\infty \pi B_\lambda \, d\lambda$.
- Substitute $x = hc/(\lambda kT)$, reduce to $\int_0^\infty \dfrac{x^3}{e^x-1} dx = \dfrac{\pi^4}{15}$.
- Arrive at $E = \sigma T^4$ with $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$.
- **Deduction of factor 16 (2 marks):** If $T \to 2T$, then $E \to \sigma (2T)^4 = 16 \sigma T^4$. Hence radiated power becomes **16 times**. Since visible fraction also increases superlinearly with $T$, efficacy rises dramatically — this is the entire physical motivation for halogen lamps.

---

**Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEEET418, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **Halogen Regenerative Cycle** with a labelled block diagram. Why is the bulb wall maintained above 600 °C? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 230 V, 60 W incandescent lamp has a rated life of 1000 hours and a luminous flux of 800 lm. Estimate the **(i)** new power, **(ii)** new flux, and **(iii)** new life when operated at 240 V. Take $n = 13$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Halogen Cycle — 7 marks**

- **(i) Cycle steps (4 marks):** Tungsten evaporates from filament → diffuses to cooler bulb wall → combines with halogen to form volatile halide (e.g., WI₂) → convection carries halide back to filament → halide dissociates at high temperature, redepositing W and releasing free halogen → cycle repeats.
- **(ii) Bulb-wall temperature (2 marks):** The forward reaction (halide formation) requires the wall to be hot enough ($>$ 600 °C) for the reaction to proceed at a useful rate but cool enough that the equilibrium favours the halide. Below 600 °C, the cycle stalls and the bulb blackens; above $\sim$ 1000 °C, the quartz may be damaged.
- **(iii) Cycle is sustained and self-cleaning (1 mark):** Net result is no loss of filament material during operation, hence no bulb blackening and longer life.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

Voltage ratio: $V_2/V_1 = 240/230 = 1.0435$.

**(i) New power:** $P_2 = P_1 (V_2/V_1)^{1.6} = 60 \times (1.0435)^{1.6}$.

$$
(1.0435)^{1.6} = e^{1.6 \times 0.04256} = e^{0.0681} = 1.0705
$$

$$
P_2 = 60 \times 1.0705 = 64.23\;\text{W}
$$

**(ii) New flux:** $\Phi_2 = \Phi_1 (V_2/V_1)^{3.5} = 800 \times (1.0435)^{3.5}$.

$$
(1.0435)^{3.5} = e^{3.5 \times 0.04256} = e^{0.1490} = 1.1607
$$

$$
\Phi_2 = 800 \times 1.1607 = 928.6\;\text{lm}
$$

**(iii) New life:** $L_2 = L_1 (V_2/V_1)^{-13} = 1000 \times (1.0435)^{-13}$.

$$
(1.0435)^{-13} = e^{-13 \times 0.04256} = e^{-0.5533} = 0.575
$$

$$
L_2 = 1000 \times 0.575 = 575\;\text{hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Don't use $P = V^2/R$** to recompute power. The empirical exponents ($V^{1.6}$, $V^{3.5}$, $V^{-13}$) are **mandatory** for incandescent-lamp KTU problems and are explicitly listed in the syllabus.
> - **Don't confuse life exponent $n$ with the power exponent 1.6.** A common mistake is using $n = 1.6$ for life; in KTU problems $n$ is **separately given** and is always 12 – 14.
> - **Units:** Always report life in **hours**, flux in **lumens**, power in **watts**. Forgetting units costs 0.5 mark.
> - **In part (a),** if you draw the diagram without labelling the four stages of the halogen cycle, expect to lose 1 mark. KTU examiners award marks for **labelled** diagrams, not just shapes.
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann derivation:** marks are awarded for showing the substitution $x = hc/(\lambda kT)$ explicitly. Skipping this step loses 1 – 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Incandescent lamp** = temperature radiator; tungsten filament, $T \approx 2800$ K, glass bulb, inert-gas filled.
- **Halogen lamp** = incandescent + halogen cycle; quartz bulb, $T \approx 3200$ K, I₂/Br₂ additive, **wall temp $>$ 600 °C**.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $E = \sigma T^4$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.
- **Wien's law:** $\lambda_m T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K → peak is in IR for both lamps.
- **Voltage scaling:** $P \propto V^{1.6}$, $\Phi \propto V^{3.5}$, $\eta \propto V^{1.9}$, $L \propto V^{-n}$, $n \approx 13$.
- **Lamp efficacy:** 10 – 18 lm/W (incandescent), 15 – 25 lm/W (halogen).
- **Lamp life:** $\sim$ 1000 h (incandescent), 2000 – 4000 h (halogen).
- **Filament hot/cold resistance ratio** $\approx$ 12:1 → inrush current ~ 12× steady-state → MCB derating needed.
- **Halogen cycle:** W(evaporate) → WI₂(form at wall) → WI₂(transport) → W(deposit at filament) → repeat.
- **Bulb wall must be hot** for halogen cycle → small bulb size + quartz envelope.
- **CRI = 100** for both (continuous spectrum).
- **Tungsten melting point:** 3422 °C — **highest of all metals**.
- **Inert gas** (Ar / N₂) reduces evaporation by **~ 10×** via Langmuir sheath.
- **5 % over-voltage** → ~ 50 % life loss, ~ 19 % flux gain (must memorize for KTU).
- **683 lm/W at 555 nm** is the **maximum possible luminous efficacy of radiation** (photopic, monochromatic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluorescent Lamps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluorescent Lamps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluorescent Lamp** is a **low-pressure mercury vapor gas-discharge lamp** that produces visible light through the process of **fluorescence**. In this lamp, an electric discharge through mercury vapor generates **ultraviolet (UV) radiation at 253.7 nm**, which strikes a phosphor coating on the inner surface of the glass tube. The phosphor absorbs the UV energy and re-emits it as **visible light**, a phenomenon known as the **Stokes Shift** or **fluorescent emission**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A fluorescent lamp is a low-pressure discharge source in which light is produced partly by the discharge in mercury vapor and partly by the luminescence of phosphor coatings excited by the UV radiation generated during the discharge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **musical tuning fork** that vibrates and produces a high-pitched, inaudible-to-most sound (like UV). Now, you place this tuning fork against a **guitar string** — the guitar string picks up the energy and starts vibrating at its own natural, audible frequency (visible light). This is essentially what a fluorescent lamp does:

- The **mercury discharge** is the "tuning fork" producing invisible **UV photons (253.7 nm)**.
- The **phosphor coating** is the "guitar string" that converts UV into **visible light**.

So a fluorescent lamp is like a **two-step energy translator**: **Electrical Energy → UV Photons → Visible Light**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Luminous Efficacy** | 45 – 100 | **lm/W** |
| **Color Rendering Index (CRI)** | 50 – 90 | Ra (unitless) |
| **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)** | 2700 – 6500 | **K (Kelvin)** |
| **Lamp Life** | 7,500 – 20,000 | **hours** |
| **Tube Diameter (T5/T8/T12)** | 15.9 / 25.4 / 38.1 | **mm** |
| **Operating Pressure (mercury)** | 0.006 – 0.01 | **torr (≈ 1 Pa)** |
| **Striking/Ignition Voltage** | 200 – 1000 | **V** |
| **Resonant UV Line of Mercury** | **253.7 nm** (≈ 4.88 eV) | nm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **253.7 nm UV line** is the most efficient mercury emission, accounting for nearly **70%** of the discharge energy — making it ideal for exciting phosphors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Fluorescent Lamp

A fluorescent lamp consists of the following components:

- **Glass Tube**: Long cylindrical tube (T5, T8, T12 standards) coated internally with phosphor.
- **Phosphor Coating**: A thin layer of inorganic phosphor (e.g., calcium halophosphate, rare-earth triphosphors) coated inside the tube.
- **Electrodes (Cathodes)**: Two tungsten filaments coated with **barium, strontium, and calcium oxides** to act as oxide-coated emitters (low work function).
- **Inert Gas Filling**: Argon (Ar) at ~2-3 torr, sometimes mixed with a small amount of **neon (Ne)** to aid starting.
- **Mercury Vapor**: A small drop of liquid mercury (≈ 50 mg) that vaporizes during operation.
- **Base Caps**: Bi-pin bases (G5 or G13) at each end to connect to the lamp holder.
- **Starter (Glow Switch)**: A bimetallic switch in a neon-filled glass bulb (used in conventional circuits).
- **Ballast (Choke)**: An inductor that limits the current and provides the initial high voltage.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Initial State**: When the supply is switched ON, the **starter (glow switch)** closes the circuit, allowing current to flow through the **ballast → electrode filaments → starter** loop.
2. **Preheating**: The current heats the **oxide-coated cathodes** to ~800–1000 °C, causing **thermionic emission** of electrons.
3. **Starter Opening**: After a fraction of a second, the **bimetallic strip in the starter** cools and opens, causing the **ballast (inductor)** to induce a high-voltage spike (**Lenz's Law: e = -L·di/dt**).
4. **Breakdown / Ignition**: This high voltage (≈ 600–1000 V) ionizes the inert gas, which then vaporizes mercury and strikes an **arc** between the electrodes.
5. **Discharge Stabilization**: The arc operates at low pressure; the **ballast** limits the current to a safe value (typically 0.4 A for T8 lamps).
6. **UV Generation**: Free electrons collide with mercury atoms, exciting them. As they de-excite, they emit **UV photons (mainly at 253.7 nm)**.
7. **Fluorescence**: These UV photons strike the **phosphor coating**, which absorbs the energy and re-emits **visible light** (longer wavelength, lower energy per photon).
8. **Steady-State Operation**: The lamp settles into a stable discharge at its rated wattage.

## 2.3 Role of the Ballast

The ballast is **mandatory** because fluorescent lamps have a **negative resistance characteristic** — once the discharge starts, current tends to increase uncontrollably, destroying the lamp. The ballast:

- **Limits current** to safe operating value.
- **Provides high striking voltage** to initiate the arc.
- Can be **electromagnetic (choke)** or **electronic (high-frequency 25–50 kHz)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic ballasts** operate the lamp at high frequency (typically **30–50 kHz**), which:
> - Increases efficacy by **10–15%** (avoids stroboscopic flicker).
> - Eliminates the need for a separate starter.
> - Reduces power loss in the ballast itself.

## 2.4 Types of Fluorescent Lamps

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot Cathode (Pre-heat)** | Uses bimetal starter; electrodes preheated | Most common (T8, T12) |
| **Rapid Start (RS)** | Electrodes continuously heated; no starter | Commercial offices |
| **Instant Start (IS)** | High voltage applied directly; no preheating | Industrial, sign lighting |
| **Cold Cathode (CCFL)** | No preheating; electrodes are bare metal | Backlit LCDs, signage |
| **Compact Fluorescent Lamp (CFL)** | Folded tube, integrated ballast (older types) | Residential retrofit |
| **Induction Lamp** | No electrodes; electromagnetic field excites gas | Long-life (100,000 h) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Ballast Inductance (impedance) | $L = \dfrac{V_s - V_L}{2 \pi f \cdot I_L}$ | $L$ in H, $V_s$ = supply voltage, $V_L$ = lamp voltage |
| Inductor Back-EMF | $e = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V (volts); Lenz's law |
| Striking Voltage | $V_s \geq V_L + \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{\text{ballast}}$ | Approximate condition |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P} \; \vert\; \text{(lm/W)}$ | $\Phi$ = luminous flux, $P$ = input power |
| Stokes Shift | $\lambda_{\text{emission}} > \lambda_{\text{absorption}}$ | Energy loss = heat in phosphor |
| Photon Energy | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | eV |
| UV Photon Energy (253.7 nm) | $E = \dfrac{1240}{253.7} \approx 4.88$ | eV |
| Visible Photon Energy (550 nm) | $E = \dfrac{1240}{550} \approx 2.25$ | eV |
| Energy Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{conv}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{visible}}}{E_{\text{UV}}} \times 100$ | % |
| Lamp Life Decay (lumen maintenance) | $\Phi(t) = \Phi_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha t}$ | $\alpha$ = decay constant |
| Power Factor (with choke) | $pf = \cos(\phi) \approx 0.5$ | Lagging; needs correction |
| Color Temperature (CCT) | $T_c = 1 / (a + b \cdot \text{CIE} \cdot x + ...)$ | K (computed from chromaticity) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the choke-ballast formula, students often confuse $V_s$ with $V_L$. Always use the **vector (phasor) subtraction** for the inductor voltage drop, NOT arithmetic subtraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ballast Inductance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 40 W fluorescent lamp operates on 230 V, 50 Hz supply. The lamp voltage is 108 V and lamp current is 0.41 A. Determine the inductance of the ballast (choke) used in series.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**

- Supply voltage: $V_s = 230$ V
- Lamp voltage (running): $V_L = 108$ V
- Lamp current: $I_L = 0.41$ A
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Power factor of lamp: assume $\cos\phi_L \approx 0.95$ (typical)

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the series loop**

The supply voltage equals the vector sum of the lamp voltage and the inductive drop:

$$
V_s^2 = V_L^2 + V_{choke}^2
$$

(since the choke is purely inductive and is in series with the resistive-arc load; for simplicity, we assume the lamp behaves resistively for vector balance — the KTU-standard approach.)

**Step 3 — Solve for the choke voltage drop**

$$
V_{choke} = \sqrt{V_s^2 - V_L^2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{choke} = \sqrt{(230)^2 - (108)^2}
$$

$$
V_{choke} = \sqrt{52900 - 11664}
$$

$$
V_{choke} = \sqrt{41236}
$$

$$
V_{choke} \approx 203.06 \; \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the inductive reactance formula**

$$
X_L = 2 \pi f L = \dfrac{V_{choke}}{I_L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L = \dfrac{V_{choke}}{2 \pi f I_L}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
L = \dfrac{203.06}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 0.41}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{203.06}{128.81}
$$

$$
L \approx 1.576 \; \text{H}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result**

$$
\boxed{L \approx 1.58 \; \text{H}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **standard KTU 14-mark problem**. Always state the assumptions: lamp is treated as purely resistive, choke as purely inductive, and write the phasor equation explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Luminous Efficacy of a Fluorescent Lamp

**Given:** A 40 W fluorescent lamp produces 3200 lumens.

**Step 1 — Apply the efficacy formula**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\text{Luminous Flux } (\Phi)}{\text{Electrical Power Input } (P)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{3200 \; \text{lm}}{40 \; \text{W}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta = 80 \; \text{lm/W}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical T8 fluorescent lamps have efficacy between **50–90 lm/W**, much higher than incandescent (≈ 15 lm/W). This is why fluorescent lamps are preferred for office and industrial lighting.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Striking Voltage Using Phasor Analysis

In a starter-operated preheat circuit, the voltage across the lamp during starting is:

$$
V_{\text{start}} = \sqrt{V_s^2 + (\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{\text{choke}})^2}
$$

**Derivation rationale:** When the starter opens, the inductor produces a transient spike equal to $\sqrt{2}$ times the RMS drop (peak value), added vectorially to the supply.

For our previous example:

$$
V_{\text{start}} = \sqrt{(230)^2 + (\sqrt{2} \times 203.06)^2}
$$

$$
V_{\text{start}} = \sqrt{52900 + 164944}
$$

$$
V_{\text{start}} = \sqrt{217844}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{start}} \approx 466.7 \; \text{V}}
$$

This high voltage is sufficient to break down the gas and initiate the arc.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Energy Conversion Chain

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{electrical}} &\rightarrow E_{\text{electron KE}} \; (\text{accelerated in electric field}) \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{UV photon}} \; (\text{mercury de-excitation at } 253.7 \text{ nm}) \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{visible photon}} \; (\text{phosphor fluorescence at } 400{-}700 \text{ nm}) \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{useful light output}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy at each stage (per photon):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{UV}} &= \dfrac{1240}{253.7} \approx 4.88 \; \text{eV} \\
E_{\text{visible (avg)}} &= \dfrac{1240}{550} \approx 2.25 \; \text{eV} \\
\text{Quantum defect loss} &= 4.88 - 2.25 = 2.63 \; \text{eV} \; (\text{dissipated as heat in phosphor})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** The energy difference between absorbed UV and emitted visible light is lost as **heat in the phosphor** (Stokes Loss). This is why fluorescent lamps are **NOT** 100% efficient despite high luminous efficacy — the lumen/watt metric is weighted to the eye's sensitivity (photopic curve), masking the real energy loss.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
