<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: Define "Fluorescence" and explain how it is utilized in a fluorescent lamp.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fluorescence is the phenomenon in which a substance absorbs radiation of one wavelength (typically UV) and re-emits radiation of a longer wavelength (visible light) almost instantaneously (within 10⁻⁸ s). In a fluorescent lamp, the **mercury vapor discharge** generates UV photons at **253.7 nm**, which strike the **phosphor coating** on the inner wall of the tube. The phosphor absorbs this UV energy and re-emits it as **visible light**. The wavelength shift from UV to visible is known as the **Stokes Shift**, and the energy difference is lost as heat in the phosphor. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Q: Why is a ballast (choke) necessary in a fluorescent lamp circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fluorescent lamp exhibits a **negative resistance characteristic** during discharge. Once the arc is struck, the current tends to increase uncontrollably, which would destroy the lamp. The ballast (an inductor in series) **limits the current** to a safe operating value. Additionally, during starting, when the starter switch opens, the ballast induces a **high voltage spike (L di/dt)** that is sufficient to **break down the gas** and initiate the arc. Thus, the ballast performs two critical functions: **current limiting** and **voltage transformation for ignition**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a fluorescent lamp. Discuss the role of starter and ballast in detail.**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**

1. **Glass Tube**: A long cylindrical tube of standard diameter (T5, T8, or T12) coated internally with phosphor.
2. **Phosphor Coating**: Calcium halophosphate or rare-earth triphosphors (for better CRI).
3. **Electrodes**: Two **oxide-coated tungsten filaments** (coated with Ba, Sr, Ca oxides) that emit electrons via thermionic emission.
4. **Fillings**: A small drop of **liquid mercury** (~50 mg) + **inert gas** (argon at 2–3 torr).
5. **End Caps**: Bi-pin bases (G5/G13) for electrical connection.

**Working (4 marks):**

1. When supply is switched ON, the **starter (glow switch)** glows due to neon discharge.
2. The bimetallic strip bends and **closes the contact**, allowing current to flow through the **ballast → electrode 1 → electrode 2 → starter** loop.
3. Electrode filaments heat to **~850 °C**, releasing electrons by **thermionic emission**.
4. After a fraction of a second, the bimetallic strip **opens the contact**.
5. The ballast (inductor) produces a **high voltage spike (L di/dt)**, ionizing the gas and striking the **mercury arc**.
6. The arc generates **UV photons (253.7 nm)**, which excite the **phosphor coating**.
7. The phosphor emits **visible light** by fluorescence.

#### Part (b) — Role of Starter and Ballast (7 marks)

**Ballast (Choke) — 3.5 marks:**
- **Current Limiting**: Once the arc is established, the lamp has negative resistance. The ballast's inductive reactance $X_L = 2\pi f L$ limits current to rated value (e.g., 0.41 A for 40 W lamp).
- **Voltage Boosting**: At starting, when the starter opens, the inductor opposes the sudden change in current (Lenz's law) and produces a voltage spike of ~600–1000 V to break down the gas.
- **Stabilization**: Maintains stable arc throughout lamp life.

**Starter (Glow Switch) — 3.5 marks:**
- A small **neon-filled glass bulb** containing a **bimetallic strip** and a fixed contact.
- On applying voltage, the **neon glow discharge** produces heat, which bends the bimetallic strip to **close the contact**.
- Current now flows through the ballast and electrode filaments, **preheating the cathodes**.
- The bimetallic strip **cools and opens** the contact, causing the ballast to produce the **high-voltage spike** needed to strike the arc.
- The starter is **bypassed** during normal operation (open circuit).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Marks lost for not drawing the circuit diagram** — Always include the choke, starter, and lamp connection in a single-line diagram.
> - **Confusing "preheat" and "instant start" lamps** — Pre-heat uses a starter; instant start uses a high voltage transformer directly.
> - **Forgetting to mention the choke loss** — The ballast itself consumes 8–10 W; this must be considered for true system efficacy.
> - **Wrong formula usage**: For finding $L$, use phasor (vector) equation $V_s^2 = V_L^2 + V_{choke}^2$, NOT arithmetic subtraction.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: A 40 W fluorescent lamp operates on a 230 V, 50 Hz supply with a choke ballast. The lamp voltage is 108 V and the lamp current is 0.41 A. Calculate: (a) The inductance of the choke. (b) The power loss in the choke. (c) The overall luminous efficacy if the lamp produces 3200 lumens.**

#### Part (a) — Inductance of the Choke (5 marks)

**Step 1:** Apply the phasor equation (lamp treated as resistive, choke as inductive):

$$
V_s^2 = V_L^2 + V_{choke}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for choke voltage:

$$
V_{choke} = \sqrt{(230)^2 - (108)^2} = \sqrt{52900 - 11664} = \sqrt{41236} \approx 203.06 \; \text{V}
$$

**Step 3:** Using $V_{choke} = I_L \cdot X_L = I_L \cdot 2\pi f L$:

$$
L = \dfrac{V_{choke}}{2\pi f I_L} = \dfrac{203.06}{2\pi \times 50 \times 0.41}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{203.06}{128.81} \approx 1.576 \; \text{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 1.58 \; \text{H}} \quad \text{(5 marks)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Power Loss in the Choke (4 marks)

The choke is purely inductive, so it consumes **reactive power only**, with **zero active (real) power loss** in the ideal case.

$$
P_{choke}(\text{real}) = I_L^2 \cdot R_{choke} \approx 0 \; \text{W (ideal)}
$$

However, practically, the choke has a small resistance (copper + core losses). The **reactive power** absorbed is:

$$
Q_{choke} = V_{choke} \times I_L = 203.06 \times 0.41 \approx 83.25 \; \text{VAR}
$$

If choke resistance is given (say $R_c = 5 \;\Omega$):

$$
P_{choke} = I_L^2 \cdot R_c = (0.41)^2 \times 5 = 0.84 \; \text{W}
$$

> **Assume ideal choke for 4 marks:**

$$
\boxed{P_{choke} \approx 0 \; \text{W (ideal, reactive only)}} \quad \text{(4 marks)}
$$

#### Part (c) — Overall Luminous Efficacy (5 marks)

**Step 1:** Total power input to the system (lamp + ballast):

$$
P_{total} = V_s \times I_L \times \cos\phi
$$

For a slightly lagging system ($\cos\phi \approx 0.9$):

$$
P_{total} \approx 230 \times 0.41 \times 0.9 \approx 84.87 \; \text{W}
$$

**Step 2:** System efficacy:

$$
\eta_{system} = \dfrac{\Phi}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{3200}{84.87} \approx 37.7 \; \text{lm/W}
$$

**Step 3:** Lamp-only efficacy (often asked):

$$
\eta_{lamp} = \dfrac{3200}{40} = 80 \; \text{lm/W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{lamp} = 80 \; \text{lm/W}, \quad \eta_{system} \approx 37.7 \; \text{lm/W}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often compute the **lamp efficacy (80 lm/W)** but forget to include the **choke losses** to find **system efficacy**.
> - Use **vector (phasor) equation** for choke voltage, not direct arithmetic subtraction.
> - Always state the **assumptions** (ideal choke, lamp as resistive load).
> - Don't confuse **luminous efficacy (lm/W)** with **electrical efficiency (%)**. They are different metrics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fluorescent Lamps**

- **Definition**: Low-pressure mercury vapor discharge lamp using **fluorescence** to convert UV → visible light.
- **Construction**: Glass tube + phosphor coating + oxide-coated tungsten electrodes + mercury + argon.
- **Key UV Wavelength**: **253.7 nm** (resonant mercury line, 4.88 eV).
- **Working**: Starter preheats electrodes → ballast produces high-voltage spike → arc strikes → UV excites phosphor → visible light emitted.
- **Components**:
  - **Ballast/Choke**: Limits current + provides starting voltage spike.
  - **Starter (Glow Switch)**: Bimetallic switch in neon bulb for preheating electrodes.
- **Formulas to Memorize**:
  - Choke voltage: $V_{choke} = \sqrt{V_s^2 - V_L^2}$
  - Inductance: $L = V_{choke} / (2\pi f I_L)$
  - Efficacy: $\eta = \Phi / P$
  - Photon energy: $E = 1240 / \lambda(\text{nm})$ eV
- **Types**: Hot cathode (preheat, rapid start, instant start), cold cathode (CCFL), induction, CFL.
- **Electronic Ballast Advantage**: Operates at **30–50 kHz** → eliminates flicker, increases efficacy by 10–15%, no starter needed.
- **Lamp Standards**: T5 (15.9 mm), T8 (25.4 mm), T12 (38.1 mm) — diameter in 1/8 inch units.
- **Efficacy Range**: 45–100 lm/W (system efficacy lower due to ballast losses).
- **CRI**: 50–90 Ra; CCT: 2700–6500 K.
- **Advantages**: High efficacy, long life (7,500–20,000 h), low heat, diffuse light.
- **Disadvantages**: Requires ballast + starter, contains mercury (hazardous), flickers at 50 Hz (with magnetic ballast), slow start.
- **Phosphors Used**: Calcium halophosphate (older), rare-earth triphosphors (modern, better CRI).
- **Stokes Loss**: Energy difference between absorbed UV and emitted visible light → dissipated as heat in phosphor.
- **Negative Resistance**: Fluorescent lamp has falling V-I characteristic → ballast is **mandatory**.
- **Striking Voltage**: Typically **600–1000 V** to break down the gas.
- **CFL**: Compact Fluorescent Lamp — folded tube with integrated ballast (older types) or external (modern).
- **CCFL**: Cold Cathode Fluorescent Lamp — used in LCD backlighting; very long life; high voltage AC needed.

> **End of Notes — Fluorescent Lamps | PEEET418 | Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Lamps (LPSV, HPSV, Mercury, Metal Halide Lamps)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vapour Lamps (LPSV, HPSV, Mercury, Metal Halide Lamps)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vapour Lamp?

A **Vapour Lamp** (also called a **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamp** family member) is an electric arc-discharge light source in which light is produced by passing an electric current through a **vaporized or gaseous metallic element** (Sodium, Mercury, or Halide compounds) sealed inside a transparent arc tube. The arc discharge causes the metal atoms to become excited; as they return to their ground state, they emit characteristic spectral lines (resonance radiation), which constitute the visible output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> "A Vapour Lamp is an electric discharge lamp in which the light-producing medium is a vapor of one or more metals or metallic compounds, the discharge taking place through this vapor under controlled pressure conditions, leading to a characteristic line-spectrum emission."

---

## 1.2 The Four Major Vapour Lamp Variants (Module 1 Syllabus)

The KTU Module-1 syllabus mandates the study of four specific vapour-discharge lamps:

| # | Lamp Type | Abbreviation | Operating Pressure |
|---|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Low Pressure Sodium Vapour Lamp | **LPSV** | ~0.5 – 1.0 Pa (very low) |
| 2 | High Pressure Sodium Vapour Lamp | **HPSV** | ~25 – 35 kPa |
| 3 | Mercury Vapour Lamp | **MV** | ~200 – 500 kPa |
| 4 | Metal Halide Lamp | **MH** | ~200 – 700 kPa |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Pot" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pressure Cooker Principle**
> Imagine a sealed pressure cooker on a stove. At normal atmospheric pressure, water boils at 100 °C and produces thin, transparent steam. But if you seal and pressurize it, the boiling point rises, the steam becomes denser, and the light/color effects from the contents change. **Vapour lamps work exactly like this**:
> - **LPSV** = a low-pressure vessel → produces a thin, pure, single-color (monochromatic) "spectral steam" of yellow sodium light.
> - **HPSV/MV/MH** = high-pressure vessels → the dense "spectral steam" broadens, producing richer multi-color output (white-ish light) at the cost of some efficiency.
> - The **current through the vapour** is the "stirring spoon" that excites atoms into emitting photons.

This single analogy explains the entire taxonomy of vapour lamps and why their color and efficiency characteristics change with pressure.

---

## 1.4 General Constructional Anatomy (Common to all Vapour Lamps)

Every vapour lamp consists of **three universal structural elements**:

1. **Arc Tube (Discharge Tube)** — A sealed, refractory envelope (quartz or ceramic) containing the vapour-producing element plus a starting inert gas (Argon/Neon/Xenon).
2. **Outer Envelope (Bulb/Jacket)** — A larger glass bulb surrounding the arc tube; usually evacuated or nitrogen-filled to provide **thermal insulation** and protect against UV.
3. **Auxiliary Components** — **Ballast** (current-limiting reactor), **Igniter/Starter** (high-voltage kick to start the arc), and **Lamp Holders** (E27/E40 base).

---

## 1.5 Quick Comparison Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At a Glance (Memorize this block before exams):**
> - **Highest Efficacy:** LPSV (~150–200 lm/W) — the undisputed efficiency champion
> - **Best Color Rendering:** MH (CRI 65–85) — closest to daylight among vapour lamps
> - **Most Widely Used:** HPSV — the modern street-lighting workhorse
> - **Oldest Technology:** Mercury Vapour — historically first practical HID lamp
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Low Pressure Sodium Vapour Lamp (LPSV)

### 2.1.1 Construction

- **Inner U-shaped arc tube:** Borosilicate glass coated internally with sodium-resistant layer; contains a small amount of **solid sodium** (or sodium-mercury amalgam) and an inert starting gas (**Neon + 1% Argon**) at ~10 mbar.
- **Outer vacuum jacket:** Double-walled glass envelope with the space between walls **evacuated to a high vacuum** to minimize heat loss. This thermal insulation is the key reason LPSV is so efficient — the cold spot temperature must remain above **270 °C** to maintain adequate sodium vapor pressure.
- **Electrodes:** Tungsten oxide-coated electrodes at each end of the U-tube.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle

1. **Starting Phase (Cold State):** The neon-argon mixture at low pressure ionizes easily when the igniter applies a high-voltage pulse (~400–600 V). A characteristic **reddish-orange neon glow discharge** is initially established.
2. **Warm-up Phase (5–10 minutes):** Heat from the discharge gradually vaporizes the sodium. The lamp color transitions from red → orange → yellow → the final monochromatic yellow.
3. **Steady-State:** Sodium vapor pressure stabilizes around **0.5 Pa**; the discharge shifts entirely to the **sodium D-line resonance radiation** at **589.0 nm and 589.6 nm** (the famous "Na doublet").

### 2.1.3 Characteristics of LPSV

- **Luminous Efficacy:** **150 – 200 lm/W** (highest of any commercial light source)
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI):** **−44** (worst possible — monochromatic yellow, no color discrimination)
- **Lamp Life:** **14,000 – 18,000 hours**
- **Color Temperature:** ~1700 K (warm yellow)
- **Power Range:** 18 W – 180 W (standard ratings)
- **Re-strike Time:** Immediate (no cooling required)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why monochromatic?**
> At low sodium vapor pressure, only the lowest excitation state (3p → 3s transition) is populated. This produces the single, sharp D-line doublet. The lamp is thus extremely efficient but useless where color recognition matters.

---

## 2.2 High Pressure Sodium Vapour Lamp (HPSV)

### 2.2.1 Construction

- **Arc Tube:** Fabricated from **polycrystalline alumina (PCA) ceramic** or **synthetic sapphire**, because ordinary quartz would be chemically attacked by hot sodium vapor. The tube is sealed with niobium feedthroughs.
- **Filling:** Sodium (Na) + **Mercury (Hg) amalgam** + Xenon (Xe) starting gas.
  - **Sodium:** the active light-producing vapor.
  - **Mercury:** raises the operating voltage, reduces arc current, increases efficiency, and prevents sodium migration.
  - **Xenon:** low thermal conductivity — lowers starting voltage requirement and speeds warm-up.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle

1. **Starting:** Xenon ignites first (Penning mixture effect); the lamp strikes at ~2–4 kV pulse from the igniter.
2. **Warm-up (3–5 minutes):** Sodium and mercury vaporize progressively; lamp voltage and light output rise.
3. **Steady State:** Sodium partial pressure reaches **~10 kPa**, mercury partial pressure ~50 kPa, total ~**25–35 kPa**. Pressure broadening of the sodium D-line occurs — the spectrum broadens from a sharp doublet into a self-reversed, wider band producing warm-white light.

### 2.2.3 Characteristics of HPSV

- **Luminous Efficacy:** **80 – 150 lm/W**
- **CRI:** **20 – 25** (poor, but vastly better than LPSV)
- **Lamp Life:** **20,000 – 30,000 hours** (longest among HIDs)
- **Color Temperature:** ~2000 K (golden white)
- **Re-strike Time:** 1–3 minutes (must cool to allow vapor pressure to drop)

---

## 2.3 Mercury Vapour Lamp

### 2.3.1 Construction

- **Arc Tube:** Fused **quartz (silica)** capable of withstanding high temperatures and UV radiation; contains liquid mercury and argon starting gas.
- **Outer Bulb:** Hard glass envelope, coated internally with **fluorescent phosphor** in colour-corrected (deluxe white) versions to convert UV to visible light.
- **Auxiliary Electrode (Starting Electrode):** A third electrode placed near one main electrode, connected through a high resistance to the opposite main electrode, facilitates starting.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle

1. **Starting:** The auxiliary electrode initiates a glow discharge in argon near one main electrode; this ionizes the gap and allows the main arc to strike at normal mains voltage.
2. **Warm-up (4–7 minutes):** Heat progressively vaporizes mercury. As vapor pressure rises, the arc constricts and intensifies; lamp voltage climbs.
3. **Steady State:** Mercury pressure reaches **~2–10 atmospheres**. The discharge emits strong UV (mainly at **253.7 nm and 365 nm**) plus visible lines at **404.7 nm (violet), 435.8 nm (blue), 546.1 nm (green), 577–579 nm (yellow)**.

### 2.3.3 Characteristics of Mercury Vapour

- **Luminous Efficacy:** **35 – 65 lm/W** (low compared to sodium)
- **CRI:** **40 – 50** (clear type), up to **60** (deluxe-white phosphor-coated)
- **Lamp Life:** **16,000 – 24,000 hours**
- **Color Temperature:** ~3500 – 4500 K (cool white to bluish-white)
- **Re-strike Time:** 3–6 minutes (must cool completely)

---

## 2.4 Metal Halide Lamp (MH)

### 2.4.1 The "Halide Cycle" Concept (★ Key Innovation ★)

A pure mercury lamp emits poorly in the red region of the spectrum. The brilliant insight behind metal halide lamps is the **Halide Cycle**:

1. In the **hot core** of the arc (~5000–6000 K), metal halides (e.g., NaI, ScI₃, DyI₃, HoI₃) **dissociate** into metal atoms and iodine.
2. The **metal atoms become excited** and emit their characteristic spectral lines in the visible region (especially red, which mercury lacks).
3. In the **cooler outer regions** of the arc tube (~1500 K), the metal atoms and iodine **recombine** to reform the original halide salt, which then **diffuses back** into the hot core.
4. This cycle **regenerates** the metal atoms continuously without them depositing on the cooler tube walls (which would happen with pure metals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Without the halide cycle, only mercury and thallium could be used. The halide cycle unlocks the use of nearly every metal, vastly improving the **spectral quality** (CRI) of the lamp while retaining much of mercury's high arc efficiency.

### 2.4.2 Construction

- **Arc Tube:** Small fused-quartz tube filled with mercury, argon starting gas, and a mixture of **metal halide salts** (typically iodides of sodium, scandium, thallium, indium, dysprosium, etc.).
- **Outer Jacket:** Borosilicate glass, often evacuated, sometimes with a UV-blocking coating.
- **Electrode spacing:** Very small (~1 cm) for high current density.

### 2.4.3 Working Principle

1. **Cold Start:** Igniter produces ~3–5 kV pulse; argon ionizes.
2. **Warm-up (3–5 minutes):** Heat vaporizes mercury (dominant) and gradually the halides. Lamp transitions from bluish (mercury dominant) → bright white.
3. **Steady State:** Total pressure ~5–7 atm; halides fully participate in the cycle; full spectrum with high CRI is achieved.

### 2.4.4 Characteristics of Metal Halide

- **Luminous Efficacy:** **75 – 125 lm/W**
- **CRI:** **65 – 85** (excellent — closest to natural daylight among HIDs)
- **Lamp Life:** **6,000 – 15,000 hours** (shorter due to halide attack on electrodes and tube wall)
- **Color Temperature:** 3000 – 6000 K (selectable via halide chemistry)
- **Re-strike Time:** 5–20 minutes (longest — must fully cool)
- **Applications:** Sports stadiums, TV/film studios, automotive headlamps (HID xenon variant), warehouse lighting, aquarium/greenhouse lighting.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are **board-tested** and appear frequently in KTU University Exam questions. Practice solving problems until these are second nature.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectral Line Wavelength (Na D-line)** | $\lambda = 589.0 \text{ nm and } 589.6 \text{ nm}$ | Sodium resonance doublet; visible in LPSV/HPSV |
| **Resonance Radiation Condition** | $E = h\nu = hc \div \lambda$ | Energy of emitted photon; $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| **Planck-Einstein Relation** | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{1239.84}{\lambda(\text{nm})} \text{ eV}$ | Quick photon-energy calculator |
| **Vapour Pressure (Clausius-Clapeyron)** | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$ | Describes sodium/mercury vapor pressure vs. cold-spot temperature |
| **Luminous Efficacy** | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi \text{ (lumens)}}{P \text{ (watts)}} \left[\dfrac{\text{lm}}{\text{W}}\right]$ | Crucial comparison metric across lamps |
| **Lamp Power Balance** | $P_{input} = P_{radiation} + P_{electrode\,losses} + P_{ballast\,losses}$ | Used in efficiency problems |
| **Color Rendering Index (CRI)** | $R_a = 100 - \dfrac{4.6 \cdot \Delta E}{}$ | Standardized: 100 (incandescent/sun) → 0 (monochromatic); negative values possible for LPSV |
| **Inverse Square Law (Illuminance)** | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | Where $E$ = illuminance (lux), $I$ = luminous intensity (cd), $d$ = distance (m) |
| **Striking Voltage (Typical)** | $V_{strike} = 400\text{–}600$ V (LPSV), $2\text{–}5$ kV (HPSV/MH) | Required to initiate discharge |
| **Lamp Re-strike Time** | $t_{restrike} \propto \dfrac{1}{P_{vapor}} \rightarrow \infty$ at hot state | Vapour must recondense before restart |
| **Arc Temperature (Core)** | $T_{arc} \approx 4000\text{–}6000$ K | Affects spectral emission via Planck's law |

---

## 2.6 Comparative Engineering Applications Matrix

| Lamp | Best Application | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **LPSV** | Motorway junctions, airport runways, prison yards | Maximum visual acuity under fog due to yellow light's high scotopic/photopic ratio |
| **HPSV** | City streets, parking lots, industrial yards, greenhouse supplemental lighting | Good balance of efficacy, life, cost |
| **Mercury (clear)** | Industrial high-bay lighting (legacy), UV curing, sterilization (germicidal) | Strong UV-C emission in special quartz types |
| **Mercury (deluxe)** | General indoor commercial lighting (legacy installations) | Cheap, acceptable white light |
| **Metal Halide** | Sports stadiums, TV/film shoots, retail showrooms, automotive HID headlamps | High CRI + high efficacy + compact size |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Numerical problems on vapour lamps frequently test three derivations:
> (i) Photon-energy from sodium D-line, (ii) Comparison of efficacies, and (iii) Vapor pressure estimation using Clausius-Clapeyron. Master all three below.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Energy and Frequency of the Sodium D-line Photon

### Problem Setup
A Low Pressure Sodium Vapour Lamp emits the characteristic D-line at $\lambda = 589.0$ nm. Calculate:
- (a) The photon energy in Joules and electron-volts.
- (b) The frequency of emission.
- (c) Compare with the visible-spectrum threshold.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Wavelength: $\lambda = 589.0 \text{ nm} = 589.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- Speed of light: $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the frequency $\nu$ from $c = \nu \lambda$

$$
\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3.00 \times 10^{8}}{589.0 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\nu = \dfrac{3.00 \times 10^{8}}{5.89 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
\nu = 5.093 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate photon energy in Joules

$$
E = h \nu = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (5.093 \times 10^{14})
$$

$$
E = 3.375 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to electron-volts

$$
E = \dfrac{3.375 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.107 \text{ eV}
$$

**Step 4:** Comparison with visible spectrum
Visible range: $1.65 \text{ eV (red)} \rightarrow 3.26 \text{ eV (violet)}$
The 2.107 eV D-line falls in the **yellow-green region** — consistent with the observed monochromatic yellow color.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Many students forget the unit conversion from nm to m, costing 2 marks. Always write $589.0 \text{ nm} = 589.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ as a separate step.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Luminous Efficacy Comparison & Total Lumens Calculation

### Problem Setup
A street-lighting installation uses 250 W HPSV lamps with an efficacy of 110 lm/W. Each luminaire loses 8 W in the ballast. If 150 such lamps illuminate a roadway of dimensions 2 km × 15 m, calculate:
- (a) Total luminous flux emitted by the installation.
- (b) Total power consumed.
- (c) System luminous efficacy.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Lamp rated power: $P_{lamp} = 250$ W
- Lamp efficacy: $\eta_{lamp} = 110$ lm/W
- Ballast loss per luminaire: $P_{ballast} = 8$ W
- Number of lamps: $N = 150$

**Step 1:** Luminous flux per lamp
$$
\Phi_{lamp} = \eta_{lamp} \times P_{lamp} = 110 \times 250 = 27{,}500 \text{ lm}
$$

**Step 2:** Total luminous flux of installation
$$
\Phi_{total} = N \times \Phi_{lamp} = 150 \times 27{,}500 = 4{,}125{,}000 \text{ lm}
$$

**Step 3:** Total power consumed (lamp + ballast per luminaire)
$$
P_{luminaire} = 250 + 8 = 258 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4:** Total installation power
$$
P_{total} = 150 \times 258 = 38{,}700 \text{ W} = 38.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5:** System luminous efficacy (including ballast losses)
$$
\eta_{system} = \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{4{,}125{,}000}{38{,}700}
$$

$$
\eta_{system} = 106.6 \text{ lm/W}
$$

**Interpretation:** The ballast reduces system efficacy by ~3 lm/W. This is why modern electronic ballasts (lower losses) are preferred over electromagnetic ballasts.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Clausius-Clapeyron Vapor Pressure Analysis

### Problem Setup
The vapour pressure of sodium follows the relation:
$$
\log_{10} P (\text{torr}) = -\dfrac{5660}{T} + 8.354
$$
Determine the sodium vapour pressure when the cold-spot temperature is 270 °C (typical LPSV cold-spot temperature).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Cold-spot temperature: $T = 270 \text{ °C} = 270 + 273 = 543 \text{ K}$
- Vapor pressure equation: $\log_{10} P = -\dfrac{5660}{T} + 8.354$

**Step 1:** Substitute temperature
$$
\log_{10} P = -\dfrac{5660}{543} + 8.354
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate fraction
$$
\dfrac{5660}{543} = 10.4236
$$

**Step 3:** Complete the calculation
$$
\log_{10} P = -10.4236 + 8.354 = -2.0696
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for P
$$
P = 10^{-2.0696} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-0.0696}
$$

$$
P = 0.01 \times 0.851 = 8.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ torr}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Pascals (1 torr = 133.32 Pa)
$$
P = 8.51 \times 10^{-3} \times 133.32 = 1.135 \text{ Pa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This very low vapor pressure (~1 Pa) confirms the "Low Pressure" classification of LPSV. Any drop in cold-spot temperature below 270 °C causes pressure to fall, sodium to recondense, and efficacy to collapse catastrophically. **This is why the double-walled vacuum jacket is mandatory.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Trade-off Across All Four Lamps

| Parameter | LPSV | HPSV | Mercury (Clear) | Mercury (Deluxe) | Metal Halide |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Efficacy (lm/W)** | 150 – 200 | 80 – 150 | 35 – 55 | 40 – 60 | 75 – 125 |
| **CRI (Ra)** | −44 | 20 – 25 | 40 – 50 | 50 – 60 | 65 – 85 |
| **CCT (K)** | ~1700 | ~2000 | ~3500 – 4500 | ~4000 | 3000 – 6000 |
| **Life (hours)** | 14k – 18k | 20k – 30k | 16k – 24k | 16k – 24k | 6k – 15k |
| **Warm-up (min)** | 8 – 10 | 3 – 5 | 4 – 7 | 4 – 7 | 3 – 5 |
| **Re-strike (min)** | <0.5 | 1 – 3 | 3 – 6 | 3 – 6 | 5 – 20 |
| **Arc Tube Material** | Borosilicate glass | PCA ceramic | Fused quartz | Fused quartz | Fused quartz |
| **Starting Gas** | Ne + Ar | Xe | Ar | Ar | Ar |
| **Dominant Spectral Lines** | 589 nm (Na-D) | 540 – 640 nm (broadened) | 254, 365, 436, 546, 577 nm | Above + phosphor | Full spectrum |
| **Aux. Electrode?** | No | No | **Yes** | Yes | No |

---

## 3.5 The Fundamental Trade-Off Equation

The single most important engineering principle in vapour-lamp design is:

$$
\boxed{\text{Color Quality} \;\; \uparrow \;\;\Longleftrightarrow \;\;\text{Luminous Efficacy} \;\;\downarrow}
$$

**Reasoning:** Monochromatic emission (LPSV) gives the highest possible efficacy because ALL electrical energy is concentrated into a single wavelength to which the human eye is maximally sensitive. As pressure increases, the spectrum broadens (improving CRI) but energy spreads across wavelengths where eye sensitivity is lower, reducing efficacy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Vapour Lamp System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the universal functional architecture shared by every vapour-lamp installation, including ballast, igniter, arc tube, and protective circuitry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Circuit Breaker and Fuse Protection]
    B --> C[Igniter Circuit High Voltage Pulse Generator]
    C --> D[Ballast Reactor Current Limiting Inductor]
    D --> E[Arc Tube Discharge Chamber Sodium or Mercury Vapor]
    E --> F[Outer Envelope Vacuum Jacket Thermal Insulation]
    E --> G[Starting Gas Neon Argon Xenon Mixture]
    F --> H[Visible Light Output and UV Emission]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Phosphor Coating Optional UV to Visible Conversion]
    I --> J[Final Luminaire Output to Illuminated Surface]
    E --> K[Electrode Assembly Tungsten Oxide Coated]
    K --> D
```

---

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Construction of All Four Vapour Lamps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LPSV[Low Pressure Sodium LPSV]
        LPSV_A[Vacuum Jacket Double Walled] --> LPSV_B[U Shaped Arc Tube Borosilicate]
        LPSV_B --> LPSV_C[Sodium Amalgam Solid Filling]
        LPSV_B --> LPSV_D[Neon Argon Starting Gas 10 mbar]
        LPSV_B --> LPSV_E[Tungsten Oxide Electrodes]
    end

    subgraph HPSV[High Pressure Sodium HPSV]
        HPSV_A[Hard Glass Outer Jacket] --> HPSV_B[PCA Ceramic Arc Tube]
        HPSV_B --> HPSV_C[Sodium Plus Mercury Amalgam]
        HPSV_B --> HPSV_D[Xenon Starting Gas]
        HPSV_B --> HPSV_E[Niobium Feedthrough Electrodes]
    end

    subgraph MV[Mercury Vapour Lamp MV]
        MV_A[Phosphor Coated Outer Bulb Optional] --> MV_B[Fused Quartz Arc Tube]
        MV_B --> MV_C[Liquid Mercury Dose]
        MV_B --> MV_D[Argon Starting Gas Few torr]
        MV_B --> MV_E[Auxiliary Starting Electrode]
        MV_B --> MV_F[Main Operating Electrodes Pair]
    end

    subgraph MH[Metal Halide Lamp MH]
        MH_A[Borosilicate Outer Jacket] --> MH_B[Small Quartz Arc Tube]
        MH_B --> MH_C[Mercury Plus Metal Halide Salts NaI ScI3]
        MH_B --> MH_D[Argon Starting Gas]
        MH_B --> MH_E[Compact High Current Density Electrodes]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Operational State Machine — Lamp Warm-Up Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> IGNITION: Mains On plus Igniter Pulse
    IGNITION --> STARTING_GLOW: Argon or Neon Glow Discharge
    STARTING_GLOW --> WARMUP: Vapor Pressure Rising
    WARMUP --> STEADY_STATE: Full Vapor Pressure and Color Established
    STEADY_STATE --> OPERATING: Normal Service
    OPERATING --> COOLDOWN: Mains Off
    COOLDOWN --> RESTRIKE_DELAY: Vapor Recondensation
    RESTRIKE_DELAY --> OFF
    OFF --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Mercury Vapour Lamp with Auxiliary Starting Electrode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Line L 230V AC] --> F1[Fuse]
    F1 --> SW[Switch]
    SW --> B[Ballast Inductor]
    B --> N[Neutral N]
    L1 --> R[High Resistor 40 to 60 kilo ohm]
    R --> SE[Auxiliary Starting Electrode]
    SE -.-> GAP1[Small Gap to Main Electrode M1]
    M1[Main Electrode M1 Inside Arc Tube] --> ARC[Mercury Vapor Arc]
    ARC --> M2[Main Electrode M2]
    M2 --> N
    M1 -.-> ARC
    M2 -.-> ARC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> The auxiliary electrode is positioned close to M1 and connected via a high resistor to L1 (bypassing the ballast). When switch closes, a small glow discharge starts between M1 and the auxiliary electrode; the resulting ionization bridges the larger gap between M1 and M2, striking the main arc.

---

## 4.5 Performance Comparison Chart (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LPSV] -->|Highest Efficacy 200 lm/W| B[Best Energy Saver]
    C[HPSV] -->|Best Life 30000 hrs plus Balanced Performance| D[Modern Street Light Standard]
    E[Mercury] -->|Cheapest HID plus UV Rich| F[Legacy Industrial plus UV Curing]
    G[Metal Halide] -->|Best CRI 85 plus White Light| H[Stadium plus Studio plus Automotive HID]
```
