# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**"Why does a Low Pressure Sodium Vapour Lamp have the highest luminous efficacy among all artificial light sources, despite having the worst color rendering index?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Low Pressure Sodium Vapour (LPSV) lamp operates at a very low sodium vapour pressure (~1 Pa), which means only the lowest excited energy level of sodium (3²P) is populated. This produces **resonance radiation concentrated in the sodium D-line doublet (589.0 nm and 589.6 nm)** — a single, narrow spectral emission to which the **human eye is highly sensitive (photopic luminous efficacy of radiation ≈ 520 lm/W at this wavelength)**.

Because virtually all input electrical energy is converted into photons at the eye's peak sensitivity wavelength, the lamp's **luminous efficacy reaches 150–200 lm/W** — the theoretical and practical maximum for any white-light source.

However, this monochromatic yellow output contains **no blue or red components**, so the eye cannot differentiate colours of illuminated objects. The Color Rendering Index (CRI) therefore falls to **−44**, the lowest possible value. This is the fundamental **trade-off between efficacy and colour fidelity** in discharge-lamp design.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**"Explain the function of mercury in a High Pressure Sodium Vapour Lamp."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mercury serves **three critical functions** in an HPSV lamp:

1. **Raises the operating arc voltage:** Mercury has a high ionization potential (~10.4 eV) and high vapour pressure; it adds impedance to the discharge, allowing the lamp to operate at a higher, more stable voltage on standard 230 V mains.

2. **Reduces sodium migration and wall attack:** Sodium vapour chemically attacks quartz glass at high temperatures. The mercury-sodium amalgam buffer prevents direct contact of sodium with the arc-tube wall.

3. **Improves luminous efficacy by ballast-friendly operation:** The higher arc voltage means lower arc current for the same power, reducing electrode and ballast losses, thereby boosting overall system efficacy to 80–150 lm/W.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students wrongly say mercury "produces light" in HPSV lamps. **It contributes very little light** — sodium does. Mercury's role is **physical and electrical**, not optical. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions on Vapour Lamps from Module 1 typically carry **two 7-mark sub-parts**: (a) construction + working, and (b) characteristics comparison or numerical analysis.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — Choice 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working principle of a **Low Pressure Sodium Vapour (LPSV) Lamp**. Explain why a double-walled vacuum jacket is essential.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A road lighting scheme uses 180 W LPSV lamps with an efficacy of 175 lm/W. Each lamp is fitted with an autotransformer ballast having 6 W core losses. Calculate:
   1. Total lumens per luminaire
   2. Total system power input
   3. Overall system efficacy
   4. The number of lumens per watt lost in the ballast

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (4 marks):**
- **Inner arc tube:** U-shaped borosilicate glass tube internally coated with a thin sodium-resistant film (often indium oxide). The tube contains:
  - A few grams of solid **sodium** (or Na-Hg amalgam)
  - **Neon + 1% Argon** as the starting gas at ~10 mbar
  - Two **tungsten oxide-coated electrodes** at the open ends of the U-tube
- **Outer envelope:** A larger cylindrical glass envelope surrounds the arc tube. The space between inner tube and outer jacket is **evacuated to a high vacuum (10⁻⁴ Pa)** and the jacket is sealed — this is the **vacuum jacket**.
- **Lamp base:** Standard E27 or E40 Edison screw cap.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. **Starting:** When mains voltage is applied, the ballast limits current while the **igniter** produces a high-voltage pulse (~400–600 V). This ionizes the neon-argon mixture, producing a characteristic **red glow discharge**.
2. **Warm-up:** Heat from the discharge vaporizes the sodium. The arc colour progressively shifts: red → orange → yellow. Full warm-up takes **8–10 minutes**.
3. **Steady State:** Sodium vapour pressure stabilizes at ~0.5–1 Pa; emission occurs almost entirely at the **589.0 nm and 589.6 nm sodium D-line doublet**. Lamp attains full output (~175 lm/W efficacy) with luminous flux ~31,500 lm.

**Why the vacuum jacket is essential:**
Sodium vapor pressure depends critically on the **cold-spot temperature** (the coolest point on the arc tube, typically at the bottom bend of the U-tube). To keep this above **270 °C**, the tube must be thermally insulated. The vacuum jacket eliminates convective and conductive heat losses, preventing the sodium from re-condensing onto the tube wall (which would otherwise extinguish the lamp). Without the vacuum jacket, the cold-spot would fall below 270 °C, sodium pressure would collapse, and the lamp would not produce its characteristic yellow light.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_{lamp} = 180$ W
- $\eta_{lamp} = 175$ lm/W
- $P_{ballast} = 6$ W (autotransformer losses)

**Step 1: Lumens per luminaire (2 marks)**
$$
\Phi = \eta_{lamp} \times P_{lamp} = 175 \times 180 = 31{,}500 \text{ lm}
$$

**Step 2: System power per luminaire (1 mark)**
$$
P_{sys} = 180 + 6 = 186 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Overall system efficacy (2 marks)**
$$
\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{\Phi}{P_{sys}} = \dfrac{31{,}500}{186}
$$

$$
\eta_{sys} = 169.35 \text{ lm/W}
$$

**Step 4: Lumens lost per watt of ballast loss (2 marks)**
$$
\text{Loss per watt} = \dfrac{31{,}500 - (169.35 \times 180)}{6} = \dfrac{31{,}500 - 30{,}483}{6} = \dfrac{1017}{6}
$$

$$
= 169.5 \text{ lm/W of ballast loss}
$$

Equivalently, ballast loss fraction:
$$
\% \text{ loss} = \dfrac{6}{186} \times 100 = 3.23\%
$$

The ballast consumes 3.23% of input power but causes efficacy to drop from 175 lm/W to 169.35 lm/W — a 3.2% relative reduction.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — Choice 2 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction, working, and **Halide Cycle** of a **Metal Halide Lamp**. How does it achieve better colour rendering than a pure mercury vapour lamp?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the four vapour lamps (LPSV, HPSV, Mercury, Metal Halide) in a tabular form across the following parameters: Efficacy, CRI, Colour Temperature, Lamp Life, Warm-up Time, Re-strike Time, and one typical application each.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Metal Halide Lamp (7 marks)**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Outer envelope:** Borosilicate glass bulb, often evacuated, sometimes with UV-blocking coating.
- **Arc tube:** Small fused-quartz discharge tube containing:
  - A precisely measured dose of **mercury** (provides the bulk of input power and UV base)
  - **Metal halide salts** (typically iodides): e.g., **NaI, ScI₃, TlI, DyI₃, HoI₃** — chosen for their spectral contributions
  - **Argon** starting gas at a few torr
- **Electrodes:** Compact, high current-density tungsten electrodes
- **Auxiliary circuit:** Ballast + igniter

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Igniter pulse (~3–5 kV) ionizes argon.
2. Heat vaporizes mercury → forms the dominant plasma.
3. As temperature rises, halide salts vaporize and enter the arc.
4. Steady state achieved in ~3–5 minutes; lamp emits brilliant white light.

**The Halide Cycle (3 marks):**

The halide cycle is the **regenerative process** that enables metal halides to participate efficiently in the discharge without being destroyed or deposited on cold walls:

1. **Hot zone (core, T ≈ 5000–6000 K):** Metal halide salt MX **dissociates** into metal atom M and halogen X (iodine).
2. The metal atom becomes **electronically excited** by collisions with mercury ions/electrons and emits its **characteristic spectral lines** (filling in the red, blue, or green regions where mercury is weak).
3. The metal atom and halogen **diffuse outward** into the cooler wall region (T ≈ 1000–1500 K).
4. In the cool region, M and X **recombine** to reform MX.
5. MX **diffuses back** to the hot core, where the cycle repeats.

**Why this gives better CRI than pure mercury:**
Pure mercury emits predominantly in the UV, blue (436 nm), and green (546 nm) regions but **very weakly in the red**. Metal halides supply **strong red (Na, Dy), green (Tl), and other visible lines**, building up the missing spectral regions. The result is **continuous-spectrum-like emission** with CRI of 65–85 (vs mercury's 40–50) and efficacy still high (75–125 lm/W).

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Table (7 marks)**

| Parameter | LPSV | HPSV | Mercury (Clear) | Metal Halide |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Efficacy (lm/W)** | 150 – 200 | 80 – 150 | 35 – 55 | 75 – 125 |
| **CRI** | −44 (worst) | 20 – 25 | 40 – 50 | 65 – 85 (best) |
| **Colour Temp. (K)** | ~1700 | ~2000 | ~4000 | 3000 – 6000 |
| **Lamp Life (hrs)** | 14k – 18k | 20k – 30k | 16k – 24k | 6k – 15k |
| **Warm-up Time** | 8 – 10 min | 3 – 5 min | 4 – 7 min | 3 – 5 min |
| **Re-strike Time** | <0.5 min | 1 – 3 min | 3 – 6 min | 5 – 20 min |
| **Typical Application** | Motorway lighting, fog-prone areas | City street lighting, parking lots | Industrial high-bay (legacy), UV curing | Stadium lighting, TV studios, automotive HID headlamps |

**Conclusion:** The choice of vapour lamp is governed by the **efficacy-vs-CRI trade-off**. LPSV wins on efficiency, MH wins on colour, HPSV is the practical compromise for outdoor lighting, and mercury is the legacy choice for industrial/UV applications.

---

## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Vapour Lamp questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LPSV and HPSV pressure values** — LPSV operates at ~0.5–1 Pa (NOT 1 atm). Mixing these up costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting ballast losses in numerical problems** — Always include ballast loss when computing system efficacy. Skipping this loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Writing "mercury produces light in HPSV"** — Wrong. Mercury only buffers the discharge electrically; **sodium produces the light**.
> 4. **Missing the halide cycle in MH answers** — Examiners expect the **regenerative M → MX → M mechanism**. Without it, full marks are not awarded.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert nm → m in photon-energy calculations** — A common unit-conversion slip costing 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Stating wrong arc-tube materials** — LPSV uses **borosilicate glass**; HPSV uses **PCA ceramic** (NOT quartz — quartz is attacked by sodium vapour); Mercury/MH use **fused quartz**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and tick each box before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] LPSV: Lowest pressure (~1 Pa), highest efficacy (150–200 lm/W), **monochromatic 589 nm yellow**, worst CRI (−44)
- [ ] LPSV requires **vacuum-insulated outer jacket** to maintain cold-spot temp > 270 °C
- [ ] HPSV: Pressure ~25–35 kPa, ceramic arc tube (PCA), broadened Na-D spectrum, CRI 20–25
- [ ] HPSV contains **Na-Hg amalgam + Xenon** starting gas; Hg raises arc voltage, Xe aids starting
- [ ] Mercury Vapour: Pressure ~2–10 atm, fused quartz tube, **auxiliary starting electrode**, bluish-white light, low efficacy 35–55 lm/W
- [ ] Mercury emits strong lines at **254 nm (UV), 436 nm (blue), 546 nm (green), 577 nm (yellow)** — weak in red
- [ ] Metal Halide: Mercury + **metal halide salts** (NaI, ScI₃, TlI, DyI₃); Halide Cycle regenerates metal atoms
- [ ] Metal Halide: best CRI (65–85) among vapour lamps; efficacy 75–125 lm/W; used in stadiums and studios
- [ ] Fundamental equation: $E_{photon} = hc \div \lambda$, with $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- [ ] Sodium D-line photon energy ≈ **2.10 eV** at 589 nm (yellow-green region)
- [ ] Efficacy trade-off: **↑ colour quality ⇒ ↓ luminous efficacy** (and vice versa)
- [ ] Re-strike time order: **LPSV < HPSV < Mercury < Metal Halide**
- [ ] Longest lamp life: **HPSV** (20,000 – 30,000 hrs)
- [ ] Lamp Power Balance: $P_{in} = P_{radiation} + P_{electrode} + P_{ballast\,losses}$
- [ ] Inverse Square Law: $E = I \div d^{2}$ (lux at distance $d$ from source of intensity $I$)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LED Lamps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** LED Lamps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of LED Lamps

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a two-terminal, solid-state semiconductor p–n junction device that converts electrical energy directly into **visible light** through the physical process of **electroluminescence**. When the diode is forward-biased, charge carriers (electrons from the n-region and holes from the p-region) recombine across the depletion region of a **direct bandgap semiconductor**. Each recombination event releases a quantum of energy equal to the bandgap energy ($E_g$), which is radiated as a **photon** whose wavelength is governed by the Planck–Einstein relation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Verbatim):** "An LED is essentially a forward-biased p–n junction diode fabricated from a direct bandgap semiconductor such as Gallium Arsenide (GaAs), Gallium Phosphide (GaP), Aluminium Gallium Indium Phosphide (AlGaInP), or Indium Gallium Nitride (InGaN), in which light is generated by spontaneous emission of photons during electron–hole recombination."

### 1.2 Core Construction Materials

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| Semiconductor Die | Light generation site (active layer) | InGaN, AlGaInP, GaAs, GaP |
| Anode Lead | Positive terminal current path | Gold-plated Kovar/Alloy 42 |
| Cathode Lead | Negative terminal (with flag) | Tin-plated copper |
| Reflector Cup | Optical directionality (parabolic) | Polished plastic / silver-coated metal |
| Bond Wire | Electrical connection to anode | Gold wire ($\phi \approx 25\,\mu\text{m}$) |
| Lens / Encapsulant | Index-matching, beam shaping | Epoxy resin or silicone ($n \approx 1.5$) |
| Heat Slug / Sink | Thermal management | Aluminium, MCPCB |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Waterfall" Analogy:** Imagine a staircase built of pebbles. An electron sitting on a high pebble (conduction band) "jumps" down to a hole in a low pebble (valence band). The drop in height releases energy — like water falling over a waterfall releases the energy as spray. In an LED, this energy is released as a tiny packet of light (a photon) instead of as heat or sound. The *height of the staircase* is fixed by the semiconductor material, which is why each LED material produces a single, characteristic colour of light.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}$ | $\text{J}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^{8}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Elementary charge | $q$ | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$ | $\text{C}$ |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}}$ | $\text{J/K}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** LEDs differ from traditional incandescent and fluorescent lamps because they produce **monochromatic radiation** (single wavelength band) at the source itself. White light is *synthesised* downstream using phosphor down-conversion or RGB mixing — never generated directly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Five-Stage Electroluminescence Cascade

The functioning of an LED lamp can be analytically decomposed into the following sequential stages:

1. **Forward Bias Application:** An external DC voltage $V_F$ exceeding the diode's cut-in (knee) voltage is applied across the terminals. Typical $V_F$ values are **1.8 V to 3.5 V** depending on bandgap.
2. **Carrier Injection:** Electrons are injected from the n-region into the active (depletion) layer; holes are injected from the p-region into the same active layer.
3. **Radiative Recombination:** Electrons and holes recombine. The fraction of recombinations that emit photons is called the **Internal Quantum Efficiency (IQE)**.
4. **Photon Emission:** The emitted photon has energy $h f = E_g$. Because the bandgap is fixed for a given alloy, the emission spectrum is narrow ($\Delta \lambda \approx 20$–$40\,\text{nm}$ FWHM).
5. **Photon Extraction:** Generated photons must escape the high-refractive-index semiconductor ($n_s \approx 2.5$–$3.5$) into air ($n_{air} \approx 1.0$) through the encapsulant, governed by the **critical angle** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_{air}/n_s)$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why direct bandgap?** Indirect bandgap semiconductors (like Si, Ge) release recombination energy primarily as lattice vibrations (phonons — heat) because momentum conservation requires a third body. Direct bandgap materials conserve momentum with just the photon, making them efficient light emitters.
- **Why a reflector cup?** Total Internal Reflection (TIR) traps $>80\%$ of photons inside the die. The reflector cup redirects these photons into the forward hemisphere, multiplying the **External Quantum Efficiency (EQE)**.
- **Why an epoxy lens?** The lens has a refractive index $n \approx 1.5$ which acts as an impedance-matching medium between the semiconductor and air, increasing the escape cone.
- **Why a heat sink?** Even at 50% wall-plug efficiency, half the input power becomes heat. LEDs are extremely temperature-sensitive — every 10 °C rise typically halves the lifetime.

## 2.3 Generation Methods of White Light

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked Concept:** A *blue LED* alone cannot produce white light. The three industrially established methods are:

| Method | Mechanism | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PC-LED** (Phosphor-Converted) | Blue LED + Yellow YAG:Ce³⁺ phosphor | High CRI, simple, low cost | Stokes loss (~25%), "blue peak" hazard |
| **RGB LED** | Separate Red, Green, Blue chips | Tunable spectrum, no phosphor loss | Complex driver, colour drift over time |
| **Hybrid / RGGB** | Blue die + Red die + phosphor | Best CRI (>95), high efficacy | Cost, thermal management complexity |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Rendering Safeguard:** All absolute-value bars are typeset as $\vert\,\cdot\,\vert$ instead of the pipe character `\|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h f = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{nm}}$ | eV / J | Determines LED colour from bandgap |
| 2 | Bandgap ↔ Wavelength | $\lambda_{nm} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g (eV)}$ | nm | Material selection |
| 3 | Luminous Flux | $\Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \, \Phi_{e,\lambda} \, d\lambda$ | lm (lumen) | Total visible light output |
| 4 | Luminous Efficacy of Source | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{electrical}}$ | $\text{lm/W}$ | Energy efficiency benchmark |
| 5 | Luminous Efficiency (radiometric) | $\eta_{lum} = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{\Phi_e}$ | $\text{lm/W}$ | Spectral quality metric |
| 6 | Wall-Plug Efficiency (WPE) | $\eta_{WPE} = \dfrac{P_{optical}}{V_F \cdot I_F}$ | dimensionless | True electrical-to-optical efficiency |
| 7 | External Quantum Efficiency | $\eta_{EQE} = \eta_{IQE} \cdot \eta_{extraction}$ | dimensionless | Die-level performance |
| 8 | Junction Temperature | $T_j = T_a + (P_{th} \cdot R_{th(j-a)})$ | °C or K | Reliability & lifetime predictor |
| 9 | Thermal Resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{P_{dissipated}}$ | °C/W | Heat sink design |
| 10 | Critical Angle | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_{air}}{n_{semi}}\right)$ | degrees | Light extraction cone |
| 11 | Lambertian Intensity | $I(\theta) = I_0 \cos^{m}(\theta)$ | cd | Beam profile modelling |
| 12 | $m$-factor | $m = \dfrac{-\ln 2}{\ln(\cos\theta_{1/2})}$ | — | View-angle characterisation |
| 13 | Number of Photons / sec | $\dot{N} = \dfrac{P \cdot \lambda}{h c}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Radiometric count |
| 14 | Heat Dissipated | $P_{th} = P_{el} - P_{opt}$ | W | Thermal design |
| 15 | Approximate Bandgap Voltage | $V_F \approx \dfrac{E_g}{q}$ | V | Forward voltage estimation |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World Fields

LED technology is foundational in:
- **General Illumination:** Domestic, commercial, street-lighting, architectural façade lighting.
- **Automotive:** Headlamps (matrix LED), DRLs, interior ambient lighting.
- **Displays:** LCD backlights, Micro-LED TVs, digital signage.
- **Medical:** Phototherapy (blue-light jaundice treatment), surgical lamps, dental curing.
- **Communications:** Visible Light Communication (VLC / Li-Fi), IR remote controls.
- **Horticulture:** Tunable spectrum grow-lights for indoor vertical farming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Photon Energy & Wavelength from Bandgap

This derivation underpins **every colour-of-LED** calculation expected in KTU board exams.

**Given:** Recombination of one electron-hole pair across a direct bandgap of energy $E_g$ releases one photon.

**Step 1 — Energy conservation between electron transition and emitted photon:**

$$E_{photon} = E_{conduction} - E_{valence} = E_g$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Planck–Einstein relation** $E = h f$ where $f$ is the frequency of the emitted photon:

$$h f = E_g$$

**Step 3 — Express frequency in terms of wavelength** using $c = f \lambda$:

$$h \cdot \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = E_g$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda$:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{E_g}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical constants** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}$, $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$, and convert $E_g$ from eV to Joules using $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{E_g(\text{eV}) \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the constant numerator** $\dfrac{h c}{1\,\text{eV}}$:

$$\dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.240 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m·eV} = 1240\,\text{nm·eV}$$

**Step 7 — Final compact formula:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_{nm} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g\,(\text{eV})}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Example:** A GaAs LED has $E_g = 1.42\,\text{eV}$. Then $\lambda = 1240 / 1.42 \approx 873\,\text{nm}$, which lies in the *near-infrared* region — consistent with textbook data for GaAs emitters.

## 3.2 Derivation — Luminous Efficacy from Spectral Power Distribution

**Given:** A LED has spectral power distribution $\Phi_{e,\lambda}(\lambda)$. We need to compute luminous flux $\Phi_v$ and luminous efficacy $\eta_v$.

**Step 1 — Spectral luminous efficacy function:**

The human eye responds non-uniformly across the visible spectrum. The CIE-defined photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ peaks at **555 nm** with a maximum value $K_m = 683\,\text{lm/W}$.

**Step 2 — Differential luminous flux at wavelength $\lambda$:**

$$d\Phi_v = K_m \cdot V(\lambda) \cdot \Phi_{e,\lambda}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the visible band (380 nm to 780 nm):**

$$\Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \cdot \Phi_{e,\lambda}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Luminous efficacy** is the luminous flux per unit input electrical power $P_{el}$:

$$\eta_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{el}} = \dfrac{K_m \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \Phi_{e,\lambda}(\lambda)\, d\lambda}{V_F \cdot I_F}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting a Gaussian SPD approximation** centred at $\lambda_0$ with FWHM $\Delta\lambda$:

$$\Phi_{e,\lambda}(\lambda) \approx \Phi_e^{peak} \exp\!\left[-4 \ln 2 \left(\dfrac{\lambda - \lambda_0}{\Delta\lambda}\right)^{2}\right]$$

For narrow-band LEDs ($\Delta\lambda \ll \lambda_0$), we approximate $V(\lambda) \approx V(\lambda_0)$ within the band, yielding:

$$\Phi_v \approx K_m \cdot V(\lambda_0) \cdot \Phi_e^{peak} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta\lambda \sqrt{\pi}}{2\sqrt{\ln 2}}$$

**Step 6 — Final simplified luminous efficacy:**

$$\boxed{\eta_v \approx \dfrac{683 \cdot V(\lambda_0) \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot \sqrt{\pi}}{2\sqrt{\ln 2} \cdot V_F \cdot I_F / \Phi_e^{peak}}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style numerical problem worked out fully.**

**Problem:** A blue InGaN LED has peak emission $\lambda_0 = 470\,\text{nm}$, FWHM $\Delta\lambda = 25\,\text{nm}$, forward voltage $V_F = 3.2\,\text{V}$, forward current $I_F = 350\,\text{mA}$, and total radiant flux $\Phi_e = 700\,\text{mW}$. Compute: (a) Photon energy in eV, (b) Wall-plug efficiency, (c) Luminous efficacy assuming $V(470\,\text{nm}) \approx 0.09$.

**Part (a) — Photon Energy:**

$$E_{photon} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{nm}} = \dfrac{1240}{470} \approx 2.638\,\text{eV}$$

**Part (b) — Wall-Plug Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{WPE} = \dfrac{P_{optical}}{V_F \cdot I_F} = \dfrac{0.700\,\text{W}}{3.2 \times 0.350} = \dfrac{0.700}{1.120} \approx 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

**Part (c) — Luminous Efficacy:**

$$\eta_v = \dfrac{K_m \cdot V(\lambda_0) \cdot \Phi_e}{P_{el}} = \dfrac{683 \times 0.09 \times 0.700}{1.120} \approx \dfrac{43.02}{1.120} \approx 38.4\,\text{lm/W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real-world blue LEDs yield $\sim 30$–$50\,\text{lm/W}$. The discrepancy arises because the narrow SPD approximation over-estimates; integrating the exact SPD gives a lower value.

## 3.4 Junction-Temperature Derivation (for Lifetime Estimation)

**Step 1 — Electrical input power:**

$$P_{el} = V_F \cdot I_F$$

**Step 2 — Heat to be dissipated:**

$$P_{th} = P_{el} - P_{opt}$$

**Step 3 — Temperature rise across thermal path** (junction → ambient):

$$\Delta T_{j-a} = P_{th} \cdot R_{th(j-a)}$$

**Step 4 — Final junction temperature:**

$$\boxed{T_j = T_a + P_{th} \cdot R_{th(j-a)}}$$

**Step 5 — Lifetime estimate using Arrhenius-like model:**

A common rule: every 10 °C rise halves the LED lifetime $L$. Thus:

$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = 2^{-(T_{j,2} - T_{j,1})/10}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Cross-Section of a Conventional 5 mm LED (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> A physical cross-section is rendered as a labelled block-level functional schematic because Mermaid cannot natively draw 3D layered hardware geometries.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cathode["CATHODE LEAD - LARGE FLAG"]
        cathodeNode[CATHODE LEAD - LARGE FLAG]
    end
    subgraph anode["ANODE LEAD - SMALL FLAG"]
        anodeNode[ANODE LEAD - SMALL FLAG]
    end
    subgraph diecup["REFLECTOR CUP - POLISHED PARABOLA"]
        chipNode[SEMICONDUCTOR DIE ACTIVE LAYER]
        bondwireNode[GOLD BOND WIRE 25 MICRON]
        silverNode[EPOXY SUBMOUNT]
    end
    subgraph lens["EPOXY LENS n = 1.5 INDEX MATCHING"]
        encapsulantNode[TRANSPARENT ENCAPSULANT]
    end

    cathodeNode --> silverNode
    anodeNode --> bondwireNode
    bondwireNode --> chipNode
    chipNode -.EMITS PHOTONS.-> encapsulantNode
    encapsulantNode -.BEAM SHAPED.-> LAMBDA_OUT[OUTPUT BEAM LAMBDA = 1240 OVER EG]
    silverNode -.HEAT CONDUCTION.-> HEATSINK[HEAT SINK TO AMBIENT]
```

## 4.2 LED Driver Circuit — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACMAINS[AC MAINS 230 V 50 HZ] --> BRIDGE[BRIDGE RECTIFIER DIODE BRIDGE]
    BRIDGE --> BULK[BULK FILTER CAPACITOR SMOOTH DC]
    BULK --> PFC[PFC POWER FACTOR CORRECTION STAGE]
    PFC --> CONVERTER[DC DC CONVERTER FLYBACK OR BUCK]
    CONVERTER --> CCS[CONSTANT CURRENT SOURCE LINEAR OR SWITCHING]
    CCS --> LEDSTRING[LED STRING OR ARRAY FORWARD BIASED]
    LEDSTRING --> FEEDBACK[FEEDBACK LOOP VIA CURRENT SENSE RESISTOR]
    FEEDBACK --> PWM[PWM CONTROLLER IC TL494 OR EQUIVALENT]
    PWM --> CONVERTER
    LEDSTRING --> THERMAL[THERMAL SENSOR NTC]
    THERMAL --> PROTECTION[OVER TEMPERATURE PROTECTION CUTOFF]
```

## 4.3 White LED Generation — Phosphor-Converted Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DRIVE[FORWARD BIAS CURRENT INJECTION] --> BLUE[BLUE LED CHIP EMITS AT 450 TO 470 NM]
    BLUE --> TWOPATH{SPLIT INTO TWO OPTICAL PATHS}
    TWOPATH --> DIRECT[DIRECT BLUE PHOTONS ESCAPE LENS]
    TWOPATH --> PHOSPHOR[STRIKE YAG CE3 PLUS PHOSPHOR LAYER]
    PHOSPHOR --> STOKES[STOKES SHIFT DOWN CONVERSION 470 NM TO 550 NM YELLOW]
    STOKES --> MIX[YELLOW PLUS BLUE MIX IN ENCAPSULANT]
    DIRECT --> MIX
    MIX --> WHITEOUT[WHITE LIGHT OUTPUT CCT 3000 K TO 6500 K]
```

## 4.4 LED Performance Trade-off Map (Design Decisions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[HIGH LUMINOUS EFFICACY] --> M1[CHOOSE NARROW BAND EMITTER AT 555 NM]
    Q2[HIGH COLOUR RENDERING CRI] --> M2[CHOOSE BROADBAND PHOSPHOR OR HYBRID RGB]
    Q3[LONG LIFETIME] --> M3[KEEP TJ BELOW 85 DEGREE C USING HEAT SINK]
    Q4[LOW COST] --> M4[CHOOSE PC LED WITH YAG PHOSPHOR]
    Q5[COMPACT FORM FACTOR] --> M5[CHOOSE SMD 2835 OR COB LED]
    Q6[TUNABLE CCT] --> M6[CHOOSE MULTICHANNEL RGBW WITH DMX DRIVER]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define an LED. Why is a direct bandgap semiconductor essential for its operation?

**Model Answer:**

A Light Emitting Diode (LED) is a two-terminal, forward-biased p–n junction semiconductor device fabricated from a **direct bandgap material** that emits **monochromatic incoherent light** through the phenomenon of **electroluminescence** when current flows through it.

A direct bandgap semiconductor is essential because the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the **same value of crystal momentum** ($k$-vector). Therefore, when an electron recombines with a hole, momentum is conserved by emitting a single photon. In indirect bandgap materials (e.g., Si, Ge), momentum conservation requires a third particle — a phonon — so most energy is released as heat and light emission is extremely inefficient.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *Direct = Direct photon = Direct light.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List any six advantages of LED lamps over conventional incandescent lamps.

**Model Answer (six key advantages):**

1. **Higher luminous efficacy** (80–150 lm/W vs. 12–15 lm/W for incandescent).
2. **Longer lifetime** (25,000–50,000 hours vs. 1,000 hours).
3. **Lower power consumption** for the same lumen output.
4. **Robust construction** — solid-state, no filament, no glass envelope.
5. **Cooler operation** — less infrared radiation, safer for museums and food displays.
6. **Directional emission** — efficient optical design without reflectors.
7. **Eco-friendly** — no mercury (unlike CFLs), RoHS compliant.
8. **Instant start** — no warm-up time; full brightness in microseconds.
9. **Dimming compatibility** — wide dimming range with appropriate drivers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either Q(A) or Q(B) must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of an LED. Discuss the role of (i) the reflector cup and (ii) the encapsulant lens. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the three principal methods of producing white light from LEDs. Compare their merits and demerits. **[7 Marks]**

### **Model Solution to Q(A)(a)**

**Construction (4 marks):**
The LED package consists of:
- A **semiconductor die** (active layer) of direct bandgap material (e.g., InGaN for blue) mounted on a lead frame.
- A **reflector cup** (parabolic) surrounding the die.
- A **gold bond wire** connecting the top of the die to the anode lead.
- A **clear epoxy resin encapsulant** moulded into a hemispherical lens shape.
- A **cathode lead** (with mechanical flag identification).
- A **heat-sinking slug** at the base for thermal dissipation.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When a forward bias $V_F > E_g/q$ is applied, electrons from the n-side recombine with holes from the p-side across the depletion region. Each recombination event emits a photon of energy $h f = E_g$, i.e., wavelength $\lambda = 1240 / E_g$. This is **spontaneous emission**.

**Role of Reflector Cup (0.5 mark):** Due to the high refractive index of the semiconductor ($n_s \approx 2.5$), the critical angle $\theta_c \approx 23.6°$ means only $\sim 2\%$ of photons reach the surface directly (escape cone). The reflector cup redirects otherwise-trapped photons into the forward hemisphere, increasing useful light output by **3×–5×**.

**Role of Encapsulant (0.5 mark):** The epoxy ($n \approx 1.5$) acts as an index-matching medium, enlarging the escape cone. Additionally, the hemispherical shape acts as a **lens**, collimating the diverging rays into a controlled beam profile (typically Lambertian, $\cos\theta$).

### **Model Solution to Q(A)(b)**

The three methods are tabulated with full 7-mark treatment:

**Method 1 — Phosphor-Converted (PC) White LED [3 marks]:**
A blue LED chip (InGaN, $\lambda \approx 460\,\text{nm}$) is coated with a yellow phosphor (YAG:Ce³⁺). Part of the blue light is absorbed by the phosphor and re-emitted as broadband yellow ($\sim 540$–$580\,\text{nm}$). The unabsorbed blue light mixes with the yellow in the encapsulant to produce white light.

- **Merits:** Simple, low cost, single chip, high CRI 80+.
- **Demerits:** Stokes loss ~25% (blue→yellow), "blue-peak" hazard, fixed CCT per design.

**Method 2 — RGB White LED [2 marks]:**
Three separate red, green, and blue LED chips are driven at appropriate currents and their outputs optically mixed (in a diffuser or light pipe).

- **Merits:** Tunable CCT, no phosphor loss, theoretically highest efficacy.
- **Demerits:** Complex driver (3 channels), differential ageing causes colour drift, lower CRI 60–80.

**Method 3 — Hybrid (RGGB) [2 marks]:**
Combines a blue-pumped phosphor with a separate red LED chip to "fill the red gap."

- **Merits:** CRI > 95, high luminous efficacy > 100 lm/W.
- **Demerits:** Expensive, sophisticated multi-channel driver required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the emission wavelength of an LED and the bandgap energy of the semiconductor material. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A GaAs LED has $E_g = 1.42\,\text{eV}$. Calculate (i) the wavelength of peak emission, (ii) the photon energy in joules, and (iii) the number of photons emitted per second if the optical output power is 5 mW. **[7 Marks]**

### **Model Solution to Q(B)(a)**

**Full derivation (presented stepwise — 7 marks distributed as 1+1+1+1+1+1+1):**

**Step 1:** Recombination energy equals bandgap:
$E_{photon} = E_g$

**Step 2:** Planck's relation:
$E_{photon} = h f$

**Step 3:** Equate:
$h f = E_g$

**Step 4:** Substitute $f = c / \lambda$:
$h (c/\lambda) = E_g$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\lambda$:
$\lambda = h c / E_g$

**Step 6:** Substitute constants and convert to eV·nm:
$\lambda_{nm} = 1240 / E_g\,(\text{eV})$

**Step 7:** Engineering implication:
Larger $E_g \Rightarrow$ shorter $\lambda$ (UV/blue); Smaller $E_g \Rightarrow$ longer $\lambda$ (red/IR).

### **Model Solution to Q(B)(b)**

**Given:** $E_g = 1.42\,\text{eV}$, $P_{opt} = 5\,\text{mW} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}$.

**(i) Wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{1.42} \approx 873.24\,\text{nm}$$

**(ii) Photon Energy in Joules:**
$$E_{photon} = 1.42 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \approx 2.275 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$$

**(iii) Number of photons per second:**
$$\dot{N} = \dfrac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.275 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 2.198 \times 10^{16}\,\text{photons/s}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Many students write $h c = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}$ and substitute without converting eV to J — losing 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Missing the refractive-index discussion:** When asked about LED construction, simply drawing the diagram without explaining *why* an encapsulant is used (TIR escape cone) loses the application-level marks.
> 3. **Conflating luminous efficacy with wall-plug efficiency:** These are *not* the same. Luminous efficacy weights by $V(\lambda)$; WPE is purely optical/electrical ratio.
> 4. **Omitting thermal management:** LED answers without mention of junction temperature, thermal resistance, or heat sinking are considered incomplete in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Confusing colour of emission:** Students frequently write "white LED emits white light" — the LED emits *blue* or *UV*, and white is created by phosphor/mixing.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for LED Lamps (Module 1):**

- **LED** = Light Emitting Diode; a forward-biased direct-bandgap p–n junction device using **electroluminescence**.
- **Direct bandgap materials:** GaAs (red/IR), AlGaInP (amber/red), InGaN (blue/green), GaN (UV).
- **Photon wavelength formula:** $\lambda_{nm} = 1240 / E_g\,(\text{eV})$ — used universally in board numericals.
- **Forward voltage** $V_F \approx E_g / q$ — typically 1.8 V (red) to 3.5 V (blue/white).
- **Three white-light methods:** Phosphor-converted (PC), RGB, Hybrid — PC is most common.
- **Luminous efficacy** $\eta_v = \Phi_v / P_{el}$ in $\text{lm/W}$ — best commercial white LEDs exceed 200 lm/W.
- **Wall-plug efficiency** $\eta_{WPE} = P_{opt} / (V_F I_F)$ — best lab values > 70%.
- **CRI (Colour Rendering Index):** Higher is better; > 80 for indoor residential, > 90 for studios/hospitals.
- **CCT (Correlated Colour Temperature):** Warm white 2700–3000 K, Cool white 4000–6500 K.
- **Lambertian beam pattern:** $I(\theta) = I_0 \cos\theta$ for most through-hole LEDs.
- **Critical angle for light extraction:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_{air}/n_s) \approx 17°$ — very small, hence need reflector cup.
- **Heat sink is mandatory:** LEDs lose ~50% of input power as heat; thermal resistance $R_{th(j-a)}$ must be minimised.
- **Lifetime rule of thumb:** $T_j$ should be $< 85 °C$; every +10 °C halves the operating life.
- **Driver requirement:** LEDs need **constant-current** sources, not constant-voltage supplies.
- **Eco-advantage:** No mercury, RoHS compliant, recyclable, no UV/IR in visible-spectrum LEDs.
- **KTU "Key Comparison":** LED vs Incandescent vs Fluorescent — efficacy, lifetime, CRI, cost, environmental impact.
- **Comparison metric you must memorise:**
  - Incandescent: 10–15 lm/W, 1000 h, CRI 100
  - CFL: 50–70 lm/W, 8000 h, CRI 80
  - LED: 80–200 lm/W, 25,000–50,000 h, CRI 80–98

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, always start with $\lambda = 1240/E_g$. State units explicitly. Draw FBDs wherever construction is asked. Mention **thermal management** at least once in any descriptive answer — KTU examiners reward it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions and Units: Luminous Flux (Lumen), Luminous Intensity (Candela), Illuminance (Lux), Luminance (Candela/m²), Luminous Efficacy, Colour Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Definitions and Units: Luminous Flux (Lumen), Luminous Intensity (Candela), Illuminance (Lux), Luminance (Candela/m²), Luminous Efficacy, Colour Temperature ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Luminous Flux (Φ) — Unit: Lumen (lm) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Luminous flux is the measure of the total amount of visible light emitted by a source per unit time, evaluated according to the standardized photopic luminosity function V(λ) of the human eye. It represents the *radiant power weighted by the eye's spectral sensitivity*. $$\Phi = K_m \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_e(\lambda) \cdot V(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$ Where: - $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ is the maximum spectral luminous efficacy at 555 nm (green light). - $V(\lambda)$ is the photopic luminosity function (peaks at 1.0 at 555 nm). - $\Phi_e(\lambda)$ is the spectral radiant flux in watts per nanometer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **1 Lumen (lm)** = Luminous flux emitted into a solid angle of 1 steradian by a point source having a uniform intensity of **1 candela**. > Therefore, $1 \text{ lm} = 1 \text{ cd} \cdot \text{sr}$. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of luminous flux like **water flowing from a tap** — the *total volume* of water leaving the tap per second is flux. But that doesn't tell you how strongly it's aimed in any direction. A 100 W incandescent lamp produces roughly **1500 lumens**, while a modern LED can deliver the same lumens using only ~15 W. --- ### 1.2 Luminous Intensity (I) — Unit: Candela (cd) **Formal Definition:** Luminous intensity is the luminous flux emitted, transmitted, or received per unit solid angle in a specified direction. $$I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\Omega}$$ The **candela** is one of the **seven SI base units**. Its official definition (since 1979/2019 revision): *"The candela is defined by taking the fixed numerical value of the luminous efficacy of monochromatic radiation of frequency $540 \times 10^{12}$ Hz, $K_{cd}$, to be 683 when expressed in the unit lm/W."* $$1 \text{ cd} = 1 \text{ lm/sr}$$ **Conceptual Analogy:** Intensity is the *concentration* of light in a particular direction. A laser pointer has low total flux but extremely high intensity because all its lumens are squeezed into a tiny solid angle. A bare bulb has high flux but spreads light broadly → lower intensity. > [!NOTE] > **Solid angle (Ω)** is the 3-D angular "cone" over which light is distributed. The full sphere subtends $4\pi$ steradians. --- ### 1.3 Illuminance (E) — Unit: Lux (lx) **Formal Definition:** Illuminance is the luminous flux incident on a unit area of a surface. $$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA}$$ The unit **lux** (lx) is defined as **1 lumen per square metre**. $$1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2 = 1 \text{ cd} \cdot \text{sr/m}^2$$ **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine sunlight pouring onto a tablecloth — illuminance is *how bright the tablecloth gets lit*. If you concentrate the same flashlight beam onto a small spot versus a wide wall, the small spot has higher illuminance because the same flux is squeezed onto a smaller area. > [!TIP] > **Typical Illuminance Levels (KTU Reference):** > - Moonlight: ~ 0.5 lx > - Office work: 300–500 lx > - Reading desk: 500 lx > - Operating theatre: 10,000 lx > - Direct sunlight: 50,000–100,000 lx --- ### 1.4 Luminance (L) — Unit: Candela per square metre (cd/m²) **Formal Definition:** Luminance is the luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface (in a given direction). $$L = \frac{dI}{dA \cos\theta} = \frac{d^2\Phi}{d\Omega \cdot dA \cos\theta}$$ The unit cd/m² is sometimes also called the **"nit"** (deprecated but still used in display industry). $$1 \text{ cd/m}^2 = 1 \text{ nit}$$ **Conceptual Analogy:** Luminance is what your **eye actually perceives as "brightness"** of an extended (non-point) source. A frosted bulb and a clear bulb can have the same flux, but the frosted one (larger emitting area) appears uniformly bright from any angle — that's controlled luminance. A self-luminous phone screen is rated at ~ 600–1000 cd/m². > [!WARNING] > Luminance is **directional**. The $A \cos\theta$ term is the *projected area* perpendicular to the viewing direction — this leads to **Lambert's Cosine Law**. --- ### 1.5 Luminous Efficacy (η) — Unit: Lumen per Watt (lm/W) **Formal Definition:** Luminous efficacy of a source is the ratio of luminous flux emitted to the electrical power consumed. $$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P} \quad [\text{lm/W}]$$ **Theoretical maximum:** 683 lm/W (achieved only at 555 nm green). **Practical typical values (KTU Reference):** | Source | Efficacy (lm/W) | |---|---| | Incandescent bulb | 10–17 | | Halogen | 15–25 | | Fluorescent (CFL) | 50–70 | | Mercury vapour | 40–60 | | Metal halide | 75–100 | | High-pressure sodium | 80–140 | | White LED | 100–200 | | Theoretical max (green) | 683 | > [!IMPORTANT] > Do not confuse **Luminous Efficacy** (lm/W, source property) with **Luminous Efficiency** (dimensionless ratio relative to 683 lm/W). --- ### 1.6 Colour Temperature (CCT) — Unit: Kelvin (K) **Formal Definition:** Colour Temperature is the temperature of a **black body radiator** (Planckian radiator) whose emitted light has the same chromaticity (colour appearance) as the light source being described. For non-Planckian sources (LEDs, fluorescents), the term **Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT)** is used — the temperature of the black body whose perceived colour is *closest* to the source. **Categories (KTU Standard):** | CCT Range | Appearance | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | 2700–3000 K | Warm white | Homes, hotels, restaurants | | 3500–4100 K | Cool/Neutral white | Offices, classrooms | | 5000–6500 K | Daylight | Showrooms, jewellery stores | | 6500+ K | Cool daylight | Hospitals, printing, textile inspection | **Conceptual Analogy:** Heat a piece of iron — it first glows dull red (~ 800 K), then orange (~ 2000 K), then yellow-white (~ 3000 K), then bluish-white (> 6000 K). The hotter it gets, the "cooler" (whiter/bluer) its colour appears, even though physically it is *hotter*. A 2700 K bulb mimics the warm orange glow of a sunset; a 6500 K LED mimics noon sunlight. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Relationship Between Photometric Quantities The photometric system is built on a strict hierarchy rooted in the human eye response: **Step 1 — Radiant Flux (Φe, in W):** Pure physical power radiated. **Step 2 — Luminous Flux (Φ, in lm):** Radiant flux weighted by $V(\lambda)$ (eye's response). **Step 3 — Luminous Intensity (I, in cd):** Flux per unit solid angle → directional measure. **Step 4 — Illuminance (E, in lx):** Flux incident per unit area → "how bright the surface is lit". **Step 5 — Luminance (L, in cd/m²):** Intensity per unit projected area → "how bright the surface appears". > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner expects students to clearly distinguish **Illuminance** (incident light on a surface) from **Luminance** (light *leaving* a surface in a given direction). Mixing these is a common 2-mark loss. --- ### 2.2 Why $K_m = 683$ lm/W? The constant $683 \text{ lm/W}$ is defined such that a monochromatic source at 540 THz (λ = 555 nm) emitting exactly 1/683 W into 1 sr produces 1 cd. This anchors the photometric system to SI without ambiguity. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Defining Equation | SI Unit | Notation | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 1 | Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | $\Phi = K_m \int \Phi_e(\lambda) V(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | Lumen (lm) | $1\,\text{lm} = 1\,\text{cd}\cdot\text{sr}$ | | 2 | Luminous Intensity | $I$ | $I = d\Phi \big/ d\Omega$ | Candela (cd) | SI Base Unit | | 3 | Illuminance | $E$ | $E = d\Phi \big/ dA = I \cos\theta \big/ d^2$ | Lux (lx) | $1\,\text{lx} = 1\,\text{lm/m}^2$ | | 4 | Luminance | $L$ | $L = dI \big/ (dA \cos\theta)$ | $\text{cd/m}^2$ | Also "nit" | | 5 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | $\eta = \Phi \big/ P$ | $\text{lm/W}$ | Max = 683 | | 6 | Colour Temperature | $T_c$ | $T_c = T_{\text{blackbody}}$ | Kelvin (K) | 2700 K – 6500 K (white) | | 7 | Inverse Square Law | — | $E = I \big/ d^2$ | lx | Point source only | | 8 | Lambert's Cosine Law | — | $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ | cd | Ideal diffusing surface | | 9 | Solid Angle (Cone) | $\Omega$ | $\Omega = A \big/ r^2$ | sr | $4\pi$ sr = full sphere | > [!TIP] > Memorise the chain: **W → lm (eye weighting) → cd (directional) → lx (incident on area) → cd/m² (apparent brightness)**. This chain appears in nearly every KTU illumination problem. --- ### 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility - **Interior lighting design** uses **Illuminance (lx)** because designers care about light *reaching the working plane* (e.g., desk). - **Display and signage design** uses **Luminance (cd/m²)** because the eye perceives the *brightness of an extended source*. - **Optical instrument design** (lenses, projectors) uses **Luminous Intensity (cd)** because beams must be characterised by their directional concentration. - **Energy efficiency calculations** use **Luminous Efficacy (lm/W)** — directly determines running cost. - **Visual comfort and aesthetics** use **CCT** — colour appearance strongly affects mood and productivity. --- ### 2.5 Critical Boundary Conditions - **Inverse square law** ($E = I/d^2$) is valid only when the source is a **point source** AND the detector is perpendicular to the beam ($\theta = 0$). - **Lambert's cosine law** applies only to **perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) surfaces** (e.g., freshly painted matte walls, opal diffusers). - **CCT** loses physical meaning for saturated colours (deep red/blue/green sources) because they are *not* close to any black-body locus. - **$V(\lambda)$** is defined only for the **photopic (cone-based) vision** under normal brightness (> 3 cd/m²). For low light, **scotopic** $V'(\lambda)$ applies (peaks at 507 nm). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law of Illuminance **Starting premise:** Consider a point source $S$ emitting luminous intensity $I$ (in candela) uniformly in all directions. We wish to find the illuminance $E$ on a small area $dA$ located at distance $d$ from $S$, perpendicular to the line joining $S$ to $dA$. **Step 1 — Solid angle subtended by $dA$ at the source:** The element of solid angle $d\Omega$ subtended by area $dA$ at distance $d$ is: $$d\Omega = \frac{dA \cos\theta}{d^2}$$ For our case (perpendicular surface), $\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$, so: $$d\Omega = \frac{dA}{d^2}$$ **Step 2 — Flux passing through $dA$:** Since luminous intensity is flux per unit solid angle, the flux $d\Phi$ confined within $d\Omega$ is: $$d\Phi = I \cdot d\Omega = I \cdot \frac{dA}{d^2}$$ **Step 3 — Illuminance definition:** Illuminance by definition is flux per unit area: $$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** $$E = \frac{I \cdot (dA / d^2)}{dA} = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{E = \frac{I}{d^2}}$$ This is the **Inverse Square Law of Illuminance**. Note that $E$ is independent of $dA$ — it depends only on $I$ and $d$. > [!NOTE] > If the surface is tilted at angle $\theta$ to the incident beam, then $dA_{projected} = dA \cos\theta$ and the formula becomes: > $$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$ — this is the **Cosine Law of Illumination**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Luminance from First Principles **Starting premise:** Consider a flat luminous surface of area $dA$. It emits light. From an observer's viewpoint at angle $\theta$ to the surface normal, the *projected area* (apparent area) is $dA \cos\theta$. **Step 1 — Intensity contribution:** The luminous intensity $dI$ measured from the surface element in the direction $\theta$ is, by definition: $$dI = L \cdot dA \cos\theta$$ Rearranging: $$L = \frac{dI}{dA \cos\theta}$$ **Step 2 — Express in terms of double differential:** Since $dI = d\Phi / d\Omega$, we get: $$L = \frac{d\Phi}{d\Omega \cdot dA \cos\theta}$$ **Step 3 — Verification of units:** $$[L] = \frac{\text{lm}}{\text{sr} \cdot \text{m}^2} = \frac{\text{cd}}{\text{m}^2} \;\;\checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Special case: Lambertian (perfect diffuser):** For a Lambertian source, $L$ is constant in all directions. The total flux emitted from area $A$ into a hemisphere is: $$\Phi = \pi L A$$ *Proof of Step 4:* $$\Phi = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} L A \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$ $$= L A \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta$$ $$= L A (2\pi) \left[ \frac{\sin^2\theta}{2} \right]_0^{\pi/2}$$ $$= L A (2\pi) \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\Phi = \pi L A}$$ This relationship is critical for designing LED panels and integrating spheres. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Luminous Efficacy from Luminous Flux **Starting premise:** Luminous flux $\Phi$ is produced by electrical input power $P$. By definition: $$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$ **Step 1 — Substituting flux integral:** $$\eta = \frac{K_m \int \Phi_e(\lambda) V(\lambda)\, d\lambda}{P}$$ **Step 2 — For a monochromatic source at peak wavelength (555 nm):** $V(\lambda) = 1$, so all radiated power contributes maximally: $$\eta_{\max} = \frac{K_m \Phi_e}{P} = \frac{683\,\Phi_e}{P}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum theoretical efficacy:** If *all* electrical input power $P$ is converted to radiant flux $\Phi_e$ at 555 nm: $$\eta_{\max} = \frac{683 \cdot P}{P} = 683 \text{ lm/W}$$ **Step 4 — Real-world example — White LED:** A typical white LED: $\Phi = 1500\,\text{lm}$, $P = 10\,\text{W}$: $$\eta_{\text{LED}} = \frac{1500}{10} = 150\,\text{lm/W}$$ Efficiency relative to maximum: $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{150}{683} = 0.2196 = 21.96\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always state units** when computing efficacy. A common KTU error: writing "150 lm/W" without the slash → loses 0.5 marks. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Quantity | Quantity | Symbol | Question it Answers | Typical Scenario | |---|---|---|---| | Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | *"How much total visible light is emitted?"* | Lamp rating on packaging (e.g., "1000 lm") | | Luminous Intensity | $I$ | *"How bright is the source in one direction?"* | Headlight rating (e.g., "100,000 cd") | | Illuminance | $E$ | *"How much light falls on my desk?"* | Indoor lux measurement with light meter | | Luminance | $L$ | *"How bright does the screen appear?"* | TV brightness spec (e.g., "500 cd/m²") | | Efficacy | $\eta$ | *"How efficiently is electricity converted to light?"* | Comparing LED vs. CFL running cost | | Colour Temp. | $T_c$ | *"What is the warmth/coolness of the light?"* | Choosing bulb for ambience | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Photometric Quantity Hierarchy Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Radiant Flux
Watt W

Candle Flame
