Overcast Shade"] T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6 --> T7 style T1 fill:#ff8c00,color:#000 style T2 fill:#ffb060,color:#000 style T3 fill:#ffd180,color:#000 style T4 fill:#fff0c0,color:#000 style T5 fill:#ffffff,color:#000 style T6 fill:#d6e9ff,color:#000 style T7 fill:#9ec5fe,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **luminous flux**. State its SI unit and give the relationship between lumens and candela. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Luminous flux is the total quantity of visible light energy emitted per unit time by a source, weighted by the standard photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ of the human eye. **(1 mark)** $$\Phi = K_m \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_e(\lambda) \cdot V(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$ Its SI unit is the **lumen (lm)**. **(1 mark)** Relationship: A source of intensity **1 candela** emits **1 lumen** per steradian. Hence: $$1 \text{ lm} = 1 \text{ cd} \cdot \text{sr}$$ **(1 mark)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is **colour temperature**? Mention any two light sources and their typical CCT values. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Colour temperature is the temperature (in Kelvin) of a black body radiator whose emitted light has the same chromaticity (colour appearance) as the light source under consideration. **(2 marks)** Examples: - **Incandescent bulb:** ≈ **2700 K** (warm white) - **Cool daylight LED:** ≈ **6500 K** (bluish-white) **(1 mark)** --- ### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Define the following photometric quantities with their SI units:     (i) Luminous Intensity     (ii) Illuminance     (iii) Luminance     (iv) Luminous Efficacy   **(7 marks)** **(b)** A point source emits a luminous intensity of **200 candela** uniformly in all directions.     (i) Calculate the total luminous flux emitted by the source.     (ii) Find the illuminance on a surface placed **3 m** away perpendicular to the beam.     (iii) If the same surface is tilted at **60°** to the incident beam, what is the new illuminance?   **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) — Definitions and Units (7 marks) **(i) Luminous Intensity (I):** Luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a specified direction. $$I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\Omega}$$ **SI Unit: Candela (cd)** — an SI base unit. **(1.75 marks)** **(ii) Illuminance (E):** Luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface. $$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA}$$ **SI Unit: Lux (lx)**, where $1\,\text{lx} = 1\,\text{lm/m}^2$. **(1.75 marks)** **(iii) Luminance (L):** Luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction. $$L = \frac{dI}{dA \cos\theta}$$ **SI Unit: candela per square metre (cd/m²)** — also called a "nit". **(1.75 marks)** **(iv) Luminous Efficacy (η):** Ratio of luminous flux emitted by a source to the electrical power consumed. $$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$ **SI Unit: lumen per watt (lm/W)**. Theoretical maximum = 683 lm/W (at 555 nm). **(1.75 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $I = 200\,\text{cd}$, $d = 3\,\text{m}$, $\theta = 60°$ for part (iii). **(i) Total luminous flux emitted:** For an isotropic (uniformly radiating) point source, the flux is distributed over the full sphere, $4\pi$ sr: $$\Phi = I \cdot \Omega_{total} = I \cdot 4\pi$$ $$\Phi = 200 \times 4\pi = 200 \times 12.566 = 2513.27\,\text{lm}$$ $$\boxed{\Phi \approx 2513\,\text{lm}}$$ **(2 marks)** --- **(ii) Illuminance on perpendicular surface at d = 3 m:** Using the inverse square law (surface perpendicular → $\theta = 0$): $$E = \frac{I}{d^2} = \frac{200}{(3)^2} = \frac{200}{9}$$ $$\boxed{E = 22.22\,\text{lx}}$$ **(2 marks)** --- **(iii) Illuminance when surface tilted at 60°:** Applying the cosine law of illumination: $$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} = \frac{200 \times \cos 60°}{9}$$ $$\cos 60° = 0.5$$ $$E = \frac{200 \times 0.5}{9} = \frac{100}{9}$$ $$\boxed{E = 11.11\,\text{lx}}$$ **(3 marks)** **Note:** Tilt reduces illuminance by the factor $\cos\theta = 0.5$, exactly halving it. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **photometric chain** from radiant flux to luminance. Mention the eye response weighting function used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A white LED lamp consumes **12 W** and produces a luminous flux of **1500 lm**.     (i) Find the luminous efficacy of the lamp.     (ii) What is its luminous efficiency as a percentage of the theoretical maximum?     (iii) State the typical CCT and one application of this type of lamp. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — Photometric Chain (7 marks) The photometric system converts physical power into psychophysical quantities weighted by the human eye response. **Block diagram of the chain:** | Stage | Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Operation | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 1 | Radiant Flux | $\Phi_e$ | W | Pure physical power | | 2 | Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | lm | $\Phi = K_m \int \Phi_e(\lambda) V(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | | 3 | Luminous Intensity | $I$ | cd | $I = d\Phi / d\Omega$ | | 4 | Illuminance | $E$ | lx | $E = d\Phi / dA$ | | 5 | Luminance | $L$ | cd/m² | $L = dI / (dA\cos\theta)$ | **Eye Response Weighting Function:** **(3 marks for diagram + explanation)** The photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ represents the spectral sensitivity of the human eye under normal (cone-based) daylight vision conditions. Its key features: - $V(\lambda) = 0$ for $\lambda < 380\,\text{nm}$ and $\lambda > 780\,\text{nm}$ (invisible). - Peak value $V(\lambda) = 1.0$ at $\lambda = 555\,\text{nm}$ (green). - Curve drops to ≈ 0.5 at 510 nm and 610 nm. The constant $K_m = 683\,\text{lm/W}$ scales the conversion so that 1 W of monochromatic 555 nm radiation equals 683 lm. **(4 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $P = 12\,\text{W}$, $\Phi = 1500\,\text{lm}$. **(i) Luminous Efficacy:** $$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P} = \frac{1500}{12}$$ $$\boxed{\eta = 125\,\text{lm/W}}$$ **(2 marks)** **(ii) Luminous Efficiency (relative to theoretical max):** $$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta}{K_m} = \frac{125}{683}$$ $$\eta_{rel} = 0.1830 = 18.30\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{rel} \approx 18.3\%}$$ **(2 marks)** **(iii) Typical CCT and application:** CCT of such a white LED typically falls in the range **4000 K – 5000 K** (neutral white to cool white). **(1.5 marks)** **Application:** Used in **office lighting, street lighting, and task lighting** where good colour rendering and energy efficiency are required simultaneously. **(1.5 marks)** --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in this Topic:** > > 1. **Mixing up Illuminance and Luminance** — Illuminance is *flux incident on a surface* (lx), Luminance is *intensity per projected area* (cd/m²). Don't interchange them. > 2. **Forgetting $4\pi$ in flux calculation** — When asked for total flux from an isotropic point source, the formula is $\Phi = 4\pi I$, *not* $\Phi = I$. > 3. **Not multiplying by $\cos\theta$** — When the surface is tilted, always include the cosine factor. A student writing $E = I/d^2$ without the tilt loses 2 marks. > 4. **Writing "lm" without "lm/W" for efficacy** — Units are graded strictly. Always write $\text{lm/W}$ for efficacy and K for colour temperature. > 5. **CCT units confusion** — Colour temperature is in **Kelvin (K)**, NOT in °C. Writing "2700 °C" is wrong and loses 0.5 mark. > 6. **Using $K_m = 1000$** — Some textbooks still use the older value. KTU 2024 uses $K_m = 683\,\text{lm/W}$ exclusively. > 7. **Applying inverse square law to extended sources** — The law is strictly for *point sources*. If the source's diameter is comparable to $d$, use the *cosine cube* or exact photometric integration. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Luminous Flux (Φ)** — total visible light per second → **lumen (lm)** = $\text{cd}\cdot\text{sr}$. - **Luminous Intensity (I)** — flux per steradian → **candela (cd)** — SI base unit. - **Illuminance (E)** — flux per square metre of *incident surface* → **lux (lx)**. - **Luminance (L)** — intensity per unit *projected* area → **cd/m²** (nit). - **Luminous Efficacy (η)** — flux per watt → **lm/W** → max 683, practical LEDs ≈ 100–200. - **Colour Temperature (CCT)** — black-body equivalent temperature → **Kelvin (K)** → 2700 K (warm) to 6500 K (daylight). - **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I/d^2$ — point source, perpendicular surface. - **Cosine Law:** $E = (I\cos\theta)/d^2$ — point source, tilted surface. - **Lambert's Law:** $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ — perfectly diffusing surface. - **Luminance–Flux relation (Lambertian):** $\Phi = \pi L A$ — important for LED panel design. - **Conversion constant:** $K_m = 683\,\text{lm/W}$ at 555 nm photopic peak. - **Hierarchy memory aid:** Watt → Lumen → Candela → Lux → cd/m². - **Difference between Illuminance and Luminance:** Illuminance measures incident light; Luminance measures how bright a surface *appears* in a direction. - **Always specify direction** for intensity and luminance; flux and illuminance are integrated over an area or angle. - **CCT is for white-ish sources only** — meaningless for saturated red/green/blue LEDs. - **Typical lux levels:** Office 500 lx, Reading 500–750 lx, Operating 10,000 lx.

topics.title: Candle Power: M.H.C.P., M.S.C.P. and M.H.S.C.P. of Lamp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Candle Power: M.H.C.P., M.S.C.P. and M.H.S.C.P. of Lamp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Candle Power"?

**Candle Power** (also called **Luminous Intensity**) is the luminous flux emitted by a light source per unit solid angle in a *specific direction*. Historically, the unit was based on a standard sperm candle, but today it is defined in the SI system as the **candela (cd)**.

Mathematically, it is the ratio of luminous flux ($d\Phi$) emitted in a small solid angle ($d\omega$) in a given direction:

$$I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\omega}$$

The unit of candle power is **candela (cd)** or simply **candle power (CP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Candle power is a *directional* quantity. A single lamp can have different candle powers in different directions. The terms M.H.C.P., M.S.C.P., and M.H.S.C.P. are simply *averaging techniques* applied to this directional quantity.

## 1.2 The Three Averages — Intuitive Overview

Think of a **floodlight at a stadium**. It does not emit light equally in all directions — it beams light forward. If you stand in front of it, you see bright light; behind it, you see almost nothing. So "candle power in a given direction" can vary wildly.

To *characterize* the lamp, engineers need a **single number**. They achieve this by averaging the candle power over different geometric regions. This gives us the three fundamental averages:

| Acronym | Full Form | Geometric Region of Averaging |
|---------|-----------|-------------------------------|
| **M.H.C.P.** | Mean Horizontal Candle Power | A **horizontal plane** passing through the lamp (a circle, $2\pi$ geometry) |
| **M.S.C.P.** | Mean Spherical Candle Power | A **complete sphere** surrounding the lamp ($4\pi$ geometry) |
| **M.H.S.C.P.** | Mean Hemispherical Candle Power | A **hemisphere** (either upper or lower) around the lamp ($2\pi$ geometry) |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The Water Sprinkler

Imagine a garden **lawn sprinkler** spraying water:

- **Candle Power in a direction** = the strength of water spray aimed in that particular direction.
- **M.H.C.P.** = the *average* water spray strength measured at the same horizontal height as the sprinkler's nozzle (around the circular ring of ground it wets).
- **M.S.C.P.** = the *average* water spray strength in *every* 3D direction around the sprinkler (a full sphere of water).
- **M.H.S.C.P.** = the *average* water spray strength in only the *upper half* sphere (or only the *lower half* sphere, since the sprinkler sits on the ground).

If you collect *all* the water the sprinkler throws out in one second, divide it by $4\pi$ (the full spherical area) — you get M.S.C.P. Divide it by $2\pi$ — you get M.H.C.P. or M.H.S.C.P.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Relationship:** Luminous flux is the *total* light energy emitted per second (in **lumens**). Candle power is flux per unit solid angle. Therefore:
>
> $$\Phi_{total} = \omega \times I_{mean}$$
>
> where $\omega$ is the solid angle of the region of averaging.

## 1.4 Formal Definitions

### 1.4.1 Mean Horizontal Candle Power (M.H.C.P.)
It is defined as the **average candle power of a lamp measured in all directions in a horizontal plane passing through the centre of the lamp**.

$$\text{M.H.C.P.} = \frac{\text{Total flux in the horizontal plane}}{2\pi}$$

### 1.4.2 Mean Spherical Candle Power (M.S.C.P.)
It is defined as the **average candle power of a lamp measured in all directions over the surface of a complete sphere surrounding the lamp**.

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{\text{Total luminous flux emitted by the lamp}}{4\pi}$$

### 1.4.3 Mean Hemispherical Candle Power (M.H.S.C.P.)
It is defined as the **average candle power of a lamp in all directions within either the upper hemisphere or the lower hemisphere** surrounding the lamp.

$$\text{M.H.S.C.P.} = \frac{\text{Total flux in the hemisphere}}{2\pi}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Basis of Averaging

A complete sphere subtends a solid angle of $4\pi$ steradians. A hemisphere or a horizontal plane (treating it as a 2D circle in 3D space) subtends $2\pi$ steradians.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $2\pi$ and $4\pi$?** These are the solid angles subtended by a hemisphere and a full sphere respectively, at the centre. Any average is *Total Quantity / Total Region*. Since the region is measured in steradians, we divide by $2\pi$ or $4\pi$.

## 2.2 Why Three Different Averages Exist

The choice of average depends on **practical engineering application**:

- **M.S.C.P.** is used when the lamp emits light *uniformly in all directions* (e.g., a clear incandescent bulb) — gives the true omnidirectional characteristic.
- **M.H.C.P.** is used for lamps where measurement in a horizontal plane is most practical (e.g., a bare filament lamp, since rotation in a horizontal plane is mechanically simple).
- **M.H.S.C.P.** is used for lamps that direct light primarily into one hemisphere (e.g., a street lighting luminaire with a reflector pointing downward).

## 2.3 The Spherical Reduction Factor (Critical KTU Concept)

In practice, M.S.C.P. is *not* measured directly — it is computed from M.H.C.P. using a dimensionless multiplier called the **Spherical Reduction Factor (SRF)**.

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times \text{Spherical Reduction Factor}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{M.H.C.P.} = \frac{\text{M.S.C.P.}}{\text{Spherical Reduction Factor}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Values of Spherical Reduction Factor:**
>
> - Clear incandescent lamp ≈ **0.78 to 0.85**
> - Frosted incandescent lamp ≈ **0.90 to 0.96**
> - Opal / inside-frosted lamp ≈ **0.95 to 1.00**
> - Fluorescent tube (axial) ≈ **0.55 to 0.65**
> - Mercury vapour lamp ≈ **0.75 to 0.80**
>
> A frosted or opal globe *diffuses* light more uniformly, pushing the SRF closer to unity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield and frequently tested in numericals.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{d\Phi}{d\omega}$ | Candle power in a direction | cd (candela) |
| 2 | $\text{M.S.C.P.} = \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi}$ | Mean spherical candle power | cd |
| 3 | $\text{M.H.C.P.} = \dfrac{\Phi_{horizontal}}{2\pi}$ | Mean horizontal candle power | cd |
| 4 | $\text{M.H.S.C.P.} = \dfrac{\Phi_{hemisphere}}{2\pi}$ | Mean hemispherical candle power | cd |
| 5 | $\Phi_{total} = 4\pi \times \text{M.S.C.P.}$ | Total luminous flux | lumens (lm) |
| 6 | $\Phi_{hemisphere} = 2\pi \times \text{M.H.S.C.P.}$ | Flux in one hemisphere | lumens (lm) |
| 7 | $\text{M.S.C.P.} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times \text{SRF}$ | Reduction factor relation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{M.H.S.C.P.} = \text{M.S.C.P.} \times \cos\theta_{0}$ | Hemisphere flux relation (if cutoff angle $\theta_0$) | cd |
| 9 | $\text{Lumen} = 4\pi \times \text{Candela}$ | Unit conversion identity | — |

## 2.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering

- **Lamp manufacturers** print M.S.C.P. on lamp data-sheets to characterize luminous output.
- **Lighting designers** use M.H.C.P. to compute illumination on a horizontal work plane.
- **Optical designers** use M.H.S.C.P. to size reflectors for down-lights and street luminaires.
- **Photometric laboratories** measure candle power using a *photometer* on a *photometric bench* — rotating the lamp through horizontal planes and integrating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For a *perfectly uniform* point source (a true isotropic radiator), M.H.C.P. = M.S.C.P. = M.H.S.C.P. = $I_{any direction}$. In practice, *all* real lamps have M.S.C.P. < M.H.C.P. because of self-shading (lamp base, filament supports, gas discharge arc curvature).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation: M.S.C.P. from Total Flux

**Starting Principle:** Luminous flux in a small solid angle is the candle power times the solid angle.

$$d\Phi = I \cdot d\omega$$

**Step 1 — Integrate over the entire sphere** ($4\pi$ steradians):

$$\Phi_{total} = \int_{sphere} I \cdot d\omega = I_{mean} \int_{sphere} d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral** of $d\omega$ over a full sphere:

$$\int_{sphere} d\omega = 4\pi \text{ steradians}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$\Phi_{total} = I_{mean} \times 4\pi$$

**Step 4 — Identify $I_{mean}$ as M.S.C.P.** (the average over the full sphere):

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi}}$$

> **Physical Insight:** Dividing total flux by total solid angle (in steradians) yields the *average* candle power. This is the rigorous mathematical foundation of the M.S.C.P. definition.

## 3.2 Derivation: M.H.C.P. from Horizontal Flux

**Step 1 — Total flux confined to a horizontal plane** subtends a solid angle of $2\pi$ sr.

$$\Phi_{horizontal} = I_{mean,horiz} \times 2\pi$$

**Step 2 — Identify $I_{mean,horiz}$ as M.H.C.P.**:

$$\Phi_{horizontal} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times 2\pi$$

**Step 3 — Solve for M.H.C.P.**:

$$\boxed{\text{M.H.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{horizontal}}{2\pi}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Spherical Reduction Factor (SRF)

**Step 1 — Start with the two definitions:**

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi}, \quad \text{M.H.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{horizontal}}{2\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio** M.S.C.P. / M.H.C.P.:

$$\frac{\text{M.S.C.P.}}{\text{M.H.C.P.}} = \frac{\Phi_{total}/(4\pi)}{\Phi_{horizontal}/(2\pi)} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{2 \cdot \Phi_{horizontal}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Spherical Reduction Factor** as this ratio:

$$\text{SRF} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{2 \cdot \Phi_{horizontal}}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore:**

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times \text{SRF}}$$

> **Physical Insight:** If a lamp emits *all* its flux uniformly in the horizontal plane (and zero elsewhere), $\Phi_{total} = 2\Phi_{horizontal}$, so SRF = 1. If a lamp emits flux in 3D (e.g., upwards and downwards), then $\Phi_{total} > 2\Phi_{horizontal}$ and SRF > 1. But because M.H.C.P. typically *overestimates* the omnidirectional average (horizontal plane is usually the brightest), the conventional SRF is **less than 1**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #1 (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A lamp emits a total luminous flux of **1256 lumens** uniformly in all directions. Calculate its (a) M.S.C.P., (b) M.H.C.P. (assuming isotropic distribution), and (c) total flux in the upper hemisphere.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — M.S.C.P.:**

Using $\text{M.S.C.P.} = \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi}$:

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{1256}{4 \times 3.1416} = \frac{1256}{12.5664}$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = 99.94 \approx 100 \text{ candles}}$$

**Part (b) — M.H.C.P.** (for isotropic source, M.H.C.P. = M.S.C.P.):

$$\boxed{\text{M.H.C.P.} = 100 \text{ candles}}$$

**Part (c) — Upper Hemisphere Flux:**

For an isotropic source, the upper hemisphere carries *half* the total flux:

$$\Phi_{upper} = \frac{1256}{2} = \boxed{628 \text{ lumens}}$$

**Verification using M.H.S.C.P.:**

$$\text{M.H.S.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{upper}}{2\pi} = \frac{628}{6.2832} = 99.94 \approx 100 \text{ candles} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a *perfect isotropic* source, M.S.C.P. = M.H.C.P. = M.H.S.C.P. = 100 candles. This is the *baseline* against which all real lamps are compared.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #2 (With SRF — Real Lamp)

> **Problem:** A frosted incandescent lamp has M.H.C.P. of **160 candles** and spherical reduction factor of **0.90**. Find (a) M.S.C.P., (b) total flux emitted, and (c) the flux emitted in the upper hemisphere (assuming symmetry).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — M.S.C.P.:**

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times \text{SRF} = 160 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = 144 \text{ candles}}$$

**Part (b) — Total flux:**

$$\Phi_{total} = 4\pi \times \text{M.S.C.P.} = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 144$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{total} = 1809.6 \text{ lumens}}$$

**Part (c) — Upper hemisphere flux** (assuming symmetric distribution):

$$\Phi_{upper} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{2} = \frac{1809.6}{2} = \boxed{904.8 \text{ lumens}}$$

**Verification — M.H.S.C.P.:**

$$\text{M.H.S.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{upper}}{2\pi} = \frac{904.8}{6.2832} = 144 \text{ candles} \checkmark$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | M.H.C.P. | M.S.C.P. | M.H.S.C.P. |
|----------|----------|----------|------------|
| Geometric region | Horizontal circle ($2\pi$ sr) | Full sphere ($4\pi$ sr) | Hemisphere ($2\pi$ sr) |
| Typical magnitude (real lamp) | Highest | Lowest | Intermediate |
| Most common application | Bare filament lamps | Omnidirectional characterisation | Down-light / street luminaire design |
| Direct measurement difficulty | Easy (rotate in horizontal plane) | Difficult (requires integrating sphere) | Difficult (requires integrating hemisphere) |
| Formula relating to flux | $\Phi_H = 2\pi \times$ M.H.C.P. | $\Phi_T = 4\pi \times$ M.S.C.P. | $\Phi_{hs} = 2\pi \times$ M.H.S.C.P. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Visualization Block (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the **functional relationship** between the three candle power averages and the geometric regions of integration around a lamp.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lamp Source L] --> B[Luminous Flux Phi]
    B --> C1[Phi Horizontal]
    B --> C2[Phi Total]
    B --> C3[Phi Upper Hemisphere]
    B --> C4[Phi Lower Hemisphere]
    C1 --> D1[MHCP equals Phi Horizontal divided by 2 pi]
    C2 --> D2[MSCP equals Phi Total divided by 4 pi]
    C3 --> D3[MHSCP Upper equals Phi Upper divided by 2 pi]
    C4 --> D4[MHSCP Lower equals Phi Lower divided by 2 pi]
    D1 --> E[Spherical Reduction Factor SRF]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Relation MSCP equals MHCP times SRF]
    F --> G[Engineer selects correct average per application]
    G --> H1[Photometric Bench Measurement]
    G --> H2[Integrating Sphere Measurement]
    G --> H3[Reflector Design for Down Light]
```

## 4.2 Lamp Polar Diagram (Conceptual)

A real lamp's candle power is *not* uniform. The polar diagram (candle power vs. angle) is typically:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[0 degrees Forward] -->|Max intensity| P2[Strong beam forward]
    P2 --> P3[90 degrees Horizontal]
    P3 --> P4[180 degrees Back]
    P4 --> P5[270 degrees Behind plane]
    P5 --> P6[360 degrees Full circle]
    P6 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Polar Diagram:**
> - **M.H.C.P.** = mean of all radial points around the **0°–360° circle** at the horizontal level.
> - **M.S.C.P.** = mean of the *full 3D surface* obtained by rotating this polar curve about the vertical axis.
> - **M.H.S.C.P. (upper)** = mean of only the *upper half* of this 3D surface.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Experimental Determination of M.S.C.P. via M.H.C.P.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Lamp under test] --> S2[Photometric Bench]
    S2 --> S3[Rotate in horizontal plane]
    S3 --> S4[Record candle power I at each theta]
    S4 --> S5[Compute MHCP by averaging I values]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Spherical Reduction Factor from lamp type]
    S6 --> S7[MSCP equals MHCP times SRF]
    S7 --> S8[Total Phi equals 4 pi times MSCP]
    S8 --> S9[Lamp data sheet entry]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The SRF is determined experimentally once per lamp type and *tabulated* by manufacturers. It is *not* calculated from the polar diagram of an individual lamp — it is a property of the lamp *design* (clear, frosted, opal, coated, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the terms **M.H.C.P.** and **M.S.C.P.** of a lamp. State the relation between them.

**Model Answer:**

**Mean Horizontal Candle Power (M.H.C.P.):** It is defined as the average candle power of a lamp measured in all directions in a horizontal plane passing through the centre of the lamp.

$$\text{M.H.C.P.} = \frac{\text{Total flux in the horizontal plane}}{2\pi}$$

**Mean Spherical Candle Power (M.S.C.P.):** It is defined as the average candle power of a lamp measured in all directions in a sphere surrounding the lamp.

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{\text{Total luminous flux emitted by the lamp}}{4\pi}$$

**Relation:**

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times \text{Spherical Reduction Factor (SRF)}}$$

The SRF is a dimensionless constant (typically 0.78–1.00) that depends on the lamp's geometry and bulb finish.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is meant by **Mean Hemispherical Candle Power (M.H.S.C.P.)**? When is it preferred over M.S.C.P. in illumination design?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** M.H.S.C.P. is the average candle power of a lamp measured in all directions within *one hemisphere* (either upper or lower) surrounding the lamp.

$$\text{M.H.S.C.P.} = \frac{\text{Total flux in the hemisphere}}{2\pi}$$

**When Preferred Over M.S.C.P.:**

1. **Street lighting design** — luminaires direct flux *downward*; the upper hemisphere is essentially dark.
2. **Down-light / spot-light design** — flux is intentionally concentrated in the lower hemisphere.
3. **Indirect lighting design** — flux is concentrated in the *upper* hemisphere (bounces off the ceiling).
4. **When the lamp is mounted on a surface** (e.g., a ceiling fixture), the *blocked* hemisphere is irrelevant.

In these cases, using M.S.C.P. would *underestimate* the effective intensity in the working hemisphere. M.H.S.C.P. gives a true picture of the flux the luminaire actually delivers.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between M.S.C.P., M.H.C.P., and Spherical Reduction Factor. Explain why M.S.C.P. is generally less than M.H.C.P. for a clear incandescent lamp. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clear incandescent lamp has M.H.C.P. of **200 candles** and SRF = **0.80**. Calculate:
   (i) M.S.C.P. of the lamp.
   (ii) Total luminous flux emitted.
   (iii) Flux emitted in the lower hemisphere.
   (iv) M.H.S.C.P. of the lower hemisphere. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Write the defining equations:**

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\text{M.H.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{horizontal}}{2\pi} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio (i) / (ii):**

$$\frac{\text{M.S.C.P.}}{\text{M.H.C.P.}} = \frac{\Phi_{total} / (4\pi)}{\Phi_{horizontal} / (2\pi)} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{2 \cdot \Phi_{horizontal}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Spherical Reduction Factor (SRF):**

$$\text{SRF} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{2 \cdot \Phi_{horizontal}}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times \text{SRF}}$$

**Why M.S.C.P. < M.H.C.P. for a Clear Incandescent Lamp:**

In a clear bulb, the **filament concentrates brightness in the horizontal plane** (perpendicular to the filament axis). The flux distribution in the *vertical* direction is non-uniform — light is weak directly above and below the filament. When you average over the *full sphere* (4π), the strong horizontal values are diluted by weak vertical values. Hence M.S.C.P. < M.H.C.P., and SRF < 1. A typical clear incandescent lamp has SRF ≈ 0.78–0.85.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** M.H.C.P. = 200 candles, SRF = 0.80

**(i) M.S.C.P.:**

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \text{M.H.C.P.} \times \text{SRF} = 200 \times 0.80$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = 160 \text{ candles}}$$

**(ii) Total Luminous Flux:**

$$\Phi_{total} = 4\pi \times \text{M.S.C.P.} = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 160$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{total} = 2010.6 \text{ lumens}}$$

**(iii) Lower Hemisphere Flux** (assuming symmetric vertical distribution):

$$\Phi_{lower} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{2} = \frac{2010.6}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{lower} = 1005.3 \text{ lumens}}$$

**(iv) M.H.S.C.P. of the Lower Hemisphere:**

$$\text{M.H.S.C.P.}_{lower} = \frac{\Phi_{lower}}{2\pi} = \frac{1005.3}{2 \times 3.1416} = \frac{1005.3}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.H.S.C.P.}_{lower} = 160 \text{ candles}}$$

**Verification:** This equals M.S.C.P. (160 candles), confirming the symmetric distribution assumption. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Define **luminous intensity** and **luminous flux**. Derive the expression for M.S.C.P. in terms of total luminous flux. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lamp emits **3000 lumens** in the upper hemisphere and **2000 lumens** in the lower hemisphere. Calculate:
   (i) M.S.C.P. of the lamp.
   (ii) M.H.S.C.P. of the upper hemisphere.
   (iii) M.H.S.C.P. of the lower hemisphere.
   (iv) M.H.C.P. of the lamp (assume horizontal flux = 60% of upper hemisphere flux). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation:**

**Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** The total quantity of light energy emitted by a source per second, weighted by the standard luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ that models the sensitivity of the human eye. **Unit: lumen (lm).**

**Luminous Intensity ($I$):** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction. **Unit: candela (cd) = lm/sr.**

$$I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\omega}$$

**Derivation of M.S.C.P.:**

**Step 1 — Total flux in an elemental solid angle:**

$$d\Phi = I \cdot d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the entire sphere:**

$$\Phi_{total} = \int_{4\pi} I \cdot d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the mean intensity (M.S.C.P.):**

$$\Phi_{total} = I_{mean} \int_{4\pi} d\omega = \text{M.S.C.P.} \times 4\pi$$

**Step 4 — Solve for M.S.C.P.:**

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\Phi_{upper} = 3000$ lm, $\Phi_{lower} = 2000$ lm

**Total Flux:**

$$\Phi_{total} = 3000 + 2000 = 5000 \text{ lumens}$$

**(i) M.S.C.P.:**

$$\text{M.S.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi} = \frac{5000}{4 \times 3.1416} = \frac{5000}{12.5664}$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.S.C.P.} = 397.9 \approx 398 \text{ candles}}$$

**(ii) M.H.S.C.P. (Upper Hemisphere):**

$$\text{M.H.S.C.P.}_{upper} = \frac{\Phi_{upper}}{2\pi} = \frac{3000}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.H.S.C.P.}_{upper} = 477.5 \text{ candles}}$$

**(iii) M.H.S.C.P. (Lower Hemisphere):**

$$\text{M.H.S.C.P.}_{lower} = \frac{\Phi_{lower}}{2\pi} = \frac{2000}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.H.S.C.P.}_{lower} = 318.3 \text{ candles}}$$

**(iv) M.H.C.P.:**

$$\Phi_{horizontal} = 0.60 \times 3000 = 1800 \text{ lumens}$$

$$\text{M.H.C.P.} = \frac{\Phi_{horizontal}}{2\pi} = \frac{1800}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{\text{M.H.C.P.} = 286.5 \text{ candles}}$$

**Sanity Check:** M.H.S.C.P.(upper) > M.S.C.P. > M.H.C.P. ✓ (because upper hemisphere is the brightest region).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $4\pi$ vs. $2\pi$:** Many students divide total flux by $4\pi$ when computing M.H.C.P. (should be $2\pi$) or divide by $2\pi$ when computing M.S.C.P. (should be $4\pi$). **Memorize the geometry of averaging.**
> 2. **Confusing the SRF direction:** A student writes "M.H.C.P. = M.S.C.P. × SRF" — the *correct* form is M.S.C.P. = M.H.C.P. × SRF. SRF < 1 means M.S.C.P. is *smaller*.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Candle power is in **candela (cd)** or *candle*; flux is in **lumens (lm)**. Mixing these in a single equation loses marks.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps:** KTU examiners allocate 3–4 marks specifically for the *algebraic derivation*. A numerical-only answer (no derivation) loses 30–50% of marks.
> 5. **Forgetting that SRF is dimensionless** — students occasionally write a unit (e.g., "cd") on SRF. Always state it is *dimensionless*.
> 6. **In symmetric problems, lower + upper hemisphere flux = total flux.** Do not double-count.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Candle Power Concepts:**

- **Candle Power (Luminous Intensity)** = flux per unit solid angle; unit is **candela (cd)**.
- **M.H.C.P.** — average in horizontal plane; formula: $\Phi_H / (2\pi)$.
- **M.S.C.P.** — average over full sphere; formula: $\Phi_{total} / (4\pi)$.
- **M.H.S.C.P.** — average in one hemisphere; formula: $\Phi_{hemisphere} / (2\pi)$.
- **Total flux** $= 4\pi \times \text{M.S.C.P.}$ — this is the *single most important equation* of the topic.
- **Hemisphere flux** $= 2\pi \times \text{M.H.S.C.P.}$
- **Spherical Reduction Factor (SRF)** = M.S.C.P. / M.H.C.P.; dimensionless; usually **0.78–1.00**.
- **Frosted / opal bulbs** have SRF closer to 1 (more uniform).
- **Clear bulbs** have SRF ≈ 0.78–0.85 (less uniform; filament shines brightest in horizontal plane).
- **Symmetric lamp** ⇒ M.H.S.C.P.(upper) = M.H.S.C.P.(lower) = M.S.C.P.
- **Asymmetric lamp** (e.g., street luminaire) ⇒ M.H.S.C.P.(upper) ≠ M.H.S.C.P.(lower) ≠ M.S.C.P.
- **Steradian (sr)** is the SI unit of solid angle; a full sphere = $4\pi$ sr; a hemisphere = $2\pi$ sr.
- **Lumen = candela × steradian** (dimensional identity).
- **Isotropic source** — the *only* case where M.H.C.P. = M.S.C.P. = M.H.S.C.P.
- **M.H.C.P. is the easiest to measure** (rotate lamp on a photometric bench) — it is the *starting* point in lamp characterisation.
- **Practical values (memorize):** SRF for clear bulb ≈ 0.8; frosted bulb ≈ 0.9; opal bulb ≈ 0.95; fluorescent tube ≈ 0.6.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency of a Lamp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Efficiency of a Lamp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Lamp Efficiency

In Illumination Engineering, the **Efficiency of a Lamp** is a critical performance metric that quantifies how effectively a lamp (light source) converts the **electrical input power** it consumes into **useful visible light** (luminous flux). The standard metric used universally (and expected in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations) is the **Luminous Efficacy (η)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Luminous Efficacy (η):** It is defined as the ratio of the **total luminous flux (Φ)** emitted by a lamp to the **electrical power input (P)** consumed by it.  
> $$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$  
> **Standard Unit:** **lumens per watt (lm/W)** — a higher value indicates a more efficient lamp.

There are three related but distinct efficiency concepts you must master for KTU exams:

| # | Efficiency Type | Formula | Unit / Nature | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Luminous Efficacy** (Overall Lamp Efficiency) | $\eta_L = \Phi / P$ | **lm/W** | Useful light produced per watt consumed |
| 2 | **Radiant Efficiency** | $\eta_r = \Phi_e / P$ | **dimensionless** (ratio) | Fraction of input power radiated as electromagnetic energy |
| 3 | **Luminous Efficiency** (of radiation) | $\eta_v = \frac{\Phi}{683 \cdot \Phi_e}$ | **dimensionless** (ratio) | Fraction of radiated energy that the human eye perceives as light |

> [!NOTE]  
> The constant **683 lm/W** is the **maximum theoretical luminous efficacy** of monochromatic radiation at $\lambda = 555$ nm (the peak sensitivity wavelength of the human eye under photopic vision). This is a CIE-defined standard.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mileage of Light"

> [!TIP]  
> **Think of a lamp like a car engine, and electricity as fuel.** A car that travels **15 km per litre** is more efficient than one that travels only **8 km per litre**. Similarly, a lamp that emits **150 lumens per watt** is far more efficient than one that emits only **12 lumens per watt**. The "fuel mileage" for a lamp is measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)**, and the goal of illumination engineering is to maximise this mileage while delivering acceptable colour quality.

## 1.3 Why Efficiency Matters

- **Energy Conservation:** Higher efficiency means lower electricity bills and reduced carbon footprint.
- **Thermal Management:** Inefficient lamps (e.g., incandescent) waste up to **90%** of their input as heat, increasing cooling loads in buildings.
- **Design Economics:** A more efficient lamp allows smaller conductor sizing, lower transformer ratings, and lower running costs.
- **Environmental Compliance:** KTU-aligned sustainable design (per NEP 2020) emphasises efficient lighting in green building projects.

## 1.4 Reference Luminous Efficacies (Industry Benchmarks)

| Lamp Type | Typical Luminous Efficacy (lm/W) |
|---|---|
| Incandescent (GLS) | **8 – 15** |
| Tungsten Halogen | **15 – 25** |
| Compact Fluorescent Lamp (CFL) | **45 – 75** |
| Tubular Fluorescent (T5/T8) | **50 – 100** |
| Mercury Vapour (HPMV) | **35 – 60** |
| Metal Halide (MH) | **70 – 115** |
| High Pressure Sodium Vapour (HPSV) | **80 – 150** |
| Low Pressure Sodium Vapour (LPSV) | **100 – 200** |
| LED (modern white) | **100 – 200+** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Conversion Chain Inside a Lamp

Every lamp is, at its core, an **energy transducer**. The electrical power $P$ (in watts) supplied to a lamp is converted into three competing channels:

1. **Useful Visible Light Flux** $\Phi$ (in lumens) — what we want.
2. **Non-Visible Electromagnetic Radiation** — primarily **Infrared (IR)** heat and some **Ultraviolet (UV)**.
3. **Conduction, Convection, and Conduction Losses** — heat carried away by the lamp envelope, fixture, and surrounding air.

The block-level energy balance for **any lamp** is:

$$P = \Phi_{visible} + \Phi_{IR} + \Phi_{UV} + P_{thermal}$$

where the **left side** is the **electrical input** and the **right side** partitions all outputs. Only the visible portion $\Phi$ produces illumination; everything else is essentially "waste" from a lighting standpoint.

## 2.2 Why Most Lamps are Inefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The human eye responds to electromagnetic radiation only in the **380 nm to 780 nm** band, with peak sensitivity at **555 nm** (green). Any radiation emitted at other wavelengths (IR, UV) — even if abundant — contributes **zero lumens** to the output.  
> Example: An incandescent tungsten filament at 2700 K emits over **85%** of its energy as IR (heat), and only a small fraction lies in the visible band. This is why its luminous efficacy is so poor (~10–15 lm/W).

## 2.3 Detailed Breakdown of the Three Efficiencies

### (a) Radiant Efficiency $\eta_r$

- **Definition:** The fraction of the input electrical power that emerges from the lamp as **total electromagnetic radiation** (visible + IR + UV combined).
- **Range:** $0 < \eta_r < 1$ (typically 0.6 – 0.95 for most practical lamps).

$$\eta_r = \frac{\text{Total Radiant Flux}}{\text{Electrical Power Input}} = \frac{\Phi_{e,total}}{P}$$

### (b) Luminous Efficiency (of radiation) $\eta_v$

- **Definition:** The fraction of the radiated energy that is **perceived by the human eye as light**, weighted by the photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$.
- **Range:** $0 < \eta_v < 1$.

$$\eta_v = \frac{\Phi}{K_m \cdot \Phi_{e,total}} = \frac{\Phi}{683 \cdot \Phi_{e,total}}$$

### (c) Overall Luminous Efficacy (Lamp Efficiency) $\eta_L$

The product of the two gives the **overall efficiency** in converting electricity to perceived light:

$$\boxed{\eta_L = \eta_r \times \eta_v \times 683 = \frac{\Phi}{P}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> When KTU asks simply for "**efficiency of the lamp**", they almost always mean the **luminous efficacy in lm/W** — i.e., $\eta_L = \Phi / P$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | **lm/W** | $\Phi$ = lumens, $P$ = watts |
| 2 | Radiant Efficiency | $\eta_r = \dfrac{\Phi_e}{P}$ | dimensionless | $\Phi_e$ = radiant flux (W) |
| 3 | Luminous Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\Phi}{683 \cdot \Phi_e}$ | dimensionless | $K_m = 683$ lm/W |
| 4 | Overall Relation | $\eta_L = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v$ | lm/W | Combined form |
| 5 | Total Lumen Output | $\Phi = \eta_L \cdot P$ | lumens | Useful for design |
| 6 | Specific Power | $S = \dfrac{\sum P_i}{A}$ | **W/m²** | For lighting design |
| 7 | Power for given lumens | $P = \dfrac{\Phi}{\eta_L}$ | watts | Selection criterion |
| 8 | Utilisation Factor | $UF = \dfrac{\Phi_{utilised}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | dimensionless | Not lamp efficiency but related |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lighting Design:** Engineers use luminous efficacy to compute the **number of lamps** required to achieve a target illuminance $E$ (lux) on a work plane.
- **Energy Auditing:** Star-rated appliances (BEE 5-star) directly quote lumens per watt as a selling metric.
- **Fixture Selection:** For street lighting, LPSV (≈ 150 lm/W) is preferred despite poor colour rendering, because efficiency dominates.
- **Horticulture & Photobiology:** The luminous efficiency $V(\lambda)$ is replaced by a different action spectrum (e.g., McCree curve), and the analogous "efficacy" is computed in $\mu mol/J$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between the Three Efficiencies

We start from the **energy balance** inside a lamp. The electrical power $P$ produces a **total radiant flux** $\Phi_{e,total}$, which is then weighted by the **eye's luminosity function** to give the luminous flux $\Phi$.

**Step 1 — Write the radiant efficiency** (fraction of input that is radiated):

$$\eta_r = \frac{\Phi_{e,total}}{P}$$

Solve this for the radiant flux:

$$\Phi_{e,total} = \eta_r \cdot P$$

**Step 2 — Write the luminous efficiency** (fraction of radiation perceived as light):

$$\eta_v = \frac{\Phi}{K_m \cdot \Phi_{e,total}}$$

where $K_m = 683$ lm/W is the maximum spectral luminous efficacy.

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{\Phi}{683 \cdot (\eta_r \cdot P)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the luminous flux $\Phi$:**

$$\Phi = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v \cdot P$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $P$ to get the overall lamp efficiency (luminous efficacy):**

$$\frac{\Phi}{P} = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v$$

Recognising that the left-hand side is **by definition** the **luminous efficacy** $\eta_L$:

$$\boxed{\eta_L = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v \quad \text{[lm/W]}}$$

This is the **KTU-favourite master equation**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selection of Lamp from Given Illuminance Requirement

Given a work-plane illuminance $E$ (lux), area $A$ (m²), coefficient of utilisation $CU$, and maintenance factor $MF$, the **total lumens required** are:

$$\Phi_{required} = \frac{E \cdot A}{CU \cdot MF}$$

If each lamp has luminous efficacy $\eta_L$ and consumes $P$ watts, the lumen output per lamp is:

$$\Phi_{lamp} = \eta_L \cdot P$$

The number of lamps required:

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{required}}{\Phi_{lamp}} = \frac{E \cdot A}{CU \cdot MF \cdot \eta_L \cdot P}$$

**Total power consumption:**

$$P_{total} = N \cdot P = \frac{E \cdot A}{CU \cdot MF \cdot \eta_L}$$

This shows that **higher $\eta_L$ directly reduces the total electrical load** for a given illuminance target — the core reason efficient lamps are mandated by energy codes (ECBC, IS 3646, etc.).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A high-pressure sodium vapour (HPSV) street lighting lamp consumes **150 W** and produces **19,500 lumens**. A competing LED streetlight consumes **80 W** and produces **12,000 lumens**.  
> (a) Compute the luminous efficacy of each.  
> (b) For an installation requiring **9,000 lumens** on the road surface, compare the input power required.  
> (c) Comment on the energy saving.

### Solution

**(a) Luminous Efficacy**

For the HPSV lamp:

$$\eta_{HPSV} = \frac{\Phi_{HPSV}}{P_{HPSV}} = \frac{19{,}500}{150} = 130 \;\text{lm/W}$$

For the LED lamp:

$$\eta_{LED} = \frac{\Phi_{LED}}{P_{LED}} = \frac{12{,}000}{80} = 150 \;\text{lm/W}$$

**(b) Power required for 9,000 lumens**

For HPSV:

$$P_{HPSV} = \frac{\Phi_{required}}{\eta_{HPSV}} = \frac{9{,}000}{130} = 69.23 \;\text{W}$$

For LED:

$$P_{LED} = \frac{\Phi_{required}}{\eta_{LED}} = \frac{9{,}000}{150} = 60.00 \;\text{W}$$

**(c) Energy Saving**

$$\text{Saving} = 69.23 - 60.00 = 9.23 \;\text{W per lamp}$$

$$\text{Percentage saving} = \frac{9.23}{69.23} \times 100 = 13.33\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Insight:** Although the LED is only **20 lm/W** more efficient numerically, in a city with 10,000 streetlights running 12 hours/day, the daily energy saving is **1,107 kWh** — enough to power 30 average Indian households for a day.

## 3.4 Inverse Problem — Finding Radiant and Luminous Efficiencies

> **Problem:** A 200 W fluorescent lamp emits 12,000 lumens. If 90% of the input power is radiated as electromagnetic energy, calculate: (a) Radiant efficiency, (b) Luminous flux per watt of radiated power, (c) Overall luminous efficacy, (d) Luminous efficiency (dimensionless).

### Solution

**(a) Radiant Efficiency**

The radiated power is $0.90 \times 200 = 180$ W.

$$\eta_r = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

**(b) Luminous flux per watt of radiated power**

$$\frac{\Phi}{\Phi_{e,total}} = \frac{12{,}000}{180} = 66.67 \;\text{lm/W}$$

This is the **luminous efficacy of the radiation**.

**(c) Overall Luminous Efficacy**

$$\eta_L = \frac{\Phi}{P} = \frac{12{,}000}{200} = 60 \;\text{lm/W}$$

**(d) Dimensionless Luminous Efficiency**

$$\eta_v = \frac{66.67}{683} = 0.0976 = 9.76\%$$

**Verification using master equation:**

$$\eta_L = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v = 683 \times 0.90 \times 0.0976 \approx 60 \;\text{lm/W} \;\checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Flow Inside a Lamp

The following Mermaid flowchart visualises how electrical input power is partitioned inside a generic lamp and how each efficiency emerges from a specific node in the energy flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["ELECTRICAL POWER INPUT P WATTS"] --> n2["LAMP CORE FILAMENT ARC OR LED JUNCTION"]
    n2 --> n3["TOTAL RADIANT FLUX PHI E WATTS"]
    n2 --> n4["CONDUCTION AND CONVECTION LOSSES Q WATTS"]
    n3 --> n5["VISIBLE BAND 380 TO 780 NM"]
    n3 --> n6["INFRARED HEAT RADIATION"]
    n3 --> n7["ULTRAVIOLET RADIATION"]
    n5 --> n8["LUMINOUS FLUX PHI LUMENS"]
    n8 --> n9["OVERALL LAMP EFFICIENCY ETA L LM PER W"]
    n3 --> n10["RADIANT EFFICIENCY ETA R DIMENSIONLESS"]
    n5 --> n11["LUMINOUS EFFICIENCY ETA V DIMENSIONLESS"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: How the Three Efficiencies Interlock

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1["INPUT ELECTRICAL POWER P"] --> nA["STEP ONE RADIANT EFFICIENCY ETA R"]
    nA --> nB["STEP TWO LUMINOUS EFFICIENCY ETA V"]
    nB --> nC["STEP THREE OVERALL LUMINOUS EFFICACY ETA L"]
    nC --> nD["STEP FOUR NUMBER OF LAMPS N EQUALS E TIMES A BY CU TIMES MF TIMES ETA L TIMES P"]
    nD --> nE["STEP FIVE TOTAL INSTALLED POWER FOR DESIGN"]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Luminous Efficacy of Common Lamp Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nA["COMMON LAMP FAMILIES"] --> nB["INCANDESCENT AND HALOGEN 8 TO 25 LM PER W"]
    nA --> nC["FLUORESCENT T5 T8 CFL 45 TO 100 LM PER W"]
    nA --> nD["HID LAMPS HPMV MH HPSV 35 TO 150 LM PER W"]
    nA --> nE["LOW PRESSURE SODIUM LPSV 100 TO 200 LM PER W"]
    nA --> nF["LED WHITE LIGHT 100 TO 200 PLUS LM PER W"]
    nB --> nG["LOWEST EFFICACY POOR COLOUR FOR GENERAL USE"]
    nC --> nH["MODERATE EFFICACY GOOD COLOUR FOR OFFICES"]
    nD --> nI["HIGH EFFICACY USED IN INDUSTRY AND OUTDOOR"]
    nE --> nJ["HIGHEST EFFICACY MONOCHROMATIC YELLOW USED FOR HIGHWAYS"]
    nF --> nK["BEST COMBINED EFFICACY AND COLOUR USED IN MODERN DESIGN"]
```

## 4.4 Computation Flowchart for KTU Numerical Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["GIVEN LUMINOUS FLUX PHI AND POWER P"] --> n2{"WHAT IS ASKED"}
    n2 -->|LUMINOUS EFFICACY| n3["ETA L EQUALS PHI BY P LM PER W"]
    n2 -->|RADIANT EFFICIENCY| n4["ETA R EQUALS PHI E BY P"]
    n2 -->|LUMINOUS EFFICIENCY| n5["ETA V EQUALS PHI BY 683 TIMES PHI E"]
    n2 -->|OVERALL CHECK| n6["VERIFY ETA L EQUALS 683 TIMES ETA R TIMES ETA V"]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Examiner's Visual Tip:** Whenever a question asks you to "compare" lamps, draw a simple bar-style comparison in your answer script — even a hand-drawn axis with lamp names on the x-axis and lm/W on the y-axis earns easy method marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define the term **luminous efficacy of a lamp**. State its SI unit and explain why incandescent lamps have a low value compared to fluorescent lamps.

**Model Answer:**  
Luminous efficacy of a lamp is defined as the ratio of the total luminous flux (in lumens) emitted by the lamp to the electrical power (in watts) consumed by it.  
$$\eta_L = \frac{\Phi}{P} \quad [\text{lm/W}]$$  
An incandescent lamp has a low luminous efficacy (typically 8–15 lm/W) because most of the input electrical energy is radiated as infrared (heat) by the hot tungsten filament; only a small fraction falls in the visible band (380–780 nm). In contrast, a fluorescent lamp produces light by exciting mercury vapour, which emits predominantly at 254 nm (UV); this UV is converted to visible light by a phosphor coating — a process that is far more efficient at producing visible radiation. Hence fluorescent lamps achieve 50–100 lm/W.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**  
Differentiate between **radiant efficiency** and **luminous efficiency** of a lamp.

**Model Answer:**  
- **Radiant Efficiency ($\eta_r$):** It is the ratio of the total radiant flux emitted by a lamp to the electrical power input. It is dimensionless and indicates what fraction of the input energy is radiated as electromagnetic energy.  
  $$\eta_r = \frac{\Phi_e}{P}$$  
- **Luminous Efficiency ($\eta_v$):** It is the ratio of the luminous flux to the maximum possible luminous flux that could be obtained from the same radiant flux. It accounts for the eye's spectral sensitivity. It is also dimensionless.  
  $$\eta_v = \frac{\Phi}{683 \cdot \Phi_e}$$  
Radiant efficiency tells us how much energy is radiated, while luminous efficiency tells us how much of that radiation is useful for vision. Both together determine the overall lamp efficiency: $\eta_L = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v$.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> • Always **state units** explicitly. Writing "15" instead of "15 lm/W" can cost **1 mark**.  
> • In numerical problems, students often confuse the **luminous efficiency of radiation (lm/W)** with the **dimensionless luminous efficiency** — read the question carefully!  
> • For "compare" questions, mention **at least two technical parameters** (efficacy, CRI, life) — single-parameter comparison loses marks.  
> • Do **not** write the formula $\eta = \Phi / P$ without defining $\Phi$ and $P$ — at least one mark is allotted for proper variable definition.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)  
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **radiant efficiency**, **luminous efficiency**, and **luminous efficacy** of a lamp. Clearly state the role of the constant $683$ lm/W. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A workshop is to be illuminated to a level of **200 lux** over an area of **500 m²**. Two lamp options are available:  
- **Option 1:** Metal Halide lamp, **400 W**, **36,000 lumens** each.  
- **Option 2:** LED high-bay, **150 W**, **18,000 lumens** each.  
Assume **Coefficient of Utilisation $CU = 0.6$** and **Maintenance Factor $MF = 0.8$**.  
Calculate for each option: (i) number of lamps required, (ii) total connected load, (iii) luminous efficacy. Recommend the better option. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation:**  
Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. The result is:  
$$\eta_L = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v \quad [\text{lm/W}]$$  
The constant **683 lm/W** is the **maximum spectral luminous efficacy** defined by the CIE for monochromatic radiation at 555 nm. It represents the highest possible "light output per watt" if all radiated energy were concentrated at the eye's peak sensitivity wavelength. The product $\eta_r \cdot \eta_v$ expresses the fraction of input power converted into "perfectly visible" light, and multiplying by 683 converts that dimensionless fraction back into the practical unit lm/W.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Total lumens required on the work plane:**

$$\Phi_{required} = \frac{E \cdot A}{CU \cdot MF} = \frac{200 \times 500}{0.6 \times 0.8} = \frac{100{,}000}{0.48} = 208{,}333.33 \;\text{lumens}$$

**Option 1 — Metal Halide (400 W, 36,000 lm):**

(i) Number of lamps:

$$N_1 = \frac{208{,}333.33}{36{,}000} = 5.79 \Rightarrow 6 \;\text{lamps (round up)}$$

(ii) Total connected load:

$$P_{total,1} = 6 \times 400 = 2{,}400 \;\text{W} = 2.4 \;\text{kW}$$

(iii) Luminous efficacy:

$$\eta_{MH} = \frac{36{,}000}{400} = 90 \;\text{lm/W}$$

**Option 2 — LED (150 W, 18,000 lm):**

(i) Number of lamps:

$$N_2 = \frac{208{,}333.33}{18{,}000} = 11.57 \Rightarrow 12 \;\text{lamps (round up)}$$

(ii) Total connected load:

$$P_{total,2} = 12 \times 150 = 1{,}800 \;\text{W} = 1.8 \;\text{kW}$$

(iii) Luminous efficacy:

$$\eta_{LED} = \frac{18{,}000}{150} = 120 \;\text{lm/W}$$

**Recommendation:**  
The LED option is recommended because it requires **0.6 kW less** connected load (**25% energy saving**), has higher luminous efficacy (120 vs 90 lm/W), and typically longer life (50,000 hrs vs 15,000 hrs for metal halide). Although more lamps are needed (12 vs 6), the lifetime operating cost is significantly lower.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative  
**[KTU University Exam — June 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of efficiencies** used to characterise the performance of a lamp. Draw a neat energy flow diagram. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 100 W incandescent lamp has a luminous efficacy of **12 lm/W**. If **85% of the input power** is radiated as radiant energy, calculate: (i) total luminous flux, (ii) radiant efficiency, (iii) luminous efficiency (dimensionless), (iv) verify the master equation. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Explanation of Efficiencies:**  
Refer to Section 2.3 of these notes for the full explanation. The three key efficiencies are:

1. **Radiant Efficiency ($\eta_r$):** Ratio of total radiant flux to input power.
2. **Luminous Efficiency of radiation ($\eta_v$):** Ratio of luminous flux to maximum possible luminous flux from the radiant flux (dimensionless).
3. **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta_L$):** Overall ratio of luminous flux to input power (in lm/W) — the most commonly quoted "lamp efficiency".

The energy flow diagram (to be drawn by the student) must show electrical input → radiant flux + heat losses → visible + IR + UV → weighted by $V(\lambda)$ → luminous flux.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $P = 100$ W, $\eta_L = 12$ lm/W, $\eta_r = 0.85$.

**(i) Total Luminous Flux:**

$$\Phi = \eta_L \times P = 12 \times 100 = 1{,}200 \;\text{lumens}$$

**(ii) Radiant Efficiency:**

$$\eta_r = 0.85 = 85\% \;(\text{given})$$

**(iii) Luminous Efficiency (dimensionless):**

Total radiant flux: $\Phi_e = 0.85 \times 100 = 85$ W.

Luminous efficacy of the radiation: $K = \Phi / \Phi_e = 1{,}200 / 85 = 14.12$ lm/W.

Dimensionless luminous efficiency:

$$\eta_v = \frac{K}{683} = \frac{14.12}{683} = 0.02067 = 2.07\%$$

**(iv) Verification:**

$$\eta_L = 683 \times \eta_r \times \eta_v = 683 \times 0.85 \times 0.02067 \approx 12.00 \;\text{lm/W} \;\checkmark$$

The master equation is verified.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (must memorise before the exam):**

- **Luminous Efficacy** $\eta_L = \Phi / P$ in **lm/W** is the default "lamp efficiency" asked in KTU questions.
- The **constant 683 lm/W** is the peak photopic luminous efficacy at **555 nm** (CIE standard).
- **Master Equation:** $\eta_L = 683 \cdot \eta_r \cdot \eta_v$ — this appears in nearly every KTU past paper.
- **Radiant efficiency** $\eta_r$ is **dimensionless**; **Luminous efficacy** is in **lm/W**; do not interchange them in answers.
- **Lamp ranking (high to low efficacy):** LED > LPSV > HPSV > MH > Fluorescent > HPMV > Halogen > Incandescent.
- **Number of lamps:** $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{CU \cdot MF \cdot \eta_L \cdot P_{lamp}}$
- **Total power for design:** $P_{total} = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{CU \cdot MF \cdot \eta_L}$
- An incandescent lamp wastes **~85%** of its input as IR; a fluorescent lamp uses phosphors to convert UV to visible — hence the higher efficiency.
- **HPSV** and **LPSV** are preferred for street lighting (efficacy 100–200 lm/W) despite their poor colour rendering.
- **LEDs** dominate modern installations because of high efficacy, long life (>50,000 hrs), and excellent CRI.
- For numerical answers, **always** state the unit (lm/W, %, W, lumens, lux).
- In "compare" questions, mention **at least 2 parameters** (efficacy + life, or efficacy + CRI).
- KTU ESE Module 2 typical weightage: 14-mark question (with sub-parts) on efficiency derivations + numerical, plus one 3-mark definitional question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of CIE 1931 Colour Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of CIE 1931 Colour Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **CIE 1931 Colour Space** is the foundational standardized mathematical model defined by the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE)** in **1931** that quantitatively describes all colours perceivable by the **standard human observer**. It is built upon experimental tristimulus colour-matching data and converts any spectral power distribution into three numerical coordinates (X, Y, Z) that uniquely represent a colour's appearance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The CIE 1931 system is the **international standard** for photometric and colorimetric specification of light sources, luminaires, and displays. Every quantitative specification of colour in illumination engineering MUST be referenced back to this space.

The system emerged from independent visual experiments by **W. David Wright (1928–1929)** and **John Guild (1931)**, where human observers matched monochromatic test colours using mixtures of three reference primaries (Red 700 nm, Green 546.1 nm, Blue 435.8 nm).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "Recipe Card" for Colour:**

Imagine every colour of light you have ever seen is a unique dish prepared in a cosmic kitchen. Instead of listing ingredients by name, the chef gives you a **three-number recipe card** (e.g., "32 grams of imagination, 78 grams of warmth, 12 grams of depth"). These three numbers — **X, Y, Z** — are the universal recipe.

- **X** represents how much **reddish** stimulus the colour contains.
- **Y** represents how much **greenish** stimulus it contains (and conveniently equals luminance).
- **Z** represents how much **bluish** stimulus it contains.

When you place every possible recipe on a 3D graph, you get a **gigantic colour cloud**. Slicing this cloud flat at constant brightness gives you the famous **horseshoe-shaped chromaticity diagram**, which is essentially the "top-down view" of all perceivable colours at a given luminance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it shaped like a horseshoe?** The curved arc (called the **spectrum locus**) traces pure spectral colours from violet (380 nm) through blue, green, yellow, orange, and red (700 nm). The straight line connecting the two ends is called the **purple boundary** (or line of purples), because mixtures of extreme red + extreme blue produce colours that don't exist in the natural spectrum.

## 1.3 Key Physical Standards Established

| Standard Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CIE Standard Observer** | Hypothetical human with average colour vision (defined 1931) |
| **2° Standard Observer** | Represents foveal central vision, used for small angular subtense (< 4°) |
| **10° Supplementary Observer** | Recommended for fields > 4° (added in 1964) |
| **x̄(λ), ȳ(λ), z̄(λ)** | The three colour-matching functions (1931) |
| **x, y** | CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates (dimensionless) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** The CIE XYZ primaries are **imaginary (non-physical)**. They were deliberately chosen to lie *outside* the visible spectrum so that the colour-matching functions remain strictly positive across all visible wavelengths (380 nm – 780 nm). This is a mathematical convenience to avoid negative matching coefficients.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The CIE 1931 colour space is built in **three sequential layers**, each mathematically derived from the previous one:

### Layer 1 — Tristimulus Values (X, Y, Z)

Given a light source with spectral power distribution $I(\lambda)$, the tristimulus values are obtained by integrating the product of the spectrum and the three **CIE colour-matching functions** over the visible range (typically **380 nm to 780 nm**):

$$X = k \int_{380}^{780} I(\lambda) \cdot \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

$$Y = k \int_{380}^{780} I(\lambda) \cdot \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

$$Z = k \int_{380}^{780} I(\lambda) \cdot \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

where:
- $I(\lambda)$ = spectral power distribution of the light source (W/nm)
- $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ = CIE 1931 colour-matching functions (dimensionless)
- $k$ = normalizing constant

### Layer 2 — Normalizing Constant (k)

The normalizing constant $k$ is chosen so that **Y = 100** for a perfectly diffusing white surface illuminated by standard illuminant (e.g., D65):

$$k = \frac{100}{\int_{380}^{780} I(\lambda) \cdot \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda}$$

### Layer 3 — Chromaticity Coordinates (x, y, z)

To reduce the 3D colour information into a 2D chromaticity diagram (since $x + y + z = 1$, only two are independent):

$$x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}, \quad z = \frac{Z}{X + Y + Z}$$

### Layer 4 — Inverse Calculation (Spectrum → XYZ)

For a **discrete spectral line** at wavelength $\lambda_i$ with relative power $P_i$:

$$X = k \sum_{i} P_i \cdot \bar{x}(\lambda_i) \cdot \Delta\lambda$$

$$Y = k \sum_{i} P_i \cdot \bar{y}(\lambda_i) \cdot \Delta\lambda$$

$$Z = k \sum_{i} P_i \cdot \bar{z}(\lambda_i) \cdot \Delta\lambda$$

## 2.2 The Three Colour-Matching Functions — How They Work

The CIE colour-matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda)$, $\bar{y}(\lambda)$, $\bar{z}(\lambda)$ describe how much of each imaginary primary is needed to match a monochromatic stimulus of unit power at each wavelength.

| Function | Peak Wavelength | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\bar{x}(\lambda)$ | Two peaks: ~445 nm & ~595 nm | Red component matching |
| $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ | ~555 nm | **Photopic luminous efficiency function V(λ)** — green component |
| $\bar{z}(\lambda)$ | ~445 nm | Blue component matching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Beauty of CIE 1931:** The $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ function is mathematically identical to the photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$. This means **Y = Luminance** directly. This was the masterstroke of the CIE — they unified photometry (brightness measurement) and colorimetry (colour specification) into one elegant framework.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Use / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Tristimulus X | $X = k \int_{380}^{780} I(\lambda) \cdot \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Continuous spectrum |
| Tristimulus Y | $Y = k \int_{380}^{780} I(\lambda) \cdot \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Y = luminance |
| Tristimulus Z | $Z = k \int_{380}^{780} I(\lambda) \cdot \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Continuous spectrum |
| Normalizing constant | $k = \frac{100}{\int I(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda}$ | Forces Y = 100 for white |
| Chromaticity x | $x = X / (X+Y+Z)$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le x \le 1$ |
| Chromaticity y | $y = Y / (X+Y+Z)$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le y \le 1$ |
| Chromaticity z | $z = Z / (X+Y+Z)$ | Implicit, $z = 1 - x - y$ |
| Discrete XYZ | $X = k \sum P_i \bar{x}(\lambda_i) \Delta\lambda$ | Line spectra |
| Dominant Wavelength | Draw line from white point through (x,y) to spectrum locus | Colorimetry |
| Excitation Purity | $P_e = \frac{\vert (x-x_w)(y_s-y_w) - (x_s-x_w)(y-y_w) \vert}{\vert (x_s-x_w)(y_s-y_w) - (x_s-x_w)(y-y_w) \vert}$ | Where subscripts s = spectrum, w = white |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of CIE 1931 |
|---|---|
| **LED Manufacturing** | Binning LEDs by chromaticity tolerance quadrangles |
| **Display Technology** | Calibrating monitors, TVs, and phone screens (sRGB is derived from CIE 1931) |
| **Architectural Lighting** | Specifying lamp colour temperature and CRI |
| **Automotive Headlamps** | ECE Regulations require chromaticity bounds |
| **Cinematography & Photography** | White-balance reference |
| **Printing Industry** | Converting CMYK to absolute XYZ for ICC profiles |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From RGB Colour-Matching Experiment to CIE XYZ

### Step 1 — The Original RGB Wright–Guild Experiment

Observers were shown a **monochromatic test colour** of wavelength $\lambda$ (in the range 380–780 nm) on one half of a bipartite field and asked to **adjust the intensities** of three reference primaries — Red ($R$, 700 nm), Green ($G$, 546.1 nm), Blue ($B$, 435.8 nm) — on the other half until visual match was achieved.

This yielded three **negative-valued** functions for some wavelengths (because some spectral colours are so saturated they cannot be matched by any positive R+G+B mixture; you must add R to the test side to "desaturate" it).

Define RGB matching functions $\bar{r}(\lambda), \bar{g}(\lambda), \bar{b}(\lambda)$.

### Step 2 — Transformation to Non-Negative XYZ Primaries

The CIE applied a **3×3 linear transformation** to convert the RGB primaries into XYZ primaries that satisfy three constraints:

1. **The $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ function equals the photopic V(λ)** → unifies photometry and colorimetry.
2. **All three colour-matching functions $\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}$ are strictly ≥ 0** for all visible wavelengths.
3. **Equal stimuli of X, Y, Z yield an equal-energy white** (i.e., $X = Y = Z$).

The transformation matrix used by the CIE (built from data of 17 standard observers) is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
X \\
Y \\
Z
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.49 & 0.31 & 0.20 \\
0.17697 & 0.81240 & 0.01063 \\
0.00 & 0.01 & 0.99
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
R \\
G \\
B
\end{bmatrix}
$$

(This matrix is one common formulation; alternative normalizations exist.)

### Step 3 — Why Are the Primaries Imaginary?

For the **second constraint** ($\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z} \geq 0$) to hold, the inverse transformation forces the new primaries to lie **outside the visible spectrum locus** on the chromaticity diagram:

- **X primary** chromaticity: $x = 1.0, y = 0$ (extends to red limit)
- **Y primary** chromaticity: $x = 0, y = 1.0$ (extends to green limit)
- **Z primary** chromaticity: $x = 0, y = 0$ (extends to blue limit, $z = 1$)

These are **mathematically valid** but **physically unrealizable** — hence "imaginary."

### Step 4 — Derivation of Chromaticity Coordinates

We are given $X, Y, Z$ and we want a 2D representation. By definition:

$$
x + y + z = \frac{X}{X+Y+Z} + \frac{Y}{X+Y+Z} + \frac{Z}{X+Y+Z} = 1
$$

Since the three chromaticity coordinates sum to unity, **only two are independent**. The CIE chooses to plot $(x, y)$ in 2D:

$$
x = \frac{X}{X+Y+Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X+Y+Z}
$$

Solving back for tristimulus values:

$$
X = \frac{x}{y} Y, \quad Z = \frac{1 - x - y}{y} Y
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A light source has tristimulus values $X = 25.4$, $Y = 23.8$, $Z = 18.2$.

**Solution:**

Sum: 
$$X + Y + Z = 25.4 + 23.8 + 18.2 = 67.4$$

Chromaticity x: 
$$x = \frac{25.4}{67.4} = 0.3769$$

Chromaticity y: 
$$y = \frac{23.8}{67.4} = 0.3531$$

Chromaticity z: 
$$z = \frac{18.2}{67.4} = 0.2700 \quad (\text{or equivalently } 1 - 0.3769 - 0.3531 = 0.2700)$$

**Verification:** $x + y + z = 0.3769 + 0.3531 + 0.2700 = 1.0000$ ✓

This point lies near the warm-white region of the chromaticity diagram.

### Step 6 — Derivation of Dominant Wavelength

Given a sample chromaticity $(x, y)$ and a white-point $(x_w, y_w)$:

1. Draw a straight line from white point through the sample point.
2. Extend this line until it intersects the **spectrum locus**.
3. The wavelength at the intersection is the **dominant wavelength** $\lambda_d$.

The excitation purity is the ratio:

$$P_e = \frac{\text{Distance from white to sample}}{\text{Distance from white to spectrum locus}}$$

In analytic form (using determinant formulation):

$$P_e = \frac{(x - x_w)(y_s - y_w) - (x_s - x_w)(y - y_w)}{(x_s - x_w)(y_s - y_w) - (x_s - x_w)(y - y_w)}$$

where $(x_s, y_s)$ is the spectrum locus intersection.

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Domain | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tristimulus | $(X, Y, Z) \in \mathbb{R}^3_{\ge 0}$ | 3D colour cube | Absolute colour stimulus |
| Chromaticity | $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ | 2D diagram | Colour "shade" ignoring brightness |
| Dominant Wavelength | $\lambda_d \in [380, 700]$ nm | Spectrum locus | "Hue" of the colour |
| Excitation Purity | $P_e \in [0, 1]$ | Line from white to locus | "Saturation" of the colour |
| Standard Observer | 2° or 10° | 17 observers | Average human colour vision |

## 4.4 Photometric vs. Colorimetric Specification — Block Architecture

| Block | Input | Output | Physical Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Photometric Block** | $I(\lambda)$ | Luminous flux $F = 683 \int I(\lambda) V(\lambda) d\lambda$ (lumens) | Brightness only |
| **Colorimetric Block** | $I(\lambda)$ | $(X, Y, Z)$ → $(x, y)$ | Colour + brightness |
| **Y-function Link** | $\bar{y}(\lambda) \equiv V(\lambda)$ | Y = Luminance factor | **Bridge between the two domains** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The CIE 1931 framework elegantly bridges photometry (the science of measuring visible light intensity) and colorimetry (the science of measuring colour). This unification is one of the most important achievements in modern illumination engineering and is essential knowledge for any KTU illumination technology examination.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
Define **CIE 1931 Colour Space**. Mention the role of the standard observer in colour specification.

**Model Answer:**

The **CIE 1931 Colour Space** is the international standard mathematical model developed by the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage** in 1931 for numerically specifying any perceivable colour using three tristimulus values $(X, Y, Z)$.

The **CIE Standard Observer** represents the average human colour perception derived from colour-matching experiments on 17 visually normal observers (carried out independently by W.D. Wright and John Guild). It is mathematically encoded in the three colour-matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$, which define how much of each XYZ primary is required to match a unit-power monochromatic stimulus at every visible wavelength.

The 2° standard observer is used for small angular subtense (< 4°), representing **foveal central vision**. The standard observer is essential because it removes inter-observer variability and provides a **reproducible, device-independent** colour specification.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
Explain the significance of the **CIE chromaticity diagram**. Why are the XYZ primaries considered "imaginary"?

**Model Answer:**

The **CIE chromaticity diagram** is the 2D graphical representation of the CIE 1931 colour space obtained by plotting $(x, y)$ chromaticity coordinates. The diagram has the following key features:

1. **Spectrum Locus (curved boundary):** The arc represents all pure monochromatic (spectral) colours from 380 nm (violet) through 700 nm (red).
2. **Purple Boundary (straight line):** Connects the two ends of the spectrum locus; contains non-spectral colours (magenta/purple).
3. **Planckian Locus:** Inside the diagram, the curved path of black-body radiators at different temperatures.
4. **All real colours** lie inside the closed region bounded by these two curves.

The **XYZ primaries are called "imaginary"** because they lie **outside the visible spectrum locus** on the chromaticity diagram (X at $x=1, y=0$; Y at $x=0, y=1$; Z at $x=0, y=0$). They are **physically unrealizable** light sources. They were deliberately chosen this way to satisfy the constraint that the colour-matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ must all be **non-negative** for all visible wavelengths — making colour specification mathematically simple.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | Module 2, Question 2A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and salient features of the **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram**. Discuss dominant wavelength and excitation purity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The spectral power distribution of a fluorescent lamp (relative values) is given below. Calculate the tristimulus values $X, Y, Z$ and chromaticity coordinates $(x, y)$. Use a $\Delta\lambda = 10$ nm sampling interval and the colour-matching functions from standard tables. **(7 Marks)**

| $\lambda$ (nm) | 480 | 520 | 560 | 600 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I(\lambda)$ | 0.20 | 0.80 | 1.00 | 0.40 |
| $\bar{x}(\lambda)$ | 0.091 | 0.318 | 0.595 | 0.627 |
| $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ | 0.133 | 0.811 | 0.955 | 0.381 |
| $\bar{z}(\lambda)$ | 0.677 | 0.181 | 0.029 | 0.001 |

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram

The chromaticity diagram is constructed by:
1. Computing tristimulus values $(X, Y, Z)$ from a light source spectrum using the colour-matching functions.
2. Projecting these onto 2D by computing $(x, y)$ via $x = X/(X+Y+Z)$, $y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$.

**Salient Features:**
- **Spectrum locus** — boundary curve from 380 nm to 700 nm
- **Purple boundary** — straight chord connecting endpoints
- **Planckian locus** — curve of black-body colours
- **Equal-energy white** at $x = y = z = 1/3$

**Dominant Wavelength** is the wavelength of the pure spectral colour that, when mixed with white, reproduces the sample colour. Found by extending a line from white point through sample point until it meets the spectrum locus.

**Excitation Purity** is the ratio of distances (sample from white) / (spectrum locus from white). Indicates how saturated the colour is.

#### Part (b) — Tristimulus Calculation

Using $\Delta\lambda = 10$ nm:

**Step 1:** Compute $X, Y, Z$ contributions:

$$X = \Delta\lambda \cdot \sum I(\lambda_i) \cdot \bar{x}(\lambda_i)$$

For each row:
- 480 nm: $0.20 \times 0.091 = 0.0182$
- 520 nm: $0.80 \times 0.318 = 0.2544$
- 560 nm: $1.00 \times 0.595 = 0.5950$
- 600 nm: $0.40 \times 0.627 = 0.2508$

$$X = 10 \times (0.0182 + 0.2544 + 0.5950 + 0.2508) = 10 \times 1.1184 = 11.184$$

**Step 2:** Compute Y:

For each row:
- 480 nm: $0.20 \times 0.133 = 0.0266$
- 520 nm: $0.80 \times 0.811 = 0.6488$
- 560 nm: $1.00 \times 0.955 = 0.9550$
- 600 nm: $0.40 \times 0.381 = 0.1524$

$$Y = 10 \times (0.0266 + 0.6488 + 0.9550 + 0.1524) = 10 \times 1.7828 = 17.828$$

**Step 3:** Compute Z:

For each row:
- 480 nm: $0.20 \times 0.677 = 0.1354$
- 520 nm: $0.80 \times 0.181 = 0.1448$
- 560 nm: $1.00 \times 0.029 = 0.0290$
- 600 nm: $0.40 \times 0.001 = 0.0004$

$$Z = 10 \times (0.1354 + 0.1448 + 0.0290 + 0.0004) = 10 \times 0.3096 = 3.096$$

**Step 4:** Sum of tristimulus values:

$$X + Y + Z = 11.184 + 17.828 + 3.096 = 32.108$$

**Step 5:** Chromaticity coordinates:

$$x = \frac{X}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{11.184}{32.108} = 0.3483$$

$$y = \frac{Y}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{17.828}{32.108} = 0.5552$$

$$z = \frac{Z}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{3.096}{32.108} = 0.0964$$

**Verification:** $x + y + z = 0.3483 + 0.5552 + 0.0964 = 0.9999 \approx 1.000$ ✓

**Answer:** $X = 11.18$, $Y = 17.83$, $Z = 3.10$; chromaticity $(x, y) = (0.348, 0.555)$. This point lies in the **green** region of the chromaticity diagram, consistent with a fluorescent lamp peaking at 560 nm.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | Module 2, Question 2B]**

**(a)** Explain the **Grassmann's Laws** governing additive colour mixing. Show how they lead to the linear structure of the CIE XYZ tristimulus system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two light sources A and B have chromaticity coordinates $A = (0.40, 0.45)$ and $B = (0.20, 0.20)$. Their luminances are $Y_A = 80$ cd/m² and $Y_B = 40$ cd/m².  
(i) Calculate the tristimulus values $(X_A, Y_A, Z_A)$ and $(X_B, Y_B, Z_B)$.  
(ii) If they are additively mixed in equal luminance proportions, find the chromaticity of the mixture. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Grassmann's Laws

**Grassmann's Laws of additive colour mixing** (1853) establish that human colour perception obeys linear algebra. They are:

1. **Law of Additive Mixture:** To specify a colour, three independent variables suffice.
