2. **Law of Proportionality:** If a stimulus is scaled by intensity $k$, the tristimulus values scale identically.
3. **Law of Additivity (Superposition):** The tristimulus values of a mixture equal the sum of individual tristimulus values: $(X_{mix}, Y_{mix}, Z_{mix}) = (X_1 + X_2, Y_1 + Y_2, Z_1 + Z_2)$.
4. **Law of Linearity:** The mixture of two colours lies on the straight line connecting them in colour space.

**Consequence for CIE XYZ:**

Because tristimulus values are **linear functionals** of the spectral power distribution $I(\lambda)$, integration and summation can be interchanged freely. This is why:

$$X_{mix} = \int I_{mix}(\lambda) \bar{x}(\lambda) d\lambda = \int (I_1(\lambda) + I_2(\lambda)) \bar{x}(\lambda) d\lambda = X_1 + X_2$$

The linearity of integration, combined with Grassmann's laws, guarantees that the **3D XYZ colour space is a true vector space** in which additive mixtures behave as vector sums. This mathematical structure makes the CIE 1931 system both physically meaningful and computationally tractable.

#### Part (b) — Chromaticity Calculation

**Step 1:** Compute $X_A, Z_A$ from chromaticity and $Y_A$:

$$X_A = \frac{x_A}{y_A} \cdot Y_A = \frac{0.40}{0.45} \times 80 = 71.11$$

$$Z_A = \frac{1 - x_A - y_A}{y_A} \cdot Y_A = \frac{1 - 0.40 - 0.45}{0.45} \times 80 = \frac{0.15}{0.45} \times 80 = 26.67$$

Therefore: $(X_A, Y_A, Z_A) = (71.11, 80.00, 26.67)$

**Step 2:** Compute $X_B, Z_B$:

$$X_B = \frac{x_B}{y_B} \cdot Y_B = \frac{0.20}{0.20} \times 40 = 40.00$$

$$Z_B = \frac{1 - x_B - y_B}{y_B} \cdot Y_B = \frac{1 - 0.20 - 0.20}{0.20} \times 40 = \frac{0.60}{0.20} \times 40 = 120.00$$

Therefore: $(X_B, Y_B, Z_B) = (40.00, 40.00, 120.00)$

**Step 3:** Equal-luminance additive mixture:

$$X_{mix} = X_A + X_B = 71.11 + 40.00 = 111.11$$

$$Y_{mix} = Y_A + Y_B = 80 + 40 = 120.00$$

$$Z_{mix} = Z_A + Z_B = 26.67 + 120.00 = 146.67$$

**Step 4:** Chromaticity of mixture:

$$x_{mix} = \frac{X_{mix}}{X_{mix} + Y_{mix} + Z_{mix}} = \frac{111.11}{111.11 + 120.00 + 146.67} = \frac{111.11}{377.78} = 0.2941$$

$$y_{mix} = \frac{Y_{mix}}{X_{mix} + Y_{mix} + Z_{mix}} = \frac{120.00}{377.78} = 0.3176$$

**Answer:** $(x_{mix}, y_{mix}) = (0.294, 0.318)$

**Verification:** The mixture chromaticity lies on the straight line between A and B in the chromaticity diagram, closer to B (which has higher Z). ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the normalizing constant $k$** — Many students compute only $\int I(\lambda)\bar{x}(\lambda)d\lambda$ without dividing by $\int I(\lambda)\bar{y}(\lambda)d\lambda$. Without $k$, your $Y$ will not equal the requested luminance.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in chromaticity** — Forgetting that $z = 1 - x - y$ instead of computing it independently. Marks are deducted if you compute $z$ as $Z/(X+Y+Z)$ but arithmetic is off.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the standard observer angle** — 2° observer is for small fields (< 4°), 10° observer for large fields (> 4°). Mixing them in a calculation problem results in wrong $\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}$ values.
>
> 4. **Units confusion** — Tristimulus values $X, Y, Z$ are dimensionless ratios. Luminance $Y$ has units of cd/m². Don't confuse them.
>
> 5. **Integration limits** — Always use **380 nm to 780 nm** (or 400 nm to 700 nm in simplified problems). Exceeding these bounds is a common error.
>
> 6. **Skipping verification** — Always verify $x + y + z = 1$. Examiners specifically look for this final sanity check.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CIE 1931** = Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage standard colour space, established 1931 from Wright & Guild experiments.
- **Three pillars**: (1) Standard Observer, (2) Colour-Matching Functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$, (3) XYZ Tristimulus Values.
- **XYZ primaries are imaginary** (non-physical) — chosen to ensure non-negative matching functions.
- **The $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ function = photopic $V(\lambda)$** — meaning Y = luminance directly (this is the key unification).
- **Chromaticity coordinates**: $x + y + z = 1$, so 2D chromaticity diagram is sufficient for "colour" specification.
- **Standard observers**: 2° (foveal, < 4°) and 10° (supplementary, > 4°).
- **Spectrum locus** (curved boundary) + **Purple boundary** (straight chord) = full visible colour gamut.
- **Dominant Wavelength** = spectral hue; **Excitation Purity** = saturation indicator.
- **Grassmann's Laws** justify linear superposition of tristimulus values for additive mixtures.
- **KTU-Favourite Question Pattern**: (a) Draw/Explain chromaticity diagram (7 marks) + (b) Calculate tristimulus/chromaticity from given SPD table (7 marks).
- **Memorize**: $x = X/(X+Y+Z)$, $y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$, $z = Z/(X+Y+Z)$, and $X = (x/y)Y$, $Z = ((1-x-y)/y)Y$ for inverse calculation.
- **Integration range** for KTU problems: typically 380 nm – 780 nm with $\Delta\lambda = 5$ nm or 10 nm.
- **Verification check**: Always end your solution by confirming $x + y + z = 1.000$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws of Illumination: Inverse Square Law of Illumination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Laws of Illumination: Inverse Square Law of Illumination ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inverse Square Law of Illumination (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):** > *The illumination ($E$) produced upon a surface by a point source of light is **inversely proportional** to the **square of the distance** ($d$) between the source and the surface, provided the surface is held **perpendicular** to the direction of the incident light rays.* In SI units, the law is mathematically expressed as: $$E \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$$ $$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ Where the physical quantities and standard metrics are: - **$E$** = Illumination on the surface, measured in **lux (lx)** or **lumens per square meter (lm/m²)**. - **$I$** = Luminous intensity of the point source, measured in **candela (cd)**. - **$d$** = Perpendicular distance from the point source to the illuminated surface, measured in **meters (m)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Terminology Note:** This law is strictly valid only for a **point source** of light and for a surface whose **normal is aligned** with the direction of the incident light. For a tilted surface, the **Lambert's Cosine Law** is applied as a multiplier. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are blowing up a **balloon** with a marker pen. The ink represents the total light energy (luminous flux) leaving your mouth (the source), and the balloon's surface represents the wavefront of light expanding outward. - When the balloon is **small (close distance $d$)**, the ink is concentrated on a tiny area → **bright illumination**. - When the balloon is **big (far distance $d$)**, the same amount of ink is spread over a much larger area → **dim illumination**. Because the area of a sphere grows as $4\pi d^2$, doubling the distance makes the area **4 times larger**, so the brightness drops to **1/4th**. This is the very essence of the inverse square relationship. A more practical real-world analogy is a **candle flame in a dark room**: - Hold a paper **10 cm** away → the light patch is small but very **bright**. - Move the paper to **20 cm** → the patch is larger but **dimmest** (exactly 1/4th the brightness). - Move it to **40 cm** → it becomes **1/16th** the original brightness. > [!TIP] > **Photographer's Rule of Thumb:** Professional photographers exploit this law when adjusting flash power. To double the flash-to-subject distance, you must quadruple the flash output to maintain identical exposure. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Symbol of Unit | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | | Luminous Intensity | $I$ | candela | $\text{cd}$ | | Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | lumen | $\text{lm}$ | | Illumination / Illuminance | $E$ | lux | $\text{lx}$ | | Solid Angle | $\omega$ | steradian | $\text{sr}$ | | Distance | $d$ | meter | $\text{m}$ | **Key Standard Relationship:** $1 \text{ candela} = 1 \text{ lumen per steradian}$ ($1 \text{ cd} = 1 \text{ lm/sr}$). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown The Inverse Square Law is built on a **fundamental geometric principle**: light from a point source radiates uniformly in all directions, forming an expanding **spherical wavefront**. The logic can be broken into five rigorous steps: 1. **Point Source Assumption:** The luminous source is mathematically idealized to have zero physical dimensions, so rays diverge radially from a single point in space. 2. **Uniform Spherical Propagation:** The total luminous flux ($\Phi$ lumens) is distributed evenly over the surface area of any sphere centered on the source. 3. **Surface Area Growth:** At radius $d$, the surface area of the sphere is $4\pi d^2$. As $d$ increases, the area grows with the **square of the distance**. 4. **Flux Dilution:** Since the same total flux $\Phi$ is spread over a progressively larger area, the flux per unit area (illumination $E$) must decrease. 5. **Square Relationship:** The ratio of area at distance $d_2$ to area at distance $d_1$ is $(d_2/d_1)^2$, so the illumination ratio is the inverse of this — i.e., $(d_1/d_2)^2$. --- ### 2.2 Why and How — The Governing Physics - **Why "Inverse" and not "Linear"?** Because in 3D space, energy from a point spreads over an area that grows quadratically (proportional to $d^2$). In 1D (a line source) it would be $1/d$, and in 2D (a planar source) it would be constant. - **How is it measured?** A luxmeter placed at distance $d$ directly measures $E$. The luminous intensity $I$ is then back-calculated as $I = E \times d^2$. - **Engineering Utility:** Used to determine **luminaire mounting heights** in street lighting, stadium lighting, and indoor office illumination design. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU Board-Exam essentials. Memorize the boundary conditions carefully.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Boundary Conditions | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ | Basic Inverse Square Law (perpendicular surface) | Point source, normal incidence | | 2 | $\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$ | Ratio form for comparing two distances | Same source, normal surface | | 3 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}\cos\theta$ | When the surface is **inclined** (Lambert's Cosine multiplied) | $\theta$ is angle of incidence | | 4 | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi}$ | Luminous intensity from total flux of isotropic source | Uniform spherical source | | 5 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2}$ | Illumination from a source of total flux $\Phi$ | Isotropic point source | | 6 | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{E}}$ | Distance required to produce a desired illumination | Normal incidence | | 7 | $I = E \times d^2$ | Recovering intensity from measured illumination | Standard inverse-square derivation | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Notation Rule (KTU Board):** When writing ratio form $\dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$, always ensure the distance terms are **flipped** (cross-multiplied). A common student error is writing $\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \left(\dfrac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2$ which is the **wrong** direction and leads to zero marks. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Street Lighting Design:** Engineers use the law to space light poles. If a lamp produces 1000 cd axially, the illumination on the road directly below at 8 m height is $1000/64 = 15.6 \text{ lx}$. - **Cinema Projector Focus:** Projectionists adjust throw distance to achieve the required screen luminance for the inverse square fall-off. - **Solar Photovoltaic Sizing:** Although solar radiation is not strictly a point source at Earth's distance, the inverse square principle is fundamental in photovoltaic engineering to estimate solar constant ($1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ at 1 AU). - **Optical Bench Experiments:** The law is the backbone of the **photometric bench** used in the KTU lab to measure luminous intensity of unknown lamps by comparing with a standard lamp. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Inverse Square Law **Statement to Prove:** The illumination on a surface held perpendicular to the rays from a point source varies inversely as the square of the distance from the source. **Given Setup:** - A point light source $S$ with luminous intensity $I$ candela (uniform in all directions). - Consider an imaginary sphere of radius $d$ centered at $S$. - A small patch of area $dA$ on the sphere's surface, lying perpendicular to the radial direction. **Step 1 — Solid Angle Definition:** The solid angle $\omega$ subtended at the source $S$ by the area $dA$ on the sphere is defined as the area on the sphere divided by the square of the radius: $$\omega = \frac{dA}{d^2}$$ *Explanation:* This is the 3D analogue of the planar angle (radians). In 2D, a planar angle is arc/radius; in 3D, a solid angle is area/radius². Its unit is the **steradian (sr)**. **Step 2 — Luminous Flux through $dA$:** By the definition of luminous intensity, the flux $d\Phi$ emitted by the source within a solid angle $\omega$ is: $$d\Phi = I \cdot \omega$$ Substituting $\omega = dA/d^2$: $$d\Phi = I \cdot \frac{dA}{d^2}$$ *Explanation:* One candela is defined as one lumen per steradian. Multiplying intensity by the solid angle collects all the lumens flowing through that cone of light. **Step 3 — Definition of Illumination:** Illumination $E$ is defined as the luminous flux received **per unit area** of the illuminated surface: $$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA}$$ **Step 4 — Final Substitution:** Substituting $d\Phi$ from Step 2 into the illumination equation: $$E = \frac{I \cdot \frac{dA}{d^2}}{dA}$$ The $dA$ terms cancel out: $$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ **Conclusion:** $\boxed{E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}}$, which is the Inverse Square Law of Illumination. Since $I$ is a constant for a given source, $E \propto 1/d^2$, proving the law. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Ratio Form (Used in Comparison Problems) **Starting Point:** At two distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from the same source: $$E_1 = \frac{I}{d_1^2}, \quad E_2 = \frac{I}{d_2^2}$$ Dividing the two equations: $$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{\dfrac{I}{d_1^2}}{\dfrac{I}{d_2^2}}$$ The luminous intensity $I$ cancels: $$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{d_2^2}{d_1^2}$$ Rewriting as a squared ratio: $$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$$ *Engineering Insight:* This is the most frequently tested form in KTU numerical problems. Notice the **inversion** of the distance subscripts. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Valuation Insight:** The examiner follows a strict 3-step marking scheme: (1) Formula statement — 2 marks, (2) Substitution — 2 marks, (3) Final numerical answer with units — 3 marks. Skipping any step costs partial marks. **Standard Problem Template:** *"A lamp of luminous intensity $I$ cd is placed at height $h$ above a working plane. Find the illumination directly below the lamp. If the working plane is moved to a new distance, find the new illumination."* **Solution Algorithm:** 1. Identify the given: source intensity $I$ in candela, distance $d$ in meters. 2. Apply $E = I/d^2$ for the first configuration. 3. For the second configuration, use the ratio form $E_2 = E_1 \times (d_1/d_2)^2$ to avoid recomputing $I$. 4. State the final answer in **lux (lx)** with proper units. **Numerical Worked Example (KTU Model):** *Problem:* A lamp emits light with a luminous intensity of 500 candela uniformly in all directions. Calculate the illumination on a surface placed (a) 2 m away, and (b) 4 m away, perpendicular to the light rays. *Solution:* **Part (a):** At $d = 2 \text{ m}$: $$E_1 = \frac{I}{d_1^2} = \frac{500}{(2)^2} = \frac{500}{4}$$ $$\boxed{E_1 = 125 \text{ lux}}$$ **Part (b):** At $d = 4 \text{ m}$: $$E_2 = \frac{I}{d_2^2} = \frac{500}{(4)^2} = \frac{500}{16}$$ $$\boxed{E_2 = 31.25 \text{ lux}}$$ **Verification via Ratio Form:** $$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{2}\right)^2 = 4$$ $$E_2 = \frac{E_1}{4} = \frac{125}{4} = 31.25 \text{ lux} \quad \checkmark$$ **Interpretation:** When the distance is doubled (2 m → 4 m), the illumination drops to one-fourth (125 → 31.25 lx), confirming the inverse square behavior perfectly. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Geometric Visualization of the Inverse Square Law Below is a Mermaid block diagram illustrating the radial propagation of light from a point source, the spherical wavefronts, and the geometric relationships used in the derivation. ```mermaid graph TD A[Point Source S
Luminous Intensity I candela] --> B[Emission of light rays
in all directions] B --> C[Spherical Wavefront at distance d1
Surface Area equals 4 times pi times d1 squared] B --> D[Spherical Wavefront at distance d2 greater than d1
Surface Area equals 4 times pi times d2 squared] C --> E[Illumination E1 equals I divided by d1 squared
Flux per unit area is HIGH] D --> F[Illumination E2 equals I divided by d2 squared
Flux per unit area is LOW] E --> G[Same total flux Phi
Spread over SMALLER area] F --> H[Same total flux Phi
Spread over LARGER area] G --> I[Result: E1 is GREATER than E2] H --> I I --> J[Final Relationship
E1 by E2 equals d2 by d1 squared] ``` --- ### 4.2 Photometric Bench Block Diagram (Practical Application) The following functional architecture shows how the Inverse Square Law is **operationally used in the KTU Photometric Lab** to measure the luminous intensity of an unknown lamp. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Bench[Photometric Bench Setup] SL[Standard Lamp
Known Intensity I sub s] -->|At distance d sub s| PD[Photometric Sensor
Luxmeter Head] TL[Test Lamp
Unknown Intensity I sub t] -->|Slided to distance d sub t| PD end PD --> JR[Jockeying for
Equal Lux Reading
E sub s equals E sub t] JR --> CF[Apply Inverse Square Law
I sub s by d sub s squared equals I sub t by d sub t squared] CF --> RES[Solve for I sub t equals I sub s times d sub t by d sub s squared] RES --> OUT[Unknown Luminous Intensity
Computed in candela] ``` --- ### 4.3 Comparative Illumination vs. Distance Plot (Conceptual Map) ```mermaid graph TD P0[At d equals 0: Illumination is INFINITE] --> P1[At d equals 1 m: E equals I] P1 --> P2[At d equals 2 m: E equals I by 4] P2 --> P3[At d equals 3 m: E equals I by 9] P3 --> P4[At d equals 4 m: E equals I by 16] P4 --> P5[Curve is a HYPERBOLA
of the form y equals k by x squared] ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Takeaway:** If you plot $E$ (y-axis) versus $d$ (x-axis), the curve is a rectangular hyperbola. As $d \to 0$, $E \to \infty$ (mathematically). In practice, the law breaks down at very short distances because the source can no longer be treated as a "point." ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]** *State the Inverse Square Law of Illumination. Mention the two essential conditions under which it is valid.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Statement:** The illumination ($E$) on a surface produced by a point source of light is directly proportional to the luminous intensity ($I$) of the source and inversely proportional to the square of the distance ($d$) between the source and the surface, provided the surface is normal to the incident light. > > **Mathematical Form:** $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ > > **Two Essential Conditions:** > 1. The light source must be a **true point source** (negligible physical dimensions). > 2. The illuminated surface must be held **perpendicular (normal)** to the direction of the incident light rays. > > *(Word count: ~60 words — exact length expected for 3-mark answers in KTU ESE.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "luminous intensity" and state its SI unit. How is it related to illumination and distance?* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** Luminous intensity is the luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction. > > **SI Unit:** **candela (cd)**, where $1 \text{ cd} = 1 \text{ lumen/steradian}$. > > **Relation:** From the inverse square law, $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$, hence $I = E \times d^2$. Intensity is numerically equal to the illumination produced on a surface placed 1 meter perpendicular from the source. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) --- **Question A — Choice 1: [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the Inverse Square Law of Illumination from first principles, clearly defining the solid angle concept. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A lamp with a uniform luminous intensity of 800 candela is suspended at a height of 4 meters above a horizontal table. Find (i) the illumination directly below the lamp, and (ii) the illumination at a point on the table 3 meters horizontally away from the foot of the lamp. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Step 1 — Consider a point source $S$ of luminous intensity $I$ candela emitting light uniformly in all directions. Step 2 — At distance $d$, consider a small surface element $dA$ on a sphere of radius $d$. The solid angle $d\omega$ subtended by $dA$ at $S$ is: $$d\omega = \frac{dA}{d^2} \text{ (in steradians)}$$ Step 3 — The luminous flux $d\Phi$ emitted within this solid angle is: $$d\Phi = I \cdot d\omega = \frac{I \cdot dA}{d^2}$$ Step 4 — Illumination $E$ is the flux received per unit area: $$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore E \propto \frac{1}{d^2}} \text{ — Inverse Square Law proved.}$$ *(3 marks for solid angle concept, 2 marks for flux equation, 2 marks for final derivation.)* **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Part (i) — Directly below the lamp:** Distance $d_1 = 4 \text{ m}$ (perpendicular height). $$E_1 = \frac{I}{d_1^2} = \frac{800}{(4)^2} = \frac{800}{16}$$ $$\boxed{E_1 = 50 \text{ lux}}$$ **Part (ii) — At 3 m horizontally away from foot of lamp:** The actual slant distance from the lamp to this point is: $$d_2 = \sqrt{(4)^2 + (3)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$ Since the surface (table top) is horizontal and the rays are incident at an angle, we use Lambert's Cosine Law. The angle of incidence $\theta$ is the angle between the ray and the normal to the horizontal surface. The vertical drop is 4 m and hypotenuse is 5 m, so: $$\cos\theta = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$ Illumination at the tilted incidence: $$E_2 = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d_2^2} = \frac{800 \times 0.8}{(5)^2} = \frac{640}{25}$$ $$\boxed{E_2 = 25.6 \text{ lux}}$$ --- **Question B — Choice 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Two lamps of luminous intensities 200 candela and 400 candela are mounted at heights of 2 m and 4 m respectively above a horizontal floor. Compare the illuminations produced by each lamp directly beneath itself. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the experimental determination of the luminous intensity of a test lamp using a standard lamp on a photometric bench. State the governing formula. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison of Illuminations:** For Lamp 1: $I_1 = 200 \text{ cd}$, $d_1 = 2 \text{ m}$. $$E_1 = \frac{200}{(2)^2} = \frac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ lux}$$ For Lamp 2: $I_2 = 400 \text{ cd}$, $d_2 = 4 \text{ m}$. $$E_2 = \frac{400}{(4)^2} = \frac{400}{16} = 25 \text{ lux}$$ **Ratio:** $$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{50}{25} = 2 : 1$$ $$\boxed{\text{Lamp 1 produces illumination twice that of Lamp 2 directly below itself.}}$$ **Inference:** Although Lamp 2 has double the intensity, its double mounting height causes a 4-times distance penalty, resulting in net half the illumination at the floor. **(b) Photometric Bench Experiment:** **Apparatus Description:** - A standard lamp (known intensity $I_s$) and a test lamp (unknown intensity $I_t$) are mounted on a calibrated **optical bench** (a long horizontal rail with a meter scale). - A **photometric head / luxmeter sensor** is placed between them. - The bench must be in a **darkened room** to eliminate stray light. **Procedure:** 1. Switch on the standard lamp and allow it to stabilize. 2. Place the luxmeter at a known distance $d_s$ from the standard lamp and record the illumination $E_s$. 3. Replace the standard lamp with the test lamp (same position). 4. Slide the test lamp along the bench until the luxmeter reads the **same illumination** $E_t = E_s$. 5. Record the new distance $d_t$ of the test lamp from the luxmeter. **Governing Formula (Inverse Square Law Applied):** Since the illumination at the sensor is the same for both lamps: $$E_s = E_t$$ $$\frac{I_s}{d_s^2} = \frac{I_t}{d_t^2}$$ Solving for the unknown intensity: $$I_t = I_s \times \left(\frac{d_t}{d_s}\right)^2$$ The unknown luminous intensity is thus determined by simple distance measurements. *(3 marks for apparatus, 2 marks for procedure, 2 marks for formula derivation.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Direction Error in Ratio Form:** Writing $\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \left(\dfrac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2$ instead of the correct $\left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$. This single error wipes out the entire 7-mark numerical. > 2. **Forgetting Lambert's Cosine for Tilted Surfaces:** If the problem mentions "horizontal table" and the point is "offset horizontally," students often forget to compute the slant distance and multiply by $\cos\theta$. Full 7 marks at risk. > 3. **Missing Units:** Writing $E = 50$ instead of $E = 50 \text{ lux}$. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit omission in numericals. > 4. **Conflating "Intensity" and "Illumination":** Intensity ($I$) is a property of the **source** in candela. Illumination ($E$) is a property of the **surface** in lux. Mixing these terms in Part A definitions loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Skipping the "Why Point Source" Condition:** The law is valid ONLY for point sources. Failing to state this condition in derivations costs 1 mark on the KTU valuation key. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Ultra-Rapid Revision Checklist for the Last Hour of Exam Prep:** > - **Core Equation:** $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ (perpendicular case only). > - **Ratio Form:** $\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$ — **distances are inverted**. > - **Units:** $E$ in **lux (lm/m²)**, $I$ in **candela (cd)**, $d$ in **meters (m)**. > - **Two Validity Conditions:** (1) Point source assumption, (2) Normal (perpendicular) incidence. > - **Tilted Surface Modification:** Multiply by $\cos\theta$ (Lambert's Cosine Law). > - **Quadratic Nature:** Doubling distance → 1/4 illumination; Tripling distance → 1/9 illumination. > - **Isotropic Source Formula:** $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2}$ when total flux $\Phi$ is given. > - **Photometric Bench Use:** $I_t = I_s \times (d_t/d_s)^2$ for equal illumination matching. > - **Curve Shape:** $E$ vs. $d$ plot is a **rectangular hyperbola**. > - **Common Trap:** Never write $E = I \times d^2$ — that is intensity recovery, not the law itself. > - **Photographer's Intuition:** Quadruple the light power when you double the distance.

topics.title: Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination** (also called the **Cosine Law of Incidence**) is a fundamental photometric law which states that: > *The illumination (E) on a surface is directly proportional to the cosine of the angle (θ) made by the incident light ray with the normal to that surface, when the light source is at a fixed distance.* Mathematically, the law is expressed as: $$E \propto \cos\theta$$ When combined with the **Inverse Square Law**, the complete governing equation for a point source becomes: $$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta$$ where the symbols carry their standard photometric meanings defined below in the notation block. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** This law is the foundation for calculating illumination on tilted planes such as drafting boards, cinema screens, road surfaces, and the working planes of machinery. It is a **direct-frequently-asked concept** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE), typically clubbed with a numerical on a horizontal/tilted plane. ### 1.2 Notation Glossary | Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $E$ | Illumination at the point | **lux (lx)** | | $I$ | Luminous intensity of the source | **candela (cd)** | | $d$ | Distance from source to point | **metre (m)** | | $\theta$ | Angle of incidence w.r.t. normal | **degree / radian** | | $h$ | Vertical height of source above point | **metre (m)** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding Imagine you are holding a sheet of white paper under a ceiling lamp (a point source). The lamp is directly above you. * **Case 1 – Flat paper (Normal incidence):** The full circular "patch of light" projected by the lamp falls *entirely* on the paper. The illumination is **maximum** ($E = I/d^{2}$). * **Case 2 – Tilted paper (Oblique incidence):** Now slowly tilt the paper away from the lamp. Even though the lamp's output and your distance to it have not changed, the *projected area* of the paper as seen from the lamp **shrinks** (becomes an ellipse). The same bundle of light energy is now spread over the *same physical area of paper*, but the effective "catching" cross-section is smaller. The smaller the projected area, the **lower** the illumination. The reduction factor is exactly $\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the tilt angle. > [!NOTE] > **Day-to-day example:** This is why a reading desk lamp is angled to strike your book nearly *perpendicularly* ($\theta \approx 0^{\circ}$, so $\cos\theta \approx 1$). If the lamp were far to the side ($\theta \approx 80^{\circ}$), $\cos(80^{\circ}) \approx 0.17$, meaning you would get only ~17% of the useful light! ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles The Cosine Law is a direct consequence of **three pillars of photometric geometry**: 1. **Inverse Square Law:** The illumination from a point source falls off with the square of the distance: $E \propto 1/d^{2}$. 2. **Solid Angle Definition:** The solid angle subtended by an elemental surface $dA$ at a point source is $d\omega = dA_{n}/d^{2}$, where $dA_{n}$ is the area projected normal to the line of sight. 3. **Projected Area Theorem:** For a tilted elemental surface, $dA_{n} = dA \cdot \cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the surface normal and the direction of light propagation. Combining these three pillars yields the cosine law in its most fundamental integral form, which we shall derive rigorously in Section 3. ### 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Reasoning * **Step 1 — Flux emitted by the source:** A point source of luminous intensity $I$ candela emits flux $dF = I \cdot d\omega$ into any elemental solid angle $d\omega$. * **Step 2 — Solid angle of the receiver:** The receiver surface of area $dA$, tilted at angle $\theta$ to the line of incidence, subtends a solid angle $d\omega = (dA \cdot \cos\theta)/d^{2}$. * **Step 3 — Flux intercepted by the receiver:** Substituting, $dF = I \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta / d^{2}$. * **Step 4 — Illumination definition:** By definition, illumination is flux per unit area, so $E = dF/dA$. * **Step 5 — Final form:** $E = (I/d^{2}) \cos\theta$. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Significance:** Every illumination design problem on a non-horizontal plane (machine tool tables, road lighting, stadium floodlighting) uses this law. It is also the basis of the **cosine-corrected luxmeter probe**, which physically compensates for the angle of incoming light. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Condition | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta$ | General Cosine Law (point source, tilted plane) | | 2 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | Pure Inverse Square Law ($\theta = 0^{\circ}$, i.e., normal incidence) | | 3 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ | Law expressed in terms of vertical height $h$ and angle $\theta$, since $d = h/\cos\theta$ | | 4 | $d = \dfrac{h}{\cos\theta}$ | Geometry relation between slant distance $d$ and vertical height $h$ | | 5 | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{d}$ | From the right-angled geometry of mounting height | | 6 | $\dfrac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} = \dfrac{d_{2}^{\,2}}{d_{1}^{\,2}}$ | Ratio of illuminations at two points equidistant from the normal | | 7 | $I = \dfrac{E \cdot d^{2}}{\cos\theta}$ | Rearranged to find required luminous intensity of a lamp | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Street Lighting Design:** Luminaires are mounted at a height $h$ and tilted/aimed such that the useful beam falls on the road at the prescribed angle. The illumination uniformity depends on $\cos^{3}\theta$ behaviour. * **Indoor Working Plane Illumination:** Designing lighting for factory floors, drafting rooms, and examination halls where the working plane may be tilted (e.g., a tilted conveyor). * **Luxmeter Calibration:** The diffuser on a luxmeter is designed with a cosine-corrected profile so that its response follows $E \propto \cos\theta$ for oblique light. * **Photovoltaic / Solar Panel Output:** The electrical output of a solar cell follows an identical cosine response to light intensity — directly analogous to Lambert's law. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Lambert's Cosine Law **Statement to Prove:** *The illumination on a surface varies as the cosine of the angle of incidence.* **Given / Setup:** * $S$ — a point source of uniform luminous intensity $I$ candela in all directions. * $P$ — a point on a surface $AB$ at slant distance $d$ metres from $S$. * $\theta$ — angle between the normal $PN$ to surface $AB$ and the line of incidence $SP$. * $dA$ — an infinitesimal area element of surface $AB$ surrounding point $P$. **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Flux emitted into elemental solid angle:** The luminous flux $dF$ emitted by source $S$ into an elemental solid angle $d\omega$ steradian, in a direction making angle $\theta$ with the normal, is by definition of luminous intensity: $$dF = I \cdot d\omega$$ **Step 2 — Geometric meaning of solid angle:** The solid angle $d\omega$ subtended at the source $S$ by the area $dA$ (viewed from $S$) is defined as the projected area of $dA$ perpendicular to the line of sight, divided by the square of the distance: $$d\omega = \frac{dA \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$ > *Explanation:* Only the component of the surface "facing" the source — namely its normal projection $dA \cos\theta$ — contributes to the solid angle. This is the cornerstone of the cosine effect. **Step 3 — Flux intercepted by the elemental area:** Substituting the expression for $d\omega$ from Step 2 into Step 1: $$dF = I \cdot \frac{dA \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Definition of Illumination:** Illumination $E$ at a point is defined as the luminous flux received per unit area of the surface surrounding that point: $$E = \frac{dF}{dA}$$ **Step 5 — Substituting and simplifying:** $$\begin{aligned} E &= \frac{1}{dA} \cdot \frac{I \cdot dA \cos\theta}{d^{2}} \\ &= \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Conclusion:** Since $I$ and $d$ are constants for a given source and point, we have: $$E \propto \cos\theta$$ Hence proved. The illumination at any point on a surface varies directly as the cosine of the angle between the incident ray and the normal to the surface at that point. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Useful Alternative Form: The $\cos^{3}\theta$ Form In many practical problems (especially street lighting), the source is at a vertical mounting height $h$ above the working plane, and the angle $\theta$ is measured from the vertical normal. From the right-angled triangle geometry: $$\cos\theta = \frac{h}{d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{h}{\cos\theta}$$ Squaring this relation: $$d^{2} = \frac{h^{2}}{\cos^{2}\theta}$$ Substituting into the main Cosine Law equation: $$\begin{aligned} E &= \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta \\ &= \frac{I}{\left(\dfrac{h^{2}}{\cos^{2}\theta}\right)} \cos\theta \\ &= \frac{I \cdot \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}} \cdot \cos\theta \\ &= \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}} \end{aligned}$$ This form is **extremely high-yield for KTU numericals**, because $h$ is generally a known mounting height. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cosine vs. Inverse Square Law | Feature | Inverse Square Law | Lambert's Cosine Law | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Variable Parameter | Distance $d$ | Angle of incidence $\theta$ | | Surface Orientation | Fixed (normal to source) | Variable / Tilted | | Functional Form | $E \propto 1/d^{2}$ | $E \propto \cos\theta$ | | Maximum at | $d \rightarrow 0$ (theoretically) | $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ (normal incidence) | | Zero at | $d \rightarrow \infty$ | $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ (grazing incidence) | | Combined Master Equation | $E = (I/d^{2})\cos\theta$ | (same as inverse square when $\theta = 0$) | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Photometric Geometry of Lambert's Cosine Law (Mermaid Flow) The following diagram illustrates the **functional geometry** of the Cosine Law — the relationships between the source $S$, the tilted surface, the normal, and the slant distance. Mermaid cannot render a true physical vector drawing, so the schematic below maps the geometric entities and the corresponding algebraic transformations as a block-architecture flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["POINT LIGHT SOURCE S
Luminous Intensity I candela"] --> B["ELEMENTAL AREA dA
on tilted surface AB"] B --> C["ANGLE theta between
normal PN and ray SP"] C --> D["SLANT DISTANCE d
metres from S to P"] D --> E["PROJECTED AREA dA cos theta
perpendicular to line of sight"] E --> F["SOLID ANGLE d omega
equals dA cos theta divided by d squared"] F --> G["FLUX dF equals I times d omega"] G --> H["ILLUMINATION E equals dF by dA"] H --> I["FINAL EQUATION
E equals I cos theta divided by d squared"] H --> J["ALT FORM USING HEIGHT h
E equals I cos cubed theta divided by h squared"] I --> K["MAX ILLUMINATION
at theta equals 0 degrees"] I --> L["ZERO ILLUMINATION
at theta equals 90 degrees"] ``` ### 4.2 Vector-Geometric Schematic (Mermaid Block View) This second diagram maps the geometric decomposition of the slant distance $d$ into its vertical and horizontal components — the standard setup used in all KTU street-lighting and machine-tool numericals. ```mermaid flowchart LR S["SOURCE S
at top of pole"] --- M["MOUNTING HEIGHT h
vertical drop"] M --- P["POINT P
on working plane"] S --- N["SLANT DISTANCE d
hypotenuse"] N --- H["HORIZONTAL OFFSET x"] P --> Q["NORMAL to surface
vertical upward line"] S --> R["INCIDENT RAY SP
at angle theta from normal"] Q --> R R --> T["ANGLE theta
between normal and incident ray"] H --> U["cos theta equals h divided by d"] U --> V["sin theta equals x divided by d"] V --> W["tan theta equals x divided by h"] ``` ### 4.3 Trigonometric Identification Table | Element in Diagram | Geometric Meaning | Standard Symbol | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Vertical line $S \to$ ground | Mounting height | $h$ | | Hypotenuse $S \to P$ | Slant distance | $d$ | | Horizontal line on ground | Horizontal offset | $x$ | | Angle at $S$ | Angle of incidence | $\theta$ | | Adjacent side to $\theta$ | $h$ (opposite to angle at $P$) | $\cos\theta = h/d$ | | Opposite side to $\theta$ | $x$ | $\sin\theta = x/d$ | > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip:** Always mark the **normal to the receiving surface** first, then measure $\theta$ from that normal — *not* from the surface itself. Many students lose marks in KTU papers by incorrectly taking the angle with respect to the surface, leading to using $\sin\theta$ instead of $\cos\theta$. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *State Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination. Mention its significance in illumination engineering.* **Model Answer:** Lambert's Cosine Law states that the illumination $E$ at a point on a surface is directly proportional to the cosine of the angle $\theta$ between the normal to the surface and the direction of the incident light. $$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta$$ **Significance:** * It is essential for designing lighting on **tilted or inclined surfaces** such as drafting boards, conveyor belts, cinema screens, and road surfaces. * It forms the basis of the **cosine-corrected luxmeter** used for photometric measurements. * In street lighting, illumination on the road surface follows the $\cos^{3}\theta$ law (where $h$ is mounting height). * It helps in determining the **uniform illumination ratio** and selecting the optimum mounting height and tilt angle of luminaires. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplied Paper]** *What happens to the illumination on a working plane if the source is moved from normal incidence to grazing incidence? Justify mathematically.* **Model Answer:** At normal incidence, $\theta = 0^{\circ}$, so $\cos(0^{\circ}) = 1$, and the illumination is maximum: $$E_{\max} = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$ At grazing incidence, $\theta \to 90^{\circ}$, so $\cos(90^{\circ}) = 0$, and the illumination becomes: $$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cdot 0 = 0$$ Therefore, the illumination **decreases from a maximum value at $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ to zero at $\theta = 90^{\circ}$**. The reduction is monotonic and follows the cosine of the angle. This is why luminaires are designed to deliver maximum flux along the normal direction to the working plane. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (Choice 1)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Derive the expression $E = (I/d^{2})\cos\theta$ for the illumination on a surface inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the line of incidence. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A street lamp emits 1000 candela uniformly in all directions. It is mounted at a height of 8 m. Find the illumination on the street surface at a horizontal distance of 6 m from the foot of the pole. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Full derivation — see Section 3.1 of these notes. **Steps to be written in the answer script:** 1. Define a point source $S$ with luminous intensity $I$. 2. Consider an elemental area $dA$ on a tilted surface with normal $PN$. 3. Angle $\theta$ between $SP$ and $PN$. 4. Solid angle $d\omega = (dA \cos\theta)/d^{2}$. 5. Flux $dF = I \cdot d\omega$. 6. Illumination $E = dF/dA = (I \cos\theta)/d^{2}$. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** * Luminous intensity $I = 1000$ cd * Mounting height $h = 8$ m * Horizontal offset $x = 6$ m **Step 1 — Calculate slant distance $d$:** $$d = \sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}} = \sqrt{8^{2} + 6^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate $\cos\theta$:** $$\cos\theta = \frac{h}{d} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8$$ **Step 3 — Apply Lambert's Cosine Law:** $$\begin{aligned} E &= \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta \\ &= \frac{1000}{10^{2}} \cdot 0.8 \\ &= \frac{1000}{100} \cdot 0.8 \\ &= 10 \cdot 0.8 \\ &= 8 \text{ lux} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** The illumination on the street surface is **8 lux**. --- #### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a)** State and prove Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination. Explain why it is called the "cosine" law. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two lamps of luminous intensity 200 cd and 400 cd are placed 4 m and 6 m above a horizontal surface respectively. Compare the illumination produced by them on the surface directly below each lamp. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Statement and derivation — refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. The law is called the "cosine law" because the illumination $E$ on a surface depends directly on the **cosine of the angle of incidence** $\theta$ between the incident light ray and the normal to the surface. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** * Lamp 1: $I_{1} = 200$ cd, $h_{1} = 4$ m * Lamp 2: $I_{2} = 400$ cd, $h_{2} = 6$ m * Surface is horizontal, so $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ and $\cos\theta = 1$ **Step 1 — Illumination directly below Lamp 1:** Since the point is directly below, the slant distance equals the vertical height: $$d_{1} = h_{1} = 4 \text{ m}, \quad \theta_{1} = 0^{\circ}$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_{1} &= \frac{I_{1}}{d_{1}^{2}} \cos\theta_{1} \\ &= \frac{200}{4^{2}} \cdot 1 \\ &= \frac{200}{16} = 12.5 \text{ lux} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Illumination directly below Lamp 2:** $$d_{2} = h_{2} = 6 \text{ m}, \quad \theta_{2} = 0^{\circ}$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_{2} &= \frac{I_{2}}{d_{2}^{2}} \cos\theta_{2} \\ &= \frac{400}{6^{2}} \cdot 1 \\ &= \frac{400}{36} \approx 11.11 \text{ lux} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Comparison:** $$\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} = \frac{12.5}{11.11} \approx 1.125$$ **Answer:** Lamp 1 produces approximately **1.125 times** (or ~12.5% more) the illumination directly below it compared to Lamp 2, despite Lamp 2 being twice as intense. This highlights the dominating effect of the **inverse square of distance**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > 1. **Angle Reference Error (~3 marks):** Measuring $\theta$ from the surface instead of from the **normal**. KTU examiners deduct full marks if $\sin\theta$ is used instead of $\cos\theta$. > 2. **Missing units (~1–2 marks):** Always write the answer with the unit **lux (lx)**. Bare numerical answers are penalized. > 3. **Slant vs. Vertical Distance (~2 marks):** When the source is not directly above the point, students often use $h$ instead of $d$ in the $1/d^{2}$ term. The height $h$ goes into the $\cos^{3}\theta$ form, while the form $E = (I\cos\theta)/d^{2}$ strictly requires slant distance $d$. > 4. **Skipping the projection step (~2 marks):** When asked to *derive*, students jump directly to the final formula without showing the $d\omega = (dA\cos\theta)/d^{2}$ step. KTU expects the geometric reasoning explicitly. > 5. **Misidentifying normal:** For a horizontal surface, the normal is **vertical**. For a vertical wall, the normal is **horizontal**. This affects the angle calculation entirely. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember * **Lambert's Cosine Law** is also known as the **Cosine Law of Incidence**. * Master Equation: $E = (I/d^{2}) \cos\theta$ — applicable to a **point source** and a **flat surface**. * For a **horizontal surface directly below** the source: $\theta = 0^{\circ} \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$, reducing to pure Inverse Square Law. * For a **vertical surface** with the source horizontally away: $\theta = 90^{\circ} \Rightarrow E = 0$ at the surface itself (but not zero above/below it). * Street-lighting variant: $E = (I \cos^{3}\theta)/h^{2}$ — extremely common in KTU numericals. * The angle $\theta$ is **always measured from the normal**, never from the surface. * The cosine law is a direct consequence of the **projected-area (Lambertian) response** of a surface. * Real-world applications: luxmeter cosine correction, solar cell output, street-lighting design, photography light metering, indoor working-plane illumination, and stadium floodlighting. * **Highest-value memory hook:** $E \propto 1/d^{2}$ for distance, $E \propto \cos\theta$ for tilt — combine them and you have the master photometric equation. * **Examiner tip:** Always accompany a numerical with a small labelled diagram (even a hand-drawn one) — KTU awards step marks for correct geometric setup.

topics.title: Numerical Problems Based on Laws of Illumination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Numerical Problems Based on Laws of Illumination ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Laws of Illumination** are fundamental photometric laws that govern the distribution of luminous flux from a light source onto a surface. They form the mathematical foundation for solving practical lighting design problems encountered in illumination engineering. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Definition):** > The Laws of Illumination consist of three primary laws: > 1. **Inverse Square Law** — Relates illuminance to distance from a point source. > 2. **Lambert's Cosine Law** — Relates illuminance to the angle of incidence on the receiving surface. > 3. **Cosine Cubed Law** — Combines both effects for a non-normal surface at an oblique distance. Mathematically, the governing relationship is expressed as: $$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$$ where: - $E$ = **Illuminance** (in **lux** or **lumens/m²**) - $I$ = **Luminous Intensity** (in **candela** or **cd**) - $\theta$ = **Angle of incidence** between the incident light ray and the normal to the surface - $d$ = **Distance** from the source to the surface (in **meters**) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Flashlight and the Wall:** > Imagine you shine a flashlight (point source) straight onto a wall. The bright spot on the wall is the **illuminance**. Now, as you move the flashlight **farther away**, the spot becomes **dimmer and larger** — this is the **Inverse Square Law** in action. > > If you tilt the flashlight so the light strikes the wall at an **angle** (not perpendicular), the same amount of light now spreads over a **larger elliptical area**, making the wall appear dimmer. This is **Lambert's Cosine Law**. > > If you do **both** — move the light away **and** tilt it — the dimming compounds, and the result is the **Cosine Cubed Law**. ### 1.3 Standard Photometric Quantities and Units | Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Abbreviation | |----------|--------|---------|--------------| | Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | Lumen | **lm** | | Luminous Intensity | $I$ | Candela | **cd** | | Illuminance | $E$ | Lux | **lx** | | Luminance | $L$ | Candela/m² | **cd/m²** | | Solid Angle | $\omega$ | Steradian | **sr** | | Distance | $d$ or $r$ | Meter | **m** | | Area | $A$ | m² | **m²** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Conversion to Remember:** > 1 candela = 1 lumen/steradian, and 1 lux = 1 lumen/m². > A source of 1 candela emits **4π ≈ 12.57 lumens** of total flux (uniformly in all directions). ### 1.4 Physical Constants for Numerical Problem Solving > [!NOTE] > **Constants Used in KTU Numerical Problems:** > - **Solid angle of a complete sphere:** $\Omega = 4\pi \text{ sr} \approx 12.566 \text{ sr}$ > - **Half hemisphere (one ceiling/floor):** $\Omega = 2\pi \text{ sr} \approx 6.283 \text{ sr}$ > - **Angle subtended by a cone of half-angle $\alpha$:** $\omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\alpha) \text{ sr}$ > - **Area of a sphere surface:** $A_{sphere} = 4\pi r^{2}$ > - **Luminous efficacy (typical incandescent):** ≈ 12–15 lm/W > - **Luminous efficacy (typical fluorescent):** ≈ 50–60 lm/W > - **Luminous efficacy (typical LED):** ≈ 100–150 lm/W --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Laws of Illumination — Detailed Explanation #### 2.1.1 Inverse Square Law (Point Source) **Statement:** *For a point source radiating uniformly in all directions, the illuminance on a surface held normal to the rays is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source.* **Why it works — The Geometric Reasoning:** A point source emits light in straight lines (rays) spreading outward in a sphere. Since the total flux is conserved (no loss in air), the same amount of light must pass through every spherical surface centered at the source. Since the area of a sphere grows as $4\pi r^{2}$, the flux per unit area (illuminance) must fall as $1/r^{2}$. **Mathematical Form:** $$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \quad \text{(surface normal to incident rays)}$$ **Ratio Form (Highly Useful for KTU Problems):** $$\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} = \left(\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right)^{2}$$ #### 2.1.2 Lambert's Cosine Law (Cosine Law of Incidence) **Statement:** *The illuminance on a surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence between the direction of light and the normal to the surface.* **Why it works — Projection Geometry:** When light strikes a tilted surface, the same flux spreads over a larger projected area. The effective area grows as $1/\cos\theta$, so illuminance falls as $\cos\theta$. **Mathematical Form:** $$E = E_{0} \cos \theta$$ where $E_{0}$ is the illuminance when $\theta = 0°$ (normal incidence). #### 2.1.3 Cosine Cubed Law (Combined Effect) **Statement:** *When both the distance effect and the incidence angle effect act together, the illuminance is proportional to the cube of the cosine of the angle.* **Derivation Logic (also derived in Section 3):** $$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos^{3} \theta \quad \text{(when vertical height is fixed)}$$ **Why it appears as cubed and not just one cosine:** - One factor of $\cos\theta$ comes from Lambert's cosine law (surface tilt). - Two additional factors of $\cos\theta$ come from the fact that as the light source is fixed at a given height above a horizontal plane, the slant distance $d = h/\cos\theta$ increases with tilt angle, adding a $\cos^{2}\theta$ factor to the inverse-square term. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master This Table — These Are the ONLY Formulas Needed for KTU Numerical Problems on Laws of Illumination** | # | Law / Concept | Governing Formula | Variables | Units | |---|---------------|-------------------|-----------|-------| | 1 | Inverse Square Law | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | $E, I, d$ | lx, cd, m | | 2 | Inverse Square (Ratio) | $\dfrac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} = \left(\dfrac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right)^{2}$ | ratio | dimensionless | | 3 | Lambert's Cosine Law | $E = E_{0}\cos\theta$ | $E, E_{0}, \theta$ | lx, lx, rad/deg | | 4 | Cosine Cubed Law (fixed $h$) | $E = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ | $E, I, h, \theta$ | lx, cd, m, rad/deg | | 5 | Combined (slant distance) | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | $E, I, d, \theta$ | lx, cd, m, rad/deg | | 6 | Total Flux from Source | $\Phi = 4\pi I$ | $\Phi, I$ | lm, cd | | 7 | Flux in a Cone | $\Phi_{\omega} = I \cdot \omega$ | $\Phi, I, \omega$ | lm, cd, sr | | 8 | Solid Angle of Cone | $\omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\alpha)$ | $\omega, \alpha$ | sr, deg/rad | | 9 | Illuminance from Flux | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | $E, \Phi, A$ | lx, lm, m² | | 10 | Average Illuminance (MS) | $E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times CU \times MF}{A}$ | $E, \Phi, N$ | lx, lm, m² | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why These Laws Matter in Practice:** > - **Street Lighting Design** — Determines pole height and spacing. > - **Indoor Office Lighting** — Lumen method calculations require the inverse square law. > - **Floodlight Aiming** — Cosine law governs beam tilt and target illuminance. > - **Photometric Bench Calibration** — Standard inverse square setups use $d = 1$ m to 10 m. > - **Energy Audits** — Efficacy calculations from flux and wattage rely on these laws. ### 2.4 Conceptual Hierarchy of the Three Laws - **Use Inverse Square Law** when the surface is **perpendicular** to the light ray and the source is a **true point source**. - **Use Lambert's Cosine Law** when the **distance is fixed** and only the **angle changes** (surface tilt). - **Use the Combined Form** $E = I\cos\theta/d^{2}$ when both **distance and angle vary** independently. - **Use Cosine Cubed Law** specifically when the source is at a **fixed vertical height $h$** above a horizontal plane and we measure at a horizontal distance $x$ from the foot (so that $\tan\theta = x/h$ and the slant distance varies with angle). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law **Step 1 — Starting Assumption:** A point source of luminous intensity $I$ candela emits light uniformly in all directions. Consider a hypothetical sphere of radius $r$ centered on the source. **Step 2 — Total Luminous Flux from Source:** The total flux $\Phi$ emitted by the source is the product of intensity and the total solid angle (which is $4\pi$ sr for a complete sphere): $$\Phi = I \cdot \Omega = I \cdot 4\pi \quad \text{(in lumens)}$$ **Step 3 — Flux Per Unit Area at Distance $r$:** If the flux is distributed uniformly over the inner surface of the sphere, the illuminance on the sphere is: $$E = \frac{\Phi}{A_{sphere}} = \frac{I \cdot 4\pi}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Cancellation and Final Form:** The $4\pi$ terms cancel out, leaving the iconic inverse square relationship: $$\boxed{E = \frac{I}{r^{2}}}$$ **Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:** Notice that doubling the distance reduces illuminance to **one-fourth**; tripling the distance reduces it to **one-ninth**. This is why lighting designers carefully control mounting heights. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Lambert's Cosine Law **Step 1 — Geometry Setup:** Consider a flat surface of area $A$ tilted at angle $\theta$ with respect to the incoming light rays (where $\theta$ is measured between the ray and the surface normal). **Step 2 — Projected Area:** A surface tilted at angle $\theta$ presents an effective (projected) area to the incoming light of: $$A_{projected} = A \cos \theta$$ **Step 3 — Illuminance Definition:** Illuminance is flux per unit projected area. If the illuminance on a perpendicular (un-tilted) surface is $E_0$, the flux received by area $A$ is: $$\Phi = E_0 \cdot A_{projected} = E_0 \cdot A \cos \theta$$ **Step 4 — Illuminance on the Tilted Surface:** Dividing by the actual surface area $A$: $$E_{tilted} = \frac{\Phi}{A} = E_0 \cos \theta$$ **Step 5 — Final Form:** $$\boxed{E = E_0 \cos \theta}$$ **Step 6 — Limiting Case Check:** - At $\theta = 0°$ (perpendicular): $E = E_0$ (maximum illuminance). ✓ - At $\theta = 90°$ (grazing): $E = 0$ (light runs parallel to surface). ✓ - At $\theta = 60°$: $E = 0.5 E_0$ (illuminance halved). ✓ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Cosine Cubed Law **Step 1 — Setup with Fixed Vertical Height:** A point source $S$ is fixed at vertical height $h$ above a horizontal plane. We want to find the illuminance at point $P$ located at horizontal distance $x$ from the foot of the perpendicular. **Step 2 — Define the Geometry:** - The slant distance from $S$ to $P$ is: $d = \sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}}$ - The angle of incidence $\theta$ (between the ray $SP$ and the vertical normal to the horizontal plane) satisfies: $\cos \theta = \dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}}}$ **Step 3 — Apply the General Combined Formula:** The illuminance on the horizontal surface at $P$ is: $$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $d = h/\cos\theta$:** $$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{\left(\dfrac{h}{\cos \theta}\right)^{2}}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the Denominator:** $$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{\dfrac{h^{2}}{\cos^{2}\theta}} = \frac{I \cos \theta \cdot \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Combine the Cosine Powers:** $$E = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$$ **Step 7 — Final Form:** $$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}}$$ **Step 8 — Alternative Form Using Horizontal Distance:** Substituting $\cos\theta = h/\sqrt{h^2 + x^2}$: $$E = \frac{I \cdot h^{3}}{h^{2} \cdot (\sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}})^{3}} = \frac{I \cdot h}{(h^{2} + x^{2})^{3/2}}$$ **Step 9 — Limiting Case Check:** - At $x = 0$ (directly below source): $E = I/h^2$ (matches pure inverse square law). ✓ - At $x \to \infty$: $E \to 0$ (illuminance vanishes at the horizon). ✓ - At $x = h$ (45°): $E = I \cos^{3}(45°)/h^{2} = I(0.707)^3/h^2 \approx 0.354 I/h^{2}$ (about 35% of directly-below value). ✓ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Problem Patterns in KTU > [!NOTE] > **Pattern Recognition for KTU Numerical Problems:** | Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | |--------------|-------|------|-------------| | Single-point illuminance | $I$, $d$, $\theta$ | $E$ | $E = I\cos\theta/d^2$ | | Compare two distances | $E_1$, $d_1$, $d_2$ | $E_2$ | $E_1/E_2 = (d_2/d_1)^2$ | | Compare two angles (fixed $h$) | $I$, $h$, $\theta_1$, $\theta_2$ | $E_1/E_2$ | $E = I\cos^3\theta/h^2$ | | Lamps below working plane | $I$, $h$, $x$ (or $\theta$) | $E$ at point $P$ | $E = I h/(h^2+x^2)^{3/2}$ | | Lamps above working plane | $I$, $h$, $\theta$ | $E$ at point $P$ | $E = I \cos^3\theta / h^2$ | | Multiple lamps superposition | $E_i$ at each lamp | $E_{total}$ | $E_{total} = \sum E_i$ | --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard Style (with full working) > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example A — Two-Stage Inverse Square + Cosine Problem** > > **Problem:** A lamp having a luminous intensity of 200 cd in all directions is suspended at a height of 4 m above the floor. Calculate: > (i) The illuminance directly below the lamp on the floor. > (ii) The illuminance at a point on the floor 3 m away from the foot of the lamp. > (iii) The ratio of these two illuminances. **Solution:** **(i) Directly below the lamp:** The point is at horizontal distance $x = 0$, so $\theta = 0°$ and the slant distance equals the vertical height, $d = 4$ m. $$E_1 = \frac{I}{d^{2}} = \frac{200}{(4)^{2}} = \frac{200}{16}$$ $$E_1 = 12.5 \text{ lux}$$ **(ii) At horizontal distance $x = 3$ m:** First, find the slant distance $d$: $$d = \sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}} = \sqrt{4^{2} + 3^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$ The angle of incidence satisfies: $$\cos \theta = \frac{h}{d} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$ Apply the combined formula: $$E_2 = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{200 \times 0.8}{(5)^{2}} = \frac{160}{25}$$ $$E_2 = 6.4 \text{ lux}$$ **Verify using Cosine Cubed Law:** $$E_2 = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}} = \frac{200 \times (0.8)^{3}}{(4)^{2}} = \frac{200 \times 0.512}{16} = \frac{102.4}{16} = 6.4 \text{ lux} \checkmark$$ **(iii) Ratio:** $$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{12.5}{6.4} = 1.953 \approx 1.95$$ **Equivalent verification using cosine cubed:** $$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{1/\cos^{3}\theta}{1} = \frac{1}{(0.8)^{3}} = \frac{1}{0.512} = 1.953 \checkmark$$ --- > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example B — Lamps Below Working Plane (Unique KTU Pattern)** > > **Problem:** Two lamps, each of 100 cd in all directions, are installed 2 m **below** the working plane. The horizontal distance between the lamps is 6 m. Find the illuminance at a point P on the working plane midway between the two lamps. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Geometry:** - Vertical height of lamps below the plane: $h = 2$ m - Point P is midway between the lamps, so the horizontal distance from P to the foot of each lamp is: $x = 6/2 = 3$ m - Slant distance from each lamp to P: $d = \sqrt{2^{2} + 3^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$ m - Angle: $\cos \theta = h/d = 2/\sqrt{13} \approx 0.5547$ **Step 2 — Illuminance from one lamp:** $$E_{one} = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{100 \times 0.5547}{13} = \frac{55.47}{13} \approx 4.267 \text{ lux}$$ **Step 3 — Total Illuminance from Both Lamps (by Superposition):** $$E_{total} = 2 \times E_{one} = 2 \times 4.267 = 8.534 \text{ lux}$$ $$E_{total} \approx 8.53 \text{ lux}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip for "Lamps Below Plane" Questions:** > When lamps are **below** the working plane, the angle of incidence is measured between the light ray and the **upward-pointing normal** of the working plane. The formula $E = I\cos\theta / d^2$ still applies — just be careful with direction sign of the cosine (always use the magnitude, i.e., absolute value). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Geometric Setup of the Three Laws ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["INVVERSE SQUARE LAW SETUP"] L1["Point Source S
Intensity = I candela"] D1["Distance d from source"] A1["Surface area A normal to ray"] L1 -->|"rays diverge"| D1 D1 -->|"illuminance E = I/d²"| A1 end subgraph S2["LAMBERT COSINE LAW SETUP"] L2["Point Source S"] R2["Light ray direction"] N2["Surface normal N"] A2["Tilted surface at angle theta"] L2 --> R2 R2 --> A2 N2 -.->|"angle theta between R and N"| R2 A2 -->|"E = E0 cos theta"| OUT2["Reduced illuminance"] end subgraph S3["COSINE CUBED LAW SETUP"] L3["Source S at height h above plane"] P3["Point P at horizontal distance x"] SP3["Slant ray SP of length d = h/cos theta"] PL3["Horizontal plane (working plane)"] L3 --> SP3 SP3 --> P3 L3 -.->|"vertical h"| PL3 PL3 -.->|"horizontal x"| P3 P3 -->|"E = I cos³theta / h²"| OUT3["Illuminance at P"] end S1 --> SUMMARY["All three laws derive from
ONE master equation:
E = I cos theta / d²"] S2 --> SUMMARY S3 --> SUMMARY style S1 fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1 style S2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style S3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style SUMMARY fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8 ``` ### 4.2 Spherical Flux Distribution — Inverse Square Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["Point Source S
Intensity I cd"] --> R1["Ray at distance d₁
Area A₁ = 4π d₁²"] SRC --> R2["Ray at distance d₂
Area A₂ = 4π d₂²
d₂ = 2 d₁"] SRC --> R3["Ray at distance d₃
Area A₃ = 4π d₃²
d₃ = 3 d₁"] R1 --> E1["E₁ = I / d₁²"] R2 --> E2["E₂ = I / 4 d₁² = E₁/4"] R3 --> E3["E₃ = I / 9 d₁² = E₁/9"] E1 --> NOTE["Doubling distance → 1/4 illuminance
Tripling distance → 1/9 illuminance"] style SRC fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c style E1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style E2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style E3 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style NOTE fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Problem-Solving Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB START["KTU Numerical Problem Given"] --> Q1{"Is the surface
perpendicular
to the light ray?"} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Is only the
distance varying?"} Q1 -->|No| Q3{"Is only the
angle varying?"} Q2 -->|Yes| USE1["Use: E = I / d²"] Q2 -->|No| USE2["Use: E = I cos theta / d²"] Q3 -->|Yes| USE3["Use: E = E0 cos theta"] Q3 -->|No| Q4{"Is the source height
fixed above/below
a horizontal plane?"} Q4 -->|Yes| USE4["Use: E = I cos³theta / h²"] Q4 -->|No| USE2 USE1 --> MULTIPLE{"Multiple
lamps?"} USE2 --> MULTIPLE USE3 --> MULTIPLE USE4 --> MULTIPLE MULTIPLE -->|Yes| SUPER["Apply Superposition:
E_total = sum of E_i"] MULTIPLE -->|No| ANSWER["Final Answer in lux"] SUPER --> ANSWER style START fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style USE1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style USE2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style USE3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style USE4 fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6 style ANSWER fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Cosine Cubed Law Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VERT["VERTICAL AXIS (DOWN)"] S["S = Source
Intensity I candela
Suspended at height h"] end S -.->|"h (vertical)"| H["Foot of perpendicular
Point F on plane"] subgraph HORIZ["HORIZONTAL PLANE (WORKING PLANE)"] H --> X["Horizontal distance x"] X --> P["Point P on working plane
(where illuminance is required)"] end S -->|"slant ray of length d"| P ANG["Angle theta
= angle between SP and vertical normal at P"] S -.-> ANG P -.-> ANG FORM["Geometric relations:
d = h / cos theta
cos theta = h / sqrt h² + x²
tan theta = x / h
sin theta = x / sqrt h² + x²"] ANG --> FORM RESULT["Resulting illuminance at P:
E = I cos³theta / h²
E = I h / h² + x²^3/2"] FORM --> RESULT style S fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c style H fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style P fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style RESULT fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State and explain the **Inverse Square Law of Illumination**. Mention the conditions under which it is applicable. **Model Answer:** > The Inverse Square Law states that *the illuminance $E$ on a surface held normally to the rays from a point source is inversely proportional to the square of the distance $d$ between the source and the surface*. > > **Mathematical Form:** > > $$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$ > > where $I$ is the luminous intensity of the source in candela. > > **Conditions of Applicability:** > 1. The source must be a **point source** (dimensions negligible compared to distance). > 2. The receiving surface must be held **normal (perpendicular)** to the incident rays. > 3. The medium of propagation must be **homogeneous and transparent** (no absorption/scattering). > > **Physical Basis:** The total luminous flux $4\pi I$ is distributed over a sphere of area $4\pi d^{2}$, so the flux per unit area falls as $1/d^{2}$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Lambert's Cosine Law** and the **Cosine Cubed Law** of illumination. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Lambert's Cosine Law | Cosine Cubed Law | |--------|----------------------|------------------| | **Statement** | Illuminance is proportional to $\cos\theta$ | Illuminance is proportional to $\cos^{3}\theta$ | | **Formula** | $E = E_{0} \cos\theta$ | $E = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ | | **When Used** | Distance is fixed, only angle changes | Source is at fixed height $h$ above a horizontal plane | | **Source Effect** | Independent of source intensity directly | Depends on intensity $I$ of source | | **Effect of Tilt** | Only one cosine factor | Three cosine factors (one from surface tilt, two from increased slant distance) | | **Example** | Tilting a workbench under a fixed ceiling lamp | Walking away from directly under a street lamp | --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** (a) **Derive the relationship $E = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ for the illuminance at a point on a horizontal plane due to a point source suspended at a height $h$ above the plane.** Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)** (b) A lamp of luminous intensity 250 cd in all directions is suspended 5 m above the floor. Find the illuminance on the floor at:     (i) A point directly below the lamp.     (ii) A point on the floor 4 m away from the foot of the lamp.     (iii) Comment on the variation of illuminance with horizontal distance. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question A (a) **Assumptions:** 1. The lamp is a true point source. 2. The source emits uniformly in all directions. 3. The medium is homogeneous with no absorption. 4. The working plane is horizontal. **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Geometry:** Let source $S$ be at vertical height $h$ above a horizontal plane. Consider a point $P$ on the plane at horizontal distance $x$ from the foot of the perpendicular $F$. **Step 2 — Slant Distance:** The slant distance from $S$ to $P$ is: $$d = \sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Angle of Incidence:** The angle $\theta$ between the ray $SP$ and the vertical normal at $P$ satisfies: $$\cos \theta = \frac{h}{d} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}}}$$ Hence: $d = h/\cos\theta$ **Step 4 — Apply the Combined Formula:** The illuminance on the horizontal surface at $P$ is given by: $$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $d = h/\cos\theta$:** $$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{\left(\dfrac{h}{\cos \theta}\right)^{2}} = \frac{I \cos \theta \cdot \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Simplify:** $$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}}$$ This is the required cosine-cubed relationship. ∎ --- ### Model Solution to Question A (b) **Given:** $I = 250$ cd, $h = 5$ m. **(i) Directly below the lamp ($x = 0$):** At this point, $\theta = 0°$, so $\cos\theta = 1$ and $d = h = 5$ m. $$E_1 = \frac{I}{h^{2}} = \frac{250}{(5)^{2}} = \frac{250}{25} = 10 \text{ lux}$$ **(ii) At horizontal distance $x = 4$ m:** First, compute the slant distance: $$d = \sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}} = \sqrt{5^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{25 + 16} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.403 \text{ m}$$ Compute $\cos\theta$: $$\cos \theta = \frac{h}{d} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{41}} = \frac{5}{6.403} \approx 0.7809$$ Alternatively, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(4/5) = 38.66°$, $\cos(38.66°) = 0.7809$. Apply the cosine-cubed law: $$E_2 = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}} = \frac{250 \times (0.7809)^{3}}{(5)^{2}}$$ $$E_2 = \frac{250 \times 0.4762}{25} = \frac{119.05}{25} = 4.762 \text{ lux}$$ $$E_2 \approx 4.76 \text{ lux}$$ **Cross-check using combined formula:** $$E_2 = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{250 \times 0.7809}{41} = \frac{195.23}{41} \approx 4.76 \text{ lux} \checkmark$$ **(iii) Comment on Variation:** The illuminance decreases rapidly as the horizontal distance from the foot of the lamp increases. In this case, the illuminance drops from **10 lux** at the center to **4.76 lux** at 4 m away — a reduction to less than half. This is due to the **combined effect** of: - The slant distance increasing from 5 m to 6.4 m (inverse square effect). - The angle of incidence increasing from $0°$ to $38.66°$ (cosine effect). The combination yields the steep cosine-cubed fall-off, which is why tall mounting heights are preferred for uniform lighting in large spaces. --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** (a) **State and explain Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination.** A surface is illuminated by a light source producing 500 lux on a plane perpendicular to the rays. Find the illuminance when the surface is tilted at **(i) 30°**, **(ii) 60°**, and **(iii) 75°** with respect to the incident rays. **(7 Marks)** (b) A corridor is illuminated by two lamps, each of 200 cd, suspended at a height of 3 m. The lamps are placed 4 m apart along the length of the corridor. Calculate the illuminance at a point on the floor directly below the midpoint between the two lamps. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question B (a) **Statement of Lambert's Cosine Law:** *The illuminance on a surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence between the incident light rays and the normal to the surface.* **Mathematical Form:** $$E = E_0 \cos \theta$$ **Given:** $E_0 = 500$ lux (illuminance at normal incidence). **(i) At $\theta = 30°$:** $$E = 500 \times \cos 30° = 500 \times 0.8660 = 433.01 \text{ lux}$$ **(ii) At $\theta = 60°$:** $$E = 500 \times \cos 60° = 500 \times 0.5 = 250 \text{ lux}$$ **(iii) At $\theta = 75°$:** $$E = 500 \times \cos 75° = 500 \times 0.2588 = 129.41 \text{ lux}$$ **Tabulated Results:** | Angle $\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | Illuminance (lux) | % of Normal | |----------------|--------------|-------------------|-------------| | 0° | 1.000 | 500.00 | 100% | | 30° | 0.866 | 433.01 | 86.6% | | 60° | 0.500 | 250.00 | 50.0% | | 75° | 0.259 | 129.41 | 25.9% | | 90° | 0.000 | 0.00 | 0% | --- ### Model Solution to Question B (b) **Given:** - Luminous intensity of each lamp: $I = 200$ cd - Height above floor: $h = 3$ m - Distance between lamps: $4$ m **Step 1 — Geometry:** The point P is directly below the midpoint of the two lamps. The horizontal distance from each lamp's foot to P is: $$x = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Slant Distance from Each Lamp to P:** $$d = \sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}} = \sqrt{3^{2} + 2^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Angle of Incidence:** $$\cos \theta = \frac{h}{d} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{13}} = \frac{3}{3.606} \approx 0.8321$$ Or $\theta = \tan^{-1}(2/3) = 33.69°$. **Step 4 — Illuminance from One Lamp:** $$E_{one} = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{200 \times 0.8321}{13} = \frac{166.41}{13} \approx 12.80 \text{ lux}$$ **Step 5 — Verification Using Cosine Cubed Law:** $$E_{one} = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}} = \frac{200 \times (0.8321)^{3}}{9} = \frac{200 \times 0.5761}{9} = \frac{115.22}{9} \approx 12.80 \text{ lux} \checkmark$$ **Step 6 — Total Illuminance (Superposition of Two Lamps):** $$E_{total} = 2 \times E_{one} = 2 \times 12.80 = 25.61 \text{ lux}$$ $$\boxed{E_{total} \approx 25.61 \text{ lux}}$$ --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Using $I$ as "lumens" instead of "candela"** — When a problem gives the flux $\Phi$ (in lumens) of a uniform source, students forget to convert to intensity using $I = \Phi / (4\pi)$ before substituting into the illuminance formula. This leads to answers off by a factor of 12.57. > > 2. **Forgetting the cosine term** — Using the bare inverse square law $E = I/d^2$ even when the surface is tilted. Always check if the surface is normal to the ray first. > > 3. **Confusing $\theta$ with the angle from the surface (rather than the normal)** — The angle in the cosine law is measured from the **normal**, not from the surface itself. If the angle from the surface is given, convert: $\theta_{normal} = 90° - \theta_{surface}$. > > 4. **Sign errors with "lamps below the working plane"** — When lamps are below the plane (e.g., in a mine shaft or task lamp under a table), the cosine is still positive (use the absolute value of $\cos\theta$), since the normal points upward. > > 5. **Mixing up the Cosine Cubed Law condition** — The cosine cubed law is valid **only** when the source is at a fixed vertical height $h$ above (or below) a horizontal plane. Do not apply it for arbitrary geometry. > > 6. **Unit errors** — Distances must be in **meters** for lux; intensities in **candela**; flux in **lumens**. Mixing units (e.g., distances in cm) is a common mark-deduction trap. > > 7. **Omitting superposition for multiple lamps** — When the problem says "two lamps" or "an array," students often calculate for one lamp and forget to add the contributions. The total illuminance is the **sum** of individual illuminances (principle of superposition). --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam** > > ✅ The **three laws of illumination** are: (1) Inverse Square Law, (2) Lambert's Cosine Law, (3) Cosine Cubed Law. > > ✅ The **master equation** is $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$, valid for a point source on a surface tilted at angle $\theta$ from the normal. > > ✅ The **inverse square law** $E = I/d^2$ applies when the surface is **perpendicular** to the light ray. > > ✅ The **cosine cubed law** $E = I\cos^{3}\theta / h^{2}$ applies when the source is at **fixed vertical height** $h$ above a horizontal plane. > > ✅ **Luminous flux to intensity conversion** (for a uniform source): $I = \Phi / (4\pi)$ and $\Phi = 4\pi I$. > > ✅ **Solid angle of a cone** of half-angle $\alpha$: $\omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\alpha)$ steradians. > > ✅ **Flux within a cone:** $\Phi_{\omega} = I \cdot \omega$ lumens. > > ✅ For **lamps below the working plane**, the formula $E = I\cos\theta / d^2$ still applies, but the geometry has the source below and the normal pointing up. > > ✅ For **multiple lamps**, always apply **superposition**: $E_{total} = \sum E_i$. > > ✅ **Unit conversions to remember:** > - $1 \text{ lumen} = 1 \text{ cd} \cdot \text{sr}$ > - $1 \text{ lux} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$ > - $4\pi \text{ sr} \approx 12.566 \text{ sr}$ for a full sphere > - $2\pi \text{ sr} \approx 6.283 \text{ sr}$ for a hemisphere > > ✅ **Key shortcuts to avoid errors:** > - When in doubt, always compute $d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2}$ and $\cos\theta = h/d$ before using any formula. > - $\cos^3\theta$ vs. $\cos\theta$ — remember the difference: cubed is for **fixed height**; single cosine is for **fixed distance**. > - For "directly below" problems, $\theta = 0°$ and $d = h$. > - For "infinity far away" or "at the horizon," $E \to 0$. ---

topics.title: Practical Application of the Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical Application of the Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are the "Laws" in Illumination Engineering?
