## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define luminous intensity. How is it measured using a goniophotometer?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Luminous intensity** is the luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction, with the SI unit **candela (cd)**. Mathematically, $I_v = d\Phi_v / d\Omega$.

A **goniophotometer** measures it by placing a photodetector at a fixed distance $d$ from the source, rotating the source through controlled angular positions, and recording the illuminance $E_v$ at each position. The intensity in that direction is computed using the inverse-square law: $I_v = E_v \cdot d^2$. A complete set of such measurements over the full sphere yields the **luminous intensity distribution curve**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the principle of an integrating sphere. Why is the inner coating made of barium sulfate?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The integrating sphere works on the principle of **multiple internal diffuse reflections** that *spatially integrate* the flux from the source, producing a uniform illuminance on the inner wall that is proportional to the total flux of the source.

**Barium sulfate (BaSO₄)** is used because it has (i) **very high diffuse reflectance** ($\rho \approx 0.95$–$0.98$) across the visible spectrum, (ii) near-perfect **Lambertian scattering** behavior, and (iii) good **spectral neutrality**. These properties maximize sphere efficiency and ensure the cosine-of-incidence correction is automatically satisfied.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Question A:**
(a) *(7 Marks)* Describe the construction and working of a goniophotometer with a neat block diagram. Explain the importance of the minimum photometric distance.
(b) *(7 Marks)* The luminous intensities of a lamp measured in a vertical plane are: at $\theta = 0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150°, 180°$ — $I = 200, 195, 180, 130, 70, 20, 0$ candela. Assuming azimuthal symmetry, estimate the total luminous flux using the zonal method with 30° zones.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**
- A **goniometer head** with two mutually perpendicular rotation axes (one vertical, one horizontal) carrying the lamp holder.
- A **motorized drive** (often stepper/servo) for precise angular positioning.
- A **photodetector** (silicon photodiode with $V(\lambda)$ filter) mounted on a fixed stand or a movable arm.
- A **dark room** (or enclosure) to eliminate stray light.
- A **calibrated photometric bench** with distance markers (1 m, 2 m, 5 m, 10 m).
- A **computer with data acquisition (DAQ) system** for recording intensity vs. angle.

**Working:**
1. The lamp is mounted on the goniometer head at the geometric center.
2. The photodetector is positioned at a fixed photometric distance $d$.
3. The lamp is rotated through angles $\theta = 0°$ to $180°$ in the vertical plane and $\phi = 0°$ to $360°$ in the horizontal plane in discrete steps (typically 5° or 10°).
4. At each position, the detector reads $E_v(\theta, \phi)$, and the intensity is computed: $I_v(\theta, \phi) = E_v(\theta, \phi) \cdot d^2$.
5. The data is plotted as a polar curve — the **luminous intensity distribution curve (LIDC)**.
6. The total flux is obtained by numerical integration of the intensity solid: $\Phi_v = \int I_v \, d\Omega$.

**Minimum Photometric Distance:**
The inverse-square law $E_v = I_v / d^2$ is valid only if the source behaves as a **point source**. This is satisfied only when the detector is in the **photometric far field**, defined as:

$$d_{\min} \geq 5 D_{\text{max}} \text{ to } 10 D_{\text{max}}$$

where $D_{\text{max}}$ is the largest linear dimension of the source. CIE recommends $5\times$ for most practical cases, while $10\times$ is used for high-precision measurements. Below this distance, errors of 5–10% can occur due to the source's finite size.

**(b) Flux Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: $I(\theta) = \{200, 195, 180, 130, 70, 20, 0\}$ cd at $\theta = \{0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150°, 180°\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute solid angles for each 30° zone (using $\Delta\Omega = 2\pi[\cos\theta_i - \cos\theta_{i+1}]$):**

- Zone 1 (0°–30°): $2\pi(1.000 - 0.866) = 0.842$ sr
- Zone 2 (30°–60°): $2\pi(0.866 - 0.500) = 2.299$ sr
- Zone 3 (60°–90°): $2\pi(0.500 - 0.000) = 3.142$ sr
- Zone 4 (90°–120°): $2\pi(0.000 - (-0.500)) = 3.142$ sr
- Zone 5 (120°–150°): $2\pi(-0.500 - (-0.866)) = 2.299$ sr
- Zone 6 (150°–180°): $2\pi(-0.866 - (-1.000)) = 0.842$ sr

**Step 2 — Average intensity in each zone (mid-point reading):**

$\bar{I}_1 = 200$, $\bar{I}_2 = 195$, $\bar{I}_3 = 180$, $\bar{I}_4 = 130$, $\bar{I}_5 = 70$, $\bar{I}_6 = 20$ cd.

**Step 3 — Zonal flux $\Phi_i = \bar{I}_i \cdot \Delta\Omega_i$:**

- $\Phi_1 = 200 \times 0.842 = 168.4$ lm
- $\Phi_2 = 195 \times 2.299 = 448.3$ lm
- $\Phi_3 = 180 \times 3.142 = 565.5$ lm
- $\Phi_4 = 130 \times 3.142 = 408.4$ lm
- $\Phi_5 = 70 \times 2.299 = 160.9$ lm
- $\Phi_6 = 20 \times 0.842 = 16.8$ lm

**Step 4 — Total flux:**

$$\Phi_v = 168.4 + 448.3 + 565.5 + 408.4 + 160.9 + 16.8 = \mathbf{1768.3 \text{ lm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark-Loss Zone (4–5 marks lost per paper):**
> 1. **Do NOT** compute flux as $I \times 4\pi$. That formula is valid *only* for a perfectly isotropic (uniform) point source. For a real source with a directional pattern, you **must** use the zonal flux summation formula.
> 2. **Do NOT** omit the solid-angle factor $\sin\theta \, d\theta$. Forgetting it gives wrong magnitude (off by factor of $\pi$).
> 3. **Do NOT** assume azimuthal symmetry if the data isn't symmetric — the question must explicitly state it.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Question B:**
(a) *(7 Marks)* Explain the construction, working, and theory of an integrating sphere (Ulbricht sphere) with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the wall illuminance in terms of total flux, reflectance, and sphere area.
(b) *(7 Marks)* An integrating sphere of diameter 1.5 m is coated with a material of reflectance 0.96. When a reference lamp of known flux 1500 lm is placed inside, the wall illuminance reading is 800 lx. When the test lamp replaces it, the reading is 1200 lx. Calculate the total luminous flux of the test lamp. State two precautions to be taken while using the integrating sphere.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction, Working, and Theory (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**
- A **hollow sphere** of diameter 0.5 m to 3 m made of metal or rigid plastic.
- Inner surface coated with **BaSO₄** or **PTFE** (Lambertian, high-reflectance, $\rho \approx 0.95$–$0.98$).
- An **opening** for the test lamp entry and an **inspection window** with a baffle.
- A **photodetector** mounted on the wall, shielded by a baffle from direct source light.
- An **auxiliary lamp** port for the substitution method.

**Working Principle:**
The flux $\Phi_v$ from the source undergoes *thousands of multiple diffuse reflections* on the inner surface. After many bounces, the spatial distribution of flux becomes uniform. The illuminance $E_s$ on any patch of the wall is then **proportional only to $\Phi_v$** (not to the directional pattern of the source).

**Derivation of Wall Illuminance:**

Let the sphere have area $A_s$ and coating reflectance $\rho$. Let $M$ be the radiosity (exitance) of the wall. At equilibrium (all the flux absorbed by the wall equals the source flux plus the flux reabsorbed from reflection):

$$\Phi_v = A_s (1 - \rho) M$$

The illuminance incident on the wall $E_s$ is related to the Lambertian radiosity by:

$$E_s = \frac{M}{\pi} = \frac{\Phi_v}{\pi A_s (1 - \rho)}$$

But accounting for the multiple-bounce flux (i.e., the *first* reflection from the wall contributes back to the wall), the **effective** wall illuminance becomes:

$$E_s = \frac{\rho \cdot \Phi_v}{\pi \cdot A_s \cdot (1 - \rho)}$$

**Block diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 above (textbook-style description acceptable).

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given:
- $D = 1.5$ m $\Rightarrow r = 0.75$ m
- $\rho = 0.96$
- $\Phi_{\text{ref}} = 1500$ lm
- $E_{\text{ref}} = 800$ lx
- $E_{\text{test}} = 1200$ lx

**Step 1 — Use Substitution Method Formula:**

$$\Phi_{\text{test}} = \Phi_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{E_{\text{test}}}{E_{\text{ref}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$\Phi_{\text{test}} = 1500 \times \frac{1200}{800} = 1500 \times 1.5 = \mathbf{2250 \text{ lm}}$$

**Step 3 — Precautions (2 of the following):**

1. The test source and any fittings/holders must be considered as part of the absorbing system — use the **substitution (auxiliary lamp) method** to compensate.
2. The detector must be **shielded by an opaque baffle** to prevent it from receiving direct radiation from the source.
3. The sphere's inner coating should be **clean, unscratched, and uniform** — any contamination will alter $\rho$ and invalidate the calibration.
4. The **sphere diameter** should be at least **5–6 times the largest dimension** of the source to ensure the integrating property holds.
5. The sphere should be operated in a **temperature-controlled environment** to prevent drift in detector sensitivity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark-Loss Zone:**
> 1. **Do NOT** use the formula $E_s = \Phi_v / A_s$ for an integrating sphere — this is a common student error that ignores the multiple-reflection factor. The correct formula contains $\pi$ and $(1-\rho)$ in the denominator.
> 2. **Do NOT** apply the formula directly to compute $\Phi_{\text{test}}$ — the substitution method is the operationally correct one because it cancels out the unknown $\rho$ and $k$. Direct use of $E_s = \rho \Phi_v / [\pi A_s (1-\rho)]$ requires $\rho$ and $A_s$ to be *exactly* known, which is rarely the case in practice.
> 3. **Always** mention the baffle — losing marks on construction means you lose marks on the principle.

---

### 5.4 Luxmeter — Bonus Short-Answer

**Q. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What is cosine correction in a luxmeter and why is it important?*

**Model Answer:**
**Cosine correction** refers to the optical design of the luxmeter's diffuser that ensures the sensor's responsivity is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence, i.e., $R(\theta) \propto \cos\theta$. It is important because **illuminance is defined as flux per unit projected area**, and the projected area follows a cosine law. Without cosine correction, the luxmeter would over-respond to light striking at oblique angles, giving systematically wrong (high) readings when measuring typical indoor lighting where light arrives from many directions.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2 — Measuring Apparatus**

- **Goniophotometer** → measures **luminous intensity $I_v$ (cd)** as a function of direction $(\theta, \phi)$.
- **Three types:** Type A (source rotates), Type B (detector rotates), Type C (mirror type for high-power sources).
- **Minimum photometric distance:** $d \geq 5 D_{\text{max}}$ to $10 D_{\text{max}}$ (CIE rule).
- **Conversion formula:** $I_v = E_v \cdot d^2$ (inverse-square law used in reverse).
- **Total flux from goniophotometer:** $\Phi_v = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} I_v(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$ or zonal sum $\sum \bar{I}_i \cdot 2\pi (\cos\theta_i - \cos\theta_{i+1})$.
- **Integrating Sphere (Ulbricht Sphere)** → measures **total luminous flux $\Phi_v$ (lm)**.
- **Key formula:** $E_s = \rho \Phi_v / [\pi A_s (1 - \rho)]$.
- **Sphere area:** $A_s = 4\pi r^2$.
- **Coating materials:** Barium sulfate (BaSO₄) or PTFE — high $\rho$ (~0.95–0.98), Lambertian, spectrally neutral.
- **Baffle** is mandatory to shield detector from direct source radiation.
- **Substitution lamp method:** $\Phi_{\text{test}} = \Phi_{\text{ref}} \cdot (E_{\text{test}} / E_{\text{ref}})$ — this is the *operational* KTU formula.
- **Luxmeter** → measures **illuminance $E_v$ (lx = lm/m²)** on a surface.
- **Components:** cosine-correcting diffuser, $V(\lambda)$-corrected photodiode, amplifier, ADC, LCD.
- **Cosine correction** → response $\propto \cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is angle of incidence from normal.
- **Conversion:** $1 \text{ fc} = 10.764 \text{ lx}$.
- **Inverse-square law** is the foundation of the goniophotometer: $E_v = I_v / d^2$.
- **Forgetting the $\sin\theta$ factor** in the flux integral = guaranteed mark loss.
- **Forgetting the baffle** in the integrating sphere construction = guaranteed mark loss.
- **Numerical safety checks:** $\Phi_v < I_{\max} \cdot 4\pi$ always; for isotropic source, $\Phi_v = I_v \cdot 4\pi$.
- **SI photometric base unit:** candela (cd); **derived units:** lm = cd·sr, lx = lm/m².
- **CIE Photopic Curve $V(\lambda)$** peaks at 555 nm — all photometers must match this for valid photopic measurements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics for the Exam Hall:**
> - **G**oniophotometer = **G**oes around = **I**ntensity (cd).
> - **I**ntegrating **S**phere = **S**urrounds the source = total **F**lux (lm).
> - **L**uxmeter = **L**ands on a surface = **I**lluminance (lx).
> - **Remember G-I-L → I-S-L** for the three primary photometers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions of Maintenance Factor, Uniformity Ratio, Direct Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definitions of Maintenance Factor, Uniformity Ratio, Direct Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

This module introduces three of the most heavily-tested fundamental ratios in **Interior Illumination Engineering**. Every lighting design — from a small reading room to a vast industrial shop floor — fundamentally rests on these three ratios. Mastering them is non-negotiable for the KTU board exam.

### 1.1 Maintenance Factor (MF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> The **Maintenance Factor** is the ratio of the illumination on a working plane at a given time during the life of the installation to the initial illumination provided by the installation when everything is new and clean.
>
> Mathematically, $MF = \dfrac{E_{\text{at time } t}}{E_{\text{initial}}} = \dfrac{\text{Lumens after depreciation}}{\text{Initial Lumens}}$

- It is **always less than or equal to 1** (i.e., $MF \leq 1$).
- It is a **dimensionless quantity** (a pure ratio).
- The reciprocal $\left(\dfrac{1}{MF}\right)$ gives the **Illumination Depreciation Factor** — useful when reverse-calculating the *initial* lumens from a *maintained* lumens specification.
- In modern lighting codes, this is also called the **Light Loss Factor (LLF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Ageing Headlight":**
> Imagine buying a brand-new car. The headlight beam is razor-sharp, white, and far-reaching. After 2 years of use, dust settles on the lens, the bulb dims (lumen depreciation), and the reflector oxidises. The road ahead is illuminated with *less intensity* than the first day. The **ratio of today's light to day-1's light is the Maintenance Factor**. A maintenance factor of **0.7** means the lamp now gives you only **70 %** of its first-day brightness.

The Maintenance Factor is the product of four sub-factors:

$$
MF = \text{LLD} \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD}
$$

where:
- **LLD** = Lamp Lumen Depreciation
- **LBF** = Lamp Burnout Factor
- **LDD** = Luminaire Dirt Depreciation
- **RSDD** = Room Surface Dirt Depreciation

---

### 1.2 Uniformity Ratio (UR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> The **Uniformity Ratio** is the ratio of the *minimum* illumination to the *average* illumination on a given (working) plane.
>
> Mathematically, $UR = \dfrac{E_{\min}}{E_{\text{avg}}}$

- It quantifies **how evenly** light is spread across a task area.
- Range: $0 < UR \leq 1$. A value of **1.0** represents *perfect* uniformity (almost never achieved in practice).
- For an **evenly illuminated plane**, $E_{\min} = E_{\text{avg}} = E_{\max}$, giving $UR = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Classroom Spotlight Problem":**
> Picture a classroom lit by a single bulb in the centre of the ceiling. The student directly beneath the bulb sits in a pool of bright light, while the corner student reads in gloom. The illumination is *non-uniform*. If the average illumination on the desks is **300 lux** and the dimmest corner desk reads only **90 lux**, the Uniformity Ratio is $90 \div 300 = 0.30$. Poor uniformity causes **visual fatigue**, even when the average lux is technically "adequate".

**IS / KTU Recommended Uniformity Ratios (recall-friendly):**

| Application | Recommended UR |
|---|---|
| General office work | $\geq 0.80$ |
| Detailed industrial work | $\geq 0.70$ |
| Rough industrial work | $\geq 0.50$ |
| Circulation corridors | $\geq 0.40$ |

---

### 1.3 Direct Ratio (DR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> The **Direct Ratio** at a point is the ratio of the *direct* illumination (light reaching the point directly from the luminaires without any prior reflection) to the *total* illumination (direct + indirect, i.e., light received after one or more reflections from walls, ceiling, and floor) at that point.
>
> Mathematically, $DR = \dfrac{E_{\text{direct}}}{E_{\text{direct}} + E_{\text{indirect}}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{direct}}}{E_{\text{total}}}$

- Range: $0 \leq DR \leq 1$.
- $DR \to 1$ ⇒ Lighting is *almost entirely direct* (e.g., a bare downlight in a non-reflective room).
- $DR \to 0$ ⇒ Lighting is *almost entirely indirect* (e.g., a cove light reflecting off a white plaster ceiling).
- Strongly dependent on **room index (k)**, **reflection factors** of ceiling/walls/floor, and **luminaire distribution type**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Mirror Trick":**
> Sit under a desk lamp with the shade off. Most of the light hits your book *directly* from the bulb — that is the **direct component**. Now place the lamp inside a white-lined box that bounces light off the ceiling before reaching the book. The light still reaches the book, but only after reflecting off the ceiling. That is the **indirect component**. The **Direct Ratio** tells you what fraction of the light on your book came straight from the source versus via the bounce.

A high Direct Ratio produces *crisp shadows* and *good contrast*; a low Direct Ratio produces *soft, diffuse, shadow-free* light.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Maintenance Factor — Component-wise Deconstruction

The four multiplicative sub-factors collectively describe **why** an installation loses light over time. Understanding each is essential for KTU derivation-type questions.

| Symbol | Factor | Physical Cause | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LLD** | Lamp Lumen Depreciation | Gradual reduction in lamp output as the arc tube ages, electrodes erode, and phosphors degrade | $0.80 - 0.95$ |
| **LBF** | Lamp Burnout Factor | Accounts for the fact that not all lamps in a group are replaced at once; some are burnt out, reducing effective lumens | $0.95 - 1.00$ |
| **LDD** | Luminaire Dirt Depreciation | Dust, oil mist, and smoke accumulation on the luminaire's optical surfaces (lens, reflector, diffuser) | $0.70 - 0.95$ |
| **RSDD** | Room Surface Dirt Depreciation | Darkening of ceiling and walls reduces their reflectance, lowering the indirect (reflected) component | $0.90 - 1.00$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a product and not a sum?** Because each factor acts as an *independent multiplicative loss* on the original lumens. If light loses 10 % to lamp ageing and another 20 % to dirt, the *total* loss is $1 - (0.90 \times 0.80) = 1 - 0.72 = 28$ %, **not** 30 %. This compounding effect is what makes regular cleaning so valuable in industry.

**The Master Equation used in KTU problems:**

$$
\boxed{\;E_{\text{maintained}} = \dfrac{\phi \times N \times \text{CU} \times MF}{A}\;}
$$

where:
- $E_{\text{maintained}}$ = Working-plane illumination **after depreciation** (lux)
- $\phi$ = Initial luminous flux per lamp (lumens)
- $N$ = Number of lamps
- $CU$ = Coefficient of Utilization
- $MF$ = Maintenance Factor
- $A$ = Area of working plane (m²)

---

### 2.2 Uniformity Ratio — Geometric Meaning

Mathematically:

$$
UR = \dfrac{E_{\min}}{E_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{E_{\min} \times A}{\sum E_i \cdot \Delta A_i}
$$

In a **regular grid of luminaires** with equal spacing, the minimum typically occurs at *midway between two luminaires* (or at the farthest corner of a room). The average is computed over the entire working plane.

For symmetric lighting layouts, the maximum is at the centre of the luminaire footprint, and:

$$
E_{\max} = 4 \times E_{\text{at midpoint between two lamps}} \quad \text{(approximately, for point sources)}
$$

The **Uniformity Ratio Gradient** (a related KTU concept):

$$
\text{Gradient} = \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{E_{\min}} \times 100\ \%
$$

A low gradient (≤ 20 %) is desirable for task areas.

---

### 2.3 Direct Ratio — The Vector Component Split

The total illumination at a point P is the **scalar sum** of two components:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{direct}} + E_{\text{indirect}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
DR = \dfrac{E_{\text{direct}}}{E_{\text{direct}} + E_{\text{indirect}}}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
1 - DR = \dfrac{E_{\text{indirect}}}{E_{\text{total}}}
$$

The **Direct Ratio depends on three parameters** (a guaranteed KTU question):

1. **Room Index** $k = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$
   where $L$ = length, $W$ = width, $H_m$ = mounting height above working plane.
2. **Reflection factors** $\rho_c, \rho_w, \rho_f$ of ceiling, walls, floor.
3. **Luminaire distribution class** (Direct, Semi-direct, General diffuse, Semi-indirect, Indirect).

> [!TIP]
> The **Coefficient of Utilization (CU)** itself is the sum of two utilisation factors:
> $CU = \text{UF}_{\text{direct}} + \text{UF}_{\text{indirect}}$
> And the **Direct Ratio can be expressed as**:
> $DR = \dfrac{\text{UF}_{\text{direct}}}{\text{UF}_{\text{direct}} + \text{UF}_{\text{indirect}}}$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table verbatim — it is the single most tested cluster in Module 3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maintenance Factor | $MF = \dfrac{E_t}{E_0} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD}$ | ratio | $0 < MF \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Light Loss Factor | $\text{LLF} = MF \times \text{BF}$ | ratio | $0 < \text{LLF} \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Maintained Lux Formula | $E_m = \dfrac{\phi N \cdot \text{CU} \cdot MF}{A}$ | lux | — |
| 4 | Average Illumination | $E_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\phi N \cdot \text{CU} \cdot MF}{A}$ | lux | — |
| 5 | Uniformity Ratio | $UR = \dfrac{E_{\min}}{E_{\text{avg}}}$ | ratio | $0 < UR \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Uniformity Gradient | $\nabla U = \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{E_{\min}}$ | ratio | $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Direct Ratio | $DR = \dfrac{E_{\text{direct}}}{E_{\text{direct}} + E_{\text{indirect}}}$ | ratio | $0 \leq DR \leq 1$ |
| 8 | Indirect Ratio | $IR = 1 - DR$ | ratio | $0 \leq IR \leq 1$ |
| 9 | Room Index | $k = \dfrac{LW}{H_m (L+W)}$ | ratio | $0.6 \leq k \leq 5$ |
| 10 | Number of Luminaires | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot MF}$ | integer | round up |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Maintenance Factor** is the foundation of the *maintained-illuminance* concept used in the **National Lighting Code (NBC 2016, IS 3646)**. No tender for a stadium, factory, or hospital lighting is approved without specifying $E_{\text{maintained}}$, not $E_{\text{initial}}$.
- **Uniformity Ratio** directly affects **occupant health and productivity**. IS 3646 mandates UR $\geq 0.7$ for sustained office work. Poor UR is the #1 cause of "tired eyes" complaints in open-plan offices.
- **Direct Ratio** is a **glare and visual-comfort lever**. Indoor sports halls (badminton, table tennis) need $DR > 0.6$ for clear ball tracking; art galleries often use $DR < 0.3$ to avoid harsh reflections on paintings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Maintenance Factor from the Master Flux Equation

**Start with the fundamental lumen method:**

$$
E_{\text{avg, initial}} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot N \cdot \text{CU}}{A}
$$

**Reasoning:** When the installation is *new*, every luminaire gives its full rated flux $\phi$, the coefficient of utilisation CU captures the geometry and reflectances, and the lamps are all working.

**Apply losses over time $t$:** Replace $\phi$ with an effective depreciated flux $\phi_t = \phi \cdot MF$, where the *MF* absorbs all the sub-factor losses (LLD, LBF, LDD, RSDD):

$$
\phi_t = \phi \times \text{LLD} \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD}
$$

**Substitute back into the lumen method:**

$$
E_{\text{avg, time } t} = \dfrac{\phi_t \cdot N \cdot \text{CU}}{A} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot N \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{LLD} \cdot \text{LBF} \cdot \text{LDD} \cdot \text{RSDD}}{A}
$$

**Divide the time-$t$ equation by the initial equation:**

$$
\dfrac{E_{\text{avg, time } t}}{E_{\text{avg, initial}}} = \dfrac{\phi_t}{\phi}
$$

**The left-hand side is, by definition, the Maintenance Factor:**

$$
\boxed{\;MF = \dfrac{E_t}{E_0} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD}\;}
$$

**Key insight:** This derivation shows *why* MF is a *multiplicative* product — each sub-factor independently scales the surviving lumens. KTU examiners love to test this independence assumption.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Direct Ratio and Its Connection to CU

**Let the total light reaching the working plane have two origins:**

1. Direct flux from the luminaire downward (no reflection).
2. Indirect flux reflecting off the ceiling, walls, and floor.

**Define two sub-utilisation factors:**

$$
\text{UF}_d = \dfrac{\text{Direct lumens reaching working plane}}{\text{Total lamp lumens}}
$$

$$
\text{UF}_i = \dfrac{\text{Indirect lumens reaching working plane}}{\text{Total lamp lumens}}
$$

**By definition of the Coefficient of Utilisation:**

$$
\text{CU} = \text{UF}_d + \text{UF}_i
$$

**Express the direct illumination on the working plane of area $A$:**

$$
E_{\text{direct}} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot N \cdot \text{UF}_d}{A}
$$

**Express the total illumination:**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot N \cdot (\text{UF}_d + \text{UF}_i)}{A}
$$

**Form the Direct Ratio:**

$$
DR = \dfrac{E_{\text{direct}}}{E_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{\phi N \text{UF}_d / A}{\phi N (\text{UF}_d + \text{UF}_i)/A}
$$

**Cancel common terms** $\phi$, $N$, $A$:

$$
\boxed{\;DR = \dfrac{\text{UF}_d}{\text{UF}_d + \text{UF}_i} = \dfrac{\text{UF}_d}{\text{CU}}\;}
$$

**Equivalent alternate forms (useful in KTU numericals):**

$$
DR = 1 - \dfrac{\text{UF}_i}{\text{CU}}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\dfrac{\text{UF}_i}{\text{UF}_d} = \dfrac{1 - DR}{DR}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical #1 (Maintenance Factor)

> **Problem:** A factory hall uses 40 metal-halide lamps, each rated 400 W, 32 000 lm. Lamp lumen depreciation after 8000 h is 0.85, lamp burnout factor 0.97, luminaire dirt depreciation 0.80, room surface dirt depreciation 0.92. Coefficient of utilisation is 0.65. Floor area is 25 m × 12 m. Find (a) the Maintenance Factor, (b) the **initial** average illumination, and (c) the **maintained** average illumination after 8000 h.

**Part (a) — Maintenance Factor**

$$
MF = \text{LLD} \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD}
$$

$$
MF = 0.85 \times 0.97 \times 0.80 \times 0.92
$$

**Compute step by step:**

$$
0.85 \times 0.97 = 0.8245
$$

$$
0.8245 \times 0.80 = 0.6596
$$

$$
0.6596 \times 0.92 = 0.6068
$$

$$
\boxed{\;MF \approx 0.607\;}
$$

**Part (b) — Initial average illumination** (at $t = 0$, all losses absent; use $\phi = 32\,000$ lm directly):

$$
A = 25 \times 12 = 300 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
E_{\text{initial}} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot N \cdot \text{CU}}{A} = \dfrac{32\,000 \times 40 \times 0.65}{300}
$$

**Compute:**

$$
32\,000 \times 40 = 1\,280\,000
$$

$$
1\,280\,000 \times 0.65 = 832\,000
$$

$$
\dfrac{832\,000}{300} = 2773.33
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{\text{initial}} \approx 2773 \ \text{lux}\;}
$$

**Part (c) — Maintained illumination** (apply MF):

$$
E_{\text{maintained}} = E_{\text{initial}} \times MF = 2773.33 \times 0.6068
$$

$$
E_{\text{maintained}} \approx 1683 \ \text{lux}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check using the direct formula:**
> $E_{\text{maint}} = \dfrac{32\,000 \times 40 \times 0.65 \times 0.6068}{300} = \dfrac{504\,860.8}{300} = 1682.9$ lux. ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical #2 (Uniformity Ratio)

> **Problem:** A 20 m × 10 m office uses a regular grid of luminaires. Average illumination on the working plane is 500 lux. The minimum, measured at a corner desk, is 350 lux. The maximum, measured directly under a luminaire, is 720 lux. Calculate (a) the Uniformity Ratio, (b) the Uniformity Gradient, and (c) comment on whether the installation meets IS 3646 for office work.

**Part (a):**

$$
UR = \dfrac{E_{\min}}{E_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{350}{500} = 0.70
$$

$$
\boxed{\;UR = 0.70\;}
$$

**Part (b):**

$$
\nabla U = \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{E_{\min}} = \dfrac{720 - 350}{350} = \dfrac{370}{350}
$$

$$
\nabla U \approx 1.057 = 105.7\ \%
$$

**Part (c) — Compliance Comment:**

> The Uniformity Ratio of 0.70 just satisfies the **IS 3646 minimum** of 0.70 for general office work. However, the gradient of 105.7 % is *very high*, meaning there is a large contrast between brightest and darkest zones. KTU verdict: **Marginally acceptable; recommend increasing the number of luminaires or improving luminaire distribution to bring UR up to 0.80 and gradient below 30 %.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical #3 (Direct Ratio)

> **Problem:** A classroom has a Direct Utilisation Factor of 0.32 and an Indirect Utilisation Factor of 0.18. Compute (a) the Coefficient of Utilisation, (b) the Direct Ratio, and (c) the Indirect Ratio.

**Part (a):**

$$
\text{CU} = \text{UF}_d + \text{UF}_i = 0.32 + 0.18 = 0.50
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CU} = 0.50\;}
$$

**Part (b):**

$$
DR = \dfrac{\text{UF}_d}{\text{CU}} = \dfrac{0.32}{0.50} = 0.64
$$

$$
\boxed{\;DR = 0.64\;}
$$

**Part (c):**

$$
IR = 1 - DR = 1 - 0.64 = 0.36
$$

$$
\boxed{\;IR = 0.36\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 64 % of the effective light on the desks arrives *directly* from the luminaire; 36 % arrives *after* at least one reflection. This is a typical **semi-direct** luminaire installation — good for classrooms where blackboard visibility needs crisp direct light, but with a soft indirect component that reduces glare.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — The Lighting Loss Chain

The diagram below traces the *chain of multiplicative losses* that converts the manufacturer's rated lumens into the *delivered, maintained* lumens on a real working plane. This is the **single most important block diagram** for Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rated Lamp Lumens phi] --> B[LLD - Lamp Lumen Depreciation]
    B --> C[LBF - Lamp Burnout Factor]
    C --> D[LDD - Luminaire Dirt Depreciation]
    D --> E[RSDD - Room Surface Dirt Depreciation]
    E --> F[Effective Lamp Lumens phi_eff]
    F --> G[Multiply by N - Number of Lamps]
    G --> H[Multiply by CU - Coefficient of Utilisation]
    H --> I[Divide by A - Working Plane Area]
    I --> J[Maintained Illumination E_maintained]
    A2[Initial Illumination E_initial] -.-> J
    MF[Maintenance Factor MF] -.-> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block along the top row is a *multiplicative loss* (a value $\leq 1$). The dotted lines from `E_initial` and `MF` emphasise that the maintained illumination equals the initial illumination scaled down by the Maintenance Factor. **In a KTU numerical, if a sub-factor is missing, the question is testing whether you know to assume it is 1.0.**

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Map — How the Three Ratios Interact

The three ratios of this module are **not independent** — they are pieces of the same lighting design puzzle. The following diagram shows how MF, UR, and DR are derived from, and feed back into, the master lumen-method equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Quantities]
        P[Lamp Flux phi]
        NVAL[Number of Lamps N]
        AVAL[Area A]
        CU[CU Tables]
        ROOM[Room Index k]
        REFL[Reflection Factors rho_c rho_w rho_f]
    end

    subgraph DEPR[Depreciation Block]
        LLD[LLD]
        LBF[LBF]
        LDD[LDD]
        RSDD[RSDD]
    end

    subgraph RATIOS[Three Module-3 Ratios]
        MF1[Maintenance Factor MF]
        UR1[Uniformity Ratio UR]
        DR1[Direct Ratio DR]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Design Outputs]
        EINIT[Initial Illumination E_0]
        EMAINT[Maintained Illumination E_t]
        EMIN[Minimum Illumination E_min]
    end

    P --> LLD --> MF1
    LBF --> MF1
    LDD --> MF1
    RSDD --> MF1
    MF1 --> EMAINT
    P --> EINIT
    CU --> EINIT
    CU --> EMAINT
    NVAL --> EINIT
    NVAL --> EMAINT
    AVAL --> EINIT
    AVAL --> EMAINT
    EINIT --> UR1
    UR1 --> EMIN
    ROOM --> CU
    REFL --> CU
    CU --> DR1
    ROOM --> DR1
    REFL --> DR1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight from the diagram:** The **Uniformity Ratio** requires the *spatial distribution* of illumination (so it needs point-by-point or grid data, not just the average), while the **Direct Ratio** is determined at the design stage from room geometry and luminaire type. The **Maintenance Factor** is the *only one* of the three that varies with time.

---

### 4.3 Visual Summary — Boundaries of Each Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MFRANGE[MF Range: 0 to 1] --> MF1[MF = 1: Brand New Installation]
    MFRANGE --> MF2[MF = 0.5: Severely Degraded]
    URRANGE[UR Range: 0 to 1] --> UR1[UR = 1: Perfect Uniformity]
    URRANGE --> UR2[UR = 0: Point Source in Dark Room]
    DRRANGE[DR Range: 0 to 1] --> DR1[DR = 1: Pure Direct Light]
    DRRANGE --> DR2[DR = 0: Pure Indirect Light]
```

> [!TIP]
> Use this boundary map as a **sanity check** in numericals. If your KTU calculation yields $MF = 1.2$ or $UR = 1.5$, you have made a sign/multiplication error — re-check immediately.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** — **Q1.** Define **Maintenance Factor**. List any two of its component sub-factors.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Maintenance Factor (MF)** is defined as the ratio of the illumination on a working plane at a specified time during the operating life of an installation to the **initial illumination** provided by the installation when everything is clean and new.
>
> $MF = \dfrac{E_t}{E_0}$
>
> Two of its component sub-factors are:
> 1. **Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD)** — gradual drop in lamp output as the arc tube ages.
> 2. **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD)** — accumulation of dust and grime on the luminaire's optical surfaces.
>
> *(The other two are Lamp Burnout Factor LBF and Room Surface Dirt Depreciation RSDD. Any two carry 1 mark each; the definition carries 1 mark.)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** — **Q2.** Distinguish between **Uniformity Ratio** and **Direct Ratio** in interior lighting design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Uniformity Ratio (UR) | Direct Ratio (DR) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ratio of minimum to average illumination on a plane | Ratio of direct illumination to total illumination at a point |
| Formula | $UR = E_{\min} / E_{\text{avg}}$ | $DR = E_{\text{direct}} / (E_{\text{direct}} + E_{\text{indirect}})$ |
| Range | $0 < UR \leq 1$ | $0 \leq DR \leq 1$ |
| Depends on | Layout geometry, luminaire spacing, lamp type | Room index, reflection factors, luminaire class |
| Purpose | Ensures even visual environment | Controls glare and contrast / shadow sharpness |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Maintenance Factor. A workshop is to be illuminated to a maintained level of **200 lux**. The installation uses **30 fluorescent luminaires**, each fitted with **2 tubes of 40 W, 3000 lumens** rating. The Coefficient of Utilisation is **0.55**, the Lamp Lumen Depreciation is **0.90**, the Luminaire Dirt Depreciation is **0.85**, and the Lamp Burnout Factor is **0.95**. The Room Surface Dirt Depreciation is **0.95**. The workshop area is **20 m × 12 m**.
Compute:
  (i) The Maintenance Factor.
  (ii) The maintained illumination actually achieved. Comment on whether it meets the design target.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is Direct Ratio? A luminaire has a Direct Utilisation Factor of 0.40 and a Total Coefficient of Utilisation of 0.62. Calculate the Indirect Ratio and the ratio of Indirect to Direct Utilisation Factors. Comment on the type of luminaire distribution.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Number of lamps** $N = 30 \times 2 = 60$ tubes.
(1/2 mark)

**Step 2 — Total rated flux** $\phi_{\text{total}} = 60 \times 3000 = 180\,000$ lumens.
(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Maintenance Factor** (definition: 1 mark; calculation: 1 mark)

$$
MF = \text{LLD} \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD} = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.85 \times 0.95
$$

**Compute:**

$$
0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.855
$$

$$
0.855 \times 0.85 = 0.72675
$$

$$
0.72675 \times 0.95 = 0.69041
$$

$$
\boxed{\;MF \approx 0.69\;}
$$

(1 mark for the value)

**Step 4 — Area** $A = 20 \times 12 = 240$ m². (1/2 mark)

**Step 5 — Maintained illumination:**

$$
E_{\text{maint}} = \dfrac{\phi N \cdot \text{CU} \cdot MF}{A} = \dfrac{180\,000 \times 0.55 \times 0.69}{240}
$$

(1 mark for the substitution; 1 mark for the result)

**Compute:**

$$
180\,000 \times 0.55 = 99\,000
$$

$$
99\,000 \times 0.69 = 68\,310
$$

$$
\dfrac{68\,310}{240} = 284.6
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{\text{maint}} \approx 285 \ \text{lux}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):** Achieved maintained level (285 lux) **exceeds** the design target of 200 lux by about 42 %. The installation is **over-designed**; the engineer could reduce the number of luminaires or use lower-wattage tubes to optimise cost. *(Conversely, in a KTU answer, "design margin" wording earns full credit.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Direct Ratio (2 marks):**
The Direct Ratio at a point on the working plane is the ratio of the illumination received **directly** from the luminaires to the **total** illumination (direct + indirect/reflected) at that point. $DR = \dfrac{E_d}{E_d + E_i}$.

**Step 2 — Given values:**

$$
\text{UF}_d = 0.40, \quad \text{CU} = 0.62
$$

**Step 3 — Indirect Utilisation Factor:**

$$
\text{UF}_i = \text{CU} - \text{UF}_d = 0.62 - 0.40 = 0.22
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Indirect Ratio:**

$$
IR = 1 - DR = 1 - \dfrac{\text{UF}_d}{\text{CU}} = 1 - \dfrac{0.40}{0.62} = 1 - 0.6452 = 0.3548
$$

$$
\boxed{\;IR \approx 0.35\;}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Ratio of Indirect to Direct UF:**

$$
\dfrac{\text{UF}_i}{\text{UF}_d} = \dfrac{0.22}{0.40} = 0.55
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 6 — Comment on luminaire type (1 mark):**
Since $DR = 0.645$ (more than half the light is direct) but with a substantial indirect component ($\text{UF}_i / \text{UF}_d = 0.55$), this is a **semi-direct** luminaire distribution. Such luminaires are typical for **workshops, classrooms, and offices** where good task illumination and modest glare reduction are both required.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Uniformity Ratio** and **Uniformity Gradient**. A rectangular hall of dimensions **30 m × 15 m** is to be lit by a regular array of 24 luminaires in 6 rows of 4. The illumination at the brightest point is **880 lux** and at the dimmest point is **440 lux**. Calculate:
  (i) The Uniformity Ratio, assuming the average illumination equals 600 lux.
  (ii) The Uniformity Gradient.
  (iii) State, with justification, whether this design is acceptable for a **machine shop** (IS requirement: $UR \geq 0.70$).

**(b) [7 Marks]** The Maintenance Factor of a lighting installation is **0.72**. After one year, the lamp lumen depreciation worsens to a value that reduces the MF to **0.60**, with all other sub-factors unchanged. If the original Lamp Lumen Depreciation was **0.90** and the Lamp Burnout Factor is **0.96** and Luminaire Dirt Depreciation is **0.90**, calculate the original Room Surface Dirt Depreciation. Then compute the new LLD.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Uniformity Ratio (UR):** Ratio of minimum to average illumination on the working plane: $UR = E_{\min} / E_{\text{avg}}$.
- **Uniformity Gradient:** Ratio of the difference between maximum and minimum to the minimum: $\nabla U = (E_{\max} - E_{\min})/E_{\min}$.

**Step 2 — Compute UR:**

$$
UR = \dfrac{E_{\min}}{E_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{440}{600} = 0.733
$$

(1 mark)

$$
\boxed{\;UR \approx 0.73\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Uniformity Gradient:**

$$
\nabla U = \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{E_{\min}} = \dfrac{880 - 440}{440} = \dfrac{440}{440} = 1.0 = 100\ \%
$$

(1 mark)

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla U = 100\ \%\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance (1 mark):**
Since $UR = 0.73 \geq 0.70$, the design **meets the IS 3646 minimum** for a machine shop. However, the gradient of 100 % is *high*, indicating significant contrast between bright and dim zones. A more even design with $UR \geq 0.80$ would be preferred.

**Step 5 — Layout cross-check (2 marks):** The hall area = $30 \times 15 = 450$ m². With 24 luminaires, the spacing density is one luminaire per 18.75 m². If each luminaire is suspended at a typical 6 m mounting height in a 9 m high industrial hall, the spacing-to-height ratio is approximately 1.5, which is within IES-recommended limits (0.6 to 1.5 for industrial direct lighting). The layout is geometrically sound.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities:**

Original: $MF_1 = 0.72$, $\text{LLD}_1 = 0.90$, $\text{LBF} = 0.96$, $\text{LDD} = 0.90$, $\text{RSDD} = ?$

**Step 2 — Solve for RSDD (1 mark for equation setup, 1 mark for value):**

$$
MF_1 = \text{LLD}_1 \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD}
$$

$$
0.72 = 0.90 \times 0.96 \times 0.90 \times \text{RSDD}
$$

**Compute the known product:**

$$
0.90 \times 0.96 = 0.864
$$

$$
0.864 \times 0.90 = 0.7776
$$

$$
\text{RSDD} = \dfrac{0.72}{0.7776} = 0.926
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{RSDD} \approx 0.926\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the new MF to back-calculate the new LLD (1 mark):**

New $MF_2 = 0.60$. All sub-factors except LLD unchanged.

$$
MF_2 = \text{LLD}_2 \times 0.96 \times 0.90 \times 0.926
$$

**Compute the constant product:**

$$
0.96 \times 0.90 = 0.864
$$

$$
0.864 \times 0.926 = 0.800
$$

$$
\text{LLD}_2 = \dfrac{0.60}{0.800} = 0.75
$$

(2 marks for value + setup)

$$
\boxed{\;\text{LLD}_2 = 0.75\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The Lamp Lumen Depreciation has worsened from **0.90 to 0.75** over the year. This represents a **16.7 % additional lumen loss** due to lamp ageing, meaning the lamps are nearing the end of their useful life and **group relamping should be scheduled immediately** to restore design illumination levels.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting RSDD / LBF.** When a numerical gives only LLD, LDD and CU, students sometimes *omit* LBF and RSDD. KTU key answer says: assume missing factors = **1.0** explicitly in your answer, **do not silently drop them**.
>
> 2. **UR formula direction.** $UR = E_{\min}/E_{\text{avg}}$ — **NOT** $E_{\text{avg}}/E_{\min}$. Reversing this gives a number > 1, which is a sure sign of an error. Validated range is $0 < UR \leq 1$.
>
> 3. **DR denominator.** Students often write $DR = E_{\text{direct}} / E_{\text{indirect}}$. The correct denominator is $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{direct}} + E_{\text{indirect}}$. This is a **2-mark loss** in the KTU valuation key.
>
> 4. **Unit confusion in MF.** Maintenance Factor is a *ratio* — write "0.72" or "0.72 (dimensionless)", **never** "0.72 lumens" or "0.72 lux".
>
> 5. **Confusing MF with depreciation.** $MF$ is a *retention* factor (always $\leq 1$). Its reciprocal $1/MF$ is the *depreciation* or *loss* factor. Writing $MF = 1.4$ because "depreciation is 40 %" is a classic KTU failure pattern.
>
> 6. **Mixing maintained and initial in one equation.** In the formula $E_{\text{maint}} = \phi N \cdot \text{CU} \cdot MF / A$, do **not** also subtract the depreciation separately — that would be double-counting.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Definitions of MF, UR, DR**
>
> - **Maintenance Factor (MF):** Ratio of illumination at time $t$ to initial illumination. $MF = E_t / E_0 \leq 1$. Always a product of independent sub-losses: $MF = \text{LLD} \times \text{LBF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD}$. Master equation: $E_{\text{maint}} = \phi N \cdot \text{CU} \cdot MF / A$.
> - **Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD):** Lamp's own ageing, typically **0.80 – 0.95**. First to deteriorate.
> - **Lamp Burnout Factor (LBF):** Accounts for spot-burnouts in a group, typically **0.95 – 1.00**. Becomes relevant for spot/group replacement strategy.
> - **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD):** Dirt on luminaire, **0.70 – 0.95**. Depends on cleaning interval and ambient dust.
> - **Room Surface Dirt Depreciation (RSDD):** Walls/ceiling darkening, **0.90 – 1.00**. Largest effect in low-reflectance industrial rooms.
> - **Uniformity Ratio (UR):** $E_{\min}/E_{\text{avg}}$, range $(0, 1]$. Higher = more even. IS 3646 mandates UR $\geq 0.70$ for offices, $\geq 0.50$ for rough industrial.
> - **Uniformity Gradient:** $(E_{\max} - E_{\min})/E_{\min}$. Lower = more even. $\leq 20$ % is excellent, $\leq 30$ % is acceptable.
> - **Direct Ratio (DR):** $E_{\text{direct}} / (E_{\text{direct}} + E_{\text{indirect}})$, range $[0, 1]$. $> 0.6$ = direct/semi-direct luminaire; $< 0.4$ = indirect/semi-indirect. Controls glare and shadow character.
> - **Indirect Ratio (IR):** $1 - DR$.
> - **DR from CU tables:** $DR = \text{UF}_d / \text{CU} = \text{UF}_d / (\text{UF}_d + \text{UF}_i)$.
> - **Dependencies of DR:** Room Index $k$, ceiling/wall/floor reflectances $\rho_c, \rho_w, \rho_f$, and luminaire class.
> - **Room Index formula:** $k = LW / [H_m (L + W)]$. Typical range $0.6$ to $5.0$.
> - **Numerical traps:** Never compute $UR$ with $E_{\max}$ in numerator; never set $MF = 1.4$; never write $DR = E_d / E_i$.
> - **Reverse engineering MF:** If a sub-factor is unknown, isolate it: $\text{sub-factor} = MF / (\text{product of known sub-factors})$.
> - **Time behaviour:** Only MF varies with time; UR and DR are fixed at the design stage (assuming the installation layout is not changed).
> - **KTU high-yield linkage:** $E_{\text{initial}} = \phi N \cdot \text{CU} / A$ → $E_{\text{maint}} = E_{\text{initial}} \times MF$ → $E_{\min} = E_{\text{maint}} \times UR$ (for design-checking). The full chain is: **flux → initial lux → maintained lux → minimum lux**, with MF, CU, and UR applied successively.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coefficients of Utilisation and Factors Affecting It
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coefficients of Utilisation and Factors Affecting It

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — COEFFICIENTS OF UTILISATION & FACTORS AFFECTING IT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coefficient of Utilisation (CU)**, often denoted as **η** or **UF (Utilization Factor)**, is defined as the ratio of the total luminous flux (lumens) effectively received on the *working plane* to the total luminous flux (lumens) emitted by the lamp(s) within the luminaire.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$CU \;=\; \eta \;=\; \frac{\text{Lumens received on the working plane}}{\text{Lumens emitted by the lamp}}$$

The **Coefficient of Utilisation** is a dimensionless quantity (always less than unity, typically between **0.3 and 0.8** for most practical installations) and serves as a primary benchmark for evaluating the effectiveness of an indoor lighting installation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Plane**: The imaginary horizontal plane (usually at a height of **0.85 m** above the floor for offices, **1.0 m** for industrial workbenches) on which the actual task is performed and where the illuminance is measured or specified.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

Imagine you order a large pizza (the **lamp's total light output**). However, by the time the pizza reaches your plate, some slices have been lost — crusts left in the box, toppings fallen off, grease absorbed by the paper. What actually *fills your stomach* is the **"utilised"** portion.

- **Total pizza baked** = Total lumens emitted by lamp
- **Pizza on your plate** = Lumens reaching the working plane
- **CU** = Plate portion ÷ Total portion

A high CU means your luminaire is a "well-behaved waiter" — directing nearly all light downwards where it is needed, with minimal wastage on walls, ceilings, or fixtures.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Working plane height (offices) | **0.85 m** | Above finished floor |
| Working plane height (industrial) | **1.0 m** | Above finished floor |
| Room Index range (KTU standard) | **0.6 to 5.0** | Dimensionless |
| Typical CU range | **0.3 to 0.8** | Higher = better utilisation |
| Reflection factors (standard) | Ceiling: **0.7**, Walls: **0.5**, Floor: **0.2** | Used in IES tables |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember: **CU is a system property**, not a lamp property. The same lamp can have very different CUs in different rooms!

---

### 1.4 Why Coefficient of Utilisation Matters in Illumination Engineering

The CU bridges the gap between *what the lamp produces* and *what the user perceives*. In KTU 2024 scheme outcomes, this concept is tied directly to **CO3 (Design of indoor illumination systems)** — students are expected to compute the **number of luminaires** required for a given area, for which CU is the critical multiplier.

The fundamental design equation linked to CU is:

$$N \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times CU \times MF}$$

where the symbols will be detailed in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Basis of CU

The Coefficient of Utilisation is derived from the principle of **interreflection of light** between the room's three principal bounding surfaces:

1. **Ceiling** (upper reflecting surface)
2. **Walls** (side reflecting surfaces)
3. **Floor** (lower reflecting surface, often the working plane itself)

The luminaire emits light in two principal downward components:

- **Direct component**: Light rays travelling directly from the luminaire to the working plane.
- **Reflected (indirect) component**: Light rays that first strike the ceiling/walls, undergo one or more reflections, and then eventually reach the working plane.

Mathematically:

$$\phi_{\text{utilised}} \;=\; \phi_{\text{direct}} \;+\; \phi_{\text{reflected}}$$

$$\therefore CU \;=\; \frac{\phi_{\text{direct}} + \phi_{\text{reflected}}}{\phi_{\text{lamp}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interreflection principle**: Walls reflect about **40–70%** of incident light back into the room. A bright wall acts as a "secondary light source," significantly increasing CU.

## 2.2 Room Index (Room Cavity Ratio) — The Key Geometric Driver

The **Room Index** $k$ (also called **Room Cavity Ratio**, RCR) is the single most important geometric factor governing CU. It is defined as:

$$k \;=\; \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$$

where:
- $L$ = Length of the room (m)
- $W$ = Width of the room (m)
- $H_m$ = Mounting height of the luminaire **above** the working plane (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $H_m$ is measured from the *working plane*, not from the floor. For a ceiling-mounted luminaire in an office: $H_m = \text{Ceiling height} - 0.85\,\text{m}$.

### Behaviour of CU with Room Index

| Room Geometry | Room Index $k$ | Typical CU | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Narrow, tall room | $k < 1$ | Low (0.3–0.4) | Light lost on tall walls |
| Squarish room | $k = 1 \text{ to } 2$ | Moderate (0.4–0.6) | Balanced |
| Wide, low room | $k > 2$ | High (0.6–0.8) | Most light falls directly on working plane |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CU = \dfrac{\phi_{\text{util}}}{\phi_{\text{lamp}}}$ | Utilisation coefficient | dimensionless | $0.3 \le CU \le 0.8$ |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m(L+W)}$ | Room index | dimensionless | $0.6 \le k \le 5.0$ |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\phi \times CU \times MF}$ | Number of luminaires | integer | round up |
| 4 | $A = L \times W$ | Floor area of room | m² | — |
| 5 | $H_m = h_c - h_{wp}$ | Mounting height above WP | m | ceiling $\approx 2.5$–$4$ m |
| 6 | $MF = \dfrac{\text{ILF} \times \text{LLF}}{1}$ | Maintenance factor | dimensionless | $0.5 \le MF \le 0.8$ |
| 7 | $n = \dfrac{N}{N_{\text{per row}}}$ | Layout design check | — | integer |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL SYMBOL SAFETY**: In the formula sheet above, every vertical pipe character `|` inside math expressions has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. (None of these expressions required it, but the safeguard is in force.)

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Office buildings**: Architects use CU tables to decide whether to use direct, indirect, or direct-indirect luminaires.
- **Industrial plants**: High-bay installations demand CUs as high as **0.7+** to minimise energy costs.
- **Smart lighting design**: Modern BIM (Building Information Modelling) software (e.g., DIALux, Relux) computes CU numerically using radiosity methods rather than the old IES tables.

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Coefficient of Utilisation

The CU depends on **four classes of parameters**:

### Class A — Geometric Parameters
1. **Room Index (k)**: As $k$ increases, CU generally **increases**.
2. **Mounting Height ($H_m$)**: Higher mounting lowers CU (light spreads over a larger area).
3. **Room Shape (L:W ratio)**: Long narrow rooms give lower CU than square rooms of equal area.

### Class B — Surface Reflectance Parameters
4. **Ceiling reflectance ($\rho_c$)**: Higher ceiling reflectance → more downward interreflected light → higher CU.
5. **Wall reflectance ($\rho_w$)**: Strong influence; doubling wall reflectance can raise CU by **10–20%**.
6. **Floor reflectance ($\rho_f$)**: Minor effect (usually 0.1 to 0.3).

### Class C — Luminaire Parameters
7. **Luminaire light distribution**: Direct, semi-direct, indirect, general diffuse — each gives a different CU for the same room.
8. **Luminaire efficiency ($\eta_L$)**: Optical losses inside the fixture (glare shields, diffusers) reduce CU.
9. **Luminaire suspension**: Pendant mounting vs surface mounting vs recessed.

### Class D — Application Parameters
10. **Working plane height**: Affects $H_m$ and thus the room index.
11. **Number of luminaires in operation**: Affects interreflection patterns (more lamps → more upward flux → more wall reflections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Insight**: "For a *given* luminaire in a *given* room, the only way to improve CU without changing fixtures is to **paint the walls lighter**."

## 2.6 Standard Reflection Factors Used in IES Tables

| Surface | Light colour | Reflectance | Medium colour | Reflectance | Dark colour | Reflectance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ceiling | 0.7 | 0.5 | 0.3 |
| Walls | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.1 |
| Floor | 0.3 | 0.2 | 0.1 |

These are tabulated in **IES Code (Illuminating Engineering Society)** lighting handbooks and form the reference points for KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Luminaires Equation

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme ESE papers for Illumination Technology.

### Given Data
- $E$ = Required illuminance on working plane (lux or lm/m²)
- $A$ = Total area of working plane (m²)
- $\phi$ = Luminous flux per lamp (lumens)
- $N$ = Total number of luminaires (to be found)
- $CU$ = Coefficient of utilisation
- $MF$ = Maintenance factor

### Step 1 — Total luminous flux required on the working plane

Illuminance is flux per unit area. Therefore, the total flux that **must** reach the working plane to achieve the design illuminance is:

$$\phi_{\text{required on WP}} \;=\; E \times A$$

This follows directly from the definition $E = \Phi / A$, rearranged as $\Phi = E \times A$.

### Step 2 — Relate $\phi_{\text{required on WP}}$ to total lamp flux

By the definition of Coefficient of Utilisation:

$$CU \;=\; \frac{\phi_{\text{required on WP}}}{\phi_{\text{total emitted by lamps}}}$$

Rearranging to find the lamp flux required:

$$\phi_{\text{total emitted by lamps}} \;=\; \frac{\phi_{\text{required on WP}}}{CU} \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{CU}$$

### Step 3 — Account for depreciation via Maintenance Factor

The **Maintenance Factor (MF)** is defined as the ratio of illuminance on the working plane *after a specified period of use* to the illuminance under *initial* (new) conditions:

$$MF \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{maintained}}}{E_{\text{initial}}}$$

Therefore, the initial design must **over-provision** flux by a factor of $1/MF$:

$$\phi_{\text{total emitted by lamps, initial}} \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{CU \times MF}$$

### Step 4 — Convert lamp flux to number of luminaires

If each luminaire contains one lamp of rating $\phi$ lumens, and there are $N$ luminaires:

$$\phi_{\text{total emitted by lamps, initial}} \;=\; N \times \phi$$

### Step 5 — Equate and solve for N

Equating the expressions from Step 3 and Step 4:

$$N \times \phi \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{CU \times MF}$$

$$\boxed{\,N \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times CU \times MF}\,}$$

This is the **canonical luminaire design equation** in KTU syllabus.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem (KTU 2022 pattern)**: An office room of size $12\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m}$ requires an illuminance of **500 lux** on the working plane. The room has a ceiling height of **3 m**, and the working plane is at **0.85 m**. Using LED luminaires of **4000 lumens** each, and a CU of **0.6** with MF of **0.8**, determine the number of luminaires required.

### Solution — Step-by-step

**Step 1: Compute the area**
$$A \;=\; L \times W \;=\; 12 \times 8 \;=\; 96 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute mounting height above working plane**
$$H_m \;=\; 3 - 0.85 \;=\; 2.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute Room Index (verifies CU choice)**
$$k \;=\; \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)} \;=\; \frac{12 \times 8}{2.15 \times (12 + 8)} \;=\; \frac{96}{2.15 \times 20} \;=\; \frac{96}{43}$$

$$k \;\approx\; 2.23$$

A room index of 2.23 is consistent with the assumed CU of 0.6 (verified against standard IES tables).

**Step 4: Apply the luminaire design equation**
$$N \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times CU \times MF} \;=\; \frac{500 \times 96}{4000 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}$$

Computing the denominator:
$$4000 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 \;=\; 1920$$

Computing the numerator:
$$500 \times 96 \;=\; 48000$$

Dividing:
$$N \;=\; \frac{48000}{1920} \;=\; 25$$

**Step 5: Round up and design layout**
$$N \;=\; 25 \text{ luminaires (exact — no rounding needed)}$$

**Layout suggestion**: A $5 \times 5$ grid in the $12\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m}$ room with row spacing $\approx 2.4\,\text{m}$ and column spacing $\approx 1.6\,\text{m}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Always round UP**, never down. Insufficient illuminance is a safety/compliance issue; excess illuminance wastes energy.

## 3.3 Effect of Varying Wall Reflectance — Analytical Comparison

Let us analyse how CU changes when wall reflectance is altered, all else being constant.

| Scenario | $\rho_c$ | $\rho_w$ | $\rho_f$ | Room Index $k$ | CU (from IES table) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1: Dark room | 0.5 | 0.1 | 0.1 | 2.23 | 0.42 | Baseline |
| 2: Medium room | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.2 | 2.23 | 0.54 | +28.6% |
| 3: Bright room | 0.7 | 0.5 | 0.3 | 2.23 | 0.66 | +57.1% |

**Inference**: Doubling wall reflectance from 0.1 to 0.5 nearly doubled the CU — this is why **interior decoration** matters in lighting design.

## 3.4 Effect of Mounting Height on CU

Using the same office room but varying $H_m$:

| $H_m$ (m) | Room Index $k$ | CU | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.5 | 3.20 | 0.72 | Pendant at low height — excellent |
| 2.15 | 2.23 | 0.60 | Standard office |
| 3.00 | 1.60 | 0.48 | High ceiling — light lost on walls |
| 4.00 | 1.20 | 0.40 | Industrial high-bay — direct fixtures preferred |

**Inference**: Lower mounting (within ergonomic and glare limits) gives higher CU.

## 3.5 Common Pitfalls in KTU Numerical Solutions

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1**: Confusing $H_m$ (mounting above working plane) with $H_c$ (ceiling height). The difference is the working plane height (usually 0.85 m).
> 
> **Pitfall 2**: Forgetting to convert $H_m$ correctly when the luminaire is *recessed* into the ceiling. In that case, $H_m = (h_c - 0.15) - h_{wp}$ where 0.15 m accounts for the recessed depth.
> 
> **Pitfall 3**: Using CU = 1.0 (which would imply no losses — physically impossible).
> 
> **Pitfall 4**: Forgetting the Maintenance Factor. Without it, the design is for "day 1" only.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Coefficient of Utilisation. Mention any three factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Coefficient of Utilisation (CU) is defined as the ratio of the total luminous flux effectively reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps.

$$CU = \frac{\text{Lumens on working plane}}{\text{Lumens emitted by lamps}}$$

It is a dimensionless number, typically between **0.3 and 0.8**, used in the design of indoor lighting installations to compute the number of luminaires required.

**Three factors affecting CU (1 mark):**
1. **Room Index (k)** — depends on room dimensions and mounting height.
2. **Wall reflectance ($\rho_w$)** — lighter walls increase CU via interreflection.
3. **Luminaire light distribution type** — direct luminaires have higher CU than indirect ones in the same room.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Room Index? How does it affect the Coefficient of Utilisation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Room Index $k$ is a dimensionless number that characterises the geometry of a room relative to the luminaire mounting height. It is given by:

$$k = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$$

**Effect on CU (1.5 marks):**
- As room index **increases** (wider, lower rooms), CU **increases** because a greater fraction of the lamp's flux falls directly on the working plane rather than being lost on the walls.
- As room index **decreases** (narrower, taller rooms), CU **decreases** because a larger proportion of the light strikes the tall walls and is lost by absorption after one or two reflections.
- In KTU design practice, the standard range of $k$ used in IES tables is **0.6 to 5.0**.

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the number of luminaires required to provide a specified illuminance in an indoor space. Define every term used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drawing hall of size **20 m × 12 m** requires an average illuminance of **750 lux** on the working plane. The ceiling height is **4 m** and the working plane is at **0.85 m**. The CU is **0.55** and the maintenance factor is **0.75**. Each fluorescent luminaire emits **5000 lumens**. Calculate the number of luminaires required and suggest a suitable layout. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the design requirement. To achieve an average illuminance $E$ (lux) over a working plane area $A$ (m²), the total luminous flux required on the working plane is:

$$\phi_{\text{required on WP}} = E \times A$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the definition of Coefficient of Utilisation. By definition:

$$CU = \frac{\phi_{\text{required on WP}}}{\phi_{\text{total emitted by lamps}}}$$

Therefore, the total lamp flux needed (neglecting maintenance) is:

$$\phi_{\text{total, new}} = \frac{E \times A}{CU}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Account for lamp/fitting depreciation via the Maintenance Factor (MF):

$$MF = \frac{E_{\text{maintained}}}{E_{\text{initial}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{\text{total, with MF}} = \frac{E \times A}{CU \times MF}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** If each luminaire contains one lamp of rating $\phi$ lumens and there are $N$ luminaires in total:

$$\phi_{\text{total, with MF}} = N \times \phi$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Equate and solve for $N$:

$$N \times \phi = \frac{E \times A}{CU \times MF}$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times CU \times MF}}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Term definitions:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of luminaires | dimensionless (integer) |
| $E$ | Required illuminance on working plane | lux (lm/m²) |
| $A$ | Area of working plane | m² |
| $\phi$ | Luminous flux per lamp | lumens (lm) |
| $CU$ | Coefficient of Utilisation | dimensionless |
| $MF$ | Maintenance Factor | dimensionless |

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute area**
$$A = L \times W = 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Compute mounting height above working plane**
$$H_m = h_c - h_{wp} = 4 - 0.85 = 3.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Compute room index for verification**
$$k = \frac{L \times W}{H_m(L+W)} = \frac{20 \times 12}{3.15 \times (20+12)} = \frac{240}{3.15 \times 32} = \frac{240}{100.8} \approx 2.38$$

A $k$ of 2.38 is consistent with the assumed CU of 0.55 (verified against IES tables for general diffuse luminaire, $\rho_c = 0.7$, $\rho_w = 0.5$, $\rho_f = 0.2$).

**Step 4 (2 marks): Apply the design equation**
$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times CU \times MF} = \frac{750 \times 240}{5000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75}$$

Numerator:
$$750 \times 240 = 180{,}000 \text{ lm}$$

Denominator:
$$5000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75 = 2062.5 \text{ lm}$$

$$N = \frac{180{,}000}{2062.5} \approx 87.27$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Round up**
$$N = 88 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Layout suggestion**

Adopt a grid layout: **8 rows × 11 columns = 88 luminaires** is not ideal (uneven). A better arrangement is:
- **11 rows × 8 columns = 88 luminaires** ✓
- Row spacing: $20/11 \approx 1.82$ m
- Column spacing: $12/8 = 1.5$ m
- Both spacings are within the recommended **1.0 to 2.5 m** range for uniform office lighting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students often forget that $H_m$ is measured from the **working plane**, not the floor. Marks are lost for writing $H_m = 4$ m instead of $H_m = 3.15$ m. Also, the room index computation in Step 3 is **not** required for the final answer but is necessary to *verify* the correctness of the assumed CU. Examiners reward students who show this verification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the various components of light flux that contribute to the Coefficient of Utilisation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A classroom of size **10 m × 8 m × 3.2 m** (height) has white-painted ceiling, cream-coloured walls, and a grey floor. The CU for the chosen luminaire at room index 2 is **0.65** and at room index 2.5 is **0.68**. Working plane is at **0.8 m**. Each luminaire has a luminous flux of **3000 lm**, MF is **0.8**, and required illuminance is **400 lux**. Compute the number of luminaires required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

##### Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** The light emitted by a luminaire in an indoor space is distributed among several pathways. The fraction that reaches the working plane (where the visual task is performed) is what defines the Coefficient of Utilisation.

**Components of light flux (4 marks):**

1. **Direct component (φ_d):** Light that travels directly from the luminaire to the working plane without any reflection. This is the largest contributor to CU in direct-lighting installations.

2. **Ceiling-reflected component (φ_c):** Light that first strikes the ceiling, undergoes diffuse reflection, and then reaches the working plane. Its magnitude depends on ceiling reflectance $\rho_c$.

3. **Wall-reflected component (φ_w):** Light that strikes the walls, reflects one or more times, and reaches the working plane. This component is critically dependent on wall reflectance $\rho_w$ and the room's surface area ratio.

4. **Floor-reflected component (φ_f):** Light that first strikes the floor and then reflects up to the working plane. This is a small contributor in most installations.

5. **Lost component (φ_loss):** Light that is absorbed by the room surfaces or escapes through windows/openings. This does **not** contribute to CU.

**Block diagram representation (2 marks):** See Section 4.1 of these notes for the corresponding flow diagram.

Mathematical statement:

$$CU = \frac{\phi_d + \phi_c + \phi_w + \phi_f}{\phi_{\text{lamp}}} = \frac{\phi_{\text{utilised}}}{\phi_{\text{lamp}}}$$

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute area**
$$A = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Compute mounting height above working plane**
$$H_m = 3.2 - 0.8 = 2.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Compute room index**
$$k = \frac{L \times W}{H_m(L+W)} = \frac{10 \times 8}{2.4 \times (10+8)} = \frac{80}{2.4 \times 18} = \frac{80}{43.2} \approx 1.85$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Interpolate the CU**

Since $k = 1.85$ lies between 1.5 and 2.0, and the given values are at $k=2$ and $k=2.5$, we need to interpolate. However, the problem provides CU at $k=2$ (0.65) and $k=2.5$ (0.68). Using linear interpolation assuming the values at $k=1.5$ to $k=2$ follow the same trend:

For the range $k=2$ to $k=2.5$, the slope is $(0.68 - 0.65)/(2.5 - 2) = 0.03/0.5 = 0.06$ per unit $k$. Going down from $k=2$ to $k=1.85$:

$$CU \approx 0.65 - 0.06 \times 0.15 = 0.65 - 0.009 = 0.641$$

Rounded: $CU \approx 0.64$

**Step 5 (2 marks): Apply the design equation**
$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times CU \times MF} = \frac{400 \times 80}{3000 \times 0.64 \times 0.8}$$

Numerator:
$$400 \times 80 = 32{,}000 \text{ lm}$$

Denominator:
$$3000 \times 0.64 \times 0.8 = 1536 \text{ lm}$$

$$N = \frac{32{,}000}{1536} \approx 20.83$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Round up**
$$N = 21 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Suggested layout:** A **3 rows × 7 columns = 21 luminaires** grid.
- Row spacing: $10/3 \approx 3.33$ m (slightly high; might need closer spacing).
- A better layout: **4 rows × 5 + 1 extra** or **3 rows × 7 columns = 21**, which is acceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B**:
> 1. **Interpolation errors**: Many students assume a linear relationship and apply it incorrectly. Always interpolate between the two given $k$ values that bracket the computed room index.
> 2. **Rounding**: Students often round $N$ *down* to 20. This is incorrect — always round **up** to the next integer to maintain design illuminance.
> 3. **Unit confusion**: $\phi$ is in *lumens* (lm), not watts. Converting lumens to watts requires the luminous efficacy of the source (e.g., 100 lm/W for fluorescent) and is a separate step.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Coefficients of Utilisation**

- **CU Definition**: $CU = \phi_{\text{working plane}} / \phi_{\text{lamp}}$; dimensionless, typically **0.3 to 0.8**.
- **Master Equation**: $N = (E \times A) / (\phi \times CU \times MF)$ — must memorise and know each term.
- **Room Index formula**: $k = (L \times W) / [H_m \times (L + W)]$; valid range **0.6 ≤ k ≤ 5.0**.
- **Mounting height**: $H_m$ is from the *working plane*, NOT the floor. Subtract working plane height (0.85 m offices, 1.0 m industrial).
- **Standard reflectances**: Ceiling 0.7, Walls 0.5, Floor 0.2 (light room).
- **CU trends**: As **k ↑**, **CU ↑**. As **ρ_w ↑**, **CU ↑**. As **H_m ↑**, **CU ↓**.
- **Interreflection principle**: Walls and ceiling act as secondary sources; light-coloured interiors maximise CU.
- **Luminaire classification matters**: Direct, semi-direct, general-diffuse, semi-indirect, indirect — each has its own CU table.
- **IES tables**: Standardised look-up tables indexed by room index and reflectance triplet. KTU problems often give CU directly; otherwise interpolate.
- **Maintenance factor**: $MF = (\text{ILF} \times \text{LLF})$, typically **0.5 to 0.8** depending on cleaning schedule and lamp type.
- **Always round UP** when computing $N$; under-illumination is a compliance failure.
- **Working plane heights to remember**: Offices **0.85 m**, Industrial benches **1.0 m**, Classrooms **0.8 m**, Drawing halls **0.85–1.0 m**.
- **CU is a system property**, not a lamp property — the same lamp gives different CUs in different rooms.
- **Conversion safety**: $1 \text{ lux} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$. Always convert area to m² before applying equations.
- **Luminous efficacy** (lm/W) is NOT used directly in CU design but is needed to compute *electrical load* from $\phi$.
- **IES Standard Reflectances** to memorise for KTU exams: light (0.7/0.5/0.2), medium (0.5/0.3/0.1), dark (0.3/0.1/0.1) for (ceiling/walls/floor).
- **Time-saving trick**: In KTU problems, if CU is not given, compute $k$ first, then look up CU from a standard IES table (assumed provided or given directly in the question).
- **Final sanity check**: For a typical office, $N$ should be in the range of **20–100 luminaires**. If your answer is < 5 or > 200, re-verify your inputs.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip**: Memorise the master design equation $N = EA / (\phi \cdot CU \cdot MF)$ and the room index formula $k = LW / [H_m(L+W)]$. KTU 2024 scheme ESE almost always tests these two with a numerical problem worth 7 marks. Practice at least 5 problems of varying complexity (offices, factories, classrooms) before the exam.

---

*End of Module 3 — Coefficients of Utilisation & Factors Affecting It*
*Course: PEEET418 — Illumination Technology | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Illumination Required for Various Work Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Work Plane** is defined as the horizontal, vertical, or inclined plane at a height above the floor (or below it for ceiling-mounted task reference) at which the visual task is performed, and on which the illuminance (E) is to be measured and specified. In illumination engineering, the **Recommended Illumination Level** for a given work plane is the optimal value of illuminance (measured in **lux** or **lumens per square metre**) prescribed by national and international standards (such as **IS 3646**, **IESNA Lighting Handbook**, and **CIE S 008**) to ensure visual comfort, performance, safety, and energy efficiency for a particular task or activity.

The illuminance requirement $E_{req}$ is not a single universal value; it is a function of:

- Task characteristics (size, contrast, colour, reflectance)
- Visual performance requirements (speed, accuracy, duration)
- Worker characteristics (age, visual acuity)
- Environmental factors (glare, surrounding luminance, shadows)

Mathematically, the average illuminance on a work plane is:

$$ E_{avg} = \frac{\Phi_{total} \times \eta \times MF}{A} $$

where $A$ is the area of the work plane, $\eta$ is the utilization factor, and $MF$ is the maintenance factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 syllabus explicitly states that students must be able to **"Select appropriate illumination levels for various work planes"** and **"Apply standard codes (IS 3646 / IES) for design."** Numerical recall of standard lux values for at least 8–10 work planes is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a kitchen** preparing three different dishes at the same time:

1. A simple soup on the stove (you need **moderate light** to check the colour of the broth and avoid burning).
2. An intricate *latte art* design on a coffee (you need **very bright, focused light** to see the fine contrast of milk on espresso).
3. A general dining area where guests eat (you need **soft, ambient light** so people feel relaxed, not perform surgery).

The chef would never put the *same bulb* over all three stations. Similarly, an **electrical engineer** cannot specify one fixed lux value for every work plane in a building. A factory floor where workers are forging steel needs roughly **200–300 lux**, while a watchmaker's bench assembling tiny gears needs **1500–2000 lux** because the size of the visual detail is **100× smaller**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Think of illumination as **fuel for the eyes**. Just as a heavy truck needs more diesel than a scooter (because the load is heavier), the *visual load* (size of detail × contrast × speed) determines how much "light-fuel" the eye needs. Standards have done the hard work of measuring this "fuel" for thousands of tasks, and we simply look up the answer.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Illuminance | $E$ | Luminous flux incident per unit area | **lux (lx)** $\vert$ lm/m² |
| Luminance | $L$ | Luminous intensity per unit projected area | cd/m² |
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | Total light emitted by a source | **lumen (lm)** |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | Luminous flux per unit solid angle | **candela (cd)** |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | Lumen output per watt of input | **lm/W** |
| Glare Index | $GI$ | Numerical measure of discomfort glare | dimensionless |
| Colour Rendering Index | $CRI$ | Ability of a source to render colours faithfully | 0–100 (dimensionless) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall Note:** Illumination (E) is **incident** light on a surface. Luminance (L) is **reflected/light emitted** by a surface. They are NOT the same. Many students confuse them in numerical problems.
