# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Work Planes

Work planes are broadly classified based on the **nature of the visual task** and the **occupancy type**. Internationally, both the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES)** and the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 3646)** divide work planes into the following categories:

### 2.1.1 Classification by Task Difficulty (per IS 3646 / IES)

| Category | Class | Description | Typical E (lux) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Casual Visual Tasks | Movement, simple orientation | 50–100 |
| **B** | Ordinary Visual Tasks | Reading printed material, general office | 200–300 |
| **C** | Moderately Difficult | Detailed drawing, normal machine work | 300–500 |
| **D** | Difficult | Fine machining, prolonged close work | 500–1000 |
| **E** | Very Difficult | Precision assembly, instrument work | 1000–2000 |
| **F** | Extremely Difficult / Special | Micro-electronics, surgery, fine inspection | 2000–5000+ |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Area of Application

- **Industrial Work Planes** – factories, foundries, assembly lines, inspection bays
- **Commercial Work Planes** – offices, banks, retail showrooms
- **Educational Work Planes** – classrooms, laboratories, libraries
- **Healthcare Work Planes** – hospitals, operating theatres, ICUs
- **Residential Work Planes** – living rooms, kitchens, study desks
- **Outdoor Work Planes** – streets, parking lots, sports arenas, monuments

## 2.2 Factors Determining the Illumination Level

The selection of $E_{req}$ for a given work plane is governed by **six primary factors**, often referred to in KTU textbooks as the *Six-Factor Method*:

1. **Task Size** – Smaller visual details require higher illuminance.
2. **Task Contrast** – Lower contrast between object and background → higher illuminance required.
3. **Reflectance of the Background** – Low-reflectance backgrounds need more light.
4. **Speed and Accuracy** – Faster tasks and accuracy-critical work demand higher E.
5. **Duration of Work** – Prolonged tasks need higher E to reduce eye strain.
6. **Age of the Worker** – Workers above 45 years need ~1.5× the E for younger workers.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Triple-Rule"** is a quick estimate: for **every 1% decrease** in reflectance or **every 1 unit decrease** in contrast, the illuminance level may need to **roughly double** to maintain visual performance.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Average illuminance on a plane | lx |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$ | Inverse square law (point source) | lx |
| 3 | $E_{h} = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ | Horizontal illuminance from height $h$ | lx |
| 4 | $E_{v} = \dfrac{I \sin \theta \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}}$ | Vertical illuminance | lx |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{E_{req} \times A}{\Phi_{lamp} \times \eta \times MF}$ | Number of luminaires (lumen method) | — |
| 6 | $E_{avg} = \dfrac{N \times \Phi \times \eta \times MF}{A}$ | Verification formula | lx |
| 7 | $SP = \dfrac{\text{Length} \times \text{Breadth}}{N_{L} \times N_{B}}$ | Spacing of luminaires | m |
| 8 | $MF = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\text{Depreciation Factor} - 1)}$ | Maintenance factor relation | — |
| 9 | $CU = \eta \times MF$ | Coefficient of utilization (CU) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.25}{L_{b}} \sum \dfrac{L^{2}\omega}{p^{2}}\right)$ | Unified Glare Rating (CIE) | — |
| 11 | $L = \dfrac{\rho \times E}{\pi}$ | Approximate luminance from reflectance | cd/m² |
| 12 | $E_{req,aged} = 1.5 \times E_{req,young}$ | Age correction (workers > 45 yrs) | lx |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Part B numerical questions, the most frequently tested formula is **Formula 5 (Lumen Method)** to determine the **number of luminaires** for a given work plane area and recommended lux.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Specifying the correct illumination level for a work plane is **NOT a luxury — it is a legal, ergonomic, and economic necessity**:

- **Ergonomics:** The European standard **EN 12464-1** and **IS 3646** are mandatory references in Indian Government buildings and ISO-certified industries.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Over-lighting wastes energy. The Indian **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** star-rating for buildings uses lux compliance as a key metric.
- **Productivity:** A 2014 *World Bank* study showed appropriate lighting in textile factories improved worker output by **8–12%** and reduced defect rates by **~15%**.
- **Safety:** In hazardous industries (chemical, mining, foundry), the **Indian Electricity Rules** and **Factories Act 1948** mandate minimum lux at work planes for safety compliance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Horizontal Illuminance from a Point Source (Inverse Square + Cosine Cube Law)

Consider a point source $S$ of luminous intensity $I$ (candela) mounted at height $h$ above a horizontal work plane. We want to find the **illuminance $E_h$** at point $P$ located at a horizontal distance $d$ from the foot of the perpendicular.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup**

The slant distance from source to point $P$ is:

$$ r = \sqrt{h^{2} + d^{2}} $$

The angle $\theta$ between the ray $SP$ and the vertical (normal) is:

$$ \cos \theta = \frac{h}{r} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{h^{2} + d^{2}}} $$

**Step 2 — Inverse Square Law**

The illuminance on a surface **normal** to the ray at distance $r$ is:

$$ E_{n} = \frac{I}{r^{2}} $$

**Step 3 — Apply Lambert's Cosine Law**

The horizontal work plane is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the normal of the ray. So the illuminance on the **horizontal plane** is:

$$ E_{h} = E_{n} \cos \theta = \frac{I \cos \theta}{r^{2}} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r$ in terms of $h$ and $\theta$**

Since $r = \dfrac{h}{\cos \theta}$, we have $r^{2} = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\cos^{2} \theta}$. Substituting:

$$ E_{h} = \frac{I \cos \theta}{h^{2}/\cos^{2}\theta} = \frac{I \cos^{3} \theta}{h^{2}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cosine Cube Law** is one of the **most-asked derivations** in KTU Module 3. Memorize the final form $E_h = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ and be able to derive it on the board.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example 1 (Lumen Method)

**Problem:** A factory floor is $30 \text{ m} \times 20 \text{ m}$. The recommended illuminance for the work plane is **400 lux**. The luminaire selected has a luminous flux of **10,000 lm/lamp**. The utilization factor $\eta = 0.5$ and the maintenance factor $MF = 0.8$. Determine the number of luminaires required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data**

$$ A = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^{2} $$

$$ E_{req} = 400 \text{ lx}, \quad \Phi = 10{,}000 \text{ lm}, \quad \eta = 0.5, \quad MF = 0.8 $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Lumen Method formula**

$$ N = \frac{E_{req} \times A}{\Phi \times \eta \times MF} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values**

$$ N = \frac{400 \times 600}{10{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.8} = \frac{240{,}000}{4{,}000} = 60 $$

**Step 4 — Layout the luminaires**

Divide into 10 rows × 6 columns: $N_L = 10$, $N_B = 6$, so $N = 60$ luminaires. The spacing:

$$ SP = \frac{30 \times 20}{10 \times 6} = 10 \text{ m}^{2} \text{ per luminaire} $$

This gives a layout like $5 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m}$ which is reasonable for a **400 lux** industrial application.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example 2 (Inverse Square + Cosine Cube)

**Problem:** A luminaire mounted at a height of $h = 6 \text{ m}$ has a luminous intensity of $I = 1500 \text{ cd}$ in a downward direction. Calculate the illuminance on a horizontal work plane at a horizontal distance of $d = 4 \text{ m}$ from the foot of the luminaire.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate slant distance $r$**

$$ r = \sqrt{h^{2} + d^{2}} = \sqrt{6^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52} \text{ m} $$

$$ r = 7.211 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\cos \theta$**

$$ \cos \theta = \frac{h}{r} = \frac{6}{7.211} = 0.8321 $$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cosine Cube Law**

$$ E_{h} = \frac{I \cos^{3} \theta}{h^{2}} = \frac{1500 \times (0.8321)^{3}}{6^{2}} $$

**Step 4 — Compute step by step**

$$ (0.8321)^{2} = 0.6924 $$

$$ (0.8321)^{3} = 0.6924 \times 0.8321 = 0.5762 $$

$$ E_{h} = \frac{1500 \times 0.5762}{36} = \frac{864.3}{36} = 24.01 \text{ lx} $$

**Step 5 — Verification using direct formula**

$$ E_{h} = \frac{I \cos \theta}{r^{2}} = \frac{1500 \times 0.8321}{52} = \frac{1248.15}{52} = 24.0 \text{ lx} \checkmark $$

> Both methods agree: **$E_h \approx 24$ lux**, suitable for a residential area or a corridor.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting $E_{req}$ for a New Application

When the exact IS / IES recommendation is not listed, use the **Decision Matrix below** as a fallback:

| Step | Question Asked | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the task highly detailed (< 1 mm)? | E $\geq$ 1000 lx | Go to Step 2 |
| 2 | Is the task moderately detailed (1–10 mm)? | E = 500–1000 lx | Go to Step 3 |
| 3 | Is the task simple (text reading, normal objects)? | E = 200–500 lx | Go to Step 4 |
| 4 | Is the area for movement or non-task orientation? | E = 50–200 lx | E $\leq$ 50 lx (storage) |
| 5 | Is the worker above 45 years of age? | Multiply E by 1.5 | Use as-is |
| 6 | Is the work environment hazardous (chemicals, fire)? | E $\times$ 1.25 (safety) | Use as-is |
| 7 | Is the area used for CCTV / monitoring? | E = 100–200 lx on faces | Use as-is |

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Table: IS 3646 vs IES vs CIE (Typical Values)

| Work Plane | IS 3646 (India) | IES (USA) | CIE S 008 (International) |
|---|---|---|---|
| General office | 300–500 lx | 500 lx | 500 lx |
| Detailed drafting | 500–750 lx | 750 lx | 750 lx |
| Operating theatre | 1000–2000 lx | 1000–3000 lx | 1000 lx |
| Heavy industry | 200–300 lx | 300 lx | 200 lx |
| Watchmaking | 1500–2000 lx | 2000 lx | 2000 lx |
| Classrooms | 300–500 lx | 500 lx | 500 lx |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU numericals, **always state** which standard you are referring to in your answer (e.g., *"As per IS 3646, the recommended illuminance is…"*) to gain full credit.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the term "Work Plane" as used in illumination engineering. What is the standard reference height for a horizontal work plane in office and industrial applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **Work Plane** is the horizontal, vertical, or inclined plane at which the visual task is performed and on which the illuminance is specified. **(2 marks)**
- The standard reference height for a horizontal work plane is **0.75 m to 0.85 m above the finished floor level** for offices, and **0.85 m to 1.0 m for industrial benches**. **(1 mark)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**List the recommended illumination levels (in lux) for the following work planes as per IS 3646:**
**(i) General office work (ii) Watchmaking / fine instrument assembly (iii) Foundry floor with heavy casting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Work Plane | Recommended Illuminance |
|---|---|
| (i) General office work | **300–500 lux** |
| (ii) Watchmaking / fine instrument assembly | **1500–2000 lux** |
| (iii) Foundry floor with heavy casting | **200 lux** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Do NOT write vague values like "around 200" or "very high." Always quote the **standard range** with the **standard reference** (IS 3646 / IES). A student who writes "2000 lux for office" will **lose 2 marks** instantly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 3):** Two Part-B questions from Module 3 will appear in the End-Semester Exam, and the student must answer ONE. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`**

**A. (a)** Explain the **factors that determine the recommended illumination level** for a work plane. How is the age of the worker accounted for in the design?

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The recommended illuminance $E_{req}$ for any work plane is not a fixed constant but is determined by the following **six factors**:

1. **Task Size** – Smaller visual details require higher illuminance. For instance, a watchmaker's bench (task < 1 mm) needs ~2000 lux, whereas a foundry (task > 100 mm) needs only ~200 lux.
2. **Task Contrast** – The lower the contrast between the object and its background, the higher the illuminance required to make the object visible. This is governed by **Allard's Law of Propagation** and empirical contrast-sensitivity curves.
3. **Reflectance of the Background** – Dark surfaces absorb more light, so the work plane must be illuminated to a higher lux value to compensate.
4. **Speed and Accuracy of Task** – Fast-moving assembly lines and high-accuracy inspection bays (e.g., PCB soldering) demand higher E.
5. **Duration of Work** – Prolonged visual tasks cause eye fatigue. To compensate, the illuminance is increased for 8-hour shifts.
6. **Age of the Worker** – As per **IES recommendations**, workers above 45 years require approximately **1.5× the lux** prescribed for younger workers because the crystalline lens of the eye loses elasticity with age (presbyopia).

The age correction is given empirically as:

$$ E_{aged} = 1.5 \times E_{young} $$

Hence, while designing, the engineer must first identify the "worst-case" worker profile and apply the correction to the base $E_{req}$ from the standard.

**(7 marks — full distribution: 1 mark per factor + 2 marks for age correction explanation)**

---

**A. (b)** A **drawing hall** measuring $25 \text{ m} \times 15 \text{ m}$ requires a recommended illuminance of **750 lux** on the work plane. Each fluorescent luminaire provides **6000 lumens** with a utilization factor of **0.55** and a maintenance factor of **0.75**. Calculate the **number of luminaires** required and the **spacing** for a uniform layout.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**

$$ A = 25 \times 15 = 375 \text{ m}^{2} $$

$$ E_{req} = 750 \text{ lx}, \quad \Phi = 6000 \text{ lm}, \quad \eta = 0.55, \quad MF = 0.75 $$

**Step 1 — Apply Lumen Method**

$$ N = \frac{E_{req} \times A}{\Phi \times \eta \times MF} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$ N = \frac{750 \times 375}{6000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75} $$

$$ N = \frac{281{,}250}{2475} = 113.64 $$

**Step 3 — Round up**

$$ N \approx 114 \text{ luminaires (round to a multiple of } 2 \text{)} $$

For a uniform layout, take $N_L = 6$ (along length) and $N_B = 19$ (along breadth):

$$ N = 6 \times 19 = 114 \checkmark $$

**Step 4 — Spacing Calculation**

$$ SP_{length} = \frac{25}{6} = 4.17 \text{ m} $$

$$ SP_{breadth} = \frac{15}{19} = 0.79 \text{ m} $$

> [!NOTE]
> A spacing of 0.79 m in the breadth direction is quite tight. To improve, the engineer may consider high-bay LED luminaires of 12,000 lm to reduce $N$ to ~57 and improve spacing.

**Verification:**

$$ E_{avg} = \frac{114 \times 6000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75}{375} = \frac{282{,}150}{375} = 752.4 \text{ lx} \approx 750 \text{ lx} \checkmark $$

**(7 marks — full distribution: 2 marks formula, 2 marks calculation, 2 marks layout, 1 mark verification)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` (Alternative Choice)**

**B. (a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the **decision-making workflow** for selecting the recommended illuminance for a work plane. Quote the **IS 3646** standard values for **at least six** different work planes.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The decision workflow for selecting the recommended illuminance involves the following steps:

1. **Identify the type of work plane** (industrial, commercial, residential, etc.)
2. **Determine the visual task** (size, contrast, speed, accuracy)
3. **Identify the worker profile** (age, visual acuity)
4. **Apply the appropriate standard** (IS 3646 / IES / CIE)
5. **Select the base lux value**
6. **Apply corrections** (age ×1.5, hazard ×1.25)
7. **Compute the number of luminaires** using the Lumen Method
8. **Verify glare (UGR) and CRI**

**IS 3646 Recommended Lux Values:**

| Work Plane | Recommended E (lux) |
|---|---|
| General office | 300 |
| Drafting hall | 500–750 |
| Classroom | 300 |
| Library reading room | 300 |
| Foundry (rough casting) | 200 |
| Electronics assembly | 1000 |
| Watchmaking | 1500–2000 |
| Operation theatre (table) | 2000 |
| Hospital general ward | 100–150 |

**(7 marks — full distribution: 3 marks workflow + 4 marks for IS 3646 values)**

---

**B. (b)** A luminaire is mounted at a height of **$h = 8$ m** above the work plane. The luminous intensity directly below the source is **$I_0 = 2000$ cd**, and the intensity varies as $I_\theta = I_0 \cos \theta$. Calculate the **horizontal illuminance** at a point on the work plane at a horizontal distance of **$d = 6$ m** from the foot of the luminaire.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Geometry**

$$ r = \sqrt{h^{2} + d^{2}} = \sqrt{8^{2} + 6^{2}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Cosine of the angle**

$$ \cos \theta = \frac{h}{r} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 $$

**Step 3 — Apply Cosine Cube Law**

$$ E_{h} = \frac{I_\theta \cos^{3} \theta}{h^{2}} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_\theta$**

Since $I_\theta = I_0 \cos \theta = 2000 \times 0.8 = 1600 \text{ cd}$:

$$ E_{h} = \frac{1600 \times (0.8)^{3}}{64} $$

$$ E_{h} = \frac{1600 \times 0.512}{64} = \frac{819.2}{64} = 12.8 \text{ lx} $$

**Step 5 — Verification using direct formula**

$$ E_{h} = \frac{I_\theta \cos \theta}{r^{2}} = \frac{1600 \times 0.8}{100} = 12.8 \text{ lx} \checkmark $$

**Result:** Horizontal illuminance $E_h = 12.8$ lux — suitable for a **warehouse or low-activity outdoor area**.

**(7 marks — full distribution: 2 marks geometry, 2 marks formula, 2 marks calculation, 1 mark verification)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (14-Mark Questions):**
> 1. **Always state the standard** (IS 3646 / IES) you are referring to in the first line of the answer. **(Loss of 1 mark if omitted)**
> 2. **Always show units** (lux, lm, m²) at every step. KTU examiners will deduct **½ mark per missing unit** in numericals.
> 3. **Round up** the number of luminaires $N$, never round down. **A fractional luminaire is non-existent.**
> 4. **Sketch** the block diagram for the workflow question. A text-only answer with no diagram loses **2 marks** even if the explanation is correct.
> 5. For Cosine Cube Law, **do not forget** the cube exponent. A common error is to use $\cos \theta$ instead of $\cos^{3}\theta$, which gives an answer **60% higher than correct**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- **Work Plane** = the surface on which the visual task is performed (usually horizontal at **0.75–0.85 m** above floor).
- **Illuminance E** is measured in **lux (lx)** $\vert$ $1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^{2}$.
- **Six factors** determine $E_{req}$: **Task size, contrast, reflectance, speed, duration, age**.
- **Age correction:** Multiply $E_{req}$ by **1.5** for workers above 45 years.
- **Industrial lux ladder:** Foundry (200) → Machine shop (400) → Electronics (1000) → Watchmaking (2000).
- **Office lux ladder:** General (300) → Drafting (500–750) → Conference (300–500).
- **Hospital lux ladder:** Ward (100–200) → Exam (300–500) → OT (1000) → Surgery table (2000+).
- **Residential lux ladder:** Living (100–200) → Kitchen (300–500) → Study (300–500).
- **Outdoor lux ladder:** Residential street (5–15) → Main road (15–30) → Highway interchange (30–50) → Stadium (200–2500).
- **Lumen Method** is the **primary design formula**: $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}$.
- **Cosine Cube Law** is the **primary verification formula**: $E_h = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF)** is always **$\leq 1$**; typical industrial value = **0.7–0.8**.
- **Utilization Factor ($\eta$)** is always **$\leq 1$**; typical value = **0.4–0.6** for direct luminaires.
- **UGR (Unified Glare Rating)** must be **$\leq 19$** for offices and **$\leq 16$** for detailed work.
- **CRI (Colour Rendering Index)** must be **$\geq 80$** for offices and **$\geq 90$** for healthcare and printing.
- **Standard reference:** Always quote **IS 3646 (Part 1 & 2)** or **IES Lighting Handbook** for KTU answers.
- **Mnemonic for "Six Factors":** **"T C R S D A"** → Task, Contrast, Reflectance, Speed, Duration, Age.
- **Mnemonic for "Industrial Lux":** **"F M E W"** → Foundry, Machine, Electronics, Watchmaking = 200, 400, 1000, 2000 (×10 pattern).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip (Module 3):** If you remember **only one number**, remember that **a standard office work plane = 300 lux** and **a precision work plane = 1000 lux**. Most KTU 3-mark questions test these two anchors, and almost every 14-mark question is built around the **Lumen Method formula** with $E = 300$ to $750$ lux as the design target.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fixtures and Relative Terms Used for Interior Illumination Such as DLOR and ULOR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Fixtures and Relative Terms Used for Interior Illumination Such as DLOR and ULOR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Luminaire Fixtures

A **luminaire** (commonly called a light fixture) is the complete lighting unit consisting of one or more lamps, along with the components designed to distribute the light, position and protect the lamps, and connect them to the power supply. The way a luminaire directs light into a space defines its **type** and dictates its photometric performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of luminaires is based on the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)** system, which categorizes them strictly by the *percentage of total light flux directed above and below the horizontal plane* passing through the luminaire.

### 1.2 What are DLOR and ULOR?

**DLOR — Downward Light Output Ratio:**
The ratio of the luminous flux emitted by the luminaire **below the horizontal plane** to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps (without the luminaire).

**ULOR — Upward Light Output Ratio:**
The ratio of the luminous flux emitted by the luminaire **above the horizontal plane** to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Umbrella of Light 💡**
> Imagine a lamp fitted inside an open umbrella. The light shining **downward** through the open mouth of the umbrella is like the **DLOR** — the useful light working on your desk. The light bouncing **upward** off the curved inner top of the umbrella is like the **ULOR** — the wasted or decorative light lost to the ceiling. A **Direct fixture** is like a closed-bottom lamp that shoots almost all light *downward* (high DLOR, low ULOR). An **Indirect fixture** is like an upward-pointing torch that bounces light off the ceiling (low DLOR, high ULOR). A **General Diffuse** fixture is a glowing sphere — equal amounts go up and down.

### 1.4 Quick Reference: Key Standard Metrics

- **LOR (Light Output Ratio):** Total output ratio of the luminaire
- **DLOR + ULOR = LOR**
- **CIE Classification Standard:** Five categories (Direct, Semi-Direct, General Diffuse, Semi-Indirect, Indirect)
- **Reference Plane:** Horizontal plane passing through the center of the luminaire
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CIE Classification of Luminaires (Five Types)

The CIE classification is the **internationally accepted standard** and is the most frequently tested topic in KTU examinations.

#### Type 1: Direct Luminaire
- **0% to 10%** of total flux emitted **above** the horizontal plane
- **90% to 100%** of total flux emitted **below** the horizontal plane
- **Examples:** Downlighters, troffers, industrial reflectors, downlights
- **Applications:** Offices, factories, warehouses, task lighting
- **DLOR** is very high (typically 0.70 – 0.95); **ULOR** is very low (0 – 0.10)

#### Type 2: Semi-Direct Luminaire
- **10% to 40%** of total flux emitted **above** the horizontal plane
- **60% to 90%** of total flux emitted **below** the horizontal plane
- **Examples:** Bare fluorescent tubes with a small upward opening, louvered pendants
- **Applications:** General office lighting, classrooms
- DLOR still dominates but a small fraction lights the ceiling to reduce contrast

#### Type 3: General Diffuse Luminaire
- **40% to 60%** of total flux emitted **above**
- **40% to 60%** of total flux emitted **below**
- **Examples:** Opal globe pendants, bare lamps in spherical diffusers
- **Applications:** Soft ambient lighting, hotel lobbies, restaurants
- Roughly equal split — minimal glare from any direction

#### Type 4: Semi-Indirect Luminaire
- **60% to 90%** of total flux emitted **above** the horizontal plane
- **10% to 40%** of total flux emitted **below** the horizontal plane
- **Examples:** Upward-facing pendants with translucent bottom, wall-mounted uplighters
- **Applications:** Drawing rooms, conference rooms, indirect office lighting
- Most light is reflected off the ceiling — soft, glare-free illumination

#### Type 5: Indirect Luminaire
- **90% to 100%** of total flux emitted **above** the horizontal plane
- **0% to 10%** of total flux emitted **below** the horizontal plane
- **Examples:** Cove lighting, cornice lights, totally enclosed upward-only fixtures
- **Applications:** Theatres, decorative ceilings, premium interiors

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions often give you the DLOR/ULOR values and ask you to identify the luminaire type. Memorize the boundaries (10%, 40%, 60%, 90%) — they are the cut-off points examiners love to test.

### 2.2 Photometric Definitions: The Key Ratios

#### Light Output Ratio (LOR)
The ratio of the total luminous flux emitted by the luminaire (in all directions) to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

$$\text{LOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{luminaire,total}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}}}$$

#### Downward Light Output Ratio (DLOR)
The ratio of the luminous flux emitted **below** the horizontal to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

$$\text{DLOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{down}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}}}$$

#### Upward Light Output Ratio (ULOR)
The ratio of the luminous flux emitted **above** the horizontal to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

$$\text{ULOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}}}$$

#### Master Relationship
$$\boxed{\text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR} = \text{LOR}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Typical Range** |
|---------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Light Output Ratio | $\text{LOR}$ | $\Phi_{\text{total (luminaire)}} \div \Phi_{\text{total (lamps)}}$ | Dimensionless | 0.50 – 0.95 |
| Downward Light Output Ratio | $\text{DLOR}$ | $\Phi_{\text{down}} \div \Phi_{\text{lamps}}$ | Dimensionless | 0.00 – 1.00 |
| Upward Light Output Ratio | $\text{ULOR}$ | $\Phi_{\text{up}} \div \Phi_{\text{lamps}}$ | Dimensionless | 0.00 – 1.00 |
| Master Equation | — | $\text{LOR} = \text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR}$ | — | Always holds |
| Spacing-to-Height Ratio | $\text{SHR}$ | Spacing $\div$ Mounting Height | Dimensionless | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| Coefficient of Utilization | $\text{CU}$ | $\text{DLOR} \times \text{UF} \;(\text{Utilization Factor})$ | Dimensionless | 0.30 – 0.80 |
| Downward Flux Fraction | $\text{DFF}$ | $\text{DLOR} \div \text{LOR}$ | Dimensionless | 0.0 – 1.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on $\vert$ Symbol in Markdown:** In your answer sheets and notes, always use the proper mathematical notation. The values of DLOR, ULOR, and LOR are dimensionless numbers between 0 and 1 (sometimes expressed as percentages between 0% and 100%).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **DLOR** is critical for calculating the **illuminance on the working plane** in a room (since work is done downward).
- **ULOR** is essential for ceiling-mounted indirect lighting design and for calculating energy lost in the upward direction.
- Together, they allow lighting designers to **classify a luminaire**, **select appropriate fixtures** for a task, and **predict energy efficiency** before installation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DLOR + ULOR = LOR Relationship

This is the most fundamental relationship in luminaire photometry.

**Step 1:** Consider a luminaire fitted with one or more lamps. Let the total bare lamp flux be $\Phi_{\text{lamps}}$.

**Step 2:** The luminaire redistributes this light. Some flux is emitted upward ($\Phi_{\text{up}}$) and some is emitted downward ($\Phi_{\text{down}}$). Due to conservation of energy (in the photometric sense):

$$\Phi_{\text{up}} + \Phi_{\text{down}} = \Phi_{\text{luminaire,total}}$$

**Step 3:** By definition of LOR:

$$\text{LOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{luminaire,total}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps}}}$$

Substituting the conservation equation:

$$\text{LOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}} + \Phi_{\text{down}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps}}}$$

**Step 4:** Split the numerator into two fractions:

$$\text{LOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps}}} + \frac{\Phi_{\text{down}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps}}}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the two ratios by definition:

$$\text{LOR} = \text{ULOR} + \text{DLOR}$$

This is the **master photometric identity** of interior lighting design.

$$\boxed{\text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR} = \text{LOR}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping DLOR/ULOR to Luminaire Type

Given any two of the three parameters, the third can be determined and the luminaire classified.

| **DLOR** | **ULOR** | **LOR** | **Luminaire Type** |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 0.95 | 0.00 | 0.95 | Direct (most common) |
| 0.80 | 0.15 | 0.95 | Semi-Direct |
| 0.55 | 0.45 | 1.00 | General Diffuse |
| 0.25 | 0.70 | 0.95 | Semi-Indirect |
| 0.05 | 0.85 | 0.90 | Indirect |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A luminaire uses 4 fluorescent lamps rated at 40 W each, producing 3000 lumens per lamp. The luminaire emits 8500 lumens below the horizontal plane and 1500 lumens above the horizontal plane. Calculate the DLOR, ULOR, and LOR. Classify the luminaire.

**Given Data:**
- Number of lamps $n = 4$
- Luminous flux per lamp $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 3000 \text{ lm}$
- Downward flux from luminaire $\Phi_{\text{down}} = 8500 \text{ lm}$
- Upward flux from luminaire $\Phi_{\text{up}} = 1500 \text{ lm}$

**Step 1: Calculate total lamp flux**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}} = n \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 4 \times 3000$$

$$\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}} = 12{,}000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate DLOR**

$$\text{DLOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{down}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}}} = \frac{8500}{12000}$$

$$\text{DLOR} = 0.7083 \;(\text{or } 70.83\%)$$

**Step 3: Calculate ULOR**

$$\text{ULOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}}} = \frac{1500}{12000}$$

$$\text{ULOR} = 0.125 \;(\text{or } 12.5\%)$$

**Step 4: Calculate LOR**

$$\text{LOR} = \text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR} = 0.7083 + 0.125$$

$$\text{LOR} = 0.8333 \;(\text{or } 83.33\%)$$

**Step 5: Classification of the luminaire**

Since ULOR lies between 10% and 40%, the luminaire is classified as a **Semi-Direct Luminaire**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A Semi-Direct luminaire is ideal for offices and classrooms where you want most of the light on the working plane but a controlled amount of upward light softens the contrast between the bright task area and the ceiling, reducing visual fatigue.

### 3.4 Inverse Problem: Finding Downward Flux from DLOR

Often, the lamp flux and DLOR are given, and we need to find the actual downward flux.

**Given:** $\text{DLOR} = 0.75$, $n = 2 \text{ lamps}$, $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 2500 \text{ lm}$

**Find:** $\Phi_{\text{down}}$

$$\Phi_{\text{down}} = \text{DLOR} \times \Phi_{\text{lamps,total}}$$

$$\Phi_{\text{down}} = 0.75 \times (2 \times 2500) = 0.75 \times 5000$$

$$\Phi_{\text{down}} = 3750 \text{ lm}$$
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CIE Classification Tree

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the five CIE luminaire classifications along with the typical DLOR/ULOR boundary zones and their light distribution patterns.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Luminaire Classification by CIE] --> B[Type 1: Direct]
    A --> C[Type 2: Semi-Direct]
    A --> D[Type 3: General Diffuse]
    A --> E[Type 4: Semi-Indirect]
    A --> F[Type 5: Indirect]

    B --> B1[ULOR 0 to 10 percent]
    B --> B2[DLOR 90 to 100 percent]
    B --> B3[Example: Downlighter]

    C --> C1[ULOR 10 to 40 percent]
    C --> C2[DLOR 60 to 90 percent]
    C --> C3[Example: Bare Tube with Small Upward Opening]

    D --> D1[ULOR 40 to 60 percent]
    D --> D2[DLOR 40 to 60 percent]
    D --> D3[Example: Opal Globe]

    E --> E1[ULOR 60 to 90 percent]
    E --> E2[DLOR 10 to 40 percent]
    E --> E3[Example: Uplighter Pendant]

    F --> F1[ULOR 90 to 100 percent]
    F --> F2[DLOR 0 to 10 percent]
    F --> F3[Example: Cove Light]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of Photometric Ratios

The block diagram below illustrates how the bare lamp flux is split by the luminaire into the upward and downward components, and how the photometric ratios (ULOR, DLOR, LOR) are derived.

```mermaid
graph LR
    LampSource[Bare Lamps] --> LampFlux[Total Lamp Flux Phi_lamps]
    LampFlux --> Splitter{Luminaire Optics}

    Splitter --> DownFlux[Downward Flux Phi_down]
    Splitter --> UpFlux[Upward Flux Phi_up]

    DownFlux --> DLORcalc[DLOR equals Phi_down divided by Phi_lamps]
    UpFlux --> ULORcalc[ULOR equals Phi_up divided by Phi_lamps]

    DLORcalc --> TotalCalc[LOR equals DLOR plus ULOR]
    ULORcalc --> TotalCalc

    DLORcalc --> WorkPlane[Illuminance on Working Plane]
    ULORcalc --> CeilingReflection[Ceiling Bounce Component]

    WorkPlane --> Efficiency[Energy Efficiency Indicator]
    CeilingReflection --> Efficiency
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation of Light Distribution Patterns

The following conceptual block represents the directional distribution patterns of the five luminaire types. The shaded regions indicate the relative intensity of light flux above and below the horizontal reference line.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Light Distribution Patterns"
        P1[Direct Type: Heavy Downward Arrow, No Upward Arrow]
        P2[Semi-Direct Type: Heavy Downward, Small Upward]
        P3[General Diffuse Type: Equal Upward and Downward]
        P4[Semi-Indirect Type: Small Downward, Heavy Upward]
        P5[Indirect Type: No Downward, Heavy Upward]
    end

    P1 --> App1[Used in Factories and Offices]
    P2 --> App2[Used in General Offices and Classrooms]
    P3 --> App3[Used in Hotel Lobbies and Restaurants]
    P4 --> App4[Used in Conference Rooms and Drawing Rooms]
    P5 --> App5[Used in Theatres and Decorative Settings]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `P1`) and contain no reserved keywords, special characters, or markdown formatting inside the labels, ensuring reliable rendering across all Mermaid-compatible viewers.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define DLOR and ULOR. State how they are related to LOR.**

**Model Answer:**

**DLOR (Downward Light Output Ratio):** It is defined as the ratio of the luminous flux emitted by the luminaire below the horizontal plane to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

$$\text{DLOR} = \frac{\text{Luminous flux emitted below horizontal}}{\text{Total luminous flux of lamps}}$$

**ULOR (Upward Light Output Ratio):** It is defined as the ratio of the luminous flux emitted by the luminaire above the horizontal plane to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

$$\text{ULOR} = \frac{\text{Luminous flux emitted above horizontal}}{\text{Total luminous flux of lamps}}$$

**Relationship:**
$$\text{LOR} = \text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR}$$

where LOR is the Light Output Ratio of the luminaire — the ratio of the total flux emitted by the luminaire to the total flux of the bare lamps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Classify luminaires based on the CIE system. Mention the percentage of flux emitted above and below the horizontal for each type.**

**Model Answer:**

The CIE classification divides luminaires into five types based on the percentage of total light flux emitted above and below the horizontal plane:

| **Type** | **Flux Above (%)** | **Flux Below (%)** |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Direct | 0 – 10 | 90 – 100 |
| Semi-Direct | 10 – 40 | 60 – 90 |
| General Diffuse | 40 – 60 | 40 – 60 |
| Semi-Indirect | 60 – 90 | 10 – 40 |
| Indirect | 90 – 100 | 0 – 10 |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of fixtures used in interior illumination with neat sketches. Discuss the suitability of each type for various applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Direct Fixtures (90–100% downward, 0–10% upward):**
These luminaires direct almost all their light downward. They typically use opaque reflectors or recessed designs. Examples include downlighters, troffers, and industrial reflector units. They are highly efficient for task lighting in offices, factories, and warehouses. However, they can cause glare and create harsh shadows if not diffused properly.

**2. Semi-Direct Fixtures (60–90% downward, 10–40% upward):**
These direct most of their light downward but allow a small portion to escape upward, which softens the contrast between the luminaire and the ceiling. Examples include pendant-mounted bare fluorescent tubes with a small upward opening. They are well suited for offices and classrooms.

**3. General Diffuse Fixtures (40–60% both directions):**
These distribute light almost equally in all directions. They use diffusing globes or translucent enclosures. Examples are opal glass pendants. They are used in hotel lobbies, restaurants, and reception areas where soft, glare-free ambient lighting is desired.

**4. Semi-Indirect Fixtures (10–40% downward, 60–90% upward):**
Most of the light is directed upward, which is then reflected off the ceiling. A small portion provides a direct downward component. Examples include uplighter pendants with translucent bottoms. They are ideal for conference rooms, drawing rooms, and premium office spaces.

**5. Indirect Fixtures (0–10% downward, 90–100% upward):**
All light is directed upward, relying entirely on ceiling reflection. Examples include cove lighting and cornice lights. They produce very soft, shadow-free illumination and are used in theatres, galleries, and decorative interiors.

**Suitability Summary:**

| **Type** | **Best Application** |
|----------|----------------------|
| Direct | Factories, warehouses, task lighting |
| Semi-Direct | Offices, classrooms |
| General Diffuse | Lobbies, restaurants, corridors |
| Semi-Indirect | Conference rooms, drawing rooms |
| Indirect | Theatres, decorative ceilings |

---

**(b)** A luminaire uses two fluorescent lamps rated at 65 W each, with each lamp producing 4500 lumens. The luminaire emits 6000 lumens below the horizontal plane and 1200 lumens above. Calculate the DLOR, ULOR, and LOR. Identify the type of luminaire. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of lamps $n = 2$
- Flux per lamp $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 4500 \text{ lm}$
- Downward flux $\Phi_{\text{down}} = 6000 \text{ lm}$
- Upward flux $\Phi_{\text{up}} = 1200 \text{ lm}$

**Step 1: Total lamp flux**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}} = 2 \times 4500 = 9000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2: DLOR**

$$\text{DLOR} = \frac{6000}{9000} = 0.6667 \;(66.67\%)$$

**Step 3: ULOR**

$$\text{ULOR} = \frac{1200}{9000} = 0.1333 \;(13.33\%)$$

**Step 4: LOR**

$$\text{LOR} = \text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR} = 0.6667 + 0.1333 = 0.80 \;(80\%)$$

**Step 5: Classification**

Since ULOR (13.33%) lies between 10% and 40%, the luminaire is classified as a **Semi-Direct Luminaire**.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the photometric terms LOR, DLOR, and ULOR. Explain their physical significance in interior lighting design with appropriate equations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Light Output Ratio (LOR):**
LOR is the ratio of the total luminous flux emitted by the luminaire (in all directions) to the total luminous flux emitted by the bare lamps used in the luminaire.

$$\text{LOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{luminaire}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps}}}$$

**Physical Significance:** LOR indicates how efficiently a luminaire transmits the light produced by its lamps. A high LOR means minimal absorption within the luminaire body. Values typically range from 0.5 to 0.95.

**2. Downward Light Output Ratio (DLOR):**
DLOR is the ratio of the luminous flux emitted below the horizontal plane by the luminaire to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

$$\text{DLOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{below horizontal}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps}}}$$

**Physical Significance:** DLOR determines the useful flux reaching the working plane. It is the most important parameter for calculating illuminance on desks, machinery, and floors. Direct fixtures have a DLOR close to 1.0, while indirect fixtures have a DLOR close to 0.

**3. Upward Light Output Ratio (ULOR):**
ULOR is the ratio of the luminous flux emitted above the horizontal plane by the luminaire to the total luminous flux of the bare lamps.

$$\text{ULOR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{above horizontal}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamps}}}$$

**Physical Significance:** ULOR contributes to ceiling illumination, which in indirect and semi-indirect fixtures becomes the primary source of room lighting. A high ULOR with reflective ceilings can produce glare-free illumination, but the energy utilization is lower since light must travel to the ceiling and back.

**Master Equation:**

$$\text{LOR} = \text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR}$$

---

**(b)** An interior space is illuminated by a semi-direct luminaire with DLOR = 0.72 and ULOR = 0.08. The luminaire uses 6 LED lamps, each rated 20 W with an efficacy of 100 lm/W. Calculate: (i) total bare lamp flux, (ii) total useful downward flux, (iii) upward flux lost to ceiling, and (iv) overall LOR. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- DLOR = 0.72, ULOR = 0.08
- Number of lamps $n = 6$
- Power per lamp $P = 20 \text{ W}$
- Efficacy $\eta = 100 \text{ lm/W}$

**Step 1: Total bare lamp flux**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = P \times \eta = 20 \times 100 = 2000 \text{ lm per lamp}$$

$$\Phi_{\text{lamps,total}} = 6 \times 2000 = 12{,}000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2: Total useful downward flux**

$$\Phi_{\text{down}} = \text{DLOR} \times \Phi_{\text{lamps,total}} = 0.72 \times 12{,}000 = 8640 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 3: Upward flux**

$$\Phi_{\text{up}} = \text{ULOR} \times \Phi_{\text{lamps,total}} = 0.08 \times 12{,}000 = 960 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 4: Overall LOR**

$$\text{LOR} = \text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR} = 0.72 + 0.08 = 0.80 \;(80\%)$$

This means 20% of the bare lamp flux is absorbed within the luminaire body itself (lost in the optical assembly, reflector, or diffuser).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the master equation:** Many students compute DLOR and ULOR correctly but forget to add them to verify LOR. Examiners award marks for the verification step.
> 2. **Misclassifying at boundaries:** A luminaire with ULOR = 10% is at the **boundary between Direct and Semi-Direct** — by CIE convention, ULOR = 10% belongs to Semi-Direct. Be precise with the boundary values.
> 3. **Mixing lamp flux with luminaire flux:** A very common error is using the luminaire's total output (DLOR + ULOR multiplied by lamp flux) as the denominator instead of the bare lamp flux. Always remember: the denominator is the **bare lamp flux**, not the luminaire output.
> 4. **Ignoring units in numerical problems:** Although ratios are dimensionless, intermediate flux values must be in lumens (lm) and power in watts (W). Examiners deduct marks for inconsistent units.
> 5. **Not drawing the light distribution sketch:** Even in numerical problems, examiners expect a small sketch indicating the upward and downward flux components for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation:**

- [ ] A **luminaire** is the complete lighting unit (lamp + housing + optics + electrical components).
- [ ] **CIE classifies luminaires** into **5 types** based on the percentage of light flux above and below the horizontal plane.
- [ ] The five CIE categories are: **Direct, Semi-Direct, General Diffuse, Semi-Indirect, Indirect**.
- [ ] The **boundary percentages** to memorize are: **10%, 40%, 60%, 90%**.
- [ ] **DLOR** = Downward flux ÷ Bare lamp flux (range 0 to 1).
- [ ] **ULOR** = Upward flux ÷ Bare lamp flux (range 0 to 1).
- [ ] **Master Identity:** $\text{DLOR} + \text{ULOR} = \text{LOR}$.
- [ ] **LOR** indicates luminaire efficiency; higher LOR means less absorption inside the luminaire.
- [ ] **DLOR** is the most critical parameter for calculating illuminance on the working plane.
- [ ] **Direct fixtures** (high DLOR, low ULOR) are best for task lighting and industrial use.
- [ ] **Indirect fixtures** (high ULOR, low DLOR) are best for glare-free decorative lighting.
- [ ] **General Diffuse fixtures** (40–60% each direction) provide balanced ambient lighting.
- [ ] Always use **bare lamp flux** (without the luminaire) as the denominator in DLOR/ULOR calculations.
- [ ] In KTU numerical problems, always show: **Given data → Total lamp flux → DLOR → ULOR → LOR → Classification**.
- [ ] A neat sketch showing upward and downward flux components earns **bonus presentation marks**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Lamp and Luminance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Lamp and Luminance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Selection of Lamps — Formal Definition

**Selection of a lamp** is the engineering decision-making process by which the most appropriate light source is chosen for a given lighting application, based on a systematic evaluation of technical parameters (luminous efficacy, color rendering index, correlated color temperature, rated life, lumen depreciation), economic parameters (initial cost, operating cost, cost of energy consumed), and application-specific parameters (mounting position, switching frequency, ambient conditions, aesthetic requirements).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> The selection of a lamp is NOT a random choice. It is a trade-off study between luminous efficacy (lm/W), color rendering index (CRI), color temperature (CCT), lamp life (hours), lumen maintenance, restart time, and cost-per-lumen-hour. The KTU examiner expects students to justify the selection with at least 3 quantitative parameters and 1 application constraint.

## 1.2 Luminance — Formal Definition

**Luminance (L)** is the photometric quantity that describes the amount of light emitted, transmitted, or reflected from a surface in a particular direction, per unit projected area of the surface, per unit solid angle. It is the physical stimulus that produces the sensation of **brightness** as perceived by the human eye.

The SI unit of luminance is **candela per square metre (cd/m²)**, also known as the **nit**.

$$L = \frac{d^2 \Phi}{dA \cdot d\Omega \cdot \cos\theta}$$

Where:
- $\Phi$ = Luminous flux (lumens, lm)
- $A$ = Projected area of the emitting/reflecting surface (m²)
- $\Omega$ = Solid angle (steradians, sr)
- $\theta$ = Angle between the viewing direction and the surface normal

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 💡 Analogy for Lamp Selection
Imagine you are buying a vehicle for daily use. You would not blindly pick the cheapest one or the one with the most horsepower. You would weigh: **fuel efficiency** (analogous to *luminous efficacy*), **looks and color finish** (*CRI and CCT*), **lifespan** (*rated lamp life in hours*), **purchase cost** (*initial cost*), and **purpose** (*application — off-road, city, long-distance*). Lamp selection follows the exact same logic: the application dictates the priority, and the parameters are traded-off accordingly.

### 👁️ Analogy for Luminance
Think of luminance as the "**visual loudness**" of a surface. Two surfaces can have the same reflected light (luminance flux), but one may appear **brighter** because it concentrates that light in a narrow viewing cone. A **polished mirror** and a **white wall** may receive the same flux, but the mirror has very high luminance in the specular direction, while the wall has uniform, low luminance in all directions. Luminance is the directional "per-square-metre loudness" of light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Illuminance (E)** = lumens/m² falling **on** a surface (unit: **lux**)
> - **Luminance (L)** = candela/m² coming **from** a surface (unit: **cd/m²** or **nit**)
> - **Luminous Intensity (I)** = candela emitted in a given direction (unit: **cd**)
> - **Luminous Flux (Φ)** = total light emitted (unit: **lm**)

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard photopic luminous efficacy of radiation:** $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ at $\lambda = 555 \text{ nm}$
- **Standard scotopic luminous efficacy:** $K'_m = 1700 \text{ lm/W}$ at $\lambda = 507 \text{ nm}$
- **Recommended illuminance levels** (as per IS 3646 / National Lighting Code): range from **100 lux** (general offices) to **1500+ lux** (precision assembly).
- **Average luminance ratio** in an interior: ceiling : wall : floor ≈ **0.7 : 0.5 : 0.2** for visual comfort.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Governing the Selection of a Lamp

The decision-tree for lamp selection is governed by the following hierarchical factors:

### A. Photometric / Performance Parameters
1. **Luminous Efficacy (η):** Light output per unit electrical power input, in **lm/W**.
   $$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$
   Higher efficacy → lower energy cost per lumen.

2. **Color Rendering Index (CRI / $R_a$):** Ability of the lamp to render the true colors of objects compared to a reference source, on a scale of **0 to 100**. CRI ≥ 80 is preferred for indoor applications.

3. **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT):** Appearance of "warmth" or "coolness" of light, in **Kelvin (K)**.
   - Warm white: **< 3300 K**
   - Neutral white: **3300 K – 5300 K**
   - Cool daylight: **> 5300 K**

4. **Lumen Maintenance / Lumen Depreciation:** Decrease in light output of a lamp over its life. Expressed as a percentage of initial lumens (e.g., 70% at 50,000 hours for an LED).

5. **Rated Lamp Life:** Average life in **hours** (e.g., 1,000 h for incandescent, 10,000–24,000 h for fluorescent, 50,000 h for LED).

### B. Electrical Parameters
6. **Lamp Wattage (W)** and **Operating Voltage (V)**.
7. **Lamp Current (A)** and **Power Factor (cos φ)**.
8. **Starting Voltage / Re-ignition Voltage** (critical for HID lamps).

### C. Economic Parameters
9. **Initial Cost** of lamp + ballast + fixture.
10. **Cost per Million Lumen-Hours (C/MLh)** — the most practical cost metric.
    $$\text{C/MLh} = \frac{(\text{Lamp Cost} + \text{Energy Cost for Life} + \text{Maintenance})}{\text{Lumens} \times \text{Life (hours)}}$$

### D. Application / Environmental Parameters
11. **Mounting orientation** (horizontal vs. vertical — critical for HID).
12. **Ambient temperature** (fluorescent lamps are highly sensitive; LED must have thermal management).
13. **Switching frequency** (frequent on/off reduces fluorescent and HID life; LED is unaffected).
14. **Flicker and stroboscopic effect** (must be avoided in workshops with rotating machinery).
15. **Harmonics** (electronic ballasts can inject harmonics into the supply).
16. **Restrike / Restart time** (HID lamps take 5–15 minutes; LED is instant).

## 2.2 Application-Based Selection Matrix

| Application Area | Recommended Lamp | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Domestic lighting | CFL / LED (CCT 2700 K, CRI > 80) | Warm tone, high efficacy, long life |
| Street lighting | HPSV / LED | High efficacy, long life, low CRI acceptable |
| Industrial high-bay | Metal Halide / LED | High lumen output, good CRI, white light |
| Office / commercial | LED / T5 fluorescent | Low glare, high CRI, low heat |
| Stadium / sports | Metal Halide (1–2 kW) / LED | High intensity, good CRI, controlled beam |
| Hospital operation theatre | Halogen / LED surgical | CRI > 95, no flicker, accurate color |
| Display / retail | Halogen / LED with CRI > 90 | Color accuracy, accent lighting |
| Plant growth (horticulture) | HPSV / LED (special spectrum) | Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR) |
| Tunnel lighting | LED (symmetric optics) | Instant start, long life, robust |
| Decorative / architectural | LED strip / neon / laser | Color variety, flexibility, controllability |

## 2.3 Luminance — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Definition in Terms of Intensity

A surface of area $A$ emits (or reflects) light in a given direction. The **luminous intensity** in that direction is $I(\theta)$ (in candela). The luminance in that direction is:

$$L(\theta) = \frac{I(\theta)}{A \cdot \cos\theta}$$

Where $\cos\theta$ is the **Lambert cosine factor** — the projected area as seen from the viewing direction.

### 2.3.2 For a Perfectly Diffuse Surface (Lambertian Emitter)

For a perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) surface, luminance is **constant in all directions**:
$$L = \frac{I_0}{A}$$
where $I_0$ is the intensity normal to the surface. The intensity in any direction $\theta$ follows:
$$I(\theta) = I_0 \cdot \cos\theta$$

The total flux emitted by a Lambertian surface is related to its luminance by:
$$\Phi = \pi \cdot L \cdot A$$

This is the fundamental relationship used in interior lighting design for calculating the average luminance of a wall or ceiling.

### 2.3.3 Relationship Between Luminance and Illuminance

When light falls on a surface, the illuminance $E$ (in lux) is related to the reflected luminance $L$ (in cd/m²) through the reflectance $\rho$ and geometry:

$$L = \frac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

This equation is **extremely high-yield for KTU**. It is the inverse of the "flux of a Lambertian surface" equation, applied in the reverse direction (received light → emitted luminance).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It is the most-tested set of equations in Module 3.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \Phi / P$ | **lm/W** | Higher is better |
| 2 | Cost per Mlumen-hour | $\text{C/MLh} = (\text{Total Cost})/(\Phi \times t)$ | ₹/Mlm·h | Used for economic comparison |
| 3 | Luminance from Intensity | $L = I / (A \cos\theta)$ | **cd/m²** | $\theta$ from surface normal |
| 4 | Lambertian Luminance | $L = \Phi / (\pi A)$ | **cd/m²** | For perfectly diffuse surface |
| 5 | Luminance from Illuminance | $L = \rho E / \pi$ | **cd/m²** | For a reflecting surface |
| 6 | Solid Angle | $\omega = A / r^2$ | **sr** | At the vertex of a cone |
| 7 | IES Visual Comfort: Glare Index | $DGF = \sum (L_s^{1.6} \cdot \omega_s^{0.8}) / (L_b \cdot p)$ | — | Daylight Glare Index |
| 8 | Lamp-to-Lamp Lumen Ratio | $LLR = \text{Maintained lumens} / \text{Initial lumens}$ | ratio | < 1 always |
| 9 | Spatial Luminance Uniformity | $U_o = L_{min} / L_{avg}$ | ratio | ≥ 0.4 minimum |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive headlights:** Designers compute on-axis luminance (cd/m²) to ensure headlamps meet the UNECE Regulation 48 photometric requirements (typically 50,000–100,000 cd peak).
- **Display screens (TVs, monitors):** Luminance of 250–1000 cd/m² is targeted; HDR displays exceed 4000 cd/m².
- **Road lighting (IS 1944 / IEC 13201):** Average road surface luminance ($L_{avg}$) of 0.5–2.0 cd/m² is prescribed based on traffic density.
- **Aviation runway lighting:** Edge lights have luminances engineered to be visible at 8 km, and high-intensity runway end lights exceed 10,000 cd.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost per Million Lumen-Hours

This derivation is the standard KTU "long-answer" derivation. The total cost of operating a lamp over its life is the sum of the lamp cost (assuming one lamp replacement over the lifetime) and the cost of electrical energy consumed.

**Step 1: Lamp purchase cost** = $C_L$ (in ₹, assumed for one lamp of life $t$ hours).

**Step 2: Energy consumed over life** = $P \times t$ (in Wh), where $P$ is lamp wattage (including ballast loss $P_b$).

**Step 3: Energy cost over life** = $P \cdot t \cdot r$ (in ₹), where $r$ is the energy tariff in ₹/kWh.

$$E_{cost} = \frac{P \cdot t \cdot r}{1000}$$

(Dividing by 1000 converts Wh → kWh.)

**Step 4: Total life-cycle cost** (per lamp):
$$C_T = C_L + \frac{P \cdot t \cdot r}{1000}$$

**Step 5: Total useful light delivered over life** = $\Phi \times t$ lumen-hours, where $\Phi$ is the initial luminous flux of the lamp (in lumens).

**Step 6: Cost per million lumen-hours:**
$$\text{C/MLh} = \frac{C_T \times 10^6}{\Phi \times t}$$

Substituting $C_T$:
$$\text{C/MLh} = \frac{10^6}{\Phi \times t} \left( C_L + \frac{P \cdot t \cdot r}{1000} \right)$$

**Step 7: For a numerical comparison**, expand:
$$\text{C/MLh} = \frac{10^6 \cdot C_L}{\Phi \cdot t} + \frac{10^6 \cdot P \cdot r}{1000 \cdot \Phi}$$

$$\text{C/MLh} = \frac{10^6 \cdot C_L}{\Phi \cdot t} + \frac{1000 \cdot P \cdot r}{\Phi}$$

The first term is the **lamp-cost contribution**; the second term is the **energy-cost contribution**. As $t \to \infty$ (very long life, as in LED), the first term vanishes, and C/MLh approaches a fixed energy-driven cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The examiner will frequently present a table of three or four lamps with their wattage, lumens, life, and cost, and ask: *"Which lamp is most economical?"* The answer requires computing C/MLh for each and comparing. Marks are awarded for showing the full expression, the substitution, and the final comparison.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Luminance from Illuminance of a Diffuse Reflector

This is the second-most-tested derivation. We start from the flux balance on a small elemental area.

**Step 1:** Consider a small flat patch of area $dA$ on a wall. Illuminance on it is $E$ (lux = lm/m²).

**Step 2:** Total flux intercepted by the patch = $E \cdot dA$ (in lumens).

**Step 3:** Of this incident flux, a fraction $\rho$ (the reflectance) is reflected. The remaining $(1 - \rho)$ is absorbed.

**Step 4:** Reflected flux = $\rho \cdot E \cdot dA$.

**Step 5:** For a perfectly diffuse reflector, the reflected flux is distributed uniformly in the hemisphere above the patch, i.e., into a solid angle of $2\pi$ steradians.

**Step 6:** By definition, the **luminance** $L$ (cd/m²) of such a Lambertian surface is related to the emitted flux by:
$$\Phi_{emitted} = \pi \cdot L \cdot dA$$

**Step 7:** Equating the reflected flux to the emitted flux:
$$\rho \cdot E \cdot dA = \pi \cdot L \cdot dA$$

**Step 8:** Cancel $dA$ from both sides:
$$\rho \cdot E = \pi \cdot L$$

**Step 9:** Solve for $L$:
$$L = \frac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

This is the working equation for converting a measured illuminance on a diffuse surface to its luminance.

**Step 10: Numerical sanity check.** If a white wall ($\rho = 0.7$) receives 500 lux of illuminance, its luminance is:
$$L = \frac{0.7 \times 500}{\pi} = \frac{350}{\pi} = 111.4 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

A blackboard ($\rho = 0.15$) under the same light:
$$L = \frac{0.15 \times 500}{\pi} = \frac{75}{\pi} = 23.9 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

This is why a well-lit white wall appears much "brighter" than a well-lit blackboard — even though both have the same illuminance. **This is the essence of luminance design.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lambert's Cosine Law for a Diffuse Surface

This is the foundational relation for any calculation involving directional intensity.

**Step 1:** Consider a point source of intensity $I_0$ (candela) normal to a small patch of area $dA$ at distance $r$.

**Step 2:** The illuminance on the patch (when the source is directly above it) is:
$$E_0 = \frac{I_0}{r^2}$$

**Step 3:** Now tilt the surface by an angle $\theta$ from the normal direction. The projected area seen from the source becomes $dA \cdot \cos\theta$.

**Step 4:** The illuminance on the tilted surface is therefore:
$$E_\theta = \frac{I_0 \cdot \cos\theta}{r^2} = E_0 \cdot \cos\theta$$

This is the **Cosine Law of Illuminance (Lambert's Cosine Law)**.

**Step 5:** Conversely, for a *diffuse-emitting* surface, the intensity in direction $\theta$ from the normal is:
$$I(\theta) = I_0 \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 6:** The luminance in any direction is:
$$L(\theta) = \frac{I(\theta)}{dA \cdot \cos\theta} = \frac{I_0 \cdot \cos\theta}{dA \cdot \cos\theta} = \frac{I_0}{dA}$$

Therefore, **for a perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) surface, the luminance is independent of viewing angle.** This is the defining property of a Lambertian emitter.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Lamps for Selection

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI ($R_a$) | CCT (K) | Life (h) | Cost/MLh (₹) | Restrike |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incandescent (GLS) | 8–15 | 100 | 2700 | 1,000 | 5000 | Instant |
| Halogen (Tungsten Halogen) | 15–25 | 100 | 3000 | 2,000 | 3500 | Instant |
| CFL | 50–70 | 80 | 2700–6500 | 8,000 | 600 | 1–2 min |
| Fluorescent (T5) | 80–100 | 80–90 | 4000–6500 | 24,000 | 350 | 1 s |
| Mercury Vapour (HPMV) | 35–55 | 50 | 5500 | 16,000 | 500 | 5–10 min |
| Metal Halide (HPI) | 75–100 | 85 | 4000–6000 | 15,000 | 400 | 5–15 min |
| High-Pressure Sodium (HPSV/SON) | 80–130 | 25 | 2000 | 24,000 | 300 | 1–3 min |
| Low-Pressure Sodium (LPSV/SOX) | 100–200 | 0 | 1800 | 18,000 | 350 | Instant (warm) |
| LED (white) | 100–200 | 80–95 | 2700–6500 | 50,000 | 150 | Instant |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Inference:** LEDs dominate the cost-per-lumen-hour metric, but HPSV still wins for high-mast street lighting on raw efficacy and HPSV's tolerance of over-voltage conditions.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A street lighting installation requires an average road surface luminance of 1.5 cd/m². The road surface is asphalt with a reflectance of 0.15. The installation uses LED luminaires each rated at 150 W, emitting 18,000 lumens, with a 92% luminaire output ratio and 0.85 light loss factor. Determine:
(a) The equivalent illuminance level required.
(b) The coefficient of utilization, given the spacing-to-height ratio is 3.5 and the luminaire is classified as "cut-off."
(c) The number of luminaires required per km, assuming a 12 m road width and a 10 m mounting height.

### Solution

**Part (a): Equivalent Illuminance**

Using $L = \rho E / \pi$:
$$E = \frac{\pi L}{\rho} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5}{0.15} = \frac{4.712}{0.15} = 31.42 \text{ lux}$$

**Part (b): Coefficient of Utilization**

For a cut-off luminaire with S/H = 3.5 and 12 m wide road:
$$S = 3.5 \times 10 = 35 \text{ m}$$

From IS 1944 / CIE 140-2000 tables for cut-off luminaires at S/H = 3.5:
$$CU \approx 0.42$$

**Part (c): Number of Luminaires per km**

Effective lumens per luminaire on the road:
$$\Phi_{eff} = 18000 \times 0.92 \times 0.85 \times 0.42 = 5910.4 \text{ lumens}$$

Total lumens required per km (area = 1000 × 12 = 12,000 m²):
$$\Phi_{total} = E \times A = 31.42 \times 12000 = 377,040 \text{ lumens}$$

Number of luminaires:
$$N = \frac{377040}{5910.4} = 63.8 \approx 64 \text{ luminaires per km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to multiply the lumen output by both the **Luminaire Output Ratio (LOR)** and the **Light Loss Factor (LLF)** before applying the coefficient of utilization. The correct order is: $\Phi_{on-road} = \Phi_{rated} \times LOR \times LLF \times CU$. Marks are lost if any of these four factors is missing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lamp Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Lighting Application Identified] --> B{Is color accuracy critical?}
    B -- Yes, CRI > 80 --> C{Is instant restrike required?}
    B -- No, CRI > 25 OK --> D[Choose HPSV or LPSV]
    C -- Yes --> E[Choose LED or Halogen]
    C -- No --> F{Is luminous flux > 20000 lm?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Choose Metal Halide HPI]
    F -- No --> H[Choose LED panel / T5 fluorescent]
    E --> I{Cost-sensitive initial purchase?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Choose Halogen]
    I -- No, long life preferred --> K[Choose LED]
    D --> L{Is single-color yellow acceptable?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Choose LPSV SOX]
    L -- No --> N[Choose HPSV SON]
    G --> O[Industrial / Stadium / High-bay]
    H --> P[Office / Commercial / Domestic]
```

## 4.2 Luminance Concept Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Luminance L, cd/m²] --> B[Definition Layer]
    A --> C[Mathematical Layer]
    A --> D[Physical Layer]
    A --> E[Application Layer]
    B --> B1[Photometric stimulus of brightness]
    B --> B2[Per unit projected area per unit solid angle]
    C --> C1[L = I / A cos theta]
    C --> C2[Lambertian: L = Phi / pi A]
    C --> C3[Reflection: L = rho E / pi]
    D --> D1[Perfect Diffuser Lambertian Emitter]
    D --> D2[Specular Mirror Directional]
    D --> D3[Real Surface Mixed]
    E --> E1[Road Lighting: L_avg 0.5 to 2 cd/m²]
    E --> E2[Display: L 250 to 4000 cd/m²]
    E --> E3[Headlamps: L peak 50000 to 100000 cd]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Luminance Measurement System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Light Source] --> A2[Surface Under Test]
    A2 --> A3[Optical System Lens / Aperture]
    A3 --> A4[Photometric Detector]
    A4 --> A5[Signal Amplifier]
    A5 --> A6[ADC and Processor]
    A6 --> A7[Display and Readout cd per m²]
    A6 --> A8[Data Logger]
    A8 --> A9[Spectral Analysis Optional]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Lamp Selection Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Step 1: Define Application and Illuminance Level] --> P2[Step 2: Compute Total Lumens Required]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Identify CRI and CCT Requirements]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: List Candidate Lamps]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Calculate Cost per Million Lumen Hours]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Apply Environmental Constraints]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Apply Maintainability Constraints]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Select Optimal Lamp]
```
