# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
**Q: Define luminance. State its SI unit and explain how it differs from illuminance.**

**Model Answer:**
**Luminance (L)** is the photometric quantity representing the luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction. It is the physical stimulus that produces the sensation of brightness. SI unit is **candela per square metre (cd/m²)**, also called **nit**.

**Difference from Illuminance:**

| Property | Illuminance (E) | Luminance (L) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Incident on a surface | Emitted/reflected from a surface |
| Unit | lux (lm/m²) | cd/m² |
| Depends on | Source intensity and distance | Reflectance, surface geometry, viewing direction |
| Photometric role | Quantity of light received | Quantity of light leaving |
| Measured by | Lux meter (cosine corrected) | Luminance meter (with imaging optics) |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]**
**Q: List any six factors that govern the selection of a lamp for a given application.**

**Model Answer:**
The six factors governing the selection of a lamp are:
1. **Luminous efficacy** (lm/W) — energy efficiency.
2. **Color rendering index (CRI / $R_a$)** — color accuracy.
3. **Correlated color temperature (CCT)** — warm vs. cool appearance.
4. **Rated lamp life (hours)** — replacement frequency.
5. **Lumen maintenance / depreciation** — light output decline with time.
6. **Initial cost and operating cost per million lumen-hours** — economics.

Additional factors include: restrike time, ambient temperature tolerance, switching endurance, harmonics, flicker, and mounting position.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**Q: (a) Explain the various factors that govern the selection of a lamp for indoor industrial lighting application. (7 marks)**
**(b) Compare the merits and demerits of LED lamps over HPSV lamps for street lighting. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part A (a)

For an **indoor industrial lighting application**, the lamp selection is governed by the following categories of factors:

**1. Photometric Factors:**
- **High luminous efficacy** is essential because industrial installations have high load (often 50–500 lux across a large floor area), and energy cost dominates lifecycle cost.
- **Good color rendering ($R_a \geq 80$)** is required if the task involves color discrimination (e.g., painting, textile sorting, food processing).
- **Suitable CCT (4000–5000 K)** for visual alertness; warm light in a factory induces drowsiness.
- **Adequate lumen maintenance** — depreciation should be slow to reduce relamping frequency.

**2. Electrical Factors:**
- Lamp must operate on the available supply (typically 415 V three-phase).
- Power factor should be ≥ 0.85 (with capacitor-corrected ballast).
- Low harmonic distortion (THD < 10%) for electronic ballasts, to comply with IEEE 519.

**3. Economic Factors:**
- **Cost per million lumen-hour** must be minimized. Computation includes lamp cost, ballast cost, energy cost over life.
- Group replacement vs. spot replacement economics should be considered.

**4. Application / Environmental Factors:**
- **Mounting height** is often 6–12 m. The luminaire photometry (narrow vs. wide distribution) must match the mounting height.
- **Ambient temperature** of the factory (steel mills may be 40–50 °C; cold storage may be –20 °C) must be within lamp operating range.
- **Vibration tolerance** is important near heavy machinery.
- **Flicker-free operation** is mandatory to avoid stroboscopic illusion of rotating parts (e.g., lathes, drills).
- **Instant restrike** is critical for safety during power interruptions.

**Conclusion:** For a typical indoor industrial setup, **T5 fluorescent or LED luminaires with high CRI and electronic ballasts** are the most suitable choice in 2024.

#### Model Solution for Part A (b)

| Parameter | LED Lamp | HPSV Lamp |
|---|---|---|
| Luminous efficacy | 100–200 lm/W (modern) | 80–130 lm/W |
| CRI ($R_a$) | 80–95 (excellent) | 20–25 (poor) |
| CCT | 2700–6500 K (selectable) | ~2000 K (warm yellow) |
| Lamp life | 50,000 h (excellent) | 24,000 h (good) |
| Restrike time | Instant | 1–3 minutes |
| Switching endurance | Excellent (unaffected) | Poor (each start reduces life) |
| Warm-up time | Instant | 5–10 minutes |
| Cost per MLm·h | ~₹150 | ~₹300 |
| Initial cost | Higher (1.5–2×) | Lower |
| Directional control | Excellent (chip-level) | Requires reflector |
| Environmental impact | No mercury | Contains sodium and mercury |
| Cold-weather performance | Excellent | Good (no warm-up issue) |
| Vibration tolerance | Good (solid-state) | Fair (arc tube) |

**Conclusion:** LEDs are superior in nearly every parameter except possibly initial cost, which is offset by lower energy and maintenance costs. KTU's 2024 syllabus recommendation favors LEDs for new installations; HPSV remains acceptable for cost-constrained replacements of existing HPSV infrastructure.

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
**Q: (a) Define luminance. Derive the relationship between luminance, illuminance, and reflectance for a perfectly diffuse reflecting surface. (7 marks)**
**(b) A roadway is to be illuminated to an average luminance of 1.2 cd/m². The road surface has a reflectance of 0.18. Calculate: (i) the equivalent average illuminance required, (ii) the total lumen output needed per km for a 10 m wide road, and (iii) the number of LED luminaires required, given each LED luminaire is 120 W, 14,000 lm, with 0.90 LOR, 0.85 LLF, and 0.45 CU. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Definition of Luminance** (as above, 2 marks).

**Derivation:**

Consider a small elemental patch of area $dA$ on a perfectly diffuse reflecting surface. Let $E$ be the illuminance (in lux) on the patch.

Total flux incident on the patch:
$$\Phi_{in} = E \cdot dA$$

Of this, a fraction $\rho$ (reflectance) is reflected uniformly into the hemisphere above the patch (solid angle = $2\pi$ sr).

Flux reflected by the patch:
$$\Phi_{out} = \rho \cdot E \cdot dA$$

For a perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) surface, the relationship between emitted flux and luminance is:
$$\Phi_{out} = \pi \cdot L \cdot dA$$

Equating:
$$\rho \cdot E \cdot dA = \pi \cdot L \cdot dA$$

Canceling $dA$:
$$\rho \cdot E = \pi \cdot L$$

Solving for $L$:
$$L = \frac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

This is the required relationship. (5 marks for derivation.)

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Given:**
- $L_{avg} = 1.2 \text{ cd/m}^2$
- $\rho = 0.18$
- Road width $W = 10 \text{ m}$
- Per km length $L_k = 1000 \text{ m}$
- LED wattage $P = 120 \text{ W}$
- LED flux $\Phi_L = 14{,}000 \text{ lm}$
- LOR = 0.90
- LLF = 0.85
- CU = 0.45

**Step 1: Average illuminance required**
$$E = \frac{\pi \cdot L}{\rho} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2}{0.18} = \frac{3.7699}{0.18} = 20.94 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 2: Total lumens per km**
$$\Phi_{total} = E \times A = 20.94 \times (10 \times 1000) = 20.94 \times 10{,}000 = 209{,}400 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 3: Effective lumens per luminaire**
$$\Phi_{eff} = \Phi_L \times \text{LOR} \times \text{LLF} \times \text{CU}$$
$$\Phi_{eff} = 14{,}000 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.45$$
$$\Phi_{eff} = 14{,}000 \times 0.3443 = 4820.6 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 4: Number of luminaires per km**
$$N = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{\Phi_{eff}} = \frac{209{,}400}{4820.6} = 43.4 \approx 44 \text{ luminaires per km}$$

**Spacing:**
$$S = \frac{1000}{44} = 22.7 \text{ m between luminaires}$$

If mounted at height $h = 10$ m, S/H ratio = 22.7 / 10 = 2.27, which is in the typical range (2 to 4) for street lighting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Marks Are Lost on This Topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up Illuminance and Luminance:** Students frequently interchange the two. Remember: illuminance is "received," luminance is "departed." The question is about luminance of the road → you need $L = \rho E / \pi$, not $E = I/r^2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the LOR and LLF multipliers:** The luminaire's rated 14,000 lm is the bare-lamp value. The "lumens reaching the road" = $\Phi_L \times LOR \times LLF \times CU$. Missing any of these loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Not using $\pi$ in the right places:** The factor $\pi$ appears in $L = \rho E / \pi$ AND in $\Phi = \pi L A$ for a Lambertian. Some students use $1/\pi$ instead of $\pi$ in the reverse formula.
> 4. **Ignoring the units of $\rho$:** $\rho$ is dimensionless. The ratio gives $E$ in lux if $L$ is in cd/m².
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state "assuming a perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) reflecting surface" — examiners award a mark for this assumption being explicit.
> 6. **In lamp selection questions, students list factors without ranking them.** Always rank the top 3 factors for the specific application.
> 7. **In C/MLh calculations, omitting ballast loss.** Include the ballast wattage in $P$ when computing energy cost.
> 8. **Confusing CRI with CCT.** CRI is the *quality* of color rendering; CCT is the *temperature* of the white tone. They are independent parameters.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lamp selection** is governed by **photometric, electrical, economic, and environmental** factors, with the application driving the priority order.
- **Luminous efficacy** (lm/W) is the most energy-related parameter; **CRI** is the most color-related; **rated life** is the most maintenance-related.
- **Luminance (L, cd/m²)** is a *directional* quantity, while **Illuminance (E, lux)** is *non-directional* (it represents total incident flux per area).
- The two foundational equations to memorize:
  - $L = \rho E / \pi$ — for a diffuse reflector.
  - $L = \Phi / (\pi A)$ — for a diffuse emitter.
- **Cost per million lumen-hour (C/MLm·h)** is the standard metric for lamp economic comparison.
- For **street lighting**, design is done in *luminance* (cd/m²), not illuminance. IS 1944 specifies $L_{avg}$ of 0.5–2.0 cd/m² based on traffic class.
- **LEDs** are now the default KTU-recommended choice for almost all new installations due to high efficacy, long life, instant restrike, and good CRI.
- **HPSV** is preferred where absolute minimum cost-per-lumen is needed and color rendering is unimportant (e.g., rural roads, high-mast yards).
- **Metal halide** is preferred where high CRI and high flux from a single source are required (e.g., stadiums, large industrial sheds).
- For perfectly **diffuse (Lambertian)** surfaces, luminance is **constant in all directions**.
- For **specular** surfaces (mirrors, polished metal), luminance is concentrated in a narrow angular range.
- The KTU examiner values **clean unit tracking**: every numerical answer should clearly state its unit (lux, cd/m², lm, lm/W, etc.).
- **Practice the derivation** of $L = \rho E / \pi$ and the **C/MLh** equation — both are *guaranteed* in the question bank.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Utilisation Factor, Reflection Factor and Maintenance Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Utilisation Factor, Reflection Factor and Maintenance Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Big Picture: Three Pillars of Lumen Method Design

In illumination engineering, the **Lumen Method** (also called the *Lumen or Light Flux Method*) is the standard procedure for calculating the number of lamps required to achieve a desired average illuminance in an interior space. This method depends critically on **three correction factors**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Holy Trinity of Interior Illumination Design**
> 1. **Utilisation Factor (UF)** — *How much light actually reaches the working plane?*
> 2. **Reflection Factor (ρ)** — *How efficiently do room surfaces bounce light back?*
> 3. **Maintenance Factor (MF)** — *How much light survives the harsh realities of dirt, dust, and lamp aging?*

The governing equation that brings them together is the **Lumen Formula**:

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times UF \times MF}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Number of lamps required
- $E$ = Required average illuminance on the working plane (**lux**)
- $A$ = Area of the working plane (**m²**)
- $\phi$ = Luminous flux per lamp (**lumens**)
- $UF$ = **Utilisation Factor** (dimensionless, 0 < UF < 1)
- $MF$ = **Maintenance Factor** (dimensionless, 0 < MF < 1)

---

## 1.2 Utilisation Factor (UF) — The "Light Efficiency" of Geometry

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)
> The **Utilisation Factor** is defined as the ratio of the total luminous flux (lumens) reaching the *working plane* to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps.
> 
> $$UF = \frac{\text{Total lumens reaching the working plane}}{\text{Total lumens emitted by the lamps}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy 💡
> [!NOTE]
> **The Shower Analogy for Utilisation Factor**
> 
> Imagine you turn on a shower (your lamp emits light in all directions). The water that actually reaches your body (working plane) versus the water that hits the walls, floor, and ceiling (room surfaces) — that ratio is the **UF**. A poorly designed bathroom where most water hits the walls is like a poor UF. A well-designed shower where most water hits you is a high UF!

### Physical Meaning
- **UF = 1.0** → Every photon emitted by the lamp reaches the working plane (impossible in reality).
- **UF = 0.4** → Only 40% of the light produced reaches the desk; 60% is lost to walls, ceiling, floor, or absorbed by fixtures.
- Typical UF values range from **0.3 to 0.8** for most industrial and commercial interiors.

---

## 1.3 Reflection Factor (ρ) — The "Bounce" of Light

### Formal Definition
> The **Reflection Factor (Reflectance)** is the ratio of the luminous flux reflected by a surface to the luminous flux incident upon it.
> 
> $$\rho = \frac{\text{Lumens reflected by surface}}{\text{Lumens incident on surface}}$$

It is commonly expressed as a percentage, where:
$$\rho_{\%} = \rho \times 100$$

### Conceptual Analogy 🎾
> [!NOTE]
> **The Tennis Ball Bounce Analogy**
> 
> Drop a tennis ball on a polished wooden floor — it bounces high. Drop it on thick carpet — it barely bounces. The "bounce" is your reflection factor. **White-painted walls = tennis floor (ρ ≈ 0.7)**, while **dark wooden paneling = thick carpet (ρ ≈ 0.1)**. The smoother and lighter the surface, the more light it returns to the room instead of swallowing it.

### Components of Room Reflection
The three principal surfaces in any interior lighting design are:

| Surface | Role in Light Distribution |
|---|---|
| **Ceiling** | Receives upward light, reflects it back down |
| **Walls** | Receive sideway light, redistribute to room |
| **Floor (Working Plane)** | Final destination of most light |

---

## 1.4 Maintenance Factor (MF) — The "Time Penalty"

### Formal Definition
> The **Maintenance Factor** is the ratio of the *illuminance on the working plane after a certain period of use* to the *illuminance when everything was brand new*.
> 
> $$MF = \frac{\text{Illuminance after depreciation}}{\text{Initial Illuminance (when new)}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy 🕰️
> [!NOTE]
> **The Smartphone Battery Analogy**
> 
> Your new phone holds 100% charge. After 2 years, it might hold only 80%. That ratio (0.8) is your **Maintenance Factor**. Similarly, after months of dust accumulation on luminaires, aging of lamps, and yellowing of walls, the room's brightness drops. MF tells you: *"Design for the *worst day* of the lamp's life, not its first day."*

### Decomposition of MF
The overall Maintenance Factor is a *product* of individual depreciation components:

$$MF = LLF \times LLD \times LSF \times DF$$

Where:
- $LLF$ = Luminaire Lumen Factor (dust on luminaire)
- $LLD$ = Lamp Lumen Depreciation (lamp aging)
- $LSF$ = Lamp Survival Factor (lamp burnout)
- $DF$ = Depreciation Factor (room surface dirtiness)

For typical design, engineers use a single combined **MF** as a simplification.

---

## 1.5 Why Selection Matters — The Designer's Dilemma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection is not lookup — it is a multi-parameter decision.**
> 
> - A wrong **UF** (too high) → *under-lit room* (occupants complain, productivity falls).
> - A wrong **UF** (too low) → *over-designed, wasteful installation* (capital and energy wasted).
> - A wrong **MF** (too high, ignoring dust) → *illuminance falls below statutory minimums within months*.
> - A wrong **ρ** (assuming white walls in a brown-painted room) → *catastrophic 50% loss in delivered light*.

These three factors interlock: **ρ directly affects UF** (more reflective room = higher UF), and **MF accounts for the long-term drift of both**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Utilisation Factor — The Geometry-Dependent Parameter

The Utilisation Factor is **not a constant**. It depends on:
1. **Room Index (RI)** — the geometry of the room.
2. **Reflection factors** of the ceiling and walls.
3. **Suspension height** of the luminaire.
4. **Distribution pattern** of the luminaire (direct, indirect, general diffuse).

### 2.1.1 Room Index (RI) — The Shape Number

The Room Index quantifies how "open" or "tall" a room is. A short, wide room has different light distribution than a tall, narrow one.

$$RI = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Length of the room (m)
- $W$ = Width of the room (m)
- $H_m$ = **Mounting height** of the luminaire *above the working plane* (m)
- $H_m = H - h_w$ where $H$ is the height of luminaire above floor and $h_w$ is the working plane height (typically 0.85 m for desks, 0 m for floor work)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention for Hm:**
> $H_m$ is measured from the **luminous center of the luminaire** to the **working plane**, NOT from the ceiling. Many students lose marks by using total ceiling height instead!

### 2.1.2 Classification of Rooms by Room Index

| Room Index Range | Room Shape | UF Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| RI < 0.7 | Very tall, narrow | UF is low (light trapped between walls) |
| 0.7 ≤ RI ≤ 1.0 | Small/cubical | UF moderate |
| 1.0 < RI ≤ 2.0 | Normal office/classroom | UF optimal |
| 2.0 < RI ≤ 4.0 | Large hall | UF high |
| RI > 4.0 | Very long warehouse | UF plateaus (geometry saturates) |

### 2.1.3 The Standard UF Selection Procedure

```
Step 1: Calculate Room Index (RI) using L, W, Hm
        ↓
Step 2: Round RI to the nearest standard tabulated value
        (typically: 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0)
        ↓
Step 3: Determine ceiling reflectance (ρc) and wall reflectance (ρw)
        from surface colour and material tables
        ↓
Step 4: Refer to manufacturer's UF table for the chosen luminaire
        using the rounded RI and reflectance pair
        ↓
Step 5: Interpolate if necessary for non-tabulated values
```

### 2.1.4 Sample UF Table (Direct-type Luminaire, Hypothetical Manufacturer Data)

> The values below illustrate the **structure** of standard published tables used in KTU problem statements.

| RI | ρc = 0.7, ρw = 0.5 | ρc = 0.7, ρw = 0.3 | ρc = 0.5, ρw = 0.5 | ρc = 0.5, ρw = 0.1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.6 | 0.32 | 0.28 | 0.27 | 0.22 |
| 0.8 | 0.41 | 0.36 | 0.35 | 0.30 |
| 1.0 | 0.47 | 0.42 | 0.41 | 0.35 |
| 1.25 | 0.52 | 0.47 | 0.46 | 0.40 |
| 1.5 | 0.55 | 0.50 | 0.49 | 0.43 |
| 2.0 | 0.60 | 0.55 | 0.54 | 0.48 |
| 2.5 | 0.63 | 0.58 | 0.57 | 0.51 |
| 3.0 | 0.65 | 0.60 | 0.59 | 0.53 |
| 4.0 | 0.68 | 0.63 | 0.62 | 0.56 |
| 5.0 | 0.70 | 0.65 | 0.64 | 0.58 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** UF **increases** with RI (geometry becomes more efficient) and **increases** with reflection factors (more light bounces back). The most efficient combination is high RI + high reflectance.

---

## 2.2 Reflection Factor — Surface-by-Surface Selection

### 2.2.1 Standard Reflection Factor Table (BIS / IES Standard Values)

| Surface | Colour / Material | Reflection Factor (ρ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Ceiling — White** | White paint, white plaster | **0.70 – 0.80** |
| Ceiling — Light cream | Off-white paint | 0.60 – 0.70 |
| Ceiling — Medium | Light grey, beige | 0.50 – 0.60 |
| Ceiling — Dark | Dark grey, exposed concrete | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| **Walls — Light** | Pale yellow, light blue, white | **0.50 – 0.70** |
| Walls — Medium | Pastel green, wood, light brown | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| Walls — Dark | Dark wood, dark red brick | 0.10 – 0.20 |
| **Floor — Light** | Pale concrete, light tiles | **0.30 – 0.40** |
| Floor — Medium | Medium wood, plain concrete | 0.15 – 0.30 |
| Floor — Dark | Dark carpet, asphalt | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Special: Mirror | Polished mirror | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Special: Aluminium foil | Polished | 0.70 – 0.80 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three "Whites" Rule of Thumb (KTU Quick Recall):**
> - Bright white ceiling → **ρc ≈ 0.7**
> - Light-coloured walls → **ρw ≈ 0.5**
> - Medium floor → **ρf ≈ 0.2**
> - This trio is the most common KTU exam assumption unless stated otherwise.

### 2.2.2 How Reflection Factor Is Determined in Practice

For a surface where ρ is unknown, the **luminance method** is used:

$$\rho = \frac{\pi \times L}{E_s}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Luminance of the surface (cd/m²)
- $E_s$ = Illuminance falling on the surface (lux)

Or via a simple **photometric ratio**:
$$\rho = \frac{\text{Lumens reflected}}{\text{Lumens incident}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conservation Check:** $\rho + \alpha + \tau = 1$, where $\alpha$ = absorption factor and $\tau$ = transmission factor. For an opaque wall, $\tau = 0$, so $\rho + \alpha = 1$.

---

## 2.3 Maintenance Factor — The "Real-World" Discount

### 2.3.1 Depreciation Categories (BIS 3646 / CIE Standard)

| Depreciation Source | Description | Typical Loss |
|---|---|---|
| **Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD)** | Lamp output decreases with burning hours | 10–20% over life |
| **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD)** | Dust on luminaire reflector/diffuser | 5–30% depending on environment |
| **Room Surface Dirt Depreciation (RSDD)** | Walls/ceiling darken over time | 5–20% |
| **Lamp Burnout (non-replacement)** | Burnt lamps not immediately replaced | 0–10% |
| **Voltage variation** | Supply voltage deviation | ±5% |

### 2.3.2 Standard MF Selection Table (Industry Practice)

| Environment | Cleaning Interval | Typical MF |
|---|---|---|
| **Clean office / classroom** (low dust, AC) | Every 1–2 years | **0.80** |
| Normal industrial (moderate dust) | Every year | **0.70** |
| **Dirty industrial** (smoke, dust, fumes) | Every 6 months | **0.60** |
| **Very dirty** (foundry, forge, kiln) | Every 3 months | **0.50** |
| Clean rooms (hospitals, labs) | Quarterly | **0.85–0.90** |
| Outdoor / hazardous | Frequent service | **0.40–0.50** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a problem does not specify MF, use:
> - **MF = 0.8** for "clean" environments (offices, classrooms, hospitals)
> - **MF = 0.7** for "normal" industrial applications
> - **MF = 0.6** for "dirty" environments (cement, steel, chemical)
> - Never use MF = 1.0 in design — that is a *guaranteed deduction* in valuation.

### 2.3.3 The Multiplicative Model (Detailed Analysis)

For rigorous design, the CIE recommends:

$$MF = LLF \times LDD \times RSDD \times LSF$$

Example calculation for a *clean office with 3-year lamp replacement*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LLF &= 0.92 \quad \text{(luminaire cleaned annually)} \\
LDD &= 0.85 \quad \text{(lamp lumen depreciation at 70% of rated life)} \\
RSDD &= 0.95 \quad \text{(room surfaces clean, repainted every 5 years)} \\
LSF &= 0.95 \quad \text{(10% of lamps burnt, not immediately replaced)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$MF = 0.92 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 = 0.706 \approx 0.70$$

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of KTU 2024 ESE numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary / Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RI = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L+W)}$ | Room Index | 0.6 to 5.0 |
| 2 | $H_m = H - h_w$ | Mounting height above working plane | 2 m to 10 m typical |
| 3 | $UF = \dfrac{\Phi_{wp}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | Utilisation Factor | 0.25 to 0.75 |
| 4 | $\rho = \dfrac{\Phi_{reflected}}{\Phi_{incident}}$ | Reflection Factor | 0.05 to 0.95 |
| 5 | $MF = \dfrac{E_{after}}{E_{initial}}$ | Maintenance Factor | 0.4 to 0.9 |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\phi \times UF \times MF}$ | Number of lamps | Positive integer |
| 7 | $E_{avg} = \dfrac{N \times \phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$ | Average illuminance | Task-dependent |
| 8 | $E_{point} = \dfrac{I_\theta \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$ | Point-source illuminance | Inverse square law |
| 9 | $MF = LLF \times LDD \times RSDD \times LSF$ | Combined MF | ≤ 1.0 always |
| 10 | $SP = \dfrac{L \times W}{N_{lum}}$ | Spacing-to-height ratio check | 0.7 to 1.5 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Spotlight**
> 
> In a **1000 m² warehouse** with a 10 m ceiling, the designer must:
> 1. Pick a high-bay LED luminaire (typically 20,000 lm each).
> 2. Set $H_m = 9.15$ m (10 m − 0.85 m working plane for floor stacking).
> 3. Calculate $RI = (40 \times 25) / (9.15 \times 65) = 1.68$.
> 4. Round to 1.5 or 2.0; pick reflectance 0.5/0.3/0.1 (typical industrial).
> 5. Read UF ≈ 0.55 from the manufacturer's table.
> 6. Use MF = 0.7 (dusty warehouse, yearly cleaning).
> 7. Compute $N$ for the desired 200 lux.
> 
> Every percentage point of MF or UF error translates to **₹ lakhs of capital cost and annual electricity bills** in a real industrial setting.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Utilisation Factor Formula from First Principles

The UF is not a *fitted* parameter; it emerges from the **interchange of light between room surfaces** through multiple reflections.

### Step 1: Single-Surface Reflection (First Bounce)
When a flux $\Phi_0$ is emitted by the lamps, only a fraction reaches the working plane directly. The rest strikes the ceiling, walls, and floor.

Let:
- $\Phi_{d}$ = Flux reaching working plane *directly* (no reflection).
- $\Phi_{c}, \Phi_{w}, \Phi_{f}$ = Flux reaching ceiling, walls, floor directly.

For an opaque room:
$$\Phi_0 = \Phi_d + \Phi_c + \Phi_w + \Phi_f$$

### Step 2: First-Reflection Contribution
Each surface reflects a fraction of its incident flux:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{c,reflected} &= \rho_c \times \Phi_c \\
\Phi_{w,reflected} &= \rho_w \times \Phi_w \\
\Phi_{f,reflected} &= \rho_f \times \Phi_f
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Direct Utilisation Factor
The direct UF (no inter-reflection) is defined as:
$$UF_0 = \frac{\Phi_d}{\Phi_0}$$

### Step 4: Adding the First Reflection
The UF including first reflection is:
$$UF_1 = \frac{\Phi_d + (\text{flux from first reflection that reaches working plane})}{\Phi_0}$$

If $k_c, k_w, k_f$ are the fractions of reflected flux from ceiling, walls, floor that reach the working plane:

$$UF_1 = UF_0 + k_c \rho_c \frac{\Phi_c}{\Phi_0} + k_w \rho_w \frac{\Phi_w}{\Phi_0} + k_f \rho_f \frac{\Phi_f}{\Phi_0}$$

### Step 5: Infinite-Series Expansion
Each reflected flux itself strikes other surfaces and gets re-reflected. Summing this infinite series gives the **final Utilisation Factor**:

$$UF = UF_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (\text{all } n\text{-bounce contributions})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Guarantee:** Because $\rho < 1$ for real surfaces, the series converges. The infinite sum reduces to a closed-form expression involving $\rho_c, \rho_w, \rho_f$ and the room geometry constants $k_c, k_w, k_f$. This is how manufacturers generate the UF tables — by solving the full inter-reflection equation computationally or experimentally in a *photometric laboratory* using a scale model room.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Number of Lamps Equation

Starting from the definition of average illuminance:
$$E_{avg} = \frac{\text{Total lumens reaching working plane}}{\text{Area of working plane}}$$

The total lumens *emitted* by $N$ lamps, each producing $\phi$ lumens, is $N \phi$.
By definition of UF, the lumens *reaching* the working plane is $N \phi \times UF$.
Applying the MF for real-world depreciation: lumens *effectively usable* = $N \phi \times UF \times MF$.

Therefore:
$$E_{avg} = \frac{N \phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$$

Solving for $N$:
$$\boxed{N = \frac{E_{avg} \times A}{\phi \times UF \times MF}}$$

### Detailed Unit Verification
$$
\begin{aligned}
[N] &= \frac{[\text{lux}] \times [\text{m}^2]}{[\text{lumens}] \times [\text{dimensionless}] \times [\text{dimensionless}]} \\
&= \frac{[\text{lm/m}^2] \times [\text{m}^2]}{[\text{lm}]} \\
&= \frac{[\text{lm}]}{[\text{lm}]} \\
&= \text{dimensionless} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This dimensional check is a *favourite KTU 2-mark question*. Always verify units to confirm correctness.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Full Selection Procedure

**Problem (KTU-Standard):**
> A classroom has dimensions $L = 12$ m, $W = 8$ m, total ceiling height $H = 3.5$ m. The working plane is at desk height $h_w = 0.85$ m. The room has white ceiling ($\rho_c = 0.7$) and light walls ($\rho_w = 0.5$). The floor is medium ($\rho_f = 0.2$). The lighting designer wants to use a direct-type luminaire (use the sample UF table in Section 2.1.4). Required illuminance is **300 lux**. Each lamp produces 2500 lumens. The environment is clean. Calculate the number of lamps.

### Step 1: Calculate Mounting Height
$$H_m = H - h_w = 3.5 - 0.85 = 2.65 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Room Index
$$RI = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)} = \frac{12 \times 8}{2.65 \times (12 + 8)} = \frac{96}{2.65 \times 20} = \frac{96}{53} = 1.81$$

### Step 3: Round to Nearest Tabulated RI
Standard UF tables use values: 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0
$$RI = 1.81 \rightarrow \text{nearest is 2.0 (closer than 1.5)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For interpolation (not required here), use:
> $$UF_{actual} = UF_{1.5} + \frac{(RI - 1.5)}{(2.0 - 1.5)} \times (UF_{2.0} - UF_{1.5})$$

### Step 4: Read UF from Table
For RI = 2.0, $\rho_c = 0.7$, $\rho_w = 0.5$ → from the sample table in Section 2.1.4:
$$UF = 0.60$$

### Step 5: Select MF
Clean classroom → $MF = 0.80$

### Step 6: Calculate Area
$$A = L \times W = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 7: Apply Lumen Formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times UF \times MF} \\
&= \frac{300 \times 96}{2500 \times 0.60 \times 0.80} \\
&= \frac{28{,}800}{1200} \\
&= 24 \text{ lamps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Practical Layout
- If each luminaire holds 2 lamps, number of luminaires = 24/2 = **12 luminaires**.
- Spacing = arrange as 3 rows × 4 columns.
- Row spacing = 8/3 = 2.67 m; column spacing = 12/4 = 3.0 m.
- Check Spacing-to-Height Ratio: SP/Hm = 3.0/2.65 = 1.13 ✓ (within 0.7–1.5 acceptable range).

### Final Answer
> **24 lamps (12 luminaires of 2 lamps each) required.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Effect of Wrong Reflection Factor

**Problem:** Same classroom, but walls are painted **dark brown** ($\rho_w = 0.1$ instead of 0.5). Recalculate.

### Modified Step 4:
For RI = 2.0, $\rho_c = 0.7$, $\rho_w = 0.1$ → from the sample table:
$$UF = 0.48$$

### Modified Step 7:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{300 \times 96}{2500 \times 0.48 \times 0.80} \\
&= \frac{28{,}800}{960} \\
&= 30 \text{ lamps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analysis
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Changing $\rho_w$ from 0.5 → 0.1 (white walls to dark walls) increased the lamp requirement from **24 to 30** — a **25% jump**! This shows that painting walls a darker colour can have severe cost implications. Designers should *coordinate with architects* on interior colours before fixing the lighting design.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Dirty Environment Effect

**Problem:** Same classroom, but it's actually a **cement factory control room** — dusty environment with quarterly cleaning.

### Modified Step 5:
Cement factory → very dirty → $MF = 0.50$

### Modified Step 7 (using UF = 0.60 from RI 2.0, white ceiling, light walls):
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{300 \times 96}{2500 \times 0.60 \times 0.50} \\
&= \frac{28{,}800}{750} \\
&= 38.4 \rightarrow 39 \text{ lamps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Forgetting to apply MF (taking MF = 1.0) gives $N = 19.2 → 20$ lamps. The difference of 19 lamps means the room will be **40% under-lit** after one year of dust accumulation — a serious code violation and ergonomics failure.

---

## 3.6 Sensitivity Summary Table — How Each Factor Affects N

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This table is the "single-image summary" of the entire topic for KTU 2-mark conceptual questions.

| Change | Effect on UF | Effect on N | Designer Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Larger room (↑RI) | ↑ | ↓ | Re-tabulate UF |
| White ceiling vs dark | ↑ | ↓ | Specify ceiling colour |
| Light walls vs dark | ↑ | ↓ | Coordinate with architect |
| Increase mounting height (↑Hm) | ↓ | ↑ | Use higher-output lamps |
| Cleaner environment (↑MF) | — | ↓ | Schedule maintenance |
| Use indirect luminaire | ↓ (typically) | ↑ | Trade-off with aesthetics |
| Higher lamp flux (↑φ) | — | ↓ | Capital cost trade-off |

---

## 3.7 Exhaustive Numerical Verification

To double-check the formula consistency, derive $E$ given $N$, and verify the inverse:

Given: $N = 24$, $\phi = 2500$ lm, $UF = 0.60$, $MF = 0.80$, $A = 96$ m²

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{N \times \phi \times UF \times MF}{A} \\
&= \frac{24 \times 2500 \times 0.60 \times 0.80}{96} \\
&= \frac{24 \times 2500 \times 0.48}{96} \\
&= \frac{24 \times 1200}{96} \\
&= \frac{28{,}800}{96} \\
&= 300 \text{ lux} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The round-trip consistency is confirmed.
