# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define Utilisation Factor and Maintenance Factor. State one factor on which each of them depends.**

#### Model Answer:

**Utilisation Factor (UF):** It is defined as the ratio of the total luminous flux (in lumens) reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps.

$$UF = \frac{\text{Lumens reaching the working plane}}{\text{Total lumens emitted by lamps}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity, always less than 1.

**Dependence:** UF depends primarily on **(i) Room Index (RI)** — a function of room length, width, and mounting height, and **(ii) the reflection factors of the ceiling and walls**.

**Maintenance Factor (MF):** It is defined as the ratio of the illuminance on the working plane after a certain period of lamp use (allowing for depreciation) to the initial illuminance when the installation was new.

$$MF = \frac{\text{Illuminance after depreciation}}{\text{Initial illuminance (when new)}}$$

**Dependence:** MF depends primarily on **(i) the environment cleanliness (dust level)** and **(ii) the cleaning and lamp replacement schedule**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do **not** write "UF is the ratio of useful light to total light" without specifying "reaching the working plane." Many students lose 1 mark for missing the word "working plane." Similarly, mention *at least one dependence* for the second mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: What is Reflection Factor? State typical reflection factor values for (i) a white ceiling and (ii) a light-coloured wall.**

#### Model Answer:

**Reflection Factor (ρ):** It is the ratio of the luminous flux reflected by a surface to the luminous flux incident upon it.

$$\rho = \frac{\text{Lumens reflected}}{\text{Lumens incident}}$$

It is dimensionless, expressed as a decimal or percentage.

**Typical values:**
- **(i) White ceiling:** $\rho_c \approx 0.70$ to $0.80$ (typically taken as **0.70** in design).
- **(ii) Light-coloured wall:** $\rho_w \approx 0.50$ to $0.70$ (typically taken as **0.50**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Always state the range and the design value separately. Saying only "white ceiling = 0.7" without context is incomplete. Mention that these are *typical assumed design values* based on BIS / CIE standards.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks per question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **one choice only** out of the two.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern]

**Q (A): (a)** Define Utilisation Factor. Explain in detail the procedure for selecting the Utilisation Factor for interior lighting design. List any four factors on which UF depends. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A room of size $10 \text{ m} \times 6 \text{ m} \times 3.5 \text{ m}$ (L × W × H) is to be illuminated to an average illuminance of 200 lux. The working plane is at 0.85 m above floor. Using fluorescent lamps of 3000 lumens each, mounted 0.5 m below ceiling, with a direct-type luminaire, find the number of lamps required. Assume the following data from the manufacturer's UF table for RI = 1.5, $\rho_c = 0.7$, $\rho_w = 0.5$: UF = 0.55. Take MF = 0.8. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) Model Answer:

**Definition (2 marks):** Utilisation Factor is the ratio of lumens reaching the working plane to the lumens emitted by the lamps. (UF ≤ 1, dimensionless.)

**Selection Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Calculate the **mounting height** $H_m$ above the working plane.
2. Calculate the **Room Index** $RI = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$.
3. Round the RI to the **nearest standard tabulated value** (0.6, 0.8, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0).
4. Identify the **reflection factors** of ceiling and walls based on surface colour (e.g., white ceiling → 0.7, light wall → 0.5).
5. Refer to the **manufacturer's UF table** for the chosen luminaire type and read off the UF corresponding to the rounded RI and the reflection factor combination.
6. If the RI lies between two tabulated values, use **linear interpolation**.

**Factors on which UF depends (any 4):**
1. Room Index (geometry of the room)
2. Reflection factors of ceiling, walls, and floor
3. Type of luminaire (direct, semi-direct, indirect, general diffuse)
4. Suspension / mounting height of the luminaire
5. Distribution of light (spill, glare control)
6. Colour of the lamp and surrounding surfaces

### Part (b) Numerical Solution:

**Step 1: Mounting Height**
The luminaire is 0.5 m below the ceiling. So, height of luminaire above floor = $3.5 - 0.5 = 3.0$ m.
$$H_m = 3.0 - 0.85 = 2.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Room Index**
$$RI = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)} = \frac{10 \times 6}{2.15 \times (10 + 6)} = \frac{60}{2.15 \times 16} = \frac{60}{34.4} = 1.744$$

**Step 3: Selection from given data**
The problem *directly* provides UF for RI = 1.5, $\rho_c = 0.7$, $\rho_w = 0.5$: **UF = 0.55**.

**Step 4: Lumen Formula**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times UF \times MF} \\
&= \frac{200 \times (10 \times 6)}{3000 \times 0.55 \times 0.8} \\
&= \frac{200 \times 60}{3000 \times 0.44} \\
&= \frac{12{,}000}{1320} \\
&= 9.09
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Round up**
$$N = 10 \text{ lamps (rounded up)}$$

**Final Answer:** **10 fluorescent lamps required.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake 1:** Using total ceiling height (3.5 m) as $H_m$ — that gives $H_m = 3.5 - 0.85 = 2.65$ m and $RI = 1.41$, leading to UF mismatch. Always compute $H_m$ from the **luminous centre of the luminaire** to the working plane.
> 
> **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting MF (using MF = 1.0) gives $N = 7.27 → 8$ lamps. The design would be under-lit by 25% within 1 year.
> 
> **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to round *up* to the next integer (using $N = 9$). Always round up because a fraction of a lamp is meaningless and partial lighting will fall short of design illuminance.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q (B): (a)** Define Reflection Factor. With a neat table, list typical reflection factor values for ceiling, walls, and floor under different surface colours. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory hall of $30 \text{ m} \times 15 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$ is to be illuminated to 150 lux. The working plane is at floor level. Use 400 W metal halide lamps, each producing 32,000 lumens, suspended 6 m above the floor. The room has a dark ceiling ($\rho_c = 0.3$) and medium walls ($\rho_w = 0.3$). For RI = 1.5, $\rho_c = 0.3$, $\rho_w = 0.3$, the manufacturer's table gives UF = 0.45. The environment is dirty, with a 6-month cleaning cycle. Determine the number of lamps and the luminaire layout. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) Model Answer:

**Definition (2 marks):** Reflection Factor (ρ) is the ratio of luminous flux reflected by a surface to the luminous flux incident on it: $\rho = \Phi_{ref}/\Phi_{inc}$.

**Reflection Factor Table (5 marks):**

| Surface | Colour / Material | Reflection Factor (ρ) |
|---|---|---|
| Ceiling | White paint / plaster | 0.70 – 0.80 |
| Ceiling | Light cream / off-white | 0.60 – 0.70 |
| Ceiling | Medium (light grey, beige) | 0.50 – 0.60 |
| Ceiling | Dark (concrete, dark grey) | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| Walls | White / very light | 0.70 – 0.80 |
| Walls | Light (pale yellow, light blue) | 0.50 – 0.70 |
| Walls | Medium (wood, pastel green) | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| Walls | Dark (red brick, dark wood) | 0.10 – 0.20 |
| Floor | Light (pale tiles) | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| Floor | Medium (concrete) | 0.15 – 0.30 |
| Floor | Dark (asphalt, dark carpet) | 0.05 – 0.10 |

### Part (b) Numerical Solution:

**Step 1: Mounting Height**
Working plane is at floor level, so $h_w = 0$.
Luminaire suspended at 6 m, so $H_m = 6 - 0 = 6$ m.

**Step 2: Room Index**
$$RI = \frac{30 \times 15}{6 \times (30 + 15)} = \frac{450}{6 \times 45} = \frac{450}{270} = 1.67$$

**Step 3: Use given UF** = 0.45 (the value for RI = 1.5 is closest; for higher precision, interpolation would be done).

**Step 4: Select MF**
Dirty environment, 6-monthly cleaning → **MF = 0.60**

**Step 5: Lumen Formula**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times UF \times MF} \\
&= \frac{150 \times (30 \times 15)}{32{,}000 \times 0.45 \times 0.60} \\
&= \frac{150 \times 450}{32{,}000 \times 0.27} \\
&= \frac{67{,}500}{8640} \\
&= 7.81
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Round up**
$$N = 8 \text{ lamps}$$

**Step 7: Luminaire Layout (Assume 1 lamp per luminaire for metal halide)**

For uniform lighting, the **Spacing-to-Height ratio (SP/Hm)** should be ≈ 1.0 to 1.5.
- Maximum allowable spacing: $1.0 \times 6 = 6$ m (conservative) to $1.5 \times 6 = 9$ m (for wide distribution).

Try a 2 × 4 grid:
- 2 rows × 4 columns = 8 luminaires ✓
- Row spacing: 15/2 = 7.5 m; Column spacing: 30/4 = 7.5 m.
- SP = 7.5 m. SP/Hm = 7.5/6 = 1.25 ✓ (within acceptable range).

**Final Answer:** **8 metal halide lamps**, arranged in a **2 × 4 grid** with 7.5 m spacing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake 1:** Using $h_w = 0.85$ m (typical office desk) when working plane is at floor level — this would reduce $H_m$ from 6 m to 5.15 m and change $RI$ from 1.67 to 1.94, with cascading effects on UF.
> 
> **Common Mistake 2:** Choosing MF = 0.8 for a "dirty factory" — that violates the CIE standard and would be a serious design flaw.
> 
> **Common Mistake 3:** Not verifying the Spacing-to-Height ratio — KTU examiners often award 1 mark for "layout check" or "SP/Hm ratio verification."

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic (Consolidated Pitfall List):**
> 
> 1. **Mounting height error (most common, 2 marks):** Using $H_m = H$ (ceiling-to-floor) instead of $H - h_w$. The mounting height is from the **luminous centre of the luminaire** to the **working plane**, *not* from the ceiling.
> 2. **RI calculation error (1 mark):** Forgetting the sum $(L+W)$ in the denominator, writing $RI = L \times W / H_m$ instead of the correct formula.
> 3. **Missing MF (1–2 marks):** Using MF = 1.0 silently. Always state and use MF explicitly.
> 4. **Rounding error (1 mark):** Rounding N *down* to 9 or 7. Always round *up*.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency (0.5–1 mark):** Mixing lux with foot-candles, or metres with feet. Use SI throughout.
> 6. **Ignoring reflection factors (1 mark):** Picking a UF without specifying $\rho_c$ and $\rho_w$. Show the table lookup clearly.
> 7. **Forgetting to layout (1 mark in (b) parts):** KTU 7-mark numerical parts usually expect *both* lamp count and a layout suggestion or SP/Hm check.

---

## 5.4 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for the Night Before the Exam)**
> 
> ✅ **The Lumen Method equation** $N = \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times UF \times MF}$ — memorize verbatim.
> 
> ✅ **Room Index** $RI = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L+W)}$ — note the $(L+W)$ sum, *not* $L \times W$.
> 
> ✅ **Mounting height** $H_m$ is from the **luminaire's luminous centre** to the **working plane**.
> 
> ✅ **Reflection Factor** is dimensionless, $\rho \in [0, 1]$. White ceiling ≈ 0.7, light wall ≈ 0.5, medium floor ≈ 0.2 are the design default values.
> 
> ✅ **Maintenance Factor** is *always* less than 1. Default: 0.8 (clean), 0.7 (normal industrial), 0.6 (dirty), 0.5 (very dirty).
> 
> ✅ **UF increases with RI** (better geometry for light) and **with higher reflection factors** (more bounce).
> 
> ✅ **MF decreases with time** (dust, aging, dirt). The design uses the *worst-case* MF (at the end of the maintenance cycle), not the *initial* MF.
> 
> ✅ **Always round N up** to the next integer; partial lamps are not allowed.
> 
> ✅ **Always verify SP/Hm** ratio is in the 0.7–1.5 range for uniform illumination.
> 
> ✅ **Three causes of light loss** the design must account for: (a) geometric loss (captured by UF), (b) surface absorption (captured by ρ), (c) time-based depreciation (captured by MF).
> 
> ✅ **UF is *not* the same as efficacy.** UF is the fraction of emitted light reaching the work plane; efficacy (lm/W) is the light output per unit electrical power. Do not confuse them.
> 
> ✅ **Maintenance Factor is *not* the same as Utilisation Factor.** UF is fixed by geometry/surfaces; MF is fixed by maintenance policy/environment. Conflating them is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 
> ✅ **Reflection Factor depends on surface, not on lamp.** It is purely a property of the room's interior finish.
> 
> ✅ **The three factors are *multiplied*, not added**, in the denominator of the lumen formula: $N = \frac{E \times A}{\phi \times (UF) \times (MF)}$.
> 
> ✅ **KSB standard reference values (BIS 3646 / CIE S 007):** White ceiling 0.7, light walls 0.5, medium floor 0.2 — memorize for KTU multiple-choice and short-answer questions.
> 
> ✅ **If the problem provides a UF table directly**, use it. If it provides a generic table, look up the row matching the rounded RI and the column matching the $(\rho_c, \rho_w)$ combination.
> 
> ✅ **Photometric units reminder:** 1 lux = 1 lumen/m². 1 foot-candle = 10.76 lux. KTU uses SI (lux, lumens, metres) — convert any imperial data before computation.
> 
> ✅ **Last-step check:** Always verify dimensional consistency of the final answer — $N$ should be dimensionless and a positive integer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Wattage of Each Lamp and Number of Lamps Needed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Wattage of Each Lamp and Number of Lamps Needed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **calculation of wattage of each lamp and number of lamps needed** refers to the systematic photometric engineering procedure used in interior lighting design to determine, for a given area and required illumination level, either (a) the total number of luminaires of a fixed wattage that must be installed, or (b) the wattage rating of each individual lamp when the number of fixtures is pre-decided. This procedure is formally known as the **Lumen Method of Lighting Design** and is governed by the inverse relationship between the luminous flux emitted by a source and the illuminance delivered at the working plane, modified by two critical practical factors: the **Utilization Factor (UF)** and the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Illumination design is the process of computing the lumens required on the working plane, dividing by the lumen output and the practical efficiency factors (UF × MF), and then converting the result into a number of lamps (or a wattage per lamp) using the **luminous efficacy** of the chosen lamp technology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Filling a Bathtub with Water"

Imagine you are filling a rectangular bathtub to a required water depth (this is your **illumination level, E in lux**). The bathtub area is your **floor area, A in m²**. The total water you need equals **E × A (lumens)**. Now, your tap (the lamp) does not pour all its water straight into the tub — some splashes on the walls and outside; this loss is captured by the **Utilization Factor (UF)**. Also, after a few months, the tap gets partially clogged with dust, so flow reduces; this is the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**. The actual useful water per tap is **tap-flow × UF × MF**. Divide the total water required by the useful water per tap, and you get the **number of taps (lamps)** needed. Conversely, if your boss tells you to use exactly *N* taps, then each tap's flow rate (wattage × efficacy) is fixed by simple division.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Metrics to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Luminous Flux (Φ):** measured in **lumens (lm)**
> - **Illuminance (E):** measured in **lux (lx)**, where **1 lx = 1 lm/m²**
> - **Luminous Efficacy (η):** measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)**
> - **Luminous Intensity (I):** measured in **candela (cd)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Lumen Method Design

The entire design philosophy rests on three multiplicative reductions between the *theoretical* lumens and the *practical* lumens reaching the worker's desk:

1. **Theoretical Total Lumens Required** — Based purely on geometry and the desired lux level.
2. **Loss due to Room Geometry and Luminaire Distribution** — Captured by the **Utilization Factor (UF)**.
3. **Loss due to Ageing, Dust, and Lamp Lumen Depreciation** — Captured by the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**.

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — Theoretical Flux Required

The illuminance equation is fundamentally:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A}$$

Rearranging for the total flux that must reach the working plane:

$$\Phi_{\text{required}} = E \times A$$

where *A* is the area of the working plane in square metres. This is the *useful* flux, not the flux generated by the lamps.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Utilization Factor (UF)

**Utilization Factor** is defined as the ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps. It is **always less than 1** and depends on:

- Room index (K) — a function of room length, width, and mounting height
- Reflection factors of ceiling, walls, and floor
- Luminaire type (direct, indirect, semi-direct)
- Suspension height of the luminaire

$$\text{UF} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{at working plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{emitted by lamps}}}$$

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — Maintenance Factor (MF)

**Maintenance Factor** accounts for the gradual reduction in illuminance over time due to:

- Lamp lumen depreciation (LLD)
- Luminaire dirt depreciation (LDD)
- Room surface dirt depreciation
- Lamp burn-out

$$\text{MF} = \frac{\text{Illumination after depreciation}}{\text{Illumination when new}} = \frac{1}{\text{Depreciation Factor (DF)}}$$

Typical MF values: 0.8 for clean offices, 0.7 for industrial, 0.6 for dirty environments.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total useful lumens required | $\Phi_{req} = E \times A$ | lumens (lm) | — |
| 2 | Number of lamps (N) | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\eta \times W \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 3 | Wattage per lamp (W) | $W = \dfrac{E \times A}{N \times \eta \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ | watts (W) | — |
| 4 | Lumen output per lamp | $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = \eta \times W$ | lumens (lm) | — |
| 5 | Depreciation Factor | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{MF}}$ | dimensionless | 1.25 to 1.67 |
| 6 | Room Index | $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$ | dimensionless | 0.6 to 5.0 |
| 7 | Spacing to Height Ratio | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{\text{Spacing}}{\text{Mounting height above working plane}}$ | dimensionless | 0.5 to 1.5 |
| 8 | Inverse Square Law | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ | lux | — |
| 9 | Cosine Law (Lambert) | $E_h = E \times \cos\theta$ | lux | — |
| 10 | Mean Spherical Candle Power | $\text{MSCP} = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi}$ | candela (cd) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - *E* = Required illuminance in **lux**
> - *A* = Area in **m²**
> - *η* = Luminous efficacy in **lm/W**
> - *W* = Lamp wattage in **W**
> - *L, W, Hₘ* = Length, Width, Mounting height in metres
> - *I* = Luminous intensity in **cd**
> - *d* = Distance from source to point in **metres**
> - *θ* = Angle of incidence

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design procedure is the **foundation of every indoor lighting installation** — from a 5 m × 5 m reading room in a Kerala school to a 100 m × 50 m industrial shop floor in a Kochi shipyard. The method is also used in street lighting, stadium floodlighting (after modification for beam spread), and office compliance audits for the National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) and the Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 3646). Without this calculation, a designer would either grossly over-light (wasting energy) or dangerously under-light (causing eye-strain and accidents).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Lumen-Method Equation

The derivation begins with the fundamental illuminance definition and proceeds by introducing the practical correction factors in sequence.

### Step 1 — Start from the basic flux–illuminance relationship

We know that illuminance equals flux per unit area. Multiplying both sides by the area gives the flux that **must reach** the working plane:

$$E = \frac{\Phi_{\text{useful}}}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi_{\text{useful}} = E \times A$$

### Step 2 — Account for the Utilization Factor

The lumens actually *emitted* by the lamps must be greater than the useful lumens by a factor of 1/UF, because UF represents the fraction that reaches the work plane:

$$\Phi_{\text{emitted}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{useful}}}{\text{UF}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF}}$$

### Step 3 — Account for the Maintenance Factor

After lamp ageing and dirt accumulation, the output drops to a fraction MF of its original value. To guarantee the *design* illuminance throughout the lamp's life, the *initial* flux must be higher by a factor of 1/MF:

$$\Phi_{\text{initial}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{emitted}}}{\text{MF}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Number of Lamps

If each lamp produces *η × W* lumens (wattage × efficacy), then the number of lamps is:

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{\text{initial}}}{\Phi_{\text{per lamp}}} = \frac{E \times A}{\eta \times W \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$$

This is the **master equation** of the lumen method. It can be algebraically rearranged to solve for **wattage per lamp** when the number of lamps is fixed.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A machine shop of dimensions **30 m × 15 m** is to be illuminated to a level of **150 lux**. The utilization factor is **0.5** and the maintenance factor is **0.8**. **Find (a)** the number of lamps required, and **(b)** the wattage per lamp, if each lamp has a luminous efficacy of **20 lm/W**. Also determine a suitable lamp layout using a space-to-height ratio of **1.0**, with a mounting height of **5 m** above the working plane.

### Solution — Part (a): Number of Lamps

**Step A.1 — Identify the given data**

$$E = 150 \text{ lx}, \quad A = 30 \times 15 = 450 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\text{UF} = 0.5, \quad \text{MF} = 0.8, \quad \eta = 20 \text{ lm/W}$$

**Step A.2 — Compute the total useful lumens required**

$$\Phi_{\text{useful}} = E \times A = 150 \times 450 = 67\,500 \text{ lm}$$

**Step A.3 — Compute the total initial lumens needed (applying UF and MF)**

$$\Phi_{\text{initial}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}} = \frac{67\,500}{0.5 \times 0.8} = \frac{67\,500}{0.4} = 168\,750 \text{ lm}$$

**Step A.4 — Assume a standard lamp wattage and compute lumen per lamp**

Since the problem does not fix *W* in part (a), we adopt a commonly available **500 W lamp**:

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = \eta \times W = 20 \times 500 = 10\,000 \text{ lm per lamp}$$

**Step A.5 — Compute the number of lamps**

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{\text{initial}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}} = \frac{168\,750}{10\,000} = 16.875 \approx 17 \text{ lamps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Rule:** Always round **up** the number of lamps to the next whole number. Never round down — under-lighting violates safety standards.

### Solution — Part (b): Wattage per Lamp (Assuming 20 Lamps)

**Step B.1 — Re-arrange the master equation to solve for W**

$$W = \frac{E \times A}{N \times \eta \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$$

**Step B.2 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$W = \frac{150 \times 450}{20 \times 20 \times 0.5 \times 0.8} = \frac{67\,500}{160} = 421.875 \text{ W}$$

**Step B.3 — Select the nearest standard commercially available rating**

The closest standard incandescent/halogen wattage is **500 W**.

$$W_{\text{standard}} = 500 \text{ W}$$

### Solution — Layout of Lamps

**Step L.1 — Compute maximum allowable spacing using SHR**

$$\text{Spacing} = \text{SHR} \times H_m = 1.0 \times 5 = 5 \text{ m}$$

**Step L.2 — Compute number of lamps along length (30 m direction)**

$$n_L = \frac{30}{5} + 1 = 7 \text{ lamps per row}$$

**Step L.3 — Compute number of rows across width (15 m direction)**

$$n_W = \frac{15}{5} + 1 = 4 \text{ rows}$$

**Step L.4 — Total lamps in this layout**

$$N_{\text{layout}} = 7 \times 4 = 28 \text{ lamps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This count (28) exceeds the minimum computed (17), confirming that the design satisfies the illumination requirement with an additional uniformity margin — a professionally acceptable practice in KTU-evaluated problems.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Calculate *N* vs *W*

| Scenario | Given | Find | Equation to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** (Standard Design) | Lamp wattage *W* fixed by catalogue | Number of lamps *N* | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\eta \times W \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ |
| **Case 2** (Retrofit/Modular Layout) | Number of lamps *N* fixed by ceiling grid | Wattage per lamp *W* | $W = \dfrac{E \times A}{N \times \eta \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ |
| **Case 3** (Lumen-Budget Audit) | Floor area *A* and existing *N, W* | Check achieved illuminance $\bar{E}$ | $\bar{E} = \dfrac{N \times \eta \times W \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}{A}$ |
| **Case 4** (High Bay Verification) | Point-by-point *E* required at a specific point | Distance *d* from a single lamp | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{I \times \cos^3\theta}{E}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Lumen-Method Design Process

The diagram below maps the decision flow, the input parameters, the two principal outputs (N and W), and the iteration loop that a lighting designer follows. It replaces a physical lighting layout drawing, which cannot be rendered in Mermaid, with a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as mandated by the engine protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Brief] --> B[Identify Room Dimensions L x W x H]
    B --> C[Compute Floor Area A]
    C --> D[Select Required Illuminance E from IS 3646 Table]
    D --> E{Designer Decision}
    E -- Fixed Wattage --> F[Compute Lumen per Lamp phi = eta x W]
    E -- Fixed Number --> F2[Skip phi step]
    F --> G[Apply Utilization Factor UF from Room Index Table]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Apply Maintenance Factor MF from Environment]
    H --> I{What is being Computed}
    I -- Number of Lamps N --> J[Apply Master Equation N = E x A divided by phi x UF x MF]
    I -- Wattage per Lamp W --> K[Apply Master Equation W = E x A divided by N x eta x UF x MF]
    J --> L[Round N up to Next Integer]
    K --> M[Round W up to Next Standard Rating]
    L --> N[Compute Spacing using SHR]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Generate Layout Grid nL x nW]
    O --> P[Verify Total N in Layout equals Required N]
    P -- Yes --> Q[Final Design Report]
    P -- No --> R[Iterate Adjust N or W]
    R --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Photometric Relations

The following block diagram shows how luminous flux, intensity, and illuminance are inter-linked and how the two practical factors modulate the system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Power W in W] --> B[Lamp with Efficacy eta lm per W]
    B --> C[Total Luminous Flux Phi in lm]
    C --> D[Utilization Factor UF less than 1]
    D --> E[Useful Flux on Working Plane]
    E --> F[Maintenance Factor MF less than 1]
    F --> G[Actual Delivered Illuminance E in lux]
    G --> H[Working Plane Area A in m2]
    H --> I[Verified Illumination Level]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Lighting Layout (Schematic)

Although the actual elevation cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the following **functional cross-section block diagram** represents the geometry that the room index *K* and the SHR depend on.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ceilingBlock[Ceiling Plane]
        C1[Luminaire 1] 
        C2[Luminaire 2]
        C3[Luminaire 3]
        C4[Luminaire Nth]
    end
    subgraph suspensionBlock[Suspension]
        S1[Mounting Height Hm]
    end
    subgraph workingBlock[Working Plane]
        W1[Desk / Machine Height = 0.85 m]
        W2[Illuminance E in lux]
    end
    ceilingBlock --> suspensionBlock
    suspensionBlock --> workingBlock
```

> [!NOTE]
> The variables in the diagrams use raw uppercase alphanumeric characters inside the quoted labels to comply with the Mermaid label formatting restriction. No bold, italic, or HTML tags appear inside the label text.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define the following terms with their standard units: (i) Luminous Flux, (ii) Luminous Efficacy, (iii) Maintenance Factor.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Luminous Flux (Φ):** The total quantity of light energy emitted per second by a source in all directions. It is measured in **lumens (lm)**.
2. **Luminous Efficacy (η):** The ratio of luminous flux emitted by a lamp to the electrical power consumed by it. It is measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)** and indicates how efficiently a lamp converts electricity into visible light.
3. **Maintenance Factor (MF):** The ratio of the illumination on the working plane under practical operating conditions (with lamp ageing and dirt accumulation) to the illumination when the installation is new and clean. It is dimensionless and is the **reciprocal of the Depreciation Factor**.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**What is the Utilization Factor in interior lighting design? Mention any four factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Utilization Factor (UF)** is defined as the ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps. It is dimensionless and always less than unity. The four factors on which UF depends are:

1. **Room Index (K)** — a function of room length, width, and mounting height above the working plane.
2. **Reflection factors** of the ceiling, walls, and working plane (floor/desk surface).
3. **Type of luminaire** — direct, semi-direct, indirect, or general diffuse.
4. **Suspension height** of the luminaire above the working plane.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY:**
> Students frequently lose **2 to 3 marks** on this topic for the following mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting the Maintenance Factor (MF)** in the master equation, leading to a value of *N* that is 20–40% too small.
> 2. **Using luminous intensity (candela) directly in the illuminance equation** instead of converting to flux via the steradian or using the point-by-point method.
> 3. **Rounding N down** instead of up, which is a serious safety and standards violation.
> 4. **Confusing Depreciation Factor with Maintenance Factor** — they are reciprocals.
> 5. **Not stating the units clearly** — KTU examiners specifically deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit in a derivation.

---

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A drawing hall of dimensions **20 m × 12 m × 4.5 m** is to be illuminated at **200 lux**. Using **5 fluorescent lamps per row**, each of wattage **80 W** and efficacy **50 lm/W**, calculate the number of rows required. Take UF = 0.6 and MF = 0.85. The lamps are mounted 3 m above the working plane.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the above design, determine a suitable lamp spacing using a space-to-height ratio of 1.2 and sketch the layout. Also calculate the total connected load in kW.

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Compute floor area**

$$A = 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute total useful lumens required**

$$\Phi_{\text{useful}} = E \times A = 200 \times 240 = 48\,000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total initial lumens needed**

$$\Phi_{\text{initial}} = \frac{48\,000}{0.6 \times 0.85} = \frac{48\,000}{0.51} = 94\,117.6 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute lumens per lamp**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 50 \times 80 = 4\,000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute total number of lamps required**

$$N_{\text{total}} = \frac{94\,117.6}{4\,000} = 23.53 \approx 24 \text{ lamps}$$

**Step 6 — Compute number of rows with 5 lamps per row**

$$N_{\text{rows}} = \frac{24}{5} = 4.8 \Rightarrow \text{adopt 5 rows}$$

This results in a total of **5 × 5 = 25 lamps**, which comfortably exceeds the minimum requirement.

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Maximum spacing from SHR**

$$S = \text{SHR} \times H_m = 1.2 \times 3 = 3.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Number of lamps along the 20 m length**

$$n_L = \frac{20}{3.6} + 1 = 5.55 + 1 \approx 6 \text{ positions per row}$$

**Step 3 — Number of rows across the 12 m width**

$$n_W = \frac{12}{3.6} + 1 = 3.33 + 1 \approx 4 \text{ rows}$$

**Step 4 — Total lamps in layout**

$$N_{\text{layout}} = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ lamps}$$

**Step 5 — Total connected load**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 24 \times 80 = 1\,920 \text{ W} = 1.92 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A workshop measuring **40 m × 20 m** requires an illumination level of **100 lux**. The lamps used are 200 W incandescent lamps with a luminous efficacy of **15 lm/W**. If the utilization factor is **0.5** and the maintenance factor is **0.75**, calculate the number of lamps required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same area is to be illuminated using **40 LED lamps** of efficacy **80 lm/W** to achieve the same illuminance, determine the wattage of each LED lamp. Comment on the energy savings.

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 — Compute floor area**

$$A = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute total useful lumens**

$$\Phi_{\text{useful}} = 100 \times 800 = 80\,000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total initial lumens**

$$\Phi_{\text{initial}} = \frac{80\,000}{0.5 \times 0.75} = \frac{80\,000}{0.375} = 213\,333.3 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute lumens per 200 W incandescent lamp**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 15 \times 200 = 3\,000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute number of lamps**

$$N = \frac{213\,333.3}{3\,000} = 71.11 \approx 72 \text{ lamps}$$

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Master equation rearranged for W**

$$W = \frac{E \times A}{N \times \eta \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$W = \frac{100 \times 800}{40 \times 80 \times 0.5 \times 0.75} = \frac{80\,000}{1\,200} = 66.67 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Select the nearest standard LED rating**

$$W_{\text{LED}} = 70 \text{ W (standard commercial rating)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total power in both cases**

$$P_{\text{incandescent}} = 72 \times 200 = 14\,400 \text{ W} = 14.4 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{\text{LED}} = 40 \times 70 = 2\,800 \text{ W} = 2.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Energy savings**

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{14.4 - 2.8}{14.4} \times 100\% = 80.56\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** Replacing 72 × 200 W incandescent lamps with 40 × 70 W LED lamps yields a **~80% reduction in connected load** for the *same* illuminance. This explains why the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) and KEREA (Kerala State Electricity Board) actively promote LED retrofits in industrial installations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **One-Glance Revision for KTU University Exam Day:**

- The **master lumen-method equation** is the **only** equation to memorize — write it three times before entering the exam hall.
- Always apply **UF first, then MF**; both factors are less than 1, so they make the denominator smaller and the lamp count **larger**.
- The **Utilization Factor** depends on **room index, reflection factors, luminaire type, and suspension height** — these four phrases will fetch 2 marks if asked as a short note.
- The **Maintenance Factor** is the **reciprocal of the Depreciation Factor** — never mix them up in formula substitution.
- **Space-to-Height Ratio (SHR)** × mounting height above working plane = maximum spacing between luminaires. Always use **Hₘ = suspension height − working plane height**.
- For layouts, the number of lamps along a length is **(Length / Spacing) + 1** — the +1 is **non-negotiable** for end-fixings.
- The unit **lux = lumen/m²** — converting between flux and illuminance **always** requires multiplication or division by area.
- **Round up** the number of lamps, **round up** the wattage to the next standard rating — this is the universally accepted engineering convention.
- For **part (b) of any 14-mark question**, always include a **comment on energy savings or design choice** to gain the full 7 marks.
- The **efficacy order** for common lamps is: Incandescent (10–15 lm/W) < Halogen (15–20) < Fluorescent (50–60) < CFL (60–70) < LED (80–150) — high-efficacy lamps are the modern KTU-preferred answer in design questions.
- Always carry the **unit** through every line of the derivation — it earns a half-mark per step in the KTU valuation key.
- For **point-illuminance checks**, use **E = (I × cos θ) / d²** and remember the **cos³θ law** for inclined surfaces.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Calculation of Wattage of Each Lamp and Number of Lamps Needed**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout of Lamp Luminaire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Layout of Lamp Luminaire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Luminaire** is a complete lighting unit consisting of a **lamp** (light source) together with the parts designed to distribute the light, position and protect the lamp, and connect the lamp to the power supply. The **Layout of a Lamp Luminaire** refers to the systematic physical and optical arrangement of its internal components — namely the **lamp holder, reflector, diffuser/lens, ballast/choke (for discharge lamps), housing, and mounting arrangement** — such that the desired photometric performance (luminous intensity distribution, beam spread, glare control, and efficiency) is achieved.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the luminaire layout is classified based on the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) distribution type** and the **flux fraction ratio** — the proportion of total luminous flux emitted above and below the horizontal plane passing through the luminaire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):**
> The *Layout of Lamp Luminaire* is a foundational topic that bridges lamp technology (Module 2) with lighting design calculations (Module 4/5). Mastery of luminaire classification, optical components, and photometric descriptors is **mandatory** for solving numerical problems on utilization factor (UF), spacing-to-mounting height ratio (SHR), and glare index.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a luminaire like a **fountain sprinkler system**:

- The **lamp** is the *water source* (raw light energy pouring out in all directions).
- The **reflector** is the *curved bowl that directs water* — without it, the water (light) splashes uselessly everywhere. With a parabolic shape, it focuses the water into a precise jet.
- The **diffuser** is the *fine mesh screen* that softens the spray — turning a harsh jet into a gentle, uniform shower.
- The **housing** is the *outer metal body* — protecting internal parts from rain, dust, and mechanical damage.
- The **ballast** (for discharge lamps) is the *pressure regulator* — discharge lamps (like fluorescent tubes) cannot regulate their own current; without a ballast, they would draw unlimited current and burn out instantly, just like a tap without a regulator would burst the pipe.

In short: **Lamp = energy source, Reflector = director, Diffuser = softener, Housing = protector, Ballast = controller.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Terms

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **Lumen (lm)** | Total light output of the lamp/luminaire |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **Candela (cd)** | Flux per unit solid angle in a given direction |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **lm/W** | Lumens produced per watt of electrical input |
| Color Rendering Index | CRI / Ra | **0–100 (dimensionless)** | Ability to render true colors of objects |
| Correlated Color Temperature | CCT | **Kelvin (K)** | Warmth/coolness appearance of light |
| Beam Angle | $\theta$ | **Degrees (°)** | Angular spread of the main light beam |
| Spacing-to-Height Ratio | SHR | **Dimensionless** | Maximum spacing ÷ mounting height |

> [!NOTE]
> The **flux fraction ratio (FFR)** is the central parameter used by KTU examiners to classify luminaire layouts. It is defined as:
> $$\text{FFR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{upper hemisphere}}}{\Phi_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{up}} + \Phi_{\text{down}}}$$
> (Or sometimes the inverse — fraction below — is used, depending on convention. KTU generally uses the **upper-hemisphere fraction**.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Luminaire — Functional Subsystems

A luminaire is not just a "lamp with a shade." It is an engineered system with **six functional sub-systems**, each having a precise role:

**1. Optical System (Light Shaping)**
- **Reflector**: A polished or aluminized surface that redirects light from the lamp. Common shapes:
  - *Parabolic* — produces a parallel/collimated beam (used in spotlights, downlights).
  - *Spherical* — produces a concentrated focal point.
  - *Elliptical* — produces a focused spot at the second focus (used in profile projectors).
  - *Hyperbolic* — produces a diverging beam.
- **Refractor / Lens**: A transparent glass/plastic element that bends light by refraction. Used for **beam control** (e.g., streetlight prismatic refractors).
- **Diffuser**: A translucent element (opal acrylic, frosted glass) that **scatters** light to reduce glare and soften shadows.

**2. Electrical System (Power Conditioning)**
- **Lamp holder / socket**: Mechanical + electrical interface to the lamp (Edison screw, bayonet, pin-type).
- **Ballast** (for HID & fluorescent lamps): Limits current. Types — *electromagnetic* (choke), *electronic* (HF inverter).
- **Starter** (for fluorescent preheat circuits): Initiates ionization.
- **Capacitor** (for power factor correction): Typically improves PF from 0.5 → 0.85+.

**3. Mechanical / Housing System**
- **Body/housing**: Sheet steel, die-cast aluminium, or polycarbonate. Provides structural rigidity and heat dissipation.
- **Mounting arrangement**: Pendant, recessed, surface, track, or pole-mounted.
- **Gasket / sealing**: For IP-rated (Ingress Protection) luminaires used outdoors or in dusty/wet environments.

**4. Thermal Management System**
- **Heat sinks / fins**: Conduct heat away from LED junctions (critical for LED luminaire longevity).
- **Ventilation slots**: Allow convective cooling in HID luminaires.

**5. Control & Auxiliary System**
- **Ignitor** (for HID sodium lamps): Generates a high-voltage pulse (2–5 kV) to start the arc.
- **Dimmers / sensors**: Modern smart luminaires integrate occupancy/daylight sensors.

**6. Aesthetic / Finishing System**
- Painted/powder-coated finish, decorative trims.

---

### 2.2 CIE Classification of Luminaire Layouts (Flux Fraction Method)

The **CIE classifies luminaires** based on the fraction of total luminous flux emitted in the **upper hemisphere** (above the horizontal plane) versus the **lower hemisphere** (below the horizontal plane). This is the most-tested concept in KTU for Module 3.

| CIE Class | Name | Flux in Upper Hemisphere | Flux in Lower Hemisphere | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | All flux downward | 0 – 10% | 90 – 100% | Offices, factories, classrooms |
| **Semi-direct** | Mostly downward | 10 – 40% | 60 – 90% | General indoor lighting, shops |
| **General Diffuse** (or *Direct-Indirect*) | Roughly equal | 40 – 60% | 40 – 60% | Drawing rooms, reception halls |
| **Semi-indirect** | Mostly upward | 60 – 90% | 10 – 40% | Hotels, conference rooms |
| **Indirect** | All flux upward | 90 – 100% | 0 – 10% | Decorative, auditorium walls |
| **Asymmetric** | Directional (one side) | Variable | Variable | Floodlights, wall-washers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams** — the percentages from 0% to 100% in steps of 10% going *upward*:
> **D**irect → **S**emi-**D**irect → **G**eneral **D**iffuse → **S**emi-**I**ndirect → **I**ndirect
> Read it as a "spectrum" of distribution: **D → SD → GD → SI → I**

---

### 2.3 Polar Curve and Photometric Representation

A **polar curve** (also called a *candela distribution curve*) is a 2D plot of **luminous intensity (cd)** versus the **angle from the downward vertical (γ)**. The luminaire is placed at the center, and intensity is plotted as a radial vector at each angle.

- For a **symmetric luminaire**, a single polar curve in any vertical plane suffices.
- For an **asymmetric luminaire**, multiple curves in different planes (C-planes, where C is the azimuthal angle) are required.
- The **solid angle** swept between two intensity vectors gives the **zonal flux**:
  $$\Delta \Phi = \int_{\gamma_1}^{\gamma_2} I(\gamma) \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{luminaire}} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{luminaire}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}}$ | Luminaire efficiency (output vs. bare lamp flux) | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $\text{FFR} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{total}}}$ | Flux fraction ratio (upper hemisphere) | 0 to 1 |
| 3 | $\Delta \Phi = I(\gamma) \cdot \omega$ | Flux in a small solid angle | $\Phi$ in lm, $I$ in cd, $\omega$ in sr |
| 4 | $I(\gamma) = I_0 \cos^n \gamma$ | Lambertian / cosine-power distribution | $n=1$ true Lambertian |
| 5 | $\omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\gamma)$ | Solid angle of a cone of half-angle $\gamma$ | Steradians |
| 6 | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{\text{Spacing between luminaires}}{\text{Mounting height above work plane}}$ | Spacing-to-Height Ratio | Typically 0.5 to 1.5 |
| 7 | $E_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{total}} \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}{A}$ | Average illuminance on work plane | Lux ($\text{lm/m}^2$) |
| 8 | $I = \dfrac{E \cdot d^2}{\cos^3 \gamma}$ (point source approx.) | Inverse square + cosine cube law | $d$ = distance, $E$ in lux |
| 9 | $\text{UF} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{received on work plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{luminaire output}}}$ | Utilization Factor | 0 to 1 (from UF table) |
| 10 | $\text{MF} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSD}$ | Maintenance Factor | Product of 3 sub-factors |

> [!NOTE]
> - **LLD** = Lamp Lumen Depreciation
> - **LDD** = Luminaire Dirt Depreciation
> - **RSD** = Room Surface Dirt Depreciation
> The MF is **always less than 1** (since luminaires and lamps lose efficiency over time).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Indoor office lighting** uses *direct* or *semi-direct* layouts for high task efficiency.
- **Hotel lobbies and atriums** use *indirect* layouts to create soft, glare-free ambiance.
- **Street lighting** uses *asymmetric / cut-off* layouts to throw light onto the road and minimize upward light pollution.
- **Industrial high-bay lighting** uses *direct* luminaires with prismatic reflectors for maximum downward flux.
- **Stage and studio lighting** uses *focusable / profile* luminaires with adjustable beam angles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Luminous Flux from a Polar Curve

**Problem Setup:** Given a symmetric luminaire with luminous intensity distribution $I(\gamma)$ as a function of the polar angle $\gamma$ (measured from the downward vertical), derive the **total luminous flux** emitted by the luminaire.

---

**Step 1 — Define a small zone of the sphere.**

Consider a thin spherical zone between polar angles $\gamma$ and $\gamma + d\gamma$. The solid angle subtended by this zone (full azimuthal revolution) is:
$$d\omega = 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma$$

**Step 2 — Write the flux in this zone.**

Since the intensity $I(\gamma)$ is the flux per unit solid angle, the flux in this small zone is:
$$d\Phi(\gamma) = I(\gamma) \cdot d\omega = I(\gamma) \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the full sphere.**

The total flux emitted is the integral over $\gamma$ from $0$ (straight down) to $\pi$ (straight up):
$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \int_{0}^{\pi} I(\gamma) \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma$$

**Step 4 — Split into upper and lower hemisphere flux.**

$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{\int_{0}^{\pi/2} I(\gamma) \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma}_{\Phi_{\text{down}}} + \underbrace{\int_{\pi/2}^{\pi} I(\gamma) \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma}_{\Phi_{\text{up}}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Flux Fraction Ratio (FFR).**

$$\text{FFR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\int_{\pi/2}^{\pi} I(\gamma) \sin\gamma \, d\gamma}{\int_{0}^{\pi} I(\gamma) \sin\gamma \, d\gamma}$$

This is the master equation KTU uses to classify luminaire layouts.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Classify the Luminaire

**Problem:** A luminaire emits **1,200 lm** in the lower hemisphere and **800 lm** in the upper hemisphere. Classify the luminaire per CIE scheme.

**Step 1 — Compute total flux.**
$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \Phi_{\text{up}} + \Phi_{\text{down}} = 800 + 1200 = 2000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute FFR.**
$$\text{FFR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{total}}} = \frac{800}{2000} = 0.40 \; (\text{or } 40\%)$$

**Step 3 — Apply CIE classification.**

| FFR | Class |
|---|---|
| 0 – 10% | Direct |
| 10 – 40% | **Semi-Direct** |
| 40 – 60% | General Diffuse |
| 60 – 90% | Semi-Indirect |
| 90 – 100% | Indirect |

Since FFR = 40%, the luminaire lies **right at the boundary** between *Semi-Direct* and *General Diffuse*. By the inclusive upper bound convention used in KTU, this is classified as a **General Diffuse luminaire** (since 40% is the upper limit of semi-direct but inclusive of general diffuse by some references). The examiner will accept *Semi-Direct / General Diffuse (boundary case)*.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Cosine-Power Distribution

**Problem:** A luminaire has an intensity distribution $I(\gamma) = I_0 \cos^n \gamma$. Find the total flux if $I_0 = 500$ cd and $n = 2$. What is the FFR?

**Step 1 — Write the flux integral.**
$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \int_{0}^{\pi} I_0 \cos^n\gamma \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma = 2\pi I_0 \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos^n\gamma \sin\gamma \, d\gamma$$

**Step 2 — Substitution.** Let $u = \cos\gamma$, then $du = -\sin\gamma \, d\gamma$. Limits: $u(0)=1$, $u(\pi)=-1$.
$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = -2\pi I_0 \int_{1}^{-1} u^n \, du = 2\pi I_0 \int_{-1}^{1} u^n \, du$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**
$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = 2\pi I_0 \cdot \left[\frac{u^{n+1}}{n+1}\right]_{-1}^{1} = \frac{2\pi I_0}{n+1}\left[1^{n+1} - (-1)^{n+1}\right]$$

For **even** $n$: $(-1)^{n+1} = -1$, so bracket = $1 - (-1) = 2$.
$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \frac{4\pi I_0}{n+1} = \frac{4\pi \times 500}{2+1} = \frac{2000\pi}{3} \approx 2094.4 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute upper-hemisphere flux.**
$$\Phi_{\text{up}} = \int_{\pi/2}^{\pi} I_0 \cos^n\gamma \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma = 2\pi I_0 \int_{-1}^{0} u^n \, du = 2\pi I_0 \cdot \frac{1}{n+1}$$

$$\Phi_{\text{up}} = \frac{2\pi \times 500}{3} \approx 1047.2 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute FFR.**
$$\text{FFR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{total}}} = \frac{2\pi I_0 / (n+1)}{4\pi I_0 / (n+1)} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50 \; (50\%)$$

**Conclusion:** This is a **General Diffuse luminaire** (50% falls in each hemisphere, regardless of $n$ when $n$ is even). The total flux is **2094 lm**.

> [!NOTE]
> For any symmetric cosine-power distribution with **even** $n$, exactly **50% of the flux** goes upward and 50% downward — a classic KTU result.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Luminaire Layouts vs. Applications

| Layout Type | FFR (%) | Glare Level | Visual Comfort | Energy Efficiency | Typical Lamp | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | 0–10 | High (unshaded) | Low | **Very High** (≥90%) | LED panel, fluorescent tube | Factory, warehouse, office |
| **Semi-Direct** | 10–40 | Moderate | Medium | High (75–90%) | Fluorescent with reflector | Classroom, retail shop |
| **General Diffuse** | 40–60 | Low | High | Medium (60–75%) | Opal globe, LED pendant | Drawing room, lobby |
| **Semi-Indirect** | 60–90 | Very Low | Very High | Lower (40–60%) | Cove-light, uplighter | Hotel, conference hall |
| **Indirect** | 90–100 | Negligible | Highest | **Lowest** (30–45%) | Concealed cove, wall washer | Auditorium, decorative |
| **Asymmetric** | Variable | Controlled | Task-specific | Task-optimized | HID floodlight, wall washer | Streetlight, stage, gallery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Luminaire Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
    B --> S[Starter or Ignitor if needed]

    LP --> RF[Reflector Parabolic Elliptical]

    LP --> DF[Diffuser Opal Frosted]

    RF --> HO[Housing Body Heat Sink]
    RF2 --> HO
    DF --> HO

    HO --> OM[Optical Output Beam]

    HO --> MT[Mounting Arrangement]

    MT --> M2[Recessed]
    MT --> M3[Surface]

    MT --> M5[Pole]

    B --> CC[PF Correction Capacitor]

    style LP fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc7a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: CIE Classification Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Measure Phi_up and Phi_down] --> B[Compute FFR = Phi_up or Phi_total]
    B --> C{FFR in 0 to 10 percent}
    C -- Yes --> D1[DIRECT LAYOUT]
    C -- No --> E{FFR in 10 to 40 percent}
    E -- Yes --> D2[SEMI DIRECT LAYOUT]
    E -- No --> F{FFR in 40 to 60 percent}
    F -- Yes --> D3[GENERAL DIFFUSE LAYOUT]
    F -- No --> G{FFR in 60 to 90 percent}
    G -- Yes --> D4[SEMI INDIRECT LAYOUT]
    G -- No --> H{FFR in 90 to 100 percent}
    H -- Yes --> D5[INDIRECT LAYOUT]
    H -- No --> D6[ASYMMETRIC or SPECIAL Layout]

    style D1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style D4 fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D5 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D6 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cross-Sectional Layout of a Typical Downlight Luminaire

```mermaid

        A1[Outer Trim Ring Decorative Bezel] --> A2[Housing Body Sheet Steel or Diecast]
        A2 --> A3[Reflector Parabolic Aluminium]
        A3 --> A4[Lamp Holder E27 or GU10 Socket]
        A4 --> A5[Lamp LED or Halogen Source]
        A5 --> A6[Optional Diffuser or Lens]
        A6 --> A7[Beam Exit Aperture Bottom Opening]
    end

    A7 --> A8[Light Cone Directed onto Work Plane]

    style A5 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc7a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A6 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
*Define the term "Luminaire." List any four essential components of a luminaire used with a discharge lamp.*

**Model Answer:**

A **luminaire** is a complete lighting device that consists of a **lamp** (the light source) along with all the auxiliary parts necessary to **distribute the light, position the lamp, protect it, and connect it to the power supply.**

**Four essential components** (for a discharge-lamp luminaire):

1. **Lamp holder / socket** — to mechanically and electrically connect the discharge lamp.
2. **Ballast (choke)** — to limit the current through the discharge lamp (since discharge lamps have a negative resistance characteristic).
3. **Reflector** — to direct and concentrate the emitted light in the desired direction.
4. **Housing / body** — to enclose and protect the internal components and provide mounting.

*(Alternative valid components: diffuser, ignitor, capacitor for PF correction, refractor lens.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, define "Flux Fraction Ratio" (FFR) and state its significance in classifying luminaire layouts.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Flux Fraction Ratio (FFR)** is the ratio of the luminous flux emitted by a luminaire in the **upper hemisphere** (above the horizontal plane through the luminaire) to the **total luminous flux** emitted by the luminaire.

$$\boxed{\text{FFR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{upper hemisphere}}}{\Phi_{\text{total}}}}$$

**Significance:** The CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) classifies every luminaire into one of five standard layout types — **Direct, Semi-Direct, General Diffuse, Semi-Indirect, or Indirect** — based on its FFR value. This classification directly determines the luminaire's suitability for a given application (e.g., Direct for factories, Indirect for decorative spaces).

*(Sketch: A horizontal dashed line through the luminaire, arrows showing flux going up and flux going down, with the upper flux shaded and labelled $\Phi_{\text{up}}$, total flux $\Phi_{\text{total}}$ shown encompassing both.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain the CIE classification of luminaire layouts in detail. State the FFR range and one typical application for each class.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A luminaire consists of a 100 W incandescent lamp having a luminous efficacy of 12 lm/W. The luminaire efficiency is 80%. The flux distribution data is as follows:*

| Zone (γ in degrees) | $0°$–$60°$ | $60°$–$90°$ | $90°$–$120°$ | $120°$–$180°$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flux (lm) | 480 | 280 | 180 | 20 |

*Calculate:*
*(i) Total flux emitted by the bare lamp.* **(2 marks)**
*(ii) Total flux emitted by the luminaire.* **(1 mark)**
*(iii) The FFR and classify the luminaire.* **(4 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The **CIE classification** is the internationally accepted method (developed by the Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) for grouping luminaires based on how they distribute their luminous flux between the upper and lower hemispheres. The horizontal plane through the luminaire is taken as the reference.

The **Flux Fraction Ratio (FFR)** is defined as:
$$\text{FFR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{up}} + \Phi_{\text{down}}}$$

**The Five CIE Classes:**

| S.No. | Class Name | FFR Range (Upper) | FFR Range (Lower) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Direct** | 0 – 10% | 90 – 100% | Factories, warehouses, offices with louvered fixtures |
| 2 | **Semi-Direct** | 10 – 40% | 60 – 90% | Classrooms, general indoor spaces, retail stores |
| 3 | **General Diffuse** (Direct-Indirect) | 40 – 60% | 40 – 60% | Drawing rooms, lobbies, reception areas |
| 4 | **Semi-Indirect** | 60 – 90% | 10 – 40% | Hotels, conference rooms, restaurants |
| 5 | **Indirect** | 90 – 100% | 0 – 10% | Decorative cove lighting, auditorium walls, art galleries |

**Note:** As the FFR increases, the luminaire progressively shifts from being purely task-oriented (direct, efficient, more glare) to being purely ambient (indirect, low efficiency, high visual comfort).

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Part (i) — Total flux of the bare lamp:**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = \text{Power} \times \text{Efficacy} = 100 \text{ W} \times 12 \text{ lm/W}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 1200 \text{ lm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Total flux emitted by the luminaire:**

The luminaire efficiency $\eta_L$ is the ratio of luminaire output flux to bare-lamp flux:
$$\eta_L = \frac{\Phi_{\text{luminaire}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}} \implies \Phi_{\text{luminaire}} = \eta_L \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}}$$

$$\Phi_{\text{luminaire}} = 0.80 \times 1200$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{\text{luminaire}} = 960 \text{ lm}}$$

**Verification using zone data:** Sum of all zones = $480 + 280 + 180 + 20 = 960$ lm ✓

**Part (iii) — FFR and classification:**

- Flux in **lower hemisphere** (γ = 0° to 90°): $\Phi_{\text{down}} = 480 + 280 = 760$ lm
- Flux in **upper hemisphere** (γ = 90° to 180°): $\Phi_{\text{up}} = 180 + 20 = 200$ lm
- Total flux: $\Phi_{\text{total}} = 760 + 200 = 960$ lm ✓

$$\text{FFR} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{up}}}{\Phi_{\text{total}}} = \frac{200}{960} = 0.2083 \approx 20.83\%$$

Since **20.83% lies in the 10–40% range**, the luminaire is classified as a **SEMI-DIRECT luminaire**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Describe, with a neat block diagram, the various functional subsystems of a typical fluorescent tube luminaire. Explain the role of the ballast and starter in its operation.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A luminaire has an intensity distribution $I(\gamma) = 600 \cos^2 \gamma$ candela. Calculate:* **(7 marks)**
*(i) The total luminous flux emitted.* **(3 marks)**
*(ii) The flux in the lower hemisphere.* **(2 marks)**
*(iii) The luminaire's CIE classification.* **(2 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

A typical fluorescent tube luminaire consists of the following **functional subsystems** (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for visual reference):

**1. Lamp Subsystem**
- A **fluorescent tube** (typically T8 or T5, 36 W or 28 W) coated internally with phosphor. The tube contains mercury vapour at low pressure and an inert gas (argon). It has two **filament electrodes** (cathodes) at each end.

**2. Electrical / Control Subsystem**
- **Ballast (Choke)**: A series inductor that **limits the lamp current** to a safe value. Without it, the lamp's negative resistance would cause runaway current and destroy the tube. Modern electronic ballasts operate at high frequency (20–60 kHz) for flicker-free operation and higher efficacy.
- **Starter**: A small glow-discharge switch (in conventional electromagnetic ballast circuits). When power is switched ON, the starter momentarily **closes the circuit** to preheat the electrodes, then **opens** abruptly. The collapse of current through the ballast inductor induces a **high-voltage spike** (~400–600 V) that ionizes the mercury vapour and strikes the arc in the tube.
- **Power Factor Correction Capacitor**: Connected across the supply to improve PF from ~0.5 (with choke alone) to ~0.85+.

**3. Optical Subsystem**
- **Reflector**: A sheet of polished/anodized aluminium fixed above the tube to direct light downward.
- **Diffuser / Louvre**: A translucent cover or parabolic-cell louvre fitted below the tube to reduce direct glare from the bright tube surface.

**4. Mechanical / Housing Subsystem**
- A **sheet-steel channel body** holds the tube holder, ballast, starter, and reflector. End caps and a wiring compartment complete the assembly.

**5. Mounting Subsystem**
- May be pendant (chain), surface, or recessed mounted.

**Role of Ballast and Starter (Operation Sequence):**
1. **Switch ON** → starter glows, heating the bimetallic electrode → contacts close.
2. **Current flows** through ballast → tube filaments → starter (now closed) → electrodes preheat (~1–2 seconds).
3. **Starter contacts open** → inductive kick from ballast generates a high-voltage pulse → arc strikes inside the tube.
4. **Arc established** → current is now limited by the ballast impedance → tube emits UV → phosphor converts to visible light.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

Given: $I(\gamma) = 600 \cos^2 \gamma$ cd, so $I_0 = 600$ cd, $n = 2$.

**Part (i) — Total luminous flux:**

Using the derived formula (Section 3.3, with even $n$):
$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \frac{4\pi I_0}{n+1} = \frac{4\pi \times 600}{2+1} = \frac{2400\pi}{3} = 800\pi$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{\text{total}} \approx 2513.27 \text{ lm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Lower hemisphere flux:**

$$\Phi_{\text{down}} = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I_0 \cos^2\gamma \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma$$

Substitute $u = \cos\gamma$, $du = -\sin\gamma \, d\gamma$. Limits: $u(0) = 1$, $u(\pi/2) = 0$.

$$\Phi_{\text{down}} = -2\pi I_0 \int_{1}^{0} u^2 \, du = 2\pi I_0 \int_{0}^{1} u^2 \, du = 2\pi I_0 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{3} = 400\pi$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{\text{down}} \approx 1256.64 \text{ lm}}$$

*(Equivalently, since this is an even-power cosine distribution, $\Phi_{\text{down}} = \Phi_{\text{up}} = \Phi_{\text{total}}/2$.)*

**Part (iii) — CIE classification:**

$$\Phi_{\text{up}} = \Phi_{\text{total}} - \Phi_{\text{down}} = 2513.27 - 1256.64 = 1256.64 \text{ lm}$$

$$\text{FFR} = \frac{1256.64}{2513.27} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

Since **FFR = 50% lies in the 40–60% range**, the luminaire is classified as a **GENERAL DIFFUSE luminaire** (Direct-Indirect type).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **FFR Boundary Confusion** — Many students misclassify a luminaire with FFR = 40% or 60%. *Always state the boundary value explicitly and show the calculation.*
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply efficacy by power** — A frequent error is to state "luminous flux = 12 lm" instead of 1200 lm. Always write: $\Phi = P \times \eta$.
> 3. **Confusing upper vs. lower hemisphere** — Some textbooks define FFR with the lower hemisphere; KTU uses the **upper hemisphere** convention. Re-read the question carefully.
> 4. **Mixing up Solid Angle and Plane Angle** — Solid angle is in **steradians (sr)**, plane angle in **radians (rad)** or degrees. Never write $\omega = 2\pi \gamma$.
> 5. **Wrong sign in substitution** — When $u = \cos\gamma$, the limits flip. Forgetting the negative sign gives half the correct answer.
> 6. **Skipping units** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 mark for missing units (e.g., writing "1200" without "lm").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Layout of Lamp Luminaire"**
>
> - **Definition** — A *luminaire* = lamp + auxiliary parts (reflector, diffuser, housing, ballast, holder, etc.).
> - **CIE classification** is based on the **Flux Fraction Ratio (FFR)** = upper-hemisphere flux / total flux.
> - **Five CIE classes with FFR ranges** — Direct (0–10%), Semi-Direct (10–40%), General Diffuse (40–60%), Semi-Indirect (60–90%), Indirect (90–100%).
> - **Key components of a luminaire** — Lamp holder, reflector, refractor/lens, diffuser, ballast, starter (for fluorescent), ignitor (for HID), housing, mounting arrangement, PF correction capacitor.
> - **Reflector types** — Parabolic (collimated), Spherical (focused), Elliptical (second-focus spot), Hyperbolic (diverging).
> - **Luminaire efficiency** $\eta_L = \Phi_{\text{luminaire output}} / \Phi_{\text{bare lamp}}$ — typically 50–90%.
> - **Total flux from polar curve** — $\Phi_{\text{total}} = \int_0^\pi I(\gamma) \cdot 2\pi \sin\gamma \, d\gamma$.
> - **For even-power cosine** $I(\gamma) = I_0 \cos^n \gamma$ (n even) — exactly 50% flux is upward, 50% downward. The luminaire is **always General Diffuse**.
> - **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)** — maximum recommended spacing of luminaires divided by mounting height above the work plane. Typical values: 0.5 (direct) to 1.5 (indirect).
> - **Inverse square law + cosine-cube law** — $I = E \cdot d^2 / \cos^3 \gamma$ — used for point-source illuminance calculations.
> - **Direct layout** is most efficient but most glaring; **Indirect** is least efficient but most comfortable. Trade-off between energy and visual comfort.
> - **Remember the abbreviation** — **FFR** stands for **Flux Fraction Ratio**, not Flux Frequency Ratio.
> - **Units to remember** — Flux in **lm**, Intensity in **cd**, Illuminance in **lux** (= lm/m²), Efficacy in **lm/W**, FFR is **dimensionless**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Space to Mounting Height Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Space to Mounting Height Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Calculation of Space to Mounting Height Ratio

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Space to Mounting Height Ratio (SHR / S/H Ratio)**: It is defined as the ratio of the horizontal distance **S** between the centres of two adjacent luminaires to the vertical **mounting height H** of the luminaires measured from the working plane (usually 0.85 m above the floor in KTU design problems).

$$
SHR \;=\; \frac{S}{H}
$$

The ratio is conventionally expressed in two ways depending on the geometry of the installation:

- **Transverse SHR** — measured across the width of the room (perpendicular to the luminaire axis).
- **Longitudinal SHR** — measured along the length of the room (parallel to the luminaire axis).

For square layouts, only a single value is required; for rectangular layouts, manufacturers quote the pair **SHR Transverse : SHR Longitudinal** (e.g., **1.3 : 1.0**).

> [!NOTE]
> **SHR is NOT a fixed property of a lamp — it is a property of the LUMINAIRE**, because the same lamp fitted inside different reflectors/diffusers will produce a completely different light spread on the working plane.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watering a square lawn with a garden sprinkler mounted on a pole. If the pole is short, the water falls close to the base — you need many sprinklers placed close together to wet the entire lawn. If the pole is tall, the water spreads further, so you can place the sprinklers farther apart. The same physical principle governs light.

- **Mounting height (H)** → how high the "sprinkler head" is.
- **Spacing (S)** → how far apart the sprinklers are.
- **SHR = S/H** → the design rule that guarantees every blade of grass (or every square metre of working plane) receives the **same** amount of water (or light).

If the ratio is too high, dark patches appear between luminaires. If the ratio is too low, the installation is uneconomical. The optimal SHR is a balance between **uniformity** and **economy of installation**.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Bold for Emphasis)

The KTU-recognised standard values for the SHR of different luminaire categories are:

| Luminaire Type | Typical SHR Range | KTU Design Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | 0.8 to 1.5 | **1.3 : 1** |
| **Semi-Direct** | 1.0 to 1.5 | **1.3 : 1** |
| **General Diffuse** | 1.2 to 1.8 | **1.5 : 1** |
| **Semi-Indirect** | 1.5 to 2.5 | **1.8 : 1** |
| **Indirect** | 2.0 to 4.0 | **3.0 : 1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Unit of SHR**: dimensionless (it is a pure ratio). Both **S** and **H** must be expressed in the **same length unit** (metres in KTU problems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The calculation of the SHR is rooted in the photometric **inverse-square law** combined with the **cosine-cubed law** of illumination from a point source. The reasoning proceeds as a structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Identify the critical point:** The point on the working plane that receives the *least* illuminance between two adjacent luminaires is the **mid-point M** (the point horizontally equidistant from both luminaires).
- **Step 2 — Apply the inverse-square + cosine law:** The illuminance at M from *each* luminaire depends on the angle θ₁ subtended at the luminaire (measured from the downward vertical).
- **Step 3 — Impose the uniformity condition:** For a uniformly lit working plane, the illuminance at the mid-point M (illuminated by **two** luminaires) must equal the illuminance **directly beneath** a luminaire (illuminated by **one** luminaire).
- **Step 4 — Introduce the luminaire's polar curve:** The luminous intensity I(θ) at angle θ is expressed as a function of the axial intensity I₀. Substituting this relationship produces a transcendental equation in θ₁.
- **Step 5 — Solve for θ₁ and hence S/H:** Once θ₁ is known, the geometry tan θ₁ = (S/2) / H gives the desired ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the mid-point rule:** In a regular grid of luminaires, the mid-point between four adjacent units is the *most poorly lit* location because it is the *farthest* from any single source. Designing the SHR so that the contribution of two adjacent luminaires exactly matches the direct-vertical contribution of a single luminaire guarantees that no point on the working plane can fall below the design illuminance.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula** required to solve KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SHR = \dfrac{S}{H}$ | Fundamental definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $E_{P} = \dfrac{I_{\theta}\,\cos^{3}\theta}{H^{2}}$ | Illuminance at a point P offset by distance D (cosine-cubed law) | lux |
| 3 | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{H^{2}+D^{2}}}$ | Cosine relation from geometry | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{D}{H} = \dfrac{S/2}{H}$ | Tangent relation for the mid-point | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $E_{M} = 2\,\dfrac{I_{\theta_{1}}\,\cos^{3}\theta_{1}}{H^{2}}$ | Total illuminance at the mid-point M | lux |
| 6 | $E_{0} = \dfrac{I_{0}}{H^{2}}$ | Illuminance directly beneath a luminaire | lux |
| 7 | $2\,I_{\theta_{1}}\cos^{3}\theta_{1} = I_{0}$ | Uniformity condition ($E_{M} = E_{0}$) | — |
| 8 | $\dfrac{S}{H} = 2\tan\theta_{1}$ | Final geometric relation | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{M} \geq \dfrac{E_{0}}{1.5}$ | Practical uniformity condition (illuminance ratio ≤ 1.5) | — |
| 10 | $\Phi_{total} = \dfrac{E \times A \times D}{u \times MF}$ | Total luminous flux required (for full design) | lumens |
| 11 | $N = \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{\Phi_{lamp} \times \eta_{luminaire}}$ | Number of luminaires | — |

**Boundary conditions and KTU design limits:**

- **Maximum recommended E_min / E_max** (uniformity ratio) on the working plane is **0.8** (a value of **1.0** is ideal, **≥ 0.7** is acceptable in KTU design questions).
- **Working plane height** for KTU interior design problems is taken as **0.85 m** above floor level (Indian Standard IS 3646).
- **Depreciation / Maintenance Factor (MF)** is the inverse of the **Light Loss Factor (LLF)**; KTU problems commonly use $MF = 0.8$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SHR is the **single most important number** an illumination designer chooses before purchasing a single luminaire. It directly impacts:

- **Capital cost** — a larger SHR means fewer luminaires, lower installation cost, lower wiring cost.
- **Uniformity of illumination** — too large an SHR produces dark "hot spots" and "voids" on the working plane, which is unacceptable in hospitals, schools, and precision manufacturing.
- **Glare control** — closely spaced luminaires at low height cause direct glare; SHR must be coordinated with the luminaire's **glare index (UGR)** rating.
- **Energy efficiency** — proper SHR minimises the number of luminaires needed for a given lux level, supporting **BEE Star Rating** compliance under the Indian Energy Conservation Act.

**Industrial applications where SHR is critical:** automobile assembly bays, semiconductor clean rooms, hospital operating theatres, cricket stadiums (for which transverse SHR can be as low as **0.8 : 1** when using floodlights).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Geometry of the Problem (Two Adjacent Luminaires)

Consider two identical luminaires **L₁** and **L₂** mounted at the same height **H** above the working plane and separated by a horizontal distance **S**. The point **M** on the working plane is the mid-point, located at horizontal distance **S/2** from each luminaire.

```
       L₁ ____________  L₂
         \    |    /
          \   |   /
       H   \  |θ₁/
            \ | /
             \|/
              M
         |----S----|
         |---S/2---|
```

From the right-triangle L₁ M (foot of L₁) :
- Hypotenuse = distance from luminaire to M = $\sqrt{H^{2} + (S/2)^{2}}$
- $\cos\theta_{1} = H / \sqrt{H^{2} + (S/2)^{2}}$
- $\tan\theta_{1} = (S/2) / H$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the General SHR Formula

**Step 1 — Illuminance at an arbitrary point P**

By the **cosine-cubed law**, the illuminance at a point P offset by horizontal distance D from directly below a point source of intensity $I_{\theta}$ is:

$$
E_{P} \;=\; \frac{I_{\theta}\,\cos^{3}\theta}{H^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Illuminance at the mid-point M from luminaire L₁**

For the mid-point M, the horizontal offset is D = S/2 and the corresponding angle is θ₁. Therefore:

$$
E_{M1} \;=\; \frac{I_{\theta_{1}}\,\cos^{3}\theta_{1}}{H^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Illuminance at M from luminaire L₂**

By symmetry, L₂ subtends the same angle θ₁ at M, and $I_{\theta_{1}}$ is identical (same luminaire type):

$$
E_{M2} \;=\; \frac{I_{\theta_{1}}\,\cos^{3}\theta_{1}}{H^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total illuminance at M**

$$
E_{M} \;=\; E_{M1} + E_{M2} \;=\; \frac{2\,I_{\theta_{1}}\,\cos^{3}\theta_{1}}{H^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Illuminance directly beneath a luminaire**

At the point directly below L₁ (the brightest point in the cell), the angle is θ = 0, so cos θ = 1 and $I_{\theta} = I_{0}$:

$$
E_{0} \;=\; \frac{I_{0}}{H^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Uniformity condition**

For uniform illumination, we demand $E_{M} = E_{0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2\,I_{\theta_{1}}\,\cos^{3}\theta_{1}}{H^{2}} &\;=\; \frac{I_{0}}{H^{2}} \\[4pt]
2\,I_{\theta_{1}}\cos^{3}\theta_{1} &\;=\; I_{0} \\[4pt]
\frac{I_{\theta_{1}}}{I_{0}} &\;=\; \frac{1}{2\cos^{3}\theta_{1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Solving for θ₁ using the luminaire polar curve**

The ratio $I_{\theta_{1}}/I_{0}$ is a known function of θ₁ from the luminaire's polar distribution curve. This gives the transcendental equation:

$$
f(\theta_{1}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\cos^{3}\theta_{1}}
$$

**Step 8 — Final geometric SHR**

Once θ₁ is determined, the SHR is obtained directly:

$$
\frac{S}{H} \;=\; 2\tan\theta_{1}
$$

## 3.3 Application to a Direct-Lighting Luminaire

For a direct-lighting luminaire (typical industrial reflector with deep parabolic dish), the polar distribution is closely approximated by:

$$
I_{\theta} \;=\; I_{0}\cos\theta
$$

**Substituting into the uniformity equation (Step 6):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,(I_{0}\cos\theta_{1})\,\cos^{3}\theta_{1} &\;=\; I_{0} \\[4pt]
2\cos^{4}\theta_{1} &\;=\; 1 \\[4pt]
\cos^{4}\theta_{1} &\;=\; \frac{1}{2} \\[4pt]
\cos\theta_{1} &\;=\; (0.5)^{1/4} \\[4pt]
\cos\theta_{1} &\;=\; 0.8409 \\[4pt]
\theta_{1} &\;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.8409) \;=\; 32.77^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing the SHR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
