<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Installation Aspects of Lighting** encompass the complete set of **mechanical**, **electrical**, and **safety engineering practices** required to physically mount, wire, protect, control, and commission luminaires and their associated control gear in residential, commercial, industrial, and outdoor environments, in strict accordance with national and international wiring regulations.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET418 – Illumination Technology)**, the *Installation Aspects* module bridges the gap between **photometric design** (Module 1 & 2 — lux levels, luminaire selection) and the **practical realization** of a working lighting scheme on-site. It is divided into two inter-dependent domains:

| Domain | Primary Concern | Typical Deliverables |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Installation** | Physical support, structural integrity, ingress protection, ergonomics | Brackets, poles, rails, suspension sets, ceiling roses, IP-rated enclosures |
| **Electrical Installation** | Power delivery, switching, protection, earthing, and control | Cables, conduits, MCBs, ELCBs, drivers, ballasts, dimmers, emergency interfaces |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Skeleton & Circulatory System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a lighting installation like the human body:**
> - The **mechanical installation** is the **skeleton** — the rigid framework that holds the luminaire (the "head") at the correct height, angle, and orientation.
> - The **electrical installation** is the **circulatory system** — the network of cables, switches, and protective devices that delivers electrical "blood" (energy) safely to the luminaire.
> - Just as a dislocated joint or a blocked artery renders a body non-functional, a poorly mounted luminaire or a wrongly sized cable can make a lighting system unsafe, inefficient, or non-compliant.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metrics used throughout this module (memorize these for KTU ESE):
> - **Standard supply voltage (India):** $V_{LL} = 415\text{ V}$ (3-phase) and $V_{LN} = 230\text{ V}$ (1-phase), **50 Hz**.
> - **Resistivity of copper:** $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ at **20 °C**.
> - **Resistivity of aluminium:** $\rho_{Al} = 2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ at **20 °C**.
> - **Maximum permissible voltage drop** in a lighting sub-circuit: **3 %** of the declared supply voltage (per **IS 732** / **IE Rules**), and **5 %** from the origin of the installation to the farthest load.
> - **Standard mounting heights:** Office — 2.4 m to 3.0 m; Street lighting — 6 m to 12 m; High-bay industrial — 8 m to 15 m.
> - **Ingress Protection (IP) rating reference:** **IP20** (indoor dry) → **IP65** (outdoor jet-proof) → **IP68** (submersible).

---

## 1.4 Why Installation Aspects Matter — The "Last Mile" of Lighting Design

A beautifully calculated lux plan is **worthless** if the luminaire cannot be safely mounted, or if the cable feeding it melts because it was undersized. The KTU 2024 scheme places explicit emphasis on this module because:

1. **Safety:** Electrical shocks, fires, and falls from height are the three dominant on-site hazards in lighting work.
2. **Compliance:** IS 732, IS 3646, IS 10322, and the Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2010 are mandatory references.
3. **Performance:** Wrong mounting height or excessive voltage drop directly reduces the delivered lumen output, nullifying the photometric design.
4. **Maintainability:** Correctly installed systems reduce **lamp replacement cost (LRG)** and **luminaire maintenance factor (LMF)** losses.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Installation — Sub-Domains

> [!NOTE]
> The mechanical side answers the question: **"How is the luminaire physically held in space, protected from the environment, and made serviceable?"**

### A. Mounting Methods for Luminaires

The selection of a mounting method depends on the **luminaire type**, **ceiling/structure type**, **aesthetic requirement**, and **maintenance access**.

1. **Surface Mounted (Direct / Ceiling Mount):**
   - Luminaire is screwed or clipped directly onto the ceiling surface.
   - Used for bulkhead, downlight, and batten holders in residential and small commercial spaces.
   - **Minimum clearance:** 25 mm between luminaire body and ceiling for heat dissipation (mandatory for incandescent and HID luminaires).

2. **Recessed Mounted (Concealed):**
   - Luminaire body sits inside a **false ceiling (gypsum board / grid tile)** cut-out.
   - Only the trim and lens are visible.
   - **Critical specification:** **Ceiling void depth (h_v)** must be $\geq$ luminaire recess depth + **25 mm** clearance for driver/ballast heat sinking.

3. **Pendant / Suspended Mount:**
   - Luminaire hangs from the ceiling by a **rod, chain, or flexible cord**.
   - **Pendant length (L_p)** typically 0.3 m to 1.5 m for task lighting; up to 6 m for decorative chandeliers.
   - **Suspension set** must be rated to support a minimum of **5× the luminaire weight** (safety factor).

4. **Wall Mounted (Sconce / Bracket):**
   - Mounted on vertical surfaces, common in corridors, staircases, façade lighting.
   - **Mounting height:** 1.8 m to 2.2 m from finished floor level (FFL).

5. **Pole Mounted (Outdoor / Street Lighting):**
   - Luminaire fixed atop a **galvanized iron (GI) pole** or **octagonal pole**.
   - **Bracket arm projection (B_a):** 0.5 m to 2.0 m.
   - Pole must withstand **wind load (W)** calculated per **IS 875 (Part 3)**.

6. **Track Mounted:**
   - Luminaires clipped onto an electrified track; allows repositioning without re-wiring.
   - **Track current rating:** typically **16 A** per circuit.

7. **High-bay / Low-bay Mount:**
   - Suspended from roof trusses using **chain links or steel wire rope**.
   - Minimum suspension height above floor: **6 m** for high-bay, **4 m** for low-bay.

### B. Ingress Protection (IP) Rating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IP rating** is a two-digit code (per **IS 60529 / IEC 60529**) specifying protection against solid objects (1st digit) and liquids (2nd digit). For KTU, memorize the most commonly asked combinations.

| IP Code | 1st Digit (Solids) | 2nd Digit (Liquids) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IP20** | Fingers (≥ 12.5 mm) | None | Indoor dry — office ceiling |
| **IP44** | Tools / wires (≥ 1 mm) | Splashing water | Bathrooms (Zone 2), kitchens |
| **IP54** | Dust-protected | Splashing | Dusty industrial sheds |
| **IP65** | Dust-tight | Jet-proof | Outdoor wall packs, facade |
| **IP66** | Dust-tight | Powerful jets | Car wash bays |
| **IP67** | Dust-tight | Temporary immersion | Fountain lighting |
| **IP68** | Dust-tight | Continuous immersion | Swimming pool, underwater |

### C. Structural & Mechanical Considerations

- **Load calculation:** The ceiling/structure must support $\geq 1.5 \times$ the total static weight of the luminaire.
- **Vibration isolation:** For luminaires mounted on vibrating structures (near HVAC, generators), use **rubber anti-vibration pads** to prevent lamp/LED failure.
- **Thermal management:** Maintain a **clearance of 50 mm** around driver/ballast compartments to allow convective cooling.
- **Accessibility:** Driver and control gear must be **reachable without dismantling the luminaire body** (per IS 10322 maintenance clause).

---

## 2.2 Electrical Installation — Sub-Domains

> [!NOTE]
> The electrical side answers the question: **"How is electrical energy delivered safely, efficiently, and controllably to the luminaire?"**

### A. Wiring Methods

| Method | Description | Typical Use | Advantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conduit Wiring (Surface/Concealed)** | PVC or GI conduits carrying single-core wires | Residential, commercial | Mechanically robust, replaceable wires |
| **Trunking (Cable Duct)** | Rectangular PVC/metal channel with removable cover | Office, data centres | Easy to add circuits |
| **Cable Tray / Ladder** | Perforated tray supporting multi-core cables | Industrial, plant rooms | Supports heavy cables |
| **Direct Burial Cable** | Armoured cable laid in trench with sand bed | Outdoor garden lighting | No conduit required |
| **Cleat Wiring** | Cables on porcelain cleats on walls | Temporary, industrial sheds | Cheapest, fastest |

### B. Cable Selection for Lighting Circuits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three decisions must be made:
> 1. **Conductor material:** Copper (preferred for lighting due to lower resistance) or Aluminium (for large feeders only).
> 2. **Conductor cross-section (A):** Sized for (i) current carrying capacity, (ii) voltage drop, (iii) short-circuit withstand.
> 3. **Insulation / sheathing:** PVC (general), XLPE (higher temperature, 90 °C), FRLS (Fire Retardant Low Smoke — mandatory in high-rise buildings per **NBC 2016**).

### C. Circuit Protection Devices

- **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker):** Protects against overload and short-circuit. Lighting circuits typically use **6 A, 10 A, or 16 A** Type B or C MCBs.
- **ELCB / RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker):** Detects earth leakage $\geq 30\text{ mA}$ and disconnects within **30 ms** — mandatory for all socket outlets; recommended for bathroom lighting.
- **HRC Fuse:** Back-up protection in older installations.
- **Isolator:** A double-pole switch used to safely isolate a luminaire or sub-circuit for maintenance.

### D. Earthing / Grounding

> [!WARNING]
> **Mandatory for every metal-bodied luminaire.** Two earthing systems are common:
> - **TN-S System:** Separate Protective Earth (PE) and Neutral (N) conductors from the transformer to the load — most common in modern Indian buildings.
> - **TT System:** Earth electrode at consumer premises; requires RCCB for safety.
> - **Earth resistance (R_e):** shall be $\leq 1\ \Omega$ for large installations and $\leq 5\ \Omega$ for small domestic, measured using a **fall-of-potential method** or **clamp-on earth tester**.

### E. Switching Arrangements

- **One-way switching:** A single SPST switch controls a luminaire from one location.
- **Two-way switching:** Two SPDT switches control one luminaire from two locations — used for staircase and corridor lighting.
- **Intermediate switching:** A 4-way switch inserted between two-way switches — enables three or more control points (large corridors, hotel rooms).

### F. Phase Load Balancing (Three-Phase Installations)

In commercial and industrial sites, lighting loads are distributed across the **R, Y, B** phases to keep the neutral current low. The **target unbalance** should be $\leq 10\%$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for solving any KTU ESE numerical on installation aspects. Commit these to memory.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage drop (1-phase, 2-wire) | $V_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{A}$ | Volts | $\rho$ = resistivity in $\Omega\cdot$m |
| 2 | Voltage drop (3-phase, 3-wire) | $V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{A}$ | Volts | Line-to-line drop |
| 3 | Percentage voltage drop | $\%V_d = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Should be $\leq 3\%$ per sub-circuit |
| 4 | Loop impedance check | $Z_s \leq \dfrac{U_0}{I_a}$ | $\Omega$ | Disconnection time $\leq 0.4\text{ s}$ |
| 5 | Earth-fault loop impedance | $Z_s = Z_e + Z_1 + Z_2$ | $\Omega$ | TN system; $U_0 = 230$ V |
| 6 | Conductor resistance | $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | $\Omega$ | At 20 °C; apply temperature correction |
| 7 | Temperature-corrected resistance | $R_\theta = R_{20}[1 + 0.00403(\theta - 20)]$ | $\Omega$ | Copper temperature coefficient = 0.00403 /°C |
| 8 | Short-circuit current (sym.) | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{U_0}{Z_s}$ | A | Used to check MCB breaking capacity |
| 9 | MCB breaking capacity check | $I_{sc} \leq I_{cn}$ of MCB | A | $I_{cn}$ typically 6 kA or 10 kA |
| 10 | Power loss in cable | $P_{loss} = I^{2} \cdot R$ | W | Heats the cable; affects derating |
| 11 | Total power demand | $P_{total} = \sum P_i \times D_f \times S_f$ | W | $D_f$ = demand factor, $S_f$ = simultaneity factor |
| 12 | Pole wind load | $W = 0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot v^{2} \cdot C_d \cdot A_p$ | N | Per IS 875 Part 3 |
| 13 | Luminous efficacy (control gear loss accounted) | $\eta_{system} = \dfrac{\Phi_{lamp}}{P_{lamp} + P_{gear}}$ | lm/W | Used for energy audits |
| 14 | Number of luminaires per phase | $n_{ph} = \dfrac{N_{total}}{3}$ (balanced) | — | For 3-phase balance |
| 15 | Operating cost per annum | $C_{yr} = P \times t \times E \times 365$ | ₹/yr | $E$ = energy tariff ₹/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, always state the **formula**, the **substitution step**, and the **final boxed answer**. Marks are awarded for showing the substitution clearly, not just the result.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Project Delivery:** Every electrical contractor in India must follow **IS 732** for wiring and **IS 10322** for luminaire safety. The skills taught here are directly used by **BEE-certified Energy Auditors** and **PEB (Pre-Engineered Building) electrical designers**.
- **Smart Cities & IoT:** Modern installation now integrates **DALI-2**, **KNX**, and **Zigbee** control buses into the wiring topology — the same physical cable carries both power and data.
- **Energy Audit & Retrofitting:** Voltage-drop and cable-loss calculations form the backbone of any **BEE Grade-1 audit report** for star-rated buildings.
- **Maintenance Engineering:** Proper IP rating and accessibility reduce **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** and improve the **Maintenance Factor (MF)** of the installation over its life cycle.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Voltage Drop in a Single-Phase Lighting Circuit

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

> A **230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase** lighting sub-circuit feeds **10 luminaires**, each rated at **$P = 40$ W, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ (lagging)**, connected in parallel along a **copper cable run of length $L = 25$ m** from the distribution board. The cable cross-sectional area is **$A = 1.5\text{ mm}^2$**. Using $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m at 20 °C, determine:
> 1. Total line current $I_L$.
> 2. Voltage drop in the cable $V_d$.
> 3. Percentage voltage drop $\%V_d$.
> 4. Actual voltage at the farthest luminaire.
> 5. Comment on IS 732 compliance.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Total Active Power of the Sub-Circuit**

The 10 luminaires are connected in **parallel**, so total power is the arithmetic sum of individual powers.

$$
P_{total} = n \times P = 10 \times 40 = 400 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Apparent Power**

Apparent power is the active power divided by the power factor.

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{400}{0.85} = 470.588 \text{ VA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Line Current in a Single-Phase Circuit**

For a single-phase, two-wire system, the line current magnitude equals the apparent power divided by the line-to-neutral voltage.

$$
I_L = \dfrac{S_{total}}{V_{LN}} = \dfrac{470.588}{230} = 2.046 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert All Quantities to SI Units**

The cable cross-section $A$ must be expressed in **square metres** to be consistent with $\rho$ in $\Omega\cdot$m.

$$
A = 1.5 \text{ mm}^2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

The length $L$ is already in metres, but the **go-and-return** distance in a single-phase loop is **$2L$** (current flows to the load and returns via the neutral).

$$
\text{Effective length} = 2L = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Voltage-Drop Formula for a Single-Phase, Two-Wire Circuit**

The general voltage drop across a two-conductor feeder is the resistance times the current, multiplied by 2 for the return path.

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi}{A}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Values**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2 \times (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 25 \times 2.046 \times 0.85}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the Numerator**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 25 \times 2.046 \times 0.85
$$

$$
= 2 \times 1.724 \times 25 \times 2.046 \times 0.85 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
= 2 \times 1.724 \times 25 = 86.20
$$

$$
86.20 \times 2.046 = 176.366
$$

$$
176.366 \times 0.85 = 149.911
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Numerator} = 149.911 \times 10^{-8} = 1.4991 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 8 — Divide by the Denominator**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{1.4991 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.9994 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute Percentage Voltage Drop**

The percentage voltage drop is the ratio of the voltage drop to the rated voltage, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\%V_d = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{LN}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.9994}{230} \times 100 = 0.4345 \%
$$

**Step 10 — Compute the Actual Voltage at the Farthest Luminaire**

The voltage at the load end is the source voltage minus the voltage drop (we are using a single lumped load equivalent; for a more rigorous analysis, integrate along the cable, but for KTU-level problems this approximation is acceptable).

$$
V_{load} = V_{LN} - V_d = 230 - 0.9994 = 229.0006 \text{ V} \approx 229 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 11 — IS 732 Compliance Check**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 732** permits a maximum of **3 % voltage drop** in any lighting sub-circuit. Our result is **0.43 %**, which is well within the limit. **Verdict: The installation is compliant.** The cable size is acceptable, and could even be reduced to **1.0 mm²** if future load increase is not anticipated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Cable Sizing Given a Voltage-Drop Constraint

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz** feeder supplies a row of **high-bay luminaires** drawing a total balanced current of **$I = 40$ A** at **$\cos\phi = 0.90$ lagging**. The feeder run is **$L = 80$ m** of **3-core copper cable (XLPE-insulated)**. If the maximum allowable voltage drop is **3 %** of $V_{LL}$, find the minimum required cross-sectional area $A$. Use $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m.

### 3.2.2 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Compute the Maximum Permissible Voltage Drop in Volts**

$$
V_{d, \max} = \dfrac{3}{100} \times V_{LL} = 0.03 \times 415 = 12.45 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the 3-Phase Voltage-Drop Formula**

For a balanced 3-phase, 3-wire system, the line-to-line voltage drop is given by:

$$
V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the Formula to Solve for $A$**

$$
A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{V_d}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Known Values**

$$
A = \dfrac{1.732 \times (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 80 \times 40 \times 0.90}{12.45}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Numerator**

$$
= 1.732 \times 1.724 \times 80 \times 40 \times 0.90 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
1.732 \times 1.724 = 2.9860
$$

$$
2.9860 \times 80 = 238.88
$$

$$
238.88 \times 40 = 9555.2
$$

$$
9555.2 \times 0.90 = 8599.68
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Numerator} = 8599.68 \times 10^{-8} = 8.5997 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by the Denominator**

$$
A = \dfrac{8.5997 \times 10^{-5}}{12.45} = 6.907 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to mm²**

$$
A = 6.907 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \times 10^{6} \frac{\text{mm}^2}{\text{m}^2} = 6.907 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 8 — Select the Next Standard Cable Size**

Standard copper cable sizes (per **IS 8130**) are: **1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120, 150, 185, 240 mm²**.

The next standard size **above** 6.907 mm² is **10 mm²**.

**Step 9 — Verify Current-Carrying Capacity**

Per **IS 3961 (Part 5)** for XLPE 3-core copper cable laid in free air:

- **6 mm²** → 41 A (insufficient margin)
- **10 mm²** → 57 A (comfortable margin for 40 A load)

**Step 10 — Verdict**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum required cable cross-section is **6.91 mm² (calculated)**. The selected **standard size is 10 mm²** copper, which also satisfies the current-carrying capacity of 40 A with a 1.42× safety margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Common Installation Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a high-frequency KTU ESE question type — *"Select the appropriate mounting, cable, and protection scheme for the following scenario."* Memorize the **logic** of mapping application → specification.

| Installation Scenario | Recommended Mounting | Recommended Cable | Protection | IP Rating | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Office cabin (false ceiling)** | Recessed downlight | 1.5 mm² Cu PVC in concealed conduit | 6 A MCB | IP20 | Maintain 25 mm void clearance |
| **Staircase corridor** | Wall-mounted bulkhead | 1.5 mm² Cu FRLS | 6 A MCB on two-way switching | IP44 | Two-way switch at each floor |
| **Bathroom (Zone 1)** | Recessed IP65 downlight | 1.5 mm² Cu in PVC conduit | 6 A MCB + 30 mA RCCB | IP65 | SELV transformer recommended for 12 V LED |
| **Industrial shop floor (8 m high)** | Suspended high-bay | 2.5 mm² Cu XLPE | 16 A MCB, Type C | IP54 | Chain suspension with vibration isolator |
| **Street road lighting** | Pole mounted with bracket arm | 4 mm² Cu XLPE armoured | 10 A MCB + timer / photocell | IP65 | Earth pit per pole; $R_e \leq 5\ \Omega$ |
| **Swimming pool underwater** | Recessed niche in pool wall | 6 mm² Cu armoured, double insulated | 30 mA RCCB + 12 V SELV | IP68 | Equipotential bonding of all metalwork |
| **Hospital OT** | Recessed sealed luminaire | 2.5 mm² Cu FRLS in EMT conduit | 6 A MCB on IPS (Isolated Power System) | IP54 | Battery-backed DC emergency, < 0.5 s transfer |
| **High-rise lift lobby** | Recessed or surface | 1.5 mm² Cu FRLS in metal trunking | 6 A MCB on essential bus | IP20 | Emergency circuit with 1 h battery backup |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Checklist — Pre-Commissioning Verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before energizing a lighting installation, a **KTU-trained engineer** must verify the following:

1. $\square$ **Continuity test** on every conductor (R + B = R-phase to phase end + return).
2. $\square$ **Insulation resistance test** (Megger) $\geq 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ between conductors and to earth at **500 V DC**.
3. $\square$ **Earth resistance test** at every earth electrode: $R_e \leq 1\ \Omega$ (large) / $5\ \Omega$ (small).
4. $\square$ **Polarity check** at every switch — line (phase) on the switch contact, neutral on the lamp holder.
5. $\square$ **Voltage drop measurement** under full load $\leq 3\%$.
6. $\square$ **Loop impedance ($Z_s$) measurement** to confirm disconnection time of MCB $\leq 0.4$ s.
7. $\square$ **RCD trip-time test** using a ramp tester — must trip at $30\text{ mA} \times 1.0 = 30\text{ mA}$ within 300 ms.
8. $\square$ **Lux level verification** at the working plane using a calibrated lux meter — must be within $\pm 10\%$ of design value.
9. $\square$ **Emergency lighting auto-test** for $\geq 1$ hour on battery backup.
10. $\square$ **Earth fault loop impedance (EFLI)** and prospective short-circuit current (PSCC) at the origin of every sub-circuit.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **IP44** and **IP65** ratings. State **two** typical applications of each in a lighting installation.

**Model Answer:**

- **IP44:** The first digit **4** indicates protection against solid objects $\geq 1$ mm (tools, wires). The second digit **4** indicates protection against **splashing water from any direction**.
- **IP65:** The first digit **6** indicates **dust-tight** (no ingress of dust). The second digit **5** indicates protection against **water jets** from a 6.3 mm nozzle at 12.5 l/min.
- **Applications of IP44:** Bathroom ceiling lights (Zone 2), kitchen task lights, covered balcony luminaires.
- **Applications of IP65:** Outdoor wall-pack luminaires, façade flood-lights, street-light luminaires, parking lot pole heads.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the two digits. Always quote **both digits separately** with their specific meaning — partial credit is awarded for the correct digit-by-digit explanation even if the application example is missing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

> **Q2.** What is the **maximum permissible voltage drop** in a lighting sub-circuit as per **IS 732**? State the formula for percentage voltage drop in a **3-phase, 3-wire** balanced system.

**Model Answer:**

- The **maximum permissible voltage drop** in a lighting sub-circuit as per **IS 732** is **3 %** of the declared nominal voltage of the sub-circuit.
- For the total installation (origin of installation to the farthest load), the cumulative limit is **5 %**.
- The **percentage voltage drop** formula for a balanced 3-phase, 3-wire system is:

$$
\%V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{A \cdot V_{LL}} \times 100
$$

- Where $\rho$ = resistivity, $L$ = one-way length in metres, $I$ = line current in amperes, $A$ = cross-sectional area in m², $V_{LL}$ = line-to-line voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students forget to mention **both** the 3% sub-circuit and the 5% total limit. Awarding full 3 marks requires both limits plus the formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** with **internal choice**. The sub-part split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

> **Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the **mechanical mounting methods** used for luminaires, with a comparison table of at least **four methods** including their **typical mounting heights**, **advantages**, and **limitations**. **(7 marks)**

> **Q.A (b)** A **230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase** lighting circuit consists of **8 fluorescent luminaires**, each rated at **$P = 65$ W with $\cos\phi = 0.90$ lagging**. The luminaires are connected in parallel along a copper cable run of **$L = 30$ m** with cross-section **$A = 2.5$ mm²**. Compute the **line current**, **voltage drop**, and **percentage voltage drop**. Is the installation **IS 732 compliant**? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The mechanical mounting method determines the luminaire's spatial orientation, accessibility, ingress protection, and aesthetic integration. The choice is governed by the ceiling type, the application's photometric requirement, and the maintenance philosophy.

**Mounting Methods (4 × 1 mark = 4 marks):**

1. **Surface Mounted:** The luminaire is fixed directly to the ceiling surface using screws, bolts, or spring clips. Typical mounting height: **2.4–3.0 m**. *Advantages:* simplest installation, lowest cost, easy maintenance. *Limitations:* protrudes into the room space, dust accumulation on top.

2. **Recessed Mounted:** The luminaire body is concealed within a false ceiling cut-out. Typical mounting height: **2.7–3.5 m** (measured to the false-ceiling line). *Advantages:* clean aesthetic, glare-controlled. *Limitations:* requires sufficient ceiling void depth ($h_v \geq 150$ mm typically), difficult to service.

3. **Pendant / Suspended:** Hangs from the structural ceiling on a rod, chain, or wire. Typical mounting height: **2.5–4.0 m** (depending on pendant length). *Advantages:* provides direct downward light, decorative appeal. *Limitations:* swing in air currents, dust on top, requires strong suspension point.

4. **Pole Mounted:** Outdoor luminaire fixed atop a GI or octagonal pole via a bracket arm. Typical mounting height: **6–12 m**. *Advantages:* wide area coverage, uniform road illumination. *Limitations:* wind-load design required, expensive foundation.

5. **Track Mounted (Bonus):** Snap-fit onto an electrified ceiling track. *Advantages:* repositionable. *Limitations:* limited to compatible luminaires and track current rating of 16 A.

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Method | Height (m) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Surface | 2.4–3.0 | Residential, store rooms |
| Recessed | 2.7–3.5 | Offices, hotels |
| Pendant | 2.5–4.0 | Dining halls, reception |
| Pole | 6–12 | Streets, parking lots |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The mounting method is not a designer's choice alone; it must satisfy the **structural**, **thermal**, **IP**, and **maintenance** requirements simultaneously.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V = 230$ V, 1-phase, 50 Hz; $n = 8$; $P = 65$ W; $\cos\phi = 0.90$; $L = 30$ m; $A = 2.5$ mm² = $2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²; $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m.

**Step 1 — Total Active Power (1 mark):**

$$
P_{total} = n \times P = 8 \times 65 = 520 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Apparent Power and Line Current (2 marks):**

$$
S = \dfrac{P_{total}}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{520}{0.90} = 577.78 \text{ VA}
$$

$$
I_L = \dfrac{S}{V} = \dfrac{577.78}{230} = 2.512 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Drop (2 marks):**

For single-phase, two-wire circuit (factor of 2 for return path):

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{A}
$$

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 30 \times 2.512 \times 0.90}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
2 \times 1.724 \times 30 \times 2.512 \times 0.90 = 233.71
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Num} = 233.71 \times 10^{-8} = 2.3371 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2.3371 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.9348 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Voltage Drop (1 mark):**

$$
\%V_d = \dfrac{0.9348}{230} \times 100 = 0.4064 \%
$$

**Step 5 — IS 732 Compliance (1 mark):**

The permissible limit is **3 %**. Our result is **0.41 %**, which is well within the limit. **The installation is fully IS 732 compliant.** The cable could even be reduced to 1.5 mm² if future load is not anticipated.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners look for the **factor of 2** in the 1-phase formula. Writing $V_d = \rho L I / A$ (without the 2) will lose **2 full marks**. Also, the unit conversion of $A$ from mm² to m² is a frequent error point.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

> **Q.B (a)** Describe the **earthing systems** used in lighting installations. Compare the **TN-S**, **TT**, and **IT** systems with suitable **Indian application examples**. **(7 marks)**

> **Q.B (b)** A **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz** feeder supplies a balanced load of **30 kW** at **$\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging** through a **3-core aluminium cable of length 100 m** and cross-section **25 mm²**. Compute the **voltage drop** in the cable and the **percentage voltage drop**. Is the installation compliant with IS 732? Use $\rho_{Al} = 2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
Earthing is the process of connecting the **non-current-carrying metal parts** of an electrical installation (luminaire bodies, conduit, distribution board enclosures) to the general mass of earth to ensure safety against earth faults and to provide a stable reference point.

**TN-S System (2 marks):**
- **T** = direct connection of the source star point to earth.
- **N** = combined neutral and protective function from source to consumer.
- **S** = separate PE and N conductors throughout the installation.
- *Application:* Most modern Indian apartments, IT parks, and commercial complexes where the supply authority provides a separate earth wire.

**TT System (2 marks):**
- **Source star point** is earthed at the substation; the **consumer installs an independent earth electrode**.
- The consumer's earth is isolated from the source earth.
- Requires a high-sensitivity **RCCB (30 mA)** for personnel safety.
- *Application:* Rural Indian households, agricultural pump sets, isolated buildings where the supply authority does not provide a dedicated earth wire.

**IT System (1 mark):**
- The source is either **isolated from earth** or connected through a **high-impedance** device.
- A **first earth fault does not trip** the system; an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** signals the fault.
- *Application:* Hospital operating theatres, mines, and critical care areas (often used as **Isolated Power Systems — IPS** for OTs).

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | TN-S | TT | IT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source earth | Direct | Direct | Isolated / Impedance |
| Consumer earth | Separate PE from utility | Independent electrode | Reference electrode |
| Earth fault | Causes high fault current → MCB trips | Low fault current → RCCB trips | No trip; IMD alarms |
| Typical use | Commercial, residential | Rural, agriculture | Hospitals, mines |

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 415$ V; $P = 30$ kW; $\cos\phi = 0.85$; $L = 100$ m; $A = 25$ mm² = $25 \times 10^{-6}$ m²; $\rho_{Al} = 2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m.

**Step 1 — Line Current in a 3-Phase Balanced System (2 marks):**

$$
I_L = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \cos\phi} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3} \times 415 = 718.74
$$

$$
718.74 \times 0.85 = 610.93
$$

$$
I_L = \dfrac{30000}{610.93} = 49.11 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Drop Formula (3-Phase, 3-Wire) (1 mark):**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (2 marks):**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{1.732 \times 2.826 \times 10^{-8} \times 100 \times 49.11 \times 0.85}{25 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Numerator step-by-step:

$$
1.732 \times 2.826 = 4.8946
$$

$$
4.8946 \times 100 = 489.46
$$

$$
489.46 \times 49.11 = 24038.7
$$

$$
24038.7 \times 0.85 = 20432.9
$$

$$
\text{Num} = 20432.9 \times 10^{-8} = 2.0433 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2.0433 \times 10^{-4}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{204.33}{25} = 8.173 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Voltage Drop (1 mark):**

$$
\%V_d = \dfrac{8.173}{415} \times 100 = 1.969 \%
$$

**Step 5 — IS 732 Compliance (1 mark):**

The permissible limit is **3 %**. Our result is **1.97 %**, which is **within the limit**. **The installation is IS 732 compliant**, but the margin is tight; for any future load increase, the cable should be upgraded to **35 mm²**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is using the **1-phase formula** (with the factor of 2) for a 3-phase system. The 3-phase formula uses **$\sqrt{3}$**, not 2. Also, students often forget the $\cos\phi$ in the voltage-drop formula — but it must be included for accurate computation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 reasons KTU students lose marks in Installation Aspects questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the 1-phase voltage-drop formula.
> 2. **Not converting mm² to m²** when substituting area in the formula.
> 3. **Confusing IP rating digits** — students interchange the 1st and 2nd digit meanings.
> 4. **Omitting the 5% cumulative limit** when stating IS 732 voltage-drop rules.
> 5. **Writing units in inconsistent notation** — always use SI base units in the substitution step.
> 6. **Skipping the "comment on compliance" step** at the end of a numerical — this is a compulsory 1-mark conclusion.
> 7. **Drawing circuit diagrams without polarity** — the line conductor must always go to the switch contact, and the neutral directly to the lamp holder. Drawing them reversed loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3 (Installation Aspects):**

- [x] **Mechanical mounting methods:** Surface, Recessed, Pendant, Pole, Track, High-bay — each with a specific application and height range.
- [x] **IP rating code:** Two digits — solids first, liquids second. IP20 (indoor), IP44 (bathroom), IP65 (outdoor), IP68 (submersible).
- [x] **Mounting heights:** Office 2.4–3.0 m; Street 6–12 m; High-bay 8–15 m.
- [x] **Wiring methods:** Conduit, Trunking, Cable Tray, Direct Burial, Cleat.
- [x] **Cable types:** PVC (general), XLPE (90 °C), FRLS (high-rise mandatory).
- [x] **Protection devices:** MCB (overload + SC), RCCB (earth leakage 30 mA / 100 mA), HRC fuse, Isolator.
- [x] **Earthing:** TN-S (commercial), TT (rural), IT (hospital). Earth resistance: $\leq 1\ \Omega$ (large), $\leq 5\ \Omega$ (small).
- [x] **Switching:** One-way (single point), Two-way (staircase), Intermediate (multi-point).
- [x] **Voltage drop limit:** 3% per sub-circuit, 5% total per IS 732.
- [x] **Single-phase $V_d$ formula:** $V_d = \dfrac{2 \rho L I \cos\phi}{A}$.
- [x] **Three-phase $V_d$ formula:** $V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \rho L I \cos\phi}{A}$.
- [x] **Phase balance target:** $\leq 10\%$ unbalance in 3-phase systems.
- [x] **Pre-commissioning tests:** Continuity, Insulation (Megger $\geq 1\ \text{M}\Omega$), Earth ($R_e \leq 5\ \Omega$), Loop impedance ($Z_s$), Polarity, Lux verification, RCD trip-time.
- [x] **Standards:** IS 732, IS 10322, IS 3646, IS 875, NBC 2016, CEA 2010.
- [x] **Emergency lighting:** 1-hour battery backup minimum for life-safety circuits; central UPS for OTs and high-rise.
- [x] **Resistivities (memorize):** $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m; $\rho_{Al} = 2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m.
- [x] **Copper temperature coefficient:** $\alpha = 0.00403$ per °C.
- [x] **Voltage drop example answer template:** "State given data → Compute current → Substitute formula → Evaluate numerically → State % → Conclude on IS 732 compliance."
- [x] **Mnemonic for IP ratings:** "**S**olids **F**irst, **L**iquids **L**ast" (SFLL).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Feature for Entrance, Staircase, Corridor Lighting, Industrial Building and Hospital Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Special Feature for Entrance, Staircase, Corridor Lighting, Industrial Building and Hospital Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Special lighting design features* refer to the customized photometric, electrical, and architectural provisions required in specific application areas where conventional general-purpose lighting fails to satisfy safety, visual performance, hygiene, and aesthetic constraints. These are governed by the National Lighting Code (NBC 2016 Part 8/Sec 2) and IS 3646 standards.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine lighting a building like a doctor prescribing medicine — you don't give the same pill for a headache, a fracture, and a heart condition. Similarly, an **entrance**, a **staircase**, a **corridor**, an **industrial shop floor**, and a **hospital operation theatre** are anatomically different "spaces" of a building, each demanding a unique lighting "prescription."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five golden pillars governing these special applications are:
> 1. **Illumination Level (lux)** – quantity of light
> 2. **Glare Control (UGR)** – comfort
> 3. **Colour Rendering Index (CRI, Ra)** – quality of colour
> 4. **Unified Glare Rating / Uniformity Ratio** – distribution
> 5. **Emergency & Backup Provision** – safety continuity

### 1.2 Standardized Design Parameters
| Application Area | Maintained Illuminance (lux) | CRI (Ra) | UGR Limit | IP Rating |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Entrance (Indoor) | 100 – 200 | $\geq 80$ | $\leq 22$ | IP44 (covered) |
| Staircase | 100 – 150 | $\geq 40$ | $\leq 25$ | IP20 |
| Corridor | 100 (day) / 50 (night) | $\geq 40$ | $\leq 25$ | IP20 |
| Industrial (General) | 200 – 500 | $\geq 60$ | $\leq 25$ | IP54 |
| Industrial (Fine work) | 500 – 1000 | $\geq 80$ | $\leq 22$ | IP54 |
| Hospital Ward | 100 – 300 | $\geq 80$ | $\leq 19$ | IP20 |
| Operation Theatre | 1000 – 2000 | $\geq 90$ | $\leq 19$ | IP54 (sealed) |

> [!NOTE]
> **IP Rating** = Ingress Protection (e.g., IP44 = protected against solid objects $>1$ mm and water splashes). Higher IP = more dust/moisture resistance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Entrance Lighting — Conceptual Logic
- **Goal:** Bridge the harsh luminance contrast between bright **outdoor daylight** (often $10\,000$ lux) and dim **indoor ambient** (around 100 lux).
- **Why:** A sudden change causes "**Black Hole Effect**" — pupils fail to dilate, making the interior look dangerously dark.
- **How:** Create a **luminance gradient** of 3 zones:
  1. Outdoor canopy/portico: $\approx 300$ lux
  2. Threshold/door zone: $\approx 200$ lux
  3. Foyer/lobby: $\approx 100$ lux
- Use **wall-mounted downlights**, **bollards**, **recessed step lights**, and decorative luminaires.
- **CRI $\geq 80$** to render skin tones and signage accurately.
- **Vandal-resistant** polycarbonate diffusers for public entrances.

### 2.2 Staircase Lighting — Conceptual Logic
- **Goal:** Eliminate **shadow traps** on treads and risers.
- **Critical principle:** Light must strike the **vertical riser** of the step at an angle of $30°$–$60°$ from horizontal.
- **Methods:**
  - Wall-mounted fixtures at every landing (every 3–4 m vertically)
  - **LED strip lighting** integrated into handrails
  - Step-recessed marker lights (low-luminance, anti-glare louvres)
- **Uniformity Ratio (E\textsubscript{min}/E\textsubscript{avg})** must be $\geq 0.4$.
- **Emergency lighting** (minimum 1 lux on floor for $\geq 1$ hour on battery backup) is mandatory under NBC 2016.
- **Avoid 3D confusion:** Staggered lighting pattern is preferred over continuous strips to preserve depth perception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photometric Trap:** If the staircase luminaire is mounted directly above the nosing, the riser falls into shadow and the walker misjudges step depth — a classic cause of falls.

### 2.3 Corridor Lighting — Conceptual Logic
- **Goal:** Provide **continuous, uniform, low-glare** wayfinding light.
- **Strategy:**
  - Indirect or semi-indirect luminaires with **uplight component** brighten the ceiling, expanding the perceived space.
  - Night-mode dimming to **50 lux** (or 20% of daytime level) saves energy and supports circadian comfort.
  - **Occupancy sensors (PIR/microwave)** are standard in modern designs.
- **Avoid:** Bright spotlights, glossy floors (mirror reflection of fixtures creates veiling glare).

### 2.4 Industrial Building Lighting — Conceptual Logic
- **Goal:** Maximize visual performance for productivity + safety.
- **Mounting height dictates luminaire type:**
  - Mounting height $H \leq 6$ m → **Low-bay** (lensed fluorescent / LED panel)
  - $6$ m $< H \leq 12$ m → **Mid-bay** (HID, LED high-bay reflector)
  - $H > 12$ m → **High-bay** (HID 400 W / LED 150–200 W with narrow-beam aluminium reflector)
- **Stroboscopic Effect:** With 50 Hz AC supply, fluorescent/HID lamps flicker at $100$ Hz. Rotary machinery (e.g., lathe chuck) at 3000 rpm appears stationary. Mitigation: Use **electronic HF ballasts** ($\geq 20$ kHz) or DC operation.
- **Glare Index (GI)** must be $\leq 25$ for industrial tasks.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF)** in dusty industries = $0.5$–$0.6$ (vs. clean rooms: $0.8$).
- **Emergency lighting** to escape routes at $\geq 0.5$ lux.

### 2.5 Hospital Lighting — The Most Demanding Application
Hospital lighting is divided into **four functional zones**, each with distinct photometry:

#### Zone A — Patient Wards
- General illuminance: **100–300 lux** (300 lux at bed-head for reading).
- Bed-head reading luminaire: **individual switch**, swing-arm, $\leq 300$ lux at pillow.
- **Night-light:** $\leq 5$ lux (red/amber to preserve dark adaptation for nurses and avoid disturbing patients).
- **Colour temperature:** $4000$ K (neutral white) — promotes wakefulness during day; tunable to $3000$ K at night.

#### Zone B — Examination Rooms
- Illuminance: **500 lux** (general) + **$1000$ lux** (local examination lamp).
- CRI $\geq 90$ for accurate diagnosis (e.g., jaundice, cyanosis).
- Avoid shadows on patient face — use **diffused indirect** or **large-area ceiling luminaires**.

#### Zone C — Operation Theatre (OT) — The Crown Jewel
- **Surgical field illuminance:** $\mathbf{10\,000}$–$\mathbf{100\,000}$ lux (focussed beam).
- General OT ceiling illuminance: **$1000$ lux** minimum.
- **Colour Rendering Index (Ra):** $\mathbf{\geq 90}$ (preferably 95).
- **Colour Temperature:** $4000$–$5000$ K (daylight white).
- **Surgical lighthead features:**
  - Multi-mirror reflector with $\geq 100$ halogen/LED sources
  - Shadow dilution through overlapping beams
  - Cool beam (infrared filtered) — keeps surgeon's head cool
  - Sterilizable handle
  - **No flicker** (DC-driven or HF ballast) — prevents stroboscopic misinterpretation

#### Zone D — ICU / Recovery / Diagnostic
- ICU: **300–500 lux** with dimming; **no direct glare** to supine patient.
- X-ray, CT, MRI: Use **non-magnetic luminaires** (aluminium, no ferromagnetic parts) and **DC operation** (no interference).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lumen Method | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times CU \times MF}$ | Number of luminaires required |
| 2 | Illumination at point | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$ (point source) | Inverse square law |
| 3 | Glare constant (DGI) | $DGI = \sum (G^{1.6} \times \omega^{0.8} / p^{1.6})$ | Daylight glare index |
| 4 | Stroboscopic frequency | $f_{s} = 2 \times f_{supply}$ | $100$ Hz for $50$ Hz mains |
| 5 | CRI | $R_{a} = 100$ (perfect reference) | Lower = poorer colour fidelity |
| 6 | UGR | $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.25}{L_{b}} \sum \dfrac{L^{2} \omega}{p^{2}}\right]$ | Unified Glare Rating |
| 7 | Emergency autonomy | $t_{em} \geq 1$ hour | Backup duration |
| 8 | Maintained illuminance | $E_{m} = E_{initial} \times MF$ | Design value after depreciation |

Where:
- $E$ = illuminance (lux)
- $A$ = area (m²)
- $\Phi$ = luminaire luminous flux (lm)
- $CU$ = Coefficient of Utilization
- $MF$ = Maintenance Factor
- $I$ = luminous intensity (cd)
- $\theta$ = angle of incidence
- $d$ = distance (m)
- $L$ = luminance, $L_{b}$ = background luminance, $\omega$ = solid angle, $p$ = Guth's position index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Units Snapshot:** $\mathbf{1\ lux = 1\ lumen/m^{2}}$; $\mathbf{1\ candela = 1\ lumen/steradian}$. These conversion identities appear routinely in KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Luminaires Using the Lumen Method

**Statement:** Determine the number of fluorescent luminaires required to illuminate an industrial shop floor of dimensions $30\ m \times 15\ m$ to a maintained illuminance of $300$ lux. Each luminaire is of $4 \times 36$ W FTL type, providing $\Phi = 10\,000$ lumens. Take $CU = 0.5$ and $MF = 0.7$.

**Step 1 — Identify total floor area**
The shop floor is rectangular with length $L = 30$ m and width $W = 15$ m.

$$A = L \times W$$

$$A = 30 \times 15 = 450\ m^{2}$$

*Logic: Total area dictates the total light flux required.*

**Step 2 — Identify the required maintained illuminance**
The design specification is $E_{m} = 300$ lux (this is the **post-depreciation** value, hence "maintained").

**Step 3 — Compute total luminous flux required**
Total flux required is the illuminance multiplied by the area.

$$\Phi_{required} = E_{m} \times A$$

$$\Phi_{required} = 300 \times 450 = 135\,000\ \text{lumens}$$

*Logic: To deliver 300 lumens on every square metre of a 450 m² area, we need 300 × 450 = 135 000 lumens incident on the work-plane.*

**Step 4 — Compute effective flux per luminaire**
Each luminaire emits $\Phi = 10\,000$ lm, but only a fraction $CU$ reaches the work-plane, and a fraction $MF$ is preserved after dirt/lamp ageing depreciation.

$$\Phi_{effective} = \Phi \times CU \times MF$$

$$\Phi_{effective} = 10\,000 \times 0.5 \times 0.7$$

$$\Phi_{effective} = 3\,500\ \text{lumens per luminaire}$$

*Logic: Of 10 000 lm emitted, only 50% (CU) reaches the work-plane due to fixture geometry, and 70% of that remains after dirt accumulation and lamp lumen depreciation.*

**Step 5 — Compute the number of luminaires**

$$N = \dfrac{\Phi_{required}}{\Phi_{effective}}$$

$$N = \dfrac{135\,000}{3\,500} = 38.57$$

**Step 6 — Round up to next whole number**
We cannot install a fractional luminaire.

$$N = 39\ \text{luminaires}$$

*Logic: Always round UP, never down — under-illumination is unsafe and non-compliant.*

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{N = 39\ \text{luminaires of 4 \times 36 W FTL type}}$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study for KTU

| Parameter | Entrance | Staircase | Corridor | Industrial | Hospital OT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Maintained E (lux) | 100–200 | 100–150 | 100 (D) / 50 (N) | 200–1000 | $\geq 1000$ (general) |
| Uniformity (E\textsubscript{min}/E\textsubscript{avg}) | 0.4 | 0.4 | 0.4 | 0.6 | 0.7 |
| CRI (Ra) | $\geq 80$ | $\geq 40$ | $\geq 40$ | $\geq 60$ | $\geq 90$ |
| Colour Temp (K) | 3000 | 4000 | 4000 | 4000–6500 | 4000–5000 |
| Glare control | Diffuser | Louvred | Indirect | Louvred reflector | Indirect + louvred |
| Emergency | Optional | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory + UPS** |
| Switching | Photo-cell + timer | PIR + manual | Occupancy sensor | Manual + group | Touchless + dimming |
| IP Rating | IP44 | IP20 | IP20 | IP54 | IP54 (gasketted) |
| Common Lamp | LED downlight, CFL | LED strip, FTL | LED panel | LED/HID high-bay | LED surgical lighthead |

### 3.3 Derivation: Verification of Point-Source Illumination

**Statement:** A corridor luminaire is mounted $4$ m above the floor. Its luminous intensity directed vertically downward is $I = 1600$ cd. Find the illuminance directly below it.

**Step 1 — Identify geometry**
The point lies on the axis of the luminaire, so $\theta = 0°$ and $\cos \theta = 1$.

$$d = 4\ m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the inverse square law**

$$E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$$

$$E = \dfrac{1600 \times 1}{(4)^{2}} = \dfrac{1600}{16}$$

$$E = 100\ \text{lux}$$

**Interpretation:** This single luminaire delivers exactly 100 lux at floor level — perfectly matching the corridor daytime standard. The design is therefore photometrically valid for a single-fixture point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Special Lighting Application Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    A --> E[Zone 4: Industrial]:::zone4
    A --> F[Zone 5: Hospital]:::zone5

    B --> B1[Canopy Downlight 300 lux]

    C --> C1[Wall Mounted FTL LED]
```

### 4.3 Staircase Lighting — Photometric Coverage Concept

```mermaid

    L3 --> R3[Riser C 30 to 60 deg incidence]

    L3 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The luminaire-to-riser angle of $30°$–$60°$ ensures the vertical face of each step receives direct light, eliminating the "shadow trap" hazard. PIR sensors trigger full brightness on detection and dim to standby otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*List the special design considerations for **staircase lighting**. Why is riser illumination critical?*

**Model Answer:**
- Wall-mounted luminaires at every landing (vertical spacing $\leq 4.5$ m).
- Step-recessed marker lights for low-level guidance.
- Handrail-integrated LED strip for tactile cue.
- **Emergency lighting** with $\geq 1$ hour battery backup ($\geq 1$ lux on tread).
- Uniformity ratio $E_{min}/E_{avg} \geq 0.4$.
- CRI $\geq 40$ acceptable; CCT 4000 K.
- **Riser illumination critical** because the human eye uses the vertical face of a step to judge depth. If the riser falls into shadow, the walker misjudges step height, leading to **trips and falls** — a leading cause of domestic and workplace injuries.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
*Explain why **CRI $\geq 90$** is mandatory for an **operation theatre** light.*

**Model Answer:**
- The surgeon must accurately identify the colour of blood, tissue, organs, and sutures.
- Poor CRI distorts red and pink tones, making it difficult to distinguish **arterial vs. venous blood** (bright red vs. dark red) or detect **cyanosis** (bluish skin discolouration indicating hypoxia).
- High CRI ($\geq 90$) ensures the light's spectrum closely matches a reference illuminant (Planckian radiator at the same CCT), enabling **true-colour diagnosis**.
- It also reduces **visual fatigue** during long procedures (4–10 hours).
- Hence, OT lightheads use **multi-spectrum LEDs** with carefully tuned phosphor blends to achieve Ra $\geq 95$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Q3 (A).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
*(a)* Describe in detail the **zonal lighting design of a hospital**, highlighting illumination levels, colour requirements, and special fixtures for at least **four distinct functional areas**. (7 marks)
*(b)* A surgical OT requires $40\,000$ lux at the operating table from a circular lighthead of diameter $0.5$ m. Compute the **luminous intensity** of the lighthead on its axis. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Zonal Hospital Lighting Design:**

| Zone | Illuminance | CRI | Special Fixture |
|---|---|---|---|
| General Ward | 100–300 lux | $\geq 80$ | Bed-head reading luminaire (individually switched), night-light $\leq 5$ lux (red), anti-glare diffuser |
| Examination Room | 500 lux (general) + 1000 lux (local) | $\geq 90$ | Examination lamp with flexible arm, diffused ceiling panel |
| Operation Theatre | $10\,000$–$100\,000$ lux (focussed) | $\geq 90$ | Multi-mirror surgical lighthead, cool beam, sterilizable handle, IP54 sealed ceiling panels |
| ICU | 300–500 lux with dimming | $\geq 80$ | Indirect luminaire above bed, individual control, no direct glare on supine patient |

Additional considerations:
- **Sterile environment:** Luminaires must be gasketted and easily wipeable.
- **No flicker:** Flicker disrupts microsurgery performed under high magnification.
- **Colour temperature:** $4000$–$5000$ K (neutral white) for clinical accuracy.
- **Backup:** Online UPS or central battery for OTs and ICUs (no break transfer).

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute the distance from lighthead to table**
Standard surgical lighthead is positioned at height $d = 1.0$ m above the operating table (typical working distance).

$$d = 1.0\ m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the point-source illuminance equation**
The illuminance on the axis is given by:

$$E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for intensity $I$**

$$I = E \times d^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**

$$I = 40\,000 \times (1.0)^{2} = 40\,000\ \text{candela}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{I = 40\,000\ \text{cd}}$

*Interpretation:* This is the luminous intensity on the central axis. Since the lighthead is a $0.5$ m diameter reflector, the solid angle of its beam is small (narrow beam), so a high candela value is realistic for surgical lightheads (which typically have $I = 50\,000$–$100\,000$ cd in the central beam).

---

**Q3 (B).** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
*(a)* Explain the **special lighting requirements of an industrial building** with respect to mounting height, glare control, and stroboscopic effect. (7 marks)
*(b)* Determine the number of LED high-bay luminaires required for a workshop of $50\ m \times 20\ m$ to achieve a maintained illuminance of $500$ lux. Each luminaire provides $\Phi = 25\,000$ lumens. Take $CU = 0.6$ and $MF = 0.65$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Industrial Lighting — Special Requirements:**

1. **Mounting Height Dictates Luminaire Type:**
   - $H \leq 6$ m: Low-bay with wide-beam reflector.
   - $6 < H \leq 12$ m: Mid-bay with medium-beam reflector.
   - $H > 12$ m: High-bay with narrow-beam aluminium reflector.

2. **Glare Control:**
   - Industrial tasks often involve looking up or horizontally — glare is severe.
   - Use **cut-off luminaires**, louvred fixtures, or prismatic diffusers.
   - Maintain Glare Index (GI) $\leq 25$.

3. **Stroboscopic Effect:**
   - Fluorescent and HID lamps flicker at $2f = 100$ Hz on 50 Hz mains.
   - Rotating machinery (lathes, drills) may appear stationary — **a serious safety hazard**.
   - Mitigation: Use **high-frequency electronic ballasts** ($f \geq 20$ kHz) or convert to **LED** (which operates on DC internally and has no perceptible flicker).

4. **Maintenance Factor:**
   - Industrial dust and dirt reduce $MF$ to $0.5$–$0.6$.
   - Luminaires must be designed for **easy cleaning** (smooth, gasket-free exteriors are unsuitable — instead, use enclosed IP54 units).

5. **Emergency Lighting:**
   - Escape route lighting $\geq 0.5$ lux.
   - High-risk task area lighting continues for $\geq$ task duration (per IS 3217).

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute floor area**

$$A = 50 \times 20 = 1000\ m^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total luminous flux required**

$$\Phi_{required} = E_{m} \times A = 500 \times 1000 = 500\,000\ \text{lumens}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective flux per luminaire**

$$\Phi_{effective} = 25\,000 \times 0.6 \times 0.65$$

$$\Phi_{effective} = 9\,750\ \text{lumens per luminaire}$$

**Step 4 — Compute number of luminaires**

$$N = \dfrac{500\,000}{9\,750} = 51.28$$

**Step 5 — Round up**

$$N = 52\ \text{luminaires}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{N = 52\ \text{LED high-bay luminaires}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** the number of luminaires — KTU awards zero marks if $N = 51$ is written.
> 2. **Confusing** Initial Illuminance ($E_{i}$) with Maintained Illuminance ($E_{m}$). If the problem says "maintained," do NOT divide again by MF.
> 3. **Unit mismatches** — Always quote flux in **lumens** and area in **m²**; mixing cm² or ft² loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring CU & MF** — Students often compute $N = E \times A / \Phi$ alone; this is treated as a **conceptual error** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **CRI omission in OT** — If the question asks about OT lighting and the answer omits CRI $\geq 90$, expect 2-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Entrance Lighting:** 3-zone luminance gradient (canopy $\to$ threshold $\to$ foyer) to avoid "Black Hole Effect."
- **Staircase Lighting:** Illuminate the **riser** at $30°$–$60°$; emergency lighting $\geq 1$ lux, $\geq 1$ hr autonomy.
- **Corridor Lighting:** Indirect luminaires; daytime 100 lux, night-time 50 lux; occupancy sensors.
- **Industrial Lighting:** Mounting height selects luminaire type; HF ballasts to kill strobe ($2f = 100$ Hz).
- **Hospital Ward:** 100–300 lux, night-light $\leq 5$ lux (amber), bed-head reading luminaire.
- **Operation Theatre:** $10\,000$–$100\,000$ lux, **CRI $\geq 90$**, CCT $4000$–$5000$ K, cool beam, no flicker, UPS-backed.
- **ICU:** Dimmable, no direct glare on supine patient.
- **Master Formula (Lumen Method):** $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times CU \times MF}$ — always round UP.
- **Point-Source Equation:** $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^{2}}$; $\cos \theta = 1$ on-axis.
- **Stroboscopic Effect:** Always $2 \times$ supply frequency; cure with HF ballast ($> 20$ kHz) or LED.
- **Emergency:** Staircase + corridor + industrial + hospital = all **mandatory** emergency lighting under NBC 2016.
- **IP Rating:** Industrial = IP54; OT ceiling = IP54 (sealed); ward = IP20.
- **Typical Mistakes:** (1) Forgetting $CU$ in Lumen Method; (2) Using Initial E instead of Maintained E; (3) Not rounding up $N$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emergency Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emergency Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Emergency Lighting** is defined as the lighting provided for use during failure of the normal mains supply, ensuring the safety of occupants by enabling safe evacuation, preventing panic, and permitting continued essential activities in critical installations.

According to **IS 3217:1989 (Reaffirmed 2014)** and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8, Section 6)**, emergency lighting is a mandatory safety provision in all public, commercial, industrial, and high-occupancy buildings, specifically designed to operate automatically when the primary power supply is interrupted.

The formal definition accepted by international standards (IEC 60598-2-22) is:

> **Emergency Lighting:** "Lighting provided for use when the supply to the normal lighting fails."

This umbrella term encompasses **three distinct sub-categories**:
1. **Emergency Escape Lighting** – Provided to ensure safe exit during emergency.
2. **Standby Lighting** – Provided to enable normal activities to continue.
3. **Safety Lighting** – Provided for the safety of people involved in potentially hazardous processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Emergency lighting is examined under Module 3 of PEEET418 and is a high-weightage topic, often featuring full 14-mark Part B questions. Students must master types, photometric requirements, battery backup sizing, and code compliance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a crowded cinema hall watching a movie. Suddenly, the main power goes out and the hall plunges into total darkness. Within seconds, a soft, evenly distributed glow appears from the ceiling edges and exit signs, guiding people calmly toward the doors. **That automatic switchover to backup illumination is Emergency Lighting in action.**

Just as a **parachute** is a backup for a skydiver, emergency lighting is a **photometric safety net** for a building's occupants. It is not meant to make the building look beautiful; it is meant to save lives.

Think of it this way:

| Everyday Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Backup parachute | Emergency escape lighting |
| Hospital backup generator | Standby lighting |
| Firefighter's helmet lamp | Safety (high-risk task) lighting |
| Exit sign glow-in-the-dark | Escape route signage illumination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric Recap:** The key performance metric of any emergency lighting system is **luminance on the floor path (lux) at end of rated battery duration** — not at switch-on. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are normative and must be memorized for KTU 2024 exams:

- **Minimum illuminance on escape route floor: 1 lux** (along centre line)
- **Minimum illuminance in open areas (anti-panic): 0.5 lux**
- **Maximum-to-minimum uniformity ratio: 40:1** (i.e., Uo ≥ 0.025)
- **Colour Rendering Index: Ra ≥ 40** for escape route lighting
- **Response time: 50% illumination within 5 s, 100% within 60 s**
- **Minimum rated duration: 1 hour** (often 2 or 3 hours for hospitals/high-rises)
- **Luminaire spacing on corridors: S ≤ 2 × Hm** (Spacing-to-Height Ratio)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Emergency Lighting

The classification follows a strict hierarchy defined by the **CIBSE Code for Lighting (2012)** and **IS 3217**:

#### 2.1.1 Emergency Escape Lighting (Subdivided into three):

- **Escape Route Lighting:** Illuminates defined escape paths — corridors, staircases, ramps, and final exits. Required minimum **1 lux on the floor**.
- **Open Area Lighting (Anti-panic Lighting):** Provided in halls, lobbies, and large floor plates where occupants are unfamiliar with the layout. Minimum **0.5 lux** at floor level, with Uo ≥ 0.025.
- **High Risk Task Area Lighting:** For areas where sudden darkness creates immediate danger — chemical plants, machine shops with moving parts, operating theatres. Minimum **10% of normal task illuminance** or **15 lux**, whichever is higher, with no break in supply.

#### 2.1.2 Standby Lighting

Allows continued normal activities in critical facilities:
- **Hospitals** (operation theatres, ICUs)
- **Control rooms** (power stations, airports)
- **Data centres**
- Typically designed to provide **same illuminance as normal lighting**, or a defined fraction (often 30–50%).

#### 2.1.3 Safety Lighting

For industrial processes where dark conditions could cause:
- Burns, explosions, mechanical injury, chemical exposure.
- Maintained continuously for the duration of the hazard.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The operational "Why" and "How" of an emergency lighting system is as follows:

- **Detection:** A mains failure sensor (relay/contact) detects voltage drop. *Why:* It must trigger within milliseconds — slower response endangers life.
- **Changeover:** The luminaire switches to battery supply via a static inverter or relay. *How:* Either **maintained**, **non-maintained**, or **sustained** mode.
- **Stabilization:** DC supply is inverted (if needed) and conditioned to drive the LED/fluorescent lamp. *Why:* LEDs need constant current drivers.
- **Operation:** Luminaires provide specified lux for the rated duration (1–3 hours). *How:* Battery capacity is sized accordingly.
- **Restoration & Recharge:** On mains restoration, batteries recharge within **24 hours** (per IS 3217) and the luminaire reverts to mains mode.

---

### 2.3 Modes of Operation

KTU frequently asks students to differentiate these:

- **Maintained System:** Lamp operates on both mains and battery. Always ON (e.g., exit signs, hospital corridors).
- **Non-Maintained System:** Lamp operates **only** during mains failure. Used where normal lighting is already adequate.
- **Sustained System:** A hybrid — two lamps in one luminaire; one on mains, one on battery. Common in older fluorescent installations.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** "**MNS** = **M**aintained (always ON), **N**on-maintained (only on failure), **S**ustained (two lamps, two supplies)."

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{F \times UF \times MF \times n}$ | Number of luminaires required | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{A}$ | Average illuminance on area | lux |
| 3 | $C_{Ah} = \dfrac{I_{load} \times t_{backup}}{\eta_{inv} \times k_{derate}}$ | Battery capacity sizing | Ah |
| 4 | $P_{batt} = V_{batt} \times I_{load}$ | Battery power output | W |
| 5 | $t_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V_{batt} \times \eta}{P_{load}}$ | Backup time from battery | h |
| 6 | $S_{max} = 2 \times H_m$ | Max luminaire spacing on corridor | m |
| 7 | $U_o = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{max}} \geq 0.025$ | Uniformity ratio (1:40) | ratio |
| 8 | $E_{floor} = \dfrac{I \times \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$ | Point illuminance inverse square | lux |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{lamp} \times \eta_{driver} \times \eta_{battery}$ | System efficiency | fraction |
| 10 | $R_{CRI} \geq 40$ | Minimum colour rendering index | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $E$ = required illuminance (lux), $A$ = area (m²), $F$ = lumens per lamp, $UF$ = utilization factor, $MF$ = maintenance factor, $n$ = lamps per luminaire, $I$ = luminous intensity (cd), $h$ = mounting height (m), $H_m$ = mounting height (m), $k_{derate}$ = derating factor (0.80 for lead-acid, 0.90 for Li-ion).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

In Indian engineering practice, emergency lighting is mandated by:
- **NBC 2016, Part 8, Section 6.4** — for all buildings over 15 m height
- **IS 3217:1989** — Installation and maintenance of emergency lighting
- **IEC 60598-2-22** — Luminaires for emergency lighting
- **BIS / CEIG approval** — required for hospitals, malls, IT parks, and metro stations

Engineers at firms like **Schneider Electric, Philips (Signify), Havells, and Crompton** design these systems using software like **DIALux** and **Relux**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Battery Capacity Sizing (Most Repeated KTU Question)

**Problem Statement:** An emergency lighting load draws **120 W** from a **24 V** DC battery bank. The system must provide backup for **3 hours** at an overall efficiency of **0.85** and a derating factor of **0.80**. Calculate the required battery capacity in **Ah**.

**Step 1: Determine Load Current**

$$I_{load} = \dfrac{P_{load}}{V_{battery}}$$

$$I_{load} = \dfrac{120 \text{ W}}{24 \text{ V}}$$

$$I_{load} = 5.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Apply Battery Capacity Formula**

The fundamental relation linking capacity, current, time, and efficiency is:

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{I_{load} \times t_{backup}}{\eta_{overall} \times k_{derate}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{5.0 \text{ A} \times 3 \text{ h}}{0.85 \times 0.80}$$

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{15.0 \text{ Ah}}{0.68}$$

$$C_{Ah} = 22.06 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 4: Apply Safety Margin (Engineering Practice — IS 3217 recommends 20% margin)**

$$C_{selected} = 1.20 \times 22.06 = 26.47 \approx 27 \text{ Ah}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** A **24 V, 27 Ah** battery bank is required. Standard market size is **30 Ah** — the next commercial rating.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Emergency Luminaires on an Escape Route

**Problem Statement:** A 2 m wide × 30 m long corridor requires **1 lux** minimum on the floor. Luminaires with **2 × 18 W fluorescent lamps** (each emitting **1200 lumens**) are to be ceiling-mounted at **2.5 m**. Given UF = 0.55, MF = 0.80, and the 2:1 spacing-to-height rule, find the number of luminaires required.

**Step 1: Apply Lumen Method**

$$N = \dfrac{E \times A}{F \times UF \times MF \times n}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Floor Area**

$$A = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Substitute All Parameters**

$$N = \dfrac{1 \times 60}{1200 \times 0.55 \times 0.80 \times 2}$$

$$N = \dfrac{60}{1056}$$

$$N = 0.0568$$

This result is **clearly wrong** — it indicates the lumen method underestimates because 1 lux is too low. We must use the **spacing criterion** instead.

**Step 4: Apply Spacing-to-Height Rule**

$$S_{max} = 2 \times H_m = 2 \times 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Number from Spacing**

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{L_{corridor}}{S_{max}} + 1$$

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{30}{5.0} + 1 = 6 + 1 = 7$$

**Step 6: Account for Both Sides of a 2 m Wide Corridor**

$$N_{total} = 7 \times 2 = 14 \text{ luminaires}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never apply the lumen method to low-illuminance escape route designs. The 2H spacing rule is the governing criterion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Point Illuminance on Floor Using Inverse Square Law

**Problem Statement:** A 24 W LED emergency luminaire emits **2400 lumens** uniformly in the downward hemisphere. Mounted at **2.8 m**, find the direct illuminance on the floor directly below it.

**Step 1: Calculate Luminous Intensity**

$$I = \dfrac{\Phi}{2\pi}$$

$$I = \dfrac{2400}{2\pi} = 381.97 \text{ cd}$$

**Step 2: Apply Inverse Square Law (point directly below)**

$$E = \dfrac{I}{h^2} = \dfrac{381.97}{(2.8)^2} = \dfrac{381.97}{7.84}$$

$$E = 48.7 \text{ lux}$$

This is the **maximum** value. At the edge of the spacing radius (2.5 m away), use:

$$E = \dfrac{I \cos^3\theta}{d^2}$$

where $\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{2.8}{\sqrt{2.5^2 + 2.8^2}} = \dfrac{2.8}{3.75} = 0.747$

$$E = \dfrac{381.97 \times (0.747)^3}{(3.75)^2} = \dfrac{381.97 \times 0.417}{14.06} = 11.3 \text{ lux}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always check the **minimum** value at the design edge. If E_min ≥ 1 lux, the design is acceptable.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Operating Modes

| Parameter | Maintained | Non-Maintained | Sustained |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lamp ON during mains | Yes | No | Yes (one lamp) |
| Lamp ON during failure | Yes | Yes | Yes (other lamp) |
| Battery usage | Continuous | Only on failure | Only on failure |
| Typical application | Exit signs, hospital corridors | Staircases, warehouses | Older malls, factories |
| Lamp life | Reduced (always on) | Long (rarely used) | Moderate |
| Test complexity | Simple | Simple | Complex (2 lamps) |
| KTU exam frequency | High | High | Moderate |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Backup Time from Given Battery

**Given:** 12 V, 100 Ah battery, total load = 200 W, inverter efficiency = 0.90, battery efficiency = 0.85.

**Step 1: Available Energy**

$$E_{stored} = V \times C \times \eta_{batt} = 12 \times 100 \times 0.85 = 1020 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 2: Output Energy to Load**

$$E_{out} = E_{stored} \times \eta_{inv} = 1020 \times 0.90 = 918 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 3: Backup Time**

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{P_{load}} = \dfrac{918}{200} = 4.59 \text{ h}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Emergency Lighting System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mains Supply 230V AC] --> B[Mains Monitor Relay]
    B -->|Mains OK| C[Mode Selector Switch]
    B -->|Mains Fail| D[Static Transfer Switch]
    C --> E[Lamp Driver 1]
    D --> F[Battery Bank 24V DC]
    F --> G[Inverter DC to AC]
    G --> E
    E --> H[LED Luminaire Array]
    H --> I[Illuminated Floor Area]
    F --> J[Battery Charger Float Mode]
    J --> A
    H --> K[Sensor: Light Level Feedback]
    K --> L[Controller MCU]
    L --> E
    M[Test Switch Monthly] --> L
    N[Exit Sign Luminaire] --> F
    O[Corridor Luminaire] --> F
    P[Staircase Luminaire] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Mains is monitored continuously. On failure detection, the static transfer switch isolates mains and routes battery power through the inverter. The controller regulates dimming/operation and accepts monthly test commands as mandated by IS 3217.

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Mains Fail Event

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Mains Voltage Detected] --> S2{Voltage in Range 200-250V}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Run in Normal Mode]
    S2 -->|No for 0.5s| S4[Trigger Alarm Timer]
    S4 --> S5{Failure Persists 1s}
    S5 -->|No| S3
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Activate Static Switch]
    S6 --> S7[Battery Supplies Inverter]
    S7 --> S8[50% Lux within 5s]
    S8 --> S9[100% Lux within 60s]
    S9 --> S10{Mains Restored}
    S10 -->|No| S9
    S10 -->|Yes| S11[Transfer to Mains]
    S11 --> S12[Recharge Battery Float Mode 24h]
    S12 --> S3
```

---

### 4.3 Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Emergency Lighting] --> T2[Escape Lighting]
    T1 --> T3[Standby Lighting]
    T1 --> T4[Safety Lighting]
    T2 --> T5[Escape Route Lighting 1 lux]
    T2 --> T6[Open Area Anti-panic 0.5 lux]
    T2 --> T7[High Risk Task Area 15 lux]
    T3 --> T8[Critical Operations 30-100% of normal]
    T4 --> T9[Hazardous Industrial Areas]
    T5 --> T10[Corridors Staircases]
    T6 --> T11[Halls Lobbies]
    T7 --> T12[Chemical Plant Machine Shop]
```
