## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Emergency Lighting. List and briefly explain its three main categories as per IS 3217.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Emergency lighting is the lighting provided for use when the supply to the normal mains lighting fails, ensuring occupant safety and orderly evacuation.

**Three Main Categories:**

1. **Emergency Escape Lighting:** Provided to ensure safe and easy identification of escape routes and to allow safe movement of occupants towards exits. Sub-divided into:
   - Escape route lighting (≥ 1 lux on floor)
   - Open area / anti-panic lighting (≥ 0.5 lux)
   - High-risk task area lighting (≥ 15 lux)

2. **Standby Lighting:** Provided to enable normal activities to continue substantially unchanged. Used in hospitals, control rooms, etc. Provides 30–100% of normal illuminance.

3. **Safety Lighting:** Provided for the safety of people working in hazardous locations where sudden darkness may cause injury (e.g., chemical plants, manufacturing floors with moving machinery).

**Conclusion:** All three categories must be **automatically activated** on mains failure and powered by a **self-contained, independent energy source** (battery or generator).

**[3 Marks Breakdown: Definition 1M + 3 categories with brief explanation 2M]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Maintained and Non-Maintained Emergency Lighting Systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Maintained System | Non-Maintained System |
|---|---|---|
| Lamp operation | Continuously ON, on both mains and battery | Operates only during mains failure |
| Power source during normal | Mains | Mains (not used) |
| Power source during emergency | Battery | Battery |
| Use case | Exit signs, hospital corridors, cinema halls | Staircases, warehouses, car parks |
| Lamp life | Shorter (always on) | Longer (rarely used) |
| Wiring complexity | Simple | Simple |
| Visual indication of operation | Always visible | Only visible during failure |
| Energy consumption | Higher | Lower |

**Conclusion:** Maintained systems are preferred where occupants are unfamiliar with the building layout, while non-maintained are chosen for energy efficiency in low-traffic areas.

**[3 Marks: 4 distinguishing points with 1M and 1 conclusion 1M.]**

---

### **Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Describe the photometric and performance requirements of an Emergency Escape Lighting system as per IS 3217 / NBC 2016. Include quantitative specifications.

**b) [7 Marks]** A hospital corridor 3 m wide and 50 m long is to be provided with maintained emergency lighting. Each luminaire contains 2 × 9 W LED modules delivering 900 lumens each, mounted at 3.0 m ceiling height. Calculate: (i) the number of luminaires using the spacing criterion, (ii) the total connected load, and (iii) the battery capacity in Ah if the system must deliver backup for 2 hours at 24 V DC with overall efficiency 0.80 and derating factor 0.80.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a): Photometric and Performance Requirements**

**1. Illuminance Levels:**

$$E_{escape\ route} \geq 1 \text{ lux on floor along centre line}$$

$$E_{open\ area} \geq 0.5 \text{ lux at floor level}$$

$$E_{high\ risk\ task} \geq 15 \text{ lux (or 10\% of normal task illuminance, whichever is higher)}$$

**2. Uniformity:**

$$U_o = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{max}} \geq 0.025 \quad \text{(Maximum ratio 40:1)}$$

**3. Colour Rendering:**

$$R_a \geq 40 \text{ (per IEC 60598-2-22)}$$

**4. Response Time:**

- 50% of required illuminance within **5 seconds**
- 100% of required illuminance within **60 seconds**
- For safety lighting: **no break** in supply

**5. Rated Duration:**

- Minimum **1 hour** (general)
- **2–3 hours** (hospitals, high-rise, assembly buildings)

**6. Spacing:**

$$S_{max} = 2 \times H_m \text{ on escape route corridors}$$

**7. Battery Recharge Time:** within **24 hours** after mains restoration.

**8. Test Facility:** A **monthly functional test** switch is mandatory on all self-contained luminaires.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b): Numerical Design**

**Given Data:**
- Corridor: 3 m wide × 50 m long
- Luminaire: 2 × 9 W LED, 900 lumens each
- Mounting height: Hm = 3.0 m
- Required backup: 2 hours
- Battery: 24 V DC
- Efficiency: 0.80, Derating: 0.80

**(i) Number of Luminaires using Spacing Criterion:**

$$S_{max} = 2 \times H_m = 2 \times 3.0 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

$$N_{along\ length} = \dfrac{50}{6.0} + 1 = 8.33 + 1 = 9.33 \approx 10$$

For a **3 m wide** corridor, place luminaires on **both sides** for uniformity:

$$N_{total} = 10 \times 2 = \boxed{20 \text{ luminaires}}$$

**(ii) Total Connected Load:**

$$P_{total} = 20 \times 18 \text{ W} = \boxed{360 \text{ W}}$$

**(iii) Battery Capacity:**

$$I_{load} = \dfrac{360}{24} = 15 \text{ A}$$

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{I_{load} \times t}{\eta \times k} = \dfrac{15 \times 2}{0.80 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{30}{0.64}$$

$$C_{Ah} = 46.875 \text{ Ah}$$

With 20% engineering margin:

$$C_{selected} = 1.20 \times 46.88 = 56.25 \approx \boxed{60 \text{ Ah (next standard size)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Students forget the "+1" in luminaire spacing → lose 1 mark.
> 2. Use of Lumen method instead of spacing criterion for low-illuminance escape routes → lose 2 marks.
> 3. Omission of the 20% safety margin in battery sizing → lose 1 mark.
> 4. Not specifying "next standard Ah rating" (50/60/75/100) → lose 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat diagrams the operating principles of Maintained, Non-Maintained, and Sustained Emergency Lighting Systems. State one application of each.

**b) [7 Marks]** A factory floor 40 m × 25 m uses 30 W LED high-bay luminaires (3000 lm each) at 8 m mounting height. Design the emergency backup system for **1-hour** operation at 0.5 lux minimum, 0.7 utilization factor and 0.8 maintenance factor. Compute (i) number of emergency luminaires, (ii) load, and (iii) Ah capacity at 48 V DC supply with 0.85 efficiency and 0.75 derating.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a): Operating Principles**

**Maintained System:**
- The lamp is **continuously energized** on both normal and emergency power.
- Two separate circuits: one from mains (always on), one from battery (energized on failure).
- **Application:** Cinema halls, exit sign luminaires, hospital waiting areas.

**Non-Maintained System:**
- The lamp is **off during normal conditions**; it is **switched on only during mains failure** via a relay.
- Battery is on **float charge** continuously, ready to discharge on demand.
- **Application:** Staircases, parking lots, back-of-house warehouses.

**Sustained System:**
- **Two lamps** in one luminaire:
  - Lamp 1 → mains fed (always on)
  - Lamp 2 → battery fed (only on failure)
- Effectively combines maintained + non-maintained behaviour.
- **Application:** Older fluorescent installations, auditoriums, factory aisles.

(Diagrams are demonstrated in SECTION 4 of this note using Mermaid block diagrams.)

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b): Factory Emergency Design**

**Given Data:**
- Area: 40 m × 25 m = 1000 m²
- Luminaire: 30 W LED, 3000 lumens
- Mounting height: 8 m
- Backup: 1 hour
- Battery: 48 V DC
- UF = 0.7, MF = 0.8
- E_required = 0.5 lux
- η = 0.85, k = 0.75

**(i) Number of Emergency Luminaires (Lumen Method — for large area):**

$$N = \dfrac{E \times A}{F \times UF \times MF \times n}$$

$$N = \dfrac{0.5 \times 1000}{3000 \times 0.7 \times 0.8 \times 1}$$

$$N = \dfrac{500}{1680} = 0.298$$

This result is below 1, which is **physically impossible**. This indicates that **0.5 lux** is below the threshold where the lumen method is valid. We use **1 luminaire per 100 m²** as a practical rule.

$$N_{practical} = \dfrac{1000}{100} = 10 \text{ luminaires}$$

With redundancy (every other luminaire on emergency):

$$N_{emergency} = \dfrac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ (using every other high-bay)} \approx 15$$

For 1000 m² floor with 8 m mounting, spacing criterion $S = 2 \times 8 = 16$ m grid:

$$N_{rows} = \dfrac{40}{16} + 1 = 3.5 \approx 4$$

$$N_{columns} = \dfrac{25}{16} + 1 = 2.56 \approx 3$$

$$N_{total} = 4 \times 3 = \boxed{12 \text{ luminaires}}$$

**(ii) Total Load:**

$$P_{load} = 12 \times 30 = 360 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Battery Capacity:**

$$I_{load} = \dfrac{360}{48} = 7.5 \text{ A}$$

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 1}{0.85 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{7.5}{0.6375} = 11.76 \text{ Ah}$$

With 20% safety margin:

$$C_{selected} = 1.20 \times 11.76 = 14.12 \approx \boxed{15 \text{ Ah}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss in Q.B (b):**
> 1. Applying lumen method to 0.5 lux without realising it gives sub-unity value → lose 2 marks. Switch to spacing rule.
> 2. Forgetting "+1" in spacing count → lose 1 mark.
> 3. Missing 20% engineering margin → lose 1 mark.
> 4. Not using "next standard Ah rating" in the final answer.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- [x] **Emergency Lighting = lighting during mains failure** (per IEC 60598-2-22).
- [x] **Three categories:** Escape, Standby, Safety — know all sub-types and illuminance levels.
- [x] **Key illuminance values:** 1 lux (escape route), 0.5 lux (open area), 15 lux (high risk task).
- [x] **Uniformity ratio (Uo) ≥ 0.025** (max:min = 40:1) — verify at end of backup time, not at start.
- [x] **Response time:** 50% lux in 5 s, 100% lux in 60 s; **no break** for safety lighting.
- [x] **Duration:** 1 h minimum, 2–3 h for hospitals/high-rise.
- [x] **Spacing rule:** $S_{max} = 2 \times H_m$ on escape route corridors.
- [x] **Operating modes:** Maintained, Non-Maintained, Sustained — remember MNS mnemonic.
- [x] **Battery sizing:** $C_{Ah} = \dfrac{I \times t}{\eta \times k}$, then add 20% margin and pick next standard rating.
- [x] **Battery recharge time ≤ 24 hours** after mains restoration.
- [x] **Codes to cite:** IS 3217, NBC 2016 Part 8, IEC 60598-2-22.
- [x] **Common exam trap:** Apply **spacing rule** for low illuminance, not lumen method.
- [x] **Common exam trap:** Floor area illuminance is measured at the **floor**, not at the working plane.
- [x] **CRI ≥ 40** mandatory for escape route luminaires.
- [x] **Test switch** mandatory on every self-contained luminaire (monthly test per IS 3217).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lighting Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lighting Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Lighting Maintenance** is the systematic planning, scheduling, and execution of activities aimed at preserving the designed illuminance levels of an installation throughout its operational life. It quantifies the progressive loss of effective light output due to three principal physical phenomena: ageing of the lamp (lumen depreciation), accumulation of dirt on the luminaire (luminaire dirt depreciation), and complete failure of individual lamps (burnout).

In the KTU 2024 / IES (Illuminating Engineering Society) framework, this degradation is captured by a single multiplicative entity called the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**. The MF converts the *initial* illuminance delivered by a new, clean installation into the *maintained* illuminance guaranteed at the worst point in the maintenance cycle.

Mathematically, the canonical relationship is:

$$E_{maintained} = E_{initial} \times MF$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Recall (Frequently asked):** The Maintenance Factor is defined as the ratio of the average illuminance on the working plane after a specified period of use to the average illuminance obtained under the same conditions with a new installation. It is always **less than or equal to 1**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Headlight" Intuition

Imagine a brand-new car. On day one, the headlight beam reaches 100 metres — that is your **initial illuminance**. Now consider what happens over the next three years:

- The **bulb dims** as its filament evaporates (this is your **Lamp Lumen Depreciation, LLD**).
- **Dust, dead insects, and moisture** coat the glass cover, scattering the beam (this is your **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation, LDD**).
- Eventually, the bulb **blows out completely** (this is your **Lamp Burnout Factor, LBOF**).

If you only service the car *after* the bulb dies, you are driving dangerously. **Lighting Maintenance** is exactly the act of "servicing the headlights on schedule" — cleaning the lens at fixed intervals, group-replacing bulbs before they die, and accepting that the *worst-day* beam (not the best-day beam) is what the lighting designer must plan for.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner expects students to think in terms of the **"worst-case maintained illuminance"** because that is the legally and ergonomically binding design value, not the showroom-fresh value.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Illuminance Symbol:** $E$, measured in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**; $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$.
- **Lamp Rated Life:** Expressed in **hours (h)** — typically 1,000 h for incandescent, 10,000–15,000 h for fluorescent, and up to 50,000 h for LED.
- **Ambient dirt classification:** Uses the IES **Category I (Clean) → Category VI (Very Dirty)** scale.
- **Maintenance Interval:** The cleaning cycle $T$ in **months**, usually 12, 24, or 36 months.
- **Lumen Maintenance threshold ($L_p$):** e.g., $L_{70}$ means the lamp is replaced when output drops to 70 % of initial. This is the modern LED standard.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU board question is: *"Why is MF always < 1?"* The answer: every lamp depreciates, every luminaire collects dirt, and not all lamps survive the cycle — so the *average* light available at the end of any maintenance period is always less than the initial output.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Maintenance Factor

The IES / KTU-recommended formulation decomposes the **Maintenance Factor (MF)** into three independent multiplicative factors. Each captures a distinct physical degradation mechanism.

### 2.1.1 Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD)

LLD represents the fractional luminous flux a lamp still emits at the end of the maintenance period, relative to its initial (100-hour) output. It is intrinsic to the lamp's physics and is **not** influenced by the environment.

- For an **incandescent lamp**, the filament evaporates and the bulb blackens → LLD ≈ 0.80–0.85 at rated life.
- For a **fluorescent lamp**, phosphor degradation and electrode wear dominate → LLD ≈ 0.85–0.90.
- For an **LED**, it is the slowest of all → LLD is reported as $L_{70}$, $L_{80}$, or $L_{90}$ (percentage of initial flux maintained at a stated hour, e.g., 50,000 h).

### 2.1.2 Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD)

LDD quantifies the fractional transmission of the luminaire's optical surfaces (reflector, diffuser, lens, lamp envelope) after a period of exposure. It depends on:

1. The **ambient dirt category** of the space.
2. The **construction of the luminaire** (open, enclosed, filtered, sealed).
3. The **elapsed time** since the last cleaning.

LDD is read from **IES dirt-depreciation curves** plotted between (a) percentage luminaire output versus (b) months in service, parameterised by dirt class.

### 2.1.3 Lamp Burnout Factor (LBOF)

LBOF accounts for lamps that have failed entirely during the cycle. If $n$ lamps are in service and $k$ have failed, and if the room surface reflectances are *not* re-calculated, the surviving lamps carry a heavier load. For a **group-relamping** strategy (the KTU-recommended industrial practice), LBOF = 1.0 because all lamps are replaced at once. For **spot replacement**, LBOF is calculated as:

$$LBOF = \frac{\text{Number of lamps still operating}}{\text{Original number of lamps installed}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a *group replacement* scheme, the KTU examiner credits the student for stating **LBOF = 1.0** because the *worst case* (just before group replacement) is when all lamps are functioning and at their lumen-maintenance minimum — a far more realistic design value than a mid-cycle average.

## 2.2 The Master Equation (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Typical Unit / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $MF = LLD \times LDD \times LBOF$ | Multiplicative loss factors | 0.3 – 0.9 (dimensionless) | Quick maintained-illuminance computation |
| 2 | $E_{maintained} = E_{initial} \times MF$ | Final design illuminance | lux (lx) | Compliance with IS/IEEL code (e.g., 150 lx office) |
| 3 | $E_{required} = E_{design} \times \frac{1}{MF}$ | Initial (boosted) illuminance needed | lux (lx) | Used to specify lamp wattage |
| 4 | $LLF = MF_{IES-1984}$ | Older IES nomenclature | dimensionless | Recognise legacy textbooks |
| 5 | $n_{actual} = \frac{n_{rated}}{1 - M}$ | Mortality-based lamp count | integer | Mortality curves (% failed vs. time) |
| 6 | $L_{p} = \frac{\Phi(t)}{\Phi_{0}}$ | Lumen maintenance fraction | dimensionless | LED $L_{70}/L_{80}/L_{90}$ rating |
| 7 | $E_{avg} = \frac{N \times \Phi \times CU \times MF}{A}$ | Full lumen method | lux | Verified class workhorse |
| 8 | $CU = \frac{E_{avg} \times A}{N \times \Phi}$ | Coefficient of utilization | 0.4 – 0.7 | Spacing / layout optimisation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety:** All absolute-value bars in the table above were typeset as vertical bars. In raw text, KTU students should write $\vert x \vert$ rather than $|x|$ when working inside a markdown table cell, to avoid breaking the table renderer.

## 2.3 Why Maintenance Multiplies (Not Adds) Losses

The factors are **multiplicative** because the three loss mechanisms occur in series along the light path. Consider a photon: it is first generated by the lamp (loss = LLD), then passes through a dirty luminaire (loss = LDD), and finally there is a probability that *any given* lamp in the array is still alive (loss = LBOF). Series probabilities combine as the product, not the sum.

In engineering fields, this is identical to the **availability** of a series system in reliability theory. If each lamp-luminaire-cleaning subsystem has availability $a_i$, the overall availability is $\prod a_i$.

## 2.4 Maintenance Strategies

1. **Spot Replacement (BR — Burnout Replacement):** Replace lamps individually as they fail. Cheap in parts, expensive in labour, poor illuminance control.
2. **Group Replacement (GR):** Replace all lamps at a fixed interval (e.g., at 70–80 % of rated life) regardless of individual failure. LBOF = 1.0; clean, predictable illuminance. **This is the KTU-recommended industrial practice.**
3. **Overhaul Cleaning:** Cleaning luminaires during group relamping. Most efficient; halves labour cost.
4. **Combined (Group + Overhaul):** Industry gold standard.

> [!TIP]
> A classic 14-mark problem: *Compare spot replacement and group replacement strategies.* Always end with: *"Group replacement combined with simultaneous cleaning gives the highest maintained illuminance for the lowest life-cycle cost, justifying its use in industrial installations."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maintained Illuminance Equation

We begin from the lumen-method illuminance formula taught in Module 1/2 of the KTU syllabus, then explicitly introduce the maintenance factor.

### Step 1 — The Initial Illuminance (New Installation)

For a room with $N$ lamps, each delivering $\Phi$ lumens, the average illuminance on the working plane is given by the **Lumen Method**:

$$E_{initial} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot CU}{A}$$

where $CU$ is the Coefficient of Utilization, and $A$ is the working-plane area.

### Step 2 — Apply Lamp Lumen Depreciation

After time $t$, the actual flux per lamp is $\Phi(t) = \Phi_{0} \cdot LLD(t)$. The illuminance becomes:

$$E(t)_{lamp} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi_{0} \cdot LLD \cdot CU}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Luminaire Dirt Depreciation

Dirt on reflector/diffuser further attenuates the flux. Multiplicative reduction by $LDD$:

$$E(t)_{lamp+dirt} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi_{0} \cdot LLD \cdot LDD \cdot CU}{A}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Lamp Burnout

If only a fraction $LBOF$ of the $N$ lamps survive, the effective lamp count is $N \cdot LBOF$:

$$E_{maintained} = \frac{(N \cdot LBOF) \cdot \Phi_{0} \cdot LLD \cdot LDD \cdot CU}{A}$$

### Step 5 — Factor Out the Initial Illuminance

Collecting terms:

$$E_{maintained} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi_{0} \cdot CU}{A} \cdot (LLD \cdot LDD \cdot LBOF)$$

$$E_{maintained} = E_{initial} \cdot MF$$

This confirms the master relationship derived in SECTION 2.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Problem)

> **Problem (14 marks, Module 3):** A factory floor of $20 \text{ m} \times 12 \text{ m}$ is to be illuminated to a *maintained* illuminance of 200 lx. The lumen method gives an *initial* average illuminance of 280 lx with the proposed luminaire layout. Estimate the maintenance factor and the three constituent loss factors, given that the lamps are group-replaced every 18 months in a Category III (moderate) dirt environment using enclosed luminaires.

### Solution Walkthrough

**Given data:**

- $E_{maintained} = 200 \text{ lx}$
- $E_{initial} = 280 \text{ lx}$
- Group relamping interval: 18 months
- Dirt category: III
- Enclosed luminaire

**Step 1 — Compute overall MF**

$$MF = \frac{E_{maintained}}{E_{initial}} = \frac{200}{280} = 0.714$$

**Step 2 — Read LLD from lamp curves**

For typical fluorescent/industrial LED, LLD at 18 months of operation ≈ **0.90**.

**Step 3 — Read LDD from IES dirt-depreciation curves**

For enclosed luminaire, dirt category III, 18 months → LDD ≈ **0.80**.

**Step 4 — Compute LBOF**

Group replacement → **LBOF = 1.0** (justified because all lamps are renewed simultaneously; the *worst case* is when all are operational and dimmest).

**Step 5 — Verify**

$$MF_{calculated} = 0.90 \times 0.80 \times 1.00 = 0.72 \approx 0.714$$

The small discrepancy (0.72 vs 0.714) reflects the rounded chart values. **This is a perfect KTU board answer.**

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Maintenance Strategy

| Criterion | Spot Replacement | Group Replacement | Group + Overhaul |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Initial cost | Low | Moderate (spare lamps) | Moderate |
| Labour cost | High (frequent calls) | Low (one campaign) | Lowest (cleaning bundled) |
| Maintained illuminance | Poor (uncontrolled) | Good (predictable) | Excellent (cleanest optics) |
| LBOF value | < 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| Best application | Domestic, low-bay offices | Warehouses, street lighting | Heavy industry, clean rooms |
| KTU preference | Not preferred | Acceptable | **Gold Standard** |

## 3.4 Quick Derivation — Required Initial Illuminance

If the design code specifies a *maintained* illuminance $E_m$ and the maintenance factor is $MF$, the engineer must size the installation for an *initial* illuminance of:

$$E_i = \frac{E_m}{MF}$$

This follows directly from $E_m = E_i \cdot MF \Rightarrow E_i = E_m / MF$. KTU students are expected to invert this relationship during design calculations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Maintenance Factor Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Maintenance Factor MF] --> B[LLD: Lamp Lumen Depreciation]
    A --> C[LDD: Luminaire Dirt Depreciation]
    A --> D[LBOF: Lamp Burnout Factor]
    B --> B1[Filament evaporation]
    B --> B2[Phosphor degradation]
    B --> B3[LED lumen decay L70 L80 L90]
    C --> C1[Dust accumulation on reflector]
    C --> C2[Diffuser yellowing]
    C --> C3[Lens contamination]
    D --> D1[Spot replacement: fraction surviving]
    D --> D2[Group replacement: equals 1.0]
    A --> E[Maintained Illuminance Em equals Einit times MF]
    E --> F[Compliance with IS 3646 / National Building Code]
```

## 4.2 Maintenance Strategy Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Plan a New Lighting Installation] --> Q{Number of Lamps and Access Difficulty}
    Q -->|Few lamps, easy access| R[Spot Replacement BR Strategy]
    Q -->|Many lamps, difficult access| S[Group Replacement GR Strategy]
    S --> T{Cleaning Done at Same Time}
    T -->|Yes| U[Group plus Overhaul GOLD STANDARD]
    T -->|No| V[Group Replacement Alone]
    R --> W[High labour cost, low parts cost]
    U --> X[Lowest life cycle cost, highest maintained illuminance]
    V --> Y[Moderate cost, moderate maintained illuminance]
```

## 4.3 Time-Domain Illumination Decay Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time equals 0: New Installation] -->|LLD curve drops| T1[Time equals T1: L70 reached]
    T1 -->|Clean lens and replace lamps| T2[Time equals T1 plus cycle: Reset to 1.0]
    T2 -->|Next cycle decay begins| T3[Time equals 2T1: L70 again]
    T3 -->|Overhaul cleaning| T4[Time equals 2T1 plus cycle: Reset again]
    T0 -.->|Without maintenance| D1[Continuous decay to unsafe levels]
    D1 --> D2[Non-compliance and visual fatigue]
```

> [!TIP]
> The schematic above is what the KTU examiner expects when a question asks: *"Illustrate, with a sketch, how illuminance varies with time under (i) no maintenance and (ii) periodic maintenance."* Your block diagram must show a **sawtooth decay-and-reset** pattern for the maintained case and a smooth monotonic decay for the no-maintenance case.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Maintenance Factor" as used in illumination engineering. List its three components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Maintenance Factor (MF)** is the ratio of the illuminance on the working plane after a specified period of use to the illuminance obtained under the same conditions when the installation is new.

$$MF = \frac{E_{maintained}}{E_{initial}}$$

The three components are:
1. **Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD)** — fraction of initial lamp flux retained.
2. **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD)** — fraction of luminaire output after dirt accumulation.
3. **Lamp Burnout Factor (LBOF)** — fraction of lamps still operating.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *omit LBOF* and write only LLD and LDD. The third factor is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if missed.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is group replacement of lamps? Why is it preferred in industrial installations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Group replacement is a maintenance strategy in which **all lamps in an installation are replaced simultaneously at a fixed interval** (typically 70–80 % of rated life), regardless of whether individual lamps have failed.

It is preferred in industry because:
1. It makes **LBOF = 1.0**, giving a predictable, well-controlled maintained illuminance.
2. It permits **bundled cleaning of luminaires** (overhaul) during the same shutdown.
3. It **minimises labour cost** by replacing many lamps in a single planned campaign.
4. It **avoids the unsafe mid-cycle dark spots** of spot replacement.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question 1(A) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the three factors that constitute the Maintenance Factor. How are they combined mathematically and why?

**(b) [7 marks]** A workshop measures 30 m × 15 m with a working-plane height of 0.85 m. Twenty-eight twin-tube fluorescent luminaires, each carrying 2 × 36 W lamps (rated 3,350 lm per lamp initially), are ceiling-mounted 4 m above the working plane. The room has medium-dirt (Category III) characteristics, the luminaires are of enclosed type, and the design group-relamping interval is 24 months. If the Coefficient of Utilization for the room is 0.55, compute the *maintained* illuminance on the working plane. Assume LLD = 0.88, LDD = 0.78, LBOF = 1.0.

**Model Solution to 1(A):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The three factors are:

1. **Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD):** As a lamp ages, its luminous flux drops due to filament evaporation, phosphor deterioration, or LED chip degradation. LLD is the ratio of the lamp's flux at the end of the maintenance period to its initial 100-hour flux.
2. **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD):** Dirt, oil, and moisture deposited on reflector, diffuser, and lens surfaces reduce the luminaire's optical transmission. LDD is read from IES dirt-depreciation charts parameterised by the ambient dirt category and luminaire type.
3. **Lamp Burnout Factor (LBOF):** The fraction of lamps still operational at the end of the cycle. In a *group relamping* scheme, LBOF = 1.0; in a *spot relamping* scheme, LBOF < 1.0.

**Why multiplicative?** Because the three loss mechanisms occur *in series* along every photon's path. The probability of a photon surviving all three independent stages is the product of the individual survival probabilities — a result identical to series-system reliability in engineering.

$$MF = LLD \times LDD \times LBOF$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 1: Total lamp count $N = 28 \text{ luminaires} \times 2 \text{ lamps each} = 56 \text{ lamps}$.

Step 2: Total initial lumens $\Phi_{total} = 56 \times 3{,}350 = 187{,}600 \text{ lm}$.

Step 3: Compute $MF$:
$$MF = 0.88 \times 0.78 \times 1.00 = 0.6864$$

Step 4: Working-plane area $A = 30 \times 15 = 450 \text{ m}^2$.

Step 5: Apply the lumen method with MF:
$$E_{maintained} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi_{0} \cdot CU \cdot MF}{A} = \frac{56 \times 3{,}350 \times 0.55 \times 0.6864}{450}$$

Step 6: Numerator computation:
$$56 \times 3{,}350 = 187{,}600$$
$$187{,}600 \times 0.55 = 103{,}180$$
$$103{,}180 \times 0.6864 = 70{,}822.7$$

Step 7: Final division:
$$E_{maintained} = \frac{70{,}822.7}{450} \approx 157.4 \text{ lx}$$

**Answer:** $E_{maintained} \approx 157.4 \text{ lx}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question 1A part b):** Students frequently forget to multiply by MF and only compute *initial* illuminance. The term "maintained" in the question is the trigger — it must appear in your formula. Lose 4 marks instantly if MF is omitted.

### Question 1(B) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 14 Marks] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare spot replacement and group replacement of lamps. Under what conditions is each strategy recommended?

**(b) [7 marks]** A maintenance engineer observes that the illuminance in an office has dropped from an initial 480 lx to 380 lx after 20 months of operation. The lamps are replaced individually as they fail. If the spot-replacement LBOF is 0.92, and the lamp manufacturer's data gives LLD = 0.85 at 20 months, determine the LDD. Comment on whether the current cleaning schedule is adequate.

**Model Solution to 1(B):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Aspect | Spot Replacement | Group Replacement |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Replace only failed lamps | Replace all lamps at fixed interval |
| Cost — Parts | Low | High (spare stock) |
| Cost — Labour | High (frequent calls) | Low (one campaign) |
| LBOF | < 1.0 (unpredictable) | 1.0 (predictable) |
| Illuminance | Uncontrolled dips | Smooth, predictable |
| Best For | Domestic, low bays, easy access | Industrial, high bays, difficult access |
| Bundled cleaning | Not feasible | Feasible (overhaul) |

**Recommendation rule of thumb:** Use group replacement whenever the **number of lamps > 50** *or* the **luminaire mounting height > 6 m** *or* the **area > 500 m²**. Otherwise, spot replacement is acceptable.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 1: Compute MF from the observed illuminances:
$$MF = \frac{E_{maintained}}{E_{initial}} = \frac{380}{480} = 0.7917$$

Step 2: Use the master equation:
$$MF = LLD \times LDD \times LBOF$$
$$0.7917 = 0.85 \times LDD \times 0.92$$

Step 3: Solve for LDD:
$$LDD = \frac{0.7917}{0.85 \times 0.92} = \frac{0.7917}{0.7820} = 1.012$$

Step 4: **Physical interpretation:** $LDD > 1$ is *physically impossible*. The result signals that either (a) the observed "initial" 480 lx was itself a degraded value (the room was not freshly cleaned when commissioned), or (b) the LLD figure used was too pessimistic. The cleaning schedule is therefore *likely adequate* — the design maintenance factor of ~0.79 is being met.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question 1B part b):** Students often write "the cleaning is inadequate" mechanically. The correct KTU-recommended answer is to *interpret* the impossible LDD as a measurement/design inconsistency and explain. Lose 2 marks if the result is not critically examined.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** Ratio of maintained illuminance to initial illuminance. Always ≤ 1.
- **Master formula:** $MF = LLD \times LDD \times LBOF$.
- **Inversion for design:** $E_{initial} = E_{maintained} / MF$.
- **Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD):** Read from lamp manufacturer curves; intrinsic to lamp technology.
- **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD):** Read from IES dirt-depreciation charts; depends on dirt category + luminaire type + elapsed time.
- **Lamp Burnout Factor (LBOF):** Equals 1.0 in group replacement; < 1.0 in spot replacement.
- **Dirt Categories:** IES uses six categories — I (Clean) to VI (Very Dirty). KTU often uses Roman numerals I–VI.
- **Group replacement interval:** Typically 70–80 % of rated lamp life.
- **Combined strategy:** Group replacement + simultaneous cleaning (overhaul) is the **gold-standard industrial practice**.
- **Why multiplicative?** Series-system reliability; photons traverse three independent loss stages in succession.
- **LED-specific term:** $L_{p}$ rating (e.g., $L_{70}$ = 70 % flux retained at rated hours).
- **IES-1984 legacy term:** *Light Loss Factor (LLF)* ≡ current *Maintenance Factor (MF)*.
- **Lumen-method link:** $E_{maintained} = (N \cdot \Phi \cdot CU \cdot MF) / A$.
- **Latex safety:** In markdown tables use $\vert x \vert$, never $|x|$.
- **Examiner red flags:** Forgetting LBOF; forgetting to multiply initial illuminance by MF; mis-stating that MF is a constant across all luminaire types.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Street Lighting: Types of Street and Their Level of Illumination Required
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Street Lighting: Types of Street and Their Level of Illumination Required

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Street lighting** is a specialized branch of outdoor illumination engineering concerned with the design, installation, and operation of luminaires on public roads, highways, and pedestrian pathways to provide safe, efficient, and uniform visibility during nighttime hours. According to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 1944: 1970 / 1981)** and the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES) of North America**, street lighting is classified based on the **traffic density**, **road geometry**, and the **surrounding environmental conditions** of the thoroughfare.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *"Street lighting is the deliberate application of artificial light sources to roadways, footpaths, and public spaces to ensure visibility of obstacles, pedestrians, and vehicles at night, classified into distinct groups (A to E) based on the nature of road usage and the prescribed level of service luminance."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine walking through three different roads at night:
1. A **national highway** (broad, fast cars zipping past) — needs bright, well-distributed light, like a stadium's illumination.
2. A **city arterial road** (medium traffic, intersections) — needs moderate, focused light, like a well-lit classroom.
3. A **quiet residential lane** (rare vehicles, parked cycles) — needs just enough light to see the curb, like a reading lamp by your bedside.

Just as you would never use a reading lamp to illuminate a cricket stadium, you cannot use the same lighting standard for a highway and a by-lane. **Street classification** is essentially the engineering answer to *"How bright is bright enough for this particular road?"* — too dim causes accidents, too bright wastes energy and causes glare.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Luminance (L):** Measured in **cd/m²** (candela per square meter) — the brightness of the road surface as seen by the driver.
- **Illuminance (E):** Measured in **lux (lx)** or **lm/m²** — the light falling on the road surface.
- **Uniformity Ratio (U₀):** The ratio of **minimum to average luminance** (L_min / L_avg). For Indian roads, this is typically **0.4**.
- **Glare Index (TI):** A measure of disability glare; values below **30** are acceptable for main roads.
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI):** A metric for light source quality; **Ra ≥ 60** is preferred for streets.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** Typically **0.7 to 0.8** for outdoor luminaires (since dust and weathering reduce output).
- **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR or S/H):** Governed by the luminaire's photometric distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard **ambient temperature** assumption for street-lighting design is **27°C**, and the standard **operating voltage** is **230 V AC ± 10%** as per Indian Electricity Rules.

### 1.4 Why Street Classification Matters

Different roads serve different transport functions. A pedestrian needs to see facial features at 4 meters; a driver at 60 km/h needs to see an obstacle at 100+ meters. The Class of road (A through E) directly dictates:
- The **average luminance level (L_avg)** required.
- The **uniformity** demanded.
- The **glare restriction** allowed.
- The **type of luminaire** (cut-off category).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Streets (IS 1944 / KTU 2024 Module 4)

Indian Standards (IS 1944) classify roads into **five groups (A, B, C, D, E)** based on traffic volume, pedestrian usage, and importance:

| Street Group | Description | Typical Examples |
|--------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Group A** | Important main roads with very heavy and fast-moving traffic; mixed traffic and pedestrians | National Highways, major city arterials |
| **Group B** | Main roads with considerable traffic, allowing higher speeds | State highways, urban trunk roads |
| **Group C** | Connector roads between main and local roads | Collector streets, district roads |
| **Group D** | Local roads primarily for residential access | Residential streets, by-lanes |
| **Group E** | Lightly used roads in rural or semi-urban areas | Village lanes, internal colony roads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the types of streets as per IS 1944."* Always remember the **A–E ordering from heaviest to lightest traffic**.

## 2.2 Illumination / Luminance Levels Required

The **illuminance (in lux)** prescribed for each class of road is as follows:

| Street Group | Average Illumination (lux) | Uniformity Ratio (L_min / L_avg) | Recommended Lamp Type |
|--------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Group A** | **30 lux** | ≥ 0.4 | High-Pressure Sodium Vapour (HPSV) / LED |
| **Group B** | **15 lux** | ≥ 0.4 | HPSV / Metal Halide / LED |
| **Group C** | **10 lux** | ≥ 0.4 | HPSV / Fluorescent / LED |
| **Group D** | **8 lux** | ≥ 0.4 | Fluorescent / LED |
| **Group E** | **4 lux** | ≥ 0.25 | Fluorescent / CFL / LED |

> [!NOTE]
> The values above align with the **Indian Standard IS 1944 (Parts 1 & 2)** and are also referenced in the **National Lighting Code (NBC 2016, Part 8 Section 4)**. Modern LED streetlights, owing to higher efficacy (≥ 120 lm/W), are now preferred over HPSV for new installations.

## 2.3 Luminaires Used in Street Lighting

Luminaires are classified by their **light distribution** (cut-off category), which determines glare and overspill:

- **Cut-off luminaires:** The intensity is restricted to 90°–180° zone; no light above 80°. Best for **Group A** roads. Glare-free.
- **Semi-cut-off luminaires:** Intensity at 80° is restricted; some light at 90°. Used for **Group B & C** roads. Balanced performance.
- **Non-cut-off luminaires:** Light emitted freely in all directions. Used for **Group D & E** roads. Cheaper but causes glare.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For high-speed traffic (Group A), always choose **cut-off** or **semi-cut-off** luminaires. Failure to do so causes disability glare and is a common 3-mark question topic.

## 2.4 Arrangements of Street Lighting

The geometric layout of luminaires along a road is critical. There are **four standard arrangements**:

1. **Single-Side Arrangement:** Luminaires on one side only. Used for road width **W ≤ Mounting Height (H)**. S = H × (L/H ratio).
2. **Staggered (Zig-Zag) Arrangement:** Luminaires alternate on both sides. Used for road width **H < W < 2H**. Spacing is calculated differently.
3. **Opposite (Twin) Arrangement:** Luminaires directly opposite each other on both sides. Used for **W ≥ 2H**.
4. **Central Arrangement (Median):** Luminaires suspended on a central catenary. Used for dual carriageways with wide medians.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| $$S = W \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{H}\right)$$ | S = spacing, W = road width, L = spacing factor, H = mounting height | Single-side arrangement |
| $$S = 2 \cdot W \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{H}\right)$$ | Same symbols | Staggered arrangement |
| $$S = 2 \cdot W \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{H}\right)$$ | Same symbols (effectively) | Opposite arrangement (often simplified) |
| $$E_{avg} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot N \cdot \eta \cdot MF}{A \cdot d}$$ | φ = lamp lumens, N = number of lamps, η = utilization factor, MF = maintenance factor, A = area, d = depreciation factor | Light flux method |
| $$L_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot A_i}{\sum A_i}$$ | L_i = luminance at point i, A_i = area element | Luminance averaging |
| $$U_0 = \dfrac{L_{min}}{L_{avg}}$$ | U₀ = overall uniformity, L_min = minimum luminance | Uniformity check |
| $$U_l = \dfrac{L_{min}}{L_{max}}$$ | U_l = longitudinal uniformity | Required ≥ 0.7 for motorways |
| $$\text{Number of Lamps per km} = \dfrac{1000}{S}$$ | S = spacing in meters | Quantity estimation |
| $$TI = 65 \cdot \dfrac{L_v}{L_{avg}^{0.8}}$$ | L_v = veiling luminance | Threshold Increment (Glare) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** In the markdown table above, all absolute value bars and ratios are written using `\vert` or `\dfrac{}{}` syntax, NEVER using the bare pipe `|` symbol inside table cells, to prevent table-breaking.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban planning:** Municipal corporations use these classifications to tender contracts and decide budget allocation per km of road.
- **Energy audit:** Helps compute total connected load and electricity bill (e.g., 30 W LED every 25 m over 10 km).
- **Solar street lights:** Classification directly informs battery sizing (autonomy hours) and panel wattage.
- **Smart city projects:** Adaptive street lighting (dimming based on traffic) uses Group classifications to set dimming profiles.
- **Accident analysis:** Forensic engineers use the recommended lux levels to determine if inadequate lighting caused an accident.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spacing Between Luminaires (Single-Side Arrangement)

### Given:
- Mounting height of luminaire, **H** (meters).
- Width of the road, **W** (meters).
- Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR) of the luminaire, **L/H** (read from manufacturer's polar curve).

### To Find:
- **S**, the spacing between two consecutive luminaires in meters.

### Derivation:

For a luminaire mounted at height **H** above the road, the cone of light falling on the road surface covers a length of approximately **H × (L/H ratio)** along the road direction. This ratio is provided in the photometric data sheet as the **"Spacing to Mounting Height Ratio"** (often denoted as K or SHR).

For the **single-side arrangement**, the spacing **S** is given directly by the manufacturer's data, scaled by the mounting height:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= H \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{H}\right) \\
S &= H \cdot k
\end{aligned}
$$

Where **k = L / H** is the dimensionless spacing factor.

For example, if a luminaire has a SHR of **3.0** and is mounted at **H = 8 m**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 8 \cdot 3.0 \\
S &= 24 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means luminaires are placed **24 m apart** along the road on a single side.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spacing for Staggered (Zig-Zag) Arrangement

For roads where the width **W** is between **H** and **2H**, a staggered arrangement is used. The luminaire on one side covers half-width **W/2**. The spacing is calculated so that the half-width is properly illuminated.

### Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2 \cdot W \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{H}\right) \\
S &= 2 \cdot W \cdot k
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, with **W = 12 m**, **H = 8 m**, **k = 3**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2 \cdot 12 \cdot 3 \\
S &= 72 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

But this is too sparse. The correct formula is often written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{staggered} &= H \cdot k \quad \text{(in the direction of the road)} \\
\text{Offset between sides} &= W
\end{aligned}
$$

Where luminaires on side B are placed **midway** between luminaires on side A.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common student error:** Students multiply by 2×W blindly. The actual placement in staggered mode treats the **road width W as the lateral offset**, while the longitudinal spacing remains **S = H × k**. Always sketch a top-view to avoid mistakes.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Luminaires per Kilometer

### Given:
- Spacing **S** in meters.

### Derivation:

For a road of length **1 km = 1000 m**, the number of spacing intervals is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{intervals} &= \dfrac{1000}{S} \\
N_{luminaires} &= N_{intervals} + 1 \quad \text{(if counting both ends)} \\
N_{luminaires} &\approx \dfrac{1000}{S} \quad \text{(for long roads, end effect negligible)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example:
If **S = 25 m**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{luminaires} &= \dfrac{1000}{25} \\
N_{luminaires} &= 40 \text{ luminaires per km}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Average Illumination Using Light Flux Method

For street lighting design, the **Light Flux Method** (also called the **Lumen Method**) is the most commonly taught KTU problem.

### Given:
- Area to be illuminated: **A** = W × S (m²)
- Required illumination level: **E** (lux)
- Lamp flux: **φ** (lumens)
- Number of lamps per luminaire: **n** (typically 1)
- Utilization factor: **η** (from photometric chart, dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- Maintenance factor: **MF** (0.6 to 0.8)
- Depreciation factor: **d** = 1 / MF (typically 1.25 to 1.67)

### Derivation:

Total flux received by the road surface = E × A.

Total flux emitted by lamps = n × N × φ × η × MF / d.

Equating these (or rearranging to find N, the number of luminaires):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \cdot A &= \dfrac{n \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}{d} \\
N &= \dfrac{E \cdot A \cdot d}{n \cdot \phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example Problem:

**Question:** A straight road 1 km long and 15 m wide belongs to Group B (15 lux required). Luminaires contain 250 W HPSV lamps each rated at 25,000 lumens. Utilization factor = 0.4, MF = 0.8. Find the number of luminaires and spacing.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate area per km:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 1000 \cdot 15 = 15000 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Apply the light flux formula with d = 1/MF = 1.25:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{E \cdot A \cdot d}{n \cdot \phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF} \\
N &= \dfrac{15 \cdot 15000 \cdot 1.25}{1 \cdot 25000 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.8} \\
N &= \dfrac{281250}{8000} \\
N &= 35.16
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Round up to **N = 36 luminaires per km**.

Step 4 — Calculate spacing:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{1000}{N} = \dfrac{1000}{36} = 27.78 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adopt S ≈ 27 m** with **36 luminaires** along the 1 km road.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Glare Calculation (Threshold Increment, TI)

The **Threshold Increment (TI)** quantifies loss of visibility due to glare from street luminaires.

### Formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
TI \,\,(percentage) &= 65 \cdot \dfrac{L_v}{L_{avg}^{0.8}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **L_v** = veiling luminance (cd/m²), which depends on the glare source intensity and the angle from the line of sight.
- **L_avg** = average road surface luminance (cd/m²).

### Example:

If **L_v = 0.5 cd/m²** and **L_avg = 1.0 cd/m²**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
TI &= 65 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{1.0^{0.8}} \\
TI &= 65 \cdot 0.5 \\
TI &= 32.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For Group A roads, the acceptable **TI ≤ 10%**. Since 32.5% > 10%, this design fails the glare criterion and would need revision (e.g., cut-off luminaires).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests TI calculation as a 7-mark problem in Part B. Always compute L_avg^0.8 correctly (note it is a power, not a multiplication).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Arrangements

| Parameter | Single-Side | Staggered | Opposite | Central (Median) |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Road Width Suitability** | W ≤ H | H < W < 2H | W ≥ 2H | Wide dual carriageway |
| **Number of Luminaires** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | Medium |
| **Uniformity** | Poor on far side | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Capital Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | Medium |
| **Maintenance Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | Medium (lane closures) |
| **Aesthetic Appeal** | Low | High | High | Very High |
| **Glare Control** | Difficult | Moderate | Good | Good |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Street Lighting System

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of a street lighting design process — from classification to commissioning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Identify Road Geometry] --> A1[Classify Road Type]
    A1 --> B1[Group A: Heavy Traffic]
    A1 --> B2[Group B: Main Roads]
    A1 --> B3[Group C: Collector Roads]
    A1 --> B4[Group D: Local Roads]
    A1 --> B5[Group E: Lightly Used]
    B1 --> C1[Select Illumination Level 30 lux]
    B2 --> C2[Select Illumination Level 15 lux]
    B3 --> C3[Select Illumination Level 10 lux]
    B4 --> C4[Select Illumination Level 8 lux]
    B5 --> C5[Select Illumination Level 4 lux]
    C1 --> D1[Choose Cut-off Luminaire]
    C2 --> D2[Choose Semi-cut-off Luminaire]
    C3 --> D3[Choose Semi-cut-off Luminaire]
    C4 --> D4[Choose Non-cut-off Luminaire]
    C5 --> D5[Choose Non-cut-off Luminaire]
    D1 --> E1[Decide Mounting Height H]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1{Choose Arrangement}
    F1 --> G1[Single Side if W le H]
    F1 --> G2[Staggered if H lt W lt 2H]
    F1 --> G3[Opposite if W ge 2H]
    F1 --> G4[Central Median if Wide]
    G1 --> H1[Compute Spacing S]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Compute Number of Luminaires per km]
    I1 --> J1[Apply Light Flux Method for Verification]
    J1 --> K1[Check Uniformity and Glare Limits]
    K1 --> L1[End: Approved Design]
```

## 4.2 Top-View Schematic of the Four Arrangements

The following diagram illustrates the four geometric arrangements as seen from above.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SS[Single Side Arrangement]
        SS_A[Road Edge] --> SS_B[Luminaire at 0m]
        SS_B --> SS_C[Luminaire at 25m]
        SS_C --> SS_D[Luminaire at 50m]
    end
    subgraph ST[Staggered Arrangement]
        ST_A[Luminaire Side A 0m] --> ST_B[Luminaire Side B 12m]
        ST_B --> ST_C[Luminaire Side A 25m]
    end
    subgraph OP[Opposite Arrangement]
        OP_A[Side A 0m] --- OP_B[Side B 0m]
        OP_C[Side A 25m] --- OP_D[Side B 25m]
    end
    subgraph CT[Central Median Arrangement]
        CT_A[Center Luminaire 0m] --> CT_B[Center Luminaire 25m]
    end
```

## 4.3 Cross-Section View (Block Schematic) of Cut-off vs. Non-cut-off Luminaire

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NC[Non-Cut-off Luminaire]
        NC1[Light Source] --> NC2[Light Spread 360 degrees]
        NC2 --> NC3[Severe Upward Glare]
    end
    subgraph SC[Semi-Cut-off Luminaire]
        SC1[Light Source] --> SC2[Controlled Spread up to 90 degrees]
        SC2 --> SC3[Reduced Upward Glare]
    end
    subgraph CO[Cut-off Luminaire]
        CO1[Light Source] --> CO2[Restricted Spread Below 80 degrees]
        CO2 --> CO3[Minimal Glare Fully Downward]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of Light Flux Method Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP1[Input Road Area A m2] --> CALC
    INP2[Input Required Illumination E lux] --> CALC
    INP3[Input Lamp Flux phi lumens] --> CALC
    INP4[Input Utilization Factor eta] --> CALC
    INP5[Input Maintenance Factor MF] --> CALC
    CALC[Apply N = E A d divided by n phi eta MF] --> OUT1[Number of Luminaires N]
    OUT1 --> SPAC[Spacing S = 1000 divided by N]
    SPAC --> UNIF[Check Uniformity U0 ge 0.4]
    UNIF --> GLARE[Check Glare TI le 10 percent for Group A]
    GLARE --> DONE[Design Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node identifiers use **alphanumeric prefixed labels** (e.g., `INP1`, `CALC`, `OUT1`) to avoid reserved keyword conflicts. No markdown formatting is embedded inside double-quoted labels, ensuring smooth rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]
**"List the different groups of streets as per IS 1944 and state the average illumination level required for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per IS 1944, streets are classified into **five groups** (A through E) based on traffic volume, with the following illumination levels:

| Street Group | Average Illumination (lux) |
|--------------|----------------------------|
| **Group A** — Important main roads with heavy traffic | **30 lux** |
| **Group B** — Main roads with considerable traffic | **15 lux** |
| **Group C** — Connector roads | **10 lux** |
| **Group D** — Local roads | **8 lux** |
| **Group E** — Lightly used rural roads | **4 lux** |

*(1 mark for listing groups, 1 mark for illumination table, 1 mark for accurate mapping.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**"Differentiate between cut-off, semi-cut-off, and non-cut-off luminaires used in street lighting."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Cut-off luminaire:** The luminous intensity is completely cut off at **80°** from the downward vertical; no light is emitted above this angle. Suitable for **Group A** roads. **Glare-free**, expensive.
- **Semi-cut-off luminaire:** Intensity at **90°** is restricted to a permissible value (≤ 1000 cd); some light is emitted at angles between 80° and 90°. Used for **Group B & C** roads. **Moderate glare control**, moderate cost.
- **Non-cut-off luminaire:** Light is emitted freely in all directions with no restriction. Used for **Group D & E** roads. **High glare**, cheapest option.

*(1 mark per type with distinguishing characteristic.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In street-lighting numerical problems, students commonly lose marks at three points: (1) forgetting to convert **Maintenance Factor (MF) to Depreciation Factor (d = 1/MF)** in the light flux formula, (2) using **road length × full width** as area when the problem specifies only one carriageway, and (3) reporting the number of luminaires as a decimal instead of rounding up to the next whole number. Always cross-check units, round **upwards**, and explicitly state assumptions.

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper]
**A straight city arterial road is 1.2 km long and 12 m wide. It is classified as Group B (15 lux average required). HPSV lamps of 250 W rating, each emitting 28,000 lumens, are proposed in semi-cut-off luminaires. The utilization factor is 0.45 and the maintenance factor is 0.8. Determine:**
**(a) The number of luminaires required per km and the spacing between them. (7 marks)**
**(b) The suitability of staggered vs. opposite arrangement if the mounting height is 10 m. Justify with calculations. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- E = 15 lux
- A = 1000 × 12 = 12,000 m² (per km)
- φ = 28,000 lumens
- η = 0.45
- MF = 0.8 ⇒ d = 1 / 0.8 = 1.25
- n = 1 (one lamp per luminaire)

**Step 1 — Apply the light flux formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{E \cdot A \cdot d}{n \cdot \phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF} \\
N &= \dfrac{15 \cdot 12000 \cdot 1.25}{1 \cdot 28000 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 0.8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 15 \cdot 12000 \cdot 1.25 \\
&= 15 \cdot 15000 \\
&= 225000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 1 \cdot 28000 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 0.8 \\
&= 28000 \cdot 0.36 \\
&= 10080
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute N:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{225000}{10080} \\
N &= 22.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to the next whole number:**

$$
N = 23 \text{ luminaires per km}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute spacing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{1000}{N} = \dfrac{1000}{23} \\
S &\approx 43.48 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adopt S ≈ 43 m** in practice.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- Mounting height H = 10 m
- Road width W = 12 m

**Step 1 — Compare W with H and 2H:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 10 \text{ m} \\
2H &= 20 \text{ m} \\
W &= 12 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **H < W < 2H** (i.e., 10 < 12 < 20), the road falls in the **staggered arrangement** category as per IS guidelines.

**Step 2 — Verify with the spacing-to-height ratio:**

Assume the semi-cut-off luminaire has a SHR (L/H) = 3.0 (typical for this category).

For **single-side arrangement:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{single} &= W \cdot k = 12 \cdot 3.0 = 36 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **staggered arrangement**, the longitudinal spacing is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{staggered} &= H \cdot k = 10 \cdot 3.0 = 30 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the required spacing:**

The required spacing from Part (a) is **S ≈ 43 m**. Since the staggered arrangement provides more uniform coverage and the road width (12 m) is between H and 2H, the **staggered arrangement is the most appropriate choice**.

**Opposite arrangement** is overkill (and expensive) for a 12 m road — it is justified only when W ≥ 2H = 20 m.

**Conclusion:** Use **staggered (zig-zag) arrangement** with luminaires on both sides offset by half the longitudinal spacing (≈ 15 m apart on the same side, 12 m laterally across the road).

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]
**(a) Explain the design procedure for street lighting with reference to the light flux method. State all relevant formulas. (7 marks)**
**(b) A road is 800 m long and 7 m wide. It belongs to Group D (8 lux). Luminaires use 70 W LED lamps each rated 7000 lumens. Utilization factor = 0.5, MF = 0.75. Calculate the number of luminaires and the spacing. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Design Procedure for Street Lighting (Light Flux Method):**

**Step 1 — Classify the road:** Identify the group (A–E) per IS 1944 based on traffic volume and pedestrian use.

**Step 2 — Determine required illumination level:** Use the standard table (Group A: 30 lux, B: 15 lux, C: 10 lux, D: 8 lux, E: 4 lux).

**Step 3 — Choose luminaire type:** Select cut-off / semi-cut-off / non-cut-off based on the group and glare restrictions.

**Step 4 — Decide mounting height (H):** Typically **H = W** (road width) for single-side; **H = 0.6 W to W** for staggered.

**Step 5 — Choose arrangement:** Single-side, staggered, opposite, or central based on **W, H, and 2H** comparison.

**Step 6 — Compute area (A):**

$$
A = L \cdot W
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the light flux formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{E \cdot A \cdot d}{n \cdot \phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **E** = required illuminance (lux)
- **A** = area (m²)
- **d** = depreciation factor = 1 / MF
- **n** = number of lamps per luminaire
- **φ** = lamp flux (lumens)
- **η** = utilization factor
- **MF** = maintenance factor

**Step 8 — Compute spacing S:**

$$
S = \dfrac{1000}{N} \text{ m (per km basis)}
$$

**Step 9 — Verify uniformity and glare:** Ensure **U₀ ≥ 0.4** and **TI ≤ 10%** (for Group A).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- L = 800 m, W = 7 m
- E = 8 lux
- φ = 7000 lumens
- η = 0.5
- MF = 0.75 ⇒ d = 1 / 0.75 = 1.333
- n = 1

**Step 1 — Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 800 \cdot 7 = 5600 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply light flux formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{E \cdot A \cdot d}{n \cdot \phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF} \\
N &= \dfrac{8 \cdot 5600 \cdot 1.333}{1 \cdot 7000 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.75}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= 8 \cdot 5600 \cdot 1.333 \\
&= 44800 \cdot 1.333 \\
&= 59718.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Den} &= 1 \cdot 7000 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.75 \\
&= 7000 \cdot 0.375 \\
&= 2625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute N:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{59718.4}{2625} \\
N &= 22.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Round up:**

$$
N = 23 \text{ luminaires}
$$

**Step 7 — Spacing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{800}{23} = 34.78 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adopt S ≈ 35 m** with **23 luminaires** for the 800 m road.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Five street groups** (IS 1944): **A, B, C, D, E** — heaviest to lightest traffic.
- **Illumination levels:** **A = 30 lux, B = 15 lux, C = 10 lux, D = 8 lux, E = 4 lux** (must memorize verbatim).
- **Uniformity ratio U₀ = L_min / L_avg ≥ 0.4** for all major roads.
- **Luminaire cut-off categories:** Cut-off (Group A), Semi-cut-off (B & C), Non-cut-off (D & E).
- **Arrangements and width criteria:**
  * **Single-side** if **W ≤ H**
  * **Staggered** if **H < W < 2H**
  * **Opposite** if **W ≥ 2H**
  * **Central median** for wide dual carriageways.
- **Spacing formula (single-side):** S = W × (L/H).
- **Spacing formula (staggered):** S = H × (L/H) longitudinally; lateral offset = W.
- **Number of luminaires per km:** N = 1000 / S.
- **Light flux formula:** N = (E × A × d) / (n × φ × η × MF), with **d = 1 / MF** (commonly missed).
- **Glare control:** TI = 65 × (L_v / L_avg^0.8); acceptable **TI ≤ 10%** for Group A.
- **Maintenance factor** is dimensionless and < 1; **depreciation factor** is its reciprocal (> 1).
- **Always round UP** the number of luminaires — never down.
- **Energy-efficient choice:** Modern **LED streetlights (≥ 120 lm/W)** are replacing HPSV (80–100 lm/W) worldwide.
- **SI units to remember:** lux (illuminance), cd/m² (luminance), lumen (flux), candela (intensity).
- **KTU favorite trick question:** *"The depreciation factor is the reciprocal of the maintenance factor."* — always state this explicitly in derivations.
- **Real-world check:** Most Indian municipal corporations have switched to **semi-cut-off LED luminaires** for Group B and C roads, with **150 W HPSV or 120 W LED** at 10 m mounting height as the standard tender specification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terms Related to Street Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Terms Related to Street Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Street Lighting" Engineering?

**Street lighting** is a specialized branch of illumination engineering that deals with the design, layout, and photometric distribution of light on **public roads, highways, and byways** for nighttime visibility, safety, and security. Unlike indoor lighting (which floods a small enclosed volume), street lighting has to project a controlled, asymmetric beam of light over a **linear, horizontal surface** (the road) while simultaneously minimizing glare to the driver.

The design of a street-lighting installation revolves around a *language of its own* — a set of geometric, photometric, and electrical terms. The KTU 2024 scheme classifies these under **Module 4** because they are the foundation upon which numerical design problems (lumens per metre, pole spacing, illumination uniformity) are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4, Course Outcome **CO4** — *“Apply the principles of street lighting and flood lighting in real-world installations.”* Mastery of these terms is the prerequisite for every numerical in this module.

---

## 1.2 The Core Terms — Formal Definitions

The following terms constitute the **vocabulary of street-lighting design** as per IES (Illuminating Engineering Society) standards, which the KTU syllabus follows.

### A. Geometric Terms (Hardware-Side)

| # | Term | Formal Definition |
|---|------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Mounting Height (H)** | The vertical distance, in **metres (m)**, measured from the **road surface** to the **light centre (luminous centre)** of the luminaire. |
| 2 | **Pole Spacing / Span (S)** | The horizontal distance, in **metres (m)**, between two consecutive lamp poles along the road. |
| 3 | **Overhang (OH)** | The horizontal projection of the **bracket arm**, measured from the centre of the pole (or edge of the kerb) to the luminous centre of the luminaire. |
| 4 | **Bracket Arm (BA)** | The horizontal metallic arm that holds the luminaire away from the pole. |
| 5 | **Throw (D)** | The forward (or backward) distance, along the road axis, over which a luminaire is designed to cast its useful beam. |
| 6 | **Light Centre / Luminous Centre** | The point inside the lamp from which the light can be considered to radiate uniformly in all directions. |
| 7 | **Road Width (W)** | The horizontal width of the carriageway to be illuminated, in **metres (m)**. |

### B. Photometric Terms (Light-Side)

| # | Term | Formal Definition |
|---|------|-------------------|
| 8 | **Watt per Metre (W/m)** | The installed electrical power consumed per metre length of road. Unit: **W·m⁻¹**. |
| 9 | **Lumen per Metre (lm/m)** | The total luminous flux emitted per metre length of road. Unit: **lm·m⁻¹**. |
| 10 | **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (S/H)** | The dimensionless ratio of pole spacing to mounting height, used as a *design constant* to ensure overlapping beam patterns. |
| 11 | **Uniformity Ratio** | The ratio of the **minimum** illumination on the road surface to the **average** illumination. Higher is better. |
| 12 | **Luminance (L)** | The luminous intensity per unit projected area of the road, perceived by the driver's eye, expressed in **cd/m²**. |
| 13 | **Cut-off, Semi Cut-off, Non Cut-off** | Optical classifications of luminaires based on the maximum permissible **glare angle** above the horizontal. |
| 14 | **Utilization Factor (UF)** | The ratio of the luminous flux reaching the road surface to the total flux emitted by the lamp. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Street as a Concert Hall":** Imagine a long, narrow concert stage (the road) where every audience seat must see the singer clearly. The "spotlights" are the street-lighting luminaires. The **mounting height** is how high the spotlight is hung; the **throw** is how far down the stage it reaches; the **overhang** is how far the spotlight is pulled forward over the audience; and the **pole spacing** is how far apart the spotlights are hung. If the spacing is too large, a "dark patch" appears on the stage — just like a dark zone on a road. The **S/H ratio** is the "rule of thumb" that tells the lighting designer how to space the spotlights so every seat (every square metre of the road) is illuminated evenly. This is exactly the logic used in street-lighting design.

---

## 1.3 Significance for KTU Examination

In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 typically carries a **15-mark slot** in the End-Semester Examination (ESE) under CO4. A common 7-mark question is *"Explain the terms used in street lighting with a neat sketch"*, while 14-mark questions often ask students to **compute pole spacing, number of lamps, and verify S/H ratios** for given road dimensions. Therefore, memorizing these terms and their inter-relationships is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Anatomy of a Street-Lighting Pole

A single street-lighting installation is essentially a 3-D geometric problem. Let us dissect the typical configuration:

- A **vertical pole** of length $H$ is erected on the kerb.
- A **horizontal bracket arm** of length $OH$ extends outward from the pole.
- A **luminaire** is fixed at the end of the bracket — its **luminous centre** is at height $H$ and horizontal distance $OH$ from the pole base.
- The luminaire emits light in an **asymmetric pattern**, throwing a beam along the road.

The three most important geometric relationships are:

1. **Mounting Height ($H$)** — selected first; usually 6 m to 12 m for urban roads and 10 m to 30 m for highways.
2. **Overhang ($OH$)** — typically **0.25 H to 0.30 H** for the standard staggered arrangement. It is used to push the maximum intensity toward the road centre, improving uniformity.
3. **Pole Spacing ($S$)** — the last parameter calculated; constrained by the **S/H ratio**.

---

## 2.2 The Spacing-to-Height Ratio (S/H) — The Heart of the Design

The **S/H ratio** is a **dimensionless design constant** that ensures the illumination pattern of one luminaire overlaps sufficiently with the next, eliminating dark patches on the road. Standard values (as per IES recommendations) are:

| Arrangement Type | Recommended S/H Ratio | Reason |
|------------------|------------------------|--------|
| **Single-Side** | **1.0 to 1.5** | All light comes from one side; needs tighter spacing to illuminate the far kerb. |
| **Staggered** | **1.5 to 1.75** | Light comes alternately from both sides, so wider spacing is allowed. |
| **Opposite** | **1.75 to 2.0** | Light comes symmetrically from both sides, allowing the widest spacing. |
| **Central (Median)** | **1.0 to 1.5** | Light is thrown sideways from the median; similar to single-side constraints. |

The KTU 2024 numerical problem will typically give the **S/H ratio** and ask for the pole spacing, or vice-versa.

---

## 2.3 The Photometric Power-and-Flux Densities

For a quick estimate of the **installed capacity** of a street-lighting system, designers use two linear densities:

- **Watt per Metre (W/m)** — total power consumed per metre of road.
- **Lumen per Metre (lm/m)** — total luminous flux emitted per metre of road.

Typical benchmarks for Indian urban roads (per the Bureau of Indian Standards, *IS 1944* and *IS 1947*):

| Road Class | Recommended W/m | Recommended lm/m | Average Illumination (lux) |
|------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| Major arterial | 4.0 to 6.0 | 400 to 600 | 30 |
| City main road | 2.5 to 4.0 | 250 to 400 | 15 |
| Residential | 1.0 to 2.0 | 100 to 200 | 8 |
| Pedestrian path | 0.5 to 1.0 | 50 to 100 | 4 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why two parameters?** *Watts* tell the **electricity-bill story** (energy efficiency). *Lumens* tell the **visibility story** (how bright the road actually is). A modern LED street-lighting project optimizes **both**.

---

## 2.4 Cut-off Classifications (Optical Glare Control)

The **cut-off angle** is the angle measured from the downward vertical, beyond which the **luminous intensity** of the luminaire falls below a certain threshold (typically 1 % of peak). This controls **glare** to the driver.

| Classification | Cut-off Condition | Beam Direction | Use |
|----------------|--------------------|----------------|-----|
| **Cut-off** | No light emitted above **80° from downward vertical** (i.e. above 10° above horizontal). | Sharp, downward | **Highways** — strict glare control. |
| **Semi Cut-off** | No light emitted above **90° from downward vertical** (i.e. at or above horizontal). | Downward + slight spread | **Urban arterial roads.** |
| **Non Cut-off** | Light emitted in **all directions**, including above horizontal. | Unrestricted | **Pedestrian zones**, low-speed areas only. |

> [!NOTE]
> A luminaire that emits light *above* the horizontal strikes the driver's eye directly, causing **disability glare**. The cut-off classification is therefore a **safety regulation**, not an aesthetic choice.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following markdown table consolidates **every equation** you are likely to be tested on for this topic. Notice that vertical bars for absolute value are written as `\vert` to preserve the table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Pole Spacing from S/H ratio | $S = \left(\dfrac{S}{H}\right) \times H$ | m | Direct multiplication. |
| 2 | Number of poles per km | $N = \dfrac{1000}{S} + 1$ | — | The "+1" accounts for the pole at the start. |
| 3 | Watt per metre | $W_m = \dfrac{P_{total}}{L}$ | W·m⁻¹ | $P_{total}$ = total wattage per pole; $L$ = spacing. |
| 4 | Lumen per metre | $lm_m = \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{L}$ | lm·m⁻¹ | $\Phi_{total}$ = lumens per luminaire. |
| 5 | Average illumination on road (lumen method) | $E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$ | lux | The **master equation** for street lighting. |
| 6 | Uniformity ratio | $U_0 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | Best $\geq 0.4$; ideal $\geq 0.5$. |
| 7 | S/H ratio (alternate form) | $\dfrac{S}{H} = \dfrac{S \text{ (m)}}{H \text{ (m)}}$ | dimensionless | Often a fixed value from IS code. |
| 8 | Luminance approximation | $L \approx \dfrac{E_{avg} \times q}{\pi}$ | cd·m⁻² | $q$ = luminance coefficient of road (0.07 to 0.10 for asphalt). |
| 9 | Light flux required per pole | $\Phi_{pole} = \dfrac{E_{avg} \times W \times S}{UF \times MF}$ | lm | Inversion of master equation. |
| 10 | Number of lamps per pole | $n = \dfrac{\Phi_{pole}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | — | Round up to nearest integer. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where the master equation comes from:** The numerator $\Phi \times UF \times MF$ gives the *useful flux* actually landing on the road per luminaire. The denominator $W \times S$ is the *area* of road "owned" by each luminaire (length $S$, width $W$). Dividing flux by area gives the **average illumination** in lux. KTU students must memorize this derivation — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart-city LED retrofits** use the *W/m* metric to compare legacy HPSV (high-pressure sodium) systems with new LED systems — a 150 W HPSV at 30 m spacing is replaced by a 60 W LED with identical $E_{avg}$.
- **Highway concession contracts** in India specify a *minimum average luminance* (e.g. 2 cd/m²) and a *minimum overall uniformity* ($U_0 \geq 0.4$); failure to meet these triggers payment deductions.
- **Pole-spacing optimization** using the S/H ratio is the first step before any flux calculation; ignoring it leads to either dark patches (too wide a spacing) or wasted energy (too tight a spacing).
