- The light source itself should **not blind you** when you look at it directly (this is **glare control**).
- You must be able to distinguish the color of the **red brake light** of the car ahead, or the **white shirt** of a pedestrian (this is **color rendering**).
- The poles and lamps should give you a **sense of direction** — almost like runway lights on an aircraft (this is **optical guidance**).
- All of this must be achieved with **minimum electricity consumption** (this is **economy**).

In short: a good street lighting system is like a *silent, ever-vigilant night-time guide* that shows the road, the obstacles, and the path forward — without ever distracting the driver's attention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Domains of Street Lighting (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Photometric Domain** — Quantity of light (lux/lumens)  
> 2. **Geometric Domain** — Distribution pattern on road  
> 3. **Visual Domain** — Glare, contrast, color rendering  
> 4. **Economic Domain** — Capital + running cost  
> 5. **Safety Domain** — Optical guidance, accident reduction  

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used in Street Lighting

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Illuminance on road | $E$ | **lux (lx)** | Light falling on road surface |
| Luminance of road | $L$ | **cd/m²** | Light reflected *towards* the driver |
| Luminous flux | $\Phi$ | **lumen (lm)** | Total light emitted by lamp |
| Luminous intensity | $I$ | **candela (cd)** | Light in a particular direction |
| Spacing between poles | $S$ | **metre (m)** | Determines uniformity |
| Mounting height | $H$ | **metre (m)** | Determines light spread |
| Glare index / TI | $TI$ | **%** | Threshold Increment |
| Uniformity ratio | $U_0$, $U_1$ | dimensionless | $E_{min}/E_{av}$ or $L_{min}/L_{av}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nine Essential Requirements of Good Street Lighting

Street lighting is a *system design problem* — a single bright lamp is not necessarily good lighting. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the requirements into the following nine engineering criteria. Every board exam answer must explicitly enumerate these.

### Requirement 1: Adequate Level of Illumination

The road surface must receive sufficient illuminance so that the driver's eye, which is in a state of **photopic (cone) vision** to **mesopic (mixed) vision** at night, can perform the visual tasks of obstacle detection and lane keeping.

- For major roads / National Highways: $E_{av} = 30$ to $50$ **lux**
- For main city roads: $E_{av} = 15$ to $30$ **lux**
- For residential / minor streets: $E_{av} = 4$ to $15$ **lux**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 1944-1970 / IS 1944 (Part I & II)** prescribes the minimum average illuminance values for different classes of roads in India. KTU questions frequently ask you to *quote the standard value for a given road class*.

### Requirement 2: Uniformity of Illumination

Bright patches alternating with dark patches cause the driver's pupil to constantly dilate and contract, leading to **driver fatigue** and reduced visibility. The uniformity ratio is mathematically defined as:

$$U_0 = \frac{E_{min}}{E_{av}}$$

For street lighting, the **overall uniformity ratio** $U_0$ should be **at least 0.4** for normal roads, and **at least 0.6** for major traffic routes. Some standards also use the **longitudinal uniformity** $U_1$ along the centre line of the lane.

### Requirement 3: Freedom from Glare (Limitation of Glare)

Glare is the sensation produced by bright sources within the field of view that causes discomfort (discomfort glare) or reduces visibility (disability glare). It is quantified by the **Threshold Increment (TI)**:

$$TI = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{av}} \times 100 \ \%$$

where $\Delta L$ is the equivalent veiling luminance added to the background. For street lighting, **TI must not exceed 10 %** for major roads and **20 %** for secondary roads.

### Requirement 4: Proper Color of Light / Color Rendering

The light source should render the colors of objects (signals, brake lights, clothing) as close to natural daylight as possible. This is measured by the **Color Rendering Index (CRI, $R_a$)** on a 0–100 scale.

- Daylight: $R_a = 100$
- Sodium vapour (low pressure, SOX): $R_a \approx 20$ to 25 (poor)
- Sodium vapour (high pressure, SON): $R_a \approx 25$ to 60 (moderate)
- Metal halide: $R_a \approx 65$ to 90 (good)
- LED (modern white): $R_a \approx 70$ to 95 (excellent)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Although **HPSV (High Pressure Sodium Vapour)** is most efficient (up to 150 lm/W), it has poor CRI. **White LED** is now preferred in smart-city projects (Kerala KSEB projects) because of better visibility at *lower* luminance levels.

### Requirement 5: Correct Spacing and Mounting Height of Lamps

The **mounting height $H$** and the **spacing $S$** between successive luminaires together determine the illumination pattern on the road. The relationship is usually expressed as the **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)**:

$$SHR = \frac{S}{H}$$

A general design rule:
- For streets: $SHR \approx 3$ to $4$
- For high-mast lighting (intersections, squares): $SHR \approx 1$ to $2$

For a given lamp flux $\Phi$ and utilization factor $\eta$:

$$E_{av} = \frac{\Phi \times N \times \eta \times MF}{A}$$

where $N$ is number of lamps, $A = S \times W$ is the road area (W = width of road), and $MF$ is the **maintenance factor** (typically 0.6 to 0.8 for outdoor installations).

### Requirement 6: Proper Optical Guidance (Visual Guidance)

The arrangement of luminaires should give the driver an *unmistakable* indication of the road's path — especially on curves, gradients, and intersections. A row of equally spaced lamps on one side tells the driver "single-side road"; lamps on both sides converging into the distance tell the driver "the road goes straight". This is the principle of **optical guidance**.

### Requirement 7: Minimum Maintenance Cost / Economy

Street lighting operates 4000 to 4500 hours per year (from sunset to sunrise). Energy cost dominates the life-cycle cost. A good design balances:
- **Capital cost** of poles, cables, luminaires
- **Running cost** of electricity (kWh)
- **Maintenance cost** of cleaning and lamp replacement

Modern LED street lighting can reduce energy consumption by **40–60 %** compared to conventional HPSV systems.

### Requirement 8: Light Should Not Spill Beyond the Road

A good street lighting system should direct the light **downwards** onto the road, not into the windows of nearby houses (light pollution). This is achieved by using **cut-off** or **semi-cut-off luminaires** (Type II, III, IV distributions as per IES classification).

### Requirement 9: Provision for Switching and Voltage Regulation

Automatic switching (photocell / time switch), and stable voltage supply (to extend lamp life), are operational requirements of a good street lighting system.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{av} = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot N \cdot \eta \cdot MF}{S \cdot W}$ | Average illuminance on road | Design calculation |
| 2 | $U_0 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{av}}$ | Overall uniformity | Verify uniformity |
| 3 | $U_1 = \dfrac{L_{min}}{L_{max}}$ | Longitudinal uniformity | Check driver visibility |
| 4 | $SHR = \dfrac{S}{H}$ | Spacing-to-height ratio | Optimum pole placement |
| 5 | $TI = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{av}} \times 100$ | Threshold Increment (glare) | Glare control verification |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{flux reaching road}}{\Phi}$ | Utilization factor | Luminaire selection |
| 7 | $L = \rho \cdot E / \pi$ | Luminance (Lambertian road) | Converting illuminance to luminance |
| 8 | $L_{\text{veil}} = k \cdot \dfrac{E_{glare}}{\theta^2}$ | Veiling luminance from glare source | Disability glare |
| 9 | $P_{\text{electric}} = \dfrac{\Phi_{lamp}}{\eta_{lamp}} \times N$ | Electric power required | Energy estimation |
| 10 | $LCC = C_{cap} + C_{energy} \cdot t + C_{maint} \cdot t$ | Life-cycle cost | Economic analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship $L = \rho \cdot E / \pi$ holds for a perfectly **diffuse (Lambertian)** road surface, where $\rho$ is the reflectance of the asphalt. For real roads, $\rho$ varies from **0.10 (wet dark asphalt)** to **0.25 (dry light concrete)**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These requirements are codified in the following national/international standards that every KTU electrical engineer is expected to know:

- **IS 1944 (Parts I & II)** — Indian standard for road lighting
- **IS 10322** — Specification for road lighting luminaires
- **IES RP-8-13** — American standard for roadway lighting
- **CIE 115:2010** — International standard for road lighting installations
- **Ministry of Road Transport & Highways (MoRTH)** — Guidelines for highway lighting in India
- **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** — Star labelling of LED street lights in India

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Connect:** The Government of India launched the **Street Lighting National Programme (SLNP)** in 2015 to replace ~1.34 crore conventional street lights with LED lights. Energy Efficiency Services Limited (EESL) executed this. Kerala KSEB has been a major beneficiary. Questions on this topic can carry 1–2 marks if asked contextually.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average Illuminance Formula $E_{av}$

The most fundamental equation in street lighting design. We derive it rigorously, since it is the most-frequently asked derivation in KTU board exams.

### Step 1: Define the Luminous Flux Reaching the Road

Consider a single luminaire (street light fitting) mounted at height $H$ above a horizontal road of width $W$. The luminaire emits a total luminous flux $\Phi$ (in lumens). However, due to the geometry of the luminaire's optics, only a fraction of this flux actually falls *onto* the road surface. This fraction is defined as the **Utilization Factor (UF or $\eta$)**:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Luminous flux reaching the road surface}}{\text{Total flux emitted by the lamp}}$$

Therefore, the flux from **one lamp** that actually lands on the road is:

$$\Phi_{\text{road,1 lamp}} = \eta \cdot \Phi$$

### Step 2: Account for Multiple Lamps

If there are $N$ lamps distributed evenly over a road section of length $L_{road}$ and width $W$, the *total* road area illuminated is:

$$A = L_{road} \times W$$

But the total flux incident on this area from all $N$ lamps is:

$$\Phi_{\text{road,total}} = N \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi$$

### Step 3: Compute the Average Illuminance

By the very definition of **illuminance** (lumens per square metre, i.e., lux):

$$E_{av} = \frac{\text{Total flux on the road}}{\text{Road area}} = \frac{N \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi}{A}$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Spacing and Width

If the $N$ lamps are spaced uniformly at distance $S$ apart, then for a section of length $L_{road}$ we have $N = L_{road}/S$, and the area is $A = L_{road} \times W$. Substituting:

$$E_{av} = \frac{(L_{road}/S) \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi}{L_{road} \times W}$$

The $L_{road}$ cancels out cleanly:

$$E_{av} = \frac{\eta \cdot \Phi}{S \cdot W}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Maintenance Factor

Over time, lamp output degrades (typically 20–30 % loss over lamp life), and luminaire optics get covered with dust, reducing light output. The **Maintenance Factor (MF)** is defined as:

$$MF = \frac{\text{Light output at the worst maintenance condition}}{\text{Initial light output}}$$

Typical value: $MF = 0.7$ for outdoor street lighting. Including this, the *design* illuminance formula becomes:

$$\boxed{\,E_{av} = \frac{\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}{S \cdot W}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental street lighting design equation** that KTU expects you to remember and apply.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 12 m wide road is to be illuminated by 250 W HPSV lamps each emitting 25,000 lumens, mounted on poles at 30 m spacing on one side. The utilization factor is 0.32 and the maintenance factor is 0.75. Calculate the average illumination on the road surface. Also determine whether the design satisfies the IS 1944 standard for a major district road (required $E_{av} \geq 15$ lux).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values.

$$\Phi = 25{,}000 \ \text{lm}, \quad S = 30 \ \text{m}, \quad W = 12 \ \text{m}, \quad \eta = 0.32, \quad MF = 0.75$$

**Step 2:** Apply the derived formula directly.

$$E_{av} = \frac{\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}{S \cdot W}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$E_{av} = \frac{25{,}000 \times 0.32 \times 0.75}{30 \times 12}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the numerator.

$$25{,}000 \times 0.32 \times 0.75 = 25{,}000 \times 0.24 = 6{,}000 \ \text{lm}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the denominator.

$$30 \times 12 = 360 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 6:** Compute the final answer.

$$E_{av} = \frac{6{,}000}{360} = 16.67 \ \text{lux}$$

**Step 7:** Compare with standard.

$$16.67 \ \text{lux} \geq 15 \ \text{lux} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Design SATISFIES the IS 1944 standard.} \ \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR) Optimisation

For a given lamp flux and luminaire type, the relationship between $S$ and $H$ is governed by the *beam spread* of the luminaire.

Consider a luminaire with a horizontal beam spread angle $2\theta$ (full angle). The footprint of light on the road extends a horizontal distance $D$ from the foot of the pole:

$$D = H \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

For **overlap** of light patterns from two successive poles to give uniform illumination, the spacing $S$ should be at most equal to twice this footprint (so that footprints from adjacent lamps touch each other at midspan):

$$S \leq 2 \cdot D = 2 \cdot H \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

Rearranging for the upper bound on the spacing-to-height ratio:

$$\frac{S}{H} \leq 2 \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

For typical street-lighting luminaires with $\theta = 60^\circ$:

$$\frac{S}{H} \leq 2 \cdot \tan(60^\circ) = 2 \cdot \sqrt{3} \approx 3.46$$

This is the **theoretical upper limit**. In practice, a slightly *lower* value is used for safer design.

$$\boxed{\,SHR = \frac{S}{H} \approx 3 \text{ to } 4 \text{ (for street lighting)}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Light Sources for Street Lighting

| Parameter | HPSV (SON) | LPSV (SOX) | MH (Metal Halide) | LED (Modern) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Efficacy (lm/W) | 80–150 | 100–200 | 70–110 | 100–180 |
| CRI ($R_a$) | 25–60 | 20–25 | 65–90 | 70–95 |
| Lamp life (hours) | 24,000 | 18,000 | 15,000–20,000 | 50,000–100,000 |
| Warm-up time | 5–10 min | 5–10 min | 5–10 min | **Instant** |
| Restrike time | 1–3 min | 5–10 min | 5–10 min | **Instant** |
| Glare | Moderate | High (yellow) | Moderate | Low (with optics) |
| Cost (initial) | Low | Lowest | Moderate | Higher |
| Cost (running) | Lowest | Lowest | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| Suitability for KTU | Good | Discontinued | Good | **Best (current choice)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A 14-mark question on street lighting often asks you to **compare HPSV and LED** along the above parameters, and justify the choice of LED for a smart-city project. Memorize the four "Instant" and "Lowest running" advantages of LED.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selection of Pole Arrangement

| Arrangement | Spacing Formula | Uniformity | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-side** | $S = W \cdot SHR$ | Poor if $W \leq H$ | Narrow residential streets |
| **Staggered (zig-zag)** | $S = 1.3 \cdot W \cdot SHR$ | Better than single-side | Medium-width roads |
| **Opposite (both sides)** | $S = 2 \cdot W \cdot SHR$ | Best uniformity | Wide highways |
| **Central (median)** | $S = 2 \cdot W \cdot SHR$ | Good | Dual-carriageway highways |
| **High-mast** | Single pole, $H = 20$–40 m | Excellent over junction | Junctions, squares, toll plazas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (KTU favourite):**  
> If road width $W \leq$ mounting height $H$, use **single-side** arrangement.  
> If $W > H$, use **staggered** or **opposite** arrangement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Requirements of Good Street Lighting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["GOOD STREET LIGHTING SYSTEM"]
    nodeB["QUANTITATIVE REQUIREMENTS"]
    nodeC["QUALITATIVE REQUIREMENTS"]
    nodeD["OPERATIONAL REQUIREMENTS"]
    nodeE["Adequate Illuminance Level"]
    nodeF["Uniformity of Illumination U0"]
    nodeG["Limitation of Glare TI"]
    nodeH["Colour Rendering CRI"]
    nodeI["Optical Guidance"]
    nodeJ["Light Cutoff Control"]
    nodeK["Economy and Energy Efficiency"]
    nodeL["Proper Spacing and Height"]
    nodeM["Reliable Switching and Maintenance"]
    nodeN["Safety and Accident Reduction"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeD --> nodeN

    nodeE -. "Eav 15 to 50 lux" .-> nodeE
    nodeF -. "U0 at least 0.4" .-> nodeF
    nodeG -. "TI below 10 percent" .-> nodeG
    nodeH -. "CRI above 65 for roads" .-> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Street Lighting Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["STEP 1: Identify road class and traffic density"]
    s2["STEP 2: Select design illuminance Eav from IS 1944"]
    s3["STEP 3: Choose light source HPSV or LED"]
    s4["STEP 4: Decide pole arrangement single or opposite"]
    s5["STEP 5: Select mounting height H and spacing S using SHR"]
    s6["STEP 6: Apply formula Eav equals Phi times eta times MF over S times W"]
    s7["STEP 7: Verify uniformity U0 and glare TI"]
    s8["STEP 8: Iterate if requirements not met"]
    s9["FINAL DESIGN APPROVED"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9
    s8 -. "Back to step 5 adjust S and H" .-> s5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Street Lighting Pole

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pA["LAMP SOURCE: HPSV or LED"]
    pB["LUMINAIRE OPTICS: Reflector and Refractor"]
    pC["PHOTOMETRIC CONTROL: Directs light downward"]
    pD["POLE STRUCTURE: Galvanised Iron or Concrete"]
    pE["MOUNTING ARM: Outreach bracket"]
    pF["ELECTRICAL FITTINGS: Driver, Cable, Connector"]
    pG["CONTROL SYSTEM: Photocell and Timer"]
    pH["ILLUMINATED ROAD SURFACE"]

    pA --> pB --> pC --> pH
    pD --> pE --> pB
    pF --> pA
    pG --> pF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Architecture:** The luminaire optics (reflector + refractor) is the *photometric heart* of the system. It shapes the flux from the lamp into a controlled beam that hits the road at a precise angle. Poor optics = poor uniformity, even if the lamp is bright.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Pole Arrangements

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    arrA["ARRANGEMENT A: Single Side"]
    arrB["ARRANGEMENT B: Staggered Zig Zag"]
    arrC["ARRANGEMENT C: Opposite Both Sides"]
    arrD["ARRANGEMENT D: Central Median"]
    arrE["ARRANGEMENT E: High Mast"]

    arrA --- note1["Use when W less than H. Uniformity weak far side."]
    arrB --- note2["Better uniformity. Used for W greater than H."]
    arrC --- note3["Best uniformity. Used for wide highways."]
    arrD --- note4["Used for dual carriageway. Median has pole."]
    arrE --- note5["Used for junctions and squares. H greater than 20m."]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "street lighting" and list any four essential requirements of a good street lighting system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Street lighting is the art and science of providing adequate and uniform artificial illumination on public roads, highways, and pedestrian paths during hours of darkness, with the objectives of ensuring safety, visual comfort, and efficient traffic flow.

**Four essential requirements:**

1. **Adequate level of illuminance** on the road surface (typically 15 to 50 lux depending on road class).
2. **Uniformity of illumination** — the ratio of minimum to average illuminance should be at least 0.4, avoiding dark and bright patches.
3. **Freedom from glare** — the Threshold Increment (TI) should be limited to below 10 % to prevent driver discomfort and disability.
4. **Proper optical guidance** — the arrangement of luminaires should clearly indicate the path of the road, especially on curves and intersections.

*(Any other valid requirements like colour rendering, economy, light cutoff are also acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "Spacing-to-Height Ratio" (SHR) in street lighting design? State its typical value and significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR) is the ratio of the horizontal distance between two successive luminaires ($S$) to the mounting height of the luminaire above the road surface ($H$).

$$SHR = \frac{S}{H}$$

**Typical value:** For conventional street lighting, SHR ranges from **3 to 4**. For high-mast lighting, SHR is around 1 to 2.

**Significance:**

- It determines the **degree of overlap** of light patterns from adjacent luminaires, directly affecting uniformity.
- A higher SHR (larger spacing) gives poorer uniformity.
- A lower SHR (closer spacing) gives better uniformity but increases installation cost.
- It is the **key design parameter** in optimizing the trade-off between uniformity and economy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **nine essential requirements of a good street lighting system** in detail. State the standard illuminance values recommended by IS 1944 for at least three classes of roads.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A road of width 8 m is illuminated by 150 W HPSV lamps each emitting 14,500 lumens. The lamps are mounted at 9 m height with 36 m spacing on a single side. The utilization factor is 0.28 and the maintenance factor is 0.70. Determine (i) the average illumination on the road, and (ii) the spacing-to-height ratio. State whether the design satisfies IS 1944 for a "main road" (required $E_{av} \geq 15$ lux).

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Nine Requirements of Good Street Lighting

| # | Requirement | Explanation | Standard / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Adequate level of illumination** | Sufficient light to perform visual tasks | 15–50 lux (road-class dependent) |
| 2 | **Uniformity of illumination** | Avoid alternating dark and bright patches | $U_0 = E_{min}/E_{av} \geq 0.4$ |
| 3 | **Freedom from glare** | Driver must not be blinded | $TI \leq 10$ % |
| 4 | **Proper colour of light** | Distinguish signal colours and clothing | $R_a \geq 65$ |
| 5 | **Correct spacing and mounting height** | Light overlap and coverage | $SHR = 3$ to $4$ |
| 6 | **Proper optical guidance** | Indicates road path clearly | Arrangement dependent |
| 7 | **Light cutoff / light pollution control** | Light should not spill into homes | Cut-off or semi-cut-off luminaire |
| 8 | **Economy / energy efficiency** | Minimum life-cycle cost | LEDs preferred (100+ lm/W) |
| 9 | **Reliable switching and maintenance** | Automatic ON/OFF, easy lamp replacement | Photocell, timer switch |

**IS 1944 standard illuminance values (Part II):**
- **Arterial roads / National Highways:** 30 lux
- **Sub-arterial / major district roads:** 15 lux
- **Collector / residential streets:** 6 to 8 lux

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Road width: $W = 8$ m
- Lamp flux: $\Phi = 14{,}500$ lm (per lamp)
- Mounting height: $H = 9$ m
- Spacing: $S = 36$ m
- Utilization factor: $\eta = 0.28$
- Maintenance factor: $MF = 0.70$

**Sub-part (i): Average illuminance**

Apply the design formula:

$$E_{av} = \frac{\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}{S \cdot W}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{av} = \frac{14{,}500 \times 0.28 \times 0.70}{36 \times 8}$$

Numerator:

$$14{,}500 \times 0.28 = 4{,}060$$
$$4{,}060 \times 0.70 = 2{,}842 \ \text{lm}$$

Denominator:

$$36 \times 8 = 288 \ \text{m}^2$$

Final answer:

$$E_{av} = \frac{2{,}842}{288} = 9.87 \ \text{lux} \ (\text{approximately} \ 9.9 \ \text{lux})$$

**Sub-part (ii): Spacing-to-Height Ratio**

$$SHR = \frac{S}{H} = \frac{36}{9} = 4.0$$

**Compliance with IS 1944:**

Required: $E_{av} \geq 15$ lux for a main road  
Obtained: $E_{av} = 9.87$ lux

**Conclusion:** Since $9.87 < 15$, the design **DOES NOT satisfy** IS 1944 for a main road. It would, however, satisfy the requirement for a residential street (6 to 8 lux, borderline). To meet the main-road standard, either the lamp wattage must be increased or the spacing reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark Loss Zone:**  
> Students often forget to multiply by the **maintenance factor (MF)** in the numerator. Without MF, you would get $E_{av} = 14.08$ lux, which is *almost* close to 15 and could lead to the wrong conclusion. **Always include MF** unless the question explicitly says "initial" illuminance. Losing MF costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a modern street lighting pole** indicating the role of the luminaire, optics, mounting arm, and control system.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare **HPSV (High Pressure Sodium Vapour)** and **LED** light sources for street lighting on the basis of (i) luminous efficacy, (ii) colour rendering index, (iii) lamp life, (iv) warm-up time, (v) glare, and (vi) running cost. Conclude by stating which source is more suitable for a smart-city project in Kerala.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Functional Architecture of a Modern Street Lighting Pole

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.3** of these notes for the full Mermaid schematic. The key functional blocks and their roles are:

1. **Lamp source (HPSV or LED):** Provides the basic luminous flux.
2. **Luminaire housing (IP 65 or higher):** Protects the lamp and optics from dust and rain.
3. **Optical assembly — Reflector:** A precisely shaped mirror that directs light rays downward onto the road.
4. **Optical assembly — Refractor / lens:** A prismatic transparent cover that refracts the light into the desired IES distribution pattern (Type II, III, or IV).
5. **Mounting arm (outreach bracket):** Positions the luminaire over the carriageway, typically 0.5 to 2 m from the pole.
6. **Pole structure (GI / concrete):** Supports the entire assembly at the design height (9 to 12 m for streets, 20 to 40 m for high-mast).
7. **Electrical fittings:** Driver / ballast, cable, junction box, earthing.
8. **Control system:** Photocell (day-night sensor) and timer switch for automatic ON/OFF.
9. **Surge protection device (SPD):** Protects against lightning and voltage transients.

The **optical assembly is the photometric heart** of the system — it determines the illumination pattern on the road. Without proper optics, even a high-efficacy LED will produce poor street lighting.

---

#### Part (b) — HPSV vs LED Comparison

| Parameter | HPSV (SON) | LED (Modern White) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Luminous efficacy** | 80–150 lm/W | 100–180 lm/W | LED |
| **(ii) Colour rendering (CRI)** | 25–60 (yellow) | 70–95 (white) | **LED (far superior)** |
| **(iii) Lamp life** | ~24,000 hours | 50,000–100,000 hours | **LED (2–4 times longer)** |
| **(iv) Warm-up time** | 5–10 minutes | **Instantaneous** | **LED** |
| **(v) Glare** | Moderate (yellowish, large source) | Low (with precise optics) | **LED** |
| **(vi) Running cost** | Lower than HPMV but higher than LED | **Lowest (40–60 % saving)** | **LED** |

**Conclusion:** For the Kerala Smart-City project, **LED street lighting is the clear choice**. Although the initial capital cost is higher, the combined effect of:
- lower energy consumption (40–60 % saving),
- much longer lamp life (less frequent replacement in difficult-to-access high-mast locations),
- superior colour rendering (better visibility and signal recognition),
- instant restrike (important during monsoon power cuts),

makes LED the most economical and technically superior solution over its life cycle. The SLNP (Street Lighting National Programme) executed by EESL has already demonstrated this in over 500 cities across India.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> When comparing sources in a 7-mark question, students often list 3 to 4 parameters and leave the **conclusion unstated**. Always end with a **decisive recommendation and a one-line justification**. Missing the conclusion = loss of 1 to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for "Requirements of Good Street Lighting":**

- **The Master Formula:** $E_{av} = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF}{S \cdot W}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- **SHR rule of thumb:** $SHR = 3$ to $4$ for streets; $1$ to $2$ for high-mast.
- **Uniformity:** $U_0 = E_{min}/E_{av} \geq 0.4$ (street), $\geq 0.6$ (major road).
- **Glare:** $TI \leq 10$ % for major roads; $\leq 20$ % for secondary.
- **Lamp selection:** White LED ($R_a \geq 70$) preferred over HPSV ($R_a \approx 25$) for modern smart-city installations.
- **Pole arrangement rule:** $W \leq H \Rightarrow$ single-side; $W > H \Rightarrow$ staggered or opposite.
- **IS 1944 illumination standards:** 30 lux (NH), 15 lux (main road), 6–8 lux (residential).
- **Diffuse road approximation:** $L = \rho \cdot E / \pi$ where $\rho$ is road reflectance.
- **Optical guidance** is a qualitative requirement — do not confuse with photometric requirements.
- **Light cut-off** (cut-off or semi-cut-off luminaire) prevents light pollution into homes.
- **Maintenance factor** of 0.6 to 0.8 must always be applied in design calculations unless "initial" is specified.
- **IES light distribution types** for streets: Type II (narrow), Type III (medium), Type IV (wide).
- **SLNP (Street Lighting National Programme)** — India's flagship LED street lighting initiative by EESL (2015).
- **CIE 115:2010** is the international benchmark standard for road lighting.
- **Lamp life ordering:** LED > HPSV > LPSV > Metal Halide > Fluorescent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic (for the 9 requirements):**  
> **"A U G C S O L E S"** — Adequate, Uniformity, Glare-free, Colour rendering, Spacing, Optical guidance, Light cutoff, Economy, Switching reliability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Lamp and Luminaire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Lamp and Luminaire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lamp selection** is the systematic engineering process of choosing the most appropriate artificial light source based on a structured evaluation of luminous efficacy, color rendering index (CRI), color temperature, lamp life, lumen depreciation, re-strike time, and economic factors such as total cost of ownership for a given application.

**Luminaire selection** is the engineering process of choosing a complete light fitting (lamp + housing + reflector + diffuser + control gear) that delivers the required illumination level, distribution pattern, glare control, and aesthetic integration while maintaining the photometric efficiency of the lamp.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4):** The selection of lamps and luminaires is a design-level decision that directly impacts the Illumination Level ($E_v$), Uniformity Ratio ($U_o$), Glare Index, and Visual Comfort Probability (VCP) of any lighting installation. Students must master the **decision matrix** linking application requirements to lamp/luminaire characteristics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of selecting a lamp and luminaire as **choosing the right "pen and paper" for a specific writing task**:

- **Lamp** is the **pen** — it produces the actual "ink" (light). Different pens (ballpoint, gel, fountain) produce lines of different color, thickness, and quality.
- **Luminaire** is the **paper holder, grip, and desk setup** — it directs where the ink flows, controls how much spreads, prevents smudging (glare), and shapes the writing experience.

> Just as you would not use a fountain pen to scribble a quick shopping list (overkill) or a dull pencil to sign a marriage certificate (underkill), an engineer must match the lamp's luminous character to the **task** and the luminaire's photometric distribution to the **space geometry**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realization:** A *brilliant* lamp inside the *wrong* luminaire wastes energy and creates glare. Conversely, a *premium* luminaire fitted with a *poor* lamp under-delivers light quality. **Selection is a coupled decision.**

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in This Module

The following **bold** metrics govern the entire selection workflow:

- **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$ or $\eta_L$):** Measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)** — the light output per unit electrical input.
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI or $R_a$):** A scale from **0 to 100** measuring how faithfully a light source reveals object colors compared to a reference source.
- **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT):** Measured in **Kelvin (K)** — describes the warm (2700 K) to cool (6500 K) appearance of white light.
- **Lamp Life ($L_{70}$ or $L_{50}$):** Operating hours until luminous flux drops to 70% or 50% of initial.
- **Lumen Maintenance Factor (LMF):** Ratio of lamp output at a given time to initial output.
- **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation Factor (LDD):** Reduction in output due to accumulation of dirt.
- **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR or SHR Nominal):** Maximum distance between luminaires divided by mounting height for uniform illumination.
- **Utilance (UF):** Ratio of luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total flux emitted by the lamps.
- **Glare Index / Unified Glare Rating (UGR):** Numerical assessment of discomfort glare.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Criteria for Lamp Selection

The selection of a lamp is governed by a **multi-attribute decision matrix**. Each criterion carries a weight depending on the application context. Below is a structured breakdown:

### A. Photometric & Colorimetric Criteria

1. **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta_L$)**
   - Higher $\eta_L$ → less energy consumption per lux delivered.
   - Critical for high-bay industrial and street lighting where running hours exceed 12 hrs/day.

2. **Color Rendering Index ($R_a$)**
   - **$R_a \geq 90$:** Museums, art galleries, hospitals (color-critical diagnosis), textile showrooms.
   - **$R_a$ = 80–89:** Offices, schools, retail, residential.
   - **$R_a$ = 60–79:** Industrial assembly, warehouses.
   - **$R_a \leq 60$:** Roadways, outdoor area lighting (color accuracy not critical).

3. **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)**
   - **Warm White (2700 K – 3000 K):** Hotels, restaurants, residential, lobbies.
   - **Neutral White (3500 K – 4100 K):** Offices, classrooms, retail.
   - **Cool White / Daylight (5000 K – 6500 K):** Hospitals (operating theatres), industrial inspection, outdoor security.

4. **Spectral Power Distribution (SPD)**
   - Discontinuous spectrum (e.g., fluorescent, HID) vs. continuous (incandescent, LED).
   - Important for applications involving photosynthesis, phototherapy, or UV curing.

### B. Electrical & Operational Criteria

5. **Lamp Life and Mortality Curve**
   - LEDs: 25,000 – 100,000 hours.
   - Fluorescent (T8/T5): 15,000 – 30,000 hours.
   - HID (HPSV, MH): 10,000 – 24,000 hours.
   - Incandescent: 1,000 – 2,000 hours.

6. **Re-strike Time (Re-ignition Time)**
   - **Instant re-strike:** LED, incandescent, halogen.
   - **Hot re-strike time:** HPSV (~1–3 min), Metal Halide (~5–15 min).
   - Critical for emergency lighting, tunnels, and process industries.

7. **Lamp Voltage Sensitivity**
   - Affects performance during voltage sags — important in rural/inverter-driven supplies.

8. **Power Factor and Harmonic Distortion**
   - LED drivers may inject THD > 30% if poor quality; relevant to power-quality compliance.

### C. Economic & Environmental Criteria

9. **Initial Cost vs. Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)**
   - LCC = Initial Cost + Energy Cost + Maintenance Cost + Disposal Cost.

10. **Environmental Disposal**
    - Mercury content (fluorescent, HID) requires controlled recycling under **Minamata Convention** principles.
    - LEDs are RoHS-compliant with negligible toxic content.

11. **Dimmability**
    - LED (0–10 V, DALI), Fluorescent (1–10 V), HID (step-dimming via ballast taps), Incandescent (leading-edge).

### D. Application-Specific Criteria

12. **Heat Generation (IR/UV Output)**
    - Low heat → LEDs preferred for cold-storage, museums (artifact preservation).
    - Specific IR output → incandescent-halogen used for heating in industrial drying.

13. **Form Factor & Beam Control**
    - Directional (LED, halogen MR16) vs. omnidirectional (incandescent A-lamp, CFL).

---

## 2.2 Criteria for Luminaire Selection

A luminaire is selected to **modify, control, and protect** the light emitted by the lamp. The decision parameters are:

### A. Optical Performance

1. **Luminaire Photometric Distribution**
   - Classified by **CIE luminaire classification**:
     - Direct, Indirect, Semi-direct, Direct-Indirect, General diffuse.
   - **Beam Spread Classification (BS 5225 / NEMA):**
     - Very Narrow Spot (VNSP) < 8° → Narrow Spot (NSP) 8°–15° → Spot (SP) 15°–20° → Narrow Flood (NFL) 20°–30° → Flood (FL) 30°–60° → Wide Flood (WFL) 60°–100° → Very Wide Flood (VWFL) > 100°.

2. **Luminaire Efficacy (LOR — Light Output Ratio)**
   - $\text{LOR} = \dfrac{\text{Total flux emitted by luminaire}}{\text{Total flux emitted by lamps alone}}$
   - High-quality luminaires: LOR = 0.7 – 0.9.

3. **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)**
   - Determines uniformity; typical indoor luminaire SHR = 1.0 to 1.5.

4. **Glare Control**
   - UGR < 19 (offices), < 22 (industrial), < 25 (circulation areas).
   - Achieved via diffusers, louvers, recessed optics, specular reflectors.

### B. Mechanical & Environmental

5. **Ingress Protection (IP) Rating**
   - **IP20:** Indoor dry (offices).
   - **IP44:** Kitchens, bathrooms.
   - **IP65/IP66:** Outdoor, industrial wash-down.
   - **IP67/IP68:** Underground, submersible (fountain lighting).

6. **Impact Resistance (IK Rating)**
   - **IK07–IK10:** Vandal-prone areas (public transport, sports halls).

7. **Operating Temperature Range**
   - Cold store luminaires: –40 °C to +25 °C.
   - High-ambient industrial: up to +70 °C.

8. **Housing Material**
   - CRCA steel, aluminum die-cast, polycarbonate, PMMA, glass.

### C. Electrical

9. **Control Gear Compatibility**
   - Electronic vs. electromagnetic ballast; LED driver topology.
   - DALI/0-10 V/Step-dim compatibility.

10. **Electrical Safety Class**
    - Class I (earthed), Class II (double-insulated), Class III (SELV).

### D. Aesthetic & Architectural

11. **Form, Finish, and Visual Integration**
    - Recessed, surface, pendant, track, wall-mounted.
    - Color (RAL codes), finish (matte, gloss, anodized).

12. **Maintenance Access**
    - Tool-less access vs. screw-fixed; affects LCC and LDD management.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable under the **Selection of Lamp and Luminaire** topic and are frequently tested in **Part A (3 marks)** derivations and **Part B (14 marks)** design problems.

### Table 2.1 — Core Formulas for Lamp & Luminaire Selection

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Luminous efficacy of lamp | $\text{lm} \cdot \text{W}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $E_v = \dfrac{\Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LDD}}{A}$ | Maintained illuminance formula (interior) | $\text{lux}$ |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{E_v \times A}{\Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LDD}}$ | Number of luminaires required | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{S}{H_m}$ | Spacing-to-height ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $U_o = \dfrac{E_{\min}}{E_{avg}}$ | Uniformity ratio on working plane | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{LOR} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{luminaire}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}}$ | Light output ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_a \text{ (CRI)} = 100 - 4.6 \cdot \Delta E$ | Approx. CRI from chromaticity shift | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{VCP} \text{ (Visual Comfort Probability)}$ | % of observers finding glare acceptable | % |
| 9 | $\text{LCC} = C_{\text{init}} + \sum \left( E_{n} \cdot n \right) + M_{n} + D$ | Life-cycle cost of lamp/luminaire | ₹ or USD |
| 10 | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{\text{initial}}}{\Delta \text{Annual Saving}}$ | Payback period (years) | years |
| 11 | $I = \dfrac{I_{max}}{\cos^{n}\theta}$ | Candela distribution of reflector ($n$ = exponent) | $\text{cd}$ |
| 12 | $\text{Beam Angle} = 2 \cdot \theta_{1/2}$ | Where intensity is 50% of $I_{max}$ | degrees |

### Table 2.2 — Boundary Values & Selection Thresholds (Kerala KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Parameter | Office | Industrial | Roadway | Sports |
|-----------|--------|------------|---------|--------|
| $E_v$ (lux) | 300–500 | 150–750 | 15–50 | 200–1500 |
| $R_a$ (min) | 80 | 60 | 60 | 80 |
| UGR (max) | 19 | 25 | — | — |
| $U_o$ (min) | 0.6 | 0.6 | 0.4 | 0.7 |
| $\text{CCT}$ (K) | 4000 | 4000–5000 | 3000–4000 | 5000–6500 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lighting Designers / Consultants** use this selection matrix daily for **BEE 5-star rated building projects** under ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) compliance.
- **Municipal Corporations** apply the same framework to tender for **street lighting under Smart City Mission** with HPSV-to-LED retrofits.
- **Industrial Safety Officers** select high-CRI, low-glare luminaires for **factories, mines, and process plants** to meet IS 3646 and Indian Factory Act illumination clauses.
- **Retail Visual Merchandisers** use CCT and CRI data to tune shopping ambience for product appeal (e.g., jewelry stores prefer 3000 K + $R_a \geq 90$).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Lamp Families

The lamp selection problem reduces to a **weighted multi-criteria decision analysis**. The following analytical matrix is a high-yield KTU 2024 ESE deliverable.

### Table 3.1 — Lamp Family Selection Matrix (Quantitative)

| Parameter | Incandescent | Halogen | CFL | T5/T8 Fluorescent | HPSV | Metal Halide | LED |
|-----------|--------------|---------|-----|-------------------|------|--------------|-----|
| $\eta_L$ (lm/W) | 10–17 | 15–25 | 50–70 | 70–105 | 80–150 | 70–115 | 80–200 |
| $R_a$ | 100 | 100 | 80–85 | 80–95 | 20–30 | 65–95 | 70–98 |
| CCT (K) | 2700 | 3000 | 2700–6500 | 2700–6500 | 1900–2200 | 2700–5500 | 2200–6500 |
| Life (kh) | 1 | 2–4 | 8–15 | 15–30 | 16–32 | 10–20 | 25–100 |
| Re-strike | Instant | Instant | Instant | Instant | 1–3 min | 5–15 min | Instant |
| Dimmable | Yes | Yes | Limited | Yes (HF) | No/Tap | No | Yes (0–10/DALI) |
| Cost/lumen | High | High | Low | Low | Lowest | Low | Low–Med |
| Heat | High | Very High | Low | Low | Medium | High | Very Low |
| Mercury | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common KTU Part B (14 mark) question presents a scenario and asks: *“Justify your selection of lamp type with technical reasoning.”* Use Table 3.1 as the **backbone** of any such justification. Always quote at least **three quantitative parameters** per lamp family to score full marks.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Number of Luminaires Using Selection Criteria

This is a **derivation-type** question frequently asked in KTU ESE — worth **7 marks** in Part B.

### Problem Setup

A factory floor measures $20 \text{ m} \times 10 \text{ m}$. The design maintained illuminance $E_v = 300 \text{ lux}$. A luminaire with the following selected parameters is proposed:

- Lamp: 70 W Metal Halide, initial luminous flux $\Phi = 5600 \text{ lm}$
- Utilance (UF) = 0.65
- Lumen Maintenance Factor (LMF) = 0.85
- Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD) = 0.90

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation from the KTU formula sheet:**

$$E_v = \frac{\Phi \times N \times \text{UF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LDD}}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for the number of luminaires ($N$):**

$$N = \frac{E_v \times A}{\Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LDD}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (with explicit unit handling):**

- $E_v = 300 \text{ lux} = 300 \text{ lm} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}$
- $A = 20 \text{ m} \times 10 \text{ m} = 200 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $\Phi = 5600 \text{ lm}$
- $\text{UF} = 0.65$
- $\text{LMF} = 0.85$
- $\text{LDD} = 0.90$

$$N = \frac{300 \times 200}{5600 \times 0.65 \times 0.85 \times 0.90}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator first to maintain arithmetic clarity:**

$$5600 \times 0.65 = 3640$$

$$3640 \times 0.85 = 3094$$

$$3094 \times 0.90 = 2784.6$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$300 \times 200 = 60{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Compute final ratio:**

$$N = \frac{60{,}000}{2784.6} = 21.55$$

**Step 7 — Round up to the next integer (physical constraint — cannot have fractional luminaires):**

$$N = 22 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 8 — Verify uniform distribution using SHR:**

- Assume mounting height $H_m = 5 \text{ m}$ above working plane.
- Maximum spacing for MH luminaire: $S_{\max} = 1.2 \times H_m = 6 \text{ m}$.
- Area: $200 \text{ m}^{2}$. With 22 luminaires, possible grid: 5 rows × ~4–5 columns, spacing ~4 m × 5 m — both within SHR limit. **Selection validated.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Note:** This complete step-by-step pattern (with labeled steps, unit checks, and physical interpretation) is the **gold standard** for KTU 14-mark Part B questions. Examiner's marking key explicitly awards 1 mark for the correct governing equation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, and 2 marks for interpretation/validation.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Spacing-to-Height Ratio Validation

**Problem:** A luminaire is mounted at $H_m = 4 \text{ m}$ above the working plane in an office. The manufacturer's photometric data gives an SHR Nominal Maximum of 1.5.

**Step 1 — Recall the SHR formula:**

$$\text{SHR} = \frac{S}{H_m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for maximum permissible spacing:**

$$S_{\max} = \text{SHR} \times H_m = 1.5 \times 4 = 6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:**

- The luminaire-to-luminaire distance must **not exceed 6 m** to maintain the uniformity ratio $U_o \geq 0.6$ per IS 3646.

**Step 4 — Convert to floor planning constraint:**

- For a $12 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$ office, possible layout: 2 rows × 3 columns → spacing $4 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}$. Both values $< 6 \text{ m}$. **Selection confirmed.**

---

## 3.4 Selection Logic Flow — Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> This decision logic directly answers the question: *"Given a specific application, how do I select?"*

```
Start: Application identified
  │
  ├─ Is color critical? ──YES──> $R_a \geq 90$  → Metal Halide, Premium LED, Halogen
  │       │
  │       NO
  │       ↓
  ├─ Is the space large (H > 8 m, A > 500 m²)? ──YES──> High-bay LED or HPSV
  │       │
  │       NO
  │       ↓
  ├─ Is re-strike time critical? ──YES──> LED, Fluorescent (HF), Halogen
  │       │
  │       NO
  │       ↓
  └─ Default selection:
      Office: 36 W/40 W LED panel, 4000 K, $R_a \geq 80$
      Industrial: 70–150 W LED High-bay, 5000 K
      Roadway: 60–150 W LED Streetlight, 4000 K, IP66, IK08
      Retail: 3000 K LED Track-light, $R_a \geq 90$
```
