# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

> *List the four primary criteria used for the selection of a lamp in interior lighting installations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four primary criteria for lamp selection are:

1. **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta_L$):** Determines the electrical-to-light conversion efficiency, expressed in $\text{lm/W}$. Higher efficacy reduces energy cost over the lamp life.

2. **Color Rendering Index ($R_a$):** Determines the fidelity of object color appearance under the lamp, on a 0–100 scale. Critical for task areas such as retail, medical, and art studios.

3. **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT):** Determines the warmth/coolness of white light, in Kelvin. Influences mood, alertness, and aesthetic perception.

4. **Lamp Life and Lumen Maintenance:** Determines replacement frequency, maintenance cost, and long-term lumen depreciation. Used in computing the **Lumen Maintenance Factor (LMF)** in the maintained illuminance equation.

> Each point correctly explained: **0.75 marks × 4 = 3 marks.**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> *Define Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR) and state its typical value for an office luminaire.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** The Spacing-to-Height Ratio is the ratio of the centre-to-centre distance ($S$) between adjacent luminaires to the mounting height ($H_m$) of the luminaire above the working plane.
$$\text{SHR} = \frac{S}{H_m}$$

- **Typical Value (1 mark):** For a recessed LED troffer in an office, the SHR Nominal Maximum typically lies between **1.0 and 1.5**, ensuring a uniformity ratio $U_o \geq 0.6$.

---

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Select A or B)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question is **14 marks** and is structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with the option to choose between the two parent questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Pattern]**
>
> *A smart classroom is to be designed with dimensions 12 m × 8 m × 3.5 m (L × W × H). The work plane is 0.85 m above the floor. Target maintained illuminance is 500 lux. A luminaire is selected with the following: 36 W LED panel, 4000 K, CRI 80, initial flux 3600 lm, UF = 0.72, LMF = 0.90, LDD = 0.85, UGR = 19, SHR Nominal Max = 1.25.*
>
> **(a)** Calculate the number of luminaires required and propose a suitable layout. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Justify the lamp and luminaire selection with reference to at least four technical criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Calculation of Number of Luminaires (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the governing equation:** (1 mark)

$$N = \frac{E_v \times A}{\Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LDD}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:** (1 mark)

- $E_v = 500 \text{ lux}$
- $A = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $\Phi = 3600 \text{ lm}$
- $\text{UF} = 0.72$, $\text{LMF} = 0.90$, $\text{LDD} = 0.85$

$$N = \frac{500 \times 96}{3600 \times 0.72 \times 0.90 \times 0.85}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:** (1 mark)

$$3600 \times 0.72 = 2592$$

$$2592 \times 0.90 = 2332.8$$

$$2332.8 \times 0.85 = 1982.88$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:** (0.5 mark)

$$500 \times 96 = 48{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Final division:** (0.5 mark)

$$N = \frac{48{,}000}{1982.88} = 24.21$$

**Step 6 — Round up:** (0.5 mark)

$$N = 25 \text{ luminaires (next integer up)}$$

**Step 7 — Propose Layout:** (1 mark)

- Mounting height $H_m = 3.5 - 0.85 = 2.65 \text{ m}$ (suspension from ceiling).
- Maximum spacing: $S_{\max} = \text{SHR} \times H_m = 1.25 \times 2.65 = 3.31 \text{ m}$.
- Possible layout: 5 rows × 5 columns = 25 luminaires.
- Row spacing = $12/5 = 2.4 \text{ m}$, Column spacing = $8/5 = 1.6 \text{ m}$.
- Both less than $S_{\max} = 3.31 \text{ m}$ → **Layout valid.**

**Step 8 — Total connected load:** (1 mark)

$$P_{\text{total}} = 25 \times 36 = 900 \text{ W} = 0.9 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{\text{specific}} = \frac{0.9 \times 1000}{96} = 9.375 \text{ W/m}^{2}/\text{100 lux}$$

This is well within the ECBC benchmark of 12 W/m²/100 lux for educational spaces. **Design compliant.**

---

### Part (b) — Justification of Selection (7 marks)

| Criterion | Selected Choice | Justification (≥ 1.5 marks per criterion) |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------------------------|
| **1. Lamp Type (LED)** | 36 W LED panel | Highest efficacy (~100 lm/W), longest life (≥ 50,000 h), low maintenance, RoHS-compliant (no mercury). |
| **2. Color Temperature (4000 K)** | Neutral white | Optimal for cognitive alertness in classrooms; matches Indian reading/screen-mix usage. |
| **3. CRI ≥ 80** | CRI = 80 | Adequate for reading, writing, screen-based teaching; IS 3646 recommends $\geq 80$ for schools. |
| **4. Luminaire (Recessed troffer with diffuser)** | Direct distribution, UGR = 19 | Provides downward throw suitable for ceiling at 3.5 m, ensures glare index $\leq 19$ as per EN 12464 for classrooms. |
| **5. SHR = 1.25** | Standard recessed troffer | Provides uniform illuminance ($U_o \geq 0.6$) with the proposed 5×5 grid layout. |
| **6. Maintenance Factors (LMF, LDD)** | Conservative estimate (0.90, 0.85) | Accounts for 3-year cleaning cycle typical in educational institutions. |
| **7. Energy Compliance** | 9.375 W/m²/100 lux | Below ECBC threshold, eligible for 5-star rating under BEE. |

(Any **four** criteria fully justified score **7 marks**. Examiner's discretion: each criterion ≈ 1.75 marks.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
>
> *A warehouse of dimensions 50 m × 30 m × 12 m (L × W × H) requires general lighting. The work plane is at floor level. You are evaluating two lamp options:*
>
> - **Option 1:** 400 W Metal Halide High-bay, $\eta_L$ = 90 lm/W, CRI 70, life 20,000 h, hot re-strike 10 min, ₹ 1200/lamp.
> - **Option 2:** 200 W LED High-bay, $\eta_L$ = 130 lm/W, CRI 80, life 50,000 h, instant re-strike, ₹ 4500/luminaire (integrated).
>
> **(a)** Compare the two options using a minimum of five selection criteria and recommend the more suitable option. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** If the chosen option is operated for 12 hours/day, 300 days/year, with electricity at ₹ 8/unit, compute the **annual energy cost** and the **payback period** if the LED option has a 1.5× higher initial cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — Comparative Justification (7 marks)

| Criterion | Option 1: 400 W MH | Option 2: 200 W LED | Winner |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|--------|
| **Efficacy** | 90 lm/W | 130 lm/W | **LED** |
| **CRI** | 70 | 80 | **LED** (tied with better) |
| **Life** | 20,000 h | 50,000 h | **LED** (2.5×) |
| **Re-strike** | 10 min (hot) | Instant | **LED** |
| **Energy/lumen** | Higher (low $\eta_L$) | Lower (high $\eta_L$) | **LED** |
| **Heat Load** | High | Low | **LED** |
| **Initial Cost** | Lower (₹ 1200 + ballast) | Higher (₹ 4500 integrated) | **MH** |
| **Toxicity** | Contains Hg | No mercury | **LED** |

> **Recommendation: Option 2 (200 W LED High-bay)** is justified by 6 of 7 criteria. (Each criterion ≈ 1 mark; 5 criteria fully explained = 7 marks.)

---

### Part (b) — Annual Energy Cost and Payback (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Annual operating hours:** (0.5 mark)

$$t = 12 \times 300 = 3600 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 2 — Annual energy for MH option (per luminaire):** (1 mark)

$$E_{\text{MH}} = 400 \times 3600 = 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 1440 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Annual cost for MH (per luminaire):** (0.5 mark)

$$C_{\text{MH}} = 1440 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 11{,}520$$

**Step 4 — Annual energy for LED option (per luminaire):** (1 mark)

$$E_{\text{LED}} = 200 \times 3600 = 720{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 720 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Annual cost for LED (per luminaire):** (0.5 mark)

$$C_{\text{LED}} = 720 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 5760$$

**Step 6 — Annual savings per luminaire:** (1 mark)

$$\Delta C = 11{,}520 - 5760 = \text{₹ } 5760$$

**Step 7 — Payback period (LED cost is 1.5× MH; assume MH = ₹ 1200 → LED = ₹ 1800):** (1.5 marks)

- Initial price difference: $\Delta C_{\text{initial}} = 1800 - 1200 = \text{₹ } 600$
- Note: If we treat the question's *“1.5× higher initial cost”* as $\Delta C_{\text{initial}} = 0.5 \times 1200 = 600$ — same answer.

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{\Delta C_{\text{initial}}}{\Delta C_{\text{annual}}} = \frac{600}{5760} = 0.104 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Payback} \approx 0.10 \text{ years} = 38 \text{ days}}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:** (1 mark)

> A payback period of **~38 days** is extraordinarily attractive. The LED option is decisively the engineering choice, both technically and economically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Many students write $E_v = \dfrac{N \times \Phi}{A}$ without including the **three multiplicative factors (UF × LMF × LDD)**. This gives an *un-maintained* result, **NOT** what is asked.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to **round UP** the number of luminaires. Using $N = 24.21$ → $N = 24$ is a physical impossibility.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Failing to state **units explicitly** in substitution (lux, lm, m²).
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** In Part B justifications, students list criteria but do not **quote numerical values** (e.g., "CRI 80" not "good CRI"). Always quote the value!
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 (–3 marks):** In payback problems, students confuse *payback period* with *ROI %*. Both are different formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Save This for the Day Before the Exam!**

### Lamp Selection Essentials

- ☐ **Efficacy hierarchy:** LED > HPSV > MH > Fluorescent > Halogen > Incandescent.
- ☐ **CRI hierarchy:** Incandescent (100) > Halogen (100) > Premium LED (95+) > Fluorescent (80–95) > MH (65–95) > HPSV (20–30).
- ☐ **CCT mnemonic:** **W**arm (< 3300 K) → **N**eutral (3300–5300 K) → **C**ool (> 5300 K).
- ☐ **Re-strike:** LED = 0 sec; HPSV ≈ 1–3 min; MH ≈ 5–15 min.
- ☐ **Life:** LED (50 kh) > Fluorescent (20 kh) > MH (15 kh) > HPSV (24 kh) > Halogen (3 kh) > Incandescent (1 kh).
- ☐ **Hazardous content:** Mercury → CFL, T5/T8, HPSV, MH (requires special disposal).

### Luminaire Selection Essentials

- ☐ **LOR** is the light output ratio — choose luminaires with **LOR > 0.7** for high-efficiency designs.
- ☐ **SHR** maximum values are listed in manufacturer photometric data — use them to **validate grid layout**.
- ☐ **UGR** < 19 (offices), < 22 (industrial), < 25 (circulation).
- ☐ **IP rating** selection: IP20 (indoor dry) → IP44 (bathroom) → IP65/IP66 (outdoor) → IP68 (submersible).
- ☐ **IK rating** ≥ 08 for vandal-prone public areas.

### The Five-Step Selection Procedure (Examinable!)

1. ☐ **Define the task** (e.g., office, street, factory).
2. ☐ **Fix the illuminance** ($E_v$) per IS 3646.
3. ☐ **Choose the lamp** using efficacy, CRI, CCT, life, re-strike.
4. ☐ **Choose the luminaire** using photometric distribution, IP, IK, UGR, SHR.
5. ☐ **Verify layout** by computing $N$, spacing, uniformity, and glare.

### Critical Formulas to Memorize

- $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$
- $N = \dfrac{E_v \times A}{\Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LDD}}$
- $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{S}{H_m}$
- $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{\text{initial}}}{\Delta C_{\text{annual}}}$

### KTU Board Buzzwords (Use These for Top Marks!)

- ☐ **ECBC compliance** (Energy Conservation Building Code, India)
- ☐ **BEE 5-Star rating** (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)
- ☐ **IS 3646** (Indian Standard for interior illumination)
- ☐ **UGR** (Unified Glare Rating — EN 12464)
- ☐ **RoHS** (Restriction of Hazardous Substances)
- ☐ **LMF & LDD** (Lumen Maintenance Factor & Luminaire Dirt Depreciation)

> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Justify your selection"*, structure your answer in a **table** with three columns: **Criterion | Chosen Option | Technical Reasoning with Numbers**. This is the **examiner-friendly** format that scores the maximum marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Illumination Level Available on Road
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Illumination Level Available on Road

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Illumination Level on a Road** is defined as the *luminous flux incident per unit area on the road surface*, measured in **lux (lx)** or **lumens per square meter (lm/m²)**. For an engineering-grade design of street lighting, KTU 2024 Scheme mandates the evaluation of **Average Horizontal Illumination (E_avg)** on the carriageway, which is computed using the standard lumen-method formula adapted for linear roadway geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The illumination level on a road is *not* a single point value — it is a statistical average computed over the entire road surface area illuminated by a single luminaire (or a row of luminaires). The governing equation is:
>
> $$E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$$
>
> where every term has a strict physical meaning and standard unit, as defined in the KTU Board Examiner reference.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving on a national highway at night. The road is wide, dark, and you can barely see the white lane markings. Then you approach a stretch where evenly spaced street lights illuminate the entire road uniformly — suddenly, every pothole, pedestrian, and divider becomes visible. The "level of illumination" is essentially *how much light power (flux) is being thrown onto every square meter of that road surface*.

Think of it like this: if you have a bucket of water (light flux) and a floor of a certain size (road area), the **depth of water on the floor** is your **illumination level**. Spread the same bucket over a larger floor, and the water depth (illumination) drops. That is the heart of road illumination calculation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Road Illumination Calculation (as per IS 1944 / KTU Module 4):**
> 1. **Lamp Flux (Φ)** — total light output of the source, in **lumens**.
> 2. **Utilization Factor (UF)** — the fraction of lamp flux that *actually reaches* the road surface (not absorbed by poles, wasted above horizon, or lost sideways).
> 3. **Maintenance Factor (MF)** — derating factor to account for lamp aging, dust accumulation on luminaires, and lamp lumen depreciation (LLD).

## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standards (in Bold)

- **Luminous Efficacy (η):** typically **80–150 lm/W** for modern HPSV / LED street-lighting lamps.
- **Recommended Average Illumination (E_avg) for Roads (IS 1944 / KTU Standard Values):**
  - **Arterial roads / main roads:** **30 lux**
  - **Residential / secondary roads:** **15 lux**
  - **Footpaths / cycle tracks:** **8 lux**
  - **Major district roads:** **15 lux**
- **Coefficient of Utilization (CU) for road lighting luminaires:** ranges between **0.25 and 0.55** depending on the **Side-Index (SI)** and **S/HM ratio**.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** typically **0.7 to 0.8** for HPSV, **0.8 to 0.9** for LED.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Calculation Method

The lumen method, when applied to a *linear* road geometry, requires the following chain of reasoning:

**Step 1 — Identify the Lamp and Its Flux**
- The lamp type (HPSV, MH, LED, fluorescent, low-pressure sodium) determines the *luminous flux output* (Φ) in lumens.
- For multiple lamps per luminaire, multiply by **N**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Luminaire Photometric Distribution**
- The luminaire's *Intensity Distribution Curve (IDC)* decides how much light is directed downward onto the road.
- This is captured quantitatively by the **Utilization Factor (UF)**, defined as:

$$UF = \dfrac{\text{Luminous flux reaching the road surface}}{\text{Total luminous flux emitted by the lamp(s)}}$$

- UF depends on the **S/HM ratio**, the **transverse position** of the luminaire relative to the road edge, and the **longitudinal throw**.

**Step 3 — Apply the Maintenance Factor (MF)**
- Real installations suffer from **lamp lumen depreciation (LLD)**, **luminaire dirt depreciation (LDD)**, and **burnout factor (BOF)**.
- KTU standard:

$$MF = LLD \times LDD \times BOF$$

- A typical combined value used in KTU numericals is **MF = 0.7 to 0.8** for HPSV and **0.8 to 0.9** for LED.

**Step 4 — Define the Area to be Illuminated**
- For a single luminaire, the area = **W × S**, where:
  - **W** = width of the road (in m)
  - **S** = spacing between two consecutive luminaires (in m)

**Step 5 — Compute the Average Illumination**

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Always state the **units explicitly** in the final answer. The output of the formula is in **lux (lx)**, which is dimensionally equivalent to **lm/m²**.

## 2.2 Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio (S/HM) — Critical Design Rule

The **S/HM ratio** is a dimensionless design index that governs the longitudinal spacing of luminaires for a given mounting height. KTU 2024 prescribes the following:

| Arrangement Type | Description | Maximum S/HM Ratio | Recommended Spacing (for HM = 10 m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Side** | Luminaires on one side of road | **S ≤ HM** | S = 10 m |
| **Staggered (Zig-Zag)** | Alternate sides | **S ≤ 1.5 × HM** | S = 15 m |
| **Opposite (Twin)** | Both sides, same lateral position | **S ≤ 2 × HM** | S = 20 m |
| **Central Suspension** | Luminaires hung over center median | **S ≤ 2 × HM** | S = 20 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why S/HM Matters (the 'How'):** Beyond a certain spacing, the bright pool of light under one luminaire no longer overlaps with the next, producing a "zebra-stripe" lighting pattern that ruins **uniformity**. The S/HM ratio is empirically derived from photometric studies and is *non-negotiable* in KTU design problems.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$ | Average horizontal illumination | lux (lx) |
| 2 | $UF = \dfrac{\Phi_{road}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | Utilization factor (flux on road ÷ total flux) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $MF = LLD \times LDD \times BOF$ | Maintenance factor components | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S = k \times H_M$ | Spacing rule, k depends on arrangement | metres (m) |
| 5 | $A = W \times S$ | Area covered per luminaire | m² |
| 6 | $\Phi = P \times \eta$ | Lamp flux = wattage × efficacy | lumens (lm) |
| 7 | $E_{min} = U_0 \times E_{avg}$ | Minimum illumination (uniformity) | lux (lx) |
| 8 | $U_0 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$ | Overall uniformity ratio (IS: ≥ 0.4) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **UF (Utilization Factor)** with **CU (Coefficient of Utilization)**. In some textbooks they are used interchangeably for road lighting, but KTU 2024 module notes treat them as synonymous for the flux reaching the *road* (UF), distinct from the flux reaching a *workplane* in indoor lighting (CU).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This calculation underpins the design of:
- **Municipal street-lighting tenders** (PWD, NHAI, Smart City Mission projects).
- **Highway lighting** for expressways, NH/SH corridors.
- **Tunnel lighting** (where uniformity is critical for driver adaptation).
- **Airport taxiway lighting** (which is essentially "road lighting" for aircraft).
- **Solar-powered street lighting sizing** — flux output directly determines the **PV panel wattage and battery Ah** required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average Illumination Formula for a Road

The lumen method, originally developed for indoor lighting by **Ward Harrison (1916)**, is adapted for road geometry as follows.

### Step 1 — Start with the Fundamental Definition of Illumination

Illumination **E** is luminous flux per unit area. For a finite surface of area **A** receiving a total flux **Φ_total**:

$$E = \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{A}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Total Useful Flux on the Road

The total flux emitted by a single luminaire is **N × Φ** (where **N** is the number of lamps inside the luminaire). Of this, only a fraction actually reaches the road surface. Two correction factors are applied:

- **UF (Utilization Factor):** accounts for the fraction of lamp flux that reaches the road (depends on luminaire optics and geometry).
- **MF (Maintenance Factor):** accounts for depreciation of light over time (dust, aging, etc.).

Therefore, the *effective flux* reaching the road is:

$$\Phi_{effective} = \Phi \times N \times UF \times MF$$

### Step 3 — Express the Road Area for One Luminaire

For a road of width **W** and luminaire spacing **S** (longitudinal distance between two adjacent poles), each luminaire is responsible for illuminating an area:

$$A = W \times S$$

### Step 4 — Combine

Dividing the effective flux by the area:

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$$

This is the **governing equation** of KTU Module 4. All road illumination numericals reduce to evaluating this expression with given data.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style 14-Mark Numerical

### **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**

> A road **8 m wide** is to be illuminated by **400 W HPSV lamps** mounted at a height of **10 m** on poles located on one side. Each lamp emits **48,000 lumens**. The utilization factor is **0.4** and the maintenance factor is **0.75**. Calculate:
>
> (a) The spacing between the lamps.
> (b) The average illumination on the road surface.
> (c) The minimum illumination if the uniformity ratio is 0.4.

### **Solution (a) — Spacing Between Lamps**

For **single-side arrangement**, the KTU design rule is:

$$S = H_M = 10 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S = 10 \text{ m}}$$

### **Solution (b) — Average Illumination**

Using the governing equation:

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$$

Substituting the values:

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{48{,}000 \times 1 \times 0.4 \times 0.75}{8 \times 10}$$

Step-wise simplification:

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{48{,}000 \times 0.30}{80}$$

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{14{,}400}{80}$$

$$\boxed{E_{avg} = 180 \text{ lux}}$$

### **Solution (c) — Minimum Illumination**

Given the uniformity ratio $U_0 = 0.4$:

$$E_{min} = U_0 \times E_{avg}$$

$$E_{min} = 0.4 \times 180$$

$$\boxed{E_{min} = 72 \text{ lux}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis

The table below shows how **E_avg** varies with key parameters (holding W = 8 m, S = 10 m constant, Φ = 48,000 lm):

| Scenario | N | UF | MF | E_avg (lux) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0.30 | 0.70 | 126.0 | Older HPSV, dirty luminaire |
| 2 | 1 | 0.40 | 0.75 | 180.0 | **Standard KTU case** |
| 3 | 1 | 0.50 | 0.80 | 240.0 | New LED + clean optics |
| 4 | 2 | 0.40 | 0.75 | 360.0 | Twin-lamp luminaire |
| 5 | 1 | 0.40 | 0.60 | 144.0 | Poor maintenance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight from the matrix:** A **drop in MF from 0.75 to 0.60** (i.e., a neglected maintenance regime) reduces illumination by **20%**. This is why KTU and IS 1944 mandate *regular cleaning* and *group relamping* schedules.

---

## 3.4 Inverse Problem — Finding Number of Lamps for a Target Illumination

Sometimes KTU asks: *Given a target E_avg, find the lamp wattage or the number of lamps per km.*

**Formula inversion:**

$$N_{luminaires} = \dfrac{E_{avg} \times W \times L_{road}}{\Phi \times N_{lamps} \times UF \times MF}$$

where $L_{road}$ is the road length.

### **Quick Example:**

A 1 km stretch of 8 m road needs **E_avg = 30 lux**. Lamps: HPSV 250 W (Φ = 28,000 lm), UF = 0.4, MF = 0.75.

$$N_{luminaires} = \dfrac{30 \times 8 \times 1000}{28{,}000 \times 1 \times 0.4 \times 0.75}$$

$$N_{luminaires} = \dfrac{240{,}000}{8{,}400}$$

$$N_{luminaires} \approx 28.6 \implies 29 \text{ luminaires}$$

Spacing:

$$S = \dfrac{1000}{29} \approx 34.5 \text{ m}$$

This is *too large* for a single-side arrangement (rule: S ≤ HM). So either **staggered** (S ≤ 1.5 × HM) or **opposite** (S ≤ 2 × HM) arrangement must be adopted. The problem is solved iteratively.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Road Illumination Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: KTU Road Illumination Problem] --> B[Identify Lamp Type and Flux Phi in lumens]
    B --> C[Identify Number of Lamps N per Luminaire]
    C --> D[Select Mounting Height HM]
    D --> E{Choose Arrangement Type}
    E -->|Single Side| F[Set S equals HM]
    E -->|Staggered| G[Set S equals 1.5 times HM]
    E -->|Opposite| H[Set S equals 2 times HM]
    E -->|Central Suspension| I[Set S equals 2 times HM]
    F --> J[Identify Road Width W]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Lookup Utilization Factor UF from Photometric Table]
    K --> L[Apply Maintenance Factor MF]
    L --> M[Compute Average Illumination using Eavg equals Phi times N times UF times MF divided by W times S]
    M --> N[Check Uniformity Ratio U0 equals Emin divided by Eavg]
    N --> O{Is U0 greater than or equal 0.4}
    O -->|Yes| P[Design ACCEPTED]
    O -->|No| Q[Reduce Spacing or Change Arrangement]
    Q --> E
    P --> R[END: Final Spacing and Illumination Level]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Illumination System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Lamp HPSV or LED] --> L2[Luminaire Optics Reflector and Refractor]
    L2 --> L3[Light Distribution IDC Curve]
    L3 --> L4[Flux on Road Surface Phi times UF]
    L4 --> L5[Maintenance Derating MF]
    L5 --> L6[Effective Flux Phi times UF times MF]
    L6 --> L7[Area Coverage W times S]
    L7 --> L8[Average Illumination Eavg in lux]
    L8 --> L9[Uniformity Check U0]
    L9 --> L10[Compliance with IS 1944]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Layout of a Road with Staggered Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Road_Width_W
        R1[Lane 1]
        R2[Lane 2]
        R3[Lane 3]
        R4[Lane 4]
    end
    subgraph Left_Side
        P1[Pole A at HM]
        P2[Pole B at HM]
        P3[Pole C at HM]
    end
    subgraph Right_Side
        Q1[Pole D at HM]
        Q2[Pole E at HM]
        Q3[Pole F at HM]
    end
    P1 -. illuminates .-> R1
    Q2 -. illuminates .-> R4
    P2 -. illuminates .-> R2
    P3 -. illuminates .-> R1
    Q1 -. illuminates .-> R4
    Q3 -. illuminates .-> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In a staggered (zig-zag) arrangement, poles on opposite sides are *not aligned laterally*. This produces overlapping pools of illumination that greatly improve the **uniformity ratio** without reducing spacing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define *Utilization Factor* as applied to road lighting. What is its typical range for street-lighting luminaires?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Utilization Factor (UF)** is defined as the ratio of the luminous flux that reaches the road surface (working plane) to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamp(s) inside the luminaire. Mathematically:

$$UF = \dfrac{\Phi_{road}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity. For typical street-lighting luminaires (cut-off or semi-cut-off types), UF ranges between **0.25 and 0.55**, depending on the S/HM ratio, the transverse position of the pole, and the optical design of the luminaire. Higher UF indicates a more efficient luminaire that directs a larger fraction of its light onto the road rather than wasting it above the horizon or sideways. **[3 marks — 1 mark each for definition, formula, and range]**

---

### **Q2.** What is the significance of the **S/HM ratio** in road lighting design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **S/HM ratio** is the ratio of the **longitudinal spacing (S)** between two consecutive luminaires to the **mounting height (HM)** of the luminaires. It is a critical design parameter because it governs:

1. **Uniformity of illumination** on the road surface — if S is too large relative to HM, bright and dark patches alternate, harming driver visibility.
2. **Optimal pole-density economics** — too small S increases installation cost; too large S fails the uniformity test.

Standard KTU / IS 1944 values:
- Single-side: **S ≤ HM**
- Staggered: **S ≤ 1.5 × HM**
- Opposite / Central: **S ≤ 2 × HM**

**[3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for role, 1 for the standard values]**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern | ESE Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

A 12 m wide arterial road is to be illuminated to an average level of **30 lux** using **250 W HPSV lamps** (each emitting **28,000 lumens**), mounted at a height of **10 m** on poles in a **staggered arrangement**. The utilization factor is **0.45** and the maintenance factor is **0.8**.

**(a)** Calculate the spacing between the poles and the total number of poles required per kilometre. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the wattage per km of road. If each pole costs ₹ 12,000 and the electricity tariff is ₹ 7 per kWh, estimate the **annual energy cost** assuming 12 hours of operation per day. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

For staggered arrangement, the KTU design rule is:

$$S = 1.5 \times H_M = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \text{ m}$$

**Verification of illumination level:**

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$$

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{28{,}000 \times 1 \times 0.45 \times 0.8}{12 \times 15}$$

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{10{,}080}{180} = 56 \text{ lux}$$

Since **56 lux > 30 lux** (required), the design is acceptable, but it is over-illuminated. To optimize, we may either reduce the number of lamps or accept the higher level.

**Number of poles per km:**

$$N_{poles} = \dfrac{L_{road}}{S} = \dfrac{1000}{15} \approx 66.67 \implies 67 \text{ poles}$$

**Pole cost per km** = 67 × 12,000 = ₹ 8,04,000 **[1 mark]**

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Total lamp wattage per km:**

$$P_{total} = N_{poles} \times P_{lamp} = 67 \times 250 = 16{,}750 \text{ W} = 16.75 \text{ kW}$$

**Daily energy consumption (12 hours):**

$$E_{daily} = 16.75 \times 12 = 201 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Annual energy consumption:**

$$E_{annual} = 201 \times 365 = 73{,}365 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Annual energy cost:**

$$\text{Cost} = 73{,}365 \times 7 = ₹ 5{,}13{,}555 \text{ per year}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual energy cost} \approx ₹ 5.14 \text{ lakhs per km per year}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

A 7 m wide residential road is illuminated by **150 W HPSV lamps** with **luminous flux = 14,500 lumens per lamp**. The lamps are mounted at **8 m height** in a **single-side arrangement**. The utilization factor is **0.38** and the maintenance factor is **0.7**.

**(a)** Compute the average horizontal illumination and the minimum illumination if the uniformity ratio is **0.4**. Compare with IS 1944 standards for a residential road. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the road is to be re-designed with **staggered arrangement** using the same lamps and mounting height, calculate the new spacing and the new average illumination. Comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Spacing (single side).**

$$S = H_M = 8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Average illumination.**

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S} = \dfrac{14{,}500 \times 1 \times 0.38 \times 0.7}{7 \times 8}$$

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{3{,}857}{56} = 68.87 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 3: Minimum illumination.**

$$E_{min} = U_0 \times E_{avg} = 0.4 \times 68.87 = 27.55 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 4: Comparison with IS 1944.**

| Parameter | Computed | IS 1944 Standard (Residential) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_{avg}$ | 68.87 lux | **15 lux** | ✅ Exceeds |
| $E_{min}$ | 27.55 lux | **6 lux** | ✅ Exceeds |

The design **satisfies** IS 1944 with a comfortable margin. **[7 marks — distributed across spacing, E_avg, E_min, and the IS comparison table]**

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: New spacing (staggered).**

$$S = 1.5 \times H_M = 1.5 \times 8 = 12 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: New average illumination.**

$$E_{avg,new} = \dfrac{14{,}500 \times 1 \times 0.38 \times 0.7}{7 \times 12} = \dfrac{3{,}857}{84} = 45.92 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 3: Comparison and comment.**

| Parameter | Single-Side | Staggered | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spacing | 8 m | 12 m | **+50 %** |
| E_avg | 68.87 lux | 45.92 lux | **−33 %** |
| Uniformity | Lower | **Higher** | **Improved** |

**Comment:** The staggered arrangement allows **50% more spacing** between poles (economic benefit) while still exceeding the 15-lux IS 1944 standard for residential roads. The uniformity of illumination is significantly better in staggered mode, with reduced glare and a smoother light gradient for drivers and pedestrians. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert W (road width) and S (spacing) into **metres** before computing the area in m². Always double-check units.
> 2. **Wrong arrangement (–2 marks):** Mixing up single-side (S = HM) with opposite (S = 2·HM). The arrangement dictates the spacing rule.
> 3. **Forgetting MF (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU numericals *always* include the maintenance factor. If you compute E_avg without it, you will be marked down heavily.
> 4. **Rounding errors in number of poles (–1 mark):** Always round **up** the number of poles (you cannot install 0.67 of a pole), and re-verify the spacing.
> 5. **Missing uniformity check (–1 mark):** A complete KTU answer should always verify whether $U_0 \geq 0.4$, as per IS 1944.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Road Illumination Calculation**
>
> ✅ **Master Formula:** $E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{W \times S}$ — memorize the symbol order.
> ✅ **Spacing Rules:** Single (S=HM), Staggered (S=1.5·HM), Opposite (S=2·HM), Central (S=2·HM).
> ✅ **Typical Φ values:** 250 W HPSV ≈ 28,000 lm; 400 W HPSV ≈ 48,000 lm; 150 W HPSV ≈ 14,500 lm; 100 W LED ≈ 10,000–12,000 lm.
> ✅ **UF range:** 0.25 to 0.55 for road-lighting luminaires.
> ✅ **MF range:** 0.7 to 0.8 (HPSV), 0.8 to 0.9 (LED).
> ✅ **IS 1944 Targets:** Arterial = 30 lux, Residential = 15 lux, Footpath = 8 lux.
> ✅ **Uniformity:** $U_0 = E_{min}/E_{avg} \geq 0.4$ is **mandatory** in KTU problems.
> ✅ **Inverse calculation:** $N_{poles} = L_{road}/S$; round **up** to the next integer.
> ✅ **Annual cost formula:** $\text{Cost} = N_{poles} \times P_{lamp} \times H_{daily} \times 365 \times \text{Tariff}$.
> ✅ **Staggered > Single side** in uniformity; **Opposite > Staggered** in transverse uniformity.
> ✅ Always show **all substitution steps** — KTU examiners award step-marks, not just final answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flood Lighting: Terms Related to Flood Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flood Lighting: Terms Related to Flood Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Flood Lighting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flood Lighting** is a specialized technique of artificial illumination in which a large outdoor area, object, or facade is flooded with light by projecting a controlled beam from one or more **floodlight projectors** mounted at a suitable distance and height. Unlike interior lighting, the light source is placed **away from the area** to be illuminated, and the beam is aimed (tilted) towards the target.

A **Floodlight** is, therefore, a projector-type luminaire designed to direct light in a specific direction with a well-defined **beam spread**, producing a concentrated patch of illumination on a distant surface.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Stage Spotlight vs. The Room Bulb"** 🎭
> Think of a stage performer lit by a single **spotlight from above**. The bulb itself is far away (say, 10 m up on a truss), but a tight, focused beam creates a bright, even pool of light exactly on the performer's head. Flood lighting works identically: the **lamp is mounted high and far away** (on a tower, building edge, or pole), and the **beam is angled downward** to "paint" light onto a stadium, monument, parking lot, or building facade. The brightness on the ground is engineered by carefully choosing the **mounting height**, the **aiming angle**, and the **beam spread** of the luminaire.

### 1.3 Scope of Flood Lighting Applications

| Application | Typical Area | Mounting Style |
|---|---|---|
| Sports stadiums / arenas | Cricket, football, hockey grounds | High-mast towers (20 m–45 m) |
| Building facades | Architectural heritage, monuments | Ground-mounted or roof-edge |
| Railway yards, ports | Container terminals, marshalling yards | Tall mast towers (30 m+ high-mast) |
| Parking lots, airports | Aprons, taxiways | Pole-mounted (10 m–15 m) |
| Industrial sites | Construction zones, mining | Trailer-mounted mobile towers |
| Security perimeters | Borders, prisons | Wall or pole-mounted |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When the question says "give the terms related to flood lighting", examiners expect the **terminology used by lighting designers in the field**, not the lamp-side technical terms. Focus on **geometry, beam control, and project planning vocabulary**.

### 1.4 Why Flood Lighting Has Its Own Vocabulary

Flood lighting is fundamentally a **projector-and-target** geometry problem. The vocabulary is therefore derived from three engineering domains:

1. **Geometric optics** — beam spread, throw distance, aiming angle, field angle.
2. **Illumination engineering** — lux, lumens, utilization factor, glare index.
3. **Outdoor lighting standards** — NEMA beam classification, cut-off, light trespass, spill light.

The rest of this module unpacks each of these terms rigorously.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Geometry of a Floodlight — Core Concept

A floodlight mounted at height **H** above the ground, tilted at an **aiming angle α** from the vertical, projects a circular or elliptical pool of light whose size depends on the **beam spread β** of the luminaire. The relationship between these four parameters forms the foundation of floodlight design.

### 2.2 Definitions of Each Key Term

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master List of Flood Lighting Terms (KTU Module 4 Syllabus)**

1. **Floodlight (Projector)**
   A directional luminaire that concentrates light into a defined beam, usually employing a **reflector** (specular or diffuse) and sometimes a **lens**, designed for outdoor projection of light over a large area.

2. **Beam Spread (β) — Beam Angle**
   The total angular extent of the light beam measured in the **plane containing the axis of maximum intensity**, between the two directions in which the intensity falls to **10% of the peak intensity** (some standards use 50% — half-peak). For symmetrical floodlights, beam spread is measured in both the horizontal and vertical planes.
   - **Unit:** degrees (°)
   - **Typical range:** 5° (narrow spot) to 160° (wide flood)

3. **Field Angle**
   The angular extent between the two directions in which the intensity drops to **10% of the peak**. The field angle is always **larger** than the beam spread angle.

4. **Mounting Height (H)**
   The vertical distance from the **plane being illuminated** to the **centre of the floodlight lamp**. Mounting height governs the throw, the patch size, and the glare control.

5. **Aiming Angle (α)**
   The angle that the **axis of the floodlight** makes with the **vertical (nadir)**. It determines where the brightest part of the beam falls on the ground.
   - α = 0° → straight down (nadir)
   - α > 0° → tilted toward a target

6. **Throw Distance (T)**
   The slant distance from the floodlight to the centre of the illuminated area.
   $$T = \frac{H}{\cos \alpha}$$

7. **Beam Lumens**
   The total luminous flux emitted **within the beam spread angle**. It is a fraction of the total lamp lumens, governed by the optical efficiency of the reflector.

8. **Coefficient of Utilization (CU) — Utilization Factor**
   The ratio of lumens reaching the working plane to the total lumens emitted by the lamp. It accounts for reflector efficiency, beam shape, and the geometry between the projector and the area to be lit.
   $$CU = \frac{\text{Lumens reaching the target area}}{\text{Total lamp lumens}}$$

9. **Maintenance Factor (MF)**
   The ratio of illumination on the work plane **after a period of use** to the **initial** illumination. It accounts for lamp lumen depreciation, luminaire dirt accumulation, and room/area surface depreciation.
   - Typical value: **0.7 to 0.8** for outdoor flood lighting.

10. **Waste Light Factor (WLF)**
    Fraction of light emitted by the floodlight that **does not fall on the intended target** (spilled into the sky, beyond the boundary, or absorbed by surroundings). In efficient designs, WLF ≤ 0.1.

11. **Spill Light / Light Trespass**
    Light that falls **outside the boundary** of the property being illuminated. It is a **legal and environmental concern** in urban flood lighting and is regulated by ordinances.

12. **Glare**
    The sensation of discomfort or reduction in visibility caused by **excessive luminance in the field of view**. In flood lighting, glare is controlled by selecting proper mounting height, beam cut-off, and aiming angles.

13. **Cut-off Angle**
    The angle from the nadir (vertical) beyond which the floodlight emits **negligible (typically < 2.5%) intensity**. It is critical for dark-sky compliance and neighbour-friendly designs.
    - **Full cut-off:** 0% intensity at 90° and above.
    - **Cut-off:** ≤ 2.5% intensity at 90°.
    - **Semi cut-off:** ≤ 5% intensity at 90°.

14. **Illumination Level (E)**
    The luminous flux received per unit area of the target surface, measured in **lux (lm/m²)**. For flood lighting, E typically ranges from 20 lux (parking) to 1500+ lux (TV-broadcast sports).

15. **NEMA Beam Spread Classification**
    A standardized classification defined by the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)** for outdoor floodlights. It rates projectors by their **horizontal (H) and vertical (V) beam spreads** on a 7×7 grid from NEMA 1 (very narrow, 10°–18°) to NEMA 7 (very wide, > 130°).

16. **High-Mast Lighting**
    A special flood-lighting installation where multiple floodlights are mounted on a single **mast of 20 m to 45 m height**, often with a lowering mechanism for maintenance. Used for large areas like airports and container yards.

17. **Tower-Mounted vs. Pole-Mounted vs. Ground-Mounted**
    Refers to the structural support of the floodlight — affecting wind loading, glare control, and aiming flexibility.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: How the Terms Interact

1. The **designer** first fixes the **area to be illuminated** (size, location).
2. The **mounting height H** is chosen to satisfy glare and uniformity criteria.
3. From H and the target area's farthest point, the designer computes the **required beam spread β** and the **aiming angle α**.
4. Using the desired **illumination level E**, the designer applies the **lumen method**:
   $$\text{Total lumens required} = \frac{E \times A}{CU \times MF}$$
   where **A** is the area of the target patch.
5. The **number of floodlights** = total lumens / lumens per floodlight.
6. The **beam lumens** must be confirmed from the manufacturer's photometric data (IES file) to ensure the beam actually covers the geometry.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance | $E$ | $E = \Phi / A$ | lux | 20 – 1500 |
| 2 | Solid angle subtended by beam | $\Omega$ | $\Omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos(\beta/2))$ | sr | — |
| 3 | Luminous intensity on axis | $I$ | $I = \Phi_{\text{beam}} / \Omega$ | cd | — |
| 4 | Illuminance at point below | $E_0$ | $E_0 = I / H^2$ | lux | — |
| 5 | Illuminance at point along axis | $E_\alpha$ | $E_\alpha = I \cos^3 \alpha / H^2$ | lux | — |
| 6 | Beam spread (full coverage) | $\beta$ | $\tan(\beta/2) = R / H$ | degrees | 5° – 160° |
| 7 | Diameter of circular area covered | $D$ | $D = 2H \tan(\beta/2)$ | m | — |
| 8 | Throw distance | $T$ | $T = H / \cos \alpha$ | m | — |
| 9 | Lumen method | $\Phi_{\text{tot}}$ | $\Phi_{\text{tot}} = E \cdot A / (CU \cdot MF)$ | lm | — |
| 10 | Number of floodlights | $N$ | $N = \Phi_{\text{tot}} / \Phi_{\text{one}}$ | — | integer |
| 11 | Half-peak beam spread (NEMA) | — | $\beta_{50} = 2 \tan^{-1}(D_{50}/2H)$ | degrees | — |
| 12 | One-tenth-peak field spread | — | $\beta_{10} = 2 \tan^{-1}(D_{10}/2H)$ | degrees | — |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the **vertical bar** for absolute value or separation is intentionally avoided inside cells. For example, intensity is written as just $I$, never as $\vert I \vert$. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX if absolute-value notation is needed mid-table.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

This vocabulary is the daily working language of:
- **Sports lighting designers** (FIFA, ICC, BCCI stadium standards).
- **Architectural lighting consultants** (LEED, BREEAM certifications).
- **Highways and airport engineers** (CIE 140, IS 1944, IS 6665 standards).
- **Stage and event lighting technicians** (using IES files and photometric data).
- **Solar/LED floodlight manufacturers** (publishing NEMA-class, CCT, beam-angle data on datasheets).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beam Spread Required to Cover a Circular Area

Consider a circular area of radius **R** on the ground. A floodlight is mounted at height **H** directly above the centre of the circle. The floodlight's axis is vertical (nadir). For the **edge of the beam** to just reach the perimeter of the area, the half-angle of the beam, $\beta/2$, must satisfy:

$$\tan\left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right) = \frac{R}{H}$$

Therefore, the **required beam spread** is:

$$\beta = 2 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{R}{H}\right) = 2 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D}{2H}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric interpretation:** The line from the floodlight to the edge of the illuminated circle forms a right triangle. The opposite side is the radius R, the adjacent side is the height H, and the angle at the floodlight is $\beta/2$. The arctangent of the ratio gives the half-angle, and doubling it gives the full beam spread.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Illuminance Directly Below a Tilted Floodlight (Inverse Square Law with Cosine Correction)

Let a floodlight be mounted at height **H** and tilted at angle **α** from the vertical. The luminous intensity along the axis of the floodlight is **I** (in candelas). We want the illuminance at the point **P**, which lies on the axis of the floodlight (i.e., at the centre of the beam on the ground).

**Step 1 — Slant distance from floodlight to P:**

$$d = \frac{H}{\cos \alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Illuminance from the inverse square law (perpendicular incidence):**

$$E_{\perp} = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

**Step 3 — But the surface (the ground) is horizontal, not perpendicular to the beam. The light strikes the ground at angle α. Apply Lambert's cosine law to correct:**

$$E_{\text{ground}} = E_{\perp} \cdot \cos \alpha = \frac{I}{d^2} \cdot \cos \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Substitute d = H / cos α:**

$$E_{\text{ground}} = \frac{I}{\left(H / \cos \alpha\right)^2} \cdot \cos \alpha$$

$$E_{\text{ground}} = \frac{I \cdot \cos^2 \alpha}{H^2} \cdot \cos \alpha$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{ground}} = \frac{I \cos^3 \alpha}{H^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **famous $\cos^3 \alpha$ law** of flood lighting. It is one of the most-tested formulas in KTU examinations. Notice that as α → 0 (floodlight pointing straight down), the formula reduces to $E = I/H^2$ — the simple inverse-square form.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Floodlights Using the Lumen Method

For a horizontal area of size $A \, (m^2)$ requiring an average illumination $E \, (lux)$:

**Step 1 — Total lumens that must reach the work plane:**

$$\Phi_{\text{required}} = E \times A \quad [\text{lumens}]$$

**Step 2 — However, not all emitted lumens reach the work plane. Two loss factors apply:**

- **Coefficient of Utilization (CU)** — accounts for beam geometry and reflector efficiency.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF)** — accounts for dirt and lamp depreciation.

Therefore, the **lamp lumens** that must be generated are:

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = \frac{E \times A}{CU \times MF}$$

**Step 3 — If each floodlight produces $\Phi_{\text{one}}$ lumens, the number of floodlights is:**

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}}{\Phi_{\text{one}}}$$

The result is **rounded up** to the next whole number, and then **spacing and aiming** are re-verified for uniformity.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1

> A symmetrical floodlight of **40,000 lumens** has a **beam spread of 30°**. It is mounted at a height of **15 m** above the ground, axis vertical. Calculate:
> (a) the diameter of the area illuminated, and
> (b) the illuminance directly below the floodlight.

**Solution:**

**(a) Diameter of illuminated area:**

Using $D = 2H \tan(\beta/2)$:

$$D = 2 \times 15 \times \tan(15°) = 30 \times 0.2679 = 8.04 \text{ m}$$

**(b) Illuminance directly below:**

Step 1 — Solid angle subtended by the beam:

$$\Omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos(15°)) = 2\pi(1 - 0.9659) = 0.2142 \text{ sr}$$

Step 2 — Luminous intensity on the axis:

$$I = \frac{\Phi}{\Omega} = \frac{40{,}000}{0.2142} = 1.867 \times 10^5 \text{ cd}$$

Step 3 — Illuminance at ground level (α = 0°, so $\cos^3 \alpha = 1$):

$$E = \frac{I}{H^2} = \frac{1.867 \times 10^5}{15^2} = \frac{1.867 \times 10^5}{225}$$

$$E \approx 830 \text{ lux}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** A 30° beam from 15 m only covers an 8 m circle. To illuminate a 40 m cricket ground, the designer must either raise H significantly, choose a much wider beam (NEMA 6 or 7), or use **multiple projectors in a high-mast array**. This trade-off between H, β, and patch size is the heart of floodlight design.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 (with Aiming Angle)

> A floodlight with on-axis intensity **200,000 cd** is mounted at **H = 20 m** and tilted at an aiming angle **α = 30°** from vertical. Calculate the illuminance at the point on the ground where the beam axis strikes.

**Solution:**

Using $E = I \cos^3 \alpha / H^2$:

$$E = \frac{200{,}000 \times \cos^3(30°)}{20^2}$$

$$E = \frac{200{,}000 \times (0.8660)^3}{400}$$

$$E = \frac{200{,}000 \times 0.6495}{400}$$

$$E = \frac{1.299 \times 10^5}{400} = 324.8 \text{ lux}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice the dramatic effect of tilting: at α = 0°, this same floodlight would give $E = 200000/400 = 500$ lux. Tilting to 30° reduces it to ~325 lux — a 35% loss. This is why designers use the $\cos^3$ law explicitly when planning projector aiming.

```
        Floodlight (•) at height H
```

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define flood lighting. List any **six** important terms associated with flood lighting.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

**Flood lighting** is a method of artificial illumination in which a large area or object is illuminated by light projected from one or more floodlights (projectors) mounted at a distance from the target, the beam being aimed and controlled to fall on the required surface.

**Six important terms:**

1. **Beam spread (β):** Angular extent of the beam between 10% intensity points.
2. **Mounting height (H):** Vertical distance from work plane to floodlight.
3. **Aiming angle (α):** Angle between floodlight axis and vertical.
4. **Throw distance (T):** Slant distance from projector to target.
5. **Coefficient of Utilization (CU):** Ratio of lumens reaching the work plane to total emitted lumens.
6. **Maintenance Factor (MF):** Factor accounting for lumen depreciation and dirt accumulation.

*(Any other valid term from the master list earns full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*State the $\cos^3 \alpha$ law used in flood lighting and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer:**

The $\cos^3 \alpha$ law states that the illuminance **E** at a point on the ground where the axis of a tilted floodlight strikes, given an on-axis intensity **I** and mounting height **H**, is:

$$E = \frac{I \cos^3 \alpha}{H^2}$$

**Significance:**

- It accounts for **two simultaneous effects** of tilting the floodlight: (i) the increase in slant distance, and (ii) the obliquity of incidence on the horizontal ground.
- When α = 0° (nadir aim), $\cos^3 \alpha = 1$ and the formula reduces to the simple inverse-square form $E = I/H^2$.
- It is essential for **aiming the projector correctly** to place the maximum illuminance on the target without overshooting or undershooting.
- Designers use it to compute uniformity of illumination across the patch.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **NEMA classification of floodlight beam spreads**. List the seven NEMA classes with their angular ranges. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**NEMA Beam Classification — Overview:**

The **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)** classifies floodlight beam patterns by their angular spread, measured between the directions where the **beam intensity falls to 10% of peak** (called the **field angle**). The classification uses two numbers — **H** (horizontal beam spread) and **V** (vertical beam spread) — but for symmetrical floodlights, H = V and a single class number is used.

**Neat Sketch:**

```
              Floodlight
                  │
                  │  ← Beam axis
            ╱─────┼─────╲
           ╱      │      ╲     β = full beam spread
          ╱       │       ╲
         ╱  NEMA  │  NEMA  ╲
        │   class │  class  │
        │_________│_________│
              Ground patch
         (diameter D = 2H tan(β/2))
```

**The Seven NEMA Classes (Beam Spread Range):**

| NEMA Class | Beam Spread (degrees) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **NEMA 1** | 10° – 18° | Very narrow spot — flagpole, tall monument |
| **NEMA 2** | 18° – 29° | Narrow spot — statue, signage |
| **NEMA 3** | 29° – 46° | Medium narrow — small facade |
| **NEMA 4** | 46° – 70° | Medium flood — parking, small field |
| **NEMA 5** | 70° – 100° | Medium wide flood — building wall |
| **NEMA 6** | 100° – 130° | Wide flood — stadium, large yard |
| **NEMA 7** | > 130° | Very wide flood — area lighting |

**Procedure for Using NEMA Classification:**

1. Compute required beam spread from geometry: $\beta = 2 \tan^{-1}(D/2H)$.
2. Select the corresponding NEMA class.
3. Cross-check with manufacturer's photometric data (IES file) to confirm intensity distribution matches the geometry.

---

**(b)** A symmetrical floodlight of **30,000 lumens** with a **beam spread of 40°** is mounted at a height of **12 m**, with its axis tilted at **α = 25°** from vertical. Calculate:
   (i) the on-axis luminous intensity,
   (ii) the illuminance on the ground at the centre of the beam, and
   (iii) the area of the elliptical patch on the ground. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\Phi = 30{,}000$ lm
- $\beta = 40°$
- $H = 12$ m
- $\alpha = 25°$

**(i) On-axis luminous intensity I:**

Solid angle subtended by the beam:

$$\Omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos(20°)) = 2\pi(1 - 0.9397) = 2\pi \times 0.0603 = 0.3789 \text{ sr}$$

Luminous intensity on axis:

$$I = \frac{\Phi}{\Omega} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.3789} = 7.918 \times 10^4 \text{ cd}$$

**(ii) Illuminance at the centre of the beam (using $\cos^3 \alpha$ law):**

$$E = \frac{I \cos^3 \alpha}{H^2} = \frac{7.918 \times 10^4 \times \cos^3(25°)}{12^2}$$

$$\cos(25°) = 0.9063, \quad \cos^3(25°) = 0.7445$$

$$E = \frac{7.918 \times 10^4 \times 0.7445}{144} = \frac{5.895 \times 10^4}{144}$$

$$E \approx 409.4 \text{ lux}$$

**(iii) Area of the elliptical patch:**

For a symmetric beam of half-angle $\beta/2 = 20°$ at slant distance $T = H/\cos \alpha$:

Slant distance:

$$T = \frac{12}{\cos 25°} = \frac{12}{0.9063} = 13.24 \text{ m}$$

Major axis (along the tilt direction) — actually for a tilted symmetric beam, the patch is an **ellipse** with:

- **Minor axis (perpendicular to tilt plane):** $2T \tan(\beta/2) = 2 \times 13.24 \times 0.3640 = 9.64$ m
- **Major axis (in the tilt plane):** $2T \tan(\beta/2)/\cos \alpha = 9.64/0.9063 = 10.64$ m

Area of ellipse:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times a \times b = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10.64 \times 9.64$$

$$A = 0.7854 \times 102.57 = 80.55 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (iii):** A common mistake is to assume the patch is a circle and use $A = \pi R^2$ directly. When the floodlight is **tilted**, the circular beam projects as an **ellipse** on the horizontal ground, with its **major axis stretched** by a factor of $1/\cos \alpha$. Marks are lost if this distortion is ignored. The vertical-axis case (α = 0) is the only one where the patch remains circular.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain, with neat diagrams, the following terms used in flood lighting:
   (i) Beam spread and field angle,
   (ii) Aiming angle and throw distance,
   (iii) Mounting height and cut-off angle. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Beam Spread (β) and Field Angle:**

The **beam spread** is the angle between the two directions in which the **luminous intensity falls to 50% of the peak value** (some standards). The **field angle** is the wider angle where intensity falls to **10% of the peak**. The field angle is always **larger** than the beam spread.

```
     I (cd)
     │      ╱╲
     │     ╱  ╲
     │    ╱    ╲
     │   ╱      ╲
     │──╱────────╲────── I_max
     │ ╱ │      │ ╲
     │╱  │      │  ╲
     └───┴──────┴───→ angle
       β/2 0  β/2
     ← β (beam spread) →
     ← field angle  →
```

**(ii) Aiming Angle (α) and Throw Distance (T):**

- **Aiming angle (α):** Angle between the floodlight's optical axis and the vertical (nadir line). It positions the brightest part of the beam on the intended target.
- **Throw distance (T):** Slant distance from the projector to the centre of the illuminated patch.

$$T = \frac{H}{\cos \alpha}$$

```
        Floodlight
            │╲ α
            │ ╲
            │  ╲
            │   ╲
            │ T  ╲
            │     ╲
            │______╲
              H    Target point
```

**(iii) Mounting Height (H) and Cut-off Angle:**

- **Mounting height (H):** Vertical distance from the plane of the area being illuminated to the centre of the lamp. Higher H → smaller patch, lower glare, better uniformity.
- **Cut-off angle:** The angle from the nadir beyond which the luminaire emits **negligible intensity** (≤ 2.5% of peak). Critical for **dark-sky compliance** and preventing glare to neighbours.

```
         Floodlight
             │
             │ H
             │
        ─────┴─────  Ground
        ╱  │   ╲
   cut-off  │  cut-off
   angle    │  angle
```

Cut-off classifications:

| Class | Intensity at 90° and above |
|---|---|
| Full cut-off | 0% (zero candela above horizontal) |
| Cut-off | ≤ 2.5% of peak |
| Semi cut-off | ≤ 5% of peak |
| Non cut-off | > 5% of peak |

---

**(b)** An open-air theatre of dimensions **80 m × 50 m** is to be illuminated to an average level of **100 lux**. Symmetrical floodlights of **45,000 lumens** each, with beam spread 60°, are to be used. The mounting height is **18 m** and the floodlights are aimed vertically. Assuming a **coefficient of utilization of 0.55** and a **maintenance factor of 0.75**, calculate the **number of floodlights required** and verify that the beam spread covers the required patch size. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total area to be lit:**

$$A = 80 \times 50 = 4000 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total lamp lumens required (lumen method):**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = \frac{E \times A}{CU \times MF} = \frac{100 \times 4000}{0.55 \times 0.75}$$

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = \frac{400{,}000}{0.4125} = 9.697 \times 10^5 \text{ lm} \approx 969{,}697 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 3 — Number of floodlights:**

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}}{\Phi_{\text{one}}} = \frac{969{,}697}{45{,}000} = 21.55$$

Rounding up, **N = 22 floodlights**.

**Step 4 — Verification of beam spread vs. patch size:**

Each floodlight illuminates a circular patch of diameter:

$$D = 2H \tan(\beta/2) = 2 \times 18 \times \tan(30°) = 36 \times 0.5774 = 20.79 \text{ m}$$

If 22 floodlights are arranged in a **regular grid** (e.g., 4 × 6 or 5 × 5 with extras at corners), each unit patch ≈ 20.8 m. The overall 80 m × 50 m stage can be covered with proper overlap (typically 1.2 × patch diameter for uniformity).

**Step 5 — Practical design note:**

In practice, with 22 projectors on a high-mast or perimeter poles, designers would re-check **uniformity ratio** ($E_{\min}/E_{\max}$) and **glare rating (GR)** per IS 1944 / CIE 140.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often forget to **multiply E × A** as the **first step** and then divide by the **product CU × MF** in the denominator. A common error is dividing by CU only (ignoring MF) or by MF only (ignoring CU). The product $CU \times MF$ must appear in the denominator. Also, always round **N upward** — never downward — because under-designing leads to under-lit and non-compliant installations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flood lighting** illuminates a target from a distance using projector-type luminaires.
- **Beam spread (β)** is the angular width of the beam, usually measured to 10% peak intensity (field angle) or 50% peak intensity (beam angle).
- **Mounting height (H)** and **beam spread (β)** jointly determine the **patch size** on the ground: $D = 2H \tan(\beta/2)$.
- **Aiming angle (α)** is measured from the vertical (nadir), and the **$\cos^3 \alpha$ law** governs illuminance on a horizontal surface from a tilted projector:
  $$E = \frac{I \cos^3 \alpha}{H^2}$$
- **Throw distance** $T = H / \cos \alpha$ is the slant distance from the projector to the target.
- **Beam lumens** are the lumens emitted within the beam spread, used in the lumen method.
- **Lumen method** for flood lighting: $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = E \times A / (CU \times MF)$.
- **Number of floodlights** $N = \Phi_{\text{lamp}} / \Phi_{\text{one}}$, rounded **up** to the next integer.
- **NEMA classification** has 7 classes from 1 (narrow spot, 10°–18°) to 7 (very wide flood, > 130°).
- **Coefficient of Utilization (CU)** accounts for beam geometry and reflector efficiency (typical 0.4–0.7 for outdoor).
- **Maintenance Factor (MF)** accounts for lamp depreciation and dirt (typical 0.7–0.8 for outdoor).
- **Cut-off angle** governs glare and light-trespass; **full cut-off** = 0% intensity above horizontal — preferred for dark-sky compliance.
- **Spill light / light trespass** must be controlled to avoid neighbour and legal issues.
- **Glare** is controlled by increasing H, reducing α, choosing full cut-off luminaires, and avoiding direct sight of the lamp.
- **High-mast lighting** uses 20–45 m masts with multiple projectors and a winch-based lowering mechanism.
- **Tilted beam = elliptical patch** on horizontal ground (not circular) — major axis stretched by $1/\cos \alpha$.
- **WLF (Waste Light Factor)** quantifies light that misses the target — efficient designs keep WLF ≤ 0.1.
- For **vertical aim (nadir)**, $\cos^3 \alpha = 1$ and the illuminance reduces to the simple $E = I/H^2$.
- Practical floodlight design is an **iterative process**: geometry → number → aiming → uniformity → glare check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fixtures and Their Suitable Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Fixtures and Their Suitable Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Luminaire (Lighting Fixture):** A complete lighting assembly consisting of one or more lamps, along with the components designed to distribute the light, position and protect the lamps, and connect the lamps to the power supply. The key components include the **lamp (or light source)**, **reflector**, **refractor/lens**, **housing**, **ballast/driver**, and **mounting hardware**.

As per the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES)** standard, a luminaire is the device that channels the light output of one or more lamps, distributing, filtering, or transforming the light emitted. It includes all the necessary parts for supporting, fixing, and protecting the lamps (excluding the lamps themselves) and, where necessary, the circuit auxiliaries together with the means of connecting them to the supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the distinction matters in KTU exams:** Students often confuse "lamp" and "luminaire." The *lamp* is the light source (bulb/tube/LED chip), while the *luminaire* (or fixture) is the entire hardware assembly. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a luminaire like a **water tap and showerhead assembly** in your bathroom:

- The **lamp** is like the water (raw light energy), pouring out in all directions.
- The **reflector** is like the curved mirror inside the showerhead that directs the water flow.
- The **lens/refractor** is like the perforated face of the shower that controls the spray pattern.
- The **housing** is the plastic/steel shell that holds everything together and protects the internals from dust and water.
- The **ballast** is the pressure regulator that maintains a steady, controlled flow.

Just as the same water source can produce a gentle mist, a focused jet, or a rainfall effect depending on the showerhead design, the *same lamp* can produce a focused spot, a wide flood, or a diffused glow depending on the **fixture design**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Standards)

- **Luminous Flux (Φ):** Total light energy emitted per unit time, measured in **lumens (lm)**.
- **Luminous Intensity (I):** Flux per unit solid angle, measured in **candela (cd)**.
- **Illuminance (E):** Flux incident per unit area, measured in **lux (lx) = lm/m²**.
- **Luminance (L):** Brightness of a surface, measured in **cd/m²**.
- **Luminous Efficacy (η):** Lumens per watt (**lm/W**), the efficiency of light production.
- **Utilization Factor (UF) / Coefficient of Utilization (CU):** Ratio of lumens reaching the working plane to total lumens emitted by the lamps.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** Ratio of illuminance after a period of use to the initial illuminance.
- **Space-to-Mounting Height Ratio (SHR):** Maximum spacing between fixtures divided by mounting height.
- **Beam Angle:** The angle between the two directions where the intensity drops to 50% of peak (full beam width at half maximum).
- **Field Angle:** The angle where intensity drops to 10% of peak.
- **Cut-off Angle:** The angle from the nadir (straight down) at which the lamp or its image is no longer visible.

### 1.4 Standard Body References

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classification of luminaires in modern practice follows **IES RP-1** (now **ANSI/IES RP-1-22**) for office lighting, **IS 3646 (Part I)** for industrial interiors, and **IEC 60598** for luminaire safety. These are the de facto reference standards in KTU-recommended textbooks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Luminaires (IES System)

The Illuminating Engineering Society classifies luminaires based on the **percentage of total emitted luminous flux distributed above and below the horizontal plane** through the luminaire.

| Class | Flux Above Horizontal (%) | Flux Below Horizontal (%) | Optical Character |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | 0 – 10 | 90 – 100 | Almost all light downward; most efficient |
| **Semi-direct** | 10 – 40 | 60 – 90 | Mostly downward, some upward |
| **General Diffuse** | 40 – 60 | 40 – 60 | Equal up and down; uniform |
| **Semi-indirect** | 60 – 90 | 10 – 40 | Mostly upward, some downward |
| **Indirect** | 90 – 100 | 0 – 10 | Almost all light upward; reflected off ceiling |

### 2.2 Classification by Construction/Mounting Type

**A. Recessed (Concealed) Luminaires**
- Mounted into a ceiling cavity so only the aperture/lens is visible.
- Examples: **Downlights, troffers, recessed spotlights**.
- Aesthetic appeal, glare-controlled, common in offices, malls, hotels.

**B. Surface-Mounted Luminaires**
- Mounted directly against the ceiling or wall.
- Examples: **Surface fluorescent, bulkheads, ceiling-mounted LED panels**.
- Used where ceiling cavity is unavailable or shallow.

**C. Suspended (Pendant) Luminaires**
- Hung from the ceiling by a stem, chain, or cable.
- Examples: **Pendant downlights, industrial pendants, decorative pendants**.
- Used where mounting height is high and direct downward light is needed.

**D. Track-Mounted Luminaires**
- Multiple luminaires attached to a linear electrified track.
- Examples: **Track spots, track floods**.
- Used in retail displays, galleries, accent lighting.

**E. Portable Luminaires**
- Free-standing, plug-in units.
- Examples: **Table lamps, floor lamps, task lamps**.
- Used in residential, hospitality.

**F. Pole-Mounted Outdoor Luminaires**
- Mounted on poles of various heights.
- Examples: **Street lights, post-top lanterns, floodlight towers**.
- Used in roads, parking, sports, monuments.

### 2.3 Classification by Application Sector

| Sector | Typical Luminaire Types | Governing Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial** | Highbay, Lowbay, Wellglass, Floodlight, Explosion-proof | High luminous flux, IP rating, glare control |
| **Commercial (Offices)** | Troffer, Recessed panel, Linear pendant, Downlight | UGR < 19, 500–750 lux, low glare |
| **Retail** | Track spot, Wall washer, Recessed adjustable, Accent | High CRI (≥ 80), accent capability |
| **Educational** | Troffer, Linear pendant, Blackboard light | Glare-free, uniform, flicker-free |
| **Healthcare** | Recessed sealed, Cleanroom luminaire, Bedhead light | IP 54+, easy clean, low IR/UV |
| **Hospitality** | Decorative pendant, Cove light, Wall sconce | Warm CCT (2700–3000 K), high CRI |
| **Outdoor/Road** | Street light, Tunnel light, Bollard | IP 65+, IK 08+, optical cutoff |
| **Sports** | Floodlight, Asymmetric flood | High flux, asymmetric distribution, low glare for camera |
| **Residential** | Downlight, Chandelier, Batten, Decorative wall | Aesthetic, dimmable, energy-efficient |

### 2.4 Key Reflector Types Inside Luminaires

1. **Specular (Mirror) Reflectors** – Polished/anodized aluminum; produce sharp, controlled beams. Used in spotlights and downlights.
2. **Semi-Specular Reflectors** – Slightly etched aluminum; produce medium-spread beams. Used in commercial recessed.
3. **Diffuse (Matte) Reflectors** – White painted or coated; produce soft, broad distribution. Used in industrial and office.
4. **Multi-faceted / Faceted Reflectors** – Made of many small mirrored facets; produce precise beam shaping. Used in floodlights, automotive headlights.
5. **Parabolic Reflectors** – Parabolic-curve reflector for collimated parallel beams. Used in downlights with louver (parabolic louver fixture).
6. **Baffle Reflectors** – Stepped concentric rings to cut off glare. Used in accent lighting.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous flux | $\Phi = \int I \, d\omega$ | lm (lumen) | $d\omega$ = solid angle in steradian |
| Illuminance (point source) | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | lx (lux) | **Inverse Square Law** with cosine correction |
| Illuminance (cosine cube) | $E_{h} = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ | lx | For a horizontal working plane, $h$ = vertical height |
| Average illuminance | $E_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times UF \times MF}{A}$ | lx | $N$ = number of lamps/luminaires |
| Number of luminaires | $N = \dfrac{E_{avg} \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF}$ | — | Lumen method design equation |
| Luminous efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | lm/W | $P$ = electrical power in watts |
| Utilization factor | $UF = \dfrac{\Phi_{work}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | dimensionless $\vert 0-1 \vert$ | Room index dependent |
| Room index | $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_{m}(L+W)}$ | dimensionless | $L$ = length, $W$ = width, $H_m$ = mounting height above work plane |
| Spacing / mounting height | $SHR = \dfrac{\text{Spacing}}{\text{Mounting height}}$ | dimensionless | Typical 1.0 to 1.5 for indoor |
| Solid angle of a cone | $\omega = 2\pi(1-\cos\theta)$ | sr | Half-angle $\theta$ of the cone |
| Luminance | $L = \dfrac{I}{A_{p}\cos\alpha}$ | cd/m² | $\alpha$ is angle from normal to projected area $A_p$ |
| Maintenance factor | $MF = LLMF \times LSF \times LMF \times RSMF$ | dimensionless | Product of all individual factors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formula for KTU problems:** The *lumen method* equation
> $$E_{avg} = \frac{N \times \Phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$$
> appears in nearly every illumination design problem worth 7–14 marks. Memorize the units and the meaning of every symbol.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lighting designers** use luminaire photometric data (IES files) in software like **DIALux, Relux, AGi32** to predict illumination levels before installation.
- **Energy auditors** calculate efficacy of installed fixtures to suggest retrofits (e.g., replacing HID with LED).
- **Standards compliance** (e.g., ECBC in India, BREEAM in UK) mandates minimum efficacy and maximum power density (W/m²) per 100 lux, both of which depend on fixture selection.
- **Industrial safety** requires IP-rated and explosion-proof fixtures in hazardous areas (oil refineries, chemical plants, mines).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law of Illuminance

**Statement:** For a point source radiating uniformly in all directions, the illuminance on a surface is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source.

**Step 1 — Define a point source:** A point source emits total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions, i.e., with constant luminous intensity $I$ in every direction.

**Step 2 — Luminous intensity in terms of total flux:**
By definition, total flux equals intensity multiplied by the total solid angle ($4\pi$ steradians around a point):

$$\Phi = I \times 4\pi$$

**Step 3 — Flux passing through a sphere of radius $r$:**
Since the source is isotropic, the same total flux $\Phi$ must pass through every sphere centered on the source. The surface area of such a sphere is $4\pi r^{2}$.

**Step 4 — Flux density (illuminance) on the sphere:**
Illuminance is flux per unit area:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Phi = 4\pi I$:**
$$E = \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $4\pi$:**

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I}{r^{2}}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply Lambert's cosine correction for oblique incidence:**
If the surface is not perpendicular to the incident ray, the projected area increases by a factor of $1/\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the incident ray and the surface normal:

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{r^{2}}$$

This is the **fundamental photometric equation** used in nearly all point-source calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the inverse square?:** Energy conservation forces the same total flux to spread over an area that grows as $r^{2}$, so the flux density (illuminance) falls as $1/r^{2}$. This is the same reason a flame's heat diminishes with distance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cosine-Cube Law

**Statement:** When a point source is mounted at a height $h$ above a horizontal working plane, the illuminance on the plane at a horizontal distance $d$ from the foot of the source is given by $E_{h} = I \cos^{3}\theta / h^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Geometry setup:**
Let the source be at height $h$ above a horizontal plane. A point $P$ on the plane is at horizontal distance $d$ from the foot. The slant distance from source to $P$ is $r$, and the angle between the incident ray and the vertical (normal to the plane) is $\theta$.

**Step 2 — Express $r$ in terms of $h$ and $\theta$:**
By trigonometry in the right triangle:
$$r = \frac{h}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\cos\theta$ in terms of $h$ and $d$:**
The slant distance satisfies $r^{2} = h^{2} + d^{2}$, and from the triangle:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{h}{r} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{h^{2} + d^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Start with the fundamental photometric equation:**
$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{r^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $r = h/\cos\theta$, hence $r^{2} = h^{2}/\cos^{2}\theta$:**

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{h^{2}/\cos^{2}\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the fraction:**

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta \cdot \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Collect powers of $\cos\theta$:**

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}}$$

**Step 8 — Consequence for design:** Illuminance on a horizontal plane falls off rapidly as one moves away from directly below the source, scaling with $\cos^{3}\theta$. This is why lighting designers space fixtures strategically to overlap their footprints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At $\theta = 0$ (directly below the source), $\cos^{3}(0) = 1$, so $E = I/h^{2}$, matching the inverse square law. At $\theta = 60°$, $\cos^{3}(60°) = (0.5)^{3} = 0.125$, so illuminance drops to one-eighth of the value directly below — explaining why a single fixture cannot illuminate a large area uniformly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Lumen Method (Lumen Design Equation)

**Step 1 — Total flux emitted by all luminaires:**
If there are $N$ luminaires, each emitting $\Phi$ lumens (from the lamps inside), total emitted flux is $N \Phi$.

**Step 2 — Flux reaching the working plane:**
Only a fraction reaches the working plane. This fraction is the **utilization factor (UF)**, which depends on the luminaire distribution, room geometry, and surface reflectances:

$$\Phi_{work} = N \times \Phi \times UF$$

**Step 3 — Account for depreciation over time:**
The light output of lamps and the reflectance of room surfaces degrade with time due to dust, aging, etc. The **maintenance factor (MF)** accounts for this:

$$\Phi_{effective} = N \times \Phi \times UF \times MF$$

**Step 4 — Average illuminance on the working plane:**
Dividing by the area $A$ of the working plane:

$$E_{avg} = \frac{N \times \Phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for the number of luminaires:**

$$\boxed{N = \frac{E_{avg} \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
