> This is the **workhorse equation** for KTU 14-mark design problems. Always state: (i) the given data, (ii) the formula, (iii) the substitution, (iv) the final rounded-up integer, and (v) the layout verification using SHR.

---

### 3.4 Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio (SHR) — Analytical Reasoning

**Definition:** $SHR = S / H_{m}$, where $S$ is the maximum center-to-center spacing of luminaires and $H_{m}$ is the mounting height above the working plane.

**Why it matters:** The SHR is a manufacturer-specified property of each luminaire (found on its photometric data sheet). If you space luminaires farther apart than the recommended SHR, the floor will show dark patches between them. If you space them closer, you waste energy and create non-uniform brightness.

**For example:** A typical LED panel with a wide distribution has $SHR = 1.5$. For a mounting height of 3 m, the maximum spacing is $S = 1.5 \times 3 = 4.5$ m.

**Layout check for a rectangular room:**
- Number of rows $N_{r} = \dfrac{L}{S} + 1$ (rounded to nearest integer)
- Number of luminaires per row $N_{c} = \dfrac{W}{S} + 1$
- Total $N = N_{r} \times N_{c}$

This must equal (or be very close to) the value from the lumen method. If not, iterate on the lamp type or mounting height.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Luminaire Component Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Ballast or LED Driver]
    B --> C[Lamp or LED Module]
    C --> D[Luminous Flux Generation]
    D --> E[Reflector - Directs Light]
    E --> F[Refractor or Diffuser - Shapes Beam]
    F --> G[Working Plane Illuminance E]
    H[Housing Enclosure] -.protects.-> B
    H -.protects.-> C
    H -.protects.-> E
    I[Mounting Hardware] -.supports.-> H
    J[Heat Sink] -.cools.-> C
    G --> K[Visual Task or Space]
```

### 4.2 IES Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Luminaire Classification by Light Distribution] --> B{Percent flux above horizontal?}
    B -->|0 to 10 percent| C[DIRECT]
    B -->|10 to 40 percent| D[SEMI DIRECT]
    B -->|40 to 60 percent| E[GENERAL DIFFUSE]
    B -->|60 to 90 percent| F[SEMI INDIRECT]
    B -->|90 to 100 percent| G[INDIRECT]
    C --> C1[Examples: Downlight, Highbay, Troffer]
    D --> D1[Examples: Surface fluorescent with upward slot]
    E --> E1[Examples: Opal globe, Bare lamp in reflector]
    F --> F1[Examples: Up light with downward lens]
    G --> G1[Examples: Cove light, Indirect pendant]
```

### 4.3 Application-to-Fixture Mapping Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Industrial_Zone
        IND1[Highbay LED 150W]
        IND2[Wellglass IP65]
        IND3[Floodlight 400W]
        IND4[Explosion Proof Ex d]
    end
    subgraph Commercial_Zone
        COM1[Recessed Troffer 2x2]
        COM2[Linear Pendant 40W]
        COM3[Downlight 18W]
        COM4[Track Spot 30W]
    end
    subgraph Outdoor_Zone
        OUT1[Street Light 150W IP66]
        OUT2[Bollard 12W]
        OUT3[Wall Washer 24W]
        OUT4[Tunnel Light 200W]
    end
    subgraph Residential_Zone
        RES1[Chandelier E14]
        RES2[Batten 20W]
        RES3[Cabinet Light 5W]
        RES4[Mirror Light 12W]
    end
    A1[Factory Hall] --> IND1
    A1 --> IND2
    A2[Petrol Pump] --> IND4
    A3[Sports Stadium] --> IND3
    B1[Office Space] --> COM1
    B1 --> COM2
    B2[Retail Showroom] --> COM4
    B2 --> COM3
    C1[City Road] --> OUT1
    C2[Garden Path] --> OUT2
    C3[Building Facade] --> OUT3
    C4[Highway Tunnel] --> OUT4
    D1[Living Room] --> RES1
    D2[Kitchen] --> RES2
    D3[Study Desk] --> RES3
    D4[Bathroom] --> RES4
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern LED Luminaire

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Power_Chain
        P1[Mains Input 230V AC] --> P2[Surge Protector]
        P2 --> P3[Driver PFC and Constant Current]
        P3 --> P4[LED Array 36V DC]
    end
    subgraph Optical_Chain
        O1[LED Array] --> O2[Primary Lens per LED]
        O2 --> O3[Secondary Reflector or TIR Lens]
        O3 --> O4[Diffuser or Anti Glare Baffle]
        O4 --> O5[Beam Output]
    end
    subgraph Thermal_Chain
        T1[LED Array] --> T2[MCPCB Heat Spreader]
        T2 --> T3[Aluminium Heat Sink]
        T3 --> T4[Convective Air Cooling]
    end
    subgraph Control_Chain
        C1[Sensor Input DALI or 0-10V] --> C2[Driver Control Circuit]
        C2 --> P3
    end
    P4 -.drives.-> O1
    T1 -.feeds.-> O2
    C1 -.feedback.-> O5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PEEET418]`
> **Define a luminaire. How does the IES classify luminaires based on light distribution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **luminaire** is a complete lighting assembly that includes the lamp(s), reflectors, refractors, ballasts/drivers, housing, and mounting accessories, designed to distribute, filter, and direct light from the source.

The **IES (Illuminating Engineering Society)** classifies luminaires into five categories based on the **percentage of total emitted luminous flux above and below the horizontal plane**:

| IES Class | % Flux Above | % Flux Below |
|---|---|---|
| Direct | 0 – 10 | 90 – 100 |
| Semi-direct | 10 – 40 | 60 – 90 |
| General Diffuse | 40 – 60 | 40 – 60 |
| Semi-indirect | 60 – 90 | 10 – 40 |
| Indirect | 90 – 100 | 0 – 10 |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for classification table.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PEEET418]`
> **What is the Utilization Factor (UF)? State the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Utilization Factor (UF)** is defined as the ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps.

$$UF = \frac{\Phi_{received\,on\,working\,plane}}{\Phi_{emitted\,by\,lamps}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** number between 0 and 1, and depends on:
1. The **light distribution** of the luminaire (direct, indirect, etc.).
2. The **room index** $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_{m}(L+W)}$, which captures the room's proportions.
3. The **reflectances** of the ceiling, walls, and floor.

Higher UF values indicate more efficient delivery of light to the working plane, with **direct luminaires** typically having UF = 0.6 – 0.9 and **indirect luminaires** having UF = 0.3 – 0.5.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for dependence factors.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEET418]`

**(a)** Classify the different types of lighting fixtures used for **industrial applications**. Explain the construction and use of any **two** in detail with suitable sketches.

**(b)** A workshop of size **50 m × 20 m** is to be illuminated to an average illuminance of **250 lux**. The mounting height of the luminaires above the working plane is **6 m**, and the desired space-to-mounting-height ratio is **1.2**. Using metal halide lamps of **400 W** with a luminous efficacy of **80 lm/W**, utilization factor of **0.55**, and maintenance factor of **0.7**, design the lighting installation.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) [7 marks] — Industrial Lighting Fixtures:**

Industrial lighting fixtures are designed to withstand harsh conditions (dust, moisture, vibration, chemicals) and deliver high flux efficiently to large spaces.

**Classification:**

1. **Highbay Luminaires**
   - Used for mounting heights **6 m and above**.
   - Produce a **narrow or medium distribution** to direct light downward onto the work area.
   - Common lamp sources: **Metal Halide (MH), High-Pressure Sodium (HPS), High-bay LED** (100 W – 400 W).
   - Housing: **die-cast aluminium with finned heat sink**, IP 54 or higher.
   - Reflector: **Aluminium reflector, prismatic glass or PC refractor**.
   - **Applications:** Assembly halls, warehouses, foundries, large machine shops.

2. **Lowbay Luminaires**
   - Used for mounting heights **below 6 m**.
   - Provide **wider distribution** than highbays.
   - **Applications:** Smaller workshops, garages, packaging areas.

3. **Wellglass Fittings**
   - **Heavy-duty enclosed luminaire** with a thick tempered glass cover ("well glass").
   - Sealed against dust and moisture; typically **IP 65**.
   - **Applications:** Chemical plants, cement industries, outdoor industrial yards.

4. **Floodlight Luminaires**
   - **Asymmetric or symmetric** narrow-beam projectors.
   - **Applications:** Sports stadiums, dockyards, construction sites, large outdoor industrial areas.

5. **Explosion-Proof (Ex-proof) Luminaires**
   - **Flameproof enclosures** (Ex d) that contain any internal explosion.
   - **Applications:** Oil refineries, gas plants, paint shops, mines.

6. **Vapor-Tight / Dust-Tight Luminaires**
   - Sealed gasketed housing with polycarbonate or acrylic diffuser.
   - **Applications:** Food processing, pharmaceutical, car washes.

*(Sketch a labelled diagram of a Highbay luminaire showing the lamp, reflector, heat sink, and mounting hook for full marks.)*

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Design:**

**Step 1 — Compute the total lumens per lamp:**
Lamp wattage = 400 W
Luminous efficacy = 80 lm/W
$$\Phi = P \times \eta = 400 \times 80 = 32{,}000 \text{ lumens per lamp}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the lumen method to find the number of luminaires:**

Given:
- $E_{avg} = 250$ lux
- $A = 50 \times 20 = 1000$ m²
- $UF = 0.55$, $MF = 0.7$
- $\Phi_{lamp} = 32{,}000$ lm

$$N = \frac{E_{avg} \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF} = \frac{250 \times 1000}{32{,}000 \times 0.55 \times 0.7}$$

Compute the denominator:
$$32{,}000 \times 0.55 = 17{,}600$$
$$17{,}600 \times 0.7 = 12{,}320 \text{ lm effective per luminaire}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$250 \times 1000 = 250{,}000 \text{ lumens required}$$

$$N = \frac{250{,}000}{12{,}320} = 20.29$$

Round up to the next whole number: **N = 21 luminaires**.

**Step 3 — Layout using SHR:**
- Given $SHR = 1.2$ and $H_{m} = 6$ m
- Maximum spacing: $S = SHR \times H_{m} = 1.2 \times 6 = 7.2$ m

**Step 4 — Number of rows along the 50 m direction:**
$$N_{rows} = \frac{50}{7.2} = 6.94 \rightarrow \text{round to } 7 \text{ rows}$$

**Step 5 — Number of luminaires per row along the 20 m direction:**
$$N_{per\,row} = \frac{N_{total}}{N_{rows}} = \frac{21}{7} = 3$$

**Step 6 — Verify actual spacing:**
- Along 50 m: $S_{1} = 50 / 7 = 7.14$ m (≤ 7.2 m, acceptable)
- Along 20 m: $S_{2} = 20 / 3 = 6.67$ m (≤ 7.2 m, acceptable)

**Final Design:** 21 metal halide 400 W luminaires arranged in **7 rows × 3 luminaires per row** at 6 m mounting height.

*(Mark distribution: 1 for $\Phi$, 2 for $N$ formula and substitution, 1 for $N$ value, 1 for SHR, 1 for layout, 1 for verification.)*

---

> **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEET418 - Alternative]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the different **types of reflectors** used in lighting fixtures and their effect on light distribution.

**(b)** A drawing hall of size **20 m × 10 m × 4 m** (ceiling height) requires an illuminance of **750 lux** on the working plane (0.85 m above the floor). Using fluorescent tube lights of **40 W** (luminous flux **3000 lm** per tube) with utilization factor of **0.5** and maintenance factor of **0.8**, calculate the number of tubes required. Each luminaire is fitted with 2 tubes. Also find the total connected load.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) [7 marks] — Types of Reflectors:**

A reflector is the most important optical element inside a luminaire. It controls the direction, spread, and intensity of the light beam.

**1. Specular (Mirror) Reflectors:**
- Made of **polished or anodized aluminium** with mirror-like surface.
- Produces a **sharp, narrow, controlled beam** with minimal diffusion.
- **Effect:** High intensity in the central beam; sharp cutoff at the edges.
- **Use:** Spotlights, downlights, automotive headlights, projector lamps.

**2. Semi-Specular (Semi-Mirror) Reflectors:**
- Slightly **etched or sand-blasted aluminium**.
- Produces a **medium-spread beam** with softer edges than a pure specular.
- **Use:** Commercial recessed downlights, office troffers.

**3. Diffuse (Matte) Reflectors:**
- Coated with **white paint or enamel**; rough surface scatters light in all directions.
- Produces a **soft, broad, uniform distribution** with no visible beam.
- **Use:** Industrial highbays, bare fluorescent ceiling fixtures, museum lighting.

**4. Parabolic Reflectors:**
- **Parabolic-curve surface**; light from a source at the focus emerges as **parallel rays**.
- Produces a **collimated beam** with very sharp cutoff.
- Combined with **parabolic louver** to cut glare in offices.
- **Use:** High-efficiency office luminaires, theatre spotlights, searchlights.

**5. Multi-Faceted Reflectors:**
- Composed of many **small flat mirrored facets** at calculated angles.
- Each facet directs light to a specific zone of the beam, producing a **precisely shaped distribution** (round, oval, rectangular).
- **Use:** Floodlights, automotive headlamps, signal lamps, stadium lights.

**6. Baffled Reflectors:**
- Have **concentric stepped rings (baffles)** painted matte black.
- Baffles absorb stray light, **cut off glare**, and shape the cutoff angle.
- **Use:** Accent lights, museum picture lights, theatre work lights.

*(Include a labelled sketch of a parabolic reflector with louver and a multi-faceted reflector for full marks.)*

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical:**

**Step 1 — Compute the area of the working plane:**
$$A = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mounting height:**
$$H_{m} = 4 - 0.85 = 3.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — State the data:**
- $E = 750$ lux
- $A = 200$ m²
- $UF = 0.5$, $MF = 0.8$
- $\Phi_{tube} = 3000$ lm per tube

**Step 4 — Compute the number of tubes using the lumen method:**
$$N_{tubes} = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF}$$

$$N_{tubes} = \frac{750 \times 200}{3000 \times 0.5 \times 0.8}$$

Compute denominator:
$$3000 \times 0.5 = 1500$$
$$1500 \times 0.8 = 1200 \text{ effective lumens per tube}$$

Compute numerator:
$$750 \times 200 = 150{,}000 \text{ lumens required}$$

$$N_{tubes} = \frac{150{,}000}{1200} = 125 \text{ tubes}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the number of luminaires (each with 2 tubes):**
$$N_{luminaires} = \frac{125}{2} = 62.5 \rightarrow 63 \text{ luminaires (round up)}$$

This means **63 luminaires × 2 tubes = 126 tubes** (use 63, even though the math gives 62.5, we round up to avoid under-lighting).

**Step 6 — Compute the total connected load:**
$$P_{total} = N_{luminaires} \times 2 \times 40 = 63 \times 80 = 5040 \text{ W} = 5.04 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Verify uniformity using SHR:**
For a fluorescent troffer, $SHR \approx 1.5$. Maximum spacing:
$$S = 1.5 \times 3.15 = 4.725 \text{ m}$$
Along 20 m: $20 / 4.725 = 4.23 \rightarrow 5$ rows
Along 10 m: $10 / 4.725 = 2.12 \rightarrow 3$ per row
Total: $5 \times 3 = 15$ luminaires per row? — Recheck: 63 luminaires spread over 200 m². 9 luminaires per row × 7 rows = 63. Spacing along 20 m = 20/9 ≈ 2.22 m; along 10 m = 10/7 ≈ 1.43 m. Both well within 4.725 m, so the design is conservative but valid.

**Final Answer:** **63 luminaires** with **126 tubes**, total connected load **5.04 kW**.

*(Mark distribution: 1 for area, 1 for height, 1 for substitution, 1 for $N_{tubes}$, 1 for $N_{luminaires}$, 1 for total load, 1 for verification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Writing $E$ in lumens instead of lux, or $\Phi$ in watts instead of lumens. KTU examiners explicitly check units.
> 2. **Rounding error:** Always **round up** the number of luminaires, never round down. Under-lighting fails the design.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract working-plane height:** Mounting height $H_m$ = ceiling height − working plane height. Many students use the full ceiling height and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing $N$ (lamps) and $N$ (luminaires):** The lumen method formula gives the number of *lamps*; you must divide by tubes-per-luminaire to get the number of luminaires.
> 5. **Skipping the SHR verification:** A design without a layout check is considered incomplete in 14-mark answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **luminaire** = lamp + reflector + refractor + housing + ballast/driver + mounting hardware.
- The **lamp** is the source; the **luminaire** is the whole fixture. (Frequent KTU question.)
- **IES classifies luminaires** into 5 types — Direct, Semi-direct, General Diffuse, Semi-indirect, Indirect — based on % flux above and below the horizontal.
- **Key photometric laws:** Inverse Square Law $E = I/r^2$ and Cosine Cube Law $E = I\cos^{3}\theta/h^{2}$.
- **Master equation:** $E_{avg} = (N \times \Phi \times UF \times MF) / A$ — used in 80% of design problems.
- **Room index** $K = (L \times W) / [H_m (L + W)]$; UF increases with $K$ for most luminaire types.
- **SHR (Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio)** is a property of the luminaire, NOT of the room. Use it to verify layout.
- **Reflector types:** Specular, Semi-specular, Diffuse, Parabolic, Multi-faceted, Baffled — each suits a different application.
- **Industrial fixtures:** Highbay (≥ 6 m), Lowbay (< 6 m), Wellglass (IP 65), Floodlight, Explosion-proof.
- **Commercial fixtures:** Troffer (recessed 2×2 ft or 600×600 mm), Linear pendant, Downlight, Track spot.
- **Outdoor fixtures:** Street light, Bollard, Wall washer, Tunnel light — all require IP 65+ and IK 08+ ratings.
- **Maintenance Factor** = LLMF × LSF × LMF × RSMF (Lamp Lumen, Lamp Survival, Luminaire, Room Surface Maintenance Factors).
- **For LED retrofits**, efficacy > 100 lm/W is now standard; design accordingly.
- **Always** state units, show formula, substitute, compute, round up, and verify with SHR.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** using ceiling height instead of mounting height above working plane in $H_m$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Lamp and Projector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Lamp and Projector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lamp selection** in illumination engineering is the systematic process of choosing the most appropriate artificial light source from the available family of electrical lamps (incandescent, fluorescent, HID, LED) based on quantitative photometric parameters (luminous flux, luminous efficacy, luminance), qualitative lighting parameters (colour rendering index, correlated colour temperature), economic parameters (initial cost, life span, energy cost), and application-specific constraints (interior/exterior, industrial/commercial/residential, ambient/task/decorative).

**Projector selection** is the engineering process of choosing an appropriate directional light fitting (luminaire with reflector/refractor optics) such that the required illumination level, uniformity ratio, glare control, and beam distribution are achieved at the working plane for a given mounting height and area to be illuminated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to justify the selection of a particular lamp or projector type for a given application by comparing at least three numerical/qualitative parameters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of selecting a lamp and projector like selecting a **paintbrush and paint colour** for an artist:

- The **lamp** is like the **paint colour** — it determines the *quality, tone, and warmth* of what you see (warm yellow of a tungsten bulb versus cool daylight of an LED).
- The **projector (luminaire)** is like the **paintbrush** — it determines *how* the light is spread onto the canvas (focused spotlight versus wide floodlight).
- The **mounting height and area** of the room is the **canvas size** — a larger canvas needs broader, more powerful brushes and richer paint.

Just as a master artist carefully matches brush to canvas, a lighting engineer must match the lamp-source and luminaire-optics to the application to achieve optimal visual comfort, energy efficiency, and cost-effectiveness.

### 1.3 Standard Photometric & Electrical Metrics

The following metrics govern every selection decision:

- **Luminous Flux (Φ)** — measured in **lumens (lm)**; total light energy radiated per second as perceived by the human eye.
- **Luminous Intensity (I)** — measured in **candela (cd)**; flux emitted per unit solid angle.
- **Luminous Efficacy (η)** — measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)**; indicates energy efficiency of converting electrical power to light.
- **Luminance (L)** — measured in **candela per square metre (cd/m²)**; brightness of a surface.
- **Illuminance (E)** — measured in **lux (lx) or lm/m²**; light falling on a surface.
- **Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT)** — measured in **Kelvin (K)**; warmth/coolness appearance.
- **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)** — dimensionless 0–100 scale; accuracy of colour reproduction.
- **Rated Life** — measured in **hours (h)**; useful economic-life indicator.
- **Beam Angle** — measured in **degrees (°)**; spread of the light beam from a projector.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Standard CIE Photopic Curve V(λ)** peaks at **555 nm** (green-yellow) where the human eye is most sensitive. This is why efficacy values are weighted by V(λ), not raw radiometric watts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Lamps Used in Selection

Lamps are classified by their underlying light-generation mechanism:

1. **Incandescent / Tungsten Halogen Lamps**
   - Mechanism: Resistive heating of tungsten filament to ~2500–3000 K.
   - Efficacy: 10–25 lm/W (very low).
   - CRI: 100 (excellent).
   - CCT: 2700–3200 K (warm).
   - Life: 1000–2000 h.
   - Use: Domestic, decorative, studio lighting.

2. **Fluorescent Lamps (FL)**
   - Mechanism: Mercury-vapour discharge + phosphor coating converts UV to visible light.
   - Efficacy: 50–100 lm/W.
   - CRI: 50–95 (depending on phosphor).
   - CCT: 2700–6500 K.
   - Life: 10,000–20,000 h.
   - Use: Offices, commercial halls, schools.

3. **Compact Fluorescent Lamps (CFL)**
   - Mechanism: Folded fluorescent tube with integrated ballast.
   - Efficacy: 50–70 lm/W.
   - Life: 8,000–10,000 h.
   - Use: Replacement of incandescent in domestic circuits.

4. **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps**
   - **Mercury Vapour (MV)**: 40–60 lm/W, CRI ~50, life 12,000 h, bluish-white.
   - **Sodium Vapour — Low Pressure (LPSV)**: 100–200 lm/W, CRI ~25, life 18,000 h, monochromatic yellow.
   - **Sodium Vapour — High Pressure (HPSV)**: 80–140 lm/W, CRI ~25–60, life 24,000 h, golden white.
   - **Metal Halide (MH)**: 80–110 lm/W, CRI ~65–90, life 10,000–15,000 h, excellent white light.

5. **Light Emitting Diodes (LED)**
   - Mechanism: Electroluminescence in semiconductor p-n junction.
   - Efficacy: 80–200+ lm/W.
   - CRI: 70–98.
   - CCT: 2200–6500 K (selectable).
   - Life: 25,000–50,000 h.
   - Use: Universal — replacing nearly every legacy source.

### 2.2 Lamp Selection Decision Logic

The selection follows this priority sequence:

- **Step 1 — Identify Application Category**: Industrial, commercial, road, residential, theatre, hospital, etc.
- **Step 2 — Identify Required CCT & CRI**: Hospitals (CRI > 90, CCT ~4000 K); roads (CRI > 60, CCT 3000–4000 K); textile industry (CRI > 90).
- **Step 3 — Identify Efficacy Threshold**: Energy-conscious projects demand > 80 lm/W.
- **Step 4 — Identify Rated Life & Maintenance Cycle**: Long-life sources preferred in inaccessible fittings.
- **Step 5 — Cost-Benefit Analysis**: Lifecycle cost = (Lamp cost × Number over life) + (Energy cost) + (Maintenance labour).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Mean illumination on a surface | lx (lumens/m²) |
| $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Luminous efficacy | lm/W |
| $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Inverse-square law with cos θ obliquity | lx |
| $\Phi_{total} = n \times \Phi_{lamp}$ | Total flux from n lamps | lm |
| $n = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF}$ | Number of lamps required | dimensionless |
| $MF = \dfrac{E_{maintained}}{E_{initial}}$ | Maintenance factor (0.5–0.8 typical) | dimensionless |
| $UF = \dfrac{\Phi_{received\,on\,working\,plane}}{\Phi_{emitted\,by\,lamp}}$ | Utilisation factor (depends on room index) | dimensionless |
| $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m (L + W)}$ | Room Index | dimensionless |
| $BE = \dfrac{\Phi_{beam}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | Beam efficiency of projector | dimensionless |
| $I_{max} = \dfrac{E \times d^2}{\cos^3\theta}$ | Maximum candlepower for projector | cd |
| $E_h = \dfrac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$ | Horizontal illumination from projector | lx |
| $SP = \dfrac{1}{UF \times MF}$ | Spacing/Height ratio inverse | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** The *maintained illuminance* (after MF is applied) is what design calculations use — **not the initial illuminance**. Examiners often mark this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lamp and projector selection directly influences:
- **Energy Star / ECBC compliance ratings** of buildings.
- **ISHRAE & IESNA lighting design standards**.
- **Industrial safety** — proper CRI avoids misreading colour-coded wires or hazard signs.
- **Retail psychology** — warm CCT enhances fabric appeal; cool CCT enhances electronics displays.
- **Road safety** — HPSV reduces accident rates on fog-prone highways due to better penetration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Lamps Required

**Given:** Required maintained illuminance $E$ (lx), area $A$ (m²), flux per lamp $\Phi$ (lm), utilisation factor $UF$, maintenance factor $MF$.

**Logic Flow:**

We require that the total flux reaching the working plane equals the flux needed to produce illuminance $E$ over area $A$.

$$
\Phi_{required} = E \times A
$$

But only a fraction of the total lamp flux reaches the working plane. That fraction is the product of $UF$ (geometry) and $MF$ (depreciation).

$$
\Phi_{received} = n \times \Phi \times UF \times MF
$$

Setting required = received and solving for $n$:

$$
n \times \Phi \times UF \times MF = E \times A
$$

$$
n = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF}
$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** used in virtually every interior lighting design problem.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A factory hall of dimensions $30 \text{ m} \times 20 \text{ m}$ requires a maintained illuminance of **500 lx**. Selected lamps: Metal Halide, each producing **30,000 lm**, mounted in luminaires with $UF = 0.6$ and $MF = 0.75$. Find (a) total flux required, (b) number of lamps, (c) total wattage if lamp rating is 400 W.

**Step 1 — Total flux required on working plane**

$$
\Phi_{required} = E \times A = 500 \times (30 \times 20)
$$

$$
\Phi_{required} = 500 \times 600 = 300{,}000 \text{ lm}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of lamps**

$$
n = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF} = \frac{300{,}000}{30{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
n = \frac{300{,}000}{13{,}500} = 22.22
$$

Rounding up (cannot have fractional lamp in a single fitting; we round to nearest practical grid):

$$
n = 24 \text{ lamps (arranged in a 6} \times \text{4 grid)}
$$

**Step 3 — Total wattage**

$$
P_{total} = 24 \times 400 = 9{,}600 \text{ W} = 9.6 \text{ kW}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Projector Beam Candlepower

For a projector at mounting height $h$ above the working plane, tilted at angle $\theta$ from the vertical, the horizontal distance to the illuminated point is $d = h \tan\theta$. The slant distance is $r = h / \cos\theta$.

The illuminance at angle $\theta$ is:

$$
E_h = \frac{I_\theta \cos^3\theta}{h^2}
$$

**Derivation detail:**

From the inverse-square law with obliquity factor:

$$
E_h = \frac{I_\theta \cos\theta}{r^2}
$$

Since $r = h / \cos\theta$, we have $r^2 = h^2 / \cos^2\theta$. Substituting:

$$
E_h = \frac{I_\theta \cos\theta \times \cos^2\theta}{h^2} = \frac{I_\theta \cos^3\theta}{h^2}
$$

Solving for $I_\theta$ (candlepower required at angle $\theta$):

$$
I_\theta = \frac{E_h \times h^2}{\cos^3\theta}
$$

This is the equation used to **plot the polar curve** of a floodlight projector.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Lamp Comparison for Common Applications

| Application | Recommended Lamp | Efficacy Range (lm/W) | CRI | CCT (K) | Life (h) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic living room | LED / Tungsten Halogen | 15–100 | 80–100 | 2700–3000 | 2,000–25,000 | Warm tone, high CRI for skin tones |
| Office (task) | LED Panel / FL T5 | 80–120 | 80–90 | 4000–5000 | 20,000–50,000 | Cool white, low glare, energy efficient |
| Roadway (highway) | HPSV / LED | 80–150 | 25–70 | 2000–4000 | 24,000–50,000 | High efficacy, fog penetration |
| Stadium / Sports | Metal Halide / LED | 80–110 | 65–90 | 4000–6000 | 10,000–25,000 | High CRI for TV broadcast |
| Hospital operation theatre | LED (medical grade) | 80–120 | > 95 | 4000–5000 | 30,000+ | Critical colour discrimination |
| Industrial high-bay | Metal Halide / LED | 80–130 | 60–80 | 4000 | 15,000–50,000 | High lumen package, durable |
| Photography / Film | Tungsten Halogen / HMI | 20–90 | 95–100 | 3200 / 5600 | 200–2,000 | Perfect CRI, controlled spectrum |
| Tunnel / Underpass | LPSV (low pressure sodium) | 100–200 | ~25 | 1800 | 18,000 | Maximum visibility in soot-laden air |
| Retail (fashion) | LED with CRI > 90 | 80–110 | 90–98 | 3000 | 25,000 | Accurate fabric colour rendering |
| Parking lot | HPSV / LED | 80–140 | 25–70 | 3000 | 24,000–50,000 | Cost-effective, weatherproof |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Projector Selection by Beam Angle

**Given:** A projector must illuminate an area of width $W$ at horizontal distance $D$ from the base of the pole. Mounting height is $H$.

The beam half-angle $\beta$ is:

$$
\beta = \arctan\left(\frac{W/2}{D}\right)
$$

The full beam angle is $2\beta$. The polar candlepower distribution must provide adequate $E_h$ across the full beam:

$$
E_h(\theta) = \frac{I_\theta \cos^3\theta}{H^2}
$$

For uniform illumination across the beam, $I_\theta$ must follow a **cosecant law**:

$$
I_\theta = I_0 \csc^3\theta
$$

This is the design target for **asymmetrical floodlight projectors** used in stadiums and façade lighting.

---

### 3.6 Cost Optimisation Derivation (Lifecycle Cost)

The annual running cost per lamp:

$$
C_{annual} = \left( P \times t \times c_e \right) + \left( \frac{C_{lamp}}{L} \times t \right) + \left( C_{maint} \right)
$$

where:
- $P$ = lamp wattage (W)
- $t$ = annual burning hours (h)
- $c_e$ = energy cost (₹/kWh)
- $C_{lamp}$ = lamp replacement cost (₹)
- $L$ = rated life (h)
- $C_{maint}$ = labour cost per replacement (₹)

This is the **deciding parameter** when two lamps have similar photometric performance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lamp Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Application] --> B{Interior or Exterior?}
    B -- Interior --> C{Industrial or Commercial?}
    B -- Exterior --> D{Road Stadium or Facade?}
    C -- Industrial --> E{CRI requirement greater than 80?}
    C -- Commercial --> F{Require warm tone?}
    D -- Road --> G[Select HPSV or LED with CCT 3000-4000 K]
    D -- Stadium --> H[Select Metal Halide or High-power LED CRI 80 plus]
    D -- Facade --> I[Select Narrow-beam LED projector with cosecant distribution]
    E -- Yes --> J[Select Metal Halide or High-CRI LED]
    E -- No --> K[Select HPSV or Standard LED]
    F -- Yes --> L[Select Incandescent or Warm-white LED 2700 K]
    F -- No --> M[Select FL T5 or Cool-white LED 4000-5000 K]
    J --> N[Verify Efficacy greater than 80 lm/W]
    K --> N
    G --> N
    H --> N
    I --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Perform Lifecycle Cost Analysis]
    O --> P[Final Lamp Selection Approved]
```

### 4.2 Projector Selection Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Data] --> B[Mounting Height H]
    A --> C[Area to be Lit A]
    A --> D[Required Illuminance E]
    A --> E[Distance from Pole D]
    B --> F[Geometric Calculator]
    C --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Beam Angle 2 beta]
    F --> H[Candlepower Requirement I theta]
    D --> I[Illuminance Calculator]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Polar Curve Generator]
    G --> K[Optical Designer]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Reflector Type?}
    L -- Symmetrical --> M[Spherical Parabolic Reflector]
    L -- Asymmetrical --> N[Cosecant Reflector]
    M --> O[Final Projector Selection]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Verify Spacing-to-Height Ratio]
    P --> Q[Approved Projector]
```

### 4.3 Lamp Family Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Lamps] --> B[Incandescent Family]
    A --> C[Discharge Family]
    A --> D[Electroluminescent Family]
    B --> B1[Tungsten Filament]
    B --> B2[Tungsten Halogen]
    C --> E[Low Pressure Discharge]
    C --> F[High Pressure Discharge]
    E --> E1[Fluorescent FL]
    E --> E2[Low Pressure Sodium LPSV]
    F --> F1[Mercury Vapour MV]
    F --> F2[High Pressure Sodium HPSV]
    F --> F3[Metal Halide MH]
    F --> F4[High Pressure Xenon]
    D --> D1[LED COB]
    D --> D2[LED SMD]
    D --> D3[OLED Panel]
```

### 4.4 Projector Beam Geometry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L[Lamp Source in Projector] --> R[Primary Reflector Optics]
    R --> RF[Front Refractor or Lens]
    RF --> BS[Beam Spread Control]
    BS --> TG[Target Ground Plane]
    subgraph Polar_Plot
        P1[Peak Candlepower at 0 deg] --> P2[Half Peak at 15 deg]
        P2 --> P3[Quarter Peak at 30 deg]
        P3 --> P4[Tail Off at 45 deg]
    end
    TG --> Polar_Plot
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three factors that govern the selection of a lamp for a given application.*

**Model Answer:**
The three primary factors governing lamp selection are:
1. **Luminous efficacy** (lm/W) — determines energy efficiency for the required light output.
2. **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)** — determines accuracy of colour perception; critical for hospitals, textile industry, and photography.
3. **Rated life span (hours)** — determines maintenance frequency and lifecycle cost; critical for lamps mounted at high or inaccessible locations.

(Additional valid factors: CCT, initial cost, warm-up time, restrike time, dimmability, environmental disposal concerns.)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "utilisation factor" of a luminaire and state its typical range.*

**Model Answer:**
The **utilisation factor (UF)** is the ratio of the luminous flux received on the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps inside the luminaire. Mathematically:

$$
UF = \frac{\Phi_{received\,on\,working\,plane}}{\Phi_{emitted\,by\,lamp}}
$$

It depends on the room index, the luminaire type, and the reflectance of ceiling, walls, and floor. Its typical range is **0.4 to 0.8** for most interior installations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Two independent alternative questions are given below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Compare the following lamp types on the basis of efficacy, CRI, CCT, and rated life: (i) Incandescent, (ii) Fluorescent, (iii) Metal Halide, (iv) LED. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Incandescent | Fluorescent | Metal Halide | LED |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Efficacy (lm/W) | 10–25 | 50–100 | 80–110 | 80–200+ |
| CRI | 100 | 50–95 | 65–90 | 70–98 |
| CCT (K) | 2700–3000 | 2700–6500 | 3000–6000 | 2200–6500 |
| Rated Life (h) | 1,000–2,000 | 10,000–20,000 | 10,000–15,000 | 25,000–50,000 |
| Warm-up time | Instant | 1–3 min | 2–5 min | Instant |
| Dimmable | Yes | Limited | Limited | Yes (with driver) |

**Conclusion:** LEDs dominate modern selection due to highest efficacy, longest life, and selectable CCT, with Incandescent retained only for decorative or studio applications requiring perfect CRI.

---

**(b)** An auditorium of size $20 \text{ m} \times 15 \text{ m}$ requires a maintained illuminance of **300 lx**. Selected lamps: LED panels of 4000 lm each, with $UF = 0.55$ and $MF = 0.8$. Calculate (i) number of lamps required, (ii) total wattage if each LED panel is 36 W, (iii) total lumen output of the installation, (iv) overall efficacy of the installation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of lamps:**

$$
n = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF} = \frac{300 \times (20 \times 15)}{4000 \times 0.55 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
n = \frac{300 \times 300}{1760} = \frac{90{,}000}{1760} = 51.14
$$

Rounding up to practical grid: $n = 54$ lamps (in a $9 \times 6$ layout).

**(ii) Total wattage:**

$$
P_{total} = 54 \times 36 = 1{,}944 \text{ W} \approx 1.94 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Total lumen output:**

$$
\Phi_{total} = 54 \times 4000 = 216{,}000 \text{ lm}
$$

**(iv) Overall installation efficacy:**

$$
\eta_{inst} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{P_{total}} = \frac{216{,}000}{1944} = 111.1 \text{ lm/W}
$$

This exceeds the individual LED panel efficacy (4000/36 ≈ 111 lm/W), confirming calculation consistency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a floodlight projector** and derive the relation $E_h = \dfrac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A floodlight projector consists of a high-intensity lamp (typically metal halide or high-power LED) housed inside a weatherproof enclosure with a reflector (parabolic for symmetrical, cosecant for asymmetrical) and a tempered-glass front refractor.

**Block diagram of components:**

```
Lamp --> Reflector --> Front Glass/Refractor --> Beam Shaping --> Target Area
   |                                                            |
   |--- Heat Sink / Cooling Fan -------------------------------|
```

**Derivation:**

Consider a projector at mounting height $h$ above the working plane, tilted such that the beam axis makes angle $\theta$ with the vertical.

The slant distance from projector to a point $P$ on the ground is:

$$
r = \frac{h}{\cos\theta}
$$

By the inverse-square law, illuminance on a plane perpendicular to the beam axis is:

$$
E_n = \frac{I}{r^2}
$$

The horizontal component (illumination on the actual ground plane) is:

$$
E_h = E_n \cos\theta = \frac{I \cos\theta}{r^2}
$$

Substituting $r^2 = h^2 / \cos^2\theta$:

$$
E_h = \frac{I \cos\theta \times \cos^2\theta}{h^2} = \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}
$$

Hence the candlepower required at angle $\theta$ to produce horizontal illuminance $E_h$:

$$
I = \frac{E_h \times h^2}{\cos^3\theta}
$$

---

**(b)** A stadium uses 12 floodlight projectors mounted at **25 m** height. Each projector has a maximum candlepower of **200,000 cd** along its axis. Calculate the horizontal illuminance directly beneath the projector ($\theta = 0$) and at a horizontal distance of **30 m** from the base of the pole. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: At $\theta = 0°$ (directly beneath)**

$$
E_{h1} = \frac{I \cos^3(0°)}{h^2} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 1}{25^2}
$$

$$
E_{h1} = \frac{200{,}000}{625} = 320 \text{ lx}
$$

**Case 2: At horizontal distance 30 m**

The tilt angle:

$$
\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{30}{25}\right) = \arctan(1.2) = 50.19°
$$

The slant distance:

$$
r = \frac{h}{\cos\theta} = \frac{25}{\cos(50.19°)} = \frac{25}{0.6402} = 39.05 \text{ m}
$$

The horizontal illuminance:

$$
E_{h2} = \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2} = \frac{200{,}000 \times (0.6402)^3}{625}
$$

$$
E_{h2} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 0.2624}{625} = \frac{52{,}480}{625} = 83.97 \text{ lx}
$$

**For 12 projectors illuminating the same point** (assuming overlapping beams):

$$
E_{total} = 12 \times 83.97 = 1007.6 \text{ lx}
$$

This far exceeds the IESNA recommended 500–750 lx for sports stadiums, confirming adequate design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting MF in lamp calculations** — Examiners specifically check whether you used the *maintained* illuminance (i.e., already includes MF) or the *initial* illuminance. Always apply MF when the question says "maintained".
> 2. **Mixing up $E_h$ and $E_n$** — A common error is using the inverse-square formula directly without the $\cos^3\theta$ obliquity factor. Remember: $\cos^3\theta$ applies only for **horizontal** working plane.
> 3. **Not rounding UP lamp count** — You can never install 22.2 lamps; always round up to the next practical integer.
> 4. **Confusing CRI and CCT** — CRI measures *colour accuracy*; CCT measures *colour appearance*. These are independent metrics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lamp selection criteria priority:** Application → CCT & CRI → Efficacy → Life → Cost.
- **LED has the highest efficacy** (80–200+ lm/W) and longest life (25,000–50,000 h), making it the default modern choice.
- **HPSV is preferred for roadways** due to superior fog penetration and high efficacy.
- **Metal Halide is preferred for stadiums** where high CRI is essential for TV broadcasting.
- **The fundamental lamp count formula:** $n = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF}$ — memorise this.
- **The projector illuminance formula:** $E_h = \dfrac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$ — the $\cos^3\theta$ accounts for both distance and obliquity.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF) typically = 0.7 to 0.8** for clean environments; lower (0.5–0.6) for dusty industrial locations.
- **Utilisation Factor (UF)** depends on **Room Index (K)** and luminaire type; lookup tables are essential in design practice.
- **Beam efficiency** of a projector = (flux within beam cone) ÷ (total lamp flux); typically 0.4–0.7.
- **Cosecant-squared polar distribution** is the design target for asymmetrical floodlights illuminating rectangular areas (stadiums, parking lots).
- **Always convert units:** area in m², flux in lm, illuminance in lx, distance in m.
- **Wattage estimation:** Once lamp count and per-lamp wattage are known, multiply to get total connected load.
- **Lifecycle cost** includes initial cost + energy cost + replacement cost + labour cost; energy cost dominates for long-life installations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recommended Method for Aiming of Lamp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recommended Method for Aiming of Lamp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Recommended Method for Aiming of Lamp

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Recommended Method of Aiming** (also called the *Geometric Aiming Method* or *Tilt-Angle Method*) is a systematic engineering procedure used in **floodlighting and exterior illumination design** to orient a projector/floodlight such that its **maximum candlepower beam axis** is directed to strike the *far edge* of the area to be illuminated, ensuring **uniform illuminance distribution** across the entire target surface while minimizing glare, wastage, and dark spots.

### Key Vocabulary Anchors

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Mounting Height | $h$ | Vertical height of lamp above the plane to be illuminated |
| Horizontal Throw | $d$ | Horizontal distance from lamp base to far edge of target area |
| Angle of Tilt / Aim | $\theta$ | Angle the beam axis makes with the **vertical** (downward) |
| Angle of Elevation | $\phi$ | Angle the beam axis makes with the **horizontal** (ground) |
| Beam Spread / Divergence | $2\alpha$ | Total angular width of the useful beam |
| Maximum Candle Power Direction | $I_{max}$ | The line along which the lamp emits peak luminous intensity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose on a Tilted Stand":**
> Imagine you are watering a rectangular garden patch using a high-pressure hose fixed on a pole. If you point the hose straight down ($\theta = 0°$), the water lands directly under the pole — half the garden stays dry. If you point the hose horizontally ($\theta = 90°$), the water flies far but lands in a thin line. The *sweet spot* — where water uniformly wets the *far edge* of the garden — is achieved when the hose is **tilted so that its spray axis just reaches the farthest corner of the patch**. A floodlight behaves identically: instead of water, it sprays **lumens**; instead of tilt, we speak of the **angle of aim $\theta$**.

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **1 foot-candle** = **10.76 lux** = **1 lumen per square foot**
- The luminous efficacy of a Tungsten Halogen projector: **20 – 25 lumens/watt**
- The luminous efficacy of a Metal Halide floodlight: **80 – 100 lumens/watt**
- Standard aiming assumes the **inverse-square law** holds (point source approximation, valid when $d \geq 5h$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Geometric Logic Behind the Recommended Method

The recommended aiming method rests on **three sequential design decisions**:

1. **Selection of Mounting Height ($h$):**
   The mounting height is dictated by the **glare limitation** and the **area coverage**. The IES (Illuminating Engineering Society) recommendation is:
   
   $$h \;\geq\; 0.5 \times D_{max}$$
   
   where $D_{max}$ is the maximum horizontal throw to the farthest edge of the target. For sports applications, $h$ often equals **0.6 × D** to **0.75 × D**.

2. **Determination of the Angle of Tilt ($\theta$):**
   The lamp's optical axis is tilted from the vertical until it just grazes the **far edge** of the area. Geometrically:
   
   $$\tan(\theta) \;=\; \frac{d}{h}$$
   
   This is the **single most tested relationship** in KTU examinations on this topic.

3. **Beam Spread Verification ($2\alpha$):**
   The floodlight must have a **half-beam divergence $\alpha$** large enough to also cover the **near edge** of the area. Hence:
   
   $$\tan(\theta - \alpha) \;\leq\; \frac{d_1}{h}$$
   
   where $d_1$ is the horizontal distance to the near edge. If this is violated, a dark band appears near the lamp — a common KTU exam pitfall.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Cases

#### Case A — Illumination of a **Horizontal Plane** (e.g., a stadium field, a parking lot)

The lamp is tilted at angle $\theta$ from the vertical such that the **maximum candlepower ray** is aimed at the far edge.

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{h}\right)}$$

The illuminance at any point $P$ on the plane (at horizontal distance $x$ from the base of the pole) follows the cosine-cubed law:

$$E_x \;=\; \frac{I_\theta \cdot \cos^3(\beta)}{h^2}$$

where $\beta$ is the angle from the vertical to the point $P$, and $I_\theta$ is the luminous intensity in that direction.

#### Case B — Illumination of a **Vertical Plane** (e.g., a building façade, a hoarding)

For a vertical surface located at horizontal distance $D$ from a floodlight mounted at height $h$ above ground, the angle from the downward vertical to the point on the façade is given by:

$$\tan(\gamma) \;=\; \frac{D}{h}$$

The illuminance on the vertical surface is given by the **cosine law** (only one cosine factor because the surface is vertical):

$$E_v \;=\; \frac{I_\gamma \cdot \cos(\gamma)}{D^2 + h^2}$$

The **uniformity** of illumination is optimized when the lamp is positioned at a height equal to **half the vertical height of the surface** to be illuminated, with the aim point at the **center of the vertical face**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference — Print and Memorize**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Variables & Units | Validity |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | $\tan(\theta) = d / h$ | Aim angle from vertical for horizontal plane | $d$ [m], $h$ [m], $\theta$ [°] | Point-source floodlight |
| 2 | $\tan(\phi) = h / d$ | Angle of elevation from horizontal | $\phi = 90° - \theta$ | — |
| 3 | $E_x = I_\theta \cos^3(\beta) \,/\, h^2$ | Illuminance on horizontal plane at angle $\beta$ from vertical | $I_\theta$ [cd], $E_x$ [lux] | Inverse-square + cosine-cube |
| 4 | $E_v = I_\gamma \cos(\gamma) \,/\, (D^2 + h^2)$ | Illuminance on vertical surface | $I_\gamma$ [cd] | Inverse-square + cosine |
| 5 | $h \geq 0.5 D_{max}$ | Glare-controlled mounting height rule | $D_{max}$ [m] | IES general guidance |
| 6 | $N = E_{avg} \cdot A \,/\, (\eta \cdot F)$ | Number of floodlights required | $E_{avg}$ [lux], $A$ [m²], $F$ [lm], $\eta$ [—] | Utilization factor method |
| 7 | $SP = h \cdot \tan(\theta + \alpha)$ | Aim point horizontal distance | $SP$ [m] | Beam spread verification |
| 8 | $\text{Beam axis tilt} = \theta_{max} - \alpha$ | Inner beam edge angle | $\theta_{max}$ [°] | Near-edge coverage check |

> **Variable Key:** $d$ = throw distance, $h$ = mounting height, $\theta$ = aim angle from vertical, $\phi$ = elevation from horizontal, $E$ = illuminance, $I$ = luminous intensity, $\beta$ = angle from vertical to point, $\gamma$ = angle from vertical to vertical-surface point, $D$ = horizontal distance to vertical plane, $SP$ = spot/aim point, $F$ = lamp lumens, $\eta$ = utilization factor, $N$ = number of lamps, $A$ = area.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Recommended Aiming Method is the **de-facto standard** for:

- **Sports floodlighting** (cricket stadiums, football grounds, tennis courts) — multi-tier aiming matrices
- **Architectural façade lighting** — accent and wash lighting
- **Industrial yard lighting** — container terminals, railway marshalling yards
- **Security perimeter lighting** — controlled throw, minimal light trespass
- **Billboard / hoarding illumination** — uniform vertical-plane lighting

Failure to apply this method results in **glare, hot-spots, dark zones**, and **non-compliance with IS 3646 / IS 6665** lighting standards — a frequent observation in KTU lab viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Aim Angle $\theta$ for a Horizontal Plane

**Given:** A floodlight mounted at height $h$ above a horizontal plane. The far edge of the area to be illuminated is at horizontal distance $d$ from the base of the pole.

**To Find:** The angle $\theta$ (from the vertical) at which the lamp's maximum candlepower ray must be aimed.

**Geometric Setup:**
The lamp, the base of the pole, and the far-edge target point form a right-angled triangle with the vertical leg $= h$ and the horizontal leg $= d$. The hypotenuse is the line of sight from the lamp to the far edge, and the angle this hypotenuse makes with the vertical is the desired aim angle $\theta$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In the right triangle:}\quad \tan(\theta) &= \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Adjacent side}} \\[6pt]
\tan(\theta) &= \frac{d}{h} \\[6pt]
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{h}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
The lamp must be rotated *downward from the vertical by the angle $\theta$* such that its central beam axis just reaches the far edge. This is the **Recommended Method** — the maximum-intensity ray defines the throw, and the cosine fall-off naturally compensates for the increased distance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Illuminance on Horizontal Plane (Cosine-Cube Law)

**Given:** A point source of luminous intensity $I_\theta$ (in the direction of point $P$) is at mounting height $h$. Point $P$ on the horizontal plane is at horizontal distance $x$ from the base, so the slant distance from lamp to $P$ is $r = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2}$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inverse-Square Law:}\quad E_{P} &= \frac{I_\theta}{r^{2}} = \frac{I_\theta}{x^{2} + h^{2}} \\[6pt]
\text{where}\quad \cos(\beta) &= \frac{h}{r} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{x^{2} + h^{2}}} \\[6pt]
\text{Since illuminance on the horizontal plane uses } &\cos(\beta)\text{ (projection of the surface normal):} \\[6pt]
E_{x} &= E_{P} \cdot \cos(\beta) = \frac{I_\theta \cdot \cos(\beta)}{x^{2} + h^{2}} \\[6pt]
\text{Substituting } \cos(\beta) = h/r \text{ and } r^{2} = x^{2} + h^{2}: \\[6pt]
E_{x} &= \frac{I_\theta \cdot \cos(\beta)}{r^{2}} = \frac{I_\theta \cdot \cos(\beta) \cdot \cos^{2}(\beta)}{h^{2}} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad \boxed{E_{x} &= \frac{I_\theta \cdot \cos^{3}(\beta)}{h^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the celebrated **cosine-cube law** for horizontal-plane illuminance from a point source — a **favorite KTU Part B derivation question**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Illuminance on a Vertical Plane

**Given:** A floodlight at height $h$ above the ground illuminates a vertical façade at horizontal distance $D$. A point $Q$ on the façade is at height $y$ above the ground, so the slant distance is $r = \sqrt{D^2 + (h - y)^2}$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inverse-Square Law on a surface perpendicular to the ray:} \quad E_{P} &= \frac{I_\gamma}{r^{2}} \\[6pt]
\text{The angle } \gamma \text{ is measured from the vertical at the lamp to the point } Q: \\[6pt]
\cos(\gamma) &= \frac{h - y}{\sqrt{D^{2} + (h - y)^{2}}} \\[6pt]
\text{Since the vertical surface's normal is horizontal, the projected area factor becomes } \cos(\gamma) \text{ rotated:}\\[6pt]
E_v &= \frac{I_\gamma \cdot \sin(\gamma)}{r^{2}} = \frac{I_\gamma \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{D^{2} + (h - y)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the complementary angle measured from the surface normal. For the **point of maximum illuminance** (where $dE_v/d\alpha = 0$), the lamp should be aimed at the **mid-height of the vertical surface**, and the optimal lamp position is at $h = H/2$, where $H$ is the total height of the façade.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A floodlight is mounted at a height of **15 m** to illuminate a horizontal rectangular ground area. The far edge of the area is at a horizontal distance of **30 m** from the base of the pole. Calculate: **(a)** the angle of tilt of the floodlight, **(b)** the angle of elevation, and **(c)** the horizontal distance of the aim point from the base.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:}\quad h &= 15\ \text{m}, \quad d = 30\ \text{m} \\[6pt]
\text{(a) Angle of tilt from vertical:}\quad \theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{h}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{30}{15}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2) \\[6pt]
\theta &\approx 63.43^{\circ} \\[6pt]
\text{(b) Angle of elevation from horizontal:}\quad \phi &= 90^{\circ} - \theta = 90^{\circ} - 63.43^{\circ} \\[6pt]
\phi &\approx 26.57^{\circ} \\[6pt]
\text{(c) Aim point distance (which equals } d \text{ for max-intensity aim):}\quad SP &= h \cdot \tan(\theta) = 15 \times 2 = 30\ \text{m} \\[6pt]
SP &= 30\ \text{m (verified)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The floodlight must be tilted **63.43° from the vertical** (or **26.57° above the horizontal**), and the aim point is **30 m from the base** of the pole.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Aim Strategy

| Scenario | Recommended Aim Strategy | Reason |
|----------|--------------------------|--------|
| Horizontal plane (sports field) | Aim max-candlepower ray at **far edge** | Compensates cosine-cube fall-off |
| Vertical plane (façade) | Aim at **mid-height of surface** | Maximizes uniformity along vertical |
| Building corner (90° façade) | Use **two floodlights**, one per face | Single lamp cannot cover 90° uniformly |
| Small object / statue | Aim at **geometric center** | Short throw, inverse-square negligible |
| Long narrow path | Use **multi-lamp linear array** with overlapping beams | Avoids scalloping |
| Circular area | Aim each lamp at **center** of the circle | Radial symmetry preserves uniformity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Recommended Aiming Geometry (Horizontal Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floodlight Lamp]:::lamp -->|Beam axis tilted at angle theta| B[Aim Point on Far Edge]
    C[Pole Base]:::pole -->|Horizontal distance d| B
    A -->|Vertical height h| C
    D[Near Edge]:::ground -->|Closer throw, lower E| E[Ground Plane]
    B -->|Far edge, minimum E| E
    F[Beam Spread 2 alpha]:::beam --> A
    F -->|Half angle alpha from axis| D
    F -->|Half angle alpha from axis| B
    G[Inverse-Square + Cosine Cube Attenuation]:::physics --> E
    A -. Emits lumens .-> G

    classDef lamp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef pole fill:#8B4513,stroke:#4B2511,color:#FFF
    classDef ground fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef beam fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef physics fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Aiming Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Define Target Area] --> A2{Surface Orientation?}
    A2 -->|Horizontal| B1[Choose Mounting Height h]
    A2 -->|Vertical| B2[Choose Mounting Height h and Stand-off D]
    B1 --> C1[Compute Throw d = Far Edge Distance]
    B2 --> C2[Compute Mid-Height H/2 Reference]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Aim Angle theta = arctan d/h]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Aim Point at H/2]
    D1 --> E1[Check Beam Spread 2 alpha covers near edge]
    D2 --> E2[Check Glare Limit at Observer Position]
    E1 -->|Covers| F1[Accept Aiming Configuration]
    E1 -->|Gap detected| G1[Increase Beam Spread or Reduce h]
    E2 -->|Within limit| F1
    E2 -->|Exceeds| G2[Increase h or Add Visor]
    G1 --> D1
    G2 --> B1
    F1 --> H1[Output: theta, phi, h, SP, N lamps]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Beam Geometry on a Horizontal Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Lamp at height h]:::lamp -->|Max candlepower beam| P1[Far edge at distance d]:::target
    L -->|Inner beam edge| P2[Near edge at distance d1]:::target
    L -->|Outer beam edge| P3[Beyond far edge - wasted]:::waste
    theta1[theta = aim angle from vertical] -.-> L
    alpha1[alpha = half beam spread] -.-> L
    L ---|Vertical drop h| G[Ground reference]:::ground
    P1 ---|Horizontal d| G
    P2 ---|Horizontal d1| G

    classDef lamp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef waste fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
    classDef ground fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> **Define the term "angle of tilt" of a floodlight and state its significance in the recommended method of aiming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **angle of tilt ($\theta$)** of a floodlight is the angle that the **maximum candlepower beam axis** makes with the **vertical downward direction**. In the recommended method of aiming, the lamp is tilted such that its beam axis is directed at the **far edge** of the area to be illuminated. The significance lies in **optimizing uniformity**: by aiming the peak intensity at the most distant point, the natural cosine-cube and inverse-square attenuation is exactly compensated, producing approximately equal illuminance across the entire surface. The tilt is computed from $\tan(\theta) = d/h$, where $d$ is the throw and $h$ is the mounting height. *(Examiner's note: award 1 mark each for definition, formula, and significance.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **State the cosine-cube law of illumination and explain why it applies to a horizontal plane.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **cosine-cube law** states that the illuminance $E_x$ on a horizontal plane at a horizontal distance $x$ from the base of a point source mounted at height $h$ is given by:

$$E_x = \frac{I_\theta \cdot \cos^3(\beta)}{h^2}$$

where $\beta$ is the angle from the vertical to the point under consideration, and $I_\theta$ is the luminous intensity in that direction. It applies to a horizontal plane because **two cosine factors** arise — one from the inverse-square law (the slant distance projection) and another from the **projection of the horizontal surface's normal** onto the ray direction. The third cosine factor comes from expressing the slant distance in terms of the vertical height. *(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for derivation sketch, 1 mark for physical reason.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain, with a neat diagram, the **recommended method of aiming a floodlight** for the illumination of a horizontal plane. Derive the expression for the angle of tilt and discuss how the **beam spread** is used to verify the near-edge coverage.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A stadium field of dimensions **100 m × 70 m** is to be illuminated to an average level of **500 lux** using metal-halide floodlights, each rated at **40,000 lumens** with a utilization factor of **0.55** and a maintenance factor of **0.8**. The floodlights are to be mounted on four poles, one at each corner, at a height of **25 m**. Determine: **(i)** the total lumen requirement, **(ii)** the total number of floodlights, and **(iii)** the angle of tilt for each pole aimed at the centre of the field.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Diagram and description (3 marks):** The recommended method aims the maximum candlepower beam at the **far edge** of the horizontal area. A right triangle is formed with the vertical side equal to the mounting height $h$ and the horizontal side equal to the throw $d$. The angle of tilt $\theta$ from the vertical satisfies $\tan(\theta) = d/h$. (Draw neat sketch: vertical pole, tilted lamp, far edge, near edge, beam cone with half-angle $\alpha$.)

2. **Beam spread verification (2 marks):** The half-beam-spread $\alpha$ is used to check that the *inner* edge of the beam still reaches the *near edge*. If the inner edge falls short, a dark band appears near the pole. The condition is: $\tan(\theta - \alpha) \leq d_1/h$, where $d_1$ is the near-edge distance.

3. **Significance (2 marks):** The method ensures **uniform illuminance** because the inverse-square and cosine-cube fall-off is naturally balanced by the increased throw to the far edge. It also minimizes glare, controls light spillage, and complies with IS 3646 standards.

**Part (b) — Numerical solution (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Total lumens required:}\quad F_{total} &= \frac{E_{avg} \cdot A}{\eta \cdot MF} \\[6pt]
A &= 100 \times 70 = 7000\ \text{m}^{2} \\[6pt]
F_{total} &= \frac{500 \times 7000}{0.55 \times 0.8} = \frac{3{,}500{,}000}{0.44} \\[6pt]
F_{total} &\approx 7{,}954{,}545\ \text{lumens} \\[10pt]
\text{(ii) Number of floodlights:}\quad N &= \frac{F_{total}}{F_{lamp}} = \frac{7{,}954{,}545}{40{,}000} \\[6pt]
N &\approx 199\ \text{floodlights} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{use 200 (rounded up)} \\[10pt]
\text{(iii) Aim angle:}\quad d &= \sqrt{50^{2} + 35^{2}} = \sqrt{2500 + 1225} = \sqrt{3725} \\[6pt]
d &\approx 61.03\ \text{m} \\[6pt]
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{h}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{61.03}{25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2.441) \\[6pt]
\theta &\approx 67.7^{\circ}\ \text{from vertical (or 22.3° elevation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Floodlights per pole:** $200 / 4 = 50$ floodlights per pole.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **illuminance on a vertical plane** due to a point source of luminous intensity $I$ placed at a height $h$ and horizontal distance $D$ from the plane. State the condition under which the illuminance on the vertical plane is **maximum**.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A vertical hoarding of height **12 m** and width **20 m** is to be illuminated by floodlights mounted on a pole at a horizontal distance of **15 m** from the hoarding. Each floodlight has a luminous intensity of **20,000 candela** in the direction of the hoarding centre. The pole height is to be chosen such that the illuminance is **maximum and uniform**. Calculate: **(i)** the optimum pole height, **(ii)** the illuminance at the top and bottom edges of the hoarding, and **(iii)** the average illuminance on the hoarding.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Consider a point source at height $h$ above the ground, located at horizontal distance $D$ from a vertical plane. A point on the vertical plane at height $y$ above the ground is at slant distance $r = \sqrt{D^2 + (h - y)^2}$ from the source. The illuminance perpendicular to the ray is $E_\perp = I / r^2$. For a vertical surface, the cosine of the angle between the ray and the surface normal is $\cos(\alpha) = D / r$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \frac{I \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{r^{2}} = \frac{I \cdot D}{(D^{2} + (h - y)^{2})^{3/2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $y$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dE_v}{dy} &= 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h - y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y = h
\end{aligned}
$$

**The illuminance on the vertical plane is maximum when the ray strikes the plane at the same height as the lamp** ($y = h$). For the **optimum uniformity** over a vertical surface of total height $H$, the lamp should be placed at height $h = H/2$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Optimum pole height:}\quad h &= \frac{H}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6\ \text{m} \\[6pt]
\text{(ii) Slant distance to top edge (y = 12 m):}\quad r_{top} &= \sqrt{15^{2} + (6 - 12)^{2}} = \sqrt{225 + 36} = \sqrt{261} \\[6pt]
r_{top} &\approx 16.16\ \text{m} \\[6pt]
\cos(\alpha_{top}) &= \frac{D}{r_{top}} = \frac{15}{16.16} \approx 0.928 \\[6pt]
E_{top} &= \frac{20000 \times 0.928}{261} \approx 71.1\ \text{lux} \\[10pt]
\text{Slant distance to bottom edge (y = 0 m):}\quad r_{bot} &= \sqrt{15^{2} + 6^{2}} = \sqrt{225 + 36} = \sqrt{261} \\[6pt]
r_{bot} &\approx 16.16\ \text{m (symmetric)} \\[6pt]
E_{bot} &= 71.1\ \text{lux (same as top — by symmetry, since } h = H/2) \\[10pt]
\text{(iii) Maximum illuminance at centre (y = h = 6 m):}\quad r_{max} &= D = 15\ \text{m} \\[6pt]
E_{max} &= \frac{20000}{15^{2}} = \frac{20000}{225} \approx 88.9\ \text{lux} \\[6pt]
\text{Average illuminance} &\approx \frac{E_{max}}{2} = 44.4\ \text{lux (for symmetric triangular distribution)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up $\theta$ and $\phi$:** Many students confuse the **angle of tilt from vertical** ($\theta$) with the **angle of elevation from horizontal** ($\phi$). Remember: $\theta + \phi = 90°$. Examiners award **zero** for the wrong angle in numerical problems.
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine-cube:** The illuminance on a horizontal plane is $I \cos^3(\beta) / h^2$, **NOT** $I \cos(\beta) / h^2$. This is the single most common derivation error.
> 3. **Ignoring beam spread:** Aiming the maximum candlepower ray at the far edge is **necessary but not sufficient**. You must verify that the inner beam edge covers the near edge; otherwise, a dark band appears near the pole.
> 4. **Confusing horizontal vs. vertical plane formulas:** Cosine-cube applies only to **horizontal** planes. For vertical planes, only a single cosine factor and a different slant distance apply.
> 5. **Units in lumen-method calculation:** Maintenance factor and utilization factor are **dimensionless**. Forgetting them inflates the required lamp count by 2–3× — a guaranteed mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Recommended Method for Aiming of Lamp**

- ⭐ The **recommended method** aims the **maximum candlepower beam** at the **far edge** of the target area — this is the single most important sentence of the topic.
- ⭐ The aim angle from the vertical is given by $\tan(\theta) = d/h$; from the horizontal, $\phi = 90° - \theta$.
- ⭐ The **cosine-cube law** $E_x = I \cos^3(\beta) / h^2$ governs horizontal-plane illuminance from a point source.
- ⭐ The **cosine law** (single factor) governs vertical-plane illuminance: $E_v = I \cos(\gamma) / (D^2 + (h - y)^2)$.
- ⭐ **Optimum mounting height** for uniform vertical-plane illumination: $h = H/2$ (half the surface height).
- ⭐ **Glare control rule**: $h \geq 0.5 \times D_{max}$.
- ⭐ **Beam spread check**: $\tan(\theta - \alpha) \leq d_1 / h$ — always verify to avoid dark zones.
- ⭐ **Lumen method for floodlight count**: $N = (E_{avg} \cdot A) / (\eta \cdot MF \cdot F_{lamp})$.
- ⭐ **Standard practice**: Multi-pole floodlighting (typically 4 to 6 poles) for rectangular areas; aim each pole at the geometric centre or far edge depending on the strategy.
- ⭐ **Engineering codes referenced**: IS 3646 (interior lighting), IS 6665 (floodlighting), IESNA RP-6 (sports lighting).
- ⭐ **Common KTU exam weightage**: 3–7 marks in Part A, 7–14 marks in Part B; derivations of cosine-cube law and aim angle are tested almost every cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Their Wattage and Number and Their Arrangement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Their Wattage and Number and Their Arrangement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lighting Design Calculation (Lumen Method):** The systematic engineering procedure used to determine the **number of luminaires**, their individual **wattage rating**, and their **geometric arrangement (layout)** within a given interior space so as to deliver a uniform, pre-specified level of **illuminance (lux)** on the working plane, while satisfying the visual comfort and energy-efficiency constraints dictated by the Illumination Engineering Society (IES) and Indian Standard **IS 3641**.

In formal KTU terms, the calculation of lamp wattage, number, and arrangement is collectively called **Interior Lighting Design by the Lumen (Flux) Method** and is governed by the following governing equation:

$$\text{Total Lumen Required} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$$

Where every symbol has a specific photometric meaning that we will dissect in Section 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Watering the Garden" Analogy**
> Imagine you have a rectangular garden (your room) that you need to water evenly. You have several sprinklers (your lamps) that spray water (luminous flux / lumens) in specific cone patterns. To get an even wetness (illuminance) across the whole garden:
> 1. **How wet do you want the garden?** (Required illuminance, E in lux)
> 2. **How big is the garden?** (Area, A in m²)
> 3. **How much water does each sprinkler throw?** (Lumens per lamp)
> 4. **Some water is wasted at the edges or absorbed by soil** (Utilization Factor, UF)
> 5. **Over time, sprinklers get clogged and throw less water** (Maintenance Factor, MF)
>
> From this, you calculate: **(a)** how many sprinklers to buy, **(b)** how powerful each one should be, and **(c)** where exactly to place them so no patch is left dry or over-watered.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** Measured in **lumens (lm)** — the total light energy emitted per second by a source.
- **Illuminance (E):** Measured in **lux (lx) = lm/m²** — the light falling on a surface.
- **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$):** Measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)** — efficiency of converting electrical power to light.
- **Standard Service Illuminance (IS 3641):** Office work = **300–500 lx**, Detailed work = **750–1000 lx**, Corridors = **100–150 lx**.
- **Mounting Height (h):** Vertical distance of the luminaire above the working plane (typically 0.85 m above floor).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Lighting Calculation

The complete design problem decomposes into three sequential sub-problems:

1. **Wattage Calculation** → "How strong must each lamp be?"
2. **Number Calculation** → "How many such lamps are required?"
3. **Arrangement** → "Where exactly should they be placed?"

### 2.2 Logic Flow (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

> **Step 1 — Identify Demand:** Establish the required illuminance level $E$ (in lux) from IS 3641 standards for the given task.
>
> **Step 2 — Quantify the Space:** Compute the floor area $A = L \times W$ in square metres.
>
> **Step 3 — Account for Losses:** Multiply by $\frac{1}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ to compensate for:
>   - Wasted light not reaching the work plane (**UF**)
>   - Lamp lumen depreciation and dirt accumulation over time (**MF**)
>
> **Step 4 — Determine Total Lumens Needed:** The total raw luminous flux that must be emitted by *all* lamps combined.
>
> **Step 5 — Choose Lamp Type:** Pick a commercially available lamp with known wattage $W$ and luminous efficacy $\eta$ (lm/W), giving lumens per lamp $= W \times \eta$.
>
> **Step 6 — Compute Number of Lamps:** $N = \frac{\text{Total Lumens}}{\text{Lumens per lamp}}$. Round up to the nearest whole integer.
>
> **Step 7 — Recompute Actual Wattage per Lamp:** $W_{\text{actual}} = \frac{N \times W \times \eta}{\eta} = N \times W$ → total wattage.
>
> **Step 8 — Spacing Layout:** Distribute the $N$ lamps symmetrically using the Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio (SHR).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Floor Area | $A = L \times W$ | Length, Width | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | Total Lumens Required | $\Phi_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ | $E$ = illuminance, UF = utilization factor, MF = maintenance factor | $\text{lumens}$ |
| 3 | Lumens per Lamp | $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = W \times \eta$ | $W$ = wattage, $\eta$ = efficacy | $\text{lm}$ |
| 4 | Number of Lamps | $N = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{total}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}}$ | Always round up | dimensionless |
| 5 | Actual Wattage / Lamp | $W_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{total}}}{N \times \eta}$ | After rounding $N$ up | $\text{W}$ |
| 6 | Total Electrical Load | $P_{\text{total}} = N \times W_{\text{actual}}$ | Connected load | $\text{W}$ or $\text{kW}$ |
| 7 | Room Index | $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{h \times (L + W)}$ | $h$ = mounting height above work plane | dimensionless |
| 8 | Spacing Criterion | $S_{\text{max}} = \text{SHR} \times h$ | SHR = spacing-to-height ratio (0.8 to 1.5) | $\text{m}$ |
| 9 | Number of Rows | $N_r = \dfrac{W}{S_{\text{max}}}$ | Rows across width | rounded up |
| 10 | Number per Row | $N_c = \dfrac{L}{S_{\text{max}}}$ | Lamps along length | rounded up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sanity Rule:** The product $N_r \times N_c$ must be $\geq N$. If not, you must either (a) increase $W_{\text{actual}}$ per lamp, (b) increase $h$, or (c) accept slightly lower illuminance.

### 2.4 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering

- **Energy Audit Compliance (ECBC 2017):** Indian building code mandates specific lux/W/m² benchmarks. Correct wattage calculation directly impacts star rating of buildings.
- **Visual Ergonomics:** Under-lit → eye strain, accidents; Over-lit → glare, energy wastage, heat load on HVAC.
- **Capital Cost vs. Operating Cost:** Smaller wattage × more lamps vs. larger wattage × fewer lamps — designers balance initial cost, replacement frequency, and electricity bills.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Lumen Equation

Starting from first principles, illuminance $E$ on a surface of area $A$ is:

$$
E = \frac{\text{Total Useful Lumens on the surface}}{\text{Area}}
$$

The total useful lumens actually reaching the working plane is **less** than the total lumens emitted by the lamps because of two losses:

- **Utilization Factor (UF):** The fraction of emitted lumens that *do* reach the work plane (depends on luminaire type, room geometry, reflectances). Typical UF = **0.4 to 0.8**.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** The fraction of original light *still* delivered after accounting for lamp lumen depreciation, dirt on luminaire, and dirt on room surfaces. Typical MF = **0.7 to 0.8** for clean environments, **0.6** for dirty industrial zones.

Therefore, the relationship between emitted lumens and useful lumens is:

$$
\Phi_{\text{useful}} = \Phi_{\text{emitted}} \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}
$$

Since $E = \Phi_{\text{useful}} / A$, we rearrange:

$$
\Phi_{\text{emitted}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}
$$

This is the **Governing Equation of the Lumen Method**. ∎

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Office Room Design (Full Solution)

> **Problem:** A general office measures $12\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m} \times 3.5\,\text{m}$ (height). The work plane is 0.85 m above the floor. Design the lighting installation using fluorescent lamps. Given:
> - Required illuminance $E = 400\,\text{lux}$
> - Utilization factor $\text{UF} = 0.5$
> - Maintenance factor $\text{MF} = 0.8$
> - Lamp: 40 W fluorescent, $\eta = 50\,\text{lm/W}$

**Step 1 — Compute Floor Area:**

$$
A = 12 \times 8 = 96\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Lumens Required:**

$$
\Phi_{\text{total}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}} = \frac{400 \times 96}{0.5 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
\Phi_{\text{total}} = \frac{38400}{0.40} = 96000\,\text{lumens}
$$

**Step 3 — Lumens per Lamp:**

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 40 \times 50 = 2000\,\text{lumens}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Lamps:**

$$
N = \frac{96000}{2000} = 48\,\text{lamps}
$$

**Step 5 — Recompute Actual Wattage (per lamp may be unchanged at 40 W):**

$$
W_{\text{actual}} = 40\,\text{W},\quad P_{\text{total}} = 48 \times 40 = 1920\,\text{W} = 1.92\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Mounting Height above Work Plane:**

$$
h = 3.5 - 0.85 = 2.65\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Spacing Rule:** For fluorescent direct luminaires, $\text{SHR} = 1.2$ (typical).

$$
S_{\text{max}} = 1.2 \times 2.65 = 3.18\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute Number of Rows and Lamps per Row:**

$$
N_r = \frac{W}{S_{\text{max}}} = \frac{8}{3.18} = 2.52 \rightarrow 3\,\text{rows}
$$

$$
N_c = \frac{L}{S_{\text{max}}} = \frac{12}{3.18} = 3.77 \rightarrow 4\,\text{luminaires/row}
$$

**Step 9 — Verify Total:**

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = 3 \times 4 = 12\,\text{luminaires}
$$

But a single luminaire in a fluorescent setup usually contains **2 tubes** (a twin-tube fitting), so total tubes = $12 \times 2 = 24$ tubes. This is **less than 48**!

**Step 10 — Re-design:** Each luminaire must hold **4 tubes** to deliver 48 tubes total.

$$
\text{Use } 12 \text{ luminaires} \times 4 \text{ tubes} = 48 \text{ tubes of 40 W}
$$

Or, alternatively, we **increase the lamp wattage**. Let us select 80 W fluorescent tubes ($\eta = 55\,\text{lm/W}$):

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 80 \times 55 = 4400\,\text{lm},\quad N = \frac{96000}{4400} = 21.8 \rightarrow 22\,\text{tubes}
$$
