Now, $22$ tubes = $11$ twin-tube luminaires → arrange in **2 rows × 6 columns** = 12 luminaires (with 1 spare). The 11 luminaires are placed: 6 in row 1, 5 in row 2 (offset by half-spacing for uniformity).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Arrangement Strategies

| Room Shape | Preferred Layout | Reasoning |
|------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Square ($L \approx W$) | Symmetric grid (e.g., $4 \times 4$) | Equal spacing in both directions; uniform illuminance |
| Long rectangle ($L > 2W$) | Linear rows along length | Cross-wise spacing would over-light short walls |
| Irregular (L, T, U shape) | Zonal sub-design | Treat each zone as a separate room |
| High bay ($h > 6\,\text{m}$) | Concentrated, narrow-beam | Wide-beam would cause excessive floor losses |
| Low ceiling ($h < 3\,\text{m}$) | Wide-beam, close spacing | SHR up to 1.5 acceptable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When you cannot fit $N$ lamps symmetrically, the official valuation key allows a $\pm 10\%$ deviation in achieved illuminance from the design value, and the spacing must be stated with a **maximum spacing and actual spacing**.

### 3.4 Analytical Calculation of Actual Achieved Illuminance (Verification Step)

After layout, the designer must verify the *actual* $E_{\text{actual}}$ delivered:

$$
E_{\text{actual}} = \frac{N_{\text{actual}} \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}{A}
$$

For Example 1, with 22 tubes of 4400 lm:

$$
E_{\text{actual}} = \frac{22 \times 4400 \times 0.5 \times 0.8}{96} = \frac{38720}{96} = 403.3\,\text{lux}
$$

This is within **0.83%** of the 400 lux target → **Design is acceptable.** ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Lighting Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters: E, A, UF, MF, Lamp Type] --> B[Compute Total Lumens Required]
    B --> C[Compute Number of Lumps N]
    C --> D{Is N an Integer?}
    D -- No --> E[Round Up to Next Integer]
    D -- Yes --> F[Proceed to Arrangement]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Mounting Height h]
    G --> H[Apply Spacing-to-Height Ratio SHR]
    H --> I[Compute Spacing S between Luminaires]
    I --> J[Determine Number of Rows and Columns]
    J --> K{Does N_rows x N_cols cover N?}
    K -- No --> L[Adjust Lamp Wattage or Layout]
    K -- Yes --> M[Verify E_actual on Working Plane]
    L --> J
    M --> N{Within +/- 10 percent of Target E?}
    N -- No --> O[Re-design Loop]
    N -- Yes --> P[Final Design Locked: Wattage, Number, Arrangement Documented]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Layout Patterns of Common Arrangements

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R1[Square Room: 4 x 4 Symmetric Grid]
    A1[L1] --- A2[L2] --- A3[L3] --- A4[L4]
    A5[L5] --- A6[L6] --- A7[L7] --- A8[L8]
    A9[L9] --- A10[L10] --- A11[L11] --- A12[L12]
    A13[L13] --- A14[L14] --- A15[L15] --- A16[L16]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R2[Long Rectangular Room: 2 Rows x 6 Columns]
    B1[L1] --- B2[L2] --- B3[L3] --- B4[L4] --- B5[L5] --- B6[L6]
    B7[L7] --- B8[L8] --- B9[L9] --- B10[L10] --- B11[L11] --- B12[L12]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R3[Staggered Brick Pattern for Better Uniformity]
    C1[L1] --- C2[L2] --- C3[L3] --- C4[L4]
    C5[L5] --- C6[L6] --- C7[L7] --- C8[L8]
    C9[L9] --- C10[L10] --- C11[L11] --- C12[L12]
    end
    R3 --> D[Offset by 0.5 x S from rows above and below]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional View of Mounting Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ceiling at H metres] -->|h meters| B[Luminaire Position]
    B -->|0.85 m| C[Working Plane]
    C --> D[Floor Level]
    E[Spacing S between Luminaires] --> F[Luminaire 1]
    E --> G[Luminaire 2]
    F --> H[S = SHR x h]
    G --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Block:** Each block is a *conceptual* lighting-design pipeline. In board exam answers, sketch a similar flowchart using boxes-and-arrows, but for **physical** arrangement draw a 2-D top-view plan marking the actual $N_r \times N_c$ lamp positions with the spacing dimension labelled.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Lumen Method of lighting calculation. Why is it preferred over the Point-by-Point method for general interior lighting?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Lumen Method (or Flux Method) is a lighting design technique used to determine the **number, wattage, and arrangement** of luminaires required to achieve a specified average illuminance over a working plane. It uses the equation:

$$
\Phi_{\text{total}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}
$$

It is preferred over the Point-by-Point method for **general interior lighting** because:
1. It is **faster** (single closed-form equation) — suitable when uniform illuminance is desired over a large area.
2. It accounts for **inter-reflections** within the room (via UF), which the Point-by-Point method neglects.
3. It is the **standard** for IS 3641 and IES-compliant designs.
*(1 mark per valid point + 0.5 marks for equation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Utilization Factor (UF) and Maintenance Factor (MF). What are typical values?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Utilization Factor (UF) | Maintenance Factor (MF) |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | Ratio of lumens reaching work plane to total lumens emitted by lamps | Ratio of illuminance at the end of maintenance cycle to initial illuminance |
| Depends on | Luminaire type, room index, surface reflectances | Lamp lumen depreciation, dirt accumulation, cleaning interval |
| Typical value | **0.4 to 0.8** (commercial) | **0.7 to 0.8** (clean) / 0.6 (dirty) |

*(1 mark definition each + 1 mark distinction + 0.5 marks each for typical values)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
*(a)* A drawing office of size $20\,\text{m} \times 10\,\text{m} \times 4\,\text{m}$ requires an illuminance of $750\,\text{lux}$ on the working plane (0.85 m above floor). The lamps available are fluorescent tubes of $50\,\text{W}$ rating with an efficacy of $60\,\text{lm/W}$. Assume $\text{UF} = 0.6$ and $\text{MF} = 0.75$. Calculate:
  (i) Total number of lamps required. (5 marks)
  (ii) Their arrangement in the room with proper spacing. (5 marks)
*(b)* If the same room is to be lit by LED panels of $36\,\text{W}$ each with efficacy $110\,\text{lm/W}$, recompute the number of panels and the total connected load. Compare the energy savings. (4 marks)

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a)(i): Number of Lamps (5 marks)

**Step 1 — Area:**

$$
A = 20 \times 10 = 200\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Total Lumens Required:**

$$
\Phi_{\text{total}} = \frac{750 \times 200}{0.6 \times 0.75} = \frac{150000}{0.45} = 333333.33\,\text{lumens}
$$

**Step 3 — Lumens per Lamp:**

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 50 \times 60 = 3000\,\text{lumens}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Lamps:**

$$
N = \frac{333333.33}{3000} = 111.11 \rightarrow 112\,\text{lamps}
$$

**Step 5 — Mounting Height above Work Plane:**

$$
h = 4.0 - 0.85 = 3.15\,\text{m}
$$

#### Part (a)(ii): Arrangement (5 marks)

**Step 6 — Max Spacing (SHR = 1.2 for fluorescent):**

$$
S_{\text{max}} = 1.2 \times 3.15 = 3.78\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 7 — Number of Rows (across width 10 m):**

$$
N_r = \frac{10}{3.78} = 2.65 \rightarrow 3\,\text{rows}
$$

**Step 8 — Number of Lamps per Row (along length 20 m):**

$$
N_c = \frac{20}{3.78} = 5.29 \rightarrow 6\,\text{luminaires/row}
$$

**Step 9 — Total Luminaires Available:**

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = 3 \times 6 = 18\,\text{luminaires}
$$

Each luminaire typically contains 2 tubes → 36 tubes. But we need 112 tubes! So we must use luminaires with **more tubes** or **increase wattage per tube**.

**Redesign:** Use 80 W tubes ($\eta = 55\,\text{lm/W}$):
$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 80 \times 55 = 4400\,\text{lm}
$$
$$
N = \frac{333333}{4400} = 75.76 \rightarrow 76\,\text{tubes}
$$

**Final Arrangement:** 76 tubes ÷ 4 tubes per luminaire = 19 luminaires.

Use **4 rows × 5 columns** = 20 luminaires (4 tubes each = 80 tubes, slightly over-designed — within 10% tolerance). 

Actual spacing: $S_L = 20/5 = 4.0\,\text{m}$, $S_W = 10/4 = 2.5\,\text{m}$.

#### Part (b): LED Recalculation (4 marks)

**Step 1 — Lumens per LED Panel:**

$$
\Phi_{\text{LED}} = 36 \times 110 = 3960\,\text{lumens}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of LED Panels:**

$$
N_{\text{LED}} = \frac{333333}{3960} = 84.17 \rightarrow 85\,\text{panels}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Connected Load:**

$$
P_{\text{LED,total}} = 85 \times 36 = 3060\,\text{W} = 3.06\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Fluorescent Load:**

$$
P_{\text{fluo,total}} = 76 \times 80 = 6080\,\text{W} = 6.08\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{6.08 - 3.06}{6.08} \times 100\% = 49.67\%
$$

LED system saves **~50% energy** despite delivering the same illuminance. *(4 marks)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

*(a)* Explain with neat sketches the different **arrangement patterns** of luminaires in interior lighting design. Discuss the merits of staggered (brick) layout. (7 marks)
*(b)* A workshop $30\,\text{m} \times 15\,\text{m} \times 6\,\text{m}$ requires $300\,\text{lux}$ at work plane (0.85 m). Using 250 W metal halide lamps with $\eta = 80\,\text{lm/W}$, $\text{UF} = 0.55$, $\text{MF} = 0.7$:
  (i) Calculate the number of lamps. (3 marks)
  (ii) Design the spacing arrangement. (4 marks)

### **Model Solution — Question B (Outline)**

#### Part (a): Arrangement Patterns (7 marks)

There are **four standard arrangements**:

1. **Square Symmetric Grid:** $N_r = N_c$. Used in square rooms. Spacing $S = \sqrt{A/N}$.
2. **Rectangular Grid:** $N_r \neq N_c$. Used in rectangular rooms; long axis has more lamps.
3. **Staggered (Brick) Pattern:** Alternate rows offset by $S/2$. Provides better longitudinal uniformity; eliminates dark spots between luminaires in cross-direction. (3 marks for diagrams + 1 mark for comparison)
4. **Peripheral (Wall-Washer):** Luminaires placed along walls; used in galleries.

Merits of staggered: (i) reduces "scallop" effect, (ii) provides 10-15% better uniformity, (iii) preferred for high-bay industrial installations.

#### Part (b)(i): Number of Lamps (3 marks)

$$
A = 30 \times 15 = 450\,\text{m}^2
$$

$$
\Phi_{\text{total}} = \frac{300 \times 450}{0.55 \times 0.7} = \frac{135000}{0.385} = 350649\,\text{lm}
$$

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 250 \times 80 = 20000\,\text{lm}
$$

$$
N = \frac{350649}{20000} = 17.53 \rightarrow 18\,\text{lamps}
$$

#### Part (b)(ii): Spacing (4 marks)

$$
h = 6 - 0.85 = 5.15\,\text{m}
$$

For metal halide high-bay, $\text{SHR} = 0.8$ (narrow beam).

$$
S_{\text{max}} = 0.8 \times 5.15 = 4.12\,\text{m}
$$

$$
N_r = \frac{15}{4.12} = 3.64 \rightarrow 4\,\text{rows}
$$

$$
N_c = \frac{30}{4.12} = 7.28 \rightarrow 8\,\text{per row}
$$

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = 4 \times 8 = 32\,\text{luminaires}
$$

Since 32 > 18, we have **over-capacity** → designer may use **single 400 W MH lamps** (with $\eta = 85\,\text{lm/W}$, $\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 34000\,\text{lm}$) in 18 luminaires, **or** accept 18 well-spaced luminaires with actual spacing 5 m × 3.75 m (since $18 = 3 \times 6$ → 3 rows, 6 per row).

**Final Recommendation:** $3 \text{ rows} \times 6 \text{ columns} = 18$ luminaires spaced at $S_L = 5\,\text{m}$, $S_W = 5\,\text{m}$. *(4 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** the number of lamps — always round to the next whole integer; rounding down loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up UF and MF** — UF is geometry/optics, MF is time/depreciation. Swapping them is a -2 mark error.
> 3. **Not stating mounting height above WORK PLANE**, not ceiling — using $H$ instead of $h = H - 0.85$ loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step** ($E_{\text{actual}}$ calculation) — full 14-mark questions require a closing verification.
> 5. **Drawing the arrangement diagram without dimensions** — KTU examiners require labelled $S_L$, $S_W$ on the sketch.
> 6. **Choosing wrong SHR** — it is **luminaire-type-specific**, not arbitrary. LED panels: ~1.2, fluorescent: 1.0–1.5, high-bay metal halide: 0.6–0.8.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Master equation** for total lumens: $\Phi_{\text{total}} = E A / (\text{UF} \cdot \text{MF})$.
- [x] **Number of lamps** = $\Phi_{\text{total}} / \Phi_{\text{lamp}}$; always **round up**.
- [x] **Lamp selection** is driven by efficacy (lm/W) — LED > Fluorescent > Metal Halide > Incandescent.
- [x] **Mounting height** $h$ = ceiling height − 0.85 m (work plane height).
- [x] **Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio (SHR)** is a property of the luminaire, not the room.
- [x] **SHR ranges:** Direct fluorescent = 0.8 to 1.5; Indirect = 1.0 to 1.5; High-bay = 0.5 to 0.8.
- [x] **Maximum spacing** $S_{\text{max}} = \text{SHR} \times h$.
- [x] **Number of rows** = $\text{round-up}(W / S_{\text{max}})$; **Number per row** = $\text{round-up}(L / S_{\text{max}})$.
- [x] **Total lamps in layout** = $N_r \times N_c \geq N_{\text{required}}$.
- [x] **Staggered arrangement** improves uniformity; offset alternate rows by $S/2$.
- [x] **Verification** $E_{\text{actual}}$ must be within $\pm 10\%$ of design $E$.
- [x] **Total connected load** = $N_{\text{actual}} \times W_{\text{actual}}$ (in kW) — used for ECBC energy compliance.
- [x] **Room Index** $K = L W / [h (L+W)]$ — used to look up UF from manufacturer tables.
- [x] **MF typical:** Clean office 0.8; Normal industrial 0.7; Dirty industrial 0.6.
- [x] **UF typical:** Direct luminaire 0.5–0.7; Indirect 0.3–0.5; General diffuse 0.4–0.6.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunnel Lighting Zones and Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tunnel Lighting Zones and Schemes # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition **Tunnel Lighting** is a specialized branch of road illumination engineering that addresses the unique visual adaptation challenges faced by motorists while transitioning between outdoor daylight conditions and the confined interior of a tunnel. According to **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) Publication 88:2004**, tunnel lighting must be designed zone-by-zone, where each zone has its own photometric requirement based on the human eye's adaptation state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > Tunnel lighting is divided into **five distinct zones** along the longitudinal axis of the tunnel: **Threshold Zone, Transition Zone, Interior Zone, and Exit Zone**, each requiring different luminance levels to maintain visual continuity and prevent accidents caused by the "black-hole effect" or "white-hole effect." ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine walking into a **darkened cinema hall** from a bright sunlit afternoon: - At the **entrance**, you can barely see a thing for a few seconds (this is exactly what a driver experiences at the tunnel mouth — called the **Black Hole Effect**). - As your eyes slowly adapt, you begin to recognize shapes (this is the **Transition Zone**). - Once fully adapted, you can see comfortably in the dim interior (the **Interior Zone**). - When you exit back into bright sunlight, you are momentarily blinded (the **White Hole Effect** at the exit). Tunnel lighting is essentially **engineered to assist the driver's eyes** in making these transitions smoothly and safely. ## Key Physical Metrics - **Luminance (L):** Measured in **cd/m²** (candelas per square meter). - **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD):** Typically **60 m** for design speed of **60 km/h**. - **Adaptation Time:** Human eye takes approximately **5–10 seconds** for major adaptation. - **Reference Field:** **20° cone** of vision (CIE standard). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The CIE L20 Method uses the luminance measured over a **20° field of view** from a point located at the **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** from the tunnel portal. This single parameter governs the entire entrance lighting design. ## Five Zones of Tunnel Lighting (Summary Diagram) $$ \text{Tunnel Length Profile} = \underbrace{L_{th}}_{\text{Threshold}} \rightarrow \underbrace{L_{tr}}_{\text{Transition}} \rightarrow \underbrace{L_{in}}_{\text{Interior}} \rightarrow \underbrace{L_{ex}}_{\text{Exit}} $$ | Zone | Luminance Level | Driver Adaptation State | |------|-----------------|--------------------------| | Threshold Zone | Highest (≈ L20) | Fully dark-adapted loss occurs here | | Transition Zone | Progressively decreases | Pupils slowly dilating | | Interior Zone | Lowest constant | Fully dark-adapted | | Exit Zone | Slightly elevated | Pupils contracting toward exit | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The CIE L20 Method — Foundation of Tunnel Lighting Design The L20 method is the **international standard** (CIE 88:2004) for determining the threshold zone luminance requirement. The procedure is as follows: ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Identify Design Speed (V):** Typically 60 km/h, 80 km/h, or 100 km/h depending on the highway class. 2. **Compute Stopping Sight Distance (SSD):** The distance a vehicle travels from the moment an obstacle is detected to the moment it stops. 3. **Measure L20:** Average luminance of the road surface within a **20° cone** of vision, centered on the tunnel entrance, from a point located at **SSD** before the portal. 4. **Apply Reduction Factor (k):** Use CIE-recommended k-value tables to determine threshold zone luminance. 5. **Compute Transition Zone Luminosity:** Multiply L20 by successive k-factors that decay to interior level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why L20 and not L∞?** > The 20° cone represents the **foveal vision** (central sharp vision) of the driver — the area where obstacles are first detected. Using a wider cone would over-illuminate; using a narrower cone would compromise safety. ## 2.2 Detailed Zone-by-Zone Explanation ### A. Threshold Zone (Approach Zone) - Located **just before the tunnel portal** (last few seconds before entry). - Length ≈ **SSD distance**. - Luminance **Lth = k × L20**, where k is typically between **0.04 and 0.10**. - This is the **brightest zone** because the driver's eyes are still adapted to outdoor conditions. ### B. Transition Zone - Length is divided into several sub-stages (typically 4–6 stages). - Each stage has progressively lower luminance, halving approximately every few seconds. - The reduction factor **k_i** for the i-th stage is given by CIE tables. ### C. Interior Zone - Central portion of the tunnel where the driver's eyes are **fully adapted**. - Constant luminance of approximately **2 to 10 cd/m²** depending on traffic volume. - Symmetric lighting is generally sufficient here. ### D. Exit Zone - Located near the tunnel exit portal. - Luminance is slightly elevated (≈ 5 × L_in) to assist the driver preparing for bright outdoor conditions and prevent the **"White Hole Effect"**. ## 2.3 Schemes of Tunnel Lighting ### Scheme 1: Symmetrical Lighting - Light is distributed **uniformly in all downward directions**. - Most common and economical scheme. - Used in the **Interior Zone** where road reflectance is uniform. ### Scheme 2: Pro-Beam Lighting (Asymmetric) - Light is directed **in the direction of traffic flow**. - Enhances visibility of obstacles by silhouetting them against the bright pavement. - Suitable for **two-way tunnels** where traffic direction is fixed. ### Scheme 3: Counter-Beam Lighting (Asymmetric) - Light is directed **against the direction of traffic flow**. - Provides **maximum contrast** for obstacle detection. - Recommended for **high-speed tunnels** and unidirectional traffic. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** The Channel Tunnel (UK–France) and many Alpine tunnels (e.g., Gotthard Base Tunnel) use **counter-beam lighting** in their entrance zones because it provides 30–40% better obstacle visibility with lower energy consumption. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula No. | Equation | Description | Units | |-------------|----------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^{2}}{254 \cdot f}$ | Stopping Sight Distance | m | | 2 | $L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20}$ | Threshold Zone Luminance | $\text{cd/m}^{2}$ | | 3 | $L_{tr,i} = k_{i} \cdot L_{20}$ | Transition Zone Luminance (i-th stage) | $\text{cd/m}^{2}$ | | 4 | $L_{in} \approx 2 \text{ to } 10$ | Interior Zone Luminance | $\text{cd/m}^{2}$ | | 5 | $L_{ex} \approx 5 \cdot L_{in}$ | Exit Zone Luminance | $\text{cd/m}^{2}$ | | 6 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Illuminance from Luminous Flux | $\text{lx}$ | | 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi_{useful}}{\Phi_{total}}$ | Luminaire Efficiency | dimensionless | | 8 | $U = \dfrac{L_{min}}{L_{avg}}$ | Longitudinal Uniformity | ratio | | 9 | $U_{o} = \dfrac{L_{min}}{L_{avg}}$ | Overall Uniformity | ratio | | 10 | $TI = \dfrac{L_{max} - L_{avg}}{L_{avg}}$ | Threshold Increment (Glare) | ratio | **Where:** - $V$ = vehicle speed in km/h - $t$ = perception-reaction time (typically 2.5 s) - $f$ = coefficient of longitudinal friction (typically 0.35–0.40) - $L_{20}$ = luminance of approach road over 20° cone (cd/m²) - $k$ = CIE reduction factor (dimensionless) - $\Phi$ = luminous flux (lm) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Table Notation:** Absolute value notation in formulas uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ rendering via $\mid$ to remain compatible with markdown table syntax. ## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Tunnel lighting design directly impacts: 1. **Road Safety** — Poor lighting causes 30%+ accident rate increase in tunnels. 2. **Energy Consumption** — Tunnels operate 24/7; over-lighting wastes electricity. 3. **Visual Comfort** — Incorrect luminance ratios cause driver fatigue. 4. **Compliance** — International projects (e.g., NATM, CIE 88) mandate zone-based design. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Stopping Sight Distance (SSD) The SSD formula is derived from **two physical components**: the perception-reaction distance and the braking distance. ### Step 1: Identify the Two Components of SSD $$ SSD = d_{pr} + d_{br} $$ where: - $d_{pr}$ = perception-reaction distance (m) - $d_{br}$ = braking distance (m) ### Step 2: Convert Velocity to m/s $$ V_{m/s} = V_{km/h} \cdot \dfrac{1000}{3600} = V_{km/h} \cdot 0.278 $$ This is the standard road engineering conversion factor: **0.278**. ### Step 3: Derive the Perception-Reaction Distance The vehicle travels at constant velocity during the reaction time: $$ d_{pr} = V_{m/s} \cdot t = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t $$ ### Step 4: Derive the Braking Distance Using Kinematics During braking, the vehicle decelerates uniformly. Using $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$ with $v = 0$: $$ 0 = (V_{m/s})^{2} - 2 \cdot a \cdot d_{br} $$ $$ d_{br} = \dfrac{(V_{m/s})^{2}}{2a} $$ ### Step 5: Express Deceleration in Terms of Friction On a level road, the friction force provides deceleration. The deceleration coefficient $a = g \cdot f$, where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ and $f$ is the friction coefficient. Substituting: $$ d_{br} = \dfrac{(V_{m/s})^{2}}{2 \cdot g \cdot f} $$ Converting back to km/h units (multiplier of $3.6^{2} = 12.96$ appears, then simplified): $$ d_{br} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{254 \cdot f} $$ This is the **standard road engineering form**. ### Step 6: Combine Both Distances $$ SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^{2}}{254 \cdot f} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — SSD Calculation **Problem:** Compute the SSD for a tunnel approach with design speed $V = 80 \text{ km/h}$, reaction time $t = 2.5 \text{ s}$, and friction coefficient $f = 0.35$. ### Solution: $$ d_{pr} = 0.278 \times 80 \times 2.5 = 55.6 \text{ m} $$ $$ d_{br} = \dfrac{80^{2}}{254 \times 0.35} = \dfrac{6400}{88.9} = 72.0 \text{ m} $$ $$ SSD = 55.6 + 72.0 = 127.6 \text{ m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The L20 measurement point must be located at **127.6 m** before the tunnel portal for this design. ## 3.3 Derivation of Threshold Zone Luminance The **CIE L20 Method** mathematically links the approach road luminance to the threshold zone luminance requirement: ### Step 1: Define the Measurement $L_{20}$ is the average luminance measured by a luminance meter placed at the SSD point, with a **20° acceptance cone** oriented toward the tunnel entrance. ### Step 2: Apply the Reduction Factor The reduction factor **k** accounts for the **partial adaptation** of the driver's eye during transit through the threshold zone: $$ L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20} $$ ### Step 3: Look Up k from CIE Tables For a typical 2-second transit through the threshold zone (at 60 km/h covering ~33 m), the CIE recommends: | Tunnel Length Class | k-value | |---------------------|---------| | Short (< 100 m) | 0.04 – 0.06 | | Medium (100–500 m) | 0.06 – 0.08 | | Long (> 500 m) | 0.08 – 0.10 | ### Step 4: Worked Example **Given:** $L_{20} = 4000 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$ (bright sunny day) and tunnel is long, so $k = 0.10$. $$ L_{th} = 0.10 \times 4000 = 400 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ This is the **threshold zone luminance** that must be maintained. ## 3.4 Derivation of Transition Zone Luminance The transition zone has **multiple sub-stages**, each with its own k-factor. The k-factors decay exponentially: ### Standard CIE Transition k-values (5-stage): | Stage (i) | $k_i$ | Stage Length (at 60 km/h) | |-----------|--------|----------------------------| | 1 | 0.40 × k | ~10 m | | 2 | 0.13 × k | ~10 m | | 3 | 0.05 × k | ~15 m | | 4 | 0.025 × k | ~15 m | | 5 | 0.013 × k | ~20 m | ### Worked Example (Continuation from 3.3): $$ L_{tr,1} = 0.40 \times 400 = 160 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,2} = 0.13 \times 400 = 52 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,3} = 0.05 \times 400 = 20 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,4} = 0.025 \times 400 = 10 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,5} = 0.013 \times 400 = 5.2 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ > [!NOTE] > The transition zone luminance approaches the **interior zone luminance** (~ 5–10 cd/m²) smoothly, preventing abrupt visual changes. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Zone Comparison | Parameter | Threshold | Transition | Interior | Exit | |-----------|-----------|------------|----------|------| | Length | SSD | 30–70 m | Variable | ~30 m | | Luminance | 400 cd/m² (example) | 160 → 5 cd/m² | 5 cd/m² | 25 cd/m² | | Driver State | Dark loss beginning | Adapting | Adapted | Adapting to bright | | Lighting Scheme | Counter-beam | Pro-beam | Symmetrical | Symmetrical/Pro-beam | | Primary Goal | Eliminate black hole | Smooth adaptation | Visibility | Eliminate white hole | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Tunnel Zones — Longitudinal Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["APPROACH ROAD
L20 measured here
at SSD distance"] nodeB["THRESHOLD ZONE
Lth equals k times L20
Brightest section"] nodeC["TRANSITION ZONE
Multiple sub-stages
Luminance decays"] nodeD["INTERIOR ZONE
Constant low luminance
Fully dark adapted"] nodeE["EXIT ZONE
Slightly elevated
Counter white hole"] nodeF["BEYOND EXIT
Driver exits safely
into daylight"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5 style nodeB fill:#FF6347 style nodeC fill:#FFA500 style nodeD fill:#4682B4 style nodeE fill:#87CEEB style nodeF fill:#90EE90 ``` ## 4.2 Lighting Schemes — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeX["TUNNEL LIGHTING SCHEMES"] nodeY["SYMMETRICAL
Uniform downward light
Used in Interior Zone"] nodeZ["PRO-BEAM
Light directed along traffic
Silhouettes obstacles"] nodeW["COUNTER-BEAM
Light against traffic
Maximum contrast"] nodeX --> nodeY nodeX --> nodeZ nodeX --> nodeW subgraph APPLIC ["APPLICATION ZONES"] nodeY1["Interior Zone"] nodeY2["Exit Zone"] nodeZ1["Transition Zone"] nodeW1["Threshold Zone"] end nodeY -.-> nodeY1 nodeY -.-> nodeY2 nodeZ -.-> nodeZ1 nodeW -.-> nodeW1 ``` ## 4.3 Black Hole Effect vs White Hole Effect ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP["DRIVER APPROACHING TUNNEL
Eyes adapted to daylight"] nodeQ["BLACK HOLE EFFECT
Tunnel mouth appears dark
Obstacles invisible"] nodeR["DRIVER EXITING TUNNEL
Eyes adapted to dim light"] nodeS["WHITE HOLE EFFECT
Exit appears extremely bright
Temporary blinding"] nodeP --> nodeQ nodeQ -->|"Corrected by
Threshold Zone Lighting"| nodeR nodeR --> nodeS style nodeQ fill:#1a1a1a,color:#ffffff style nodeS fill:#FFFFFF,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 L20 Measurement Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeM["MEASUREMENT POINT
Located at SSD from tunnel
Height 1.5 m above road"] nodeN["20 DEGREE CONE
Field of view towards portal
Centered on tunnel axis"] nodeO["L20 VALUE
Average luminance in cone
Used as design input"] nodeM --> nodeN --> nodeO ``` ## 4.5 Counter-Beam vs Pro-Beam Light Distribution | Scheme | Light Direction | Best For | Energy Efficiency | |--------|-----------------|----------|-------------------| | Symmetrical | All directions equally | Interior | High | | Pro-Beam | Same as traffic direction | Transition | Medium | | Counter-Beam | Opposite to traffic | Threshold | Highest visibility | # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the Threshold Zone in tunnel lighting. What is its primary design objective? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Threshold Zone** is the first section of a tunnel located immediately after the portal, with a length approximately equal to the **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** of the design vehicle. Its primary design objective is to **eliminate the black-hole effect** by providing sufficient luminance so that the driver's eyes, which are still adapted to outdoor daylight conditions, can detect obstacles within the tunnel without a sudden loss of visibility. The threshold zone luminance is computed using the **CIE L20 method**: $$ L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20} $$ where $L_{20}$ is the average luminance over a 20° cone measured at the SSD point before the tunnel, and $k$ is the CIE reduction factor (typically 0.04–0.10). --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Pro-beam and Counter-beam lighting schemes. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Pro-Beam Lighting | Counter-Beam Lighting | |-----------|-------------------|------------------------| | Light Direction | Aimed **along** traffic flow | Aimed **against** traffic flow | | Visual Effect | Obstacles silhouetted against pavement | Obstacles appear **bright against dark background** | | Contrast | Moderate contrast | High contrast | | Typical Use | Transition Zone | Threshold Zone | | Best Suited For | Two-way tunnels | Unidirectional, high-speed tunnels | Counter-beam provides **30–40% better obstacle visibility** and is preferred at tunnel entrances. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **Question A(a): [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the various zones of tunnel lighting as recommended by CIE. State the luminance requirement of each zone. **Model Answer:** The CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) recommends that tunnel lighting be designed in **four distinct longitudinal zones**, each addressing a specific stage of the driver's visual adaptation: **1. Threshold Zone:** - Located immediately after the tunnel entrance. - Length ≈ Stopping Sight Distance. - Luminance: $L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20}$, typically the **highest** zone luminance. **2. Transition Zone:** - Located after the threshold zone, divided into 4–6 sub-stages. - Luminance progressively decreases from $L_{th}$ to interior level. - Allows gradual eye adaptation. **3. Interior Zone:** - The central portion of the tunnel. - Constant low luminance, typically **2 to 10 cd/m²**. - Driver's eyes are fully dark-adapted here. **4. Exit Zone:** - Located near the tunnel exit. - Luminance slightly elevated to prevent the white-hole effect. - Approximately $5 \times L_{in}$. Refer to Section 4.1 for the diagrammatic representation. **Question A(b): [7 Marks]** A tunnel approach road has a measured $L_{20} = 3500 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$. For a long tunnel, design the luminance profile using $k = 0.08$ and the standard CIE 5-stage transition factors: 0.40k, 0.13k, 0.05k, 0.025k, and 0.013k. Interior zone luminance is 6 cd/m². **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute Threshold Zone Luminance: $$ L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20} = 0.08 \times 3500 = 280 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ **Step 2:** Compute each Transition Zone sub-stage: $$ L_{tr,1} = 0.40 \times 280 = 112 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,2} = 0.13 \times 280 = 36.4 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,3} = 0.05 \times 280 = 14.0 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,4} = 0.025 \times 280 = 7.0 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ $$ L_{tr,5} = 0.013 \times 280 = 3.64 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ **Step 3:** Note that $L_{tr,5} = 3.64 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$ is **below** the interior zone requirement of $6 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$. Therefore, the **last transition stage** must be clipped at $L_{in}$: $$ L_{tr,5}^{corrected} = 6 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ **Step 4:** Exit Zone Luminance: $$ L_{ex} = 5 \times L_{in} = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ cd/m}^{2} $$ **Summary Table:** | Zone | Luminance (cd/m²) | |------|-------------------| | Threshold | 280 | | Transition Stage 1 | 112 | | Transition Stage 2 | 36.4 | | Transition Stage 3 | 14.0 | | Transition Stage 4 | 7.0 | | Transition Stage 5 / Interior | 6.0 | | Exit | 30 | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **Question B(a): [7 Marks]** Explain the CIE L20 Method of tunnel lighting design in detail. How is the threshold zone luminance determined? **Model Answer:** The **CIE L20 Method** (CIE Publication 88:2004) is the internationally accepted procedure for designing tunnel entrance lighting. The method is based on the principle that **the threshold zone luminance must match the adaptation state of the driver's eyes** as they approach the tunnel. **Procedure:** 1. **Determine the design speed (V)** of the tunnel approach road. 2. **Compute the Stopping Sight Distance (SSD):** $$ SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^{2}}{254 \cdot f} $$ 3. **Measure $L_{20}$:** Position a luminance meter at the SSD point, oriented toward the tunnel entrance. The meter measures the average luminance within a **20° acceptance cone**, which corresponds to the driver's foveal (central) vision field. 4. **Select the CIE reduction factor (k):** Based on tunnel length class and desired adaptation time. 5. **Calculate Threshold Zone Luminance:** $$ L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20} $$ **Physical Justification:** The 20° cone represents the visual field where the human eye has the highest acuity. By matching the tunnel entrance luminance to the adaptation state derived from $L_{20}$, the black-hole effect is minimized. **Question B(b): [7 Marks]** Compare the three schemes of tunnel lighting. Which scheme is most suitable for the threshold zone and why? **Model Answer:** The three schemes of tunnel lighting are: **1. Symmetrical Lighting:** - Luminaires emit light uniformly in all downward directions. - Provides general, non-directional illumination. - **Disadvantage:** Lower contrast for obstacle detection. - **Used in:** Interior Zone where the driver is fully adapted. **2. Pro-Beam Lighting (Asymmetric):** - Light directed **along the direction of traffic flow**. - Obstacles appear as dark silhouettes against the bright pavement ahead. - **Used in:** Transition Zone of medium-length tunnels. **3. Counter-Beam Lighting (Asymmetric):** - Light directed **opposite to the direction of traffic flow**. - Obstacles appear as **bright objects against a dark pavement background**, providing maximum visual contrast. - **Energy efficient** because the same visibility is achieved with lower wattage. **Best Scheme for Threshold Zone: Counter-Beam Lighting** **Justification:** - The threshold zone suffers from the most severe black-hole effect. - The driver needs **maximum obstacle contrast** at this point. - Counter-beam lighting delivers 30–40% better visibility per lumen compared to symmetrical schemes. - It is the CIE-preferred scheme for high-speed, unidirectional tunnels. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to clip the last transition stage:** When calculated $L_{tr,5}$ falls below $L_{in}$, students often fail to note that the transition cannot go below the interior zone requirement. This loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Mixing up Pro-beam and Counter-beam:** A very common error. Remember: **Counter-beam = light directed AGAINST traffic = bright obstacle on dark background.** > 3. **Wrong SSD conversion factor:** Always use **0.278** to convert km/h to m/s. Writing 0.27 or 0.28 is a mark loss. > 4. **Not mentioning the 20° cone:** The "20" in L20 is the field angle. Writing "L20 represents road luminance" without specifying the cone angle is incomplete. > 5. **Ignoring Friction Coefficient:** Always state $f = 0.35$ to 0.40 in SSD calculations. Defaulting to a specific value without justification loses marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Four Tunnel Zones:** Threshold → Transition → Interior → Exit (CIE standard). - **L20 Method:** Measures approach road luminance over a **20° cone** at the **SSD point**. - **Threshold Formula:** $L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20}$ (k between 0.04 and 0.10). - **Transition Stages:** CIE recommends 4–6 sub-stages with **decaying k-factors** (0.40, 0.13, 0.05, 0.025, 0.013). - **Interior Zone Luminance:** Typically **2 to 10 cd/m²**. - **Exit Zone Luminance:** Approximately **5 × Interior luminance**. - **SSD Formula:** $SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^{2}}{254 \cdot f}$. - **Three Lighting Schemes:** Symmetrical, Pro-beam, Counter-beam. - **Best Scheme for Threshold:** Counter-beam lighting (maximum contrast). - **Two Visual Hazards to Prevent:** **Black-hole effect** (at entrance) and **White-hole effect** (at exit). - **Stopping Sight Distance:** Always compute before locating the L20 measurement point. - **CIE Reference:** Publication 88:2004 is the authoritative standard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** Memorize the formula $L_{th} = k \cdot L_{20}$ and the three lighting schemes with their applications. These two items carry the maximum weightage in KTU 2024 ESE examinations for Module 4.

topics.title: Special Features of Aesthetic Lighting: Decorative Lighting of Monuments, Parks and Streets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Course Outcome:** CO4 - Design and analyze special lighting applications including aesthetic and decorative systems for architectural and outdoor environments.
> - **Topic:** Special Features of Aesthetic Lighting: Decorative Lighting of Monuments, Parks and Streets
> - **Cognitive Level (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy):** Apply / Analyze / Evaluate (L3–L5)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Aesthetic Lighting** is a specialized branch of illumination engineering in which light is used as a *visual medium* — not merely to enable visibility, but to **enhance the perceived form, texture, colour, and emotional impact** of an object, space, or landscape. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Module 4), it encompasses the artistic and engineering integration of luminaires, colour, beam control, and placement to produce a deliberate visual composition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** "Aesthetic lighting is the planned use of light to emphasize the architectural, sculptural, or natural features of a structure or landscape, with the dual objective of visual delight and functional safety."

### 1.2 Sub-Domains Covered Under This Topic
- **Decorative Lighting of Monuments** — floodlighting heritage buildings, statues, towers.
- **Decorative Lighting of Parks** — landscape lighting, tree lighting, path lighting, fountain lighting.
- **Decorative Lighting of Streets** — pedestrian-scale street lighting, festival lighting, façade lighting along roads.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Stage Director 🎭**
> Imagine a theatre stage before lighting design is applied. The actors (monuments, trees, streets) are present, but the audience cannot appreciate their expressions, costumes, or movements. Now, a lighting designer steps in:
> - A **spotlight from above** highlights the hero (monument façade).
> - A **soft blue wash** from the wings creates a moonlit backdrop (sky / background trees).
> - A **warm amber ground-level lamp** (pathway) guides the audience safely in and out.
> - **Coloured gels** on side lights evoke mood (festive, solemn, celebratory).
>
> Aesthetic lighting is **exactly this** — light is the *costume, makeup, and choreography* of architecture and landscape. Engineering ensures the actors are *bright enough to be seen safely*, but art ensures they are *remembered emotionally*.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
The following benchmarks are universally referenced in KTU valuation keys for Module 4 problems:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Correlated Colour Temperature | **CCT** | **2700 K – 6500 K** | Decides "warm vs cool" appearance |
| Colour Rendering Index | **CRI (Ra)** | **≥ 80** (monuments) | True colour of stone / paint |
| Ingress Protection | **IP** | **IP 65 / IP 66 / IP 67** | Outdoor weather resistance |
| Beam Angle | **θ** | **6° – 60°** | Spot vs flood classification |
| Illuminance on façade | **E** | **50 – 200 lux** (monuments) | Visual perception |
| Pole / Mounting Height | **H** | **3 m – 30 m** | Beam coverage geometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these metrics matter for KTU 2024:** Almost every 14-mark question in Module 4 either asks you to *select an appropriate luminaire* using these parameters, or to *calculate* the number of floodlights / pole spacing. Memorize the typical numerical ranges — examiners love testing them.

### 1.5 Governing Standards (India & International)
- **IS 1944 : 1970** — Code of practice for lighting of public roads.
- **IS 1947 : 1981** — Floodlighting of buildings, monuments, and waterworks.
- **IS 10322 (Part 5/Sec 5) : 2014** — Particular requirements for floodlights.
- **IS 16108 : 2012** — Solar photovoltaic street lighting.
- **CIE 154:2003** — Maintenance of outdoor lighting systems.
- **CIE 115:2010** — Lighting of roads for motor and pedestrian traffic.
- **NBC 2016 (India)** — National Building Code, Part 8 (Building Services).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Aesthetic Lighting Design
For a KTU 14-mark design question, every answer must address these three engineering axes:

**Pillar A — Functional (Visibility & Safety)**
- Provide minimum illuminance on task surfaces (paths, façades).
- Maintain uniformity ratio (U = E_min / E_avg ≥ 0.4 typical).
- Avoid glare (GR ≤ 50 for streets per CIE 115).

**Pillar B — Aesthetic (Visual Composition)**
- Create **focal point** (monument), **layers** (foreground / midground / background), and **rhythm** (repeated poles, trees).
- Use **light-shadow contrast** to reveal 3-D form.

**Pillar C — Environmental & Economic**
- Minimize **light pollution** (uplight, glare, sky-glow).
- Use LED sources (luminous efficacy **120–180 lm/W**).
- Factor **maintenance factor (MF ≈ 0.6–0.8)** for outdoor dust/wear.

### 2.2 Classification of Decorative Lighting Techniques
This is a *very high-frequency* KTU 2-mark/3-mark question. Memorize the following list with one-line definitions:

| Sl. | Technique | Direction of Light | Visual Effect | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Floodlighting** | Direct, from a distance | Even wash of entire façade | Monuments, large buildings |
| 2 | **Accent (Spot) Lighting** | Narrow, aimed | Highlights a specific feature | Sculptures, inscriptions |
| 3 | **Wall Washing** | Broad, close, grazing | Smooth vertical gradient | Plain façade surfaces |
| 4 | **Silhouette Lighting** | From behind subject | Dark subject against bright bg | Towers, trees against sky |
| 5 | **Grazing Light** | Very close, parallel to surface | Strong shadow texture | Stone, brick, carved surfaces |
| 6 | **Moonlighting** | Downward from high tree | Dappled ground effect | Parks, gardens |
| 7 | **Underwater Lighting** | Submerged, upward | Illumination through water | Fountains, ponds |
| 8 | **Path / Bollard Lighting** | Low, ground-level | Way-finding + décor | Park walks, gardens |
| 9 | **In-ground / Well Light** | Upward from floor | Hidden source, dramatic effect | Tree uplighting, flags |
| 10 | **String / Festoon Lighting** | Decorative, low-glare | Festive ambiance | Streets during events |

### 2.3 Colour Theory in Aesthetic Lighting
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CCT Band (Correlated Colour Temperature) — must memorize for KTU:**
> - **Warm White: 2700 K – 3000 K** → heritage monuments, parks (golden, inviting).
> - **Neutral White: 3500 K – 4500 K** → contemporary façades, streets.
> - **Cool White / Daylight: 5000 K – 6500 K** → commercial plazas, modern architecture.
> - **RGB / Dynamic Colour** → festivals, events, themed parks.

> **Key Rule:** For heritage / sandstone / red-brick monuments, **always prefer warm white (≤ 3000 K) with high CRI (≥ 80)** to avoid distorting natural colour.

### 2.4 Decorative Lighting of Monuments — Specific Design Rules
1. **Beam Angle Selection**
   - For broad façade: **Flood beam (30°–60°)** placed at distance ≥ façade height.
   - For narrow vertical features (towers, minarets): **Narrow spot (6°–15°)**.

2. **Mounting Position** — Ground-mounted floodlights at 1/3 to 1/2 the height of the structure on the *opposite side* of the viewer to avoid glare and create modelling.

3. **Cross-Floodlighting** — Place floodlights on *two or more sides* to eliminate harsh shadows and reveal 3-D form.

4. **Recommended Illuminance Levels (IS 1947)** for monument floodlighting:
   - Light-coloured surface: **50 – 100 lux**
   - Medium-coloured surface: **100 – 200 lux**
   - Dark surface (black granite, basalt): **200 – 500 lux**

5. **Colorimetry Constraint** — Use **monochromatic warm white** to maintain heritage authenticity; avoid RGB on historical buildings.

### 2.5 Decorative Lighting of Parks — Specific Design Rules
1. **Three-Layer Lighting Hierarchy**
   - **High layer (tree canopy):** Moonlighting from tall trees, **30–50 lux** on ground.
   - **Mid layer (paths, shrubs):** Bollards / pole lights at **0.9–1.2 m / 3–4 m** height, **20–30 lux**.
   - **Low layer (water / flowers):** In-ground or underwater lights, **10–20 lux**.

2. **Pole Spacing for Walkways**
   - For **3–4 m mounting height**, maintain spacing = **2.5 × H = 7.5–10 m**.
   - Use **warm white 3000 K** to harmonize with foliage.

3. **Tree Uplighting**
   - Place fixture at **1/3 the tree height**, beam aimed upward, angle **30°–45°** from vertical.
   - Use **narrow beam (10°–25°)** to concentrate light on canopy.

4. **IP Rating Mandate**
   - In-ground lights: **IP 67 / IP 68**
   - Pole-top / bollard: **IP 65**
   - Submersible (fountain): **IP 68**

### 2.6 Decorative Lighting of Streets — Specific Design Rules
1. **Pedestrian vs Vehicular Streets**
   - **Vehicular arterial road:** Pole height **8–12 m**, spacing **25–35 m**, CCT **4000–5000 K**, E_avg **15–30 lux**.
   - **Pedestrian / commercial street:** Pole height **3–5 m**, spacing **8–15 m**, CCT **3000 K**, E_avg **10–20 lux**, decorative post-top luminaires.

2. **Decorative Pole-Top Luminaires** (typical in Kerala municipal projects):
   - Cast-iron / aluminium heritage-style posts with **post-top lantern** (4-arm / 5-arm / globe).
   - Modern equivalent: **LED post-top with opal diffuser**, 30–60 W, 4000 K.

3. **Festoon / String Lighting** along streets uses **low-voltage LED festoon belts (24 V)**, IP 65, mounted between poles at 5–8 m sag.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance from point source | $E = \dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$ | $I$ = intensity, $h$ = vertical height, $\theta$ = angle from vertical | lux | Floodlight design |
| 2 | Beam spread on surface | $B = 2 \, h \, \tan(\theta/2)$ | $\theta$ = full beam angle, $h$ = mounting height | m | Coverage width |
| 3 | Number of floodlights | $N = \dfrac{A \times E}{\Phi \times \eta \times MF}$ | $A$ = area, $E$ = required lux, $\Phi$ = lumen/luminaire, $\eta$ = utilization, $MF$ = maintenance | unitless | Monument design |
| 4 | Number of poles (street) | $N = \dfrac{L}{S}$ | $L$ = road length, $S$ = spacing | unitless | Street design |
| 5 | Spacing for uniform illumination | $S = k \times H$ | $H$ = pole height, $k$ = 2.5 (paths), 3.5 (roads) | m | Layout |
| 6 | Luminous efficacy | $\eta_{lum} = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | $\Phi$ = lumen, $P$ = watt | lm/W | LED selection |
| 7 | Beam throw / reach | $D = h \times \tan\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = tilt angle | m | Aim point calculation |
| 8 | Mounting height for $E$ | $h = \sqrt{\dfrac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{E}}$ | rearranging (1) | m | Reverse design |
| 9 | Uniformity ratio | $U_0 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$ | min to average | unitless | KTU check |
| 10 | Glare index (simplified) | $GR = 27 + 24 \log(L_v / L_{av})$ | CIE 115 | unitless | Street evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Formula (1) — $E = I \cos^{3}\theta / h^{2}$ — is the **single most repeated derivation** in KTU Module 4 university papers. Know it backwards and forwards.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Urban planners (Smart City missions, Kochi/Trivandrum):** Use these design rules for public beautification projects.
- **Landscape architects:** Combine the 3-layer hierarchy with native plant lighting strategies.
- **Electrical contractors (KTU B.Tech jobs):** Calculate floodlight quantities, prepare BOQ (Bill of Quantities), and ensure IS-compliance.
- **Heritage conservation (ASI guidelines):** Avoid UV-emitting sources to prevent stone/colour degradation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Illuminance from a Point Source at an Angle (Inverse Cube Cosine Law)

**Statement:** A floodlight of luminous intensity $I$ candelas is mounted at a vertical height $h$ metres above the ground, aimed at an angle $\theta$ from the vertical. Find the illuminance $E$ on the illuminated point.

**Derivation:**

Let the floodlight be at point $L$ (luminaire), and the illuminated point on the ground be $P$. The vertical drop is $h$, and the horizontal distance is $d$.

$$
d = h \tan\theta
$$

The straight-line distance $D$ from the luminaire to the point $P$ is:

$$
D = \dfrac{h}{\cos\theta}
$$

By the **Inverse Square Law**, the illuminance on a surface *normal to the light ray* at $P$ would be:

$$
E_{n} = \dfrac{I}{D^{2}} = \dfrac{I \, \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}}
$$

However, the ground is **horizontal**, not normal to the light ray. The angle between the light ray and the ground normal is exactly $\theta$. So the illuminance on the horizontal ground is reduced by another factor of $\cos\theta$:

$$
E = E_{n} \cos\theta = \dfrac{I \, \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}} \cdot \cos\theta
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E = \dfrac{I \, \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}\,}
$$

**Why "$\cos^{3}\theta$"?** Because:
1. **One** $\cos^{2}\theta$ comes from the **inverse-square distance** $D = h / \cos\theta$.
2. **One** extra $\cos\theta$ comes from the **incidence-angle correction** (Lambert's cosine).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beam Spread on the Illuminated Surface

**Statement:** A floodlight with full beam angle $\theta$ is mounted at vertical height $h$. Find the diameter $B$ of the circular patch of light on the horizontal ground.

**Derivation:**

The beam is a cone of half-angle $\theta/2$. The footprint on the ground is bounded by two extreme rays that strike the ground at distances:

$$
d_{1} = h \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \quad \text{(near edge)}
$$

$$
d_{2} = h \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = h \cot\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \quad \text{(far edge)}
$$

For a *symmetrical* beam aimed vertically downward ($\theta/2$ on either side of vertical), the **diameter** of the light patch is:

$$
B = d_{1} + d_{2} = h \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) + h \cot\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

For *small beam angles* (narrow spot, $\theta < 30°$), the near contribution is negligible, and the simpler form suffices:

$$
\boxed{\,B \approx 2 \, h \, \tan(\theta/2)\,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** If $h = 10 \text{ m}$ and $\theta = 30°$, then $B \approx 2 \times 10 \times \tan 15° = 2 \times 10 \times 0.268 = 5.36 \text{ m}$. This is the **diameter of the lit circle**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Floodlights for a Façade

**Statement:** A rectangular façade of area $A$ (m²) requires an average illuminance of $E$ lux. Each floodlight produces $\Phi$ lumens, has a utilization factor $\eta$ (fraction of lumens reaching the façade), and a maintenance factor $MF$. Find the number of floodlights $N$.

**Derivation:**

Total lumens required to fall on the façade = $A \times E$.

Effective lumens delivered per floodlight = $\Phi \times \eta \times MF$.

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,N = \dfrac{A \times E}{\Phi \times \eta \times MF}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Note:** Many KTU students *forget* to apply $\eta$ and $MF$ — examiners deduct **2–3 marks per omission**. Always write: "Here, $\eta = 0.4$ (typical for outdoor floodlighting) and $MF = 0.7$ (typical for dusty outdoor conditions, lamp lumen depreciation 0.8 × luminaire dirt depreciation 0.875)."

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pole Spacing for Uniform Street Illumination

**Statement:** For a street with a single-side row of luminaires, derive the spacing $S$ such that the illuminance on the road is uniform.

**Derivation:**

Place poles of mounting height $H$ at spacing $S$. The illuminance *directly under* a pole is:

$$
E_{max} = \dfrac{I}{H^{2}}
$$

The illuminance *midway between two poles* (horizontal distance $S/2$ from nearest pole, angle $\theta$ from vertical) is:

$$
E_{min} = \dfrac{I \, \cos^{3}\theta}{(S/2)^{2} + H^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{(S/2)^{2} + H^{2}}}
$$

Simplifying with $H$ as the reference unit ($k = S/H$):

$$
E_{min} = \dfrac{I}{H^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1 + k^{2}/4)^{3/2}}
$$

For a uniformity ratio $U_0 = E_{min}/E_{max} = 0.4$ (KTU street design target):

$$
0.4 = \dfrac{1}{(1 + k^{2}/4)^{3/2}}
$$

$$
(1 + k^{2}/4)^{3/2} = 2.5
$$

$$
1 + k^{2}/4 = 2.5^{2/3} = 1.842
$$

$$
k^{2}/4 = 0.842 \Rightarrow k = 1.835
$$

Thus:

$$
\boxed{\,S \approx 1.8 \text{ to } 2.5 \, H\,}
$$

> **Rule of thumb (Kerala municipal practice):** $S = 2.5 H$ for narrow lanes, $S = 3.5 H$ for wide arterial roads (single-side pole arrangement). For **staggered (zig-zag) arrangement**, $S$ can be up to $4 H$.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A monument façade of dimensions $20 \text{ m} \times 12 \text{ m}$ is to be floodlit to an average illuminance of $150 \text{ lux}$. Each LED floodlight delivers $24{,}000 \text{ lm}$, has a utilization factor of $0.4$, and a maintenance factor of $0.7$. The floodlights are to be mounted at a height of $6 \text{ m}$ from the ground, on the opposite side of the road, $20 \text{ m}$ away. Find:
> (a) The number of floodlights required.
> (b) The beam angle needed for uniform coverage.
> (c) The luminous intensity of the lamp.

**Solution:**

**(a) Number of floodlights $N$:**

$$
N = \dfrac{A \times E}{\Phi \times \eta \times MF} = \dfrac{(20 \times 12) \times 150}{24{,}000 \times 0.4 \times 0.7}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{36{,}000}{6{,}720} = 5.357
$$

Round **up** to the next whole luminaire (partial floodlights do not exist):

$$
\boxed{\,N = 6 \text{ floodlights}\,}
$$

**(b) Beam angle $\theta$:**

The horizontal distance from floodlight to façade base = $20 \text{ m}$, mounting height = $6 \text{ m}$. The angle from vertical to the *top* of the façade (12 m up):

$$
\tan\theta_{top} = \dfrac{20}{12 - 6} = \dfrac{20}{6} = 3.333
$$

$$
\theta_{top} = \tan^{-1}(3.333) = 73.3°
$$

The angle from vertical to the *bottom* of the façade (0 m):

$$
\tan\theta_{bot} = \dfrac{20}{0 - 6} = \dfrac{20}{-6} = -3.333
$$

$$
\theta_{bot} = 180° - 73.3° = 106.7° \quad \text{(i.e., 73.3° below horizontal)}
$$

The required **beam spread** = $73.3° + 73.3° = 146.6°$? — *This is physically impossible for a single beam.* Hence, place the floodlights *closer* to the façade. The standard practical approach is to **aim each floodlight at a different section** of the façade, each with a $30°–40°$ beam:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Use 3 floodlights aimed at top half (40° beam), 3 at bottom half (40° beam)}\,}
$$

**(c) Luminous intensity $I$:**

For a point on the façade at the centre of the beam, the illuminance from one of the 6 floodlights is approximately $E_{single} = 150 \times 2 = 300 \text{ lux}$ (assuming two floodlights overlap centrally).

For the worst-case point (lowest illuminance, farthest from aim), use $\cos^{3}\theta / h^{2}$ relation. With $D = \sqrt{20^{2} + 6^{2}} = 20.88 \text{ m}$:

$$
I = \dfrac{E \cdot D^{2}}{\cos^{3}\theta} = \dfrac{300 \times (20.88)^{2}}{\cos^{3}\theta}
$$

For $\theta = 60°$ from vertical: $\cos 60° = 0.5$, $\cos^{3}60° = 0.125$.

$$
I = \dfrac{300 \times 435.97}{0.125} = 1{,}046{,}328 \text{ cd} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{6} \text{ cd}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I \approx 1.05 \text{ Mcd per floodlight}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **High-intensity discharge (HID) and modern LED floodlights** are commercially rated in **kcd** (kilocandela). 1.05 Mcd is achievable with a high-power LED array of around **400–500 W** (efficacy ≈ 120 lm/W).

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Decorative Technique to Use?"

| Application | Surface Type | Primary Goal | Recommended Technique | CCT | Beam Angle | IP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stone temple (heritage) | Carved granite | Reveal texture | **Grazing** | 2700 K | 10°–25° | IP 65 |
| Modern glass tower | Reflective | Drama | **Floodlighting** | 4000 K | 30°–45° | IP 65 |
| Tree in park | Foliage | Canopy glow | **In-ground uplight** | 3000 K | 15°–30° | IP 67 |
| Park pathway | Concrete | Way-finding | **Bollard / Post-top** | 3000 K | Diffuse 120° | IP 65 |
| Fountain | Water | Reflection | **Submersible** | RGB / 4000 K | 30°–45° | **IP 68** |
| Street (heritage zone) | Asphalt + building | Ambience | **Decorative post-top** | 3000 K | Diffuse 120° | IP 65 |
| Statue (foreground) | Bronze | Highlight | **Accent spot** | 2700 K | 6°–15° | IP 65 |
| Skyline silhouette | Building edge | Drama | **Silhouette** | 3000–4000 K | Narrow 10° | IP 65 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Aesthetic Lighting Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Project Brief:<br>Monument / Park / Street]) --> analyze[Analyze Site:<br>Dimensions, Surroundings,<br>Viewing Angles]
    analyze --> objective{Identify<br>Primary Objective}
    objective -->|Visibility| vis[Satisfy IS Lux Levels<br>E_avg, U0, GR]
    objective -->|Beauty / Mood| aes[Define Visual Theme:<br>Heritage / Modern / Festive]
    objective -->|Both| both[Combine Functional<br>and Aesthetic Criteria]
    vis --> selectL[Select Luminaire:<br>CCT, CRI, Beam Angle,<br>Wattage, IP Rating]
    aes --> selectL
    both --> selectL
    selectL --> place[Decide Mounting:<br>Height, Distance,<br>Aim Angle, Spacing]
    place --> calc[Engineering Calculations:<br>E = I cos cubed theta over h squared<br>N = A x E over Phi x eta x MF<br>S = k x H]
    calc --> check{Meets U0 and<br>Lux Criteria?}
    check -->|No| selectL
    check -->|Yes| control[Add Controls:<br>Dimming, Timer,<br>DMX / DALI]
    control --> draw[Prepare Layout<br>and BOQ]
    draw --> done([Final Design<br>with Aesthetic Impact])
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decorative Lighting System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwr[AC Mains Supply<br>230 V / 415 V] --> driver[LED Driver /<br>Ballast]
    driver --> lum[Luminaire<br>LED Array]
    ctrl[Controller<br>DMX512 / DALI /<br>Manual Dimmer] --> driver
    sensor[Photocell / Timer /<br>Motion Sensor] --> ctrl
    lum --> optics[Optical Assembly:<br>Reflector + Lens +<br>Gel / Filter]
    optics --> beam[Beam Output:<br>Spot / Flood / Wash]
    beam --> target[(Target Surface:<br>Monument, Tree,<br>Path, Façade)]
    target --> eye[Visual Perception:<br>CCT x CRI x Intensity]
    style pwr fill:#ffe5b4
    style ctrl fill:#b4d8ff
    style target fill:#c8e6c9
    style eye fill:#f8bbd0
```

### 4.3 Concept Diagram — Three-Layer Park Lighting Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 - HIGH - Tree Canopy]
        L3a[Moonlight from<br>30 lux, 3000K<br>IP65 pole-top]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 - MID - Path and Shrubs]
        L2a[Bollard lights<br>20-30 lux, 3000K<br>IP65, 0.9-1.2 m height]
        L2b[Post-top lantern<br>20-30 lux, 3000K<br>IP65, 3-4 m height]
    end
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 - LOW - Water and Flowers]
        L1a[In-ground uplight<br>10-20 lux, IP67]
        L1b[Submersible<br>IP68, 3000K or RGB]
    end
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    style L3 fill:#fff9c4
    style L2 fill:#dcedc8
    style L1 fill:#b3e5fc
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Floodlight Aim Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[FL at mounting height h]
        FL[Floodlight Luminaire<br>I candelas<br>Beam angle theta]
    end
    subgraph B[GEOMETRY]
        h1[Vertical h metres]:::geom
        d1[Horizontal d metres<br>d = h tan theta]:::geom
        D1[Slant distance D<br>D = h over cos theta]:::geom
    end
    subgraph C[TARGET SURFACE]
        P[Illuminance E lux<br>E = I cos cubed theta over h squared]
    end
    FL --> P
    FL -.->|beam| P
    h1 --> d1
    d1 --> D1
    D1 --> P
    classDef geom fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
```
