> **Mermaid Compilation Tip:** The above four diagrams use only alphanumeric node IDs (`L1`, `L3a`, `FL`, etc.), contain **no markdown formatting** inside double-quoted labels, and avoid reserved words as standalone node names. They will render correctly in any standard Markdown viewer that supports Mermaid (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, Notion).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET418]**
*List any six special decorative lighting techniques used for monuments, with one-line descriptions of each.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Floodlighting** — broad beam from a distance to wash the entire façade evenly.
2. **Accent / Spot Lighting** — narrow beam aimed at a specific architectural feature.
3. **Wall Washing** — broad, close placement to produce a smooth vertical light gradient.
4. **Silhouette Lighting** — light placed *behind* the subject to create a dark outline against a bright background.
5. **Grazing Light** — fixture placed very close to the surface, parallel to it, to exaggerate texture.
6. **Moonlighting** — downward lighting from a high point in a tree canopy for a dappled effect.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEET418]**
*State the recommended illuminance levels for monument floodlighting as per IS 1947 for light, medium, and dark-coloured surfaces.*

**Model Answer:**

> As per **IS 1947**, the recommended average illuminance levels for monument floodlighting are:
>
> - **Light-coloured surface:** 50 – 100 lux
> - **Medium-coloured surface:** 100 – 200 lux
> - **Dark-coloured surface (e.g., black granite):** 200 – 500 lux
>
> Darker surfaces require higher illuminance because they reflect less light (lower reflectance factor). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET418]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various decorative lighting techniques used for monuments. State the factors that influence the selection of floodlights for monument lighting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monument façade of dimensions $30 \text{ m (W)} \times 15 \text{ m (H)}$ is to be illuminated to an average illuminance of $120 \text{ lux}$. Each LED floodlight has a lumen output of $30{,}000 \text{ lm}$, utilization factor of $0.5$, and maintenance factor of $0.7$. The floodlights are mounted on poles at a height of $8 \text{ m}$ on the opposite side, at a horizontal distance of $20 \text{ m}$. Determine:
   (i) The number of floodlights required.
   (ii) The luminous intensity of each floodlight, assuming the average angle of incidence is $60°$ from vertical.
   (iii) Comment on the beam angle and mounting strategy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Decorative Techniques & Selection Factors (7 marks)**

*Techniques (sketch verbally):*
- **Floodlighting:** broad uniform wash.
- **Accent / spot:** narrow beam on features.
- **Wall washing:** gradient on plain façades.
- **Silhouette:** backlighting.
- **Grazing:** texture enhancement.
- **Cross-floodlighting:** two-sided to remove shadows.
- **Color / dynamic:** RGB for festivals.

*Selection factors of floodlights (list with brief explanations):*
- **Luminous intensity & beam angle** (narrow vs broad).
- **CCT and CRI** (warm white + high CRI for heritage).
- **IP rating** (IP 65 minimum for outdoor).
- **Wattage and luminous efficacy** (prefer ≥ 120 lm/W LED).
- **Mounting method** (yoke, spike, ground stake).
- **Glare control** (use visors, anti-glare hoods).
- **Heat dissipation** and **lamp life** (≥ 50,000 hours for LED).
- **Dimmability and control protocol** (0–10 V, DALI, DMX).
- **Body material** (die-cast aluminium, powder-coated).
- **Cost and warranty.**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Number of floodlights $N$:**

$$
N = \dfrac{A \times E}{\Phi \times \eta \times MF} = \dfrac{(30 \times 15) \times 120}{30{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.7}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{54{,}000}{10{,}500} = 5.143
$$

Since partial floodlights are not possible:

$$
\boxed{N = 6 \text{ floodlights (round up)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Luminous intensity $I$:**

Given $\theta = 60°$ from vertical, $h = 8 \text{ m}$:

$$
E = \dfrac{I \, \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}} \Rightarrow I = \dfrac{E \, h^{2}}{\cos^{3}\theta}
$$

With $\cos 60° = 0.5$ and $\cos^{3}60° = 0.125$:

$$
I = \dfrac{120 \times 8^{2}}{0.125} = \dfrac{120 \times 64}{0.125} = 61{,}440 \text{ cd}
$$

$$
\boxed{I \approx 61.4 \text{ kcd per floodlight}}
$$

**Step 3 — Beam angle & mounting strategy:**

The façade spans vertically from $-15 \text{ m}$ to $0 \text{ m}$ relative to floodlight level (assuming base aligned). Angle to top of façade:

$$
\tan\theta_{top} = \dfrac{20}{15} = 1.333 \Rightarrow \theta_{top} = 53.1°
$$

Angle to bottom of façade (looking down):

$$
\tan\theta_{bot} = \dfrac{20}{0} \to \theta_{bot} = 90°
$$

Total angular span = $90° + 53.1° = 143.1°$ — too wide for a single beam. **Strategy:** Use **3 floodlights aimed at upper half (beam ≈ 40° each)** and **3 at lower half (beam ≈ 50° each)**, all with **staggered mounting positions** to provide cross-illumination and eliminate harsh shadows.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEET418]** *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss the design considerations for decorative street lighting in a heritage town centre. Compare the requirements with conventional vehicular road lighting in terms of pole height, spacing, CCT, and illuminance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A park pathway is $500 \text{ m}$ long and $3 \text{ m}$ wide. Decorative bollard lights of mounting height $1.0 \text{ m}$ are to be installed on both sides in a **staggered arrangement**. Each bollard delivers $1{,}200 \text{ lm}$, has a utilization factor of $0.3$, and a maintenance factor of $0.8$. Determine:
   (i) The recommended spacing to achieve an average illuminance of $25 \text{ lux}$ on the path.
   (ii) The total number of bollards required.
   (iii) The wattage per bollard if the LED has an efficacy of $130 \text{ lm/W}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Decorative Street Lighting in a Heritage Town Centre vs Vehicular Road (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Heritage / Pedestrian Street | Conventional Vehicular Road |
|---|---|---|
| **Pole height** | 3 – 5 m (low, human-scale) | 8 – 12 m (high, traffic-scale) |
| **Pole style** | Decorative cast-iron heritage post, post-top lantern | Standard octagonal / tubular steel pole, side-entry luminaire |
| **Spacing** | 8 – 15 m | 25 – 35 m |
| **CCT** | 2700 – 3000 K (warm) | 4000 – 5000 K (neutral) |
| **CRI** | ≥ 80 (for accurate stone colour) | ≥ 70 acceptable |
| **Illuminance E_avg** | 10 – 20 lux | 15 – 30 lux (varies by road class) |
| **Uniformity $U_0$** | ≥ 0.4 | ≥ 0.4 |
| **Glare control** | Strict (pedestrian eye level) | Standard shielding |
| **Light pollution** | Use full-cutoff optics | Use full-cutoff optics |
| **Control** | Astronomical timer, dimming at night | Astronomical timer, step-dimming |
| **Aesthetics** | Symmetry, decorative brackets, heritage finish | Functional, minimal decoration |
| **IP rating** | IP 65 | IP 65 / IP 66 |

**Additional design considerations for heritage street lighting:**
- Pole colour and finish to match heritage architecture (matte black, dark green, bronze).
- Avoid overhead wiring (use underground cabling).
- Special care for **spill light** onto residential windows.
- Coordination with municipal heritage committee.

**(b) Park Bollard Lighting Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Spacing calculation:**

Total area of pathway:
$$
A = L \times W = 500 \times 3 = 1{,}500 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Total lumens required:
$$
\Phi_{req} = A \times E = 1{,}500 \times 25 = 37{,}500 \text{ lm}
$$

Total lumens delivered per bollard (accounting for $\eta$ and $MF$):
$$
\Phi_{eff} = 1{,}200 \times 0.3 \times 0.8 = 288 \text{ lm}
$$

Total number of bollards (both sides combined):
$$
N_{total} = \dfrac{\Phi_{req}}{\Phi_{eff}} = \dfrac{37{,}500}{288} = 130.2
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{total} \approx 130 \text{ bollards}}
$$

Since installation is **staggered on both sides** (500 m length), bollards per side = $130/2 = 65$.

Spacing along each side:
$$
S = \dfrac{500 \text{ m}}{65} = 7.69 \text{ m} \approx 7.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 7.5 \text{ m (per side)}}
$$

(Consistent with $S \approx 2.5 H$ for $H = 1 \text{ m}$ → $S = 2.5 \text{ m}$ on one side; staggered → effective $S = 5 \text{ m}$ on path centre line. The 7.5 m per side achieves the same centreline coverage as 3.75 m on one side. **Acceptable.**)

**Step 2 — Number of bollards (final):** $N = 130$ **(already computed)**.

**Step 3 — Wattage per bollard:**

$$
P = \dfrac{\Phi}{\eta_{lum}} = \dfrac{1{,}200 \text{ lm}}{130 \text{ lm/W}} = 9.23 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 9 \text{ W per bollard (use standard 10 W LED bollard)}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 4 — Top 7 Mistakes**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $\cos^{3}\theta$ in the illuminance formula** — Using $E = I/h^2$ when the floodlight is *angled* is the **#1 mistake**. Always check whether the floodlight is aimed straight down (then $\cos^{3}\theta = 1$) or at an angle. **2 marks lost per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Rounding $N$ (number of floodlights) downward** — Always round **up**. Never write $N = 5.14$ as your final answer; round to $6$ and explain why partial luminaires are impossible.
>
> 3. **Omitting utilization factor $\eta$ and maintenance factor $MF$** in the $N = AE / (\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF)$ formula. Both must be in the denominator. **2–3 marks lost per omission.**
>
> 4. **Confusing beam angle conventions** — A "30° beam" can mean *full* beam angle ($2\alpha = 30°$, so $\alpha = 15°$) or *half* beam angle ($\alpha = 30°$). Always specify which convention you are using.
>
> 5. **Wrong unit for illuminance** — luminous flux is in **lumens**, area in **m²**, illuminance in **lux** (which equals lm/m²). Mixing units (e.g., using cm² for area) loses a full mark.
>
> 6. **Ignoring aesthetic considerations in a 14-mark design question** — A pure numerical answer without any comment on *aesthetic impact, mounting position, cross-floodlighting, CCT/CRI choice, or IP rating* will lose 2–3 marks on the "design" part of the question.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the maintenance factor decay** — Outdoor installations lose 20–30% of light over 1–2 years due to dust and lamp lumen depreciation. KTU examiners expect $MF \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ to be applied.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 4 Revision — Aesthetic Lighting**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Aesthetic lighting = planned use of light for visual delight + functional safety.
>
> ✅ **Three Pillars:** Functional, Aesthetic, Environmental/Economic.
>
> ✅ **Ten Techniques (must-memorize):** Flood, Accent/Spot, Wall Wash, Silhouette, Grazing, Moonlight, Underwater, Bollard, In-ground, Festoon.
>
> ✅ **Master Formula:** $E = I \cos^{3}\theta / h^{2}$ — and **derive it on paper** at least once.
>
> ✅ **Beam Spread:** $B = 2 h \tan(\theta/2)$ for symmetric vertical aim.
>
> ✅ **Number of Floodlights:** $N = A \cdot E / (\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot MF)$; always round **up**.
>
> ✅ **Pole Spacing Rule:** $S = 2.5 H$ (paths), $S = 3.5 H$ (vehicular), $S \approx 4 H$ (staggered).
>
> ✅ **CCT Bands:** 2700–3000 K (heritage, parks), 3500–4500 K (modern), 5000–6500 K (commercial).
>
> ✅ **CRI ≥ 80** for monuments and heritage structures; **CRI ≥ 70** acceptable for streets.
>
> ✅ **IP Ratings:** IP 65 (outdoor general), IP 67 (in-ground), **IP 68 (submersible fountains)**.
>
> ✅ **Monument Illuminance (IS 1947):** Light surface 50–100 lux, medium 100–200 lux, dark 200–500 lux.
>
> ✅ **Mounting Rule of Thumb:** Floodlight at **1/3 to 1/2** the height of monument, on the *opposite side* of viewer, **two-sided cross-aim** to remove shadows.
>
> ✅ **Park Lighting 3 Layers:** High (canopy 30–50 lux), Mid (paths 20–30 lux), Low (water 10–20 lux).
>
> ✅ **Street Lighting:** Pedestrian decorative 3–5 m, vehicular 8–12 m; uniformity $U_0 \geq 0.4$.
>
> ✅ **LED Efficacy:** Modern LED 120–180 lm/W; always prefer over HID.
>
> ✅ **Glare:** Pedestrian zones — use **full-cutoff**, **low-glare** luminaires with frosted diffusers.
>
> ✅ **Light Pollution Control:** No uplight from regular streetlights; aim *downward only* (full cutoff optics).
>
> ✅ **Festoon/Street Decor:** Low-voltage 24 V LED belts, IP 65, mounted between poles with 5–8 m sag.
>
> ✅ **Governing Standards to Quote:** IS 1944, IS 1947, IS 10322, IS 16108, CIE 115, CIE 154, NBC 2016.
>
> ✅ **Numerical Hygiene:** Units must be SI (m, lm, lux, cd, W). Always state assumed values of $\eta$ and $MF$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Considerations in Public Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Considerations in Public Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety in Public Lighting** refers to the comprehensive set of engineering design practices, electrical protective measures, photometric considerations, mechanical integrity provisions, and maintenance protocols implemented in street lighting, area lighting, and roadway lighting installations to ensure the safety of the public, maintenance personnel, and the luminaires themselves, while preventing electrical shock, fire hazards, structural failure, glare-induced accidents, and photobiological harm.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET418 – Illumination Technology)**, safety considerations are categorized into the following key domains:

1. **Electrical Safety** – Earthing, insulation, surge protection, and class of luminaire.
2. **Photometric Safety** – Glare control, uniformity, and visibility.
3. **Mechanical/Structural Safety** – Wind loading, pole foundation, impact resistance.
4. **Photobiological Safety** – Blue light hazard, UV emission (per IEC 62471 / IS 16108).
5. **Environmental & IP Safety** – Ingress Protection (IP) rating for outdoor luminaires.
6. **Maintenance Safety** – Lock-out / Tag-out (LOTO), safe access, and isolation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Highway Streetlight System"**

Imagine a **dark coastal highway at 2:00 AM**. A driver is travelling at **80 km/h** with a truck approaching from the opposite direction. The streetlight pole beside the road is doing **three simultaneous jobs**:

- **Lighting the road** so the driver can see obstacles (visibility).
- **Not blinding the driver** with the oncoming truck's headlights or the streetlight itself (glare control).
- **Surviving a sudden monsoon, salt-spray corrosion, and a stray kite string touching the live wire** (electrical & environmental safety).

If **any one** of these fails, the system becomes *unsafe*. Safety considerations in public lighting are essentially the **engineering "insurance policy"** that guarantees all three jobs are done **simultaneously and reliably for 15–25 years**, with minimal human intervention.

Think of public lighting safety as a **three-legged stool**:
- **Leg 1 – Electrical Leg** (shock, fire, surge protection)
- **Leg 2 – Photometric Leg** (visibility without glare)
- **Leg 3 – Structural Leg** (pole stays upright, luminaire stays sealed)

If one leg breaks, the whole system collapses — leading to accidents, electrocution, or pole collapse during storms.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **International & Indian Standards Governing Public Lighting Safety:**

| Standard | Governing Body | Scope |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| **IS 10322 (Part 1 / Sec 1)** | BIS, India | Luminaires – General safety requirements |
| **IEC 60598-1** | IEC | Luminaires – General safety & tests |
| **IEC 60598-2-3** | IEC | Luminaires for road & street lighting |
| **IEC 62471 / IS 16108** | IEC / BIS | Photobiological safety of lamps |
| **IEC 61643-11** | IEC | Surge Protective Devices (SPDs) |
| **IS 3043** | BIS | Code of practice for earthing |
| **IEC 62262 (IK code)** | IEC | Degree of mechanical impact protection |
| **IS 1944 / IS 13201** | BIS | Code of practice for road lighting |
| **NBC 2016 (India)** | BIS / Govt. of India | National Building Code – electrical safety |

**Critical Safety Metrics in Bold:**
- **IP Rating** (e.g., **IP66** for outdoor streetlights)
- **IK Rating** (e.g., **IK08** for impact resistance)
- **Earth Resistance:** $\leq \mathbf{5\ \Omega}$ (for lighting poles per IS 3043)
- **Class of Luminaire:** **Class I** (earthed) or **Class II** (double insulated)
- **Touch Voltage Limit:** $\leq \mathbf{50\ V\ AC}$ / $\leq \mathbf{120\ V\ DC}$ (per IEC 60364)
- **Glare Index (TI) Threshold:** **TI $\leq$ 10** (acceptable), **TI $\leq$ 20** (just acceptable)
- **Minimum Luminance for Roads:** $\mathbf{0.5\ to\ 2.0\ cd/m^2}$ (depending on road class)

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU 2024 Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Relevance:**
> - Frequently asked as **Part A (3-mark)** definition/normative questions.
> - Appears as **Part B (14-mark)** design-oriented questions combining IP rating, earthing, and class of luminaire.
> - Direct questions on **glare index threshold, IP code digits, and earthing resistance** are common.
> - Questions linking **LED photobiological hazard (blue light)** are trending in 2024 scheme.

This topic acts as a **bridge** between electrical engineering fundamentals (earthing, insulation), lighting design (glare, uniformity), and regulatory compliance (IS standards) — making it a high-yield area for KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Safety Framework

Public lighting safety can be broken down into **six interrelated pillars**. Each pillar addresses a distinct failure mode.

### Pillar 1: Electrical Safety

**Objective:** Prevent electric shock, fire, and damage to luminaires from abnormal voltages.

**Key Engineering Provisions:**

- **Earthing (Grounding):** All metal parts of the luminaire, pole, and junction box must be bonded to earth. The **earth electrode resistance** must be $\leq 5\ \Omega$ for lighting installations (as per IS 3043). For high-mast installations, the value is often tightened to $\leq 1\ \Omega$.

- **Double Insulation (Class II Luminaire):** A reinforced insulation barrier prevents any live part from being accessible, even if basic insulation fails. Marked with the **square-within-square** symbol $\square$.

- **Residual Current Device (RCD / RCCB):** Rated **30 mA** for street lighting circuits (additional protection against indirect contact). Trip time $\leq 40\ \text{ms}$ at $5 \times I_{\Delta n}$.

- **Surge Protective Device (SPD):** Type 2 SPD (test wave $8/20\ \mu\text{s}$) installed at the base of each pole or distribution box. Withstands surge current of **10 kA** minimum. For high-mast / coastal / lightning-prone zones, **Type 1+2 combined SPD (10/350 µs)** is used.

- **Insulation Resistance:** $\geq \mathbf{1\ M\Omega}$ (measured at 500 V DC between live conductors and earth).

- **Loop Impedance:** Low enough so that any earth fault produces a current that **trips the MCB within 0.4 s** (for final circuits).

---

### Pillar 2: Photometric Safety — Glare Control

**Objective:** Maintain visibility without inducing visual discomfort or disability that leads to accidents.

**Glare is quantified by the Threshold Increment (TI):**

$$
TI = 65 \cdot \frac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}}
$$

where:
- $L_v$ = **veiling luminance** $\left(\text{cd/m}^2\right)$, induced by the luminaire in the eye of the observer
- $L_{av}$ = **average road surface luminance** $\left(\text{cd/m}^2\right)$

**Acceptance Criteria (per IS 1944 / CIE 140):**

| Road Class | Maximum Permissible TI (%) |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Motorway / High-speed | $\leq 10$ |
| Main urban roads | $\leq 20$ |
| Residential / Local streets | $\leq 20$ – $30$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disability Glare vs. Discomfort Glare:**
> - **Disability Glare** reduces visual acuity (e.g., oncoming headlights). Controlled by **cut-off optics** (full cut-off, semi cut-off).
> - **Discomfort Glare** causes annoyance without necessarily reducing visibility. Controlled by **luminaire mounting height (MH)** and **overhang**.

---

### Pillar 3: Mechanical / Structural Safety

**Objective:** Ensure the pole, luminaire, and bracket withstand wind, vibration, and accidental impact.

- **Wind Loading Calculation (per IS 875 Part 3):**
  The basic wind speed $V_b$ for Kerala = **39 m/s** (Zone V as per IS 875). Design wind pressure:

$$
P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2
$$

where $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$ (risk, terrain, and topography factors).

- **Pole Foundation Depth:** Typically **1/6 to 1/10** of pole height above ground. For a **10 m pole**, foundation depth $\geq 1.5\ \text{m}$.

- **IK Rating (per IEC 62262):** Defines impact energy in joules.
  - **IK07 = 2 J**, **IK08 = 5 J**, **IK09 = 10 J**, **IK10 = 20 J**.
  - Public lighting poles in vehicle-accessible zones require **IK08 minimum**.

---

### Pillar 4: Photobiological Safety (LED & Lamp Hazard)

**Objective:** Prevent retinal damage from excessive blue light, especially with modern high-power white LEDs.

- **Standard:** **IEC 62471** (Photobiological safety of lamps and lamp systems) / **IS 16108**.
- **Risk Groups:**
  - **RG0 (Exempt):** No photobiological hazard under normal use.
  - **RG1 (Low Risk):** Safe for general use, but basic precaution needed.
  - **RG2 (Moderate Risk):** Eye damage possible from direct staring — warning label required.
  - **RG3 (High Risk):** Hazardous even for momentary exposure.
- **Most streetlight LEDs fall in RG0 / RG1**, but the **CCT (Correlated Color Temperature)** matters:
  - **CCT $\leq 3000$ K** (warm white) → safer (lower blue content).
  - **CCT $\geq 5000$ K** (cool white) → higher blue light hazard.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Examiners often ask *"Why is 3000 K preferred over 6500 K for residential street lighting?"* — the answer is photobiological safety + reduced skyglow.

---

### Pillar 5: Environmental / IP Safety

**Objective:** Prevent ingress of dust and water, which can cause short circuits, corrosion, and electrocution.

**IP Code (per IEC 60529):** Two digits — **IP _AB_**
- **A = Solid particle protection** (0–6)
- **B = Liquid ingress protection** (0–9K)

| IP Rating | Protection Level | Typical Use |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| **IP23** | Rain-proof (spray up to 60°) | Covered outdoor areas |
| **IP54** | Dust-protected + splashing water | Pedestrian underpasses |
| **IP65** | Dust-tight + jet-proof | Standard streetlights |
| **IP66** | Dust-tight + powerful jet-proof | Coastal / industrial zones |
| **IP67** | Dust-tight + temporary immersion | Flooded zones / underpasses |
| **IP68** | Dust-tight + continuous immersion | Underwater / fountains |

> **Recommended for Kerala public lighting:** **IP66** (heavy monsoon) or **IP67** for flood-prone areas.

---

### Pillar 6: Maintenance Safety

**Objective:** Protect maintenance workers from electrocution, falls, and traffic accidents.

- **Lock-Out / Tag-Out (LOTO):** Mandatory isolation of supply at distribution box before any work.
- **Use of **insulated tools**, **insulating mats**, and **PPE** (helmet, gloves, harness for pole climbing).**
- **Safe approach distance:** $\geq 0.5\ \text{m}$ from a 230 V live part; $\geq 1\ m$ from 11 kV lines (per Indian Electricity Rules).
- **Pole Climbing:** Only trained personnel with **pole-climbing belt** and **safety harness** anchored to a separate safety line.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it appears in nearly every KTU Illumination paper.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | KTU Use |
|---|-------------------|----------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Glare Threshold Increment** | $TI = 65 \cdot \dfrac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}}$ | $L_v, L_{av}$ in $\text{cd/m}^2$ | Glare control |
| 2 | **Threshold Increment Limit** | $TI \leq 10$ (high-speed), $\leq 20$ (urban) | % | Design check |
| 3 | **Earth Electrode Resistance (general)** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $\rho$ = soil resistivity ($\Omega\cdot$m), $L$ = rod length (m), $d$ = rod dia (m) | Earthing design |
| 4 | **Earth Resistance Limit** | $R \leq 5\ \Omega$ (lighting), $\leq 1\ \Omega$ (high-mast) | $\Omega$ | Compliance |
| 5 | **Touch Voltage Limit** | $V_t \leq 50\ \text{V AC}$, $\leq 120\ \text{V DC}$ | V | Safety threshold |
| 6 | **Disconnection Time (Final Circuit)** | $t \leq 0.4\ \text{s}$ (230 V) | s | MCB coordination |
| 7 | **Insulation Resistance** | $R_{iso} \geq 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ at 500 V DC | $\text{M}\Omega$ | Insulation test |
| 8 | **Wind Pressure on Pole** | $P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ | $P_z$ in $\text{N/m}^2$, $V_z$ in m/s | Pole design |
| 9 | **Wind Force on Luminaire** | $F = P_z \cdot A \cdot C_d$ | $F$ in N, $A$ = area (m²), $C_d$ = drag coefficient | Structural check |
| 10 | **Overhang / MH Relation** | $OH \leq 0.25 \cdot MH$ | m, m | Glare & uniformity |
| 11 | **SPD Surge Current** | $I_{imp} = 10\ \text{kA}$ (Type 1), $I_n = 5\ \text{kA}$ (Type 2) | kA, 8/20 µs wave | Surge protection |
| 12 | **Minimum Luminance (Roads)** | $L_{av} = 0.5$ to $2.0\ \text{cd/m}^2$ | $\text{cd/m}^2$ | Road class |
| 13 | **RCD Trip Current** | $I_{\Delta n} = 30\ \text{mA}$ (street lighting) | mA | Shock protection |
| 14 | **Minimum Lumen per Watt (LED)** | $\eta \geq 100\ \text{lm/W}$ (modern spec) | $\text{lm/W}$ | Energy + safety |
| 15 | **Photobiological RG Limit** | $L_B \leq 10^4\ \text{cd/m}^2$ (RG1) | $\text{cd/m}^2$ | LED safety |

> **Mnemonic to remember IP code:** *"Ingress Protection" → First digit = **S**olid, Second digit = **W**ater (SW).*

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this knowledge is essential in the field:**

1. **Municipal Smart-City Projects (e.g., Kochi Smart City):** Engineers must design streetlight networks compliant with IS 1944 + IEC 60598-2-3, including SPDs, IP66 enclosures, and Class I earthing.

2. **Accident Forensics:** A vehicle accident at night often hinges on whether the **glare index (TI)** was within limits, and whether the **luminance uniformity ($U_0$)** met the **0.4 minimum** (per CIE 115).

3. **Insurance & Liability:** Pole collapse or electrocution lawsuits often cite **non-compliance with IS 3043** (earthing) or **absence of RCD**.

4. **Energy + Safety Synergy:** Modern LED + smart controls + adaptive dimming must NOT compromise safety. A 50% dimmed road at 3 AM must still meet **minimum luminance** for safety (e.g., 0.3 cd/m² on residential streets).

5. **Photobiological Risk in Tunnels:** Sodium-vapour → LED conversion in tunnels raises blue-light hazard — design must include **RG0 or RG1 luminaires** with **4000 K maximum CCT**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Earth Electrode Resistance

> **Problem:** A 3 m long, 25 mm diameter copper-clad steel rod is driven vertically into the ground. The soil resistivity is $\rho = 100\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$. Calculate the **earth electrode resistance to remote earth**.

### Step 1 — Recall the Standard Formula for a Vertical Rod Electrode

For a single vertical rod electrode of length $L$ and diameter $d$, the resistance to remote earth is given by:

$$
R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right)
$$

This formula is derived by treating the rod as a hemispherical current-discharge electrode in homogeneous soil, integrated over the surface area of current flow.

### Step 2 — Identify the Given Quantities

From the problem statement:
- $\rho = 100\ \Omega \cdot \text{m}$ (soil resistivity)
- $L = 3\ \text{m}$ (rod length)
- $d = 25\ \text{mm} = 0.025\ \text{m}$ (rod diameter)

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values into the Formula

$$
R = \frac{100}{2 \pi \times 3} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.025}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Denominator of the First Term

$$
2 \pi \times 3 = 6 \pi \approx 18.8496
$$

So:

$$
R = \frac{100}{18.8496} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{12}{0.025}\right)
$$

$$
R = 5.3052 \cdot \ln(480)
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Natural Logarithm

$$
\ln(480) = \ln(4.8 \times 10^2) = \ln(4.8) + 2 \ln(10) = 1.5686 + 4.6052 = 6.1738
$$

### Step 6 — Multiply the Two Terms

$$
R = 5.3052 \times 6.1738 \approx 32.75\ \Omega
$$

### Step 7 — Compare Against the KTU / IS 3043 Limit

For public lighting poles, the **required earth resistance is $\leq 5\ \Omega$**.

> **Conclusion:** A **single rod** gives $R \approx 32.75\ \Omega$, which is **far above the limit**. To achieve $\leq 5\ \Omega$, we must either:
> - (a) Use **multiple rods in parallel** (e.g., 8 rods in parallel → $R \approx 4.1\ \Omega$), **or**
> - (b) Use a **chemical/ground enhancement material (GEM)** to lower $\rho_{\text{eff}}$, **or**
> - (c) Increase the rod length significantly (rod length is logarithmic, so doubling length has limited effect).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common sub-question in 14-mark problems is: *"If $R$ exceeds 5 Ω, suggest two remedial measures."* Always mention **parallel rods** + **soil treatment with salt/charcoal/GEM**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Glare Threshold Increment (TI)

> **Problem:** A street lighting installation has the following parameters: veiling luminance $L_v = 0.4\ \text{cd/m}^2$ and average road surface luminance $L_{av} = 1.2\ \text{cd/m}^2$. Determine the **Threshold Increment (TI)** and **comment on glare safety**.

### Step 1 — Recall the TI Formula (CIE 140)

$$
TI = 65 \cdot \frac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}}
$$

This formula expresses the percentage increase in the **contrast threshold** required for an object to be just visible, due to the presence of the glare source.

### Step 2 — Compute $L_{av}^{0.8}$

$$
L_{av}^{0.8} = (1.2)^{0.8}
$$

Using the identity $a^b = e^{b \ln a}$:

$$
(1.2)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \cdot \ln(1.2)} = e^{0.8 \times 0.1823} = e^{0.1458} \approx 1.1570
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Ratio

$$
\frac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}} = \frac{0.4}{1.1570} \approx 0.3457
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by 65

$$
TI = 65 \times 0.3457 \approx 22.47\ \%
$$

### Step 5 — Compare with Acceptable Limits

| Road Class | TI Limit (%) | Result |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| High-speed / Motorway | $\leq 10$ | ✗ Fails (exceeds by 12.47%) |
| Main urban road | $\leq 20$ | ✗ Fails (exceeds by 2.47%) |
| Local / Residential | $\leq 30$ | ✓ Passes |

### Step 6 — Engineering Recommendation

To reduce TI, the designer should:
- **Increase mounting height (MH)** → reduces $L_v$.
- **Use full cut-off luminaires** (no light above $90°$).
- **Increase pole spacing only if uniformity $U_0$ is maintained**.
- **Use lower-lumen luminaires** (with same spacing and MH).

> **Result:** $TI \approx 22.5\%$ — **suitable for residential streets but unsafe for high-speed roads**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Wind Force on Streetlight Pole

> **Problem:** A **10 m** streetlight pole supports a luminaire of **0.25 m² projected area**. The design wind speed at the site is $V_z = 39\ \text{m/s}$ and the drag coefficient $C_d = 1.2$. Calculate the **wind force on the luminaire**.

### Step 1 — Recall the Wind Pressure Formula (IS 875 Part 3)

$$
P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2
$$

The constant **0.6** comes from $\frac{1}{2} \rho_{air}$ where $\rho_{air} = 1.226\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

### Step 2 — Compute $V_z^2$

$$
V_z^2 = 39 \times 39 = 1521\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $P_z$

$$
P_z = 0.6 \times 1521 = 912.6\ \text{N/m}^2
$$

### Step 4 — Recall the Wind Force Formula

$$
F = P_z \cdot A \cdot C_d
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Values

$$
F = 912.6 \times 0.25 \times 1.2
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Stepwise

$$
912.6 \times 0.25 = 228.15
$$

$$
228.15 \times 1.2 = 273.78\ \text{N}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to kgf (for field reference)

$$
F = \frac{273.78}{9.81} \approx 27.9\ \text{kgf}
$$

> **Result:** The luminaire experiences approximately **274 N** of wind force. The pole + bracket design must withstand this force with a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0**, i.e., the actual design strength must be $\geq 411\ \text{N}$ minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Specific Note:** Coastal districts (Alappuzha, Ernakulam, Trivandrum) fall under **Wind Zone V (50 m/s basic wind speed)** — pole design must use $V_b = 50\ \text{m/s}$, not 39 m/s. Always check the IS 875 wind zone map for the exact site.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Safety Failure Modes & Their Design Solutions

| # | Failure Mode | Cause | Consequence | Design Solution | KTU 2024 Weightage |
|---|-------------|-------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Electric shock to pedestrian** | Loss of earth continuity, broken insulation | Fatal injury | Earth resistance $\leq 5\ \Omega$, RCD 30 mA, Class II luminaire | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | **Glare-induced road accident** | Low mounting height, non-cut-off optics | Driver disability glare | $TI \leq 10$–$20\%$, full cut-off, $OH \leq 0.25 MH$ | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | **Pole collapse in storm** | Insufficient foundation or wall thickness | Property damage, fatality | Foundation $\geq L/6$, IS 875 wind load, hot-dip galvanizing | ★★★★ |
| 4 | **LED blue-light retinal hazard** | High CCT (6500 K) LED used near residential | Long-term retinal stress | $CCT \leq 4000\ \text{K}$ (residential), RG0 / RG1 verified | ★★★★ |
| 5 | **Water ingress & short-circuit** | IP rating too low for site | Pole fires, outages | IP66 / IP67 for Kerala monsoon | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | **Lightning surge damage** | Direct or indirect lightning strike | Driver electronics destroyed | Type 1+2 SPD at pole base, equipotential bonding | ★★★ |
| 7 | **Maintenance worker electrocution** | No LOTO, no PPE | Fatal | LOTO, insulated tools, harness, trained personnel | ★★★ |
| 8 | **Insulation degradation over time** | Heat, UV, salt-spray, vermin | Earth fault after 8–10 years | Use **Class II** luminaire + UV-resistant cable + annual IR test | ★★★ |
| 9 | **Pole base corrosion** | Soil chemistry, moisture | Pole topples | Hot-dip galvanizing $\geq 85\ \mu\text{m}$, anti-corrosion paint | ★★ |
| 10 | **Uniformity failure (dark spots)** | Pole spacing too large | Pedestrian accident | Maintain $U_0 = L_{min}/L_{av} \geq 0.4$ | ★★★★ |
| 11 | **Skyglow / light trespass** | Upward light spill | Disturbs fauna, sleep | Full cut-off + CCT $\leq 3000\ \text{K}$ near residential | ★★ |
| 12 | **Vehicle impact on pole** | Pole placed too close to carriageway | Pole collapse, accident | Setback $\geq 0.6\ \text{m}$ from kerb, frangible/breakaway design | ★★★ |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — IP Rating Identification

> **Problem:** A streetlight luminaire is marked **IP66**. Explain each digit in detail and state whether it is **suitable for a coastal Kerala outdoor installation**.

### Solution:

**Digit 1 = 6** (per IEC 60529):
- "**Dust-tight**" — complete protection against dust ingress. No dust can penetrate the enclosure.

**Digit 2 = 6** (per IEC 60529):
- "**Powerful water jets**" — protection against water projected in **powerful jets** (12.5 mm nozzle, 100 litres/min, 100 kPa, from 3 m distance) for at least 3 minutes.

**Verdict for Kerala coastal outdoor installation:**
✓ **IP66 is suitable** for general Kerala street lighting (heavy monsoon, dust).
✓ For **flood-prone / underpass zones** → use **IP67** (temporary immersion up to 1 m).
✗ For **underwater fountains** → use **IP68**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **IP65** (jet-proof) vs. **IP66** (powerful jet-proof). The difference is water pressure and nozzle size. For Kerala's torrential rain, **IP66 minimum** is recommended.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Each Part A question is worth 3 marks. Answers should be precise, with a short definition, the relevant standard, and one line of explanation.*

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Ingress Protection (IP) code as per IEC 60529. Explain the significance of IP66 for a streetlight luminaire in Kerala.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The IP code is a two-digit classification system per IEC 60529, where the first digit indicates protection against **solid foreign objects** (0–6) and the second digit indicates protection against **water ingress** (0–9K).

- **IP66 meaning (1 mark):**
  - **First digit '6'** → Dust-tight. No ingress of dust.
  - **Second digit '6'** → Protection against **powerful water jets** (12.5 mm nozzle, 100 L/min, 100 kPa, 3 m distance, 3 minutes).

- **Significance for Kerala (1 mark):** Kerala experiences heavy monsoon rainfall, high humidity, and coastal salt-spray. IP66 ensures the luminaire remains safe and operational under these conditions, preventing **short-circuits, corrosion, and electrocution hazards**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Threshold Increment (TI) in street lighting? State the maximum permissible values for a motorway and a residential street as per CIE 140 / IS 1944.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Threshold Increment (TI) is a measure of **disability glare** in road lighting, defined as the **percentage increase in the contrast threshold** required for an object to be just visible, due to the presence of a glare source. It is given by $TI = 65 \cdot \dfrac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}}$ where $L_v$ is the veiling luminance and $L_{av}$ is the average road luminance.

- **Permissible values (1.5 marks):**
  - **Motorway (high-speed):** $TI \leq 10\%$
  - **Residential street:** $TI \leq 20$ to $30\%$ (typically $\leq 20\%$ for comfort)

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with internal choice. The model solutions include complete sub-part (a) and (b) derivations.*

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**A.** *(a)* Explain the **six pillars of public lighting safety** with relevant IS/IEC standards. *(7 marks)*

**A.** *(b)* A streetlight installation is to be designed for a **high-speed motorway** in Kerala. The following data is given:
- Veiling luminance $L_v = 0.25\ \text{cd/m}^2$
- Average road luminance $L_{av} = 2.0\ \text{cd/m}^2$
- Pole mounting height $MH = 12\ \text{m}$, spacing $S = 36\ \text{m}$, overhang $OH = 2.5\ \text{m}$
- Soil resistivity $\rho = 80\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, earth rod length $L = 3\ \text{m}$, diameter $d = 25\ \text{mm}$
- Basic wind speed $V_b = 50\ \text{m/s}$ (Kerala coastal Zone V)

Calculate:
1. **Threshold Increment (TI)** and verify glare safety.
2. **Earth electrode resistance** for a single rod and recommend remedial measures if it exceeds 5 Ω.
3. **Wind force on the luminaire** (assume area $A = 0.3\ \text{m}^2$, $C_d = 1.2$).
4. **Comment on MH vs S relationship and overhang ratio.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Six Pillars of Public Lighting Safety (7 marks):**

| Pillar | Scope | Relevant Standard |
|--------|-------|-------------------|
| 1. **Electrical Safety** | Earthing, RCD, Class I/II, SPD | IS 3043, IEC 60364 |
| 2. **Photometric Safety** | Glare (TI), uniformity, cut-off | IS 1944, CIE 140 |
| 3. **Mechanical/Structural Safety** | Wind load, foundation, IK rating | IS 875 Part 3, IEC 62262 |
| 4. **Photobiological Safety** | Blue light, UV, risk groups | IEC 62471 / IS 16108 |
| 5. **Environmental IP Safety** | Dust + water ingress | IEC 60529 (IP code) |
| 6. **Maintenance Safety** | LOTO, PPE, training | IS 5216, Indian Electricity Rules |

*(Each pillar explained in 1 mark, with 1 mark for the standard reference.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions (7 marks):**

**Sub-part 1: Threshold Increment (TI) — 2 marks**

$$
TI = 65 \cdot \frac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}} = 65 \cdot \frac{0.25}{(2.0)^{0.8}}
$$

Compute $L_{av}^{0.8} = (2.0)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \ln 2} = e^{0.8 \times 0.6931} = e^{0.5545} \approx 1.7411$

$$
TI = 65 \times \frac{0.25}{1.7411} = 65 \times 0.1436 \approx 9.33\ \%
$$

> **Verdict:** $TI = 9.33\% \leq 10\%$ → ✓ **Glare safety PASSES** for high-speed motorway.

**Sub-part 2: Earth Electrode Resistance — 2 marks**

$$
R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) = \frac{80}{2 \pi \times 3} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.025}\right)
$$

$$
R = \frac{80}{18.8496} \cdot \ln(480) = 4.244 \times 6.1738 \approx 26.20\ \Omega
$$

> **Conclusion:** $R = 26.20\ \Omega \gg 5\ \Omega$ → **Fails** for a single rod.
> **Remedies (any two, 1 mark):**
> 1. Use **multiple parallel rods** (8 rods in parallel → $R \approx 3.27\ \Omega$ ✓)
> 2. Use **ground enhancement material (GEM)** to lower $\rho_{\text{eff}}$
> 3. Use **plate electrode** (lower resistance than rod)

**Sub-part 3: Wind Force on Luminaire — 1.5 marks**

First, compute wind pressure (taking $V_z \approx V_b = 50\ \text{m/s}$ for design simplicity, neglecting $k$-factors):

$$
P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 = 0.6 \times 50^2 = 0.6 \times 2500 = 1500\ \text{N/m}^2
$$

Then wind force:

$$
F = P_z \cdot A \cdot C_d = 1500 \times 0.3 \times 1.2 = 540\ \text{N}
$$

> **Result:** Wind force on luminaire $\approx 540\ \text{N}$. The pole-bracket assembly must be designed for at least $540 \times 1.5 = 810\ \text{N}$ (with 1.5 safety factor).

**Sub-part 4: MH vs S and Overhang Comment — 1.5 marks**

- **MH vs S relationship:** $S = 36\ \text{m}$, $MH = 12\ \text{m}$ → $S/MH = 3.0$ → ✓ Within the typical 3 to 4 range. Good uniformity expected.
- **Overhang ratio:** $OH = 2.5\ \text{m}$, $MH = 12\ \text{m}$ → $OH/MH = 0.208$ → ✓ Within the recommended $\leq 0.25$ (so glare is controlled).
- **Verdict:** Design satisfies both geometric safety constraints.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**B.** *(a)* Discuss in detail the **photobiological safety of LED streetlights** as per **IEC 62471 / IS 16108**. Compare the suitability of **3000 K, 4000 K, and 6500 K CCT** LEDs for residential, urban arterial, and tunnel applications. *(7 marks)*

**B.** *(b)* For a **streetlight luminaire** installed in a coastal area:
- Class of luminaire: **Class I**
- Pole height: 9 m
- Soil resistivity: $\rho = 120\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$
- Single earth rod: 2.5 m long, 20 mm diameter
- Design wind speed: 47 m/s
- Luminaire area: 0.28 m², drag coefficient: 1.2
- Required TI limit: 20%; measured $L_v = 0.5\ \text{cd/m}^2$, $L_{av} = 1.0\ \text{cd/m}^2$

Calculate:
1. **Whether a single earth rod meets the $\leq 5\ \Omega$ limit.** Suggest **two improvements** if not.
2. **Wind force on the luminaire.**
3. **Threshold Increment** and verify glare safety.
4. **IP rating recommendation** with justification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Photobiological Safety of LEDs (7 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Photobiological safety refers to protection of the **eye and skin** from optical radiation (UV, visible, IR) emitted by lamps. **IEC 62471 / IS 16108** classifies lamps into four **Risk Groups (RG):**
- **RG0 (Exempt):** No hazard.
- **RG1 (Low Risk):** Safe under normal use.
- **RG2 (Moderate Risk):** Hazard from direct staring (warning label required).
- **RG3 (High Risk):** Hazard even from momentary exposure.

**Key Hazards (2 marks):**
- **Retinal blue-light hazard (300–400 nm blue component):** Dominant in high-CCT LEDs.
- **UV hazard:** Negligible in white LEDs (phosphor-converted).
- **IR thermal hazard:** Low for LEDs.

**CCT Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| CCT | Blue Content | Typical Risk Group | Best Application |
|-----|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **3000 K (Warm White)** | Low | RG0 | **Residential streets** — safest, low skyglow, sleep-friendly |
| **4000 K (Neutral White)** | Moderate | RG0 – RG1 | **Urban arterial roads** — good visibility, acceptable hazard |
| **6500 K (Daylight)** | High | RG1 – RG2 | **Tunnels / Industrial** — maximum visibility, but glare & blue-hazard concern |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** For public lighting, **lower CCT (3000–4000 K) is preferred** for photobiological safety and reduced skyglow, even though higher CCT gives marginally better visual acuity.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions (7 marks):**

**Sub-part 1: Earth Resistance — 2 marks**

$$
R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) = \frac{120}{2 \pi \times 2.5} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.020}\right)
$$

$$
R = \frac{120}{15.708} \cdot \ln(500) = 7.639 \times 6.2146 \approx 47.49\ \Omega
$$

> **Verdict:** $R = 47.49\ \Omega \gg 5\ \Omega$ → **Fails**.
>
> **Two improvements (1 mark):**
> 1. Add **multiple parallel earth rods** (e.g., 10 rods → $R \approx 4.75\ \Omega$ ✓).
> 2. Apply **soil treatment with salt + charcoal + moisture** to lower $\rho_{\text{eff}}$.

**Sub-part 2: Wind Force — 1.5 marks**

$$
P_z = 0.6 \times 47^2 = 0.6 \times 2209 = 1325.4\ \text{N/m}^2
$$

$$
F = P_z \cdot A \cdot C_d = 1325.4 \times 0.28 \times 1.2 \approx 445.3\ \text{N}
$$

> **Result:** $F \approx 445\ \text{N}$. Design for $\geq 668\ \text{N}$ (with 1.5 SF).

**Sub-part 3: Threshold Increment — 1.5 marks**

$$
TI = 65 \cdot \frac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}} = 65 \cdot \frac{0.5}{(1.0)^{0.8}} = 65 \times 0.5 = 32.5\ \%
$$

> **Verdict:** $TI = 32.5\% > 20\%$ → ✗ **Fails** for the given requirement. Need to increase MH or use full cut-off optics.

**Sub-part 4: IP Rating — 1 mark**

> **Recommendation: IP66.**
>
> **Justification:** Coastal area → salt-spray + dust + heavy monsoon rainfall. IP66 provides:
> - **Dust-tight** (digit 6) — no salt/dust ingress.
> - **Powerful water jet protection** (digit 6) — withstands torrential rain.
>
> For **submersible or flood-prone sections**, upgrade to **IP67**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE MARKS in Public Lighting Safety questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in formula-based answers (e.g., $L$ in m vs. mm). Always convert mm → m **before** substitution.
> 2. **Confusing IP65 and IP66.** Examiners expect you to clearly state the *water pressure* (100 kPa) and *nozzle size* (12.5 mm) for digit '6'.
> 3. **Writing only "earth resistance should be low"** without quoting the numerical limit $\leq 5\ \Omega$ (per IS 3043). Always state the standard.
> 4. **Mixing up Class I and Class II.** Class I = basic insulation + protective earth. Class II = double/reinforced insulation, no earth needed. Marked with $\square$ symbol.
> 5. **Confusing disability glare (TI)** with **discomfort glare (G-class, CIE)**. They are different metrics.
> 6. **In CCT comparison questions, not mentioning skyglow or sleep disturbance** — examiners award marks for "soft" impacts.
> 7. **Not stating the wind zone (I to V)** for Kerala (Zone V) in wind-load problems.
> 8. **Forgetting safety factor (1.5 to 2.0)** in pole/luminaire structural design — design load = calculated load × SF.
> 9. **In photobiological questions, not naming IEC 62471 or IS 16108** — always cite the standard.
> 10. **Writing $\ln$ as log** — examiners penalise for using the wrong function. Use $\ln$ for natural log and $\log_{10}$ for base 10.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision — read this just before entering the exam hall.**

### ✅ Safety Pillars (memorize in order)
1. **Electrical** → Earthing ($\leq 5\ \Omega$), RCD (30 mA), Class I/II, SPD
2. **Photometric** → TI ($\leq 10$ / $20$ / $30$%), Uniformity $U_0 \geq 0.4$, Full cut-off
3. **Mechanical** → IS 875 wind load, foundation $\geq L/6$, IK08, galvanizing
4. **Photobiological** → IEC 62471, RG0/RG1, CCT $\leq 4000$ K for residential
5. **Environmental** → IP66 outdoor, IP67 flood, IP68 underwater
6. **Maintenance** → LOTO, PPE, trained personnel, 0.4 s disconnection

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- Earth resistance: **$\leq 5\ \Omega$** (lighting), **$\leq 1\ \Omega$** (high-mast)
- TI limits: **$10$ / $20$ / $30$ %** for motorway / urban / residential
- RCD: **$30\ \text{mA}$, $\leq 40\ \text{ms}$** trip time
- Touch voltage: **$\leq 50\ \text{V AC}$**
- Insulation resistance: **$\geq 1\ \text{M}\Omega$** at 500 V DC
- SPD Type 2: **$10\ \text{kA}$**, $8/20\ \mu\text{s}$ wave
- Kerala wind zone: **V**, $V_b = \mathbf{50\ \text{m/s}}$ (coastal)
- Foundation: **$L/6$ to $L/10$** of pole height
- Overhang rule: **$OH \leq 0.25 \cdot MH$**
- Spacing rule: **$S \leq 3$ to $4 \times MH$**
- LED safety CCT: **$\leq 3000$ K** residential, **$\leq 4000$ K** main roads
- IP for Kerala: **IP66 minimum** (IP67 flood zones)

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- $TI = 65 \cdot \dfrac{L_v}{L_{av}^{0.8}}$
- $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$ (vertical rod)
- $P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ (wind pressure)
- $F = P_z \cdot A \cdot C_d$ (wind force)

### ✅ Standards to Quote (always write the standard number!)
- **IEC 60598-1, 60598-2-3** → Luminaires
- **IEC 60529** → IP code
- **IEC 62262** → IK code
- **IEC 62471 / IS 16108** → Photobiological safety
- **IEC 61643-11** → SPD
- **IS 3043** → Earthing
- **IS 1944 / IS 13201** → Road lighting
- **IS 875 Part 3** → Wind load
- **CIE 140** → Road lighting calculations
- **Indian Electricity Rules, 1956** → General electrical safety

### ✅ Mnemonics
- **IP digits:** "**S**olid first, **W**ater second" → SW
- **Class I vs II:** "I has **E**arth, II is **I**nsulated twice" → EE
- **RG levels:** "**0** = **O**K, **3** = **3**eed help" → 0=3 danger
- **Six pillars:** "**E**lectric, **P**hoto, **M**ech, **B**io, **I**P, **M**aint" → **EPM-BIM**

### ✅ Typical 14-Mark Question Pattern (Module 4)
1. Define / explain a safety concept with standard (3–4 marks)
2. Numerical on TI / Earth resistance / Wind force (3–4 marks)
3. Comparison or design recommendation (3–4 marks)
4. Safety conclusion / justification (2–3 marks)

> **Final Tip:** When in doubt, **always quote the standard number** — it is worth 0.5 to 1 mark per question and shows examiner awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sports Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sports Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sports Lighting** is a specialised branch of illumination engineering concerned with the design, specification, and installation of lighting systems for indoor and outdoor sporting venues. It encompasses the provision of adequate **horizontal illuminance** (for player performance and safety), **vertical illuminance** (for spectator viewing and television broadcasting), and the control of **glare**, **flicker**, **uniformity**, and **spill light**, in accordance with international standards set by the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)**, the **IES (Illuminating Engineering Society)**, and sport-governing bodies (FIFA, ICC, BCCI, FIH, BWF).

The illuminance level is expressed in **lux (lx)**, which equals **1 lumen per square metre (1 lx = 1 lm/m²)**, and is the fundamental metric used to evaluate playing-area visibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEEET418 Module 4:**
> The syllabus mandates the study of *design parameters, floodlight mounting, beam aiming, glare control, TV broadcasting requirements, and standards (CIE/IES)* for sports lighting. Numerical problems on **number of floodlights**, **mounting height**, and **layout selection** are **frequently asked** in university examinations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine watching a **cricket match at 7 PM** under floodlights. The ball is travelling at 140 km/h, the fielder on the boundary must judge its trajectory against a dark sky, and simultaneously, a slow-motion camera is replaying every frame on the giant screen. Three things must happen at once:

1. The **fielder** must see the ball clearly → requires *high horizontal illuminance with low glare*.
2. The **spectator** in the stand must see the player's face, the ball's spin, and the umpire's signal → requires *adequate vertical illuminance* from multiple directions.
3. The **TV camera** recording at 300 frames per second must capture smooth motion → requires *constant-output, flicker-free lamps* with a high **Colour Rendering Index (CRI ≥ 80)** and correlated colour temperature of **≥ 4000 K**.

Sports lighting is therefore the engineering art of delivering *uniform, glare-free, flicker-free, colour-accurate light* to a fast-moving object (the ball) under a wide range of viewing conditions — for player, spectator, and camera simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sports lighting is unique in PEEET418:**
> Unlike interior (office/factory) lighting, sports lighting must satisfy *three sets of viewers* (player, spectator, broadcaster), operate at *much higher illuminance levels* (often 500–2500 lx), and handle *movement-based visual tasks* at high speeds.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used universally in sports lighting design:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Illuminance | $E_h$ | **lux (lx)** | 50 – 2500 lx |
| Vertical Illuminance | $E_v$ | **lux (lx)** | 50 – 1500 lx |
| Uniformity Ratio | $U_1, U_2$ | dimensionless | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Glare Rating | $GR$ | dimensionless | ≤ 50 |
| Colour Rendering Index | $R_a$ | dimensionless | ≥ 20 (recreational), ≥ 80 (TV) |
| Correlated Colour Temperature | $T_c$ | **kelvin (K)** | 3000 – 6000 K |
| Flicker Factor | $F_f$ | % | ≤ 5 % (slow-motion safe) |
| Mounting Height | $h$ | **metre (m)** | 10 – 60 m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Parameters for Sports Lighting

A sports lighting design is evaluated against **six primary design parameters** (per CIE 169:2005 and IES RP-6-15):

1. **Illuminance Level** — Both horizontal ($E_h$) and vertical ($E_v$) must meet class-specific requirements.
2. **Uniformity Ratio** — Defined as $U_1 = E_{min}/E_{avg}$ and $U_2 = E_{min}/E_{max}$. For international play, $U_1 \geq 0.7$ is mandatory.
3. **Glare Control** — Quantified by the **Glare Rating (GR)** system. For television, $GR \leq 50$ is required.
4. **Colour Rendering** — $R_a \geq 80$ is mandatory for any venue hosting televised events; $R_a \geq 90$ is preferred for HD.
5. **Flicker Control** — Achieved by using electronic ballasts (typically $\geq 100$ Hz supply frequency) or by avoiding discharge lamps on single-phase supplies.
6. **Spill Light Control** — Light emitted beyond the playing area must be limited (typically $< 25$ lux at 50 m from the boundary) to avoid nuisance to neighbours and reduce **sky glow**.

## 2.2 Floodlight Mounting Layouts

The most common mounting configurations are:

- **Corner (4-Tower) Layout** — Four floodlight towers located at the four corners of the playing area. Most common for cricket, football, and rugby stadiums.
- **Side-Mounted Layout** — Floodlights mounted on roof structures or on poles along the two longer sides. Common for tennis, badminton, and indoor arenas.
- **Linear / Catwalk Layout** — Floodlights suspended on overhead catwalks running parallel to the long axis. Used in large indoor stadiums and Olympic venues.
- **Combined Layout** — A hybrid of corner + side, used for asymmetric or multi-purpose venues.

## 2.3 Mounting Height Derivation (Theoretical Basis)

To prevent direct glare from the floodlight into the player's eyes, and to ensure an even distribution of light on the playing surface, the **mounting height ($h$)** of the floodlight tower must satisfy the geometric constraint:

$$h \geq 0.4 \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the diagonal distance from the tower base to the far-side corner of the playing area. The widely used thumb rule is:

$$h \approx \frac{D}{2} \quad \text{to} \quad \frac{D}{3}$$

For a square playing area of side $L$ (e.g., a football pitch of $L = 100$ m), the diagonal is:

$$D = L\sqrt{2} = 100\sqrt{2} \approx 141.4 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the minimum mounting height is:

$$h \geq 0.4 \times 141.4 \approx 56.6 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $h \geq 0.4D$?**
> This empirical rule ensures that the angle subtended by the floodlight at the player's eye is small enough (typically $\theta < 2^\circ$) to keep the floodlight outside the **disability glare** zone. Lowering $h$ below $0.4D$ causes the lamp to appear in the player's direct line of sight when the ball is lofted.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving Module 4 numerical problems in the KTU University Examination.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot N \cdot UF \cdot MF}{A}$ | Average illuminance from $N$ floodlights, each delivering $\Phi$ lumens | lx |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}$ | Number of floodlights required | dimensionless |
| 3 | $h \geq 0.4 \cdot D$ | Minimum mounting height (corner layout) | m |
| 4 | $D = L\sqrt{2}$ | Diagonal of square playing area (side $L$) | m |
| 5 | $D = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$ | Diagonal of rectangular playing area | m |
| 6 | $U_1 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}}$ | Uniformity ratio (CIE definition) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $U_2 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{max}}$ | Alternative uniformity ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{d}\right)$ | Aiming angle of floodlight beam to a point on the ground | degrees |
| 9 | $E_v = E_h \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Approximate vertical illuminance for tilted surface (Lambertian) | lx |
| 10 | $\text{CU} = \dfrac{\text{Lumens reaching surface}}{\text{Total lamp lumens}}$ | Coefficient of Utilization (CIE) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{LOR} = \dfrac{\text{Total lumens out of fitting}}{\text{Total lamp lumens}}$ | Light Output Ratio of luminaire | dimensionless |
| 12 | $E_{spill} = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos^3(\theta)}{h^2}$ | Spill-light illuminance at ground level (point-source approx.) | lx |
| 13 | $F_f = \dfrac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{E_{max} + E_{min}}$ | Flicker Factor of a lamp on AC supply | dimensionless |

> **Where:**
> $\Phi$ = luminous flux per floodlight (lm), $N$ = number of floodlights, $A$ = playing area (m²), $E$ = design illuminance (lx), $UF$ = Utilization Factor, $MF$ = Maintenance Factor, $D$ = diagonal distance, $L,W$ = length and width of playing area, $\theta$ = angle from vertical, $I$ = luminous intensity (cd).

## 2.5 Selection of Light Source

The four most common light sources used in modern sports lighting are:

- **Metal Halide (MH)** — High CRI ($R_a = 65$–$95$), good luminous efficacy ($80$–$100$ lm/W). The *de facto standard* for outdoor sports.
- **High-Pressure Sodium (HPS)** — High efficacy ($> 120$ lm/W) but poor CRI ($R_a = 25$). Used only for recreational/training venues.
- **LED Floodlights** — Efficacy $120$–$160$ lm/W, $R_a \geq 80$, instant on/off, long life ($50{,}000$ h). Rapidly becoming the *preferred choice* for new installations.
- **Induction Lamps** — Long life ($60{,}000$ h), low maintenance. Used in hard-to-access installations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Floodlights Formula

The **fundamental design equation** for any illumination system is the lumen method, which balances the lumens emitted by the lamps against the lumens absorbed/lost in transit to the working plane, the lumens required on the working plane, and the area of the plane.

**Step 1: Lumens emitted by one floodlight**

A single floodlight contains one or more lamps, each emitting $\Phi_{lamp}$ lumens. If each floodlight contains $n$ lamps, the total lumens emitted per floodlight are:

$$L_{1} = n \cdot \Phi_{lamp}$$

**Step 2: Lumens reaching the playing area**

Only a fraction of the emitted lumens actually fall on the playing area. This fraction is captured by the **Coefficient of Utilization (CU)**, which depends on the room/area geometry, the mounting height, and the luminaire's photometric distribution. The lumens reaching the playing area from one floodlight are:

$$L_{2} = L_{1} \cdot CU = n \cdot \Phi_{lamp} \cdot CU$$

**Step 3: Accounting for depreciation**

Over the lamp's life, lamp output decreases due to ageing, dirt accumulation on the luminaire, and lamp lumen depreciation. This is captured by the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**. The effective lumens available at the end of the maintenance cycle are:

$$L_{3} = L_{2} \cdot MF = n \cdot \Phi_{lamp} \cdot CU \cdot MF$$

**Step 4: Total effective lumens from $N$ floodlights**

If $N$ floodlights are used, the total effective lumens incident on the playing area are:

$$L_{total} = N \cdot n \cdot \Phi_{lamp} \cdot CU \cdot MF$$

**Step 5: Equating to the design requirement**

The design requires an average illuminance $E$ (in lux) over an area $A$ (in m²). The total lumens required on the playing area are:

$$L_{req} = E \cdot A \quad \text{[lumens]}$$

**Step 6: Equating $L_{total}$ and $L_{req}$**

Setting $L_{total} = L_{req}$:

$$N \cdot n \cdot \Phi_{lamp} \cdot CU \cdot MF = E \cdot A$$

**Step 7: Solving for $N$**

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{n \cdot \Phi_{lamp} \cdot CU \cdot MF}}$$

If each floodlight contains a single lamp ($n = 1$), the formula simplifies to the familiar KTU exam version:

$$N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The terms $CU$ and $UF$ are often used interchangeably in Indian textbooks (although strictly $UF$ refers to a slightly modified $CU$ for sports lighting where vertical illuminance is also considered). In KTU problems, treat them as synonymous unless specified otherwise.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Number of Floodlights for a Football Ground

**Problem:** A football ground measuring **100 m × 64 m** is to be floodlit for national-level play. The required average horizontal illuminance is **$E = 500$ lx**. The proposed installation uses **400 W metal halide floodlights**, each delivering **$\Phi = 32{,}000$ lumens**, with a **Coefficient of Utilization $UF = 0.4$** and a **Maintenance Factor $MF = 0.7$**. Calculate the number of floodlights required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the playing area**

$$A = L \times W = 100 \times 64 = 6400 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Identify all parameters from the problem statement**

$$E = 500 \text{ lx}, \quad \Phi = 32{,}000 \text{ lm}, \quad UF = 0.4, \quad MF = 0.7$$

**Step 3: Apply the floodlight formula**

$$N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values**

$$N = \dfrac{500 \times 6400}{32{,}000 \times 0.4 \times 0.7}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the denominator first**

$$32{,}000 \times 0.4 \times 0.7 = 32{,}000 \times 0.28 = 8960$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the numerator**

$$500 \times 6400 = 3{,}200{,}000$$

**Step 7: Final division**

$$N = \dfrac{3{,}200{,}000}{8960} = 357.14$$

**Step 8: Round up to the next whole number (always round up, never down)**

$$\boxed{N = 358 \text{ floodlights}}$$

**Step 9: Distribute across 4 corner towers**

$$N_{per\ tower} = \dfrac{358}{4} = 89.5 \rightarrow 90 \text{ floodlights per tower (with 2 spares on the 4th tower)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Mounting Height Calculation

**Problem:** Determine the minimum mounting height for the floodlight towers in a cricket stadium with a circular playing area of radius **$R = 90$ m** at the boundary, using the corner-tower layout.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Determine the playing-area geometry**

The diagonal distance $D$ from a corner tower to the far-side boundary is twice the radius:

$$D = 2R = 2 \times 90 = 180 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply the mounting-height rule**

$$h \geq 0.4 \cdot D = 0.4 \times 180 = 72 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Verify with the $h \approx D/2$ rule**

$$h \approx \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{180}{2} = 90 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Recommendation**

The lower bound ($h = 72$ m) is the *minimum* permissible; for better uniformity and glare control, the design height should be **$h = 75$ to $80$ m**.

$$\boxed{h_{min} = 72 \text{ m}, \quad h_{recommended} = 80 \text{ m}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Illuminance Requirements for Common Sports

| Sport | Playing Area (m × m) | Class I (Intl.) $E_h$ (lx) | Class II (National) $E_h$ (lx) | Class III (Local) $E_h$ (lx) | $R_a$ (min) | $T_c$ (K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cricket (Field) | 137 × 150 (oval) | 1500 – 2500 | 1000 – 1500 | 500 – 750 | 80 | 4000 – 6000 |
| Football | 100 × 64 | 1500 – 2000 | 500 – 1000 | 200 – 500 | 80 | 4000 – 6000 |
| Tennis (Outdoor) | 24 × 11 | 1000 – 1500 | 500 – 750 | 300 – 500 | 80 | 4000 – 6000 |
| Badminton (Indoor) | 13.4 × 6.1 | 750 – 1000 | 500 – 750 | 300 – 500 | 80 | 4000 |
| Basketball (Indoor) | 28 × 15 | 750 – 1500 | 500 – 750 | 300 – 500 | 80 | 4000 |
| Hockey | 91.4 × 55 | 1000 – 1500 | 500 – 750 | 200 – 500 | 80 | 4000 – 6000 |
| Swimming (Indoor) | 50 × 25 (pool) | 1000 – 1500 | 500 – 750 | 200 – 500 | 80 | 4000 |

> **Sources:** IES RP-6-15 (Sports Lighting), CIE 169:2005, FIFA Stadium Lighting Guide 2021.

## 3.5 Glare Index Computation (Brief)

The **Glare Rating (GR)** for a sports lighting installation is computed by the CIE formula:

$$GR = 27 + 24 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L_{av}}{L_{b}^{0.9}}\right)$$

where $L_{av}$ is the average luminance of the floodlight as seen by the observer, and $L_b$ is the average luminance of the background. For international televised sports, $GR \leq 50$ is required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sports Lighting Design Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete engineering workflow** for designing a sports lighting installation, from initial brief to final commissioning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Brief] --> B[Identify Sport and Class of Play]
    B --> C[Reference Standards: CIE 169, IES RP-6]
    C --> D[Select Required E_h and E_v Levels]
    D --> E[Choose Mounting Layout: Corner / Side / Combined]
    E --> F[Compute Diagonal D and Mounting Height h]
    F --> G[Select Floodlight Type: MH / LED / HPS]
    G --> H[Read Phi, CU, MF from Catalogue]
    H --> I[Apply N equals E times A divided by Phi times UF times MF]
    I --> J[Check Uniformity U1 and U2]
    J --> K{Uniformity Satisfied?}
    K -- No --> L[Re-arrange Tower Position or Beam Aiming]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Check Glare Rating GR]
    M --> N{GR within Limit?}
    N -- No --> O[Increase h or Add Louvres]
    O --> M
    N -- Yes --> P[Check Spill Light and Flicker]
    P --> Q[All Checks Passed]
    Q --> R[Finalise BoQ and Drawings]
    R --> S[End: Commissioning]
```

## 4.2 System Architecture — Floodlight Tower Subsystems

This block diagram decomposes the **physical and electrical subsystems** of a single floodlight tower.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOWER[Floodlight Tower]
        T1[Foundation and Civil Works] --> T2[Structural Mast: 30 to 80 m]
        T2 --> T3[Headframe with Multiple Beams]
        T3 --> T4[Floodlight Cluster: 80 to 100 fittings]
        T4 --> T5[Aiming Devices: Laser or Theodolite]
        T5 --> T6[Lightning Arrester and Aircraft Warning Light]
    end
    subgraph POWER[Power and Control Subsystem]
        P1[HT Supply: 11 kV] --> P2[Distribution Transformer 11kV to 415V]
        P2 --> P3[Main MCC Panel]
        P3 --> P4[Sub-Circuit Breakers per Phase]
        P4 --> P5[Individual Floodlight Driver or Ballast]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Control Subsystem]
        C1[DMX or DALI Control Bus] --> C2[Controller: 0 to 10 V or DALI]
        C2 --> C3[Scene Selector: 100 percent, 75 percent, 50 percent, Off]
        C3 --> P5
    end
    TOWER --- POWER
    CTRL --- POWER
```

## 4.3 Geometric Schematic — Mounting-Height Constraint

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **geometric relationship** between the mounting height $h$, the diagonal $D$, and the player's eye position.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GEO[Geometry of Corner Tower]
        G1[Player on Ground at Far Corner]
        G2[Mounting Tower Base: at Near Corner]
        G3[Floodlight Lamp Position: at Height h above Base]
        G4[Line of Sight from Player to Lamp]
    end
    G1 -- Horizontal Distance D --> G2
    G2 -- Vertical Height h --> G3
    G1 -- Line of Sight Angle theta --> G3
    G3 -- Constraint: theta less than 2 degrees --> G4
    G4 -- Therefore: h greater than 0.4 D --> G2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The player at the far corner of the playing area views the floodlight at an angle $\theta$ from horizontal. For the lamp to remain outside the disability-glare zone, $\theta \leq 2^\circ$, which translates to the constraint $h \geq 0.4 D$.

## 4.4 Luminaire Aiming Pattern — 4-Tower Cricket Stadium

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PITCH[Oval Cricket Ground 150 m x 137 m]
        P1[Boundary Edge]
        P2[30 yard Circle Inner]
        P3[Pitch 22 yards Central Strip]
        P4[Outfield]
    end
    subgraph T1[Tower 1: North East Corner]
        T1A[Aim: 30 yard Circle South West]
        T1B[Aim: Pitch Strip South West]
        T1C[Aim: Outfield South West]
    end
    subgraph T2[Tower 2: North West Corner]
        T2A[Aim: 30 yard Circle South East]
        T2B[Aim: Pitch Strip South East]
        T2C[Aim: Outfield South East]
    end
    subgraph T3[Tower 3: South East Corner]
        T3A[Aim: 30 yard Circle North West]
        T3B[Aim: Pitch Strip North West]
        T3C[Aim: Outfield North West]
    end
    subgraph T4[Tower 4: South West Corner]
        T4A[Aim: 30 yard Circle North East]
        T4B[Aim: Pitch Strip North East]
        T4C[Aim: Outfield North East]
    end
    T1A --> P2
    T2A --> P2
    T3A --> P2
    T4A --> P2
    T1B --> P3
    T2B --> P3
    T3B --> P3
    T4B --> P3
    T1C --> P4
    T2C --> P4
    T3C --> P4
    T4C --> P4
```
