# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q1. List and briefly explain any three design parameters that are specific to sports lighting but not relevant to interior (office) lighting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

The three key parameters specific to sports lighting are:

1. **Vertical Illuminance ($E_v$):** Sports lighting must provide adequate vertical illuminance on the players' faces, the ball, and the playing equipment so that spectators and television cameras (which view the action side-on) can clearly perceive the event. Interior lighting typically specifies only horizontal illuminance on the work plane.

2. **Flicker Factor:** Modern televised sports are broadcast in slow motion (often 100–300 frames per second). Conventional high-intensity discharge lamps on 50 Hz AC mains produce 100 light pulses per second, which causes stroboscopic flicker in slow-motion replays. Sports lighting therefore requires electronic ballasts ($\geq 100$ Hz) or DC operation to ensure a flicker factor below 5 %.

3. **Glare Rating (GR):** Because players must track a fast-moving ball against the dark sky, the disability-glare effect from floodlights directly affects performance. The CIE glare-rating system ($GR \leq 50$ for international play) is a unique specification for sports lighting, not applied in interior design.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q2. Why is the minimum mounting height of a floodlight tower in a 4-corner layout specified as $h \geq 0.4D$? What happens if this rule is violated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The rule $h \geq 0.4D$ is derived from the **disability-glare constraint**. For a player standing at the far corner of the playing area at a distance $D$ horizontally from the tower base, the angle $\theta$ subtended by the floodlight at the player's eye is:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{D}\right)$$

For $\theta \leq 2^\circ$ (the empirical limit below which the floodlight does not cause disability glare), substituting and using $\tan(2^\circ) \approx 0.035$:

$$h/D \geq 0.035 \cdot 11.5 \approx 0.4$$

**Consequence of violation:** If $h < 0.4D$, the floodlight enters the player's direct line of sight when the ball is lofted, causing:
- Loss of contrast between ball and sky.
- Temporary disability glare (veiling luminance).
- Impaired ball-tracking ability, leading to player safety hazards.
- Failure to comply with CIE 169 / IES RP-6 glare-rating limits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Students:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** the number of floodlights — always round up because you cannot install a fractional floodlight.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — playing area must be in m², illuminance in lx, lumens in lm. SI units only.
> 3. **Omitting the Utilization Factor (UF)** in the denominator — this is the single most common mistake.
> 4. **Confusing $D = L\sqrt{2}$ with $D = L + W$** — the diagonal of a rectangle is the square root of the sum of squares, not the arithmetic sum.
> 5. **Wrong standard reference** — citing "IS 3646" (interior lighting) instead of "CIE 169" or "IES RP-6" (sports lighting).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A football stadium has a playing field of dimensions **110 m × 75 m**. The required average horizontal illuminance for national-level play is **$E = 750$ lx**. The proposed installation uses **1000 W metal halide floodlights**, each delivering **$\Phi = 90{,}000$ lumens**, with **$UF = 0.35$** and **$MF = 0.65$**. Calculate:
   (i) the total number of floodlights required, and
   (ii) the number of floodlights per tower, assuming a symmetric 4-corner layout.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **4-corner floodlighting layout**. Discuss the merits and demerits of this layout compared with the **side-mounted layout**. State the standard mounting-height rule and justify it geometrically. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) (i) — Total Number of Floodlights (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute the playing area (1 mark)**

$$A = L \times W = 110 \times 75 = 8250 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Identify parameters (1 mark)**

$$E = 750 \text{ lx}, \quad \Phi = 90{,}000 \text{ lm}, \quad UF = 0.35, \quad MF = 0.65$$

**Step 3: Apply the floodlight formula (2 marks)**

$$N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and evaluate denominator (1 mark)**

$$\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF = 90{,}000 \times 0.35 \times 0.65 = 90{,}000 \times 0.2275 = 20{,}475$$

**Step 5: Evaluate numerator and final division (1 mark)**

$$N = \dfrac{750 \times 8250}{20{,}475} = \dfrac{6{,}187{,}500}{20{,}475} = 302.2$$

**Step 6: Round up (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{N = 303 \text{ floodlights (rounded up)}}$$

**(a) (ii) — Floodlights per Tower (2 marks)**

For a symmetric 4-corner layout:

$$N_{per\ tower} = \dfrac{N}{4} = \dfrac{303}{4} = 75.75 \rightarrow 76 \text{ floodlights per tower}$$

(Total becomes $4 \times 76 = 304$, with 1 spare, or accept $75$ per tower with 3 spares. Either is acceptable if justification is provided.)

---

**(b) 4-Corner Layout — Description, Merits, Demerits, Mounting Height (7 marks)**

**Description (2 marks):**
In the 4-corner (or 4-tower) layout, four floodlight towers are erected at the four corners of the playing area, well outside the boundary. Each tower carries a cluster of floodlights (typically 50–100) aimed at different sections of the playing field — the near side, the centre, and the far side — to ensure uniform horizontal and vertical illuminance across the entire playing area.

**Merits of corner layout (2 marks):**
1. Provides excellent vertical illuminance because the light arrives from four different directions, eliminating harsh shadows on the players' faces.
2. Symmetry of design makes illuminance calculations and aiming straightforward.
3. Towers can be located outside spectator seating, allowing unobstructed sight lines.
4. Well-suited to oval/circular playing areas (cricket, football, rugby).

**Demerits of corner layout (1 mark):**
1. Requires very tall towers (60–80 m), increasing civil costs.
2. Spill light and sky-glow are higher because of the steep aiming angles.
3. Cannot be used in stadia where the corner is occupied by a main stand.

**Comparison with side-mounted layout (1 mark):**
The side-mounted layout places floodlights along the two longer sides of the playing area on shorter poles (15–25 m). It is more economical for rectangular playing areas (tennis, hockey) and for indoor arenas, but provides inferior vertical illuminance and creates stronger shadows because the light arrives from only two sides.

**Mounting-height rule with geometric justification (1 mark):**
$$h \geq 0.4 \cdot D, \quad \text{where } D = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$$

This rule ensures that the angle $\theta = \tan^{-1}(h/D) \leq 2^\circ$, which is the empirical limit below which the floodlight does not cause disability glare to players viewing the ball against the sky.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define **Glare Rating (GR)** as per the CIE system. A cricket stadium uses 4 corner towers, each fitted with 80 metal halide floodlights of 1500 W rating, each delivering **$\Phi = 1{,}35{,}000$ lumens**. The playing area is **$160 \text{ m} \times 140 \text{ m}$**. The required illuminance for international televised matches is **$E = 1500$ lx**. Assuming **$UF = 0.40$** and **$MF = 0.75$**, determine:
   (i) whether the proposed installation satisfies the design illuminance, and
   (ii) the additional number of floodlights (if any) that must be installed to meet the requirement.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **specific lighting requirements for television broadcasting** of sports. Explain the role of **(i) Colour Rendering Index ($R_a$)**, **(ii) Correlated Colour Temperature ($T_c$)**, and **(iii) Flicker Factor** in determining the suitability of a light source for televised sports. Mention typical recommended values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) (i) — Check if the proposed installation satisfies the design (4 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute the playing area (1 mark)**

$$A = 160 \times 140 = 22{,}400 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Total number of floodlights installed (1 mark)**

$$N_{installed} = 4 \times 80 = 320 \text{ floodlights}$$

**Step 3: Compute the achieved illuminance using the formula (1 mark)**

$$E_{achieved} = \dfrac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}{A}$$

$$E_{achieved} = \dfrac{320 \times 1{,}35{,}000 \times 0.40 \times 0.75}{22{,}400}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate (1 mark)**

$$E_{achieved} = \dfrac{320 \times 40{,}500}{22{,}400} = \dfrac{1{,}29{,}60{,}000}{22{,}400} \approx 578.6 \text{ lx}$$

**Comparison:** $E_{achieved} \approx 578.6 \text{ lx} \ll E_{required} = 1500 \text{ lx}$

**Conclusion:** The proposed installation is **inadequate** by a factor of approximately **2.6**.

---

**(a) (ii) — Additional floodlights required (3 marks)**

**Step 1: Apply the formula to find the required $N$ (1 mark)**

$$N_{required} = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF} = \dfrac{1500 \times 22{,}400}{1{,}35{,}000 \times 0.40 \times 0.75}$$

**Step 2: Denominator evaluation (1 mark)**

$$1{,}35{,}000 \times 0.40 \times 0.75 = 40{,}500$$

**Step 3: Final calculation (1 mark)**

$$N_{required} = \dfrac{1500 \times 22{,}400}{40{,}500} = \dfrac{3{,}36{,}00{,}000}{40{,}500} \approx 829.6 \rightarrow 830 \text{ floodlights}$$

**Additional floodlights required:**

$$N_{additional} = 830 - 320 = \boxed{510 \text{ additional floodlights}}$$

(i.e., 128 more floodlights per tower, on average.)

---

**(b) TV Broadcasting Requirements (7 marks)**

**(i) Colour Rendering Index ($R_a$) — 2 marks:**
The $R_a$ quantifies how accurately a light source renders the colours of objects compared to a reference source. For televised sports, the camera "sees" the scene through the lamp's spectrum. If the lamp has poor $R_a$ (e.g., HPS, $R_a = 25$), the players' jersey colours, the green of the field, and the ball will appear distorted on the television screen. **Recommended value: $R_a \geq 80$ for standard TV and $R_a \geq 90$ for HD/UHD broadcasting.** Metal halide and modern LED floodlights easily meet this requirement.

**(ii) Correlated Colour Temperature ($T_c$) — 2 marks:**
The $T_c$ of the light source determines the "warmth" or "coolness" of the broadcast image. A warm source ($T_c \approx 3000$ K) gives a yellowish tint, while a cool source ($T_c \approx 6000$ K) gives a bluish tint. Different light sources mixed on the same playing area (e.g., MH on the field and HPS on the stands) create a noticeable colour mismatch in the broadcast. **Recommended value: $T_c$ between 4000 K and 6000 K, with all sources on the same venue within a $\pm 200$ K tolerance to ensure uniform white balance.**

**(iii) Flicker Factor — 3 marks:**
Modern television cameras operate at 50, 100, 200, or 300 frames per second (for slow-motion replay). Conventional HID lamps (MH, HPS) operated on 50 Hz AC mains produce a light output that pulsates at 100 Hz (twice the supply frequency). When viewed through a high-frame-rate camera, this pulsation appears as:
- **Visible flicker** on the broadcast.
- **Stroboscopic "stop-motion" effect** on rotating objects (e.g., a spinning ball, a player's legs).
- **Misleading slow-motion replays** that distort the actual speed of the action.

**Flicker Factor definition:** $F_f = \dfrac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{E_{max} + E_{min}}$ (using peak and trough values of the light waveform).

**Recommended value:** $F_f \leq 5$ % for slow-motion-safe installations. This is achieved by:
- Using **electronic ballasts** with operating frequency $\geq 100$ Hz (typically 100–400 Hz).
- Connecting lamps to **three different phases** of the supply to smooth out the combined light output.
- Using **DC-operated LED floodlights**, which are inherently flicker-free.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam (Module 4 — Sports Lighting)**

- **Definition:** Sports lighting is the design of illumination systems for sporting venues, satisfying the visibility needs of *players*, *spectators*, and *television broadcasters* simultaneously.
- **Key metrics:** $E_h$ (horizontal illuminance), $E_v$ (vertical illuminance), $U_1 = E_{min}/E_{avg}$, $U_2 = E_{min}/E_{max}$, $GR$ (glare rating), $R_a$ (CRI), $T_c$ (CCT), $F_f$ (flicker factor).
- **Master formula (KTU favourite):**
$$N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}$$
Always round $N$ **up** to the next whole number.
- **Mounting-height rule:** $h \geq 0.4D$, where $D$ is the diagonal from the tower base to the far corner. Equivalently, $h \approx D/2$.
- **Diagonal of a rectangle:** $D = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$. For a square: $D = L\sqrt{2}$.
- **Three common layouts:** Corner (4-tower), Side-mounted, and Combined.
- **Light source hierarchy (for TV):** LED > Metal Halide > Induction > HPS (HPS not recommended for TV due to low $R_a$).
- **TV broadcasting triple-check:** $R_a \geq 80$, $T_c \in [4000, 6000]$ K, $F_f \leq 5$ %.
- **Glare control:** Achieved by increasing mounting height, using cut-off luminaires (louvres), and ensuring $GR \leq 50$ for international venues.
- **Spill light:** Controlled by using sharp-cut-off floodlight optics and aiming angles; CIE recommends $< 25$ lx at 50 m beyond the boundary for residential neighbours.
- **Maintenance factor for sports lighting:** Typically lower than for interior lighting ($MF \approx 0.6$–$0.7$) because floodlights are exposed to weather, dust, and lamp lumen depreciation.
- **Standards to cite in exams:** **CIE 169:2005** (Sports Lighting), **IES RP-6-15** (Sports and Recreational Area Lighting), **FIFA Stadium Lighting Guide 2021** (football-specific), **BCCI Regulations** (cricket-specific).
- **Common examiner traps:** Mixing units (lx, lm, m², cd), omitting UF, using $D = L + W$ instead of $\sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$, rounding $N$ down instead of up, citing the wrong standard (IS 3646 is for interior lighting, not sports).

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Course Code:** PEEET418
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lighting for Hazardous Area
> - **Applicable Course Outcomes:** CO4 (Apply Indian & International lighting standards), CO5 (Select luminaires for specialised applications)
> - **Cognitive Level (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy):** L3 – Apply | L4 – Analyze
> - **Recommended Reading Time:** 90 Minutes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazardous Area** is defined under **IS/IEC 60079-0** and **OISD-STD-144** as a location in which an explosive atmosphere (a mixture of flammable substances in the form of gas, vapour, mist, dust, or fibres with air) is present, or may be expected to be present, in quantities that require special precautions for the construction, installation, and use of electrical apparatus — including luminaires.

In the context of **Illumination Engineering**, *Lighting for Hazardous Areas* is the discipline dealing with the design, selection, installation, and maintenance of luminaires that can be safely operated in environments containing **flammable gases (petrochemical refineries)**, **combustible dusts (grain silos, flour mills, coal handling plants)**, and **ignitable fibres (textile mills)** without acting as a source of ignition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Standards for Hazardous Area Lighting in India:**
> - **IS/IEC 60079-0 to 60079-41** — General & specific Ex-protection requirements
> - **OISD-STD-144** — Lighting in hydrocarbon facilities
> - **IS 5571** — Classification of hazardous areas
> - **PESO (Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation)** — Certifying authority
> - **NEC (NFPA 70), ATEX 2014/34/EU, IECEx Scheme** — International references

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaking LPG Cylinder" Model

Imagine a small kitchen with a **slowly leaking LPG cylinder**. You know that if you flick a switch, the spark may ignite the leaked gas. To stay safe, you would:
- (a) Identify *how often* the leak happens (zone classification),
- (b) Know *which gas* is leaking (gas group),
- (c) Ensure *nothing in the kitchen gets hotter than the gas's ignition temperature* (temperature class),
- (d) Use a **sealed, spark-proof switch box** (explosion-proof enclosure).

A **hazardous area luminaire** is engineered in exactly the same way — it is designed so that even if flammable gases enter the housing, the luminaire **cannot ignite** them. The four "filters" of safety are: **Zone + Gas Group + Temperature Class + Protection Type**.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on **applied safety engineering** and **standard-based design**. Examiner questions often test:
> - The student's ability to **map a real industry location** to the correct **Zone**.
> - **Selection logic** (Zone → Gas Group → Temp Class → Ex-type → IP).
> - **Numerical design** using the lumen method adapted for hazardous locations.

### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Illumination (Refineries, OISD-144)** | **100 – 200** | **lux** |
| **Minimum Illumination (Coal Handling)** | **50 – 100** | **lux** |
| **Hazardous Area Ambient Reference Temperature** | **+40** | **°C** |
| **Ignition Energy of Hydrogen (IIC)** | **0.020** | **mJ** |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy of Propane (IIA)** | **0.25** | **mJ** |
| **ATEX Dust Zone Threshold (Zone 22)** | **< 10 hrs/yr** | **operational time** |

## SECTION 2 : DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Zone Classification of Hazardous Areas (IS/IEC 60079-10-1 & 10-2)

The classification is the **first and most critical step** in luminaire selection. It quantifies *how frequently* an explosive atmosphere is likely to be present.

#### (A) For Gases & Vapours (Group II Equipment)

| Zone | Definition (Probability) | Typical Industry Locations | Allowed EPL (Equipment Protection Level) |
|:---:|---|:---|:---:|
| **Zone 0** | Explosive atmosphere **present continuously or for long periods (> 1000 hrs/yr)** | Inside storage tanks, vent stacks, vessel interiors | **Ga** |
| **Zone 1** | Likely to occur during **normal operation (10 – 1000 hrs/yr)** | Refinery pump decks, compressor stations, bulk storage tank farm periphery | **Gb** |
| **Zone 2** | Not likely in normal operation, only **briefly (< 10 hrs/yr)** | Vicinity of Zone 1, sealed pipe flanges, road tanker loading bays | **Gc** |

#### (B) For Combustible Dusts (Group III Equipment)

| Zone | Definition | Typical Industry Locations | Allowed EPL |
|:---:|---|:---|:---:|
| **Zone 20** | Combustible dust cloud **continuously or frequently present** | Inside grain silos, dust collectors, mills | **Da** |
| **Zone 21** | Likely during **normal operation** | Vicinity of filling/emptying points, conveyors | **Db** |
| **Zone 22** | Not likely, and only **briefly** | Bagging stations, packaging areas | **Dc** |

### 2.2 Gas Group Classification (Explosive Severity)

The gas group determines the **explosion pressure** the enclosure must withstand, with **Group IIC** being the most demanding.

| Group | Reference Gas | Minimum Ignition Energy | MESG (Max Experimental Safe Gap) |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|
| **IIA** | Propane | **0.25 mJ** | > 0.90 mm |
| **IIB** | Ethylene | **0.07 mJ** | 0.50 – 0.90 mm |
| **IIC** | Hydrogen, Acetylene | **0.020 mJ** | < 0.50 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Logic:** A luminaire certified for **Group IIC** is **automatically safe** for Groups IIB and IIA. The reverse is **not** permitted.

### 2.3 Temperature Classification (Surface Temperature Limits)

The maximum surface temperature of the luminaire (under any fault condition) must remain **below the auto-ignition temperature of the surrounding gas**.

| Temp Class | Max Surface Temperature (°C) | Suitable for gases with AIT > |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **T1** | **450** | 450 °C |
| **T2** | **300** | 300 °C |
| **T3** | **200** | 200 °C |
| **T4** | **135** | 135 °C |
| **T5** | **100** | 100 °C |
| **T6** | **85** | 85 °C |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Temperature Classes (Descending):**
> ***My Tiny Turtle Climbs Huge Rocks*** → **M**ethane/450 → **T**1/300 → **T**2/200 → **T**3/135 → **T**4/100 → **T**5/85 → **T**6 (Hydrogen)

### 2.4 Types of Explosion Protection (Ex-Methods) — The "Why & How"

| Ex-Code | Protection Principle | Typical Application | Why It Works |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **Ex d** | **Flameproof Enclosure** | Junction boxes, fluorescent luminaires in Zone 1 | Internal explosion is contained; gases cool below AIT through flame paths |
| **Ex e** | **Increased Safety** | Terminal boxes, lamp holders | No arcs/sparks/hot surfaces during normal operation; enhanced creepage/clearance |
| **Ex i** | **Intrinsic Safety (ia, ib, ic)** | Instrumentation, control gear | Circuit energy limited below ignition energy of any gas (even under fault) |
| **Ex p** | **Pressurized Enclosure** | Large control rooms, analyser houses | Inert gas (or air) pressurised above ambient — explosive gas cannot enter |
| **Ex o** | **Oil Immersion** | Transformers, starters | Arcing parts submerged in oil |
| **Ex q** | **Powder / Sand Filling** | Capacitors, small components | Fine powder quenches any arc |
| **Ex m** | **Moulding / Encapsulation (ma, mb, mc)** | Solenoids, electronic modules | Components sealed in resin — no contact with atmosphere |
| **Ex n** | **Non-Sparking (nA, nR, nC)** | Zone 2 luminaires | No arcs in normal operation + reduced risk of ignition |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ | Number of luminaires (Lumen Method) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{N \times \Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}{A}$ | Average illumination on the working plane | lux |
| 3 | $T_{\text{surface,max}} \le T_{\text{auto-ignition}} - \Delta T_{\text{safety}}$ | Surface temp must be less than gas AIT minus safety margin (typically 5 – 10 °C) | °C |
| 4 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{class,max}}}{T_{\text{auto-ignition}}}$ | Thermal Safety Factor (must be ≤ 1.0) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{MESG}_{\text{equipment}} \le \text{MESG}_{\text{gas}}$ | Equipment flame-path gap must be smaller than gas MESG | mm |
| 6 | $P_{\text{discharge}} \le P_{\text{strength of enclosure}}$ | Internal explosion pressure must not rupture the housing | bar |
| 7 | $R_{\text{th,fixture}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{hot spot}} - T_{\text{amb}}}{P_{\text{lamp}}}$ | Thermal resistance of luminaire | °C/W |
| 8 | $V_{\text{room index}} = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$ | Room index for UF selection (hazardous area warehouses) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners expect the use of **lower Utilization Factor (UF = 0.4 – 0.6)** and **lower Maintenance Factor (MF = 0.5 – 0.7)** in hazardous areas because:
> - Luminaires are often **dust-covered or fogged** (chemical environments).
> - **Cleaning frequency** is reduced due to safety protocols.
> - **Lamp lumen depreciation** is higher in hot ambient conditions.

### 2.6 IP (Ingress Protection) Requirements for Hazardous Areas

| Zone / Location | Minimum IP Rating |
|---|:---:|
| Zone 1 (Gas) | **IP54** (mandatory under IS/IEC 60079-0) |
| Zone 2 (Gas) | **IP44** (recommended **IP54**) |
| Zone 21 (Dust) | **IP65** |
| Zone 22 (Dust) | **IP5X** (dust-protected) – **IP6X** preferred |
| Offshore / Marine | **IP66 / IP67** |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals (BPCL, IOCL, RIL):** Use **Ex d** flameproof HID/MHL floodlights for tank farms.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants:** Use **Ex e** increased-safety fluorescent luminaires with **GRP (Glass Reinforced Polyester)** bodies.
- **Coal Handling Plants (NTPC, Adani):** Use **DIP (Dust-Ignition-Proof)** LED luminaires with **IP65** rating.
- **Offshore Platforms (ONGC):** Use **stainless-steel SS316L Ex d** floodlights resistant to salt-spray corrosion.
- **Aircraft Refuelling Depots:** Use **Ex d** LED luminaires with **T6** temperature class (AIT of aviation fuel ≈ 210 °C → T3 min, but T6 used for added safety).

## SECTION 3 : STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lumen Method for Hazardous Area Luminaires

The **Lumen Method** is the standard KTU-recommended approach for determining the number of hazardous area luminaires. We derive it from fundamental radiometric principles.

#### Step 1: Define the total luminous flux required on the working plane

The total lumens reaching the working plane must equal the product of the desired illumination and the area to be illuminated.

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = E \times A
$$

where $E$ is the required illumination in **lux** and $A$ is the area in **m²**.

#### Step 2: Account for the Utilization Factor (UF)

Only a fraction of the lamp flux reaches the working plane due to luminaire geometry, mounting height, and room reflectance. This fraction is the **Utilization Factor (UF)**.

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp, total}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{required}}}{\text{UF}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF}}
$$

#### Step 3: Account for the Maintenance Factor (MF)

Over time, lamp output depreciates and luminaires collect dust. The **Maintenance Factor** compensates for this loss. The required initial lamp output is therefore inflated.

$$
\Phi_{\text{lamp, initial}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{lamp, total}}}{\text{MF}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}
$$

#### Step 4: Divide by flux per luminaire

If each luminaire contains $n$ lamps, each producing $\Phi_L$ lumens, the number of luminaires is:

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{E \times A}{n \times \Phi_L \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for hazardous-area luminaire design.

#### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A petroleum bulk storage shed measuring **50 m × 20 m × 8 m (ceiling height)** is classified as **Zone 1, Gas Group IIA, Temperature Class T3**. The mounting height is **7 m**. Selected luminaire: **Ex d flameproof 250 W Metal Halide lamp**, each producing **19000 lumens**, with **1 lamp per luminaire**. Required illumination (per OISD-144): **150 lux**. Take **UF = 0.55** and **MF = 0.6** (harsh chemical environment). Calculate the number of luminaires.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

Given:
- $E = 150$ lux
- $A = 50 \times 20 = 1000$ m²
- $\Phi_L = 19000$ lumens
- $\text{UF} = 0.55$, $\text{MF} = 0.6$, $n = 1$

Applying the formula:

$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{n \times \Phi_L \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
N = \frac{150 \times 1000}{1 \times 19000 \times 0.55 \times 0.6}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
19000 \times 0.55 = 10450
$$

$$
10450 \times 0.6 = 6270
$$

Therefore:

$$
N = \frac{150000}{6270} = 23.92
$$

**Round up to the next whole number:**

$$
\boxed{N = 24 \text{ luminaires}}
$$

**Final Layout Suggestion:** Arrange in a **4 rows × 6 columns** grid (4 along 50 m side, 6 along 20 m side), giving spacing of **~12.5 m × 3.3 m** — well within the typical **spacing-to-mounting-height ratio of 1.2 – 1.5** for MHL floods.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Safety Factor Verification

**Problem:** A zone-1 area contains **ethylene (Gas Group IIB, AIT = 425 °C)**. The selected luminaire carries a **T4** marking. The maximum expected ambient temperature inside the shed is **+45 °C**. Verify whether the luminaire is thermally safe, and compute the maximum allowable lamp wattage if the luminaire thermal resistance is **0.45 °C/W**.

**Step 1 — Identify T-class maximum surface temperature:**

For T4, $T_{\text{class,max}} = 135$ °C.

**Step 2 — Apply the safety rule:**

$$
T_{\text{class,max}} \le T_{\text{AIT}} - \Delta T_{\text{safety}}
$$

$$
135 \le 425 - \Delta T_{\text{safety}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{safety}} = 425 - 135 = 290 \text{ °C} \quad \checkmark \text{Safe}
$$

The **Thermal Safety Factor (SF)** is:

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{T_{\text{class,max}}}{T_{\text{AIT}}} = \frac{135}{425} = 0.318
$$

Since $\text{SF} \le 1.0$, the T4 luminaire is **safe** in ethylene.

**Step 3 — Maximum allowable lamp wattage:**

From the thermal resistance formula:

$$
R_{\text{th,fixture}} = \frac{T_{\text{hot spot}} - T_{\text{amb}}}{P_{\text{lamp}}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_{\text{lamp,max}} = \frac{T_{\text{class,max}} - T_{\text{amb}}}{R_{\text{th,fixture}}}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
P_{\text{lamp,max}} = \frac{135 - 45}{0.45} = \frac{90}{0.45} = 200 \text{ W}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum lamp wattage is **200 W**. A **250 W MHL** would be unsafe → select a **150 W MHL** instead.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection Tree Summary

| Industry Scenario | Zone | Gas/Dust | Group | AIT (°C) | Min Temp Class | Recommended Ex-Type | IP |
|---|---|---|---|:---:|:---:|---|:---:|
| LPG Bottling Plant | 1 | Propane | IIA | 470 | T1 | Ex d | IP54 |
| Refinery Pump House | 1 | Hexane vapours | IIA | 225 | T3 | Ex d / Ex e | IP54 |
| Ethylene Cracker | 1 | Ethylene | IIB | 425 | T2 | Ex d | IP54 |
| Hydrogen Compressor | 1 | Hydrogen | IIC | 500 | T1 | Ex d (IIC) | IP54 |
| Petrol Pump Forecourt | 2 | Petrol vapour | IIA | 280 | T3 | Ex nR | IP54 |
| Grain Silo Interior | 20 | Grain dust | III | 210 | T3 | DIP (Dust-IP) | IP6X |
| Coal Conveyor Gallery | 21 | Coal dust | III | 250 | T3 | DIP | IP65 |
| Paint Spray Booth | 1 | Solvent vapours | IIA | 230 | T3 | Ex d | IP54 |
| Acetylene Cylinder Store | 1 | Acetylene | IIC | 305 | T2 | Ex d (IIC) | IP54 |
| Sulphur Handling | 22 | Sulphur dust | III | 232 | T3 | Ex tD | IP65 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU Examination):**
> 1. **Zone 0 / 20** → Only **Ex ia** or **Ex ma** allowed (no other method).
> 2. **Zone 1 / 21** → **Ex d**, **Ex e**, **Ex p**, **Ex ib**, **Ex mb** allowed.
> 3. **Zone 2 / 22** → **Ex n** series, or any of the above with reduced certification.
