## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question Paper)]
> **Define a hazardous area. With suitable examples, explain the Zone 1 and Zone 2 classifications of hazardous locations as per IS/IEC 60079-10-1.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **hazardous area** is a location where flammable gases, vapours, mists, dusts, or fibres may be present in the atmosphere in quantities sufficient to produce an explosive mixture with air, requiring special precautions for electrical equipment as per **IS/IEC 60079-10-1**. (1 mark)

**Zone 1** is an area in which an explosive gas atmosphere is **likely to occur during normal operation** (between 10 and 1000 hours per year). *Examples:* the immediate vicinity of pump seals in a refinery, compressor stations, and the area around frequently opened pipe flanges. (1 mark)

**Zone 2** is an area in which an explosive gas atmosphere is **not likely to occur during normal operation, and if it does, only for a short period** (less than 10 hours per year). *Examples:* the periphery of Zone 1, sealed flange joints, road tanker loading bays, and areas around solvent storage tanks. (1 mark)

> **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar pattern)]
> **Explain the temperature classification (T1–T6) for explosion-proof luminaires. Why is the selection of the correct temperature class critical in hazardous areas?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **temperature classification** limits the maximum surface temperature that an explosion-proof luminaire can attain under any operational or fault condition, ensuring it is always below the **auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** of the surrounding gas. (1 mark)

| Class | Max Surface Temp (°C) |
|:---:|:---:|
| T1 | 450 |
| T2 | 300 |
| T3 | 200 |
| T4 | 135 |
| T5 | 100 |
| T6 | 85 |

(1 mark for the table)

The selection is **critical** because if the luminaire surface temperature exceeds the AIT of the surrounding gas, it can ignite the explosive mixture, causing a catastrophic explosion. Hence, the rule $T_{\text{surface,max}} < T_{\text{AIT}}$ must always be satisfied with an adequate safety margin. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — DESIGN-ORIENTED

> [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question Paper)]
> **(a)** A petroleum storage shed measuring **40 m × 25 m × 9 m** (ceiling height) is classified as **Zone 1, Group IIA, T3**. The required illumination level (per OISD-144) is **150 lux**. You are required to design the lighting scheme using **Ex d flameproof 400 W Metal Halide luminaires**, each producing **32000 lumens** (1 lamp per luminaire). Mounting height is **8 m**. Use **UF = 0.55** and **MF = 0.6**. Calculate the number of luminaires required and suggest a suitable layout. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Explain the constructional features of a **flameproof (Ex d) luminaire** with a neat sketch. Also, list the **IP rating** and **temperature class** markings typically found on its nameplate. **(7 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Area: $A = 40 \times 25 = 1000$ m²
- Required illumination: $E = 150$ lux
- Lamp flux: $\Phi_L = 32000$ lumens per luminaire
- Number of lamps per luminaire: $n = 1$
- Utilization factor: $\text{UF} = 0.55$
- Maintenance factor: $\text{MF} = 0.6$

**Formula:**

$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{n \times \Phi_L \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}
$$

**Step-by-step calculation (3 marks):**

Substituting values:

$$
N = \frac{150 \times 1000}{1 \times 32000 \times 0.55 \times 0.6}
$$

Compute denominator step-by-step:

$$
32000 \times 0.55 = 17600
$$

$$
17600 \times 0.6 = 10560
$$

Therefore:

$$
N = \frac{150000}{10560} = 14.20
$$

**Round up:**

$$
\boxed{N = 15 \text{ luminaires}}
$$

(1 mark for correct answer)

**Suitable Layout (3 marks):**

A **5 rows × 3 columns** grid arrangement is recommended. The spacing along the 40 m length is $40/5 = 8$ m, and along the 25 m width is $25/3 = 8.33$ m. The spacing-to-mounting-height ratio is approximately $8/8 = 1.0$, which is acceptable for MHL floodlights (recommended range: 0.8 – 1.5). Luminaires should be mounted on cross-brackets fixed to the roof truss at a height of 8 m, with cable entries through **Ex d certified double-compression cable glands**.

**Part (b) — Flameproof Luminaire Construction (7 Marks):**

A **flameproof (Ex d) luminaire** is constructed to contain any internal explosion and prevent the transmission of flames to the external hazardous atmosphere. The key constructional features are: (5 marks)

1. **Cast Aluminium or GRP Body:** Heavy-duty enclosure that can withstand an internal explosion pressure of typically **10 bar** without rupture.
2. **Flame Paths / Joints:** All joints have precisely machined **flame paths** of length ≥ 12.5 mm and gap < 0.15 mm (for Group IIC) — these allow hot gases to escape but cool them below AIT.
3. **Tempered Glass or Borosilicate Window:** Mechanically strong to withstand internal pressure; usually fixed with a **threaded retaining ring** (no screws).
4. **Increased Safety Terminal Block (Ex e):** Mounted in a separate **terminal compartment** isolated from the lamp chamber by a **flameproof barrier**.
5. **External Earthing Terminal:** Mandatory for equipotential bonding per IS/IEC 60079-0.
6. **Cable Entry:** Through **Ex d certified double-compression cable glands** with sealing compound.

**Nameplate Markings (2 marks):**

A typical Ex d luminaire nameplate carries the following information, in the format:

$$
\text{Ex II 2G Ex d IIB T3 IP66}
$$

Meaning:
- **Ex** = Explosion-protected
- **II** = Surface industries (Group II)
- **2G** = Category 2 (Zone 1), Gas environment
- **Ex d** = Flameproof enclosure protection
- **IIB** = Gas group IIB (Ethylene)
- **T3** = Temperature class T3 (max surface 200 °C)
- **IP66** = Ingress protection (dust-tight + powerful water jets)

It also carries: **BIS Licence number**, **PESO approval number**, **serial number**, **year of manufacture**, and **ambient temperature range** (e.g., –20 °C to +55 °C).

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — THEORY-ORIENTED (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar pattern)]
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the different **types of explosion protection methods** used for luminaires in hazardous areas. Compare the suitability of **Ex d**, **Ex e**, and **Ex i** methods with respect to their principle of protection, typical applications, and permitted zones. **(8 marks)**

> **(b)** A **Zone 1** area contains **hydrogen gas (Group IIC, AIT = 500 °C)**. You are selecting an Ex-luminaire with a **thermal resistance of 0.6 °C/W** and a **temperature class of T4**. If the maximum ambient temperature is **+50 °C**, determine the **maximum allowable lamp wattage**. Comment on whether a **250 W MHL** can be used. **(6 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Explosion Protection Methods (8 Marks):**

| # | Ex Type | Principle | Application | Permitted Zone |
|:---:|---|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | **Ex d (Flameproof)** | Contains any internal explosion; cools gases through **flame paths** | Junction boxes, MHL/LED floodlights, switches | **Zone 1 & 2** |
| 2 | **Ex e (Increased Safety)** | Prevents arcs/sparks/hot surfaces by **enhanced creepage, clearance, and IP** | Terminal boxes, lamp holders, cable glands | **Zone 1 & 2** |
| 3 | **Ex i (Intrinsic Safety)** | Limits circuit energy below **ignition threshold** (ia < 0.02 mJ, ib < 0.07 mJ, ic < 0.3 mJ) | Instrumentation, sensors, control circuits | **Zone 0/1/2** (depending on level) |
| 4 | **Ex p (Pressurized)** | Enclosure pressurised with **clean air or inert gas** above ambient | Large control rooms, analyser houses, big luminaires | **Zone 1 & 2** |
| 5 | **Ex o (Oil Immersion)** | Arcing parts **submerged in mineral oil** | Transformers, starters, switchgear | **Zone 1 & 2** |
| 6 | **Ex q (Powder Filling)** | Fine **quartz powder** quenches any arc | Capacitors, electronic assemblies | **Zone 1 & 2** |
| 7 | **Ex m (Encapsulation)** | Components sealed in **thermosetting resin** | Solenoids, electronic PCBs, LED drivers | **Zone 0/1/2** |
| 8 | **Ex n (Non-Sparking)** | No arcs in normal operation; reduced risk | Zone 2 luminaires, motors | **Zone 2 only** |

(6 marks for the table and detailed explanation)

**Comparison (2 marks):**
- **Ex d** is the **heaviest and most expensive** but most robust; ideal where internal arcs are unavoidable (HID lamps).
- **Ex e** is **lighter and cheaper** than Ex d, used where arcs are absent in normal operation (terminals).
- **Ex i** is the **lightest and safest** but limited to **low-power circuits** (≤ 1.5 W) — not suitable for general lighting.

**Part (b) — Maximum Lamp Wattage Calculation (6 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Temperature class: T4 → $T_{\text{class,max}} = 135$ °C
- Thermal resistance: $R_{\text{th,fixture}} = 0.6$ °C/W
- Ambient temperature: $T_{\text{amb}} = 50$ °C
- Gas: Hydrogen (IIC, AIT = 500 °C)

**Step 1 — Verify thermal safety:** (1 mark)

$$
T_{\text{class,max}} = 135 \text{ °C} \le T_{\text{AIT}} = 500 \text{ °C} \quad \checkmark \text{ Safe}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply thermal resistance formula:** (3 marks)

$$
P_{\text{lamp,max}} = \frac{T_{\text{class,max}} - T_{\text{amb}}}{R_{\text{th,fixture}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{\text{lamp,max}} = \frac{135 - 50}{0.6} = \frac{85}{0.6} = 141.67 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude on 250 W suitability:** (2 marks)

$$
P_{\text{lamp,max}} = 141.67 \text{ W} < 250 \text{ W}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **250 W MHL lamp cannot be used** because the fixture surface would exceed the T4 limit of 135 °C. The maximum permitted lamp wattage is approximately **140 W**. Recommended replacement: a **150 W LED** (which produces equivalent light with much lower thermal output, typically 50–60 W equivalent heat dissipation, well within limits).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up** the number of luminaires — fractional luminaires are not installable; always use the **ceiling function** $\lceil N \rceil$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing Gas Groups with Temperature Classes** — they are *independent* parameters. Group IIB is about flame-path design; T3 is about surface temperature. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Missing the nameplate format** — students often write "Ex d" alone instead of the complete string `Ex II 2G Ex d IIB T3 IP54`. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring MF and UF** in numerical problems — Examiner specifically tests whether the student uses **harsh-environment factors (UF = 0.5–0.6, MF = 0.5–0.7)** rather than the standard 0.8/0.8. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing Zone 1 and Zone 2** in selecting Ex n (which is Zone 2 only). (–1 mark)

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bulletins — Memorise Before the Exam:**

- **Zone 0** = continuous hazard (Ex ia / Ex ma only). **Zone 1** = normal operation (Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex ib, Ex mb). **Zone 2** = abnormal/brief (Ex n series or higher).
- **Gas groups** in decreasing MESG and increasing severity: **IIA → IIB → IIC**. Equipment rated for **IIC** is universally safe.
- **Temperature classes T1 → T6** represent **decreasing** maximum surface temperature: **450, 300, 200, 135, 100, 85 °C**.
- **IP54 minimum** for Zone 1; **IP65+** for Zone 21/22 (dust environments).
- The **Lumen Method** for hazardous areas uses **lower UF (0.4–0.6)** and **lower MF (0.5–0.7)** than general lighting.
- **Ex d** = flameproof (containment). **Ex e** = increased safety (prevention). **Ex i** = intrinsic safety (energy limitation).
- **PESO (India)**, **IECEx (international)**, and **ATEX (EU)** are the three main certifying bodies.
- **Thermal Safety Factor** must always satisfy $T_{\text{class,max}} \le T_{\text{AIT}} - \Delta T_{\text{safety}}$.
- The **nameplate format**: `Ex [Group] [Category] [Environment] Ex [Type] [Gas Group] [Temp Class] [IP Rating]`.
- For **Zone 20/21/22 (dust)**, the protection is designated **DIP** (Dust-Ignition-Proof) or **Ex tD** under the modern IEC scheme.
- **Room index** $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m (L + W)}$ determines UF selection from manufacturer photometric data tables.
- The **minimum illumination** for refinery process areas (OISD-144) is **100 – 200 lux**; for storage sheds: **50 – 100 lux**.
- **Cable entries** in hazardous areas must use **double-compression Ex d glands** with **sealing compound** — single-compression glands are **prohibited**.
- **Earthing** is mandatory and must be **equipotential bonded** to the plant earth grid with resistance $\le 1 \text{ \Omega}$.
- **Re-selection of lamp wattage** in hot ambient conditions: always verify that the **hot luminaire surface temperature** does not exceed the **T-class limit** for the specific gas/dust present.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Efficient Lighting Systems: Strategies and Controls Like Dimmers, Motion and Occupancy Sensors, Photo Sensors and Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Efficient Lighting Systems: Strategies and Controls Like Dimmers, Motion and Occupancy Sensors, Photo Sensors and Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Efficient Lighting Systems – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> An **Energy Efficient Lighting System** is an integrated engineered solution that combines high-efficacy light sources, optimized luminaire design, and intelligent **Lighting Control Strategies (LCS)** to deliver the required illuminance with the minimum possible consumption of electrical energy, while conforming to visual comfort, safety, and aesthetic standards prescribed by national codes (e.g., ECBC, IS 3646, NBC 2016).

The "Energy Efficiency" dimension is governed by the fundamental photometric-to-electrical ratio:

$$\eta_{system} \;=\; \frac{\Phi_{useful} \;(lumens)}{P_{input} \;(Watts)} \quad \left[\frac{lm}{W}\right]$$

This parameter — the **luminous efficacy (η)** — is the cornerstone metric. Modern solid-state lighting (LED) typically delivers **100 – 200 lm/W**, whereas legacy incandescent lamps deliver merely **10 – 17 lm/W**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Controls Matter Even with LEDs?**
> Even with a perfectly efficient LED, *wasting* the light it produces (by illuminating empty rooms, over-lighting spaces, or operating during daylight hours) defeats the purpose. Controls ensure the lamp is **ON, at the right level, only when and where needed** — this is the central philosophy of Module 4.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Smart Faucet" of Light

Imagine a traditional water tap that gushes full-flow regardless of whether you need a trickle or a torrent. It wastes water. Now imagine a **smart faucet**:
- It detects your hand's presence (**occupancy sensor**).
- It senses ambient sunlight coming through the window and reduces its flow (**photo/daylight sensor**).
- You can dial down the pressure yourself when washing gently (**manual dimmer**).
- It automatically shuts off after you leave for a set period (**timer**).

**Lighting controls function identically for light energy.** They modulate, switch, or extinguish artificial illumination in response to occupancy, ambient daylight, scheduled events, or user preference — drastically reducing the **connected electrical load hours (kWh)** without compromising task visibility.

## 1.3 Classification of Lighting Control Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes lighting controls into the following functional families:

| # | Control Type | Primary Decision Variable | Energy Saving Driver |
|---|--------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Dimmers (Manual / Automatic)** | User-set or feedback-driven light level | Reduces wattage during partial-need periods |
| 2 | **Motion Sensors** | Continuous motion detection | Switches OFF when no activity detected |
| 3 | **Occupancy Sensors** | Presence / absence detection | Auto-ON / Auto-OFF based on human presence |
| 4 | **Photo Sensors (Daylight Harvesting)** | Ambient illuminance on task plane | Reduces/dims fixtures as daylight increases |
| 5 | **Timers / Time Schedules** | Astronomical clock, fixed schedule | Switches OFF lights in unoccupied time bands |

## 1.4 Standards & Codes Governing Energy Efficient Lighting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Reference Codes:**
> - **ECBC 2017 (Energy Conservation Building Code, India)** – prescribes LPD (Lighting Power Density) limits in W/m².
> - **IS 3646 (Parts I–IV)** – Code of practice for interior illumination.
> - **ASHRAE 90.1** – International benchmark for automatic lighting controls.
> - **NBC 2016 (National Building Code of India)** – mandates occupancy sensors in selected spaces.

The standard recommended **LPD** values for various building categories under ECBC are tabulated below for ready reference:

| Building Type | ECBC LPD Limit (W/m²) | Typical Achievable (W/m²) |
|---------------|----------------------:|--------------------------:|
| Office – Enclosed | **9.68** | 6 – 8 |
| Office – Open Plan | **8.07** | 5 – 7 |
| Conference Room | **10.76** | 7 – 9 |
| Corridor / Transition | **5.38** | 3 – 4 |
| Stairwell | **4.84** | 2.5 – 3.5 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dimming Control – The Most Versatile Strategy

### 2.1.1 Definition and Principle

A **dimmer** is a power-conditioning device that reduces the RMS voltage or current delivered to a lamp, thereby lowering its luminous flux output proportionally. Modern dimmers primarily use **Phase-Control Techniques**:

- **Leading-Edge (Forward-Phase) Dimming** – Triac-based; chops the *leading* edge of the AC sine wave.
- **Trailing-Edge (Reverse-Phase) Dimming** – MOSFET/IGBT-based; chops the *trailing* edge; preferred for LEDs due to lower EMI and smoother control.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic (Stepwise)

1. The dimmer receives a control input (manual knob, 0–10 V analog signal, DALI digital command, or wireless Zigbee/Bluetooth signal).
2. The internal semiconductor switch (Triac/MOSFET) determines the **firing angle (α)** within each half-cycle of the AC mains.
3. The fraction of the AC waveform transmitted to the load is:
   
   $$ \text{Conduction Angle} \;=\; (\pi - \alpha) \; \text{radians per half-cycle} $$

4. The RMS voltage seen by the lamp is:
   
   $$ V_{rms} \;=\; V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)} $$

5. The lamp's lumen output decreases non-linearly with reduced voltage/current — for incandescent, lumen output ∝ V^3.5 (approximately); for LEDs, lumen output is almost linearly proportional to forward current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Human Eye Perception – Stevens' Power Law:**
> The human eye perceives brightness logarithmically. A dimming to **50 % electrical power** is perceived as only ~**25–30 % reduction in brightness**, allowing significant energy savings with minimal perceptual change. This is the psychological foundation of dimming-based energy conservation.

### 2.1.3 Dimming Energy Saving Formula

$$ E_{saved} \;=\; P_{rated} \times \left(1 - \frac{\Phi_{dimmed}}{\Phi_{full}}\right) \times T_{dim} \times n $$

Where:
- $E_{saved}$ = Energy saved (Wh)
- $P_{rated}$ = Rated lamp wattage (W)
- $\Phi_{dimmed} / \Phi_{full}$ = Lumen ratio
- $T_{dim}$ = Hours operated at dimmed level (h)
- $n$ = Number of luminaires

## 2.2 Motion Sensors

### 2.2.1 Definition

A **Motion Sensor** is a non-contact electronic transducer that detects physical movement within its field of view (FOV) and produces an electrical switching signal. In KTU syllabus context, **PIR (Passive Infrared)** and **Ultrasonic** types dominate.

| Technology | Detection Mechanism | Best Suited For |
|------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **PIR** | Detects change in infrared radiation (heat signature) from moving body | Line-of-sight areas (corridors, offices) |
| **Ultrasonic** | Emits 25–40 kHz waves; detects Doppler shift on reflection | Enclosed spaces with obstructions (restrooms, storage) |
| **Microwave** | Emits GHz waves; Doppler-based | Large open areas, outdoor |
| **Dual-Technology** | Combines PIR + Ultrasonic for reliability | Critical-miss zones (conference rooms) |

### 2.2.2 Operating Logic

1. Sensor continuously monitors its detection zone.
2. Upon detecting motion → outputs HIGH signal → relay closes → lights ON.
3. A **Time-Delay** (hold-time) of typically **30 s to 30 min** keeps the lights ON after the last motion event.
4. If no further motion is detected within the hold-time → lights OFF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Favourite:**
> *"Differentiate between Motion Sensor and Occupancy Sensor."*
> **Answer (Memorize):**
> - **Motion Sensor** → Detects *movement*; may use PIR or Doppler. Often turns lights ON when motion is detected (manual-OFF or auto-OFF after delay).
> - **Occupancy Sensor** → Detects *presence* (stationary + moving) using dual-tech or ultrasonic; **fully automatic** (Auto-ON / Auto-OFF) without manual intervention.

## 2.3 Occupancy Sensors

Occupancy sensors are a *supersets* of motion sensors — designed to detect even **stationary occupants** (e.g., a person reading a book, typing quietly). The two dominant sub-categories are:

- **Wall Switch Occupancy Sensor** – replaces conventional wall switch; contains PIR + sometimes microphonic sensing.
- **Ceiling-Mounted Occupancy Sensor** – 360° FOV; recommended for 360° coverage up to ~150 m².

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Parameter – Coverage Pattern:**
> Manufacturers publish **coverage patterns** as top-view diagrams. Always verify the **major and minor motion** detection zones separately — a person typing has 100–150 mm of motion (minor); a person walking has 1–2 m (major).

## 2.4 Photo Sensors (Daylight Harvesting)

### 2.4.1 Definition

A **Photo Sensor** (also called a *Daylight Sensor* or *Photocell*) is a light-to-voltage transducer that outputs a signal proportional to the ambient illuminance incident on its sensing surface.

### 2.4.2 Daylight Harvesting Control Loop

1. Photo sensor is mounted on the **ceiling** or **task plane**, oriented to measure the *combined* daylight + electric light at the workspace.
2. The sensor output (typically 0–10 V or digital DALI) is fed to a **Lighting Control Module**.
3. The controller compares actual illuminance to a **setpoint** (e.g., 500 lux for office work).
4. If daylight contribution > setpoint margin → **dim or extinguish** electric lights.
5. If daylight contribution falls below setpoint → **raise** electric light output.

The closed-loop block schematic:

$$
\text{Daylight} \;+\; \text{Electric Light} \;\xrightarrow[\text{(feedback)}]{\text{measured by sensor}} \; \text{Controller} \;\xrightarrow[\text{command}]{\text{dimming}} \; \text{LED Driver}
$$

### 2.4.3 Energy Saving Equation (Continuous Dimming)

$$ E_{saved} \;=\; P_{rated} \times \left(1 - \frac{D_{factor}}{2}\right) \times T_{day} \times n $$

Where:
- $D_{factor}$ = Average daylight contribution ratio (0 to 1)
- $T_{day}$ = Daytime operating hours with daylight available (h)
- $n$ = Number of luminaires under photo-sensor control

The factor of **1/2** arises because the electric light varies linearly from full power (no daylight) to zero power (full daylight) across the daylight hours.

## 2.5 Timer-Based Controls

A **Timer** is a programmable clock-based relay that switches luminaires ON/OFF according to a pre-defined schedule. Modern digital astronomical timers can automatically compute **sunrise/sunset** times from geographic coordinates and adjust the schedule daily.

**Operating Logic:**

1. Programmer defines ON-time and OFF-time for each day of the week.
2. Real-Time Clock (RTC) tracks current time; in astronomical mode, latitude/longitude compute sunrise/sunset.
3. At programmed times → relay energizes/de-energizes → lights ON/OFF.
4. **Override** input allows manual ON for cleaning/security staff; auto-resumes after a preset period.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization Table for KTU Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta = \Phi / P$ | Luminous Efficacy | lm/W |
| 2 | $E_{saved} = P_{rated}(1-\Phi_{dim}/\Phi_{full}) \cdot T \cdot n$ | Dimming energy saving | Wh |
| 3 | $LPD = \sum P_{i} / A_{floor}$ | Lighting Power Density | W/m² |
| 4 | $E_{saved} = P_{rated}(1 - D_{factor}/2) \cdot T_{day} \cdot n$ | Daylight harvesting saving | Wh |
| 5 | $\%Savings = \frac{E_{saved}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage savings | % |
| 6 | $Payback = \frac{C_{install}}{E_{saved,annual} \times Rate}$ | Simple payback period | years |
| 7 | $V_{rms} = V_m\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\sin^2(\omega t)d(\omega t)}$ | Phase-controlled RMS voltage | V |
| 8 | $\Phi \propto V^{3.5}$ (Incandescent) | Incandescent lumen-voltage relation | — |
| 9 | $S = 1 - \frac{LPD_{actual}}{LPD_{ECBC}}$ | Compliance margin | fraction |
| 10 | $kWh_{annual} = P \times h_{operation} \times 365$ | Annual energy consumption | kWh |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Lighting Controls

In real-world deployments, integrated lighting controls (often called **Lighting Management Systems – LMS** or **Building Management Systems – BMS**) deliver:

- **30 – 60 % energy savings** in commercial offices.
- **Demand Response capability** — peak-load shedding by centralized command.
- **Predictive maintenance** — luminaire runtime tracking.
- **Compliance** with ECBC and LEED/BREEAM/GRIHA green-building certifications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Voltage of a Phase-Controlled Waveform (Leading-Edge Dimmer)

**Goal:** Derive the expression for $V_{rms}$ delivered to a lamp when a Triac-based dimmer fires at firing angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Define the waveform.**

For a sinusoidal mains voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, the Triac remains OFF from $\omega t = 0$ to $\omega t = \alpha$, and ON from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$ in each half-cycle.

$$
v(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t < \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the general RMS definition.**

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\,dt}
$$

For a half-cycle (period $\pi$), the RMS over the half-cycle is:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} v^2(t)\,d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the dimmer waveform.**

The lamp is OFF from $0$ to $\alpha$, so its contribution is zero. The integration reduces to:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the trigonometric identity.**

$$
\sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\,d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the integration.**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\frac{1}{2}\,d(\omega t) = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{2}
$$

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\frac{\cos(2\omega t)}{2}\,d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{4}\Big[\sin(2\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{1}{4}\big[\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)\big] = -\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{4}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$
V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi - \alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{4}\right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\,\sqrt{\frac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** for dimmer operation and is occasionally tested as a 7-mark derivation in KTU ESE.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Daylight Harvesting Energy Savings

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**

A north-facing open-plan office has **120 LED luminaires**, each rated at **40 W**. The area is fitted with a photo-sensor-based daylight harvesting system that, over a typical 9 AM – 6 PM workday (9 hours), averages a **daylight contribution ratio** $D_{factor} = 0.65$. The fixtures are dimmed continuously by the controller.
Calculate:
(a) The energy saved per day by the daylight harvesting system.
(b) The annual energy saved (assume 300 working days).
(c) The annual cost saving if electricity costs ₹9.50/kWh.
(d) The simple payback period if the controller + sensor installation costs ₹2,50,000.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Daily energy saving.**

Using Equation (4):

$$
E_{saved,day} = P_{rated} \times \left(1 - \frac{D_{factor}}{2}\right) \times T_{day} \times n
$$

$$
E_{saved,day} = 40 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.65}{2}\right) \times 9 \times 120
$$

$$
E_{saved,day} = 40 \times (1 - 0.325) \times 9 \times 120
$$

$$
E_{saved,day} = 40 \times 0.675 \times 9 \times 120
$$

$$
E_{saved,day} = 40 \times 0.675 \times 1080 = 40 \times 729
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{saved,day} = 29{,}160 \text{ Wh/day} = 29.16 \text{ kWh/day}\;}
$$

**Part (b) — Annual energy saved.**

$$
E_{saved,annual} = 29.16 \times 300 = 8748 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{saved,annual} \approx 8748 \text{ kWh/year}\;}
$$

**Part (c) — Annual monetary saving.**

$$
C_{saved} = 8748 \times 9.50 = ₹83{,}106/\text{year}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{saved} \approx ₹83{,}106/\text{year}\;}
$$

**Part (d) — Simple payback period.**

$$
Payback = \frac{C_{install}}{C_{saved}} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{83{,}106} = 3.008 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Payback \approx 3.0 \text{ years}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Check:** A payback of ~3 years for daylight harvesting is realistic in Indian commercial installations (typical range: 2–5 years). If payback exceeds 7 years, the project is generally considered non-viable.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Control Strategy

| Application | Primary Control | Secondary Control | Expected Saving |
|-------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------:|
| Open-plan office | **Photo-sensor (daylight)** | Occupancy sensor | 40 – 60 % |
| Conference room | **Occupancy sensor** | Manual dimmer | 35 – 55 % |
| Corridor / Stairwell | **Motion sensor (PIR)** | Timer (off-hours) | 50 – 75 % |
| Restroom | **Occupancy sensor (dual-tech)** | Timer | 60 – 80 % |
| Outdoor parking | **Astronomical timer + Photo sensor** | Motion sensor (boost mode) | 70 – 85 % |
| Warehouse | **Motion sensor + Hi-Lo step dim** | Photo sensor at skylights | 50 – 70 % |
| Server room | **Manual dimmer (24/7 lit)** | None — continuous | 0 – 10 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When answering design-type questions, always propose **at least two complementary control strategies** rather than relying on one — this demonstrates holistic engineering thinking.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture — Integrated Lighting Control System

The diagram below maps the *functional architecture* of a modern Lighting Management System (LMS) showing how dimmers, sensors, and timers interact with luminaires and the BMS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mains Power Supply 230V AC] --> B[Distribution Board MCB]
    B --> C[Lighting Control Module LCM]
    C --> D1[Phase Controlled Dimmer 1]
    C --> D2[Phase Controlled Dimmer 2]
    C --> D3[Phase Controlled Dimmer 3]
    D1 --> L1[LED Luminaire Zone A]
    D2 --> L2[LED Luminaire Zone B]
    D3 --> L3[LED Luminaire Zone C]
    S1[PIR Occupancy Sensor Zone A] --> C
    S2[Ultrasonic Occupancy Sensor Zone B] --> C
    S3[Photo Daylight Sensor Zone C] --> C
    T1[Astronomical Timer Scheduler] --> C
    K1[Manual Keypad Dimmer Wall Mount] --> C
    W1[Wireless Bridge Zigbee or BLE] --> C
    BMS[Building Management System Server] --> C
    C --> F[Feedback Telemetry to BMS Dashboard]
    BMS --> G[Energy Analytics and Reports]
```

## 4.2 Logic Flow — Daylight Harvesting Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Photo Sensor Reading Lux] --> Q{Sensed Lux vs Setpoint}
    Q -->|Excess Daylight| R[Reduce Dimming Signal]
    R --> S[LED Driver Receives Lower Current]
    S --> L1[LED Output Decreases]
    Q -->|Insufficient Daylight| T[Increase Dimming Signal]
    T --> U[LED Driver Receives Higher Current]
    U --> L2[LED Output Increases]
    L1 --> P
    L2 --> P
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Control Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Space to be Controlled] --> Q1{Is Space Occasionally Unoccupied?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Presence of Daylight from Windows?}
    Q1 -->|No| Timer[Astronomical Timer Only]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Photo[Photo Sensor Daylight Harvesting]
    Q2 -->|No| Occ[Occupancy Sensor]
    Occ --> Q3{Critical No Miss Zone?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Dual[Dual Tech PIR plus Ultrasonic]
    Q3 -->|No| PIR[PIR Only Motion Sensor]
    Photo --> Dim[Add Step Dimming for Fine Control]
    PIR --> Dim
    Dual --> Dim
    Timer --> Dim
    Dim --> End[Integrated LMS Solution]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block — Dimming Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AC_Mains[AC Mains 50Hz Sinewave]
        A1[Positive Half Cycle 0 to Pi]
    end
    subgraph Triac_Firing[Triac Firing Angle alpha]
        F1[Triac OFF from 0 to alpha]
        F2[Triac ON from alpha to Pi]
    end
    subgraph Load_Waveform[Voltage Across Lamp]
        V1[Zero Volts 0 to alpha]
        V2[Sinusoidal Portion alpha to Pi]
    end
    A1 --> F1
    A1 --> F2
    F1 --> V1
    F2 --> V2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEET418]`
**Explain the working principle of a PIR (Passive Infrared) motion sensor used in lighting control applications.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

A PIR motion sensor detects the **infrared (IR) radiation** emitted by the human body (wavelength ~9–10 μm). Its core element is a **pyroelectric crystal** divided into two halves behind a **Fresnel lens**.

1. **Idle state:** The two halves receive equal IR flux from the background → no differential signal.
2. **Person enters the field of view:** Body IR enters one half of the crystal first, then the other → a **differential voltage** is generated.
3. **Output stage:** This differential voltage is amplified and used to trigger a relay or solid-state switch that turns ON the luminaire.
4. **Hold-time circuit:** Keeps the light ON for a preset duration (typically 30 s – 30 min) after the last motion event.

The sensor is "passive" because it does not emit any radiation — it only senses.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET418]`
**Differentiate between Leading-Edge and Trailing-Edge dimmers in 3 points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Leading-Edge Dimmer | Trailing-Edge Dimmer |
|---|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Switching Device** | Triac (SCR-based) | MOSFET / IGBT |
| 2 | **Conduction** | Begins at firing angle $\alpha$ after zero-cross → current is *chopped* at the leading portion of the half-cycle | Switch ON at zero-cross, OFF at $\alpha$ → chops the *trailing* portion |
| 3 | **Compatibility** | Resistive loads (incandescent, halogen); poor with LED drivers | Capacitive loads; ideal for LED drivers; lower EMI |
| 4 | **Noise / Hiss** | Higher (sudden current rise) | Virtually silent |
| 5 | **Cost** | Lower | Slightly higher |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat functional diagram, explain the **working of a Daylight Harvesting System** using photo sensors. Discuss its energy-saving benefits for a commercial office building.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An educational institute replaces 200 incandescent lamps (each 100 W) with 18 W LED tubes and additionally installs PIR occupancy sensors. The occupancy sensors reduce the ON-time of the LEDs from 10 h/day to 4.5 h/day on average (250 working days/year). Calculate: (i) Daily energy saved by lamp replacement alone. (ii) Annual energy saved by occupancy control alone. (iii) Total annual monetary saving at ₹10/kWh. (iv) Simple payback if total installation cost is ₹3,50,000.

---

### Model Solution A:

#### Part (a) — Daylight Harvesting Working (7 marks)

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Daylight harvesting is a lighting control strategy that uses ambient daylight to offset the requirement for artificial lighting, automatically dimming or switching off electric luminaires when sufficient daylight is available.

2. **Functional Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Sunlight through window] → [Task Plane]
       ↓                              ↑
[Photo sensor on ceiling] → [Controller] → [Dimming signal] → [LED Driver] → [Luminaire]
       ↑                                                          ↓
       └────────── Feedback illuminance signal ←─────────────────┘
```

3. **Working Steps (2 marks):**
   - Photo sensor continuously measures the illuminance on the work-plane.
   - The controller compares the measured value with a pre-set target (e.g., 500 lux).
   - If measured > setpoint, controller reduces the LED driver current (dimming).
   - If measured < setpoint, controller raises the driver current.
   - Closed-loop regulation maintains constant task illuminance.

4. **Benefits (1 mark):** 30–60 % energy reduction in daylit zones; improved occupant comfort; complies with ECBC Daylight Integration requirements.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 200$ lamps
- $P_{incandescent} = 100$ W
- $P_{LED} = 18$ W
- $T_{full} = 10$ h/day, $T_{occupied} = 4.5$ h/day
- Working days = 250/year
- Tariff = ₹10/kWh
- Cost = ₹3,50,000

**(i) Daily saving by lamp replacement alone (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta P_{lamp} = 100 - 18 = 82 \text{ W per lamp}
$$

$$
E_{saved,day,lamp} = 82 \times 10 \times 200 = 164{,}000 \text{ Wh/day} = 164 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**(ii) Annual saving by occupancy control alone (1.5 marks):**

Time saved per day = $10 - 4.5 = 5.5$ h

$$
E_{saved,annual,occ} = 18 \times 5.5 \times 250 \times 200 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
= 18 \times 5.5 \times 250 \times 200 = 4{,}950{,}000 \text{ Wh/year} = 4950 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**(iii) Total annual monetary saving (2 marks):**

Total annual kWh saved (from both measures, but the LED replacement's contribution for the same 10 h/day should also be annualised):

$$
E_{saved,annual,lamp} = 164 \times 250 = 41{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 41{,}000 + 4{,}950 = 45{,}950 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
\text{Cost saving} = 45{,}950 \times 10 = ₹4{,}59{,}500/\text{year}
$$

**(iv) Simple payback (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{4{,}59{,}500} = 0.762 \text{ years} \approx 9.1 \text{ months}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write `82 W × 10 h × 200` *without unit consistency*. Always explicitly state **Wh/day** and **kWh/year** conversions — losing 0.5 marks here is very common.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** In part (iii), students sometimes *double-count* the LED wattage saving and occupancy time saving without realising that the occupancy-controlled hours also benefit from the lower LED wattage. Read the question carefully — the two savings are independent here.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Alternative]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various **lighting control strategies** used for energy conservation in modern buildings. Compare occupancy sensors and motion sensors with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the RMS output voltage of a **leading-edge phase-controlled dimmer** firing at angle $\alpha$. If $V_m = 325$ V and $\alpha = 60°$, find the RMS voltage delivered to the lamp.

---

### Model Solution B:

#### Part (a) — Lighting Control Strategies (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Modern buildings consume 20–30 % of their total electricity in lighting. Controls can reduce this by 30–75 %.

**Strategies (5 marks):**

1. **Dimming (Manual / Automatic):**
   - Uses phase-controlled semiconductor switches.
   - Reduces lamp wattage proportionally to user-set level.
   - Best for: Conference rooms, restaurants, auditoriums.

2. **Occupancy Sensors:**
   - Detect human presence via PIR + Ultrasonic dual technology.
   - Auto-ON / Auto-OFF logic.
   - Best for: Enclosed private offices, restrooms, meeting rooms.

3. **Motion Sensors (PIR only):**
   - Detect *movement* — may not detect stationary occupants.
   - Typically used in corridors, stairwells, parking.
   - Lower cost than full occupancy sensors.

4. **Photo Sensors / Daylight Harvesting:**
   - Senses ambient lux; dims electric lights automatically.
   - Best for: Perimeter zones near windows, atriums, skylight warehouses.

5. **Timers / Astronomical Schedules:**
   - Programmed ON/OFF based on time of day; astronomical mode adjusts to sunrise/sunset.
   - Best for: Façade lighting, outdoor signage, parking lots.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Motion Sensor | Occupancy Sensor |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| Detects | Movement only | Movement + Stationary presence |
| Best Use | Corridors, stairs | Offices, meeting rooms |
| False OFF risk | Higher (person sitting still) | Lower (dual tech) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

#### Part (b) — RMS Voltage Derivation (7 marks)

**Circuit Description (1 mark):**

A leading-edge dimmer consists of a **Triac** in series with the lamp across the AC mains. A **diac** triggers the Triac gate at firing angle $\alpha$ in each half-cycle.

**Derivation Steps (4 marks):**

The derivation is identical to **Section 3.1** above. Final boxed result:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation (2 marks):**

Given: $V_m = 325$ V, $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3$ rad.

$$
\frac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi} = \frac{\pi - \pi/3}{\pi} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
$$

$$
\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(120°) = \sin(60°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660
$$

$$
\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi} = \frac{0.8660}{2\pi} \approx 0.1378
$$

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{325}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{0.6667 + 0.1378} = 229.8 \times \sqrt{0.8045}
$$

$$
V_{rms} = 229.8 \times 0.8970 \approx 206.1 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_{rms} \approx 206 \text{ V}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Students frequently commit a unit error — confusing $V_m$ (peak) with $V_{rms,mains}$. In the formula, $V_m$ is the **peak** value. For 230 V RMS mains, $V_m = 230\sqrt{2} \approx 325$ V. Always clarify this in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — KTU Module 4 (PEEET418)**

- **Energy Efficacy η (lm/W)** is the single most important efficiency metric. LEDs ≈ 100–200 lm/W; incandescent ≈ 10–17 lm/W.
- **Five Core Control Strategies:** Dimmer, Motion Sensor, Occupancy Sensor, Photo Sensor, Timer.
- **Dimmer** uses **phase-angle control** (Triac = leading-edge; MOSFET = trailing-edge). The RMS voltage equation is: $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{\pi-\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$.
- **Motion Sensor** detects *movement* only; **Occupancy Sensor** detects *presence* (stationary + moving). Always remember this distinction — favourite 2-mark question.
- **PIR** detects body IR (9–10 μm) using a **pyroelectric crystal** + **Fresnel lens**; differential voltage triggers switching.
- **Dual-Technology** sensors combine PIR + Ultrasonic — used where missed-detection is unacceptable.
- **Photo Sensor / Daylight Harvesting:** Closed-loop dimming based on measured illuminance vs setpoint; typical saving 40–60 % in daylit zones.
- **Daylight saving formula:** $E_{saved} = P_{rated}\left(1 - \frac{D_{factor}}{2}\right) T_{day} n$.
- **Timer:** Astronomical timers use **latitude/longitude** to compute sunrise/sunset daily — important for outdoor lighting.
- **LPD (W/m²)** = Lighting Power Density, the ECBC compliance metric. ECBC office limit: **9.68 W/m²**.
- **Payback period** must be < 5 years for most KTU design problems to be deemed feasible.
- **Stevens' Power Law** — eye perceives brightness logarithmically; a 50 % electrical dim feels only ~25–30 % darker.
- **Human-centric note:** For KTU answers, always state *occupant comfort, code compliance, and cost* as three pillars justifying any lighting control design.
- **Line-of-sight limitation of PIR** — PIR cannot detect around corners or through glass/walls.
- **Hold-time** settings for occupancy sensors: typically 30 s – 30 min; longer hold-time wastes energy.
- **Integration with BMS** is now considered best-practice; LMS ↔ BMS ↔ HVAC synergy can yield 25–40 % total building energy reduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Software Used for Lighting Design: DIALux and Relux (Self Study)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET418 - ILLUMINATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Software Used for Lighting Design: DIALux and Relux (Self Study)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lighting Design Software** refers to a specialized class of computer-aided engineering (CAE) tools that use photometric data, ray-tracing algorithms, and validated mathematical models to simulate, calculate, and visualize the distribution of light within a defined architectural or outdoor space, in compliance with international and national illumination standards.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET418 – Illumination Technology)**, the two industry-standard lighting design platforms formally identified for **self-study** are:

1. **DIALux** — developed by *DIAL GmbH* (Ludenscheid, Germany), the German Institute for Applied Lighting Technology.
2. **Relux** — developed by *Relux Informatik AG* (Switzerland).

Both platforms are **free-to-use professional-grade** tools adopted globally by lighting designers, electrical consultants, and luminaire manufacturers to perform quantitative lighting calculations (illuminance, luminance, glare, uniformity, energy density) and produce standards-compliant documentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters for KTU examinations:**
> Although listed as *Self Study* in the syllabus, KTU frequently tests conceptual awareness of these tools in **Part A (3-mark)** questions. Understanding their capabilities, output parameters, and the standards they implement is essential for a holistic grasp of Module 4.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a classroom**. You have chosen the room dimensions, window positions, and the type of LED panel you wish to install. Before physically mounting any fixture, you need to answer several critical questions:

- *Will every desk receive at least **500 lux** as mandated by **IS 3646 / EN 12464-1**?*
- *Will students at the back row experience disturbing glare (will the **UGR** exceed 19)?*
- *Will the light be uniform across the blackboard, or will there be dark patches?*
- *How much electrical energy will the system consume per square meter per year (kWh/m²/year)?*

You **cannot** answer these questions accurately using a hand calculator and the lumen method alone for complex geometries. Instead, you build a **virtual 3D twin** of the classroom inside the computer, place virtual luminaires inside it, and let the software simulate millions of light rays bouncing off walls, ceilings, and work surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **DIALux and Relux are precisely this "virtual twin" engine.** They replace tedious hand calculations with accurate, standards-compliant, photorealistic simulations, just as *AutoCAD* replaced hand drafting and *MATLAB* replaced manual numerical solving.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Encountered

The following are the standard photometric quantities that these software packages compute, which the KTU student must recognize:

- **Illuminance ($E$)** — measured in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**
- **Luminance ($L$)** — measured in **cd/m²**
- **Luminous Intensity ($I$)** — measured in **candela (cd)**
- **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** — dimensionless index, computed per **CIE 117:1995**
- **Uniformity Ratio ($U_0$ or $U_1$)** — dimensionless
- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$)** — measured in **lumens (lm)**
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI / $R_a$)** — dimensionless, 0–100
- **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)** — measured in **Kelvin (K)**
- **Lighting Energy Numeric Indicator (LENI)** — measured in **kWh/m²/year**
- **Maintenance Factor (MF)** — dimensionless multiplier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always remember: **1 lux = 1 lumen per square meter**, i.e., $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$. This is the foundational equation that every lighting software internally evaluates at every calculation point on every surface.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Computational Philosophy of DIALux and Relux

Both DIALux and Relux use **radiosity-based ray tracing** combined with **precomputed photometric data files** (industry-standard formats: **.LDT** and **.IES**) to evaluate light transport. The operational logic can be decomposed into the following structured steps:

**Step 1 — Geometry Construction**
- The designer constructs a 3D model: rooms (cuboid or polygon), furniture (tables, chairs, machines), outdoor terrain, roads, facades, etc.
- Surfaces are assigned **reflectances** ($\rho$) for walls, ceiling, and floor.

**Step 2 — Luminaire Placement**
- Luminaires are imported from **manufacturer plugin catalogs** (e.g., Philips, Osram, Schneider, Havells, Bajaj).
- Each luminaire's **photometric data file** contains its luminous intensity distribution curve (LIDC) in the C-$\gamma$ coordinate system.

**Step 3 — Calculation Surface Definition**
- The user defines calculation planes: a horizontal work plane (e.g., 0.85 m above floor for offices), vertical planes (for blackboards), or task-area polygons.
- A grid of **calculation points** is automatically generated across these surfaces.

**Step 4 — Photometric Computation**
- For every calculation point, the software computes the **direct component** using the inverse square law combined with the cosine of incidence:

$$E_{point} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{I_i(\alpha, \beta) \cdot \cos \theta_i}{d_i^2}$$

where:
- $I_i(\alpha, \beta)$ is the luminous intensity of luminaire $i$ in the direction of the point
- $\theta_i$ is the angle of incidence
- $d_i$ is the distance from luminaire to point

- The **interreflected component** (light bouncing off walls/ceiling) is added via radiosity/ray-tracing iterations.

**Step 5 — Result Aggregation**
- The software derives average illuminance ($\bar{E}$), minimum illuminance ($E_{min}$), maximum illuminance ($E_{max}$), uniformity $U_0 = \dfrac{E_{min}}{\bar{E}}$, and UGR.

**Step 6 — Standards Compliance Check**
- Results are automatically compared against the limit values in **EN 12464-1** (indoor workplaces), **EN 12464-2** (outdoor workplaces), **EN 12193** (sports), or **IS 3646** (India).
- A red/green pass-fail indicator is displayed.

**Step 7 — Report Generation**
- A PDF report is exported containing input data, calculation results, false-color renderings, isolux diagrams, luminaire schedule, and energy consumption.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance | $E = \Phi / A$ | **lux (lx)** | Light falling on a surface |
| 2 | Point-source inverse square | $E = I / d^2$ | **lx** | Direct illuminance from a point |
| 3 | Cosine-of-incidence | $E = (I / d^2) \cdot \cos \theta$ | **lx** | Illuminance on a tilted plane |
| 4 | Luminance | $L = I / (A \cdot \cos \alpha)$ | **cd/m²** | Brightness sensation |
| 5 | Average illuminance | $\bar{E} = \Phi_{total} \cdot MF / A$ | **lx** | Mean value on a surface |
| 6 | Uniformity | $U_0 = E_{min} / \bar{E}$ | dimensionless | Evenness of illumination |
| 7 | Uniformity (task/area) | $U_1 = E_{min} / E_{max}$ | dimensionless | Alternate uniformity index |
| 8 | Glare rating (UGR) | $UGR = 8 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.25}{L_b} \sum \dfrac{L^2 \omega}{p^2}\right)$ | dimensionless | Discomfort glare index |
| 9 | Lumen method | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot MF \cdot U}$ | count | Number of luminaires required |
| 10 | Energy efficiency | $LENI = W / (A \cdot \text{hours})$ | **kWh/m²/yr** | Specific energy consumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every DIALux/Relux output report, the student will encounter the quantities in the rows above. The **UGR formula** is a CIE-standard empirical expression; the software merely evaluates it numerically.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural Lighting Design:** Office buildings, hospitals, schools, malls — verifying EN 12464-1 compliance.
- **Industrial Lighting:** Factories, warehouses — ensuring task illuminance and uniformity.
- **Sports Lighting:** Stadiums, indoor courts — verifying EN 12193 horizontal and vertical illuminance requirements.
- **Road & Tunnel Lighting:** Street lighting design using photometric performance curves.
- **Emergency Lighting:** Escape route illuminance per **EN 1838**.
- **Facade & Accent Lighting:** Visual simulation for aesthetic verification.
- **Energy Audits:** Quantifying kWh/m²/year for green-building certifications (**LEED, GRIHA, IGBC**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is descriptive (self-study), the derivations below focus on the **foundational photometric equations** that every lighting software internally implements. A KTU student who masters these can interpret *any* software-generated output with confidence.

---

### 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law from Radiometry

The luminous intensity $I$ of a point source is defined as the luminous flux $\Phi$ emitted per unit solid angle $\Omega$:

$$
I = \dfrac{d\Phi}{d\Omega}
$$

A point source radiates uniformly into the full solid angle of a sphere, which equals $4\pi$ steradians. The flux intercepted by a sphere of radius $d$ is therefore:

$$
\Phi_{sphere} = I \cdot 4\pi
$$

This same flux is distributed over the surface area of the sphere, which is $4\pi d^2$. The illuminance (flux per unit area) on the sphere is therefore:

$$
E = \dfrac{\Phi_{sphere}}{A_{sphere}} = \dfrac{I \cdot 4\pi}{4\pi d^2}
$$

Cancelling the $4\pi$ terms yields the **Inverse Square Law of Illuminance**:

$$
\boxed{E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}}
$$

**Textual Explanation of each step:**
- *Step 1:* We start with the definition of intensity.
- *Step 2:* For a perfectly isotropic point source, all emitted flux passes through the imaginary sphere.
- *Step 3:* The same flux now must illuminate a larger surface as $d$ increases.
- *Step 4:* The $4\pi$ cancels, leaving an inverse-square relationship.

---

### 3.2 Inclusion of Cosine of Incidence (Lambert's Cosine Law)

If the receiving surface is tilted at an angle $\theta$ with respect to the direction of incident light, only the projected area $A \cdot \cos\theta$ intercepts the flux. Therefore:

$$
E_{\theta} = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}
$$

This is the **fundamental point-by-point illuminance equation** used internally by every node in DIALux and Relux.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Lumen (Utilization Factor) Method

Consider a room of area $A$ requiring an average illuminance $\bar{E}$. The total luminous flux required on the working plane is:

$$
\Phi_{required} = \bar{E} \cdot A
$$

However, the actual flux emitted by the luminaires must be greater, because of three multiplicative losses:

1. **Utilization Factor ($U$)** — fraction of lamp flux that reaches the working plane after direct + inter-reflected paths. Depends on Room Index ($k$) and surface reflectances.
2. **Maintenance Factor ($MF$)** — accounts for lamp lumen depreciation and luminaire dirt accumulation over time.
3. **Lamp flux per luminaire ($\Phi_L$)** — flux emitted by a single lamp/luminaire.

The number of luminaires required is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{total\,emitted} &= \dfrac{\bar{E} \cdot A}{U \cdot MF} \\
\text{Number of luminaires } N &= \dfrac{\Phi_{total\,emitted}}{\Phi_L} = \dfrac{\bar{E} \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot U \cdot MF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- *Step 1:* Total flux demanded by the work plane.
- *Step 2:* Multiply by reciprocal of losses to get required raw flux.
- *Step 3:* Divide by per-luminaire flux to get count $N$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While this equation is what you would use by hand, DIALux and Relux use a **point-by-point summation** (Eq. 3.1) which is far more accurate for irregular geometries — hence the need for software.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DIALux vs Relux Feature Comparison

| Feature / Capability | DIALux (DIAL GmbH) | Relux (Relux Informatik AG) |
|---|---|---|
| Developer | German Institute for Applied Lighting Technology | Relux Informatik AG, Switzerland |
| Latest Version | **DIALux evo** | **ReluxDesktop** |
| License | **Free** for designers | **Free** (ReluxEnergy, ReluxConnect) |
| Indoor Design | ✔️ Full 3D | ✔️ Full 3D |
| Outdoor / Road Lighting | ✔️ DIALux evo outdoor | ✔️ ReluxStreet module |
| Emergency Lighting | ✔️ Per EN 1838 | ✔️ Strong dedicated module |
| Sports Lighting | ✔️ Per EN 12193 | ✔️ Per EN 12193 |
| Daylight / Glare | ✔️ Integrated | ✔️ Integrated |
| Energy Evaluation | ✔️ LENI calculation | ✔️ Detailed energy dashboard |
| Manufacturer Plugins | **Massive** (1000+ brands) | Large (most major brands) |
| Photometric File Support | **.LDT, .IES** | **.LDT, .IES** |
| Standard Compliance | EN 12464-1/2, EN 12193, EN 1838, AS/NZS | EN 12464-1/2, EN 12193, EN 1838, SIA |
| BIM / Revit Integration | ✔️ IFC, Revit plugins | ✔️ ReluxConnect for Revit |
| Output Reports | **PDF, print-ready** | **PDF, print-ready** |
| Rendering Quality | Photorealistic (ray-traced) | Photorealistic (radiosity) |
| Primary User Base | Europe, Asia (including India) | Europe, project-based consultants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are Mermaid block-level architecture flowcharts representing the workflow and architecture of both lighting design platforms. These comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (purely alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown formatting inside node labels).

### 4.1 DIALux / Relux — Standard Workflow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[USER INPUT: ROOM GEOMETRY] --> A2[USER INPUT: SURFACE REFLECTANCES]
    A2 --> A3[USER INPUT: LUMINAIRE PLACEMENT]
    A3 --> A4[USER INPUT: CALCULATION SURFACES AND GRID]
    A4 --> B1[IMPORT .LDT OR .IES PHOTOMETRIC DATA]
    B1 --> C1[CORE ENGINE: RAY TRACING AND RADIOSITY]
    C1 --> C2[COMPUTE DIRECT ILLUMINANCE AT EACH POINT]
    C2 --> C3[COMPUTE INTERREFLECTED COMPONENT]
    C3 --> C4[AGGREGATE RESULTS: AVERAGE MIN MAX UNIFORMITY]
    C4 --> D1[CALCULATE UGR GLARE INDEX]
    D1 --> D2[ENERGY METRICS: LENI KWH PER M2 PER YEAR]
    D2 --> E1[STANDARDS CHECK: EN 12464 IS 3646 EN 12193]
    E1 --> F1[VISUAL OUTPUT: 3D RENDER FALSE COLOR ISOLUX]
    E1 --> F2[REPORT OUTPUT: PDF SCHEDULE BOQ]
    F1 --> G1[DESIGN REVIEW AND ITERATION]
    F2 --> G1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are model questions patterned exactly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination** pattern. Even though the topic is "Self Study," KTU examiners frequently include 3-mark definition questions from such topics. The 14-mark questions train you to integrate this knowledge with the rest of Module 4 (lighting calculations).

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
*Define lighting design software. Name any two lighting design software tools used in industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lighting design software is a specialized computer-aided engineering tool used by illumination engineers to simulate, calculate, and visualize the distribution of artificial and daylight illumination in indoor and outdoor environments. It uses photometric data files (.LDT/.IES) and ray-tracing/radiosity algorithms to compute illuminance ($E$ in **lux**), luminance ($L$ in **cd/m²**), uniformity ($U_0$), and glare (UGR) in compliance with standards such as **EN 12464-1** and **IS 3646**. The two industry-standard tools are **DIALux** (by DIAL GmbH, Germany) and **Relux** (by Relux Informatik AG, Switzerland). Both are free professional-grade software widely used by consultants and luminaire manufacturers worldwide.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
*List any six photometric or energy parameters that are output by DIALux/Relux after a lighting calculation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six parameters output by DIALux/Relux are:
1. **Average illuminance** ($\bar{E}$) in lux
2. **Minimum illuminance** ($E_{min}$) in lux
3. **Maximum illuminance** ($E_{max}$) in lux
4. **Uniformity ratio** ($U_0 = E_{min} / \bar{E}$)
5. **Unified Glare Rating** (UGR) — dimensionless
6. **Specific energy consumption** (LENI) in **kWh/m²/year**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on Module 4 carry **14 marks** and require internal choice (typically two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR a single integrated problem). Below are TWO complete 14-mark alternatives.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *DIALux-Focused*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step workflow of performing an indoor lighting design using **DIALux evo**. List the major input data, calculation engine, and output deliverables. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** A **classroom** of dimensions $12\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m} \times 3.5\,\text{m}$ (L × B × H) is to be designed using LED panel luminaires. The work plane is at $0.85\,\text{m}$ height. Target average illuminance $\bar{E} = 500\,\text{lx}$, utilization factor $U = 0.65$, maintenance factor $MF = 0.8$, and each LED panel emits $\Phi_L = 3600\,\text{lm}$. Compute:
> - (i) Number of luminaires required (round up to nearest integer).
> - (ii) Total connected load if each panel consumes $36\,\text{W}$.
> - (iii) Specific energy consumption if the room operates **8 hours/day, 250 days/year**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DIALux Workflow (7 marks):**

The workflow of indoor lighting design using DIALux evo consists of the following steps:

1. **Project Initialization:** Open DIALux evo → New Indoor Project → Define project name, location, and designer details.
2. **Geometry Construction:** Build room(s) using the Room Builder — input length, width, height; optionally insert openings (doors, windows), columns, and furniture from the object library.
3. **Surface Material Assignment:** Assign reflectances to ceiling ($\rho_c \approx 0.7$), walls ($\rho_w \approx 0.5$), and floor ($\rho_f \approx 0.2$).
4. **Luminaire Selection:** From the online catalog or manufacturer plugin (e.g., Philips, Havells), drag-drop luminaires into the room. Assign mounting type (recessed, surface, suspended) and height.
5. **Calculation Surface Definition:** Insert a horizontal calculation plane (work plane at 0.85 m) and any task-area surfaces.
6. **Calculation Execution:** Click *Calculate*. The ray-tracing engine computes illuminance at every grid point and aggregates to average, min, max, uniformity, UGR.
7. **Standards Compliance Check:** DIALux automatically compares results to EN 12464-1 limits and flags pass/fail.
8. **Visualization:** View 3D renderings, false-color illuminance maps, and isolux diagrams.
9. **Report Export:** Export a comprehensive PDF containing input data, luminaire schedule, results table, energy analysis (LENI), and rendered visuals.

The functional block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) captures this workflow.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- Room area $A = 12 \times 8 = 96\,\text{m}^2$
- Target $\bar{E} = 500\,\text{lx}$
- $U = 0.65$, $MF = 0.8$, $\Phi_L = 3600\,\text{lm}$

**(i) Number of luminaires:**

Using the lumen method equation:

$$
N = \dfrac{\bar{E} \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot U \cdot MF}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{500 \times 96}{3600 \times 0.65 \times 0.8} \\
N &= \dfrac{48000}{1872} \\
N &= 25.64
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding **up** to the nearest integer:

$$
\boxed{N = 26\,\text{luminaires}}
$$

**(ii) Total connected load:**

$$
P_{total} = N \times P_{panel} = 26 \times 36 = \boxed{936\,\text{W}}
$$

**(iii) Specific energy consumption (LENI):**

Annual energy consumption:

$$
E_{annual} = P_{total} \times t = 0.936\,\text{kW} \times (8 \times 250)\,\text{h} = 0.936 \times 2000 = 1872\,\text{kWh/year}
$$

Specific energy:

$$
LENI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A} = \dfrac{1872}{96} = \boxed{19.5\,\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Relux + Standards-Focused*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Compare **DIALux** and **Relux** in terms of (i) developer and origin, (ii) outdoor lighting capability, (iii) emergency lighting module, (iv) energy evaluation, and (v) photometric file formats supported. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** With the help of a neat flowchart, describe how **photometric data files (.LDT and .IES)** are generated and consumed by lighting design software. Why is the use of these standardized files critical for engineering accuracy? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | DIALux | Relux |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Developer / Origin | DIAL GmbH, Germany | Relux Informatik AG, Switzerland |
| (ii) Outdoor Capability | DIALux evo outdoor with street/road templates | Dedicated **ReluxStreet** and **ReluxTunnel** modules |
| (iii) Emergency Lighting | Supported per EN 1838 | **Dedicated** emergency lighting module with strong reporting |
| (iv) Energy Evaluation | LENI calculation built-in | **ReluxEnergy** — detailed energy dashboard |
| (v) Photometric Formats | .LDT, .IES | .LDT, .IES |

Both are free, both support EN 12464-1, EN 12193, EN 1838, and both offer BIM (Revit/IFC) integration. DIALux has a larger plugin ecosystem; Relux has stronger emergency and energy modules.

**(b) Photometric Data Pipeline (7 marks):**

**Flowchart (see Section 4.3 for the rendered diagram):**
- Manufacturer measures the luminaire in a **goniophotometer**.
- The instrument records luminous intensity in every direction (C-$\gamma$ coordinates).
- Software formats this data into either **.LDT** (EULUMDAT, European standard) or **.IES** (Illuminating Engineering Society, North American standard).
- The lighting designer imports this file into DIALux or Relux and assigns it to a virtual luminaire instance.
- The calculation engine uses this data to evaluate intensity in any arbitrary direction for every ray during ray tracing.

**Why standardization is critical:**
- *Interoperability:* A single .IES file can be used across dozens of software platforms.
- *Manufacturer neutrality:* Designers are not locked into one vendor's ecosystem.
- *Engineering accuracy:* The data is measured (not approximated), so simulation reflects real performance.
- *Standards compliance:* Verified photometric data ensures the calculated illuminance and UGR values are trustworthy for code-compliance submissions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing illuminance ($E$, lux) with luminance ($L$, cd/m²).** They are *not* the same. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the Maintenance Factor ($MF$)** in the lumen-method equation. Many students write $N = \bar{E} \cdot A / \Phi_L$ and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Not rounding up** the number of luminaires — it must always be rounded *up* (ceiling), never truncated.
> 4. **Wrong unit for LENI** — must be **kWh/m²/year**, not just kWh.
> 5. **In Part B diagrams**, students often skip the calculation grid and standards-compliance step — both are mandatory to mention for full marks.
> 6. **Self-study topics** still require mention of at least **two software names and their developers** for full credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **DIALux** is developed by **DIAL GmbH (Germany)**; **Relux** by **Relux Informatik AG (Switzerland)**.
- ✅ Both are **free professional-grade** lighting design tools.
- ✅ Both use **ray tracing + radiosity** with **photometric data files (.LDT, .IES)**.
- ✅ **.LDT** = EULUMDAT (European format); **.IES** = IESNA (North American format).
- ✅ Outputs include: average/min/max illuminance ($E$ in lux), uniformity ($U_0$), UGR, LENI.
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I / d^2$.
- ✅ **Cosine Law:** $E = (I/d^2) \cdot \cos\theta$.
- ✅ **Lumen method:** $N = \dfrac{\bar{E} \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot U \cdot MF}$.
- ✅ **LENI** is the energy metric in **kWh/m²/year**.
- ✅ **Key Standards implemented:** EN 12464-1 (indoor workplaces), EN 12464-2 (outdoor), EN 12193 (sports), EN 1838 (emergency), IS 3646 (India).
- ✅ **DIALux evo** has 1000+ manufacturer plugins (largest ecosystem); **Relux** has dedicated Street, Tunnel, Energy modules.
- ✅ Both support **BIM integration** via IFC and Revit plugins.
- ✅ Workflow: **Geometry → Luminaire Placement → Calculation Surface → Compute → Standards Check → Report**.
- ✅ **Maintenance Factor ($MF$)** must always be included; **Uniformity** is $E_{min}/\bar{E}$ (not $E_{min}/E_{max}$ unless specifically requested as $U_1$).
